topics.title: Fundamentals of Visual Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Visual Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Visual Testing

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Testing (VT)**, also referred to as **Visual Inspection (VI)**, is the oldest, most fundamental, and most widely used Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method. It involves the systematic, direct or indirect observation of the surface condition and overall integrity of a component or structure using the unaided human eye or aided by optical instruments such as borescopes, microscopes, magnifying glasses, and mirrors.

According to **ASNT (American Society for Nondestructive Testing)** and referenced in **ASTM E270 – Standard Practice for Contact Ultrasonic Testing** and **ASME Section V, Article 9**, Visual Testing is officially recognized as a valid NDT method. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies VT under the **Module 1: Fundamentals of NDT**, emphasizing its role as the primary baseline inspection technique performed before, during, and after all other NDT methods.

The governing standard for Visual Testing is:
- **ASTM E270** – Standard Reference Photographs for Liquid Penetrant and Magnetic Particle Indications
- **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Article 9** – Visual Examination
- **AWS D1.1** – Structural Welding Code (Visual Inspection requirements)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Car Mechanic Inspecting a Used Vehicle**
> Imagine you are buying a second-hand car. Before any computerized diagnostic is run, the mechanic first walks around the car, looks at the body for dents, checks the tires, peers under the hood, and uses a torch to look into tight engine cavities. This **systematic visual observation** to detect surface defects, corrosion, wear, cracks, and misalignments is exactly what **Visual Testing** does in engineering.
>
> The mechanic's **eyes** are the sensor, the **torch** is the illumination aid, and the **mirror on a stick** (used to see under the car) is equivalent to a **borescope** in industrial VT.

In an engineering context, the **human eye** acts as the **primary transducer/sensor**, the **brain** processes the data, and the inspector makes a **go/no-go decision** regarding the fitness of the component. VT can detect:
- Surface cracks
- Corrosion and pitting
- Weld defects (undercut, porosity, lack of fusion visible at surface)
- Misalignment of parts
- Dimensional deviations
- Surface finish irregularities
- Loose or missing fasteners

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most critical physical parameters governing VT are:
> - **Illuminance (E):** Measured in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**. The standard minimum inspection illumination is **500 lux (50 foot-candles)** as per ASME Section V.
> - **Visual Acuity:** The ability of the human eye to distinguish fine details. The accepted near-vision acuity for a qualified VT inspector is **Jaeger Number 1 (J-1) or equivalent (Snellen 20/25)** at a reading distance of approximately **30–40 cm**.

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum Inspection Illumination | $E_{min}$ | 500 | lux |
| Recommended High-Contrast Lighting | $E_{rec}$ | 1000 | lux |
| Snellen Acuity Requirement | $A_{eye}$ | 20/25 | dimensionless |
| Jaeger Chart Standard | $J$ | 1 or 2 | dimensionless |
| Standard Viewing Distance | $d$ | 30 – 40 | cm |
| Color Temperature (Daylight Reference) | $T_{c}$ | 6500 | K |
| Human Eye Spectral Response Range | $\lambda$ | 380 – 780 | nm |

## 1.4 Why Visual Testing is Performed First

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cost:** VT requires minimal equipment — only eyes and light.
> - **Speed:** Entire components can be inspected in minutes.
> - **Pre-emptive Screening:** Approximately **80% of all service-related defects** are surface-breaking and can be detected visually.
> - **Universal Applicability:** Can be used on any material (metals, composites, polymers, ceramics) and at any stage of manufacturing or service.

This is why the KTU 2024 syllabus positions Visual Testing as **Module 1, Topic 1**, establishing it as the **gateway method** upon which all other NDT techniques build.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Visual Testing

Visual Testing fundamentally relies on the interaction of **visible light** (electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range of **380 nm to 780 nm**) with the surface of the test object. The inspector perceives information when **photons** reflected, scattered, or transmitted from the object surface stimulate the **photoreceptor cells (cones and rods)** in the retina of the eye.

### 2.1.1 The Human Eye as an Optical Instrument

The eye can be modeled as a complex lens system with the following structural analogues:

| Eye Component | Optical Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Cornea | Primary refracting surface | Transmits and refracts light |
| Pupil (Iris) | Variable aperture stop | Controls light intensity (f/2 to f/16 equivalent) |
| Lens | Adjustable focus lens | Variable focal length (accommodation) |
| Retina | Image sensor (CCD/CMOS equivalent) | Photoreception (cones for color, rods for low light) |
| Optic Nerve | Data transmission cable | Transmits signal to visual cortex |
| Visual Cortex | Image processor | Interprets and analyzes the data |

### 2.1.2 Photometric vs. Radiometric Quantities

Visual testing is governed by **photometric** quantities (weighted by the eye's spectral sensitivity curve, the **CIE V(λ) photopic luminosity function**) rather than purely radiometric ones.

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi_v$):** Total light energy emitted per second, weighted by eye sensitivity. Unit: **lumen (lm)**.
- **Luminous Intensity ($I_v$):** Luminous flux per unit solid angle. Unit: **candela (cd)**.
- **Illuminance ($E_v$):** Luminous flux incident per unit area. Unit: **lux (lx) = lm/m²**.
- **Luminance ($L_v$):** Luminous intensity per unit projected area. Unit: **cd/m²** (also called "nit").

## 2.2 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The successful execution of Visual Testing follows a structured five-step methodology:

1. **Preparation of the Surface**
   - Clean the surface to remove oil, dirt, paint, and scale that may obscure defects.
   - Ensure adequate access and safe working conditions (scaffolding, lighting arrangements).

2. **Establish Adequate Illumination**
   - Provide a minimum of **500 lux** (50 foot-candles) of white light at the inspection surface.
   - Use directed or oblique lighting to enhance defect visibility (cracks, pits, undercuts).
   - Adjust for specular vs. diffuse reflection characteristics of the material.

3. **Direct Visual Examination**
   - The inspector views the surface with the unaided eye.
   - For remote areas, use mirrors, borescopes, or fiberscopes.

4. **Indirect (Aided) Visual Examination**
   - Use optical aids: **magnifying glasses (2× to 10×)**, **stereo microscopes**, **borescopes**, **videoprobes**, **photographic documentation**.
   - Record observations using **handwritten logs, sketches, digital images, or video**.

5. **Evaluation and Reporting**
   - Compare observations against **acceptance criteria** (codes, drawings, specifications).
   - Classify defects (type, size, location) and decide: **Accept / Reject / Repair**.
   - Issue a **Visual Testing Report (VTR)** signed by a qualified Level II/III inspector.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles all exam-critical equations used in Visual Testing calculations. These are the formulas KTU examiners test in numericals.

| S.No. | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance (Inverse Square Law) | $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^2}$ | lux | Light intensity at distance $d$ from source |
| 2 | Luminous Flux from Intensity | $\Phi_v = I_v \cdot \Omega$ | lumen | Total light emitted in solid angle $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Solid Angle of a Cone | $\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta)$ | steradian | Beam spread from a point source |
| 4 | Conversion: Foot-candle to Lux | $E_{lx} = 10.76 \cdot E_{fc}$ | lux | Light meter unit conversion |
| 5 | Conversion: Lux to Foot-candle | $E_{fc} = 0.0929 \cdot E_{lx}$ | fc | Light meter unit conversion |
| 6 | Magnification of Optical Aid | $M = \dfrac{25\text{ cm}}{f}$ | dimensionless | Lens magnification (where $f$ = focal length) |
| 7 | Resolving Power (Rayleigh Criterion) | $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$ | radians | Smallest distinguishable detail |
| 8 | Minimum Resolvable Size | $s = d \cdot \theta_{min}$ | mm or μm | Smallest defect detectable at distance $d$ |
| 9 | Snellen Acuity Definition | $\dfrac{\text{Test Distance}}{\text{Letter Size}}$ | dimensionless | Eye vision rating |
| 10 | Photopic Luminous Efficacy | $K = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P} = 683$ lm/W | lumen/watt | Conversion of radiant to luminous power at 555 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** When solving illumination problems, students MUST remember the **inverse square law** — this is the single most tested concept in Module 1 numericals. Always state the assumption of a **point source** in your solution.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Visual Testing is not merely a "look and see" method; in modern industry, it forms the cornerstone of:

- **Pre-service and In-service Inspection** of pressure vessels, pipelines, and nuclear reactors.
- **Weld Quality Assurance** in construction of bridges, ships, and offshore platforms.
- **Aerospace Component Inspection** (e.g., turbine blades, fuselage skin) where **borescopy** is mandatory.
- **Automotive Quality Control** on 100% of critical safety components.
- **Forensic Engineering** — failure analysis often begins with visual examination of fracture surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Fact:** According to ASNT, more than **60% of all NDT findings** in petrochemical and aerospace industries are detected first by VT before being confirmed by other techniques. This makes VT not just the oldest, but also the **most economically significant** NDT method.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Illuminance Using the Inverse Square Law

### Problem Statement
A point light source emits a luminous intensity of **200 candela (cd)**. Calculate the illuminance on a surface located at a perpendicular distance of **2 meters** from the source.

### Analytical Setup
The fundamental definition of illuminance ($E_v$) is luminous flux per unit area:

$$E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{A}$$

For a point source, the luminous flux ($\Phi_v$) spreads uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$. The relationship between luminous flux and intensity is:

$$\Phi_v = I_v \cdot \Omega$$

where the solid angle $\Omega$ subtended by a sphere as seen from its center is **$4\pi$ steradians**. Therefore:

$$\Phi_v = 4\pi \cdot I_v$$

The area of the sphere at distance $d$ is:

$$A = 4\pi d^2$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Substitute flux and area into the illuminance equation:**

$$E_v = \frac{\Phi_v}{A} = \frac{4\pi \cdot I_v}{4\pi \cdot d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel the common $4\pi$ term:**

$$E_v = \frac{I_v}{d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Inverse Square Law:**
The equation $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^2}$ is the **Illuminance Inverse Square Law**.

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E_v = \frac{200 \text{ cd}}{(2 \text{ m})^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator:**

$$(2 \text{ m})^2 = 4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$E_v = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ lux}$$

### Interpretation
The illuminance at 2 meters is **50 lux**, which is **below the ASME minimum of 500 lux**. Therefore, this light source is **inadequate** for visual inspection. The inspector must either (a) move closer, (b) use a higher-intensity lamp, or (c) add additional lamps.

### Verification Check
> If we move the surface to $d = 0.5$ m:
> $E_v = \dfrac{200}{0.25} = 800$ lux — now exceeds the minimum requirement. This confirms the inverse-square behavior.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Resolvable Defect Size (Rayleigh Criterion Applied to Human Eye)

### Problem Statement
An inspector is using a borescope to view the inside of a tube. The pupil diameter of the inspector's eye is **4 mm** under inspection lighting. Assuming the inspection is performed under **monochromatic green light of wavelength 550 nm**, calculate:
1. The minimum angular resolution.
2. The minimum linear size of a defect that can be resolved at a viewing distance of **250 mm** through the borescope.

### Analytical Setup
The Rayleigh Criterion defines the minimum angular separation $\theta_{min}$ between two point sources for them to be resolved:

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of light = $550 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $D$ = diameter of the aperture (pupil) = $4 \times 10^{-3}$ m

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities:**

$$\lambda = 550 \text{ nm} = 5.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$D = 4 \text{ mm} = 4.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$1.22 \times \lambda = 1.22 \times 5.50 \times 10^{-7} = 6.71 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$D = 4.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{6.71 \times 10^{-7}}{4.00 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\theta_{min} = 1.6775 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to arc-seconds (1 rad = 206265 arc-seconds):**

$$\theta_{min} = 1.6775 \times 10^{-4} \times 206265$$

$$\theta_{min} \approx 34.6 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the minimum resolvable linear defect size at distance $d = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m:**

For small angles, the linear size $s$ is given by:

$$s = d \cdot \theta_{min}$$

$$s = 0.25 \times 1.6775 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$s = 4.19 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to micrometers:**

$$s \approx 41.9 \text{ μm} \approx 42 \text{ μm}$$

### Interpretation
The human eye, under optimal conditions, can theoretically resolve defects as small as **~42 μm** at a 25 cm working distance. In practice, however, real-world VT resolves defects in the range of **100 μm to 500 μm** because of fatigue, vibration, poor lighting, and inspector training limitations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Between Foot-Candles and Lux

### Problem Statement
A US-manufactured industrial light meter reads **75 foot-candles** at the inspection surface. Convert this reading to **lux** to verify compliance with the KTU/ASME standard of 500 lux minimum.

### Analytical Setup
The conversion factors are exact:

$$1 \text{ foot-candle} = 10.7639 \text{ lux}$$

$$1 \text{ lux} = 0.0929 \text{ foot-candles}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the conversion:**

$$E_{lux} = E_{fc} \times 10.7639$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$E_{lux} = 75 \times 10.7639$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$E_{lux} = 807.29 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 4 — Round to appropriate precision:**

$$E_{lux} \approx 807 \text{ lux}$$

### Interpretation
The reading of **807 lux** exceeds the ASME minimum of **500 lux**, so the inspection illumination is **acceptable**. KTU students must remember this conversion — questions testing unit conversion are worth easy marks in Part A.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Direct vs. Remote Visual Testing

| Parameter | Direct Visual Testing (DVT) | Remote Visual Testing (RVT) |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment Required | Eyes + light source | Borescope, fiberscope, videoprobe |
| Working Distance | 30 cm – 1 m | 1 m – 10+ m |
| Surface Access | Must be physically reachable | Internal cavities, behind obstacles |
| Magnification | 1× (unaided) up to 10× (magnifier) | 2× to 200× (probe optics) |
| Image Recording | Manual sketches, digital camera | Digital video, still images, 3D scans |
| Typical Applications | Weld surface, tanks, structural steel | Turbine blades, engine cylinders, pipes |
| Inspection Cost | Very low | Moderate (instrument cost) |
| Limitation | Cannot access interior surfaces | Limited by probe diameter and length |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High (frequently tested) | Moderate |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Defects Detected by Visual Testing

| Defect Type | Description | Typical Cause | Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crack | Linear discontinuity | Fatigue, stress concentration | Dark thin line |
| Porosity | Cluster of small cavities | Gas entrapment in casting/welding | Round dark spots |
| Undercut | Groove at weld toe | Excessive welding current | Smooth depression |
| Spatter | Metal droplets on surface | Welding process instability | Small metal globules |
| Pitting | Localized corrosion | Chemical/electrochemical attack | Small dark craters |
| Misalignment | Offset of mating parts | Poor assembly tolerance | Step/gap at joint |
| Surface Scratch | Linear mark from contact | Mechanical handling | Bright/dark line |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Visual Testing Inspection Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a professional Visual Testing procedure, mapping the interactions between the inspector, the component, the illumination source, and the optical aids.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component for Inspection] --> B[Surface Preparation]
    B --> C[Clean and Remove Obstructions]
    C --> D{Determine Access Type}
    D -->|Direct Access| E[Direct Visual Examination]
    D -->|Internal/Remote Access| F[Remote Visual Examination]
    E --> G[Unaided Human Eye]
    F --> H[Borescope/Fiberscope]
    G --> I[Apply Illumination Source]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Verify Lux Level Minimum 500]
    J --> K{Defect Detected?}
    K -->|No| L[Accept Component]
    K -->|Yes| M[Document Defect Type and Size]
    M --> N{Compare to Acceptance Criteria}
    N -->|Within Limit| L
    N -->|Exceeds Limit| O[Reject or Repair]
    O --> P[Issue Visual Testing Report]
    L --> P
    P --> Q[Sign by Level II/III Inspector]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Node A:** The component enters the inspection workflow.
- **Node D:** A decision branch determines whether the inspection is direct (e.g., a flat plate) or remote (e.g., inside a pipe).
- **Node K:** A second decision branch determines whether a defect is observed.
- **Node N:** A third decision branch compares the defect against acceptance criteria from codes such as ASME or AWS.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Optical Equivalence of the Human Eye and Inspection Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Illumination Source 500 lux min] --> B[Light Hits Test Object Surface]
    B --> C[Reflected Light Carries Defect Information]
    C --> D[Optical Aid: Magnifier/Borescope]
    D --> E[Human Eye Cornea and Lens]
    E --> F[Pupil Aperture Control]
    F --> G[Retina Image Formation]
    G --> H[Optic Nerve Signal]
    H --> I[Visual Cortex Processing]
    I --> J{Defect Recognition}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept or Reject Decision]
    J -->|No| L[Mark as Acceptable]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Flowchart: Inspector Qualification Pyramid (ASNT SNT-TC-1A)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Candidate Inspector] --> B[Vision Test Jaeger 1 and Color Blindness]
    B --> C{Physical Capability Confirmed?}
    C -->|No| D[Disqualify Candidate]
    C -->|Yes| E[Training Hours as per SNT-TC-1A]
    E --> F[Level I Training]
    F --> G[General Exam Pass]
    G --> H[Level I Certified]
    H --> I[Experience under Level II/III]
    I --> J[Level II Training]
    J --> K[Specific Exam Pass]
    K --> L[Level II Certified]
    L --> M[Advanced Training]
    M --> N[Level III Exam]
    N --> O[Level III Certified]
```

**Explanation of Levels:**
- **Level I:** Performs inspections under supervision, cannot sign final reports.
- **Level II:** Performs and certifies inspections, trains Level I personnel.
- **Level III:** Establishes procedures, certifies personnel, audits the quality system.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be familiar with the **three-level ASNT certification pyramid**. Questions on inspector qualification appear frequently in Part A (3-mark) sections.

---

## 4.4 Functional Layout: Remote Visual Testing (Borescopy) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source Outside Pipe] --> B[Light Guide Fiber Optic Cable]
    B --> C[Probe Tip with Lens]
    C --> D[Internal Pipe Surface]
    D --> E[Reflected Image Back Through Probe]
    E --> F[Eyepiece or CCD Camera]
    F --> G[Display Monitor]
    G --> H[Inspector Observes Defect]
    H --> I[Image Storage and Documentation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions test direct recall, definitions, and short derivations. Each answer should be 80–120 words. KTU examiners strictly enforce word limits.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Visual Testing (VT). Mention any two standards used to govern VT procedures.**

**Model Answer:**
Visual Testing (VT) is a non-destructive testing method in which the surface condition and overall integrity of a component are evaluated by direct or indirect observation using the human eye, with or without the aid of optical instruments such as borescopes and magnifiers.

Two governing standards are:
1. **ASME Section V, Article 9** – Visual Examination of Welded Joints and Components.
2. **ASTM E270** – Standard Reference Photographs for Indications in NDT.

VT is the oldest and most economical NDT method and is used as a preliminary screening tool before advanced NDT methods are applied.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the minimum illumination level required for visual testing as per ASME Section V. Convert this value into foot-candles.**

**Model Answer:**
As per **ASME Section V, Article 9**, the minimum illumination level required for visual inspection is **500 lux** at the inspection surface.

Converting to foot-candles:

$$E_{fc} = E_{lux} \times 0.0929 = 500 \times 0.0929 \approx 46.45 \text{ foot-candles}$$

Therefore, the minimum requirement is approximately **46.5 foot-candles** or **50 foot-candles** when rounded up for practical use.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and consist of two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must answer ONE of the two question choices (Or option). Both sub-parts are compulsory within the chosen question.

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the various steps involved in performing a Visual Testing inspection on a welded pressure vessel. Include the acceptance criteria as per ASME standards.**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Visual Testing of a welded pressure vessel is performed in the following structured sequence:

**Step 1 — Document Review and Preparation**
The inspector first reviews the **Weld Procedure Specification (WPS)**, **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)**, and the **approved drawing**. The acceptance criteria are established based on **ASME Section VIII** (pressure vessels) and **ASME Section IX** (welding qualification).

**Step 2 — Surface Preparation**
The weld and surrounding base metal (at least **50 mm** on each side) are cleaned to remove:
- Slag, spatter, and flux residues
- Oil, grease, paint, and mill scale
- Rust and moisture

Cleaning methods include wire brushing, grinding, and solvent wiping.

**Step 3 — Illumination Verification**
A calibrated **lux meter** is used to confirm that the illumination at the inspection surface is **at least 500 lux**. For fine inspection, **1000 lux or higher** is preferred. Oblique lighting is used to enhance visibility of undercut, surface cracks, and root concavity.

**Step 4 — Visual Examination**
The inspector examines:
- **Weld profile:** Smooth and uniform transition with no abrupt changes.
- **Weld toes:** Free from undercut and overlap.
- **Crown height:** Within tolerance (typically 0–3 mm).
- **Surface defects:** Cracks, porosity, undercut, crater cracks, spatter.
- **Geometric alignment:** Distortion, angular misalignment, and dimensional checks.

**Step 5 — Documentation**
All findings are recorded in a **Visual Testing Report (VTR)** with photographs, sketches, and defect maps. The VTR is signed by a **Level II or Level III** qualified inspector.

**Step 6 — Acceptance / Rejection Decision**
As per **ASME Section VIII**, the following defects are **NOT acceptable**:
- Any **crack** in the weld or heat-affected zone.
- **Undercut** exceeding **0.8 mm depth** (for 6–25 mm plate thickness).
- **Porosity cluster** exceeding 10 mm in any 25 mm length.
- **Incomplete penetration** at root or intermediate passes.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A point light source of luminous intensity 800 cd is used for VT. Calculate the illuminance at distances of 1 m, 2 m, and 4 m. Comment on which distance is suitable for inspection as per ASME standards (minimum 500 lux).**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Luminous intensity, $I_v = 800$ cd
- Distances: $d_1 = 1$ m, $d_2 = 2$ m, $d_4 = 4$ m
- ASME minimum: $E_{min} = 500$ lux

**Step 1 — Recall the Inverse Square Law:**

$$E_v = \frac{I_v}{d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate for $d_1 = 1$ m:**

$$E_{v1} = \frac{800}{(1)^2} = 800 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for $d_2 = 2$ m:**

$$E_{v2} = \frac{800}{(2)^2} = \frac{800}{4} = 200 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate for $d_4 = 4$ m:**

$$E_{v4} = \frac{800}{(4)^2} = \frac{800}{16} = 50 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 5 — Tabulate and compare:**

| Distance (m) | Illuminance (lux) | ASME Compliance |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 800 | ✅ Acceptable |
| 2.0 | 200 | ❌ Below minimum |
| 4.0 | 50 | ❌ Far below minimum |

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Only the distance of **1 m** provides illuminance above the ASME minimum of 500 lux. The inspection must be performed at or closer than 1 m, OR a higher-intensity lamp must be used to extend the working distance. The inverse-square law is clearly demonstrated — doubling the distance reduces the illuminance to one-fourth.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of the human eye in Visual Testing. Explain the concept of visual acuity, Jaeger chart, and Snellen chart. Mention the minimum visual standards required for a certified VT inspector.**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The Human Eye as a Sensor:**
In Visual Testing, the human eye is the primary sensor. It consists of the **cornea, pupil, lens, retina, and optic nerve**. Light reflected from the inspection surface enters the eye, is focused by the lens onto the retina, where **photoreceptor cells (cones for color vision, rods for low-light vision)** convert photons into neural signals. These signals are processed in the **visual cortex** of the brain, enabling defect recognition.

**Visual Acuity:**
Visual acuity is the ability of the eye to distinguish fine details. It is quantified using standardized eye charts:

1. **Snellen Chart:** Uses letters of progressively decreasing size. Normal vision is rated **20/20** (in feet) or **6/6** (in meters). The numerator is the test distance; the denominator is the distance at which a normal eye can read the same line.
2. **Jaeger Chart:** Uses paragraphs of text of decreasing size, designed for **near-vision testing** at reading distance (30–40 cm). **Jaeger 1 (J-1)** is the smallest and represents the highest acuity.

**Minimum Standards for VT Inspector (ASNT SNT-TC-1A):**
- **Near Vision:** Jaeger 1 at not less than **30 cm (12 inches)** — equivalent to Snellen 20/25.
- **Color Vision:** Ability to distinguish between **red, green, blue, and yellow** (Ishihara test or equivalent). Critical because defect indications in other NDT methods (e.g., dye penetrant red, magnetic particle black) are color-coded.
- **Annual Recertification:** Vision must be retested at least every **12 months**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What is a borescope? Explain the difference between rigid borescope, flexible borescope, and videoprobe. State two applications of borescopy in industry.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition:**
A **borescope** is an optical instrument used in Remote Visual Testing (RVT) to inspect the internal surfaces of cavities, tubes, and components that are inaccessible to direct view. It consists of an **objective lens, light guide, relay optics, and an eyepiece**.

**Types of Borescopes:**

| Feature | Rigid Borescope | Flexible Borescope | Videoprobe |
|---|---|---|---|
| Construction | Solid metal tube with rod-lens system | Fiber-optic image bundle | Solid-state CCD/CMOS at tip |
| Flexibility | None (straight line of sight only) | Articulating tip (2-way or 4-way) | Articulating tip (4-way) |
| Image Quality | Excellent (best resolution) | Good (fiber bundle causes honeycomb pattern) | Excellent (digital, no honeycomb) |
| Diameter Range | 1.7 mm to 12 mm | 4 mm to 13 mm | 3.5 mm to 10 mm |
| Length Range | Up to 1.5 m | Up to 10 m+ | Up to 10 m+ |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Recording | Eyepiece only or camera attachment | Eyepiece or camera | Built-in digital recording |

**Applications of Borescopy:**

1. **Aircraft Engine Inspection:** The internal cooling passages and combustion chambers of gas turbine engines are inspected at routine maintenance intervals using flexible borescopes and videoprobes, eliminating the need for engine teardown.

2. **Heat Exchanger and Boiler Tube Inspection:** The internal surfaces of tubes in heat exchangers, condensers, and boilers are inspected for scaling, corrosion, and cracking using rigid or flexible borescopes, supporting predictive maintenance programs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Zone 1 — Definitions:** Students often write "borescope is used to see inside" without specifying the **optical system, light guide, and relay** components. Mention **objective lens, relay optics, and eyepiece** to gain full marks.
> 2. **Mark Loss Zone 2 — Formulas:** Many students forget to write the **assumptions** (point source, perpendicular incidence) in illumination problems. Always state the assumption before substituting values.
> 3. **Mark Loss Zone 3 — Standards:** When mentioning codes, students sometimes write "ASME" without specifying the **section (e.g., ASME Section V, Article 9)**. Always quote the full code reference.
> 4. **Mark Loss Zone 4 — Units:** Forgetting to convert nm → m or lux → foot-candles is a common numerical error costing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Concepts — Memorize Before Exam:**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Visual Testing is inspection of surface and overall integrity using the eye, with or without optical aids.
- 🔹 **Oldest NDT Method:** Most economical and frequently used — 60%+ of defects detected visually.
- 🔹 **Minimum Illumination:** **500 lux (≈ 46.5 foot-candles)** as per ASME Section V, Article 9.
- 🔹 **Inverse Square Law:** $E_v = \dfrac{I_v}{d^2}$ — Doubling distance = ¼ the illuminance.
- 🔹 **Foot-candle ↔ Lux:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$ ; $1 \text{ lx} = 0.0929 \text{ fc}$.
- 🔹 **Visual Acuity Standard:** Jaeger 1 (J-1) or Snellen 20/25 at 30 cm reading distance.
- 🔹 **Color Vision Required:** Distinguish red, green, blue, yellow (Ishihara test).
- 🔹 **Vision Test Recertification:** Every 12 months.
- 🔹 **ASNT Levels:** I (trainee), II (certified inspector), III (procedure authorizer / trainer).
- 🔹 **Rayleigh Criterion:** $\theta_{min} = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ — defines minimum angular resolution.
- 🔹 **Human Eye Resolves:** ~42 μm theoretically at 25 cm under optimal conditions.
- 🔹 **Borescope Types:** Rigid (rod lens), Flexible (fiber bundle), Videoprobe (CCD/CMOS).
- 🔹 **Governing Codes:** ASME Section V Article 9, ASTM E270, AWS D1.1, ASNT SNT-TC-1A.
- 🔹 **Defects Detected:** Cracks, porosity, undercut, spatter, pitting, corrosion, misalignment.
- 🔹 **Why VT First?** 80% of service defects are surface-breaking → VT detects them first.
- 🔹 **Inspection Process Order:** Surface prep → Illumination check → Direct/Remote examination → Documentation → Accept/Reject decision → VTR signed by Level II/III.
- 🔹 **Key Acronyms to Recall:** VT (Visual Testing), VTR (Visual Testing Report), WPS (Weld Procedure Specification), PQR (Procedure Qualification Record), RVT (Remote Visual Testing), DVT (Direct Visual Testing).

> **End of Module 1 Topic: Fundamentals of Visual Testing — KTU PESFT521 Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛠️ TOOLS IN NON-DESTRUCTIVE TESTING (NDT) — SECTION 1

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *NDT Tools* refer to the comprehensive set of **hand-held instruments, optical devices, electronic gauges, illumination systems, and reference standards** employed by a certified NDT technician to perform visual, dimensional, surface, and material-condition evaluations on a component **without altering its serviceability, geometry, or functional integrity.*

In the context of the **PESFT521** syllabus (Module 1), *Tools* constitutes the foundational physical and optical hardware that forms the **first line of NDT** — predominantly used during **Visual Testing (VT)** and pre/post inspection of components prepared for advanced methods (PT, MT, UT, RT, ET).

### 1.1.1 Classification Snapshot

| S.No | Category | Primary Function |
|:----:|:---------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Optical Tools** | Visual enhancement of surface defects |
| 2 | **Dimensional Tools** | Geometrical & tolerance verification |
| 3 | **Illumination Tools** | Adequate lighting for VT |
| 4 | **Surface Tools** | Roughness, coating & cleanliness check |
| 5 | **Reference Standards** | Calibration & defect sizing |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Think of an NDT engineer as a **doctor diagnosing a patient without performing surgery**. Just like a doctor uses a **stethoscope, otoscope, magnifying lens, and X-ray** to look *inside* and *on* the body, an NDT technician uses **borescopes, magnifiers, illuminators, and gauges** to "look inside" welds, pipelines, and engine components without cutting them open. The **tools are the senses of the inspector** — the better the tools, the more accurate the diagnosis.

### 1.3 Why Tools Matter in KTU 2024 NEP Framework

Under the **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme**, the tools module emphasizes:

- **Outcome-Based Education (OBE):** Students must demonstrate hands-on competency in selecting and operating the correct tool for a given defect scenario.
- **Skill-Based Learning:** Direct linkage to Course Outcomes (CO1, CO2) — *"Understand and apply the principles of NDT tools for defect detection."*
- **Employability:** NDT Level I/II certifications (ASNT, ISNT) require physical familiarity with every tool listed in this module.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in NDT Tooling

- **Magnification (M):** Dimensionless ratio ≥ **1× to 50×**
- **Resolution (R):** Expressed in micrometers (**µm**) or line-pairs per mm (**lp/mm**)
- **Field of View (FOV):** Measured in **mm** or **degrees (°)**
- **Working Distance (WD):** Stated in **mm**
- **Illuminance:** Measured in **lux (lx)** — minimum **500 lx** for general VT per **ASTM E1316**
- **Accuracy:** Expressed as a percentage of reading, e.g., **±0.02 mm**

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET — SECTION 2

## 2.1 Optical Tools — The Inspector's "Eyes"

### 2.1.1 Hand-Held Magnifying Glass (Loupe)

A simple convex lens used to enlarge small surface defects such as **porosity, undercuts, and micro-cracks** on weld surfaces.

**Operating Principle:** Forms a **virtual, erect, and magnified image** when the object is placed within the **focal length** of the lens.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Object is placed at distance $u$ from the lens where $u < f$.
2. The eye perceives a virtual image at the **least distance of distinct vision**, conventionally $D = 250\,\text{mm}$.
3. The angular size of the image is larger than the object, producing perceived magnification.

### 2.1.2 Borescope / Endoscope / Fiberscope

A flexible or rigid tube with an **objective lens, fiber-optic light guide, and eyepiece** used to inspect **internal surfaces** of cavities, turbine blades, and engine cylinders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid Borescope:** Provides high-resolution images (down to **~5 µm**); limited to straight-line access.
> **Flexible Fiberscope:** Can navigate bends up to **±90°** over lengths of **1–2 m**; lower resolution (~30–50 µm).

### 2.1.3 Stereo Microscope

A dual-eyepiece microscope providing **3-D depth perception** of fracture surfaces and fatigue striations. Magnification typically ranges from **10× to 100×**.

### 2.1.4 Profile Projector (Optical Comparator)

Projects a magnified shadow of the part onto a screen for **2-D dimensional comparison** against a drawing overlay. Standard magnification: **10×, 20×, 50×, 100×**.

---

## 2.2 Dimensional Measurement Tools

### 2.2.1 Vernier Caliper

Provides measurements with a typical resolution of **0.02 mm** (20 µm) or **0.05 mm** (50 µm). Uses the principle of **nonius (vernier) scale alignment** for sub-division of the main scale.

**Least Count Formula:**

$$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{Smallest main scale division} - \text{Smallest vernier scale division}}{n}$$

For a standard metric vernier with **25 divisions** on the vernier scale matching **24 divisions** of the main scale (each 1 mm):

$$\text{LC} = \frac{1\,\text{mm} - \frac{24}{25}\,\text{mm}}{25} = \frac{0.04}{25}\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.02\,\text{mm}}$$

### 2.2.2 Micrometer Screw Gauge

Based on the **screw principle** — one full rotation advances the spindle by the pitch of the screw (**typically 0.5 mm**). The thimble has **50 divisions**.

$$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of thimble divisions}} = \frac{0.5\,\text{mm}}{50} = 0.01\,\text{mm}$$

### 2.2.3 Depth Gauge & Height Gauge

Used for **weld depth, groove angle verification, and plate thickness** measurements. Resolution typically **0.02 mm** to **0.01 mm**.

---

## 2.3 Illumination Tools

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (ASTM E1316):** Minimum illumination for visual testing = **500 lx** for general surfaces, and **≥ 1000 lx** for fine surface inspection. Low-angle illumination (< 15°) reveals surface-breaking cracks as **dark lines against a bright background**.

Common types: **Halogen lamps, LED floodlights, fiber-optic illuminators, UV-A blacklights (365 nm)** for fluorescent PT/MT inspection.

---

## 2.4 Surface Condition Tools

| Tool | Function | Typical Spec |
|:-----|:---------|:-------------|
| **Surface Profile Comparator** | Compares blasted surface to a visual standard | ISO 8503-1 |
| **Coating Thickness Gauge** | Measures dry film thickness (DFT) | 0–2000 µm, ±1 % |
| **Surface Roughness Tester** | Measures Ra, Rz values | 0.05–10 µm Ra |
| **Cleaning Solvents / Wire Brushes** | Pre-cleaning for PT/MT | Per ASTM D770 |

---

## 2.5 Reference Standards & Calibration Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Standards are NOT defect samples — they are dimensionally and acoustically characterized blocks used to calibrate NDT instruments.** Examples include: **IIW V1/V2 Blocks (Ultrasonic), ASTM E127 Blocks (UT), Step Wedges (RT), Holyday Blocks (MT), and Penetration Test Blocks (PT).**

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f}$ | Magnification of simple magnifier (image at $D$) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{D}{f} = \dfrac{25}{f(\text{cm})}$ | Magnification for relaxed eye (image at infinity) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\theta = 1.22 \,\dfrac{\lambda}{D_a}$ | Rayleigh diffraction limit (resolving power) | radians |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{0.61 \,\lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | Optical resolution of a microscope | µm |
| 5 | $\text{LC} = \dfrac{s - v}{n}$ | Least count of vernier caliper | mm |
| 6 | $\text{LC} = \dfrac{p}{N_t}$ | Least count of micrometer | mm |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance (luminous flux ÷ area) | lux (lx) |
| 8 | $M_{\text{projector}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{screen}}}{d_{\text{part}}}$ | Profile projector magnification | Dimensionless |

> **Note on absolute value bars:** All $\vert x \vert$ in equations are written as `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Borescopes inspect **turbine blades for hot-corrosion cracks** during routine engine overhauls (every 3000 flight hours).
- **Nuclear Power:** Stereo microscopes analyse **fatigue striation spacing** in failed fuel cladding to compute crack-growth rates ($da/dN$).
- **Pressure Vessel Fabrication:** Vernier calipers verify **weld reinforcement height** and **root gap** before welding — a mandatory QC step per **ASME Sec IX**.
- **Pipeline Inspection:** Crawler-mounted borescopes inspect **girth weld toes** in cross-country pipelines.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX — SECTION 3

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnification of a Simple Magnifier (Eye at Least Distance)

**Problem Setup:** A convex lens of focal length $f = 5\,\text{cm}$ is used as a magnifier. The final image is formed at the **least distance of distinct vision** $D = 25\,\text{cm}$. Derive the magnification.

**Step 1 — Define Magnification Angularly:**

The angular magnification $M$ is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the image at the eye to the angle subtended by the object placed at the near point.

$$M = \frac{\theta_{\text{image}}}{\theta_{\text{object}}}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Virtual Image:**

When the object is placed at the focal point (or just inside it), the virtual image forms at distance $D = 25\,\text{cm}$ from the lens.

From the thin lens geometry:

$$\theta_{\text{image}} \approx \tan \theta_{\text{image}} = \frac{h}{D}$$

where $h$ is the object height.

**Step 3 — Geometry of the Object at Near Point (without lens):**

If the object were viewed directly at the near point:

$$\theta_{\text{object}} = \frac{h}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Use the Lens Equation:**

$$\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Object distance $u = -x$ (negative sign by sign convention), image distance $v = -D = -25\,\text{cm}$ (virtual image on same side as object).

$$\frac{1}{-25} - \frac{1}{-x} = \frac{1}{f}$$

$$\frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Linear Magnification $m$:**

$$m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{-25}{-x} = \frac{25}{x}$$

Substitute $x$:

$$m = 25 \left( \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{D} \right) = \frac{25}{f} + \frac{25}{D} = \frac{D}{f} + 1$$

$$\boxed{M = 1 + \frac{D}{f}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation** ($f = 5\,\text{cm}$):

$$M = 1 + \frac{25}{5} = 1 + 5 = \boxed{6\times}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Magnification for Normal-Relaxed Eye

**Problem Setup:** Same lens ($f = 5\,\text{cm}$) but the final image is at **infinity** (relaxed eye, no eye strain).

**Step 1 — Image at Infinity Condition:**

The object is placed exactly at the focal point: $u = f$. The image is at $v = \infty$.

**Step 2 — Angular Magnification:**

The object viewed directly at the near point subtends angle:

$$\theta_{\text{object}} = \frac{h}{D}$$

The object at the focal point subtends angle at the lens:

$$\theta_{\text{lens}} = \frac{h}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$M = \frac{\theta_{\text{lens}}}{\theta_{\text{object}}} = \frac{h/f}{h/D} = \frac{D}{f}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{25\,\text{cm}}{f(\text{cm})}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation** ($f = 5\,\text{cm}$):

$$M = \frac{25}{5} = \boxed{5\times}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optical Resolving Power (Rayleigh Criterion)

**Problem Setup:** A microscope objective has a **Numerical Aperture** $\text{NA} = 0.95$ and uses green light of wavelength $\lambda = 550\,\text{nm}$. Find the smallest resolvable feature.

**Step 1 — Rayleigh Resolution Formula:**

$$R = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$R = \frac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}}{0.95}$$

$$R = \frac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{0.95}$$

$$R = 3.5316 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.353\,\mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**

This means a stereo microscope with these specs can distinguish two adjacent cracks separated by at least **0.353 µm** — critical for analysing **stress-corrosion cracking** in stainless-steel reactor components.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vernier Caliper Least Count (Exhaustive)

**Problem Setup:** A vernier caliper has a main scale with **1 mm** smallest division. The vernier scale has **50 divisions** that exactly match **49 mm** of the main scale.

**Step 1 — Vernier Scale Division (VSD):**

$$\text{VSD} = \frac{49\,\text{mm}}{50} = 0.98\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Smallest Main Scale Division (MSD):**

$$\text{MSD} = 1\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Least Count Definition:**

$$\text{LC} = \text{MSD} - \text{VSD}$$

$$\text{LC} = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.02\,\text{mm} = 20\,\mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — General Formula Derivation:**

If $n$ vernier divisions match $(n-1)$ main scale divisions:

$$\text{VSD} = \frac{(n-1)\,\text{MSD}}{n}$$

$$\text{LC} = \text{MSD} - \frac{(n-1)\,\text{MSD}}{n} = \frac{\text{MSD}}{n}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = \frac{\text{MSD}}{n}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Illuminance (Inverse-Square Law)

**Problem Setup:** A handheld LED lamp emits a total luminous flux $\Phi = 800\,\text{lm}$ uniformly over a hemispherical area. Find the illuminance at a perpendicular distance of $0.5\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Illuminance Definition:**

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Hemispherical Area:**

$$A = 2 \pi r^{2} = 2 \pi (0.5)^{2} = 2 \pi \times 0.25 = 0.5\pi\,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$A \approx 1.5708\,\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Illuminance:**

$$E = \frac{800}{1.5708} \approx 509.3\,\text{lx}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 509.3\,\text{lx} \;\; (\text{Satisfies ASTM E1316 } \geq 500\,\text{lx})}$$

---

## 3.6 📋 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Decision Table

| Inspection Goal | Recommended Tool | Why This Tool? | Mark Justification |
|:----------------|:-----------------|:---------------|:-------------------|
| Detect **0.1 mm crack** on flat weld surface | 10× Hand Loupe + 1000 lx low-angle light | High contrast, shadow casting | CO1, CO2 |
| Inspect **inside a 6 m pipe** (ID 100 mm) | Flexible Fiberscope, 8 mm dia, 8 m length | Access to internal bend regions | CO1 |
| Measure **weld toe undercut depth** (≈0.3 mm) | Depth Gauge (LC 0.02 mm) | Direct contact measurement | CO2 |
| Verify **plate thickness 12.5 mm ± 0.1 mm** | Digital Micrometer (LC 0.001 mm) | Sub-100 µm accuracy | CO2 |
| Examine **fracture surface striations** | Stereo Microscope, 50×, SEM follow-up | 3-D depth perception | CO1, CO3 |
| Calibrate **UT flaw detector** | IIW V1 Block | Standardized reflectors at known depths | CO4 |
| Check **paint DFT 80 µm** | Magnetic Coating Thickness Gauge | Non-contact, fast | CO2 |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS — SECTION 4

## 4.2 Tool Selection Flowchart — Visual Inspection Workflow


## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Borescope Optical Path

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP — SECTION 5

---

## 5.1 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six hand tools used in Visual Testing (VT) and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. **Hand Magnifier / Loupe** – Enlarges surface defects (5×–20×).
2. **Borescope / Endoscope** – Inspects internal surfaces of cavities and pipes.
3. **Vernier Caliper** – Measures external/internal dimensions (LC 0.02 mm).
4. **Micrometer** – High-precision thickness/shaft measurement (LC 0.01 mm).
5. **Illumination Lamp (Halogen/LED)** – Provides ≥ 500 lx as per ASTM E1316.
6. **Surface Profile Comparator** – Compares blasted surface to ISO 8503 standard.
7. **UV-A Lamp (365 nm)** – Used in fluorescent PT/MT inspection.
8. **Coating Thickness Gauge** – Measures paint/coating DFT non-destructively.

*(Any 6 — ½ mark per tool + function)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Least Count" of a measuring instrument. A vernier caliper has 20 divisions matching 19 mm of the main scale. Find its least count.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Least Count:** The smallest measurable increment that an instrument can resolve.

$$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{MSD}}{n} = \frac{1\,\text{mm}}{20} = 0.05\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.05\,\text{mm} = 50\,\mu\text{m}}$$

---

## 5.2 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *(Module 1 — Tools)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Classify the various tools used in Non-Destructive Testing with a neat block diagram. Explain in detail the **optical tools** — hand magnifier, borescope, and stereo microscope — with diagrams and their applications. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the magnification formula of a simple magnifier for **(i)** image formed at the least distance of distinct vision, and **(ii)** image at infinity. A convex lens of focal length **4 cm** is used as a magnifier. Find the magnification in both cases. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of NDT Tools (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram of NDT Tools (to be drawn):**

```
                 ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                 │        NDT TOOLS                │
                 └─────────────────────────────────┘
                                  │
        ┌────────────┬────────────┼────────────┬────────────┐
        │            │            │            │            │
   ┌────▼────┐  ┌────▼────┐  ┌────▼────┐  ┌────▼────┐  ┌────▼────┐
   │ OPTICAL │  │ DIMENSION│  │ILLUMIN- │  │SURFACE  │  │REFERENCE│
   │ TOOLS   │  │ TOOLS    │  │ATION    │  │TOOLS    │  │STANDARDS│
   └─────────┘  └──────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘
```

**Optical Tools Explained:**

**1. Hand Magnifier (Loupe) – (2 Marks)**
- A simple plano-convex lens of focal length 2.5 cm to 10 cm.
- Forms a **virtual, erect, magnified image** when object is within focal length.
- Magnification $M = 1 + D/f$.
- **Application:** Detection of porosity, micro-cracks on weld toes.

**2. Borescope – (2.5 Marks)**
- Consists of objective lens, relay system (lens or fiber bundle), and eyepiece.
- Two types: **Rigid** (high resolution ~5 µm) and **Flexible** (navigates bends up to ±90°).
- **Application:** Internal inspection of turbine blades, engine cylinders, pipes.

**3. Stereo Microscope – (2.5 Marks)**
- Dual optical paths provide **true 3-D depth perception**.
- Magnification 10× to 100×.
- **Application:** Fracture surface analysis — fatigue striations, beach marks, cleavage facets.

---

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Case (i): Image at Least Distance of Distinct Vision $D = 25\,\text{cm}$**

Using thin lens equation $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$, with $v = -D$ (virtual image):

$$\frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{D}$$

$$M = 1 + \frac{D}{f}$$

**Substituting** $f = 4\,\text{cm}$, $D = 25\,\text{cm}$:

$$M_1 = 1 + \frac{25}{4} = 1 + 6.25 = \boxed{7.25\times}$$

**Case (ii): Image at Infinity (Relaxed Eye)**

Object placed at focal point; final image at infinity:

$$M = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{25}{f(\text{cm})}$$

**Substituting** $f = 4\,\text{cm}$:

$$M_2 = \frac{25}{4} = \boxed{6.25\times}$$

**Comparison:** $M_1 > M_2$, but the relaxed-eye configuration causes less eye strain during prolonged inspection.

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *(Module 1 — Tools — Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the construction, working principle, and applications of a **borescope** with a labelled diagram. Differentiate between rigid and flexible borescopes. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following readings were taken on a vernier caliper with main scale division 1 mm and 25 vernier divisions matching 24 mm:
> - **MSR = 23 mm, VSR = 7 divisions coinciding**
> Calculate the **measured dimension** and the **least count** of the instrument. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Borescope – Construction, Working, Applications (7 Marks)**

**Construction (2 Marks):**
- **Light Source:** 150 W halogen or LED coupled via fiber-optic cable.
- **Illumination Bundle:** Carries light to the tip.
- **Objective Lens:** Forms image of internal surface.
- **Relay System:** Rigid – series of relay lenses; Flexible – coherent fiber bundle (10,000–20,000 fibers).
- **Eyepiece / Camera:** Magnifies the relayed image for viewing.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
Light from external source is transmitted through the fiber bundle to illuminate the target. Reflected light is captured by the objective lens, transmitted through the relay system, and focused onto the eyepiece/CCD. Inspector views a **magnified, real-time image** of the internal surface.

**Applications (1 Mark):**
- Aircraft engine borescope inspection (compressor/turbine blades).
- Pipeline internal weld inspection.
- Nuclear reactor fuel channel examination.

**Rigid vs Flexible (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Rigid Borescope | Flexible Fiberscope |
|:--------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| Resolution | High (~5 µm) | Moderate (~30–50 µm) |
| Length | 100–500 mm | Up to 2–8 m |
| Bending | None | ±90° to ±180° |
| Image Quality | Sharp, bright | Slight honeycomb pattern |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best Use | Straight cavities | Curved pipes, complex geometry |

---

**(b) Vernier Caliper Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Least Count (2 Marks)**

$$\text{LC} = \frac{\text{MSD}}{n} = \frac{1\,\text{mm}}{25} = 0.04\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: VSR × LC (2 Marks)**

$$\text{VSR contribution} = 7 \times 0.04 = 0.28\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Total Measured Dimension (3 Marks)**

$$\text{Measurement} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VSR} \times \text{LC})$$

$$\text{Measurement} = 23\,\text{mm} + 0.28\,\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Measurement} = 23.28\,\text{mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error** in magnifier derivation — always use Cartesian sign convention with virtual image $v = -D$.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention $D = 25$ cm** — students often quote $M = 1/f$ which is dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Confusing "VSD" and "MSD"** in vernier problems — VSD is always **less than** MSD for an amplifying vernier.
> 4. **Not drawing labelled diagrams** in Part B — KTU awards **1–2 marks** specifically for neat, labelled schematics. Always include one.
> 5. **Mixing up rigid vs flexible borescope specs** — Rigid is short + high res; Flexible is long + lower res.

---

## 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> ✅ **Tools in NDT = the physical hardware an inspector uses — no tool = no inspection possible.**

**Key Takeaways to Remember:**

- 🟢 **Optical tools** (loupe, borescope, stereo microscope, profile projector) are the **first contact** of any VT inspection.
- 🟢 **Magnification formulas:** $M = 1 + D/f$ (image at $D$) and $M = D/f$ (image at infinity). $D = 25\,\text{cm}$ is **not optional** — it is the standard.
- 🟢 **Vernier Caliper LC** = MSD / $n$ (for $n$ vernier divisions matching $(n-1)$ main scale divisions).
- 🟢 **Micrometer LC** = Pitch / Number of thimble divisions = $0.5/50 = 0.01\,\text{mm}$.
- 🟢 **Rayleigh resolution:** $R = 0.61\,\lambda / \text{NA}$ — governs the smallest defect visible under a microscope.
- 🟢 **Illumination rule (ASTM E1316):** ≥ 500 lx general, ≥ 1000 lx fine; low-angle (< 15°) for crack detection.
- 🟢 **Borescope types:** Rigid (straight, high res ~5 µm) vs Flexible Fiberscope (bends ±90°, lower res ~30–50 µm).
- 🟢 **Reference standards** (IIW V1, ASTM E127, Step Wedges) are **calibration tools, NOT defect samples**.
- 🟢 **Profile projector magnification** = $d_{\text{screen}} / d_{\text{part}}$.
- 🟢 Always quote units (**mm, µm, lx, lp/mm**) in Part A answers for full marks.
- 🟢 **CO Mapping:** CO1 (Recall tools) → CO2 (Apply correct tool) → CO4 (Calibrate using reference standards).
- 🟢 Under **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 OBE**, expect **competency-based** questions — *"Which tool will you use if…?"* — not just definitions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Limitations of Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications and Limitations of Vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Visual Testing (VT)**, also referred to as **Visual Inspection (VI)** or **Visual Examination (VE)**, is the oldest, most fundamental, and most widely employed Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method. As per **ASTM E1316** and **ASTM E2709** standards, Visual Testing is defined as:

> *“The process of examining a component or material using direct human vision, either unaided (naked eye) or aided (using optical enhancement devices), to evaluate the presence, size, location, and significance of surface discontinuities and material conditions.”*

In the context of **PESFT521 – Non-Destructive Testing Methods**, this topic specifically addresses the **functional envelope** of Visual Testing — namely, *where it is deployed (applications)* and *where it cannot function effectively (limitations)*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Conceptual Hook)

Imagine you are a **jeweller examining a diamond** under a 10x loupe. You are not touching the diamond, not breaking it, not using X-rays — you are simply **using your eyes (aided) to detect surface cracks, inclusions, or polish defects**. Visual Testing in engineering works on exactly this principle: **inspecting the outer skin of a component** to look for cracks, corrosion, dents, weld beads, dimensional deviations, and other surface-level anomalies.

- **Unaided VT** = A passenger looking at a car body for dents in daylight.
- **Aided VT** = A car inspector using a torch, mirror, or magnifying glass in a workshop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Visual Testing contributes to roughly **30–40%** of all NDT activities performed in industry because it is the **cheapest, fastest, and first-line** evaluation technique used before deploying advanced methods like Radiography, Ultrasonics, or Eddy Current Testing.

## 1.3 Classification of Visual Testing (Standard Taxonomy)

Visual Testing can be classified into two broad categories:

| Classification | Description | Typical Tools | Typical Power Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unaided Visual Testing (Direct VT)** | Inspection performed by the naked human eye under adequate ambient lighting | Naked eye only | **Natural / ambient illumination (≥ 1000 lux recommended)** |
| **Aided Visual Testing (Indirect VT)** | Inspection performed using optical, illumination, or remote imaging aids | Magnifiers, mirrors, borescopes, fiberscopes, cameras, video borescopes, drones | **Artificial white-light illumination / LED ring lights** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Apex Standard Reference:** Visual Testing is governed by **ASTM E2709** (Standard Practice for Demonstrating Capability to Comply with the Qualification Requirements for Visual Inspection) and **ASME Section V, Article 9** for nuclear and pressure vessel applications.

## 1.4 The Four Foundational Attributes Inspected in VT

Every Visual Testing procedure evaluates **four principal attributes** of the component:

1. **Surface Condition** — cracks, porosity, laps, undercuts, corrosion pits.
2. **Geometric Integrity** — dimensional conformance, warping, ovality.
3. **Structural Completeness** — missing components, fastener looseness, weld profile.
4. **Surface Cleanliness** — paint, oil, scale, biological fouling.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** A KTU examiner expects students to state that VT is a **qualitative and quantitative** method — *qualitative* (presence/absence of defect) and *quantitative* (size estimation when calibrated gauges or pit gauges are used).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why & How" of Visual Testing

Visual Testing operates on a remarkably simple but rigorously standardized workflow. Below is the breakdown of its operational logic.

### Step 1: Preparation of the Test Surface
- The surface must be **clean, dry, and accessible**.
- Surface contaminants (oil, paint, scale, weld spatter) are removed via **wire brushing, solvent cleaning, or grit blasting** to expose the actual substrate.

### Step 2: Illumination Setup
- Adequate lighting is the **single most important environmental parameter**.
- The recommended minimum illuminance is **1075 lux (≈ 100 foot-candles)** for general industrial inspection.
- For fine surface defect detection, **2150 lux (≈ 200 foot-candles)** is preferred.

### Step 3: Visual Examination (Direct or Aided)
- The inspector scans the surface systematically (e.g., **left-to-right, top-to-bottom**) at a controlled distance.
- Viewing distance for human eye inspection is typically **≤ 610 mm (24 inches)**.

### Step 4: Recording and Evaluation
- Detected indications are **classified, sized, and recorded**.
- Reference standards (e.g., **ASTM A802** for steel castings, **AWS QC1** for welds) are used for acceptance.

### Step 5: Reporting and Disposition
- A formal **Visual Inspection Report (VIR)** is generated.
- Disposition can be: **Accept, Repair, or Reject**.

## 2.2 Equipment Hierarchy in Visual Testing

| Equipment | Function | Typical Magnification / Spec | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand Magnifier (Loupe)** | Enlarges small defects | 2× to 20× | Surface finish, hairline cracks |
| **Mirror (Dental/Inspection)** | Reflects light to inaccessible areas | Plane, concave, convex | Pipe backs, internal cavities |
| **Borescope (Rigid)** | Direct line-of-sight remote viewing | 0°–90° viewing angles | Engine cylinders, turbine casings |
| **Fiberscope (Flexible)** | Flexible remote viewing | 1.5 m – 10 m length | Pipes, ducts, heat exchangers |
| **Video Borescope** | Image capture + flexible insertion | Digital CCD/CMOS sensor | Aircraft engines, gas turbines |
| **CCTV / Industrial Camera** | Long-distance visual capture | High-resolution IP cameras | Storage tanks, pressure vessels |
| **Drone-Mounted Camera** | Aerial inspection of large structures | 4K UHD + thermal overlay | Chimneys, bridges, transmission towers |

## 2.3 Standard Illumination Metrics (Engineering Reference)

The intensity of illumination is quantified by **Illuminance (E)**, defined as the luminous flux per unit area.

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A}$$

Where:
- $E$ = **Illuminance** in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**
- $\Phi$ = **Luminous flux** in **lumens (lm)**
- $A$ = **Area of illuminated surface** in **square meters (m²)** or **square feet (ft²)**

The conversion between lux and foot-candle units is:

$$1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$$

The recommended illuminance values per **ASTM E2709 / E1316** are tabulated below:

| Inspection Type | Minimum Illuminance (lux) | Minimum Illuminance (fc) |
|---|---|---|
| General industrial inspection | **1000 lx** | **≈ 100 fc** |
| Detailed surface inspection | **2000 lx** | **≈ 200 fc** |
| Fine precision inspection | **5000 lx** | **≈ 500 fc** |
| Crack detection under angled light | **3000 lx + oblique 15°–45°** | **≈ 300 fc + oblique 15°–45°** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **all examinable equations, conversions, and quantitative thresholds** for the topic "Applications and Limitations of Vision." Memorize these for the KTU ESE Module Exam.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula or Standard Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Illuminance | $E = \Phi / A$ | **lux (lx)** | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Lux ↔ Foot-candle conversion | $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$ | — | Bi-directional conversion |
| 3 | Minimum illuminance (general) | $\mathbf{E \geq 1000 \text{ lx}}$ | **lux** | ASTM E2709 |
| 4 | Minimum illuminance (detailed) | $\mathbf{E \geq 2000 \text{ lx}}$ | **lux** | ASTM E2709 |
| 5 | Maximum viewing distance | $d \leq 610 \text{ mm}$ | **mm** | 24 inches |
| 6 | Resolvable defect size (naked eye) | $\approx 0.1 \text{ mm}$ (at 250 mm) | **mm** | Visual acuity limit |
| 7 | Magnification of loupe | $M = 25 / f$ (where f in cm) | **dimensionless** | Simple loupe formula |
| 8 | Visual angle (minimum resolvable) | $\theta \approx 1$ arc-minute | **arc-minute** | Snellen 20/20 standard |
| 9 | Inspection time (per meter of weld) | $t \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 2 \text{ min/m}$ | **min/m** | Rough empirical estimate |
| 10 | Acceptance criteria reference | **AWS D1.1, ASME Sec. IX, ASTM A802** | — | Standard acceptance references |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Visual Testing is the **gateway inspection technique** across virtually every engineering sector:

- **Aerospace** — pre-flight inspection of airframe skin, engine inlets (borescopes).
- **Oil & Gas** — pipeline weld joints, storage tank shell plates, riser pipes.
- **Power Generation** — boiler tubes (fiberscope), steam turbine blades, generator windings.
- **Civil Engineering** — bridge cables, concrete surface cracks, tunnel linings.
- **Manufacturing** — incoming raw material (castings, forgings) and finished machined parts.
- **Automotive** — crash-test evaluation, body panel quality, brake disc surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Stat:** Per **ASNT** statistics, Visual Testing constitutes approximately **40% of all NDT performed worldwide**, making it the single most utilized NDT method in existence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Viewing Distance and Resolvable Defect Size

The human eye can resolve a defect of minimum size $d$ at a given viewing distance $D$ by invoking the **Snellen visual acuity principle** of **1 arc-minute (1/60 of a degree)**.

### Given:
- Minimum resolvable visual angle: $\theta = 1 \text{ arc-minute} = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ degree} = \dfrac{\pi}{10800} \text{ rad}$
- Viewing distance: $D$ (in mm)

### To Find:
- Minimum resolvable defect size: $d$ (in mm)

### Derivation:

Step 1 — Apply the small-angle trigonometric relation between the defect size and the viewing distance.

$$d = D \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

Step 2 — Because $\theta = 1$ arc-minute is an extremely small angle, we apply the **small-angle approximation** $\tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ (valid when $\theta$ is in radians).

$$d \approx D \cdot \theta$$

Step 3 — Convert $\theta = 1$ arc-minute to radians.

$$\theta = 1 \text{ arc-minute} \times \frac{\pi}{10800 \text{ arc-minutes}} = \frac{\pi}{10800} \text{ rad} \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

Step 4 — For a typical inspection distance $D = 250$ mm (the standard "near point" of human vision):

$$d = 250 \text{ mm} \times \frac{\pi}{10800} \approx 0.0727 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5 — Thus, the **practical minimum resolvable defect size by the unaided eye at 250 mm is approximately 0.1 mm** (accounting for real-world contrast and lighting losses).

$$\boxed{d_{\min} \approx 0.1 \text{ mm} \text{ (at } D = 250 \text{ mm)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Simple Loupe Magnification

A hand-held loupe is a single converging lens. Its magnification $M$ is given by the ratio of the **near point of distinct vision** (conventionally $D_0 = 25$ cm) to the **focal length $f$** of the lens.

### Given:
- Near point of distinct vision: $D_0 = 25 \text{ cm} = 250 \text{ mm}$
- Focal length of loupe: $f$ (in cm)

### To Find:
- Angular magnification: $M$

### Derivation:

Step 1 — Write the standard lens-magnification formula for a simple magnifier used in "normal adjustment" mode.

$$M = \frac{D_0}{f}$$

Step 2 — Substitute $D_0 = 25$ cm.

$$M = \frac{25}{f} \quad \text{(with } f \text{ in cm)}$$

Step 3 — For a loupe of focal length $f = 5$ cm:

$$M = \frac{25}{5} = 5\times$$

Step 4 — Interpretation: a 5× loupe resolves defects as small as:

$$d_{\min}^{\text{aided}} = \frac{d_{\min}^{\text{unaided}}}{M} = \frac{0.1 \text{ mm}}{5} = 0.02 \text{ mm} = 20 \text{ \mu m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\min}^{\text{aided, } 5\times} \approx 20 \text{ \mu m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5× magnification reduces the resolvable defect size from **100 μm to 20 μm**, demonstrating the **linear inverse proportionality** between magnification and minimum resolvable defect size.

## 3.3 Derivation: Illumination Power Required for a Specified Lux Level

An inspector requires a minimum illuminance of **$E = 2000$ lx** over a circular inspection patch of **diameter = 500 mm**.

### Given:
- Required illuminance: $E = 2000$ lx
- Diameter of inspection area: $d = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$

### To Find:
- Required luminous flux $\Phi$ (in lumens)

### Derivation:

Step 1 — Compute the inspection area.

$$A = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi (0.25)^{2} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Step 2 — Rearrange the illuminance equation to solve for $\Phi$.

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = E \cdot A$$

Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.

$$\Phi = 2000 \text{ lx} \times 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2} = 392.7 \text{ lm}$$

Step 4 — Account for the typical **luminous efficacy of a white LED** (≈ 100 lm/W) to find the electrical power required.

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \frac{\Phi}{\eta} = \frac{392.7 \text{ lm}}{100 \text{ lm/W}} = 3.93 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 393 \text{ lm} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad P \approx 3.93 \text{ W (LED)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Effectiveness of VT Tools

| Inspection Scenario | Defect Type | Tool of Choice | Min. Defect Detected | Approx. Cost (INR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| External weld surface | Undercut, porosity | Naked eye + torch | **0.5 mm** | **₹ 200** (torch) |
| Hairline cracks on shaft | Fatigue cracks | 10× hand magnifier | **0.05 mm** | **₹ 1,500** |
| Internal turbine blade | FOD, corrosion | Video borescope | **0.2 mm** | **₹ 2,50,000** |
| 50 m chimney wall | Surface cracking | Drone + 4K camera | **0.3 mm** | **₹ 1,50,000** |
| Underground pipe (200 m) | Internal corrosion | Crawler + CCTV | **0.5 mm** | **₹ 5,00,000** |
| Storage tank (10,000 m²) | Shell corrosion | Drone + thermal camera | **0.1 mm** (visual), 0.5 °C (thermal) | **₹ 8,00,000** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Visual Testing Operational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start VT Procedure] --> A1[Receive Inspection Request]
    A1 --> A2[Review Drawing and Code: AWS D1.1, ASME Sec IX]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Acceptance Criteria]
    A3 --> A4[Select VT Method: Direct or Aided]
    A4 --> A5{Is Surface Accessible?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Clean Surface: Degrease, Wire Brush]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Deploy Borescope or Fiberscope]
    A6 --> A8[Setup Illumination: Minimum 1000 lx]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Perform Systematic Visual Scan]
    A9 --> A10{Is Indication Found?}
    A10 -- No --> A11[Document: No Defects]
    A10 -- Yes --> A12[Record: Type, Size, Location]
    A12 --> A13[Compare Against Acceptance Criteria]
    A13 --> A14{Acceptable?}
    A14 -- Yes --> A15[Mark: Accept]
    A14 -- No --> A16[Mark: Repair or Reject]
    A15 --> A17[Generate Visual Inspection Report VIR]
    A16 --> A17
    A17 --> A18[Archive Records for Audit]
    A18 --> A19[End Procedure]
    A11 --> A19
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Aided VT System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ILLU[Illumination Subsystem]
        I1[White LED Source]
        I2[Fiber Optic Light Guide]
        I3[Ring Light Coupler]
    end

    subgraph OPTIC[Optical Subsystem]
        O1[Objective Lens]
        O2[Relay Lens System]
        O3[Eyepiece or CCD Sensor]
    end

    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Subsystem]
        M1[Insertion Probe: Rigid or Flexible]
        M2[Articulation Cables: 4 Way]
        M3[Tip Deflection: 0 to 180 deg]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control and Display Subsystem]
        C1[Light Intensity Control]
        C2[Focus Adjustment]
        C3[LCD or OLED Display]
        C4[Image and Video Storage]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> M1
    M1 --> O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> C3
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
    C1 --> I1
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: VT Application and Limitation Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Visual Testing VT] --> App[Applications]
    Start --> Lim[Limitations]

    App --> A1[Weld Inspection: Surface Defects]
    App --> A2[Castings: Surface Cracks, Porosity]
    App --> A3[Forgings: Laps, Seams, Cracks]
    App --> A4[In-Service Inspection: Aircraft, Pressure Vessel]
    App --> A5[Dimensional Verification: Geometry, Alignment]
    App --> A6[Corrosion Mapping: Pits, Wall Loss]
    App --> A7[Pre and Post-Service Checks]

    Lim --> L1[Surface-Only: No Subsurface Defect Detection]
    Lim --> L2[Line-of-Sight: No Internal Blind Areas]
    Lim --> L3[Subjectivity: Depends on Inspector Skill]
    Lim --> L4[Lighting Dependence: Poor Light Hides Defects]
    Lim --> L5[No Permanent Record Unless Photographed]
    Lim --> L6[Surface Preparation Required: Paint, Oil Hides Defects]
    Lim --> L7[Hazardous Environments: Heat, Radiation, Toxicity]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Standards Governing Visual Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STD[Visual Testing Standards] --> S1[ASTM E1316: NDT Terminology]
    STD --> S2[ASTM E2709: VT Qualification]
    STD --> S3[ASME Section V Article 9: VT Procedure]
    STD --> S4[AWS QC1: Certified Welding Inspector]
    STD --> S5[AWS D1.1: Structural Welding Code]
    STD --> S6[NAS 410: NDT Personnel Qualification Aerospace]
    STD --> S7[EN 13018: General Principles of VT]
    STD --> S8[IS 11804: Indian Standard for VT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Visual Testing. List the four key attributes evaluated during a visual inspection.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks allocation shown):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Visual Testing (VT) is a non-destructive examination method that uses direct human vision—either unaided (naked eye) or aided (magnifiers, borescopes, fiberscopes, cameras)—to detect surface discontinuities, evaluate geometric conformance, and verify the structural integrity of a component without causing damage. It is governed by ASTM E2709 and ASME Section V, Article 9.

**Four Key Attributes (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Surface Condition** — cracks, porosity, laps, undercuts, corrosion pits.
2. **Geometric Integrity** — dimensional accuracy, ovality, warping, alignment.
3. **Structural Completeness** — missing fasteners, weld bead profile, joint fit-up.
4. **Surface Cleanliness** — paint, oil, scale, biological fouling.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*State any three limitations of Visual Testing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark each):**

1. **Surface-Only Detection:** VT can detect only surface-breaking defects. Subsurface defects (laminations, internal porosity, embedded inclusions) remain invisible to the naked eye or optical aids.

2. **Line-of-Sight Dependency:** The inspector must have a direct or indirect (borescope) line of sight to the area of interest. Hidden surfaces, internal cavities without ports, and buried regions cannot be inspected.

3. **Operator Subjectivity:** VT results depend heavily on the inspector's visual acuity, training, experience, and fatigue level. Quantification of defect size is often approximate and may vary between inspectors.

*(Acceptable third limitation: Lighting dependence, lack of permanent record without photography, or hazardous-environment limitations.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain in detail the various **applications of Visual Testing** in engineering industries. Provide at least **six specific applications** with the defect type detected in each case.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Visual Testing finds universal application as the **first-line NDT method** across all major engineering sectors. Six specific applications are:

| # | Industry / Component | Application | Defect Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Welded structures** (bridges, ship hulls, pressure vessels) | Inspection of weld bead profile and surrounding heat-affected zone | Undercut, overlap, spatter, surface porosity, crater cracks |
| 2 | **Aerospace turbine engines** | Internal inspection of compressor and turbine blades via video borescope | Foreign Object Damage (FOD), leading-edge erosion, thermal cracks |
| 3 | **Storage tanks and pipelines** | External shell and weld inspection using drones and scaffolding | External corrosion pitting, coating breakdown, weld defects |
| 4 | **Castings (sand, die, investment)** | Surface inspection of cast iron, steel, and aluminium castings | Cold shuts, sand inclusions, shrinkage cracks, blowholes on the surface |
| 5 | **Forgings (hammer, press, drop)** | Surface inspection prior to machining | Laps, seams, forging cracks, scale pits |
| 6 | **Civil infrastructure** | Bridges, chimneys, high-rise buildings, transmission towers | Concrete spalling, rebar exposure, masonry cracks, structural deformation |
| 7 | **Heat exchangers and boiler tubes** | Internal inspection via fiberscope | Internal scaling, pitting corrosion, tube wall thinning, blockage |
| 8 | **Pre-service and in-service checks** | Aircraft airframe, automotive crash components, pressure vessels | Fatigue cracks, dent depth measurement, paint delamination, fastener integrity |

> **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** Definition of VT's role as first-line NDT (1 Mark) + Six applications with defect types in tabular format (5 Marks — 0.83 each) + Industry-specific mention of standards (1 Mark — e.g., AWS D1.1, ASME Sec. IX).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
