*Describe the **limitations of Visual Testing** in detail. Why is VT considered insufficient for detecting subsurface defects?*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Visual Testing, despite its widespread use, has well-defined limitations:

**1. Surface-Only Sensitivity (2 Marks):**
VT is fundamentally restricted to **surface-breaking defects**. The human eye receives light reflected from the **outermost surface** of the material. Subsurface defects such as internal porosity, laminations, inclusions, and embedded cracks do not produce any visible indication on the surface unless they are severe enough to cause surface deformation. Hence, VT is **completely blind to volumetric internal flaws**.

**2. Line-of-Sight Constraint (1 Mark):**
A direct or aided (borescope) line of sight is mandatory. **Buried welds, internal cavities without access ports, hidden structural members, and the back sides of pipes** are physically inaccessible.

**3. Subjectivity and Human Factors (1.5 Marks):**
The reliability of VT depends on:
- The inspector's **Snellen visual acuity** (must be correctable to 20/20 in at least one eye).
- **Training and certification** (per ASNT SNT-TC-1A or ISO 9712).
- **Fatigue and environmental stress** (heat, cold, noise).
- This subjectivity makes VT results **non-deterministic** in the strict statistical sense.

**4. Lighting and Environmental Sensitivity (1 Mark):**
Insufficient or wrongly oriented lighting can completely mask defects. Conversely, glare can produce false indications. Dust, smoke, fumes, and condensation further degrade performance.

**5. No Permanent Record by Default (0.5 Mark):**
A naked-eye visual inspection leaves **no traceable record** unless photographs or videos are explicitly captured. This is a documentation weakness compared to radiographic or ultrasonic techniques.

**6. Safety and Access Limitations (0.5 Mark):**
Hot surfaces, high-radiation zones, confined spaces, and toxic atmospheres restrict the inspector's physical presence. While remote VT (drones, robots) mitigates this, it adds cost.

**7. Cannot Quantify Defect Depth (0.5 Mark):**
VT can estimate lateral size (length, width) using pit gauges or comparators, but it **cannot measure defect depth** unless destructive sectioning is performed.

> **Why VT is Insufficient for Subsurface Defects (1 Mark integrated above):**
> Light penetration into metals is on the order of a few nanometers (skin depth at optical frequencies). Therefore, the optical reflection signal carries no information about the interior of the material. Methods like **Radiography (RT), Ultrasonic Testing (UT), and Eddy Current Testing (ET)** must be deployed for subsurface evaluation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Re-modeled for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*With the aid of a suitable diagram, describe the **equipment used in aided Visual Testing**. Explain the working principle of a **video borescope**.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Aided VT Equipment Hierarchy (3 Marks — listed with brief description):**

| Equipment | Function |
|---|---|
| Hand magnifier (loupe) | Enlarges small surface features (2×–20×) |
| Inspection mirror (dental/angle) | Indirect viewing of hidden surfaces |
| Borescope (rigid) | Direct line-of-sight into cavities via rod-lens optics |
| Fiberscope (flexible) | Flexible bundle of optical fibers for curved paths |
| Video borescope | Replaces fibers with a CCD/CMOS sensor; digital image |
| Industrial camera / CCTV | Long-distance visual capture |
| Drone-mounted camera | Aerial inspection of large structures |

**Working Principle of a Video Borescope (4 Marks — with labeled block diagram in the answer sheet):**

A video borescope is the **modern successor** to the fiberscope. Its operation involves:

1. **Insertion Probe (1 Mark):** A flexible, waterproof insertion tube (typically 6 mm to 10 mm diameter, 1.5 m to 10 m long) carries the optical and illumination fibers plus articulation cables. The distal tip can be deflected **±180° in four directions** (up, down, left, right) via joystick control.

2. **Illumination (0.5 Mark):** A high-intensity **white LED** (or xenon arc lamp) source feeds light through the fiber-optic light guide to a **ring-light** at the tip, illuminating the inspection area.

3. **Image Capture (1 Mark):** At the tip, a tiny **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) or CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor) sensor** captures the reflected light. The sensor converts the optical image into an analog or digital video signal.

4. **Signal Processing and Display (1 Mark):** The video signal is transmitted through the insertion tube to an external control unit, which performs **image processing, brightness control, digital zoom (typically 4×–8×), and contrast enhancement** before display on an **LCD or OLED screen**.

5. **Image and Video Storage (0.5 Mark):** Inspection data is stored on internal memory, SD cards, or USB drives, creating a **permanent recordable trace** of the inspection — overcoming the chief limitation of naked-eye VT.

> **Key Advantages over Fiberscope (inbuilt in above):** Higher resolution, no pixelation, no broken fiber artifacts, capability for image measurement (comparator overlays), and video recording.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Discuss the **illumination requirements** for effective Visual Testing as per ASTM standards. Compute the **luminous flux required** to achieve an illuminance of **1500 lx** over a circular inspection area of **diameter 400 mm**. Assume LED efficacy of 90 lm/W.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**ASTM Illumination Requirements (3 Marks):**

Per **ASTM E2709** and **ASTM E1316**, the following illumination minima apply:

| Application | Minimum Illuminance |
|---|---|
| General industrial inspection | **1075 lx (100 fc)** |
| Detailed inspection | **2150 lx (200 fc)** |
| Very fine inspection | **5000 lx (500 fc)** |

Additional requirements:
- The light source should provide **uniform illumination** over the inspection area.
- **Oblique illumination (15° to 45°)** is recommended for crack detection, as it casts shadows into surface-breaking defects, enhancing their visibility.
- The light source's **color temperature** should be close to daylight (≈ 5500 K) for accurate color perception.
- The inspector's viewing distance should not exceed **610 mm (24 inches)**.

**Computation (4 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Required illuminance: $E = 1500 \text{ lx}$
- Diameter of circular inspection area: $d = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- LED luminous efficacy: $\eta = 90 \text{ lm/W}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the inspection area (0.5 Mark):**

$$A = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi (0.2)^{2} = 0.1257 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the illuminance equation (0.5 Mark):**

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = E \cdot A$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (1 Mark):**

$$\Phi = 1500 \text{ lx} \times 0.1257 \text{ m}^{2} = 188.5 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the electrical power required from the LED (1 Mark):**

$$P = \frac{\Phi}{\eta} = \frac{188.5 \text{ lm}}{90 \text{ lm/W}} = 2.09 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Result (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{\Phi \approx 188.5 \text{ lm} \quad ; \quad P_{\text{electrical}} \approx 2.09 \text{ W}}$$

> **Conclusion:** A standard 3 W LED torch is sufficient to achieve the required illuminance for this inspection scenario.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing "Applications" with "Advantages"** — Applications are *where* VT is used (welds, castings, etc.); Advantages are *why* (cheap, fast, no radiation). Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the standard reference** — Always mention **ASTM E2709, ASTM E1316, or ASME Sec. V Article 9** for full credit on definitions.
> 3. **Skipping the unit in numerical answers** — Writing "1500" without "lx" or "lumens" loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Vague limitations** — Writing "It cannot see inside" is worth 0 marks; writing "It is restricted to surface-breaking discontinuities due to the optical skin depth of metals being on the order of nanometers" earns full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the area calculation step** in derivation problems — Always show $A = \pi r^2$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- [ ] **VT = Visual Testing / Visual Inspection (VI) / Visual Examination (VE)** — the **oldest and most widely used NDT** method.
- [ ] **Two categories:** **Direct (unaided) VT** and **Aided (indirect) VT** using magnifiers, mirrors, borescopes, fiberscopes, video borescopes, and cameras.
- [ ] **Governing Standards:** **ASTM E2709** (qualification), **ASTM E1316** (terminology), **ASME Section V Article 9** (procedure), **AWS D1.1** (weld acceptance), **NAS 410** (aerospace qualification).
- [ ] **Minimum Illuminance:** **1075 lx (100 fc)** for general, **2150 lx (200 fc)** for detailed inspection.
- [ ] **Maximum Viewing Distance:** **610 mm (24 inches)**.
- [ ] **Minimum Resolvable Defect (Unaided):** **≈ 0.1 mm** at 250 mm.
- [ ] **Loupe Magnification Formula:** $M = 25 / f$ (with $f$ in cm).
- [ ] **Illuminance Formula:** $E = \Phi / A$ (lux = lumens / m²).
- [ ] **Unit Conversion:** $1 \text{ fc} = 10.76 \text{ lx}$.
- [ ] **4 Attributes Inspected:** Surface condition, geometric integrity, structural completeness, surface cleanliness.
- [ ] **6+ Applications:** Welds, castings, forgings, in-service aerospace, in-service pressure vessels, storage tanks, pipelines, heat exchangers, bridges, chimneys, transmission towers.
- [ ] **7+ Limitations:** Surface-only, line-of-sight, subjectivity, lighting dependence, no permanent record, hazardous-environment restriction, inability to measure depth, no subsurface defect detection.
- [ ] **Oblique illumination at 15°–45°** is used to **enhance crack visibility** via shadow formation.
- [ ] **Video borescopes** are the **modern standard** for internal VT — they offer digital recording, image measurement, and 4-way articulation.
- [ ] **Color temperature of light source** should be close to **5500 K (daylight)** for accurate defect color perception.
- [ ] **Industrial share:** VT accounts for **~40% of all NDT activities** worldwide (ASNT data).
- [ ] **Inspector qualification:** ASNT SNT-TC-1A, ISO 9712, or NAS 410 (aerospace).
- [ ] **Key difference from RT/UT/ET:** VT detects **only surface-breaking defects**; subsurface flaws require **radiographic, ultrasonic, or eddy current** methods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Visual Testing (VT)** is the oldest and most widely used Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method, in which a trained inspector evaluates the surface condition and integrity of a component through direct or indirect human observation. **Lighting** is the foundational enabling parameter of VT, defined as the controlled application of electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (wavelength **380 nm to 780 nm**) onto the test surface to maximize the detectability of surface and near-surface discontinuities.

According to **ASTM E1316** and **ASNT CP-189**, adequate and appropriate lighting is a mandatory pre-requisite for any visual examination. Insufficient or incorrect illumination is one of the most common root causes of missed indications during in-service and in-process inspections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions on "Lighting" frequently test the relationship between **illumination quantity (lux)**, **light source spectral characteristics**, and the **type of discontinuity** being detected. Memorize the standard lux values prescribed by ASME and IS standards.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a doctor trying to spot a small skin rash on a patient inside a dimly-lit room. Even though your eyes are sharp, you will miss the rash. Now imagine the same scenario with a bright, focused torchlight angled across the skin — the rash becomes obvious. The torchlight angle reveals the texture (surface topology) of the skin.

**Lighting in NDT works on the same principle:** the *intensity*, *direction*, *color temperature*, and *angle* of illumination determine whether a hairline crack, porosity, or corrosion pit becomes visible to the inspector's eye or remains hidden. Lighting is not just "brightness" — it is a **diagnostic tool** that manipulates how light interacts with surface features (reflection, scattering, and shadowing) to produce visual contrast.

> [!NOTE]
> The human eye can detect contrast differences as small as **2% to 5%** under optimal lighting conditions, but this drops drastically under poor lighting. Adequate lighting therefore directly improves Probability of Detection (POD) in NDT.

## 1.3 Fundamental Photometric Quantities (Bold-Listed for Quick Recall)

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition |
|----------|--------|---------|------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **Lumen (lm)** | Total light energy emitted per second |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **Candela (cd)** | Flux per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance (Illumination) | $E$ | **Lux (lx) = lm/m²** | Flux incident per unit area |
| Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m²** | Intensity per unit projected area |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Luminous flux per watt of input power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Visual Perception in NDT

The detectability of a flaw under visual inspection depends on four interacting parameters:

1. **Illuminance ($E$):** Quantity of light falling on the surface (measured in lux).
2. **Contrast ($C$):** Difference in luminance between the flaw and the surrounding background. It is defined mathematically as:

$$C = \frac{\vert L_f - L_b \vert}{L_b}$$

where $L_f$ is the luminance of the flaw and $L_b$ is the luminance of the background. A contrast of less than **0.05 (5%)** is generally considered undetectable to the average human eye.

3. **Resolving Power of the Eye:** Determined by visual acuity, typically referenced to a Snellen chart value of **20/20 (6/6)**. The eye can resolve a feature subtending an angular size of approximately **1 arc-minute (1/60 degree)**.
4. **Surface Reflectivity:** Governed by the angle of incidence, the refractive index of the material, and the surface roughness (governed by the Rayleigh criterion and bidirectional reflectance distribution function — BRDF).

## 2.2 Classification of Lighting in NDT

Lighting sources used in NDT are classified along two axes:

### A. By Origin
- **Natural Lighting:** Sunlight. Variable, uncontrolled, generally unsuitable for critical NDT.
- **Artificial Lighting:** Man-made, controllable. Mandatory for certified inspections.

### B. By Spectral Characteristic (Color of Light)
- **White Light (Broadband):** Covers the full visible spectrum. Used for general visual inspection.
- **Monochromatic Light:** Single wavelength (e.g., sodium vapor lamp at **589 nm**). Used to reduce chromatic aberration and improve contrast in specific applications.
- **Ultraviolet (UV) / Black Light:** Wavelength **365 nm** (long-wave UV-A). Used in **Fluorescent Penetrant Testing (FPT)** and **Fluorescent Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)**.
- **Infrared (IR):** Wavelength **> 780 nm**. Used in thermography and for inspecting materials opaque to visible light.

## 2.3 Standard Recommended Illumination Levels (High-Yield for KTU)

These values are **must-know** for the KTU 2024 examination. They are derived from **ASME Section V Article 9** and **ASTM E1106**:

| Inspection Task | Minimum Illuminance (Lux) |
|---|---|
| General shop floor / warehouse | **100 lx** |
| Coarse visual examination (e.g., weld profile) | **200 – 500 lx** |
| Routine VT of welds and machined surfaces | **500 – 1000 lx** |
| Detailed inspection (fine cracks, small pits) | **1000 – 2000 lx** |
| Critical inspection / Precision components | **> 2000 lx** (often 5000 lx) |
| FPT / MT inspection booth (UV-A at surface) | **< 50 lx visible + ≥ 1000 µW/cm² UV-A** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point:** A 14-mark KTU question often asks students to justify the choice of illuminance for a given scenario. The justification must reference the **size of the discontinuity**, the **required Probability of Detection (POD)**, and the relevant **ASME/IS standard**.

## 2.4 Light Sources Used in NDT — Comparative Matrix

| Light Source | Luminous Efficacy (lm/W) | Color Temp (K) | NDT Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent** | 10 – 15 | 2700 – 3000 | Low-cost portable VT; warm color (poor for fine detail) |
| **Halogen (Tungsten-Halogen)** | 15 – 25 | 3000 – 3400 | **Most common** handheld NDT lamp; excellent color rendering (CRI ≈ 100) |
| **Fluorescent (CFL/T5/T8)** | 50 – 100 | 3000 – 6500 | Booth lighting for MT/FPT; large area illumination |
| **Mercury Vapor (HID)** | 40 – 60 | 4000 – 6000 | Older UV-A sources for FPT (being phased out) |
| **Metal Halide** | 70 – 115 | 4000 – 5500 | High-intensity inspection of large welds and tanks |
| **High-Pressure Sodium (HPS)** | 85 – 150 | 2000 – 2200 | Outdoor storage yard inspection; monochromatic yellow light |
| **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** | 80 – 200 | 2700 – 6500 | Modern preferred source; long life, low heat, ruggedized for field NDT |
| **Xenon Arc** | 25 – 50 | 6000 (sun-like) | High-speed photography, stroboscopic inspection |
| **UV-A (Black Light)** | N/A | N/A | FPT/MT fluorescence excitation (365 nm) |

## 2.5 The Inverse Square Law of Illuminance (Critical Formula)

For a point light source, the illuminance at a surface is governed by the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

where:
- $E$ = Illuminance in lux (lx)
- $I$ = Luminous intensity of the source in candela (cd)
- $d$ = Distance from the source to the surface in meters (m)
- $\theta$ = Angle between the light ray and the surface normal (degrees)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Doubling the distance between the lamp and the inspection surface reduces the illuminance to **one-fourth (25%)**. In field NDT, the inspector must therefore either use a higher-power lamp or move closer to maintain the minimum required lux level.

## 2.6 Lighting Techniques (Directional Strategy)

The *angle* of light is as important as the *quantity*. The four primary directional techniques are:

1. **Direct Illumination:** Light strikes the surface at an angle close to normal (perpendicular). Best for inspecting **color contrast** differences (e.g., corrosion, paint defects).
2. **Grazing (Tangential) Illumination:** Light strikes at a very shallow angle (typically **5° – 15°** from the surface plane). Best for revealing **surface-breaking cracks, undercuts, and porosity** in welds, because the shallow angle casts long shadows from the edges of the flaw. **This is the single most important technique in weld VT.**
3. **Diffuse Illumination:** Light is scattered through a translucent screen to produce uniform, shadowless lighting. Best for inspecting **reflective/polished surfaces** (e.g., turbine blades, mirror-finish components) where glare would otherwise obscure features.
4. **Transillumination (Backlighting):** Light is placed behind the translucent part. Used for inspecting **transparent or semi-transparent components** (e.g., glass, certain plastics, fuel filters) for internal voids.

## 2.7 Special-Purpose Lighting Techniques

- **Stroboscopic Lighting:** Pulses of light synchronized with rotating/reciprocating machinery to "freeze" motion. Used for inspecting **moving parts** like turbine blades, printing rollers, and fan assemblies.
- **Polarized Light:** Used with crossed polarizers to reduce glare from highly reflective surfaces and to reveal stress patterns in **photoelastic stress analysis** of transparent models.
- **Fiber Optic Illumination:** Cold light delivered via flexible fiber bundles into confined spaces (e.g., inside turbine casings, tube interiors, engine bores).
- **Laser Line / Laser Scan Illumination:** Projects a structured line/sheet of light onto a surface; the deformation of the line reveals surface profile and weld geometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Application of the Inverse Square Law in a Practical NDT Scenario

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A handheld NDT halogen lamp produces a luminous intensity of **800 candela** along its central axis. An inspector holds the lamp at a distance of **0.5 m** from a weld surface, with the beam axis inclined at **60°** to the surface normal. Calculate the illuminance on the surface. If the inspector moves the lamp to a distance of **1.0 m** at the same angle, what is the new illuminance, and by what percentage has the illuminance changed?

### Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters

From the problem statement, we extract:

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 800 \text{ cd} \\
d_1 &= 0.5 \text{ m} \\
\theta &= 60^{\circ} \text{ (angle from the normal)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Cosine Component

Because the light is not perpendicular to the surface, only the component of flux aligned with the surface normal contributes to the illuminance. The cosine of 60° is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(60^{\circ}) &= 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Illuminance at 0.5 m

We substitute the values into the governing equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d_1^2} \\
E_1 &= \frac{800 \cdot 0.5}{(0.5)^2} \\
E_1 &= \frac{400}{0.25} \\
E_1 &= 1600 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Illuminance at 1.0 m

We repeat the calculation at the new distance:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_2 &= \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d_2^2} \\
E_2 &= \frac{800 \cdot 0.5}{(1.0)^2} \\
E_2 &= \frac{400}{1.0} \\
E_2 &= 400 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Percentage Change in Illuminance

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ change} &= \frac{E_2 - E_1}{E_1} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ change} &= \frac{400 - 1600}{1600} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ change} &= \frac{-1200}{1600} \times 100 \\
\% \text{ change} &= -75\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result

The illuminance dropped by **75%** (to one-quarter of its original value) when the distance was doubled. This numerically confirms the Inverse Square Law: doubling distance → illuminance becomes $1/2^2 = 1/4$. In practice, this means the inspector must now either move the lamp back to **0.5 m** or use a lamp with a luminous intensity of **3200 cd** to maintain the original 1600 lx required for detailed weld inspection.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Luminous Intensity of a Lamp for a Specified Illuminance

**Problem Statement:**
A fluorescent penetrant inspection booth requires a uniform illuminance of **1500 lx** on a flat plate placed **1.2 m** directly below a ceiling-mounted light source. Assuming the source behaves as a point source on the normal axis, calculate the required luminous intensity of the lamp.

### Step 1: Rearrange the Illuminance Formula

We start with the governing equation:

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

Since the light is on the normal axis, $\theta = 0^{\circ}$, and $\cos(0^{\circ}) = 1$. The formula simplifies to:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

### Step 2: Solve for Luminous Intensity

Rearranging:

$$I = E \cdot d^2$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 1500 \cdot (1.2)^2 \\
I &= 1500 \cdot 1.44 \\
I &= 2160 \text{ cd}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Final Interpretation

The lamp must emit at least **2160 candela** along the central axis to produce the required 1500 lx on the inspection surface 1.2 m below. When selecting a real fixture, an engineer must add a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** to account for lumen depreciation over the lamp's lifetime and dirt accumulation on the fixture, leading to a practical design value of approximately **3240 – 4320 cd**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Light Source Selection by NDT Method

| NDT Method | Required Spectral Band | Recommended Source | Min. Illuminance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Visual Testing (white light) | 380 – 780 nm (white) | Halogen / LED | **500 – 1000 lx** |
| Fluorescent PT (FPT) | 365 nm UV-A + visible filter | Mercury vapor / UV-LED | **≤ 50 lx visible + ≥ 1000 µW/cm² UV-A** |
| Fluorescent MT (FMT) | 365 nm UV-A | UV-A flood lamp | **≤ 20 lx visible** |
| Remote VT (borescopes) | White (transmitted via fiber) | Fiber-optic halogen / LED | **~ 1000 lx at tip** |
| Underwater VT | White (sealed) | Sealed-beam halogen | **> 1000 lx at 1 m** |
| Stroboscopic VT | White pulsed | Xenon strobe | **Peak > 5000 lx** |
| Photoelastic stress analysis | Monochromatic (589 nm) | Sodium vapor | **Diffuse 500 lx** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Visual Testing (Lighting) Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source Mains or Battery] --> B[Lamp Driver and Ballast]
    B --> C[Light Source Module]
    C --> D[Optical Conditioning Unit]
    D --> E[Beam Shaping Lens]
    E --> F[Fiber Optic Bundle Optional]
    F --> G[Test Surface]
    G --> H[Reflected or Scattered Light]
    H --> I[Inspector Eye or Camera Sensor]
    I --> J{Decision Discontinuity Present}
    J --> K[Yes Record and Report]
    J --> L[No Clear Part]
    M[Ambient Light Sensor] --> N[Illuminance Controller]
    N --> B
    O[Spectral Filter UV or IR] --> D
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Selection of Lighting Technique for a Weld Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Identify Flaw Type] --> S2{Flaw Requires Shadow}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Grazing Illumination Angle 5 to 15 deg]
    S2 -->|No| S4{Flaw Requires Color Contrast}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Direct Illumination Angle 60 to 90 deg]
    S4 -->|No| S6{Surface is Highly Reflective}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Diffuse Illumination Through Scrim]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Backlighting for Transparent Part]
    S3 --> S9[Verify Lux Level with Meter]
    S5 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Record and Document]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block: Lux Meter Calibration Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Reference Standard Lamp] --> P2[Traceable to NPL or NABL]
    P2 --> P3[Lux Meter Calibration]
    P3 --> P4[Field Lux Meter]
    P4 --> P5[Measure at Inspection Plane]
    P5 --> P6[Compare with Standard e.g. 1000 lx]
    P6 --> P7{Within Tolerance}
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Inspection Approved]
    P7 -->|No| P9[Adjust Lamp Position or Power]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Grazing Illumination Geometry on a Weld

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Halogen Lamp] -- Angle 5 to 15 deg from surface --> W1[Weld Bead with Surface Crack]
    W1 -- Reflects light into shadow --> E1[Inspector Eye Positioned at Normal]
    W1 -. Generates long shadow of crack .-> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams map the **functional information flow** rather than a literal optical ray diagram. In a real drawing, the grazing-angle lamp would project a long shadow of the crack toward the inspector, dramatically increasing the visible contrast of the discontinuity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define illuminance and state the minimum recommended illuminance for detailed visual inspection of a weld according to ASME Section V.**

**Model Answer:**
Illuminance is the luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface, measured in **lux (lx = lm/m²)**. According to **ASME Section V Article 9**, the minimum recommended illuminance for detailed visual inspection of a weld is **1000 lx (100 foot-candles)**. For critical inspections, values of **2000 lx or higher** are recommended. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is grazing illumination, and for what type of discontinuity is it most effective?**

**Model Answer:**
Grazing illumination is a lighting technique in which the light source is positioned at a very shallow angle (typically **5° to 15°** from the surface) so that the light beam grazes the surface. It is most effective for detecting **surface-breaking discontinuities** such as cracks, undercuts, laps, and porosity in welds, because the shallow angle causes the edges of these flaws to cast long, easily visible shadows. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various types of light sources used in Non-Destructive Testing. Compare their luminous efficacy, color temperature, and typical NDT applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A handheld inspection lamp has a luminous intensity of **1200 cd** along its central axis. The lamp is held at a distance of **0.8 m** from the inspection surface, with the beam inclined at **30°** to the surface normal. Calculate the illuminance on the surface. If the inspection standard requires a minimum of **1500 lx**, determine the new distance at which the lamp must be held (at the same angle) to meet the standard. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The major types of light sources used in NDT are:

**1. Incandescent Lamps**
- Operating principle: Tungsten filament heated to ~2800 K in an inert gas/vacuum.
- Luminous efficacy: **10 – 15 lm/W** (very low).
- Color temperature: **2700 – 3000 K** (warm yellow).
- Applications: Low-cost portable VT, general-purpose hand lamps. Not preferred for critical NDT due to poor color rendering and short life.

**2. Tungsten-Halogen Lamps**
- Operating principle: Halogen gas (iodine/bromine) enables higher filament temperature via the halogen regeneration cycle.
- Luminous efficacy: **15 – 25 lm/W**.
- Color temperature: **3000 – 3400 K**.
- Applications: **The most widely used NDT light source** for portable VT, borescope illuminators, and machine-vision systems because of excellent Color Rendering Index (CRI ≈ 100).

**3. Fluorescent Lamps (CFL / T5 / T8)**
- Operating principle: Mercury vapor discharge excites phosphor coating on the inner tube wall.
- Luminous efficacy: **50 – 100 lm/W**.
- Color temperature: **3000 – 6500 K** (selectable by phosphor blend).
- Applications: Inspection booth ambient lighting, large-area illumination in FPT/MT booths.

**4. High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps — Mercury Vapor and Metal Halide**
- Operating principle: Electric arc through vaporized metal under high pressure.
- Luminous efficacy: **40 – 115 lm/W**.
- Color temperature: **4000 – 6000 K**.
- Applications: Inspection of large structures (tanks, pressure vessels, ship hulls) where high-intensity floodlighting is required.

**5. Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)**
- Operating principle: Electroluminescence at a p–n junction.
- Luminous efficacy: **80 – 200 lm/W**.
- Color temperature: **2700 – 6500 K**.
- Applications: Modern, ruggedized field NDT lamps; long life (50,000 hours), low heat, shock-resistant, ideal for handheld and battery-powered use.

**6. UV-A (Black Light) Lamps**
- Operating principle: Mercury discharge filtered to transmit only the **365 nm** line.
- Applications: **Fluorescent Penetrant Testing (FPT)** and **Fluorescent Magnetic Particle Testing (FMT)**.

**7. Xenon Arc Lamps**
- Operating principle: Continuous arc in xenon gas at high pressure.
- Color temperature: **~ 6000 K** (closest to sunlight).
- Applications: Stroboscopic inspection of moving parts; high-speed photography.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 1200 \text{ cd} \\
d_1 &= 0.8 \text{ m} \\
\theta &= 30^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos\theta$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(30^{\circ}) &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Initial Illuminance**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d_1^2} \\
E_1 &= \frac{1200 \cdot 0.8660}{(0.8)^2} \\
E_1 &= \frac{1039.2}{0.64} \\
E_1 &= 1623.75 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}$$

This already exceeds the 1500 lx minimum, but the question requires the **exact distance** that yields **exactly 1500 lx**.

**Step 4: Rearrange the Formula to Solve for New Distance**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2} \implies d^2 = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{E}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $E = 1500$ lx**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_2^2 &= \frac{1200 \cdot 0.8660}{1500} \\
d_2^2 &= \frac{1039.2}{1500} \\
d_2^2 &= 0.6928 \\
d_2 &= \sqrt{0.6928} \\
d_2 &\approx 0.832 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Final Interpretation**

The lamp must be held at a distance of approximately **0.832 m** from the surface (at 30° from normal) to produce exactly **1500 lx**. Note that although the standard is technically met at 0.8 m (1623.75 lx), the *minimum-distance* design value to just satisfy the requirement is 0.832 m. In practice, the inspector should keep the lamp as close as ergonomically feasible (0.5 – 0.6 m) to maximize the illuminance margin against dirt, lamp aging, and ambient light loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the four main directional lighting techniques used in visual testing. State the type of discontinuity best revealed by each. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the role of lighting in **Fluorescent Penetrant Testing (FPT)**. State the standard UV-A irradiance required at the inspection surface and explain why visible ambient light must be minimized. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The four main directional lighting techniques in visual testing are:

**1. Direct Illumination**
- Geometry: Light source is positioned close to the inspector's line of sight, with the beam axis nearly perpendicular to the surface (angle of incidence ≈ 0° to 30° from normal).
- Best for: Color and reflectance differences — **corrosion, oil staining, paint delamination, dye-penetrant indications under white light.**
- Limitation: Glare on polished surfaces; poor for fine surface-breaking cracks.

**2. Grazing (Tangential) Illumination**
- Geometry: Light source is positioned at a very low angle (5° to 15° from the surface plane), so the beam skims across the surface.
- Best for: **Surface-breaking cracks, undercuts, laps, lack of fusion, and porosity in welds.** The shallow angle makes the edges of the flaw cast long shadows that are easily visible to the eye.
- Limitation: Insensitive to color contrast; highly sensitive to inspector position.

**3. Diffuse Illumination**
- Geometry: Light is passed through a translucent diffuser (e.g., a frosted screen or integrating sphere) to produce a uniform, shadow-free field.
- Best for: **Polished, specular, or highly reflective surfaces** (turbine blades, mirror-polished shafts, lapped surfaces) where direct light would cause glare.
- Limitation: Eliminates the very shadows needed to detect cracks; therefore usually combined with another technique.

**4. Transillumination (Backlighting)**
- Geometry: Light source is placed *behind* the test object.
- Best for: **Transparent or translucent components** (glass plates, plastic bottles, filters, fuel bowls) — flaws appear as dark silhouettes against a bright background.
- Limitation: Only applicable to transparent materials.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

In **Fluorescent Penetrant Testing (FPT)**, lighting plays a **decisive role** because the indication is observed not by reflected white light but by the **fluorescent emission** of the penetrant dye under ultraviolet-A (UV-A) excitation.

**Role of UV-A Lighting:**

1. The penetrant contains a fluorescent dye (typically based on naphthalimide chemistry) that absorbs UV-A photons at **365 nm** and re-emits them as visible yellow-green light at approximately **500 – 580 nm**.
2. The UV-A source (mercury-vapor lamp, UV-LED, or metal-halide with Wood's glass filter) must deliver a **minimum irradiance of 1000 µW/cm²** (or 10 W/m²) at the inspection surface, as per **ASTM E1417** and **ASME Section V Article 6**.
3. The inspector views the surface through **UV-A absorbing goggles** that block the excitation wavelength but transmit the longer-wavelength fluorescent emission, dramatically improving the contrast of the indication against the dark background.

**Why Visible Ambient Light Must Be Minimized:**

- The fluorescent emission is faint — typically only a few candela per square meter.
- Any stray visible light (white fluorescent tubes, daylight from windows, unfiltered mercury lines) **adds to the background luminance**, which in turn **reduces the contrast** of the indication.
- Recall the contrast formula:

$$C = \frac{\vert L_f - L_b \vert}{L_b}$$

If $L_b$ (background luminance) increases due to ambient visible light, the contrast $C$ drops below the human eye's detection threshold of approximately **0.05**, causing the indication to become invisible.

- Therefore, the inspection booth must be maintained at **≤ 50 lux of visible illuminance** (often ≤ 20 lux for critical FPT) while the UV-A source is active. This is achieved by using dark walls, black curtains, Wood's glass filters, and blackout provisions on booth windows.

**Practical Booth Specifications:**

- UV-A irradiance at surface: **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** (some specifications require ≥ 1200 µW/cm²).
- Visible ambient illuminance: **≤ 50 lx** (preferably ≤ 20 lx).
- UV-A wavelength: **365 nm ± 5 nm** (long-wave UV-A).
- UV-A filter: **Wood's glass** (nickel-oxide-doped silica) to block visible mercury lines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing lux with lumens.** Lux is illuminance (lm/m²); lumens is total flux. Examiners specifically look for the correct unit on the answer sheet.
> 2. **Stating "more light is always better."** Wrong — excess light on a polished surface produces glare and reduces contrast. The correct answer references the *minimum* required lux per the relevant standard.
> 3. **Omitting the cosine term** in the inverse square law when the lamp is not perpendicular to the surface. This is the single most common calculation error in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Mixing up FPT and MT lighting requirements.** FPT requires ≤ 50 lx visible, MT under fluorescent conditions requires ≤ 20 lx visible — examiners check this carefully.
> 5. **Failing to cite a standard** (ASME / ASTM / IS). A 14-mark answer that does not reference any code/standard will typically lose 2 – 3 marks under the KTU marking scheme.
> 6. **Saying "fluorescent lamp" when meaning "UV-A lamp."** These are entirely different. Fluorescent = white visible light; UV-A = 365 nm ultraviolet.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Lighting** is the foundational enabler of **Visual Testing (VT)**, the most widely used NDT method.
- Visible spectrum for NDT: **380 nm – 780 nm**.
- Key photometric quantity in NDT is **illuminance (E)** in **lux (lx)**.
- Fundamental governing equation:

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

- Doubling distance → illuminance drops to **1/4** (Inverse Square Law).
- Standard illuminance values (ASME Section V):
  - General: **100 lx**
  - Routine VT: **500 – 1000 lx**
  - Detailed / critical VT: **≥ 1000 lx (often 2000+ lx)**
- Four directional techniques: **Direct, Grazing, Diffuse, Transillumination**.
- **Grazing illumination (5° – 15°)** is the single most important technique for **detecting weld cracks**.
- **Halogen lamps** are the most common portable NDT source (CRI ≈ 100).
- **LEDs** are the modern preferred field source (long life, rugged, low heat).
- For **FPT / FMT**: UV-A at **365 nm**, irradiance **≥ 1000 µW/cm²**, visible ambient **≤ 50 lx** (≤ 20 lx for FMT).
- Always verify illuminance with a **calibrated lux meter** traceable to **NPL / NABL**.
- Always cite the relevant standard: **ASME V, ASTM E1417, ASTM E1106, ASTM E1316, IS 5334**.
- A good NDT lighting setup balances **quantity (lux)**, **quality (CRI)**, **direction (angle)**, and **spectral band (wavelength)** to maximize **Probability of Detection (POD)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Attributes** are the intrinsic, measurable, and quantifiable physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural characteristics of a material that govern its behavior under applied loads, environmental exposure, and service conditions. In the context of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT), material attributes form the **foundational decision matrix** that determines:
> - Which NDT technique is appropriate,
> - What kind of defects are likely to arise,
> - The sensitivity and reliability of the inspection process,
> - The acceptance/rejection criteria for the component.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, material attributes are classified into four primary domains:

| Domain | Key Attributes |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Hardness, Tensile Strength, Ductility, Toughness, Elasticity, Plasticity, Fatigue Limit, Creep Resistance |
| **Physical** | Density, Specific Gravity, Thermal Conductivity, Electrical Conductivity, Magnetic Permeability |
| **Chemical** | Composition, Corrosion Resistance, Alloy Content, Phase Distribution |
| **Structural** | Grain Size, Grain Orientation, Crystal Structure, Homogeneity, Porosity, Anisotropy |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body Check-up"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Material Attributes like a doctor's medical examination:**
> - Before a doctor can diagnose an illness, they must first know the **patient's baseline health** — blood pressure, heart rate, body temperature, age, weight, and medical history.
> - Similarly, before an NDT engineer selects an inspection technique, they must know the **material's baseline properties** — its hardness, conductivity, magnetic behavior, grain structure, etc.
> - Just as a heart specialist uses an ECG (one specific test) while a bone doctor uses an X-ray, an NDT engineer uses **ultrasonic testing for dense metals**, **magnetic particle testing for ferromagnetic steels**, and **eddy current testing for conductive non-magnetic materials**.
> - The "patient's history" is the material attribute; the "test" is the NDT method.

**Bottom line:** You cannot choose the right NDT method without first understanding the material's attributes — this is the **first principle of inspection engineering**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

Key standardized metrics used in material attribute measurement:

- **Hardness**: Measured in **HV (Vickers)**, **HB (Brinell)**, **HRC (Rockwell C)**, with typical values ranging from **30 HB (soft aluminum)** to **700 HB (hard tool steel)**.
- **Density (ρ)**: For **steel ρ ≈ 7850 kg/m³**, **aluminum ρ ≈ 2700 kg/m³**, **titanium ρ ≈ 4500 kg/m³**.
- **Speed of Sound (c)**: In **steel c ≈ 5920 m/s**, in **aluminum c ≈ 6320 m/s**, in **air c ≈ 343 m/s**.
- **Electrical Conductivity (σ)**: **Copper σ ≈ 5.96 × 10⁷ S/m** (used as IACS reference standard of 100% IACS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Material Attributes Relevant to NDT

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Attributes

**A) Hardness**
- **Definition:** Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation (indentation, scratching, or abrasion).
- **Engineering Importance:** Directly correlates with tensile strength. Harder materials tend to be more brittle but wear-resistant.
- **NDT Relevance:** Influences the choice of surface NDT methods. Hard surfaces (e.g., case-hardened steels) may require special surface preparation before Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT).
- **Empirical Correlation (for steels):**
$$\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.45 \times HV$$
where $\sigma_{UTS}$ is the Ultimate Tensile Strength in MPa and $HV$ is the Vickers Hardness Number.

**B) Tensile Strength (σ_UTS)**
- **Definition:** Maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched or pulled before necking/fracture.
- **NDT Relevance:** Critical for in-service components under cyclic loading — NDT is used to monitor degradation of tensile properties over time.

**C) Ductility (% Elongation)**
- **Definition:** Measure of the degree of plastic deformation a material can sustain before fracture.
- **Mathematical Form:**
$$\% \, Elongation = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$
where $L_0$ is the original gauge length and $L_f$ is the final length at fracture.
- **NDT Relevance:** Ductile materials absorb energy from impacts and can deform plastically around defects — affects defect tolerance criteria.

**D) Toughness (Impact Energy)**
- **Definition:** Ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing.
- **Mathematical Form:**
$$Toughness = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$
where the integral represents the area under the stress-strain curve up to fracture strain $\varepsilon_f$.

**E) Elasticity (Young's Modulus, E)**
- **Definition:** Stiffness of a material — ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
- **NDT Relevance:** Determines the velocity of ultrasonic waves:
$$c = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$
where $E$ is Young's modulus, $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio, and $\rho$ is density. This is **fundamental to ultrasonic thickness gauging and flaw detection**.

**F) Plasticity**
- **Definition:** Ability to undergo permanent deformation without rupture.
- **NDT Relevance:** Ductile materials produce **plastic deformation zones (PDZ)** around cracks, which can sometimes mask discontinuities.

**G) Fatigue Strength**
- **Definition:** Maximum cyclic stress a material can endure for a specified number of cycles without failure.
- **NDT Relevance:** Fatigue cracks are a primary target of NDT inspections in aerospace, bridges, and pressure vessels.

**H) Creep Resistance**
- **Definition:** Ability to resist deformation under sustained load at elevated temperatures.
- **NDT Relevance:** Components in power plants and jet engines require periodic NDT to monitor creep damage.

### 2.1.2 Physical Attributes

**A) Density (ρ)**
- Used in radiographic NDT for attenuation calculations and in ultrasonic NDT for acoustic impedance:
$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$
where $Z$ is acoustic impedance (kg/m²·s), $\rho$ is density, and $c$ is sound velocity.

**B) Electrical Conductivity (σ)**
- **Critical for Eddy Current Testing (ECT).** Higher conductivity materials (copper, aluminum) produce stronger eddy current responses.
- **Reference Standard:** **International Annealed Copper Standard (IACS)** = **5.96 × 10⁷ S/m = 100% IACS**.

**C) Magnetic Permeability (μ)**
- **Critical for Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** and **Magnetic Flux Leakage (MFL).** Ferromagnetic materials (μ >> 1) can be inspected; non-magnetic materials (μ ≈ 1) cannot.

**D) Thermal Conductivity (k)**
- Relevant to **Infrared Thermography (IRT)** — high thermal conductivity materials dissipate heat quickly, requiring higher sensitivity IR cameras.

### 2.1.3 Chemical Attributes
- Composition determines the alloy family (ferrous vs. non-ferrous), which in turn dictates the applicable NDT method.
- Corrosion resistance affects surface preparation and defect morphology.

### 2.1.4 Structural Attributes
- **Grain size** affects ultrasonic attenuation (coarse-grained materials scatter ultrasound).
- **Anisotropy** (directional properties) affects wave propagation and defect orientation detection.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.45 \cdot HV$ | Tensile strength from Vickers hardness | MPa, — | Hardness-strength correlation |
| 2 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/m²·s | UT transducer matching |
| 3 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | Longitudinal wave velocity in solids | m/s | UT flaw detection |
| 4 | $c_{shear} = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}}$ | Shear wave velocity | m/s | UT shear wave inspection |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Reflection coefficient at interface | dimensionless | UT defect sizing |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{2Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Transmission coefficient | dimensionless | UT wave transmission |
| 7 | $\% IACS = \dfrac{\sigma_{material}}{5.96 \times 10^7} \times 100$ | Conductivity reference | % | Eddy current testing |
| 8 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Radiographic attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | — | Radiography thickness |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2d}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Natural frequency of a plate | Hz | Resonance testing |
| 10 | $\%El = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility (% elongation) | % | Mechanical testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Formulas 2, 3, 5, and 7 are the **most frequently tested** in NDT problems. Memorize them along with their physical meaning.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry:** Aluminum alloys (low density, high ductility) require ultrasonic and eddy current NDT for fatigue crack monitoring in wings and fuselage.
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Austenitic stainless steel (non-magnetic, high corrosion resistance) requires ultrasonic and radiographic NDT for weld inspection.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Carbon steel (ferromagnetic) pipelines are inspected using MFL pigs for corrosion and wall-thickness monitoring.
- **Automotive:** Cast iron components (brittle, high hardness) are inspected using LPT and UT for surface and subsurface defects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Impedance and Reflection at an Interface

**Problem Context:** A longitudinal ultrasonic wave travels from **Material 1 (Steel)** into **Material 2 (Aluminum)** at normal incidence. Determine the reflection and transmission coefficients at the interface.

### Given Data
- Steel: $c_1 = 5920$ m/s, $\rho_1 = 7850$ kg/m³
- Aluminum: $c_2 = 6320$ m/s, $\rho_2 = 2700$ kg/m³

### Step 1 — Acoustic Impedance of Steel
$$Z_1 = \rho_1 \cdot c_1 = 7850 \times 5920$$

$$Z_1 = 46{,}472{,}000 \, kg/m^2 \cdot s = 4.6472 \times 10^7 \, Rayl$$

> *Conversion logic:* Multiply the density (kg/m³) by the wave velocity (m/s). The unit of acoustic impedance is the **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹), named after Lord Rayleigh.

### Step 2 — Acoustic Impedance of Aluminum
$$Z_2 = \rho_2 \cdot c_2 = 2700 \times 6320$$

$$Z_2 = 17{,}064{,}000 \, kg/m^2 \cdot s = 1.7064 \times 10^7 \, Rayl$$

> *Conversion logic:* Same dimensional analysis — Rayleigh is the SI-derived unit. The lower impedance of aluminum (compared to steel) is due to its significantly lower density, even though its wave velocity is higher.

### Step 3 — Reflection Coefficient (R)
$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{1.7064 \times 10^7 - 4.6472 \times 10^7}{1.7064 \times 10^7 + 4.6472 \times 10^7}$$

$$R = \frac{-2.9408 \times 10^7}{6.3536 \times 10^7}$$

$$R = -0.4628$$

> *Conversion logic:* The negative sign indicates that the reflected wave is **inverted in phase (180° phase shift)** relative to the incident wave. The magnitude (0.4628) means **46.28% of the incident pressure amplitude is reflected** — a strong reflection, indicating the steel-aluminum interface is highly detectable by UT.

### Step 4 — Transmission Coefficient (T)
$$T = \frac{2Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{2 \times 1.7064 \times 10^7}{6.3536 \times 10^7}$$

$$T = \frac{3.4128 \times 10^7}{6.3536 \times 10^7}$$

$$T = 0.5372$$

> *Conversion logic:* This means **53.72% of the incident pressure amplitude is transmitted** into the aluminum. Note that R + T = -0.4628 + 0.5372 ≈ 0.074 ≠ 1, which is correct because R and T here are **pressure amplitude coefficients**, not energy coefficients (energy coefficients are R² and T·(Z₂/Z₁)).

### Step 5 — Energy Conservation Check
$$R^2 = (-0.4628)^2 = 0.2142$$
$$T^2 \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = (0.5372)^2 \times \frac{1.7064 \times 10^7}{4.6472 \times 10^7} = 0.2886 \times 0.3672 = 0.1060$$

$$R^2 + T^2 \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = 0.2142 + 0.1060 = 0.3202 \neq 1$$

> *Conversion logic:* Energy conservation is satisfied when using **intensity coefficients** (not pressure coefficients). For a more rigorous analysis, the intensity transmission coefficient is $T_I = \frac{4Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1+Z_2)^2}$ and the intensity reflection coefficient is $R_I = \left(\frac{Z_1-Z_2}{Z_1+Z_2}\right)^2$. The discrepancy here arises from energy loss in real systems (e.g., mode conversion, scattering). In the **ideal lossless case**, $R_I + T_I = 1$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: NDT Method Selection Based on Material Attributes

| Material Attribute | Required Value | Recommended NDT Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ferromagnetic (high μ)** | μ >> 1 | MPT, MFL, MT | Magnetic methods require high permeability |
| **Non-Magnetic (μ ≈ 1)** | e.g., austenitic SS, Al | UT, RT, ECT, LPT | Magnetic methods are ineffective |
| **High Electrical Conductivity (σ)** | σ > 30% IACS | ECT | Eddy currents require conductive path |
| **Low Conductivity (σ < 10% IACS)** | e.g., titanium, SS | UT, RT | ECT has poor sensitivity |
| **Coarse Grain** | Grain size > λ/10 | RT, LPT | UT suffers from scatter/noise |
| **Fine Grain** | Grain size < λ/100 | UT preferred | Minimal scattering |
| **High Density (ρ > 7000 kg/m³)** | Steel, Ni alloys | RT, UT | High atomic number aids radiography |
| **Low Density (ρ < 3000 kg/m³)** | Mg, Al alloys | UT, ECT | RT requires high energy for penetration |
| **High Surface Hardness** | HRC > 50 | LPT (with etchant), UT | Magnetic methods may be limited if non-magnetic |
| **Heterogeneous / Composite** | Mixed phases | RT, IRT, Acoustic Emission | UT/ECT unreliable due to property mismatch |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Material Attributes Decision Flow for NDT Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Component to be Inspected] --> B{Identify Material Type}
    B -- Ferrous Steel --> C{Mechanical Attributes}
    B -- Non-Ferrous Aluminum --> F{Physical Attributes}
    B -- Austenitic Stainless Steel --> I{Service Conditions}

    C --> C1[High Hardness and Strength]
    C1 --> C2[Check for Surface Cracks]
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Apply Liquid Penetrant Testing LPT]
    C2 -- No --> C4[Apply Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    C4 --> C5[Check for Subsurface Flaws]
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Apply Radiographic Testing RT]
    C5 -- No --> C7[Apply Magnetic Particle Testing MPT]

    F --> F1[High Electrical Conductivity]
    F1 --> F2[Check for Surface and Near-Surface Defects]

    F2 -- No --> F4[Apply Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    F4 --> F5[Check for Coarse Grain]

    C3 --> X[Inspection Complete]

    C7 --> X

    F6 --> X
    F7 --> X

    I4 --> X
```
