## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]:**
> **Differentiate between Direct and Indirect methods of NDT. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Direct Method** | **Indirect Method** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Defect is directly seen or physically contacted | Defect is inferred via property change |
| Indication | Visual (color, bubble) | Signal (wave, image, trace) |
| Skill Level | Moderate | High |
| Examples | Visual Testing, Penetrant Testing | Ultrasonic Testing, Radiographic Testing |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for two examples of each.)*

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]:**
> **List any six NDT methods and classify each as direct or indirect.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **S.No.** | **NDT Method** | **Classification** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Visual Testing (VT) | Direct |
| 2 | Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT) | Direct |
| 3 | Leak Testing (LT) | Direct |
| 4 | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Indirect |
| 5 | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | Indirect |
| 6 | Eddy Current Testing (ET) | Indirect |

*(0.5 mark per correct entry — 6 entries × 0.5 = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**

**(a)** Explain the fundamental difference between direct and indirect methods of non-destructive testing. Discuss the physical principles, advantages, and limitations of each approach with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A liquid penetrant has a surface tension of $\gamma = 0.035$ **N/m**, contact angle $\theta = 15°$, and density $\rho = 870$ **kg/m³**. Calculate the maximum height to which the penetrant will rise in a surface crack of width 0.05 mm. Take $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Direct Methods:** Definition, principle (capillary action, pressure differential, line-of-sight observation). Advantages: simple, cheap, immediate results. Limitations: surface defects only, qualitative. Examples: VT, PT, LT, Pressure Test. *(3 marks)*

- **Indirect Methods:** Definition, principle (transmit–interact–receive–interpret). Advantages: subsurface detection, quantitative, high sensitivity. Limitations: expensive, requires trained operator, calibration critical. Examples: RT, UT, MPT, ET, AET, IRT. *(3 marks)*

- **Comparison Table:** Side-by-side merits, applications, and standards. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Full Step-by-Step Solution):**

**Given Data:**
- $\gamma = 0.035$ **N/m**
- $\theta = 15°$
- $\rho = 870$ **kg/m³**
- Crack width (diameter) = 0.05 mm = $0.05 \times 10^{-3}$ **m**
- Therefore, radius $r = 0.025 \times 10^{-3}$ **m** = $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ **m**
- $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**

**Formula:**

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos(15°)$:**

$$\cos(15°) = 0.9659$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Formula:**

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.035 \times 0.9659}{870 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$2 \times 0.035 \times 0.9659 = 0.0676 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$870 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.21335 \text{ N/m³}$$

**Step 5 — Compute h:**

$$h = \frac{0.0676}{0.21335} = 0.3169 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{h \approx 0.317 \text{ m} = 317 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The penetrant rises approximately **317 mm** into the crack, confirming that PT is highly sensitive even to very fine surface cracks. *(1 mark for interpretation)*

> Mark distribution: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks computation, 1 mark final answer with units, 1 mark physical interpretation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Internal Choice]:**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the working principle of (i) Liquid Penetrant Testing and (ii) Ultrasonic Testing. Classify each as direct or indirect and justify your classification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic wave traveling in steel ($\rho_{steel} = 7850$ **kg/m³**, $c_{steel} = 5920$ **m/s**) encounters an air-filled crack ($\rho_{air} = 1.225$ **kg/m³**, $c_{air} = 343$ **m/s**). Calculate the acoustic impedances and the reflection coefficient at the steel–air interface. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **(i) Liquid Penetrant Testing — Direct Method:** Steps: pre-cleaning → penetrant application (5–30 min dwell) → excess removal → developer application → indication interpretation. Physical principle: **capillary action** governed by $h = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$. Justification: defect is **directly seen** after development → Direct. *(3.5 marks)*

- **(ii) Ultrasonic Testing — Indirect Method:** Steps: couplant application → probe placement → pulse transmission → reflection capture → A-scan interpretation. Physical principle: **acoustic impedance mismatch** at defect. Justification: defect is **inferred from signal** → Indirect. *(3.5 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Full Step-by-Step Solution):**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ **kg/m³**, $c_{steel} = 5920$ **m/s**
- $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ **kg/m³**, $c_{air} = 343$ **m/s**

**Step 1 — Acoustic Impedance of Steel:**

$$Z_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot c_{steel}$$

$$Z_{steel} = 7850 \times 5920$$

$$Z_{steel} = 46{,}472{,}000 \text{ kg/(m²·s)} = 46.472 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

**Step 2 — Acoustic Impedance of Air:**

$$Z_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot c_{air}$$

$$Z_{air} = 1.225 \times 343$$

$$Z_{air} = 420.175 \text{ kg/(m²·s)} \approx 0.00042 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

**Step 3 — Reflection Coefficient Formula:**

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

Substituting $Z_1 = Z_{steel}$ and $Z_2 = Z_{air}$:

$$R = \left(\frac{0.00042 \times 10^6 - 46.472 \times 10^6}{0.00042 \times 10^6 + 46.472 \times 10^6}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Numerator and Denominator:**

$$\text{Numerator term} = -46.47158 \times 10^6$$

$$\text{Denominator term} = 46.47242 \times 10^6$$

**Step 5 — Compute R:**

$$R = \left(\frac{-46.47158}{46.47242}\right)^2$$

$$R = (-0.999982)^2$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.999965 \text{ or } 99.9965\%}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Nearly **100% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected** at the steel–air interface. This is why UT is exceptionally sensitive to air-filled cracks, and why couplant is mandatory to transmit sound from probe to component. *(1 mark for interpretation)*

> Mark distribution: 1 mark each for $Z_{steel}$ and $Z_{air}$, 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for interpretation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points — Avoid These at All Costs:**
>
> 1. **Misclassifying Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT):** Many students wrongly classify MPT as a Direct Method because indications are *visible*. **Correct classification:** MPT is **INDIRECT** because the magnetic particles only accumulate due to *flux leakage*, which is itself inferred from a magnetization process. The defect is **not directly seen** — the magnetic field distortion is.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** KTU examiners strictly deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units in final answers. Always write **N/m, kg/(m²·s), m, mm** explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing Sensitivity with Detectability:** Sensitivity is the *smallest flaw size* detectable. Detectability is the *ability to locate* it. These are not interchangeable.
>
> 4. **Skipping the "Justification" Step:** When asked to classify a method, always add a 1–2 line justification. Examiners award marks for the *reasoning*, not just the label.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up Standards:** Writing "ASTM" for a method governed by "IS" (Indian Standard) or "ASME" loses 0.5 marks. **Memorize the standard-test-method pairs** given in Section 2.3.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1:**

- **Direct Methods** involve **physical contact or line-of-sight** with the defect. *(VT, PT, LT, Pressure Test)*.
- **Indirect Methods** rely on **inference from physical property change**. *(RT, UT, MPT, ET, AET, IRT)*.
- The **capillary rise equation** $h = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ governs penetrant sensitivity — smaller cracks draw penetrant **more strongly**.
- The **acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ governs ultrasonic reflection. Steel–air interface gives **R ≈ 100%**.
- The **POD curve** is sigmoidal, with $d_{90}$ being the engineering acceptance threshold for reliable detection.
- **Visual Testing** is the most fundamental, oldest, and most widely used NDT method worldwide.
- **Leak Testing** detects flaws only in **sealed or pressurized systems** — it cannot find a flaw that does not leak.
- **Magnetic Particle Testing** is applicable **only to ferromagnetic materials** (iron, nickel, cobalt, and their alloys).
- **Eddy Current Testing** is applicable **only to electrically conductive materials**.
- **Radiographic Testing** uses ionizing radiation — **strict safety protocols** (lead shielding, dosimetry, exclusion zones) are mandatory.
- **Standards Mapping (must memorize):** VT → ASTM E1316; PT → ASTM E1417; LT → ASTM E493; RT → ASTM E142; UT → ASTM E164; MPT → ASTM E709; ET → ASTM E566; AET → ASTM E1106; IRT → ASTM E1862.
- **KTU Board-Favorite Topics:** Capillary rise derivation, acoustic impedance calculation, POD curve analysis, and method classification with justification.
- **Key Distinction in MPT:** The defect is **not directly seen** — the magnetic particles respond to **flux leakage**, which is an indirect effect.
- **Calibration Standards:** IIW V2 Block (UT), ASTM E428 Reference Blocks (UT), MTU-3 Comparators (PT).
- **Engineering Utility Mapping:** Aerospace → UT + ET; Pressure Vessels → RT + PT; Pipelines → UT + MPT; Nuclear → UT + RT (highest sensitivity).
- **Remember the asymmetry:** Direct methods are **cheap and simple** but **surface-limited**. Indirect methods are **expensive and complex** but **subsurface-capable**. Industry uses a **combination** of both for comprehensive quality assurance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mirrors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mirrors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the domain of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, a **Mirror (Inspection Mirror)** is a specialized optical hand-tool used primarily in **Visual Testing (VT)** — one of the most fundamental NDT methods — to assist the inspector in viewing surfaces, weld joints, internal cavities, and hidden geometries of a test specimen that are **not directly accessible to the human eye**. The mirror achieves this by reflecting light from the concealed area back to the inspector at a viewing angle, thereby enabling remote optical observation without dismantling or damaging the component.

Under **ASTM E165** and **ASME Section V, Article 9**, Visual Testing is recognized as the primary NDT method, and inspection mirrors are listed as approved ancillary equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Inspection mirrors are classified as a sub-tool under *Visual Examination / Optical Aids* in Module 1 of PESFT521. They form the simplest yet most cost-effective class of optical NDT aids, sitting at Level 1 of the visual inspection hierarchy (before borescopes, endoscopes, and video-probe systems).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a dentist trying to see the back of your molars. The dentist cannot directly look behind the tooth because your cheeks and tongue block the view. So, the dentist uses a small **angled mirror on a stick** to redirect the light bouncing off the back of the tooth back to their eyes.

An NDT inspection mirror works on **exactly the same principle**. Imagine you are an aerospace quality inspector checking the **root pass of a circumferential weld inside a pressure vessel nozzle**. The weld root is tucked deep inside the pipe. You cannot stick your head in, and you cannot break the component. You simply slide a **small angled mirror on an extendable handle** into the opening, and the mirror reflects the image of the root pass back to you, just like a rear-view mirror in a car reflects the road behind your vehicle.

## 1.3 Physical Principle — Law of Reflection

The mirror functions on the **Law of Reflection**, which states:

> The angle of incidence (measured from the normal) equals the angle of reflection, and both rays lie in the same plane.

$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$

Where:
- $\theta_i$ = Angle of incidence (degrees or radians)
- $\theta_r$ = Angle of reflection (degrees or radians)

Both angles are measured from the **normal** (a line drawn perpendicular to the mirror surface at the point of incidence), **NOT** from the mirror surface itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in NDT:** Because the law of reflection governs the geometry of the image you see through the inspection mirror, the apparent position of a defect (crack, porosity, undercut) is laterally inverted (left-right flipped) when viewed in a flat mirror. Trained NDT inspectors must mentally compensate for this inversion to correctly identify and size defects.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics of an NDT Inspection Mirror

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mirror Diameter** | **10 mm to 100 mm** | Determines the field of view |
| **Handle Length** | **150 mm to 1500 mm** (extendable to several meters) | Determines access depth |
| **Mirror Material** | **First-surface glass, polished stainless steel, acrylic** | Determines reflectance and durability |
| **Typical Reflectance (R)** | **> 85%** for first-surface glass | Determines image brightness |
| **Working Temperature** | **-20 °C to +200 °C** (special versions up to 600 °C) | Determines service environment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First-surface mirrors** are the gold standard in NDT because the reflective coating (usually aluminum or silver) is deposited on the **front** of the glass, eliminating the double-image (ghost image) caused by light bouncing off both front and back surfaces of a regular household mirror.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Mirrors Used in NDT

NDT inspection mirrors are classified by their geometric shape and by the configuration of the handle/articulating arm. Each type is engineered for a specific inspection geometry.

### A. Classification by Geometry

1. **Flat (Plane) Mirrors**
   - Produce a **virtual, erect, laterally inverted** image of the same size as the object.
   - Used for general-purpose visual inspection of flat or mildly curved surfaces.
   - *Example:* Checking the root side of a fillet weld inside a hollow structural section.

2. **Concave Mirrors**
   - Produce a **virtual, erect, magnified** image when the object is placed **between the pole and the focal point** ($u < f$).
   - Produce a **real, inverted, diminished** image when the object is placed **beyond the focal point** ($u > f$).
   - Used in NDT for **magnification of small defects** like hairline cracks in weld toes.
   - *Caution:* The focal point of a concave mirror concentrates light and heat; NDT inspectors must not leave them focused on a surface for extended periods as they may ignite coatings or burn skin.

3. **Convex Mirrors**
   - Produce a **virtual, erect, diminished** image regardless of object position.
   - Provide a **wider field of view** but at the cost of image size.
   - Used in NDT to provide an **overview** of a large internal area (e.g., the entire interior of a tank).

### B. Classification by Articulation

| Type | Description | NDT Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid Fixed-Angle Mirror** | Mirror is locked at 45° or 90° on a straight handle | Boiler tube inspection, weld root checks |
| **Swivel / Articulating Mirror** | Mirror head rotates 360° on a universal joint | Complex internal geometries |
| **Telescopic / Extendable Mirror** | Handle telescopes from 150 mm to 1500+ mm | Deep cavity inspection (tanks, pressure vessels) |
| **Illuminated Mirror** | Mirror head has an integral LED light source | Low-light or fully enclosed cavities |
| **Periscope-Style Mirror** | Series of mirrors inside a tube to relay image over long distances | Furnace tube inspection, aircraft wing internals |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The mirror equation is the primary analytical tool for concave and convex mirror problems that may appear in KTU examinations.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Mirror Equation** | $$\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{2}{R}$$ | Relates object distance $u$, image distance $v$, focal length $f$, and radius of curvature $R$ |
| **Magnification** | $$m = -\dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o}$$ | Ratio of image height to object height; sign convention: negative = real & inverted |
| **Focal Length – Radius Relation** | $$f = \dfrac{R}{2}$$ | Valid for spherical mirrors |
| **Law of Reflection** | $$\theta_i = \theta_r$$ | Angle of incidence equals angle of reflection (from normal) |
| **Reflectance (Fresnel for normal incidence)** | $$R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$ | For normal incidence on a single interface; $n_1$, $n_2$ are refractive indices |
| **Refractive Index of Crown Glass** | $n \approx 1.52$ | Standard optical glass used in inspection mirrors |
| **Refractive Index of Polished Stainless Steel (effective)** | $n \approx 2.5 - 3.0$ (complex) | Used for metal mirrors in high-temperature NDT |

### 2.2.1 Sign Convention (Cartesian — Used at KTU)

Following the **Cartesian Sign Convention** (as adopted by KTU physics/optics modules):

| Quantity | Positive (+) | Negative (-) |
|---|---|---|
| Distance | Measured in direction of incident light | Measured against direction of incident light |
| Object distance $u$ | Real object: always negative | — |
| Image distance $v$ | Real image: positive; Virtual image: negative | — |
| Focal length $f$ | Concave mirror: negative; Convex mirror: positive | — |
| Height $h$ | Erect image: positive; Inverted image: negative | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students lose marks by mixing up the New Cartesian vs. Real-is-Positive sign conventions. KTU examination problems in physics-derived modules (like optics in NDT) **standardize on the New Cartesian Convention** (Real object $u$ = negative, Concave $f$ = negative, Virtual image $v$ = negative).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Specific Application |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical** | Inspecting the inside of pressure vessel nozzles, heat-exchanger tube-sheets |
| **Aerospace** | Visual examination of wing-spar internal cavities, engine bores, landing gear bores |
| **Power Generation** | Boiler tube inner surface examination, turbine blade root inspection |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull internal cavity inspection through access ports, propeller shaft bore examination |
| **Civil / Structural** | Bridge box-girder internal weld inspection, post-tensioned duct inspection |
| **Automotive** | Engine cylinder bores, gearbox internal cavity checks during maintenance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mirror Equation

This derivation is **explicitly required** for KTU examinations when a derivation-type question appears. We derive the mirror equation from first principles using similar triangles and the geometry of a concave spherical mirror.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Geometry**

Consider a concave spherical mirror with:
- Pole $P$ at the vertex
- Centre of curvature $C$ at distance $R$ from $P$
- Focal point $F$ at distance $f$ from $P$, where $f = R / 2$
- A small object $AB$ placed perpendicular to the principal axis, with $A$ on the axis at distance $u$ from $P$
- A real image $A'B'$ formed at distance $v$ from $P$

**Step 2: Apply the Law of Reflection**

A ray from $B$ travelling parallel to the principal axis reflects through $F$. A ray from $B$ passing through $C$ retraces its path. These two reflected rays intersect at $B'$, the tip of the image.

**Step 3: Identify Similar Triangles**

In $\triangle ABP$ and $\triangle A'B'P$:

- $\angle APB = \angle A'PB'$ (common angle at the pole)
- $\angle APB = 90°$ (object and image are perpendicular to the principal axis)
- $\angle A'PB' = 90°$ (by construction)

Therefore, $\triangle ABP \sim \triangle A'B'P$ (AA similarity criterion).

This gives us Equation (1):

$$\frac{A'B'}{AB} = \frac{PA'}{PA} = \frac{v}{u}$$

*(In magnitude; signs handled by sign convention later.)*

**Step 4: Use the Second Pair of Similar Triangles**

In $\triangle PFB$ and $\triangle PFB'$:

- $PF$ is common
- $\angle PFB = \angle PFB' = 90°$
- $\angle FBP = \angle FB'P$ (alternate angles, since the parallel ray reflects through $F$)

Therefore, $\triangle PFB \sim \triangle PFB'$.

This gives us Equation (2):

$$\frac{PB'}{PB} = \frac{PF}{PF - FB'}$$

Substituting known distances (with magnitudes):

$$\frac{B'F}{BF} = \frac{f}{u - f}$$

**Step 5: Equate the Two Image Position Relations**

Since the image tip $B'$ lies on the ray from $B$ through $F$, the two expressions for the image position must be consistent. Equating the ratios:

$$\frac{v}{u} = \frac{f}{u - f}$$

**Step 6: Cross-Multiply and Simplify**

$$v(u - f) = u f$$

$$vu - vf = uf$$

Dividing throughout by $uvf$:

$$\frac{vu}{uvf} - \frac{vf}{uvf} = \frac{uf}{uvf}$$

This reduces to:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v}$$

Rearranging into the standard KTU form:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}}$$

**Step 7: Relate Focal Length to Radius of Curvature**

By definition, the focal point $F$ is the midpoint of the line segment $PC$ (the radius of curvature $R$). Therefore:

$$f = \frac{R}{2}$$

This yields the alternative form of the mirror equation:

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{2}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sign convention (New Cartesian) is applied to the final numbers during numerical problem-solving. The magnitudes derived above are then assigned signs based on whether each quantity is real/virtual and erect/inverted.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** An NDT inspector uses a concave inspection mirror with radius of curvature $R = 40 \text{ cm}$ to examine a small suspected crack on a turbine blade. The crack is placed at a distance of $15 \text{ cm}$ from the mirror. Find: (a) the focal length, (b) the position of the image, and (c) the magnification.

### Given Information
- Radius of curvature: $R = 40 \text{ cm}$
- Object distance (real object, so $u = -15 \text{ cm}$ by New Cartesian Convention)
- Mirror type: Concave

### Solution

**Part (a): Focal Length**

Using $f = R / 2$:

$$f = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{40 \text{ cm}}{2} = +20 \text{ cm}$$

*(Note: Magnitude is 20 cm; for a concave mirror in the New Cartesian Convention, $f$ is assigned the sign $-$ in the final sign-correct form. We will use $-20 \text{ cm}$ in the equation below.)*

**Part (b): Image Position**

Applying the mirror equation with the New Cartesian signs ($u = -15 \text{ cm}$, $f = -20 \text{ cm}$):

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{-15} = \frac{1}{-20}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{-20} - \frac{1}{-15}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = -\frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{15}$$

Finding a common denominator (60):

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{-3}{60} + \frac{4}{60} = \frac{1}{60}$$

$$v = +60 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation:** A **positive** value of $v$ indicates a **real image**, formed on the same side as the object, **60 cm in front of the mirror**. This is physically correct because the object (15 cm) is placed between the focal point (20 cm) and the pole, but wait — let us recheck: the object is at 15 cm, which is *less than* the focal length of 20 cm, so the image should be **virtual**...

### Correction of Sign Application

Let us reapply the convention correctly. In the **New Cartesian Convention** for a concave mirror:
- $u$ is always negative for a real object → $u = -15 \text{ cm}$ ✓
- $f$ is negative for a concave mirror (the focus is on the same side as the reflecting surface, i.e., in front of the mirror, which is the "negative" side of the Cartesian axis) → $f = -20 \text{ cm}$ ✓

Re-solving:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{-20} - \frac{1}{-15}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = -\frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{15}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{-3 + 4}{60} = \frac{1}{60}$$

$$v = +60 \text{ cm}$$

A positive $v$ in the New Cartesian Convention means the image is formed **behind the mirror** (on the side opposite the object), which is a **virtual image**. This is correct: when the object is placed *between the pole and the focal point* of a concave mirror, the image is **virtual, erect, and magnified**, formed behind the mirror.

**Part (c): Magnification**

$$m = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{(+60)}{(-15)} = +4$$

**Interpretation:** A **positive magnification of +4** means the image is **erect** and **4 times magnified**. This is exactly why a concave mirror is used in NDT to magnify small defects like hairline cracks at the weld toe.

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Focal length $f$ | $20 \text{ cm}$ (magnitude) | — |
| Image distance $v$ | $60 \text{ cm}$ behind the mirror | Virtual image |
| Magnification $m$ | $+4$ | Erect, 4× magnified |

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Plane vs. Concave vs. Convex Mirror in NDT

| Property | Plane Mirror | Concave Mirror | Convex Mirror |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image type (object in front)** | Virtual, erect | Virtual & magnified (if $u < f$); Real & inverted (if $u > f$) | Always virtual, erect, diminished |
| **Magnification** | $+1$ | Variable | Between $0$ and $+1$ |
| **Field of view** | Same as mirror size | Smaller (focused) | Larger (panoramic) |
| **Best NDT use** | Direct reflection of hidden surfaces | Magnification of fine defects | Overview of large internal cavities |
| **Risk** | Lateral inversion confuses new inspectors | Heat concentration at focal point | Distorted appearance of defect size |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Q: Define a "first-surface mirror" as used in NDT inspection. Why is it preferred over a regular household mirror for visual testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **first-surface mirror** is an optical mirror in which the reflective metallic coating (typically aluminum or silver) is deposited on the **front** of the glass substrate, rather than on the back as in household mirrors. **(1 mark)**

In a regular (back-surface) mirror, light first passes through the glass, reflects off the back coating, and passes through the glass again. This produces a faint **secondary "ghost image"** alongside the primary image, due to partial reflection from the front glass surface. **(1 mark)**

In NDT, defect sizing demands **sharp, single, high-contrast images**. Any ghost image can be misinterpreted as a separate defect, leading to false positives. First-surface mirrors eliminate this artifact, providing a single, high-resolution reflection with reflectance **> 85%**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
**Q: State the law of reflection. Why is it important in the context of inspection mirror usage in NDT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **law of reflection** states that the **angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection**, with both rays and the normal lying in the **same plane**. **(1 mark)**

$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$

**Importance in NDT (2 marks):**
1. The mirror head is **angled (typically 45°)** so that light from a hidden surface reflects toward the inspector's eye following this law. The angle choice directly determines whether the inspector can see the target.
2. Because the image is **laterally inverted** (left-right flipped) in a flat mirror, the inspector must mentally rotate the image to correctly identify and orient defects (e.g., whether a crack runs parallel or transverse to the weld axis). A misinterpretation due to inversion is a common cause of incorrect defect classification in NDT reports.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of mirrors used in NDT inspection, classifying them by geometry and by articulation. Give two examples of industrial applications for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concave inspection mirror has a radius of curvature of **60 cm**. An NDT inspector places a small surface defect at a distance of **20 cm** from the mirror to examine it under magnification. Using the mirror equation and the New Cartesian sign convention, calculate:
   - (i) the focal length of the mirror,
   - (ii) the position of the image formed,
   - (iii) the magnification of the image, and
   - (iv) state with justification whether the image is real or virtual, and erect or inverted. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Types of Mirrors in NDT (7 marks)

**Classification by Geometry (3 marks):**

1. **Plane (Flat) Mirror:**
   - Produces a virtual, erect image of the same size as the object.
   - *NDT Application 1:* Checking the root side of circumferential welds inside pipe spools.
   - *NDT Application 2:* Visual examination of interior surfaces of storage tanks through manholes.

2. **Concave Mirror:**
   - Can produce a magnified virtual image (object between pole and focal point) or a real inverted image (object beyond focal point).
   - *NDT Application 1:* Magnification of hairline cracks at the weld toe of aerospace components.
   - *NDT Application 2:* Focused light concentration for illuminating dimly lit internal cavities.

3. **Convex Mirror:**
   - Produces a diminished virtual, erect image with a wide field of view.
   - *NDT Application 1:* Panoramic overview of the entire internal surface of a large pressure vessel.
   - *NDT Application 2:* Safety inspection behind a machine where the inspector cannot directly access.

**Classification by Articulation (4 marks):**

| Type | Description | Two Industrial Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Angle Rigid Mirror** | Mirror locked at a fixed angle (e.g., 45°) on a non-adjustable handle | Boiler tube outer surface check, gear-tooth inspection |
| **Swivel / Articulating Mirror** | Mirror head rotates via a thumb-lock joint, allowing 360° orientation | Complex weld geometry in pressure vessel nozzles, aircraft engine bores |
| **Telescopic Extendable Mirror** | Multi-section handle extends from ~300 mm to 1500+ mm | Deep-tank interior inspection, chimney/flue internal examination |
| **Illuminated Mirror** | Integrated LED ring around the mirror | Underground pipeline interior checks, dark boiler header inspection |
| **Periscope-Style Mirror** | Multiple mirrors inside a rigid tube for long-distance image relay | Furnace tube inspection, nuclear containment vessel internal checks |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Radius of curvature, $R = 60 \text{ cm}$
- Object distance, $u = -20 \text{ cm}$ (real object, New Cartesian sign convention)
- Mirror type: Concave

**Solution:**

**(i) Focal Length (1 mark):**

Using the relation $f = R / 2$:

$$f = \frac{60 \text{ cm}}{2} = 30 \text{ cm}$$

In the New Cartesian sign convention, $f$ for a concave mirror is **negative**:

$$f = -30 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Image Position (2 marks):**

Using the mirror equation:

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

Substituting $u = -20$ cm and $f = -30$ cm:

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{-20} = \frac{1}{-30}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{-30} + \frac{1}{20}$$

Finding a common denominator (60):

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{-2}{60} + \frac{3}{60} = \frac{1}{60}$$

$$v = +60 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Magnification (2 marks):**

$$m = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{(+60)}{(-20)} = +3$$

**(iv) Nature of the Image (2 marks):**

- The image distance $v = +60 \text{ cm}$ is **positive** in the New Cartesian Convention, meaning the image is formed **behind the mirror** (on the side of the reflecting surface where light does not actually converge). This indicates a **VIRTUAL image**. **(1 mark)**
- The magnification $m = +3$ is **positive**, meaning the image is **ERECT** and **3 times magnified**. **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** The image is **virtual, erect, and magnified 3 times**, formed 60 cm behind the concave mirror. This is precisely the configuration used by NDT inspectors to magnify small surface defects.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, derive the **mirror equation** relating object distance ($u$), image distance ($v$), and focal length ($f$) for a concave spherical mirror. Clearly state the sign convention used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NDT inspector uses a **convex mirror** with a focal length of **15 cm** as part of a panoramic inspection tool to view the entire internal surface of a large pressure vessel. The vessel wall surface is located **10 cm from the mirror**. Calculate:
   - (i) the position of the virtual image,
   - (ii) the magnification, and
   - (iii) comment on why convex mirrors are preferred for such panoramic inspection despite image distortion. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Mirror Equation (7 marks)

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):** A concave spherical mirror with pole $P$, centre of curvature $C$, focal point $F$ (midpoint of $PC$), object $AB$ on the principal axis, and image $A'B'$. Two rays from $B$: one parallel to the axis (reflects through $F$), and one through $C$ (reflects back on itself). **(2 marks for diagram)**

**Sign Convention Stated (1 mark):** New Cartesian Sign Convention — all distances measured from the pole $P$; distances in the direction of incident light are positive, against the incident light are negative. The focal length of a concave mirror is negative.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

From similar triangles $\triangle ABP \sim \triangle A'B'P$ (AA criterion, right angles at $A$ and $A'$ and common angle at $P$):

$$\frac{A'B'}{AB} = \frac{PA'}{PA}$$

With magnitudes:

$$\frac{v}{u} = \frac{h_i}{h_o} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

From similar triangles formed by the focal point geometry:

$$\frac{v - f}{f} = \frac{v}{u} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$v(u - f) = uf$$

$$vu - vf = uf$$

Dividing through by $uvf$:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v}$$

Rearranging to the standard form:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}}$$

This is the **mirror equation**. **(4 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Convex Mirror (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Focal length of convex mirror, $f = +15 \text{ cm}$ (positive for convex in New Cartesian)
- Object distance, $u = -10 \text{ cm}$ (real object)

**Solution:**

**(i) Image Position (2 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{-10} = \frac{1}{+15}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{15} + \frac{1}{10}$$

Common denominator = 30:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{2}{30} + \frac{3}{30} = \frac{5}{30} = \frac{1}{6}$$

$$v = +6 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation:** A positive $v$ for a convex mirror in the New Cartesian Convention means a **virtual image** formed 6 cm behind the mirror (i.e., on the side opposite the object). This is consistent with the known behavior of convex mirrors. **(included in 2 marks)**

**(ii) Magnification (2 marks):**

$$m = -\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{(+6)}{(-10)} = +0.6$$

**Interpretation:** A positive magnification of 0.6 means the image is **erect** and **reduced to 60% of the original size** of the object. **(included in 2 marks)**

**(iii) Why Convex Mirrors for Panoramic Inspection (3 marks):**

Convex mirrors are preferred for panoramic NDT inspection of large internal cavities (like pressure vessels) for the following reasons:

1. **Wide Field of View:** A convex mirror diverges reflected light, capturing a much larger solid angle of the scene compared to a plane mirror of the same diameter. This allows the inspector to see a broad area of the vessel wall in a single glance. **(1 mark)**
2. **Always Virtual and Erect:** Regardless of object position, a convex mirror produces a virtual, erect image. This makes inspection ergonomic and intuitive for the inspector. **(1 mark)**
3. **Image Distortion Tolerance:** While the image is diminished, in NDT the goal of panoramic inspection is **detection** (identifying that a defect exists) rather than **sizing** (measuring the exact length of the defect). Once a defect is detected in the panoramic view, the inspector switches to a closer, higher-magnification tool (concave mirror or borescope) to size it. The minor distortion is an acceptable trade-off for the broader overview. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Loss: up to 3 marks):** The most common error in mirror problems is the incorrect application of the sign convention. Students frequently write $f = +30$ cm for a concave mirror and arrive at a physically wrong answer. **Always** use the New Cartesian Convention: $f$ is **negative** for concave mirrors, **positive** for convex mirrors, and the object distance $u$ is **always negative** for a real object.
> 2. **Confusing the Formula (Loss: 2 marks):** Do NOT confuse the mirror equation $\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$ with the lens equation $\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}$. This is a frequent error in NDT/optics papers.
> 3. **Image Nature Misinterpretation (Loss: 1-2 marks):** In the New Cartesian Convention, a **positive** $v$ for a concave mirror indicates a **virtual** image (behind the mirror). For a convex mirror, a positive $v$ also indicates a virtual image. Memorize this carefully.
> 4. **In NDT context questions, do not ignore the engineering significance.** When a question asks "why is this preferred in NDT", simply giving the physics answer (e.g., "magnification is +3") is not enough. You must explain the *practical* implication (e.g., "this 3× magnification allows the inspector to see hairline cracks that would otherwise be missed").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Module 1 — Mirrors in NDT**

- [x] **Definition:** An NDT inspection mirror is an optical aid used in **Visual Testing (VT)** to view hidden or inaccessible surfaces without damaging the component.
- [x] **Primary Law:** Operation is based on the **Law of Reflection**: $\theta_i = \theta_r$ (measured from the **normal**, not the surface).
- [x] **First-Surface Mirrors:** Always preferred in NDT to avoid the **ghost image** artifact of regular back-coated mirrors.
- [x] **Three Geometric Types:**
  - **Plane:** Virtual, erect, same-size image; standard inspection.
  - **Concave:** Virtual & magnified if $u < f$; real & inverted if $u > f$; used for defect magnification.
  - **Convex:** Always virtual, erect, diminished; wide field of view; used for panoramic overview.
- [x] **Five Articulation Types:** Fixed-angle rigid, swivel/articulating, telescopic extendable, illuminated (LED), and periscope-style.
- [x] **Mirror Equation:** $\dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{2}{R}$
- [x] **Magnification Formula:** $m = -\dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o}$
- [x] **Sign Convention (New Cartesian):** Real object $u$ = **negative**; Concave $f$ = **negative**; Convex $f$ = **positive**; Real image $v$ = **positive**; Virtual image $v$ = **negative** (for concave) or **positive** (for convex, behind the mirror).
- [x] **Lateral Inversion:** A flat mirror flips the image left-right; inspectors must mentally correct for this when identifying defect orientation.
- [x] **Safety Note:** Concave mirrors focus light/heat at the focal point — never leave them focused on a flammable surface.
- [x] **NDT Hierarchy of Visual Aids:** Naked Eye → **Inspection Mirror** → Borescope → Endoscope → Videoscope → High-resolution camera systems.
- [x] **Standards:** Inspection mirrors conform to general VT requirements of **ASTM E165**, **ASME Section V Article 9**, and **AWS QC1** for visual examination of welds.
- [x] **Common Examiner Pitfalls to Avoid:** (1) Sign convention errors, (2) Confusing mirror and lens equations, (3) Forgetting to state image nature (real/virtual/erect/inverted), (4) Ignoring engineering context in "why" questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnifier (Definition — KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **magnifier** is a single-lens or multi-lens optical instrument used in **Visual Testing (VT)** — the most fundamental branch of Non-Destructive Testing — to enlarge the apparent size of a surface or feature so that fine discontinuities (cracks, pits, corrosion, weld undercut, porosity) become visually resolvable to the human eye. The defining performance metric is **Angular Magnification (M)**, which compares the angle subtended by an object when viewed through the magnifier to the angle subtended when viewed by the naked eye at the **Least Distance of Distinct Vision (D = 25 cm)**.

A magnifier converts a small, near object into a larger, **virtual, erect, and magnified image** that the eye can comfortably focus on. In NDT, the device is held close to the specimen surface, while the inspector's eye is positioned at the **eye-relief (eye-lens distance)** on the opposite side.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading-Glass Intuition"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are trying to read the microscopic serial number etched on a metal coupon. Without help, you must bring it as close as 10–15 cm to your eye, but your eye cannot focus — the image blurs. Now, place a simple convex lens between your eye and the coupon. Suddenly, the same number looks 5× or 10× larger, and the lens allows your eye to relax as if the object were comfortably 25 cm away. **That lens is acting exactly as a magnifier does during a VT inspection of a weld bead, a casting surface, or a turbine blade leading edge.**

The closer the focal length of the lens, the higher the magnification — but the smaller the **field of view** and **working distance**. This trade-off is at the heart of magnifier design.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants Used in NDT Magnifier Calculations:**
> - **Least Distance of Distinct Vision, D = 25 cm = 0.25 m** (also called the *near point* of a normal human eye)
> - **Wavelength of visible light, λ ≈ 550 nm = 5.5 × 10⁻⁷ m** (used in resolving power)
> - **Refractive index of air, n ≈ 1.00**
> - **Standard inspection magnifications used in NDT: 2×, 5×, 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×**
> - **Typical working distance range: 2 cm – 30 cm**

### 1.4 Why Magnifiers Are Critical in NDT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Visual Testing accounts for roughly **80% of all NDT inspections** in industry, and magnifiers are its primary amplification tool. Standards such as **ASTM E1417**, **ASME Section V Article 9**, and **ISO 9712** frequently mandate specific magnifications (e.g., **10× for liquid penetrant indication evaluation**) for acceptance/rejection decisions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A magnifier is essentially a **short-focal-length convex (converging) lens** placed very close to the object. Its working principle unfolds in the following structured sequence:

1. **Object Placement:** The specimen surface (object) is placed at a distance **u** from the lens such that **u ≤ f** (object lies within or at the focal length).
2. **Image Formation:** The lens forms a **virtual, erect, and magnified image** on the **same side as the object**.
3. **Eye Accommodation:** The eye, placed on the other side of the lens, sees the enlarged virtual image. The brain interprets it as if the object were at the **near point (D = 25 cm)** or at **infinity**, depending on adjustment.
4. **Angular Gain:** The lens increases the **viewing angle** θ′ subtended at the eye, compared to the angle θ subtended without the lens. This angular gain is the **magnification M**.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Adjustment Mode

> [!NOTE]
> **Two adjustment modes govern magnifier formulae:**

- **Normal Adjustment (Image at Infinity):** The object is placed at the focal point. The emerging light rays are parallel, so the eye views the image with **zero accommodation** (relaxed eye). This is the most comfortable mode for prolonged inspection.
- **Final Image at the Near Point (D = 25 cm):** The object is placed between f and the lens so the virtual image forms exactly at D. The eye must accommodate fully, but the magnification is higher.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table lists every formula you must memorize for KTU exams on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Adjustment / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$ | M = Magnification, D = 25 cm, f = focal length | Image at infinity (normal adjustment) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f}$ | Same as above, with eye accommodated at near point | Final image at D = 25 cm | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Lens equation (Cartesian sign convention) | General thin-lens relation | cm or m |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | P = Power of lens, f = focal length in metres | Always | Dioptres (D) |
| 5 | $\text{Resolving Power (RP)} = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}}$ | λ = wavelength, NA = numerical aperture | Microscope-grade magnifier | metres |
| 6 | $\text{NA} = n \sin \theta$ | n = refractive index, θ = half-angle of cone of light | Microscope objective | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{M}_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{objective}} \times M_{\text{eyepiece}}$ | Compound microscope total magnification | Compound systems | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Limit of Resolution} = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | Rayleigh criterion limit | Compound microscope | metres |
| 9 | $\text{Working Distance (WD)} = \dfrac{f_{\text{obj}}}{M_{\text{obj}}}$ | WD decreases as magnification rises | Practical NDT use | mm or cm |
| 10 | $\text{Field of View (FOV)} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}_{\text{eyepiece}}}{M_{\text{objective}}}$ | Smaller at higher magnification | Compound microscope | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety:** In KTU exam scripts, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value (e.g., $M = \vert v \vert / \vert u \vert$) to prevent table-parsing errors in digital answer sheets.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** 10× – 30× magnifiers inspect turbine blade **fatigue cracks** and **thermal barrier coating spallation**.
- **Nuclear:** 5× illuminated magnifiers examine fuel-rod cladding for **stress-corrosion cracking**.
- **Welding Inspection:** 10× pocket magnifiers evaluate **undercut, overlap, and crater cracks** per AWS D1.1.
- **Forensic / Failure Analysis:** Stereo microscopes (10× – 50×) reveal **beach marks** and **striations** in fractography.
- **Electronics:** 20× – 100× stereo zoom magnifiers detect **solder joint voids** and **PCB trace defects**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnification in Normal Adjustment (Image at Infinity)

**Given:**
- Object of height **h** placed at the focal point **F** of a convex lens.
- The eye views parallel rays → image at infinity.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the unaided view:**
Without the magnifier, the object is placed at the near point **D = 25 cm**. The angle subtended at the eye is:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the aided view:**
With the magnifier, the object is placed at the focal point, so the angle subtended is:

$$
\theta' = \dfrac{h}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of angular magnification:**

$$
M = \dfrac{\theta'}{\theta} = \dfrac{h \,/\, f}{h \,/\, D} = \dfrac{D}{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute D = 25 cm:**

$$
\boxed{M = \dfrac{25}{f\ (\text{in cm})}}
$$

> **Textual explanation:** The magnification is simply the ratio of the standard near-point distance to the focal length. A lens of focal length **5 cm** gives **M = 5×**; a lens of focal length **2.5 cm** gives **M = 10×**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Magnification with Final Image at the Near Point

**Given:** Object placed between **f** and the lens so the virtual image forms at **D = 25 cm** on the same side as the object.

**Step 1 — Apply sign convention (Cartesian):**
- $u = -x$ (object distance, negative as object is on the incoming side)
- $v = -D = -25$ cm (virtual image on same side as object)
- Lens power = $1/f$

**Step 2 — Lens equation:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{-25} - \dfrac{1}{-x} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

$$
-\dfrac{1}{25} + \dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 1/x (the object position):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{x} = \dfrac{1}{f} + \dfrac{1}{25}
$$

**Step 5 — Linear magnification m:**

$$
m = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{-25}{-x} = \dfrac{25}{x}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute x from Step 4:**

$$
m = 25 \left( \dfrac{1}{f} + \dfrac{1}{25} \right) = \dfrac{25}{f} + 1
$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f} = 1 + \dfrac{25}{f\ (\text{in cm})}}
$$

> **Textual explanation:** This mode yields slightly higher magnification than the infinity mode because the eye accommodates to its maximum, providing an extra "1×" bonus on top of the D/f ratio.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolving Power of a Magnifier/Microscope Objective

**Given:** Two point sources separated by distance **d**, illuminated by light of wavelength **λ**, observed through a lens of numerical aperture **NA = n sin θ**.

**Step 1 — Rayleigh criterion:**
Two points are just resolvable when the central maximum of one Airy disc falls on the first minimum of the other:

$$
d = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}}
$$

**Step 2 — Resolving power definition (inverse of d):**

$$
\text{RP} = \dfrac{1}{d} = \dfrac{2 \, \text{NA}}{1.22 \, \lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard form commonly used in NDT microscopy:**

$$
\boxed{d_{\min} = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\text{NA}}}
$$

> **Textual explanation:** For green light (λ ≈ 550 nm) and NA = 0.25 (a typical 10× magnifier), $d_{\min} = 0.61 \times 550\,\text{nm} / 0.25 = 1342$ nm ≈ **1.34 µm**. This is the smallest defect width that can be *resolved* (not merely magnified).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — KTU Pattern:**
> *A NDT inspector uses a magnifying lens of focal length 4 cm to examine a weld toe. Calculate (a) the magnification when the image is at infinity, and (b) the magnification when the image forms at the near point (D = 25 cm).*

**Solution (a):**

$$
M_{\infty} = \dfrac{D}{f} = \dfrac{25}{4} = 6.25
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
M_{D} = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f} = 1 + \dfrac{25}{4} = 1 + 6.25 = 7.25
$$

**Interpretation:** The near-point mode offers ~16% higher magnification but requires the inspector to fully accommodate the eye, causing fatigue during long shifts.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Magnifiers Used in NDT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MAGNIFIERS IN NDT"] --> B["Simple Magnifier Single Convex Lens"]
    A --> C["Compound Microscope"]
    A --> D["Stereo Microscope"]
    A --> E["Specialised NDT Magnifier"]

    B --> B1["Pocket Magnifier 2X to 5X"]
    B --> B2["Illuminated Hand Magnifier 3X to 10X"]
    B --> B3["Bench Stand Magnifier 5X to 20X"]
    B --> B4["Linen Tester 8X Fixed"]

    C --> C1["Monocular Compound 40X to 1000X"]
    C --> C2["Metallurgical Microscope 50X to 500X"]
    C --> C3["Inverted Metallurgical 100X to 1000X"]

    D --> D1["Greenough Stereo 10X to 40X"]
    D --> D2["Common Main Objective CMO 10X to 60X"]
    D --> D3["Zoom Stereo 7X to 45X"]

    E --> E1["Video Inspection Scope 10X to 200X"]
    E --> E2["Borescope with Magnifier 4X to 50X"]
    E --> E3["Digital USB Microscope 10X to 200X"]
    E --> E4["Measuring Magnifier with Graticule 7X to 10X"]

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Optical Train of a Compound Microscope Magnifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ["Object Specimen Surface"] --> OBJCOND["Objective Lens f1 short"]
    OBJCOND --> REAL["Real Inverted Magnified Image at intermediate focal plane"]
    REAL --> EYE["Eyepiece Lens f2 acting as magnifier"]
    EYE --> EYEIMG["Final Virtual Image at D or infinity"]
    EYEIMG --> INSP["NDT Inspector Eye"]

    L1["Light Source Illuminator"] -.-> OBJ

    style OBJ fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000,stroke:#7f6000
    style OBJCOND fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style REAL fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style EYE fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style EYEIMG fill:#a9d08e,color:#000000
    style INSP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#ffd966,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Trade-off Triangle in Magnifier Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HQ["High Magnification M increases"] --> WD["Working Distance DECREASES"]
    HQ --> FOV["Field of View DECREASES"]
    HQ --> DOF["Depth of Focus DECREASES"]
    HQ --> RP["Resolving Power INCREASES up to NA limit"]

    WD --> NOTE1["Inspector must hold lens closer to specimen, increasing risk of contact with rough surfaces"]
    FOV --> NOTE2["Smaller area visible at one time, more scanning required"]
    DOF --> NOTE3["Only thin layer is in focus, harder to inspect curved surfaces"]

    style HQ fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style WD fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style FOV fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style DOF fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style RP fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style NOTE1 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    style NOTE2 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    style NOTE3 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1.** Define a *magnifier* as used in Non-Destructive Testing. State the standard value of the *Least Distance of Distinct Vision* used in its design.

**Model Answer:**
A magnifier is a single or multi-lens optical instrument used in Visual Testing (VT) to enlarge the apparent size of a surface feature or defect so it can be visually resolved by the human eye. It works on the principle of **angular magnification**, producing a **virtual, erect, and enlarged image**. The standard value of the Least Distance of Distinct Vision adopted in magnifier design is **D = 25 cm**.

*(3 marks: definition 2, standard value 1)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between the *normal adjustment* and the *near-point adjustment* of a simple magnifier.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Normal Adjustment | Near-Point Adjustment |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Object position | At focal point (u = f) | Between f and lens (u < f) |
| Image position | At infinity | At D = 25 cm |
| Eye accommodation | Relaxed (zero) | Fully accommodated |
| Magnification formula | $M = D / f$ | $M = 1 + D / f$ |
| Inspector comfort | Best for prolonged use | Eye fatigue possible |
| Use case | Field inspection | Detailed lab examination |

*(3 marks: distinction with formula 2, practical implication 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert focal length units** (mm ↔ cm ↔ m). A focal length of **50 mm** must be written as **5 cm** before applying $M = 25/f$.
> - Many candidates **omit the "1 +"** in the near-point formula and lose 2 marks.
> - Drawing ray diagrams **without arrows** on incident and emergent rays costs 1 mark.
> - Forgetting to mention **D = 25 cm** when stating the formula is a recurring ½-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat ray diagram, explain the principle of a simple magnifier used in NDT. Derive the expression for angular magnification when the final image is formed at the **least distance of distinct vision**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A NDT inspector uses a pocket magnifier of focal length **6.25 cm** to inspect a surface crack. Calculate (i) the magnification when the image is at infinity, and (ii) the magnification when the image is at the near point. Comment on which mode is more suitable for a 4-hour shift. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle & Derivation:**

*Ray diagram description (2 marks):*
A convex lens of short focal length **f** is placed between the eye and a small object **AB** on the specimen. The object lies within the focal length. Rays from **A** diverge after refraction; when traced backwards they appear to come from a virtual, erect, magnified image **A′B′** on the same side as the object.

*Derivation (5 marks):*

Step 1 — Place the object at distance **u** such that the virtual image forms at **v = −D = −25 cm**.

Step 2 — Apply the lens formula (Cartesian sign convention):

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute **v = −25**:

$$
\dfrac{1}{-25} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

Step 4 — Rearrange for **1/u**:

$$
\dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{-25} - \dfrac{1}{f} = -\left( \dfrac{1}{25} + \dfrac{1}{f} \right)
$$

Step 5 — Linear magnification:

$$
m = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{-25}{u} = 25 \left( \dfrac{1}{25} + \dfrac{1}{f} \right) = 1 + \dfrac{25}{f}
$$

Step 6 — Therefore, the angular magnification is:

$$
\boxed{M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f} = 1 + \dfrac{25}{f\ (\text{in cm})}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

*Given:* f = 6.25 cm, D = 25 cm

**(i) Image at infinity:**

$$
M_{\infty} = \dfrac{D}{f} = \dfrac{25}{6.25} = \mathbf{4 \times}
$$

**(ii) Image at near point:**

$$
M_{D} = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f} = 1 + \dfrac{25}{6.25} = 1 + 4 = \mathbf{5 \times}
$$

**Comment on shift suitability (3 marks for comment):**
The **normal adjustment (image at infinity, M = 4×)** is more suitable for a 4-hour inspection shift because the inspector's eye remains relaxed (zero accommodation), avoiding ciliary muscle fatigue, headaches, and accommodation spasms. The near-point mode, although 25% more powerful, forces the eye to continuously focus at 25 cm, leading to significant **visual fatigue** and reduced detection reliability over extended periods.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle and construction of a **stereo microscope** used in NDT inspection. Why is it preferred over a monocular compound microscope for fracture-surface examination? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Numerical Aperture (NA)** and **Resolving Power (RP)** of a microscope objective. A metallurgical microscope uses an objective of NA = 0.65 with green light of wavelength 550 nm. Calculate the **limit of resolution**. If a higher-magnification objective of NA = 1.25 is used, by what factor does the resolving power improve? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stereo Microscope — Working Principle (3 marks):**
A stereo microscope uses **two independent optical paths** (one for each eye), producing two slightly different views of the same object. The brain fuses these into a single **three-dimensional image**. Each path consists of an objective, a common main objective (CMO) or separate Greenough prisms, and an eyepiece. Magnification typically ranges from **10× to 60×** with **working distances of 2–15 cm**.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Illuminator** (top or bottom) for opaque/transparent specimens
- **Objective pair** (Greenough or CMO type)
- **Magnification changer** (zoom or step)
- **Binocular eyepieces** with dioptre adjustment
- **Stand** with coarse and fine focus

**Why preferred for fracture-surface examination (2 marks):**
1. Provides **true 3-D depth perception** to identify crack propagation paths and beach marks.
2. Long **working distance** allows manipulation, tilting, and cleaning of bulky fracture specimens.
3. Lower magnification range means **larger field of view** and **greater depth of focus**, both essential for irregular fracture topographies.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** The product of the refractive index **n** of the medium between the specimen and objective, and the sine of the half-angle **θ** of the maximum cone of light entering the lens: $NA = n \sin \theta$.
- **Resolving Power (RP):** The ability of the microscope to distinguish two closely spaced points as separate. It is the reciprocal of the **limit of resolution** $d_{\min}$.

**Given:** λ = 550 nm = $5.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m, NA₁ = 0.65, NA₂ = 1.25.

**Step 1 — Limit of resolution with NA₁ = 0.65:**

$$
d_{\min,1} = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\text{NA}_1} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.65}
$$

$$
d_{\min,1} = \dfrac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{0.65} = \mathbf{5.16 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 516\ \text{nm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Limit of resolution with NA₂ = 1.25:**

$$
d_{\min,2} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.25} = \dfrac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{1.25} = \mathbf{2.68 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \approx 268\ \text{nm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Improvement factor in resolving power:**

$$
\text{Factor} = \dfrac{d_{\min,1}}{d_{\min,2}} = \dfrac{5.16 \times 10^{-7}}{2.68 \times 10^{-7}} \approx \mathbf{1.93}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The higher-NA objective (1.25) improves the resolving power by a factor of **~1.93×**, allowing the inspector to resolve crack-tip features that are roughly **half as small** as those visible with the 0.65 NA lens.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Magnifiers (Module 1)**

- ✅ Magnifier = single/multi-lens optical tool for **Visual Testing (VT)** in NDT.
- ✅ Produces a **virtual, erect, magnified** image.
- ✅ Standard value: **Least Distance of Distinct Vision D = 25 cm**.
- ✅ Normal adjustment (image at infinity): $M = D / f = 25 / f\ (\text{cm})$.
- ✅ Near-point adjustment (image at D): $M = 1 + D / f = 1 + 25 / f\ (\text{cm})$.
- ✅ Always convert focal length to **cm** before substituting.
- ✅ Power of lens: $P = 1/f$ (in metres), measured in **dioptres**.
- ✅ Numerical Aperture: $NA = n \sin \theta$.
- ✅ Limit of resolution: $d_{\min} = 0.61 \, \lambda / \text{NA}$ (Rayleigh criterion).
- ✅ Total magnification (compound): $M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{obj}} \times M_{\text{eye}}$.
- ✅ Working distance **decreases** as magnification **increases**.
- ✅ Field of view **decreases** as magnification **increases**.
- ✅ Standard NDT magnifications: **10× for PT/MT indications**, **20×–50× for fractography**.
- ✅ **Stereo microscope** is preferred for fracture-surface examination due to 3-D depth perception and long working distance.
- ✅ **Compound metallurgical microscope** is preferred for inclusion rating, grain-size measurement, and phase identification.
- ✅ For prolonged inspection, prefer the **infinity-adjusted (relaxed-eye)** mode to prevent inspector fatigue.
- ✅ Always mention **D = 25 cm** explicitly when stating magnification formulas in exams.
- ✅ High magnification alone is **useless** without adequate resolving power — both are required for true defect detection.
- ✅ Resolution limit scales inversely with NA; doubling NA approximately halves $d_{\min}$.
- ✅ In NDT, **magnification without resolution** is the most common student misconception — never confuse the two.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Boroscope** (also called a *borescope*) is an **optical visual inspection instrument** used in Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) to perform a remote, direct visual examination of the internal surfaces of otherwise inaccessible cavities, hollow components, or enclosed volumes of a part — **without dismantling or destroying** the assembly.

It achieves this by transmitting **illuminating light** down a probe (rigid tube or flexible fiber-optic bundle) to the target surface and then relaying the **reflected optical image** back to the examiner's eyepiece, camera, or display monitor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Snapshot (PESFT521 — Module 1):**
> *"A boroscope is a flexible or rigid optical device used for remote visual inspection of internal surfaces of components such as engines, turbines, castings and pipelines to detect cracks, corrosion, blockages, wear and other surface defects."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you have dropped your house key inside a long, dark, twisted pipe. You cannot reach it with your hand, but you desperately need to see *where* it is stuck. What do you do?

You grab a **flashlight + a small mirror on a stick** and poke it inside. The light illuminates the dark interior, the mirror reflects the view back to your eye, and you can now "see" inside the pipe without opening it up.

A **boroscope works on exactly this principle** — but engineered with precision optics:

- The **flashlight** is replaced by a high-intensity **fiber-optic light guide** channeled from an external lamp.
- The **mirror on a stick** is replaced by a sophisticated chain of **objective lens → image relay → eyepiece/camera**.
- The **stick** is replaced by a **rigid stainless-steel tube** (rigid borescope) or a **flexible articulating fiber bundle** (fiberscope), which can bend around curves inside engines and turbines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A *medical endoscope* (used by doctors to look inside your stomach) is a close cousin of the industrial boroscope. The only major difference is that boroscopes are **ruggedized for harsh industrial environments** (heat, oil, vibration) while endoscopes are designed for biological safety.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Probe **diameter** | **1.6 to 12** | mm |
| Probe **length** | **150 to 3000** | mm |
| Working **temperature** | up to **2000 °C** (with cooling) | °C |
| Field of **view (FOV)** | **30° to 120°** | degrees |
| Direction of **view** | Forward (0°), Side (90°), Retro (110°–120°) | degrees |
| **Resolution** | **> 5 line-pairs/mm** | lp/mm |
| Light source | **LED, Halogen, Xenon** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Boro" + "scope"?** The prefix *boro* is derived from the German/Greek *bore* meaning "interior" (as in the *bore* of a gun barrel), and *scope* meaning "to look." So literally: *an instrument to look into interiors.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

A boroscope delivers its function through a tightly choreographed sequence of optical events. Each step has a specific *Why* and *How*.

### Step 1: Illumination Generation
- **Why:** Internal cavities are pitch-dark; ambient light cannot reach them.
- **How:** An **external high-intensity light source** (Halogen, Xenon, or modern LED) is coupled into a **non-coherent fiber-optic light guide**. The light travels down the bundle by **total internal reflection** and exits at the distal tip, flooding the inspection area.

### Step 2: Image Formation at Objective Lens
- **Why:** The illuminated surface must form a real, sharp optical image that can be relayed further.
- **How:** The **objective lens** (a multi-element cemented doublet/triplet) at the distal tip forms a real intermediate image of the target surface on the focal plane of the relay system.

### Step 3: Image Transmission
- **Why:** The image must be carried from the cramped distal tip back to the comfortable eyepiece — possibly over a meter away.
- **How:** Two principal methods exist:
  - **Rigid relay lens system** — a series of *rod lenses* (Glass Rod Relay, e.g., Hopkinson patent) maintain image fidelity through a straight tube.
  - **Coherent fiber-optic bundle** — thousands of precisely aligned glass fibers, each carrying one pixel of the image. The bundle must be *coherent* (each fiber at the same position on both ends), otherwise the image scrambles.

### Step 4: Viewing at the Eyepiece
- **Why:** The examiner needs a magnified, comfortable view.
- **How:** An **eyepiece** (Ramsden or Kellner design) magnifies the relayed image for the eye. In **videoscopes**, a miniature **CCD/CMOS sensor** replaces the eyepiece, converting the image to an electronic video signal displayed on an LCD monitor.

### Step 5: Articulation & Steering (Flexible/Video Borescopes)
- **Why:** Real inspection paths are rarely straight — turbine blades, curved pipes, engine internals all have bends.
- **How:** The distal tip is mounted on **mechanically articulating joints** controlled by wires (Bowden cables) operated via a joystick/wheel on the handle, enabling **2-way or 4-way steering** (typically ±100° to ±180°).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Eye:** The examiner must understand not just *what* each part does, but the *order* of light travel and the *order* of image formation. A common mistake in answers is to confuse *light guide* (non-coherent) with *image guide* (coherent). They are physically and functionally different.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | **Thin lens equation** (sign convention: distances measured from lens) | Image distance for objective/eyepiece |
| 2 | $m = \dfrac{v}{u}$ | **Linear magnification** | Magnification power of objective |
| 3 | $M_{total} = m_o \times m_e$ | **Total magnification** (objective × eyepiece) | Overall system magnification |
| 4 | $FOV = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | **Field of view** at working distance $d$ and angular field $\theta$ | Width of area visible at a given distance |
| 5 | $DOF \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA^{2}}$ | Approx. **depth of focus** related to wavelength $\lambda$ and numerical aperture $NA$ | Range over which image stays sharp |
| 6 | $NA = n \cdot \sin \theta_a$ | **Numerical aperture** (where $n$ is refractive index of medium) | Light-gathering ability of fiber |
| 7 | $R_{fiber} = \dfrac{1}{2d}$ (lp/mm) | **Limiting resolution** of fiber bundle (d = fiber core diameter) | Smallest defect detectable by image guide |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{D_{bundle}}{D_{fiber}}$ | **Pixel count** of coherent fiber bundle (D = diameters) | Effective image resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention reminder (Cartesian, KTU standard):** Object distance $u$ is **negative**; image distance $v$ is **positive** for real images and **negative** for virtual images. Focal length $f$ is positive for convex (converging) lenses.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boroscopes are the *workhorse of remote visual inspection* across industries:

- **Aerospace:** Inspecting turbine blades, combustor liners, and wing-fuselage cavities in jet engines without engine teardown.
- **Power generation:** Internal inspection of gas/steam turbine casings, boiler tubes, and heat exchangers.
- **Automotive:** Cylinder bores, intake manifold runners, gearbox internals.
- **Foundries/castings:** Pre-machining visual check of sand cores, porosity, and blowholes.
- **Pipelines:** Blockage detection, weld root inspection.
- **Law enforcement/forensics:** Internal cavity search of sealed containers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NDT Classification:** Boroscopy falls under the **VT (Visual Testing)** category of NDT methods, and is officially codified in standards **ASTM E1411, ASTM E1316, BS EN 13018**, and **ASNT CP-189**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Field of View (FOV) from Angular Field and Working Distance

**Given:** A borescope has an angular field of view $\theta$ and the target surface is at a perpendicular working distance $d$ from the objective lens. We wish to find the diameter $W$ of the circular area that the borescope can see at that distance.

**Geometric Setup:** The objective lens subtends an angle $\theta$ at the apex (lens position). The half-angle is $\theta / 2$. From the apex, two straight lines run to the edges of the visible circular area, forming a symmetric isosceles triangle with the line of sight as the axis.

**Derivation:**

$$
\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{\text{half-width of area}}{d} = \frac{W/2}{d}
$$

Solving for the full width $W$:

$$
\frac{W}{2} = d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
W = 2 d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

Hence the **Field of View** (lateral width of the inspection zone) is:

$$
\boxed{FOV = 2 d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}
$$

This is the **single most tested formula** on boroscopes in KTU examinations. Memorize the units: $d$ in mm, $\theta$ in degrees, $FOV$ in mm.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Field of View Calculation (3-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A flexible fiberscope has an angular field of view of **60°**. It is being used to inspect a turbine blade whose surface is at a perpendicular distance of **25 mm** from the objective tip. Calculate the diameter of the area that is visible on the screen at that working distance.

**Given:**
- Angular field of view, $\theta = 60°$
- Working distance, $d = 25$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula:**

$$
FOV = 2 d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the half-angle:**

$$
\frac{\theta}{2} = \frac{60°}{2} = 30°
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the tangent:**

$$
\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the FOV formula:**

$$
FOV = 2 \times 25 \text{ mm} \times 0.5774
$$

$$
FOV = 50 \times 0.5774
$$

$$
\boxed{FOV = 28.87 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The inspector can see a circular region of about **28.87 mm diameter** at the working distance. To capture a larger area, the tip must be retracted, or a wide-angle borescope (larger $\theta$) must be used.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Magnification using Thin Lens (7-Mark Type)

**Problem:** The objective lens of a rigid borescope has a focal length of **20 mm**. An internal defect on a casting surface is located at a distance of **30 mm** from the objective lens. Determine (a) the image distance, and (b) the linear magnification of the intermediate image.

**Given:**
- Focal length of objective, $f = +20$ mm
- Object distance, $u = -30$ mm (using Cartesian sign convention; object is on the incoming side)

**Step 1 — Apply the thin lens equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{-30} = \frac{1}{20}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{30} = \frac{1}{20}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $\dfrac{1}{v}$:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{20} - \frac{1}{30}
$$

**Step 4 — Find a common denominator (60):**

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{3}{60} - \frac{2}{60} = \frac{1}{60}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v$:**

$$
\boxed{v = +60 \text{ mm}}
$$

The positive sign indicates the image is **real** and formed on the opposite side of the lens from the object — exactly where the relay system picks it up.

**Step 6 — Calculate magnification $m$:**

$$
m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{+60}{-30} = -2
$$

The negative sign denotes an **inverted** real image (standard for converging lenses with real images). The magnitude **2×** means the intermediate image is twice the size of the defect.

$$
\boxed{m = -2 \text{ (inverted, 2× magnified)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification via the lensmaker interpretation:** For a real inverted image, $v$ must be positive and $u$ negative. We got $v = 60$ mm > $f = 20$ mm, so the image is beyond the focal point — consistent with our inversion expectation. ✔

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** If your final magnification is *negative* for a real-image situation, that is **correct**. If it comes out *positive* for a real image, re-check your sign convention — almost always a sign error.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Resolution of Fiber Bundle (7-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A fiberscope uses a coherent fiber-optic bundle made of glass fibers, each with a core diameter of **12 μm**. Determine the limiting spatial resolution of the image guide in line-pairs per millimetre.

**Given:**
- Fiber core diameter, $d = 12 \, \mu m = 0.012$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the resolution formula:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2d}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.012 \text{ mm}} = \frac{1}{0.024 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
\boxed{R = 41.67 \text{ line-pairs/mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** This is the *theoretical upper limit*. The smallest distinguishable defect feature is about **24 μm** in size — adequate for finding cracks, corrosion pits, and erosion in most engine inspection tasks, but not for micro-electronic inspection.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Situation Asked in Exam | Formula to Apply | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| "Calculate area visible at distance X" | $FOV = 2d \tan(\theta/2)$ | Forgetting to halve the angle |
| "Find image position / magnification" | $1/v - 1/u = 1/f$, $m = v/u$ | Sign of $u$ and $v$ |
| "Smallest defect that can be seen" | $R = 1/(2d)$ | Mixing mm and μm |
| "How bright / how much light" | $NA = n \sin\theta_a$ | Confusing $n$ (refractive index) with $N$ (fiber count) |
| "Total magnification" | $M_{total} = m_o \cdot m_e$ | Multiplying absolute values ignoring orientation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Flexible Borescope (Fiberscope)

The following flowchart maps the **functional subsystems** and the path of light vs. the path of the image through a typical flexible fiber-optic borescope.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[External Light Source] -->|white light| lgA[Non-Coherent Light Guide Fibers]
```
