```mermaid
    lgA -->|illumination rays| tipA[Distal Objective Tip]
    objA[Internal Defect Surface] -->|reflected rays| tipA
    tipA -->|focused image| igA[Coherent Image Guide Fibers]
    igA -->|relayed image| epA[Eyepiece / CCD Camera]
    epA -->|visual / video| dispA[Examiner Eye / LCD Monitor]

    artA[Articulation Joystick] -->|Bowden cables| tipA

    subgraph ILLUM[ILLUMINATION SUBSYSTEM]
        srcA
        lgA
    end

    subgraph IMAGING[IMAGE FORMATION SUBSYSTEM]
        tipA
        igA
        epA
        dispA
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[STEERING SUBSYSTEM]
        artA
    end

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef light fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000
    classDef image fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000

    class srcA source
    class lgA light
    class tipA,igA image
    class epA,dispA,objA output
    class artA control
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- **Orange nodes** (Light Source, Light Guide) form the *Illumination Subsystem* — they *deliver* light to the dark interior.
- **Blue nodes** (Tip, Image Guide) form the *Image Formation Subsystem* — they *capture and relay* the image.
- **Green nodes** (Eyepiece, Monitor, Defect Surface) are *endpoints* of the optical chain.
- **Pink node** (Joystick) is the *Steering Subsystem* — it physically aims the tip toward the defect.

## 4.2 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Three Borescope Generations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN1[GENERATION 1 - RIGID BOROSCOPE]
        r1[Rigid Stainless Tube] --> r2[Hopkins Rod Lens Relay]
        r2 --> r3[Eyepiece Only]
    end

    subgraph GEN2[GENERATION 2 - FLEXIBLE FIBERSCOPE]
        f1[Articulating Tip] --> f2[Coherent Fiber Bundle]
        f2 --> f3[Eyepiece or Still Camera]
    end

    subgraph GEN3[GENERATION 3 - VIDEO BOROSCOPE]
        v1[Articulating Tip with Miniature Camera] --> v2[Electronic Cable]
        v2 --> v3[LCD Monitor and Recording]
    end

    classDef gen1node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef gen2node fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef gen3node fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFF

    class r1,r2,r3 gen1node
    class f1,f2,f3 gen2node
    class v1,v2,v3 gen3node
```

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Rigid vs. Flexible vs. Video Borescope

| Feature | **Rigid Borescope** | **Flexible Fiberscope** | **Video Borescope (Videoscope)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image transmission** | Rod lens (Hopkins) | Coherent fiber bundle | CCD/CMOS chip + electronic cable |
| **Steering** | None (straight) | 2-way or 4-way articulation | 4-way articulation, joystick |
| **Image quality** | Highest resolution | Limited by fiber pixelation | High, digital processing possible |
| **Length** | Short (< 600 mm typical) | Long (up to 6 m) | Long (up to 10 m) |
| **Diameter** | 1.6 – 12 mm | 2 – 12 mm | 3.7 – 8 mm |
| **Recording** | Eyepiece only (camera adapter needed) | Eyepiece or photo adapter | Direct digital recording |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Typical use** | Cylinder bores, gun barrels | Engine interiors, turbines | High-end aerospace inspection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigid borescopes offer the BEST image resolution** for their cost, but they cannot bend. Flexible fiberscopes are the *versatile all-rounders*. Video borescopes are the *premium choice* for documentation and difficult access.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls for Boroscopes:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion** — many students lose 2 marks by writing $u = +30$ instead of $u = -30$ in lens problems.
> 2. **Mixing up Light Guide and Image Guide** — light guide is *non-coherent* (random fiber arrangement), image guide is *coherent* (identical arrangement at both ends). Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Skipping units** in FOV and magnification problems — always write mm, °, etc.
> 4. **Forgetting that rigid borescopes have NO articulation** — writing "the rigid borescope can be steered" is a guaranteed zero.
> 5. **Writing "fiberscope uses ordinary optical fiber"** — must specify *coherent fiber bundle*.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]

**Define a boroscope. Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A boroscope is an optical instrument used in Non-Destructive Testing for the **remote visual inspection** of internal surfaces of inaccessible cavities within a component, without disassembly. It transmits illuminating light through a fiber-optic guide and relays the reflected image through a chain of lenses or coherent fiber bundle to the examiner's eyepiece or monitor.

**Two applications:**
1. **Aerospace:** Internal inspection of aircraft engine turbine blades and combustor liners for cracks and corrosion.
2. **Foundry:** Visual inspection of casting internals for porosity, sand inclusions, and misruns before machining.

*(Any two valid applications accepted — 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two applications.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**Differentiate between coherent and non-coherent fiber bundles used in a fiberscope.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | **Coherent Fiber Bundle** | **Non-Coherent Fiber Bundle** |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Carries the **image** | Carries the **illumination light** |
| Fiber arrangement | Identical relative position of fibers at both ends | Random fiber arrangement |
| Image property | Preserves spatial information (each fiber = one pixel) | No image formed |
| Typical use | Image guide of fiberscope | Light guide of fiberscope |
| Cost | High (precise alignment) | Low |

*(1.5 marks for each — 3 marks total.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **Q. A (a)** Describe the construction and working of a flexible fiber-optic borescope (fiberscope) with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q. A (b)** A borescope has an objective lens of focal length 25 mm. The target is at 35 mm from the lens. Calculate the image position and the magnification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction of a Flexible Fiberscope (Block diagram description in text):**

1. **Distal Tip Assembly** *(1 mark)*
   - Houses the **objective lens** (multi-element doublet/triplet).
   - Contains the distal end of the **light guide** and **image guide** fiber bundles.
   - Includes a **steering section** with two pairs of articulation joints (4-way steering).

2. **Flexible Insertion Tube** *(1 mark)*
   - Stainless-steel braided outer sheath for durability.
   - Contains the **coherent fiber-optic image guide** and **non-coherent light guide**.
   - Contains **Bowden cables** for articulation control.
   - Typical length 1.5 m to 6 m.

3. **Handle / Control Body** *(1 mark)*
   - Houses the **articulation joystick/wheel** for steering the tip.
   - Contains the **eyepiece** (Ramsden/Kellner design).
   - Light source connector.

4. **Light Source** *(1 mark)*
   - External Halogen / Xenon / LED lamp coupled into the non-coherent light guide.

**Working Principle:** *(3 marks)*

1. The external light source delivers white light into the **non-coherent light guide**, which transmits the light by total internal reflection to the distal tip, illuminating the inspection zone.
2. Light reflected from the internal surface enters the **objective lens** and forms a real intermediate image at the entrance face of the **coherent image guide**.
3. The **coherent fiber bundle** transmits this image — fiber by fiber, pixel by pixel — to the proximal end of the bundle, preserving spatial fidelity.
4. The **eyepiece** magnifies this relayed image for direct viewing. Alternatively, a camera adapter or a built-in CCD can convert the image to a video signal.
5. The examiner manually steers the distal tip using the joystick to scan the cavity. Defects such as cracks, corrosion, erosion, and foreign objects are identified by direct visual interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Focal length $f = +25$ mm
- Object distance $u = -35$ mm (Cartesian sign convention)

**Step 1 — Thin lens equation:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{-35} = \frac{1}{25}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{35} = \frac{1}{25}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $\dfrac{1}{v}$:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{25} - \frac{1}{35}
$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator (175):** *(1 mark)*

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{7}{175} - \frac{5}{175} = \frac{2}{175}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v$:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{v = +87.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

A positive $v$ confirms a **real image** on the far side of the lens — correctly placed for the relay system to pick up.

**Step 6 — Magnification:** *(1 mark)*

$$
m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{+87.5}{-35} = -2.5
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** *(1 mark)*

The intermediate image is formed at **87.5 mm** from the objective, **inverted**, and **magnified 2.5×**. This image is then relayed to the eyepiece or image guide.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **Q. B (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a rigid borescope. List any four advantages and two limitations. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q. B (b)** A fiberscope has an angular field of view of 70° and is used at a working distance of 40 mm. Calculate (i) the field of view diameter, and (ii) the magnification if the objective focal length is 30 mm and the object is at 45 mm from the lens. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction of a Rigid Borescope:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Eyepiece** at the proximal end — provides magnified viewing of the image.
2. **Relay lens system** (Hopkinson rod-lens relay) — a series of precisely spaced glass rod lenses inside the barrel that transfer the image from one end of the tube to the other with minimal loss.
3. **Objective lens** at the distal end — focuses light from the target onto the relay system.
4. **Light guide / fiber-optic illumination channel** — runs parallel to the imaging path; carries light from an external source to the tip.
5. **Outer stainless-steel tube** — provides rigidity and protection.
6. **Optional angled adapters** — 0° (forward), 45°, 90° (side), 110°–120° (retro) view attachments.

**Working (brief):** Light from the source travels through the illumination guide to illuminate the cavity. The objective forms a real image. The relay rod-lens chain transmits this image to the eyepiece, where the examiner views it. *(1 mark)*

**Four Advantages:** *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. Highest optical resolution among the three borescope types.
2. Mechanically robust — no moving fibers to break.
3. Lower cost compared to fiberscopes and videoscopes.
4. Compact and lightweight; ideal for routine cylinder-bore inspections.

**Two Limitations:** *(1 mark — 0.5 each)*
1. Cannot bend; restricted to straight-line inspection paths.
2. Limited length (typically < 600 mm) due to image degradation over long rigid relays.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Angular FOV, $\theta = 70°$
- Working distance, $d = 40$ mm
- Objective focal length, $f = +30$ mm
- Object distance, $u = -45$ mm

**Part (i) — Field of View Diameter:** *(3 marks)*

**Step 1:**

$$
FOV = 2 d \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Half-angle:**

$$
\frac{\theta}{2} = 35°
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate tangent:**

$$
\tan(35°) \approx 0.7002
$$

**Step 4 — Compute FOV:**

$$
FOV = 2 \times 40 \times 0.7002 = 56.02 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{FOV \approx 56.02 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Magnification:** *(4 marks)*

**Step 1 — Thin lens equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{-45} = \frac{1}{30}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{45} = \frac{1}{30}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{30} - \frac{1}{45}
$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator (90):**

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{3}{90} - \frac{2}{90} = \frac{1}{90}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $v$:**

$$
v = +90 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnification:**

$$
m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{+90}{-45} = -2
$$

**Conclusion:** The image is **real, inverted, 2× magnified**, formed at 90 mm from the objective — relayed onward through the fiber bundle or rod-lens system. The total visible area at the working distance is **56 mm** in diameter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this and pin it!)**

- **Boroscope = optical NDT tool** for visual inspection of inaccessible internal surfaces without damage.
- **Three types:** **Rigid** (rod-lens, no steering), **Flexible Fiberscope** (coherent fiber bundle, 4-way steering), **Video Borescope / Videoscope** (CCD/CMOS chip at tip, digital output).
- **Five key components:** Objective lens, Relay (rod lenses or coherent fibers), Eyepiece/Camera, Light guide (non-coherent fibers), Articulation system (where applicable).
- **Light guide = non-coherent** (random fiber arrangement, carries light only).
- **Image guide = coherent** (identical fiber arrangement at both ends, carries the image).
- **Most important formula:** $FOV = 2 d \tan(\theta / 2)$ — practice 3–4 numericals.
- **Second most important formula:** thin lens equation $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ with **Cartesian sign convention** ($u$ negative for real objects).
- **Resolution of fiber bundle** $R = 1/(2d)$ where $d$ is core diameter — small fiber → high resolution.
- **Standards:** ASTM E1411, ASTM E1316, BS EN 13018, ASNT CP-189.
- **Direction of View options:** Forward (0°), Side (90°), Retro (110°–120°).
- **Applications memorized:** Aerospace turbines, engine cylinders, castings, pipelines, gearboxes, heat exchangers, forensic containers.
- **Key advantages:** Non-destructive, real-time, no radiation hazard, no disassembly needed, portable.
- **Key limitations:** Limited to surface-breaking or surface defects; cannot see through walls; image interpretation is operator-dependent; cannot inspect opaque or muddy environments without flushing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Memory Aid:** Think of **L-I-R-E** — **L**ight in (via non-coherent bundle), **I**mage out (via objective), **R**elay (rod lens or coherent fiber), **E**yepiece/CCD. The whole boroscope is a one-way street for light going in, and a one-way street for image coming out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibroscopes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fibroscopes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fibroscope** (also termed a **Flexible Fiber Optic Borescope**) is a precision optical non-destructive testing (NDT) instrument that uses a flexible bundle of precisely aligned **coherent optical fibers** to transmit illuminating light into, and return a magnified visible image from, the interior of a component or structure that is geometrically inaccessible to direct line-of-sight visual inspection. The image-forming bundle is complemented by a second, non-image-forming (**incoherent**) fiber bundle that delivers high-intensity cool light from an external source to the inspection zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snippet (High-Yield):** A fibroscope is *the most widely used member of the borescope family in NDT*, distinguished from rigid borescopes by its *articulated working length (typically 1 m – 8 m)* which is achieved by relaying an image through thousands of flexible glass or polymer fibers exploiting the phenomenon of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a fibroscope as a **"flexible periscope made of glass hair."** Imagine trying to inspect the inside of a jet engine turbine without disassembling it. A normal periscope (rigid mirrors) cannot bend around the curved turbine blades. Now imagine taking 10,000–50,000 ultra-thin glass strands (each thinner than a human hair, ~10 µm diameter) and bundling them inside a flexible rubber sheath. If you shine a torch at one end, each strand carries a tiny pixel of light all the way to the other end without it "leaking out" sideways — exactly like water flowing down a curved transparent pipe. If every strand is forced to keep the *same relative position* at both ends, an entire image is reproduced at the viewing end like a living "optical mosaic." That is a fibroscope.

| Component | Real-World Analogy |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Coherent fiber bundle | A jigsaw puzzle where every piece keeps its position from one end to the other |
| Incoherent bundle (light guide) | A tangled bundle of optical "hoses" carrying light just for illumination |
| Objective lens | The "eye" of the instrument focused on the target |
| Eyepiece | The magnifying glass you look through |
| Articulation cables | The "tendons" that bend the tip of the scope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property Highlighted by KTU Board Examiners:** The flexibility is achieved *without loss of image integrity* because the information travels as photons confined within each fiber, not as a free beam that would be blocked by bending. **Bending radius as tight as ~30 mm** is permissible in industrial scopes.

## 1.3 Position of Fibroscopes in the NDT Family

Fibroscopes belong to the **Visual Testing (VT)** category of NDT methods (per **ASNT TC-1A** and **ASTM E1316** terminology adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus). They are the *only* VT technique capable of inspecting complex internal geometries in real time, without dismantling, and without ionizing radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Visual Testing through fibroscopes is governed by standards such as **ASTM E1411** (for fiber optic borescopes) and **ASTM E2191** (for video image borescopes). The instrument is generally classified as a **Class A** (flexible, fiber-optic image relay) borescope.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of a fibroscope is built on **four cascaded physical phenomena**:

1. **Illumination of Target** — Cool light (typically **150 W halogen** or **LED/laser source**) is launched into the *incoherent* fiber bundle. Because the fibers are scrambled (no spatial order), the light emerges as a uniform flood at the distal tip.

2. **Image Formation at Distal End** — The reflected light from the target surface forms a real image via the **objective lens** (a fixed-focus or focusable multi-element glass lens, f-number typically f/2.8 – f/6).

3. **Image Relay via Coherent Bundle** — The image falls on the proximal face of the *coherent* fiber bundle. Each fiber samples a single picture element (**pixel**). Light travels down each fiber by repeated **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core–cladding interface, faithfully preserving spatial information.

4. **Image Viewing** — The emergent mosaic image is magnified by the **eyepiece** (typically 10× – 40×) and presented to the operator's eye, or coupled to a **CCD/CMOS camera** for video recording.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TIR? Why not just a glass rod?** A solid glass rod would lose all image information because the rays would cross-talk. By isolating each pixel within its own fiber (core) surrounded by a lower-index cladding, every ray is *channelled* without mixing with its neighbours. The core-to-cladding refractive index difference is small (typical Δn ≈ 0.01) but sufficient.

## 2.2 The Why-How of Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

- **Why TIR occurs:** When light travels from a denser medium (core, refractive index $n_1$) to a rarer medium (cladding, $n_2$ where $n_2 < n_1$), the refracted ray bends *away* from the normal. At a specific angle of incidence called the **critical angle** $\theta_c$, the refracted ray grazes the interface (refraction angle = 90°). For *any* angle of incidence greater than $\theta_c$, no refraction is possible and 100 % of the energy is reflected back into the core.

- **How it confines light:** Because the core-cladding interface is a cylindrical surface surrounding the ray, every meridional (and skew) ray that hits the interface at $\theta_i > \theta_c$ is internally reflected — the ray "bounces" down the fiber like a ball inside a tube.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Critical angle (TIR) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | $n_1$ = core RI, $n_2$ = cladding RI, $\theta_c$ in rad or deg |
| 2 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$ | $n_0$ = refractive index of medium surrounding fiber (≈ 1.0 in air), $\theta_a$ = acceptance half-angle |
| 3 | Acceptance angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}}{n_0}\right)$ | Defines the cone of light that can be coupled into the fiber |
| 4 | Acceptance cone solid angle | $\Omega = \pi\,(NA)^{2}$ | Useful for radiometric light-collection calculations |
| 5 | Resolving power (Rayleigh) | $d_{\min} = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}$ | $d_{\min}$ = smallest resolvable object, $\lambda$ = wavelength of light (typically 550 nm) |
| 6 | Fiber count (coherent bundle) | $N \approx \dfrac{D^{2}}{d^{2}}$ | $D$ = bundle diameter, $d$ = individual fiber core diameter |
| 7 | Angular magnification (eyepiece) | $M = \dfrac{\theta_i}{\theta_o} \approx \dfrac{25\ \text{cm}}{f_e}$ | $f_e$ = focal length of eyepiece; 25 cm = near-point distance |
| 8 | Image brightness factor | $B \propto (NA)^{2}$ | Higher NA → brighter image (square-law scaling) |
| 9 | Fractional Index Difference | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Typical 0.005 – 0.02 for step-index fibers |
| 10 | Bend loss critical radius | $R_c \approx \dfrac{n_1^{2}\,D^{2}}{2\,\Delta n}$ | $D$ = core diameter; below $R_c$ losses rise sharply |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (5) tells us that to inspect *finer cracks*, the fibroscope must have a *larger* NA. Industrial high-resolution scopes (e.g., Olympus IPLEX NX) advertise NA ≈ 0.18 – 0.25, giving a theoretical limit of detection of ~1.3 µm at $\lambda$ = 550 nm. Equation (6) is the *cheat sheet* for estimating bundle pixel density — a 1.0 mm bundle of 10 µm fibers contains ~10,000 picture elements (≈ 100 × 100 pixels), and modern HD scopes use 6 µm fibers giving ~250,000 elements.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical NDT Application of Fibroscope |
|----------|----------------------------------------|
| **Aerospace (Jet Engines)** | Inspection of turbine blades, combustion chamber, compressor stages for FOD (Foreign Object Damage), cracks, hot-corrosion — *without engine teardown*. |
| **Power Generation** | Internal inspection of boilers, steam generator tubes (especially U-bends), heat-exchanger shells, nuclear reactor coolant channels. |
| **Petrochemical** | Verification of weld integrity, corrosion mapping, valve-seat inspection in pressure vessels and piping. |
| **Automotive** | Cylinder bore, combustion chamber, intake manifold, gear-box internal gear teeth. |
| **Foundries & Castings** | Detection of porosity, shrinkage cavities, sand inclusions inside complex castings. |
| **Security & Forensic** | Inspection of sealed containers, hidden compartments, post-blast explosive devices. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Most marks in Part B derivations are awarded for *clear statement of the assumed model*, *correct substitution*, and *dimensional/limiting-case consistency*. The derivations below are presented in the exact sequence expected on the answer script.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Condition for Total Internal Reflection (Snell's Law + Geometry)

**Assumed model:** Step-index optical fiber, planar core–cladding interface, monochromatic light, meridional ray (lies in a plane containing the fiber axis).

**Step 1 — Snell's law at the fiber entry face (air → core):**

$$
n_0 \sin\theta_0 = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$

Here $n_0 \approx 1.0$ (air), $\theta_0$ is the angle of incidence on the fiber tip, and $\theta_1$ is the angle of refraction inside the core measured from the *fiber axis*.

**Step 2 — Internal geometry (ray crossing the core):**

When the refracted ray strikes the core–cladding interface, the angle of incidence (measured from the *normal to the interface*, which is radial) is:

$$
\theta_i = 90^{\circ} - \theta_1
$$

**Step 3 — Snell's law at the core–cladding interface:**

$$
n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t
$$

For TIR we require $\theta_t \geq 90^{\circ}$, i.e. $\sin\theta_t \geq 1$. The limiting case is $\sin\theta_t = 1$, defining the **critical angle $\theta_c$**:

$$
\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 4 — TIR criterion:**

$$
\theta_i \geq \theta_c \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \sin\theta_i \geq \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

Substituting $\theta_i = 90^{\circ} - \theta_1$:

$$
\sin(90^{\circ} - \theta_1) \geq \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \cos\theta_1 \geq \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum entry angle for TIR propagation:**

Using Step 1 to convert $\theta_1$ to $\theta_0$ (the external acceptance angle):

$$
\sin\theta_0 = n_1 \sin\theta_1
$$

with the limiting equality $\cos\theta_1 = n_2/n_1$ (so $\sin\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - (n_2/n_1)^2}$):

$$
\boxed{\;\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}\;}
$$

This is the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the fiber.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Acceptance Angle and Cone of Light

Starting from the boxed expression above, since $n_0 \approx 1$ in air:

$$
NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}\right)
$$

**Limiting case check:**
- If $n_1 = n_2$ (no cladding contrast) → $NA = 0$ → $\theta_a = 0$. Physically: no light is guided, which is correct.
- If $n_1 \gg n_2$ → $NA \to n_1$. But $NA \leq 1$ for air-coupling, so the maximum theoretical NA in air is **1.0** (for $n_1 = \sqrt{2}$, $n_2 = 1$).

**Cone solid angle** (3-D measure of accepted light):

$$
\Omega = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta_a) \;\;\approx\;\; \pi\,(NA)^{2} \quad \text{for small }\theta_a
$$

This shows the **light-gathering power** scales as $NA^2$ — doubling NA collects **four times** more light.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Resolving Power (Smallest Detectable Defect)

Adopt the **Rayleigh criterion** for a circular aperture (the objective lens of the distal tip):

The angular resolution of a lens of diameter $D$ at wavelength $\lambda$:

$$
\theta_R = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}
$$

The minimum linear distance resolved at a working distance $L$:

$$
d_{\min} = L\,\theta_R = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda\,L}{D}
$$

Using $NA \approx D / (2f)$ for a lens in image space and $L \approx f$ (object at focal plane), we obtain the imaging-side limit:

$$
\boxed{\;d_{\min} = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}\;}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (typical fibroscope):** $\lambda = 550\ \text{nm}$, $NA = 0.20$:

$$
d_{\min} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.20} \approx 1.68\ \mu\text{m}
$$

This matches the *theoretical diffraction limit* of high-end industrial scopes.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Number of Resolvable Picture Elements (Fibers) in a Coherent Bundle

A coherent bundle of overall diameter $D_b$ containing fibers of core diameter $d$ has the packing efficiency $\eta \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ (hexagonal close-pack). The number of independent image pixels:

$$
N = \eta \cdot \dfrac{\pi (D_b/2)^{2}}{\pi (d/2)^{2}} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{D_b^{2}}{d^{2}}
$$

**Numerical example:** $D_b = 1.0\ \text{mm}$, $d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $\eta = 0.7$:

$$
N = 0.7 \times \dfrac{(10^{-3})^{2}}{(10 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} = 0.7 \times 10{,}000 = 7{,}000\ \text{fibers}
$$

Equivalent image: ~84 × 84 pixel array — sufficient to detect a 0.5 mm crack across 6 fiber widths.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Coherent vs. Incoherent Bundle Comparison

| Parameter | Coherent Bundle (Image Guide) | Incoherent Bundle (Light Guide) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Spatial order of fibers | Strictly preserved (same x,y at both ends) | Random (scrambled) |
| Function | Image relay | Light transmission (illumination) |
| Individual fiber requirement | Low cross-talk, high NA | High NA, high transmission, low cost |
| Typical fiber count | 5,000 – 40,000 | 500 – 3,000 (single thick fibers suffice) |
| Cross-section diameter | 0.5 mm – 1.5 mm | 1 mm – 4 mm |
| Quality metric | Spot sharpness & contrast | Luminous flux delivered (lumens) |
| Failure consequence if fiber breaks | Permanent black spot on image | Slight reduction in brightness |
| Cost | High (precision alignment) | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Fibroscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[External Light Source] -->|-- High intensity beam --| lgtA[Light Guide Incoherent Bundle]
    lgtA -->|Illuminate| tgtA[Internal Target Surface]
    tgtA -->|Reflected rays| objA[Objective Lens Distal Tip]
    objA -->|Real image formed| cohA[Coherent Image Fiber Bundle]
    cohA -->|Mosaic image| eyeA[Eyepiece Magnifier]
    eyeA -->|Magnified virtual image| obsA[Observer Eye or CCD Camera]
    artA[Articulation Cables Operator Handle] -->|Tip steering| cohA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Optical Path Inside a Single Fiber (TIR Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pA[Photon entering fiber tip] --> qA[Refracts into core n1]
    qA --> rA[Strikes core cladding interface at angle theta_i]
    rA --> sA{Is theta_i greater than theta_critical}
    sA -->|Yes| tA[Total Internal Reflection occurs]
    sA -->|No| uA[Ray refracts out lost]
    tA --> vA[Continues bouncing down the fiber]
    vA --> wA[Exits at distal face into objective lens]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison: Rigid Borescope vs Flexible Fibroscope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subA[Comparison Criterion] --> subB[Working Length]
    subA --> subC[Bending Capability]
    subA --> subD[Image Transmission]
    subA --> subE[Typical Use Case]

    subB --> c1A[Rigid: Up to 1.5 m]
    subB --> c1B[Fibroscope: 1 m to 8 m]

    subC --> c2A[Rigid: None straight tube only]
    c2B[Fibroscope: Articulated tip 4 way steering]

    subD --> c3A[Rigid: Relay lens rod lenses]
    cD1[Fibroscope: Coherent fiber bundle]:::fibbundle

    subE --> c4A[Rigid: Simple straight bores like gun barrels]
    cE1[Fibroscope: Curved complex interiors like turbine engines]:::fibbundle

    classDef fibbundle fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — KTU 2024 NDT Method Classification (where Fibroscope Fits)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ndtA[NDT Methods] --> vtA[Visual Testing VT]
    ndtA --> stA[Surface Methods]
    ndtA --> vtB[Volumetric Methods]
    ndtA --> otA[Other Specialized]

    vtA --> dirA[Direct Visual]
    vtA --> remA[Remote Visual RVI]
    remA --> bscA[Borescopes]
    bscA --> rigA[Rigid Borescope]
    bscA --> fibA[Fibroscope Flexible]:::fibnode
    bscA --> vidA[Video Borescope]
    bscA --> uvsA[Ultraviolet Borescope]

    classDef fibnode fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Note:** The diagrams above are *intentionally block-architectural* rather than photorealistic. This is the format preferred by KTU 2024 examiners for drawing questions (each major block = 1 mark, each connecting arrow with labelled energy/signal type = 0.5 mark, total 7 marks for a 7-mark sub-part).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Differentiate between a coherent fiber bundle and an incoherent fiber bundle. Why are both required in a fibroscope? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Coherent Bundle | Incoherent Bundle |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Fiber arrangement | Spatial order preserved (top-left at one end = top-left at the other) | Random / scrambled |
| Function | Carries the image | Carries illumination light |
| Use in fibroscope | Image guide (between objective & eyepiece) | Light guide (between source & target) |

Both are required because *a single fiber cannot both transport an image and provide uniform flood illumination*; the coherent bundle must be optimized for image fidelity (low cross-talk, small core), whereas the incoherent bundle must be optimized for luminous flux (larger NA, thicker fibers, low cost). **Hence the dual-bundle architecture is essential.**

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. State its physical significance in a fibroscope. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Numerical Aperture of an optical fiber is defined as:

$$
NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}
$$

where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the surrounding medium, $\theta_a$ is the maximum acceptance half-angle, and $n_1$, $n_2$ are the refractive indices of core and cladding respectively.

**Physical significance in a fibroscope:**

1. **Light-gathering power:** Brightness of the relayed image is proportional to $(NA)^2$. A higher NA produces a brighter, more easily viewed image in deep cavities.
2. **Resolving power:** The minimum detectable defect size $d_{\min} = 0.61\,\lambda/NA$. A higher NA resolves finer cracks.
3. **Acceptance cone:** Determines the field of view at the distal tip.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a fibroscope used in NDT. List four major industrial applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (5 key sub-systems):**

1. **Light source** — 150 W halogen / LED / xenon lamp, with infrared filter (to keep distal tip cool).
2. **Light guide (incoherent bundle)** — carries illumination from source to target.
3. **Distal tip with objective lens** — focuses reflected light onto the coherent bundle.
4. **Coherent image fiber bundle** — 5,000 – 40,000 individual fibers, precisely aligned, with bending articulation at the distal 30–60 mm.
5. **Eyepiece** — 10×–40× magnifier; can be coupled to a CCD/CMOS camera for video recording.

**Working (sequential flow):**

- Step 1: Light source is switched on; light enters incoherent bundle and emerges as a uniform flood at the distal end, illuminating the target.
- Step 2: Light reflected from the target surface is collected by the objective lens which forms a real image on the proximal face of the coherent bundle.
- Step 3: Each fiber of the coherent bundle samples one pixel of the image and transmits it by *Total Internal Reflection* to the viewing end.
- Step 4: The eyepiece magnifies the emergent mosaic image, presenting it to the observer's eye or a camera.

**Industrial applications (any four):**

1. Inspection of jet engine turbine blades for cracks and FOD.
2. Internal inspection of heat-exchanger tubes in power plants.
3. Verification of weld root pass in petrochemical piping.
4. Inspection of cylinder bores and combustion chambers in automotive engines.
5. Detection of porosity and inclusions in complex castings.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Derive the expression for the Numerical Aperture of a step-index optical fiber. A fiber has $n_1 = 1.50$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate (i) NA, (ii) acceptance angle, and (iii) the smallest resolvable defect at $\lambda = 550$ nm.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full chain — presented here in compact form):

Applying Snell's law at the air–core interface and using the TIR condition $\theta_i \geq \theta_c$ at the core–cladding interface:

$$
NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

**(i) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^{2} - (1.46)^{2}} = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.1184} \approx 0.344
$$

**(ii) Acceptance angle (in air, $n_0 = 1$):**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.344) \approx 20.13^{\circ}
$$

**(iii) Smallest resolvable defect (Rayleigh criterion):**

$$
d_{\min} = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA} = \dfrac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.344} \approx 9.75 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 0.975\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Result:** $NA \approx 0.344$, $\theta_a \approx 20.1^{\circ}$, $d_{\min} \approx 0.98\ \mu\text{m}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the phenomenon of Total Internal Reflection (TIR). Derive the condition for light to propagate through an optical fiber. Why is TIR essential for image transmission in a fibroscope?**

**Model Solution:**

**Phenomenon:** When light travels from an optically denser medium to a rarer medium (i.e. $n_1 > n_2$), the refracted ray bends away from the normal. At a specific **critical angle** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$, the refracted ray grazes the interface (refraction angle = 90°). For all angles of incidence $\theta_i > \theta_c$, no refraction occurs and 100 % of the light is reflected back into the denser medium. This is *Total Internal Reflection*.

**Propagation condition (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}
$$

For TIR to be possible, the radicand must be positive, i.e.:

$$
n_1 > n_2
$$

This is why the core of every optical fiber must have a *higher* refractive index than its cladding.

**Why TIR is essential for image transmission:**

- TIR ensures that the *direction* and *intensity* of every ray entering a fiber is preserved through thousands of reflections, with negligible loss.
- Each fiber thus acts as an independent "light pipe" carrying one pixel of the image.
- The *spatial position* of each fiber at the entry and exit faces is preserved (in a coherent bundle), so the image is reconstructed faithfully at the viewing end.
- If TIR failed, rays would leak into adjacent fibers causing **cross-talk**, destroying the image completely.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A fibroscope has a coherent bundle of 1.0 mm diameter made of fibers each of core diameter 8 µm. Assuming hexagonal close packing (packing efficiency 0.907), calculate (i) number of fibers, (ii) effective pixel count, and (iii) the smallest crack detectable when the working distance corresponds to a 4 mm field of view.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of fibers:**

Total bundle area:

$$
A = \pi\,(D/2)^{2} = \pi\,(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Area of one fiber:

$$
a = \pi\,(d/2)^{2} = \pi\,(4 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Number of fibers:

$$
N = \eta\,\dfrac{A}{a} = 0.907 \times \dfrac{7.854 \times 10^{-7}}{5.027 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 14{,}170\ \text{fibers}
$$

**(ii) Effective pixel count:**

If we approximate a square array: $\sqrt{14{,}170} \approx 119$, so the image is roughly **119 × 119 ≈ 14,200 effective pixels**.

**(iii) Smallest detectable crack:**

Field of view (FOV) = 4 mm at the working distance. Each fiber samples a region:

$$
\text{Pixel size} = \dfrac{FOV}{\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{4\ \text{mm}}{119} \approx 33.6\ \mu\text{m}
$$

A crack is detectable only if it spans *at least 1–2 pixels*:

$$
d_{\text{crack, min}} \approx 33\ \mu\text{m}\ \text{to}\ 67\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Result:** ~14,200 fibers, smallest detectable crack ≈ **35 µm** (1-pixel criterion) or **70 µm** (safer 2-pixel criterion for unambiguous detection).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (avoid these to score full 7/14):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Numerical Aperture" with "Acceptance Angle":** Many students interchange these. NA is *dimensionless*; acceptance angle is in *degrees/radians*. A 1-mark deduction is applied per KTU valuation key for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $n_0$ in NA formula:** The complete form is $NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. In air $n_0 = 1$; in water/oil $n_0 \neq 1$, and the formula *must* include it.
>
> 3. **Skipping the limiting case discussion:** The examiner expects you to state "NA = 0 when $n_1 = n_2$" and "NA ≤ 1 in air" — this is a 1-mark "depth-of-understanding" check.
>
> 4. **Not drawing arrows/labels in the block diagram:** A diagram without labels for *light source, light guide, objective, image guide, eyepiece* gets only 2–3 marks out of 7. **Always label every block and every connecting arrow.**
>
> 5. **Numerical computation errors:** Keep $n_0$ in the formula; carry at least 4 significant digits until the final step; state the *unit* explicitly in the answer (µm, nm, mm, degrees).
>
> 6. **Mixing up coherent vs incoherent bundles:** A coherent bundle is for the *image*; an incoherent bundle is for *light*. Reversing this costs 2 marks in a 7-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fibroscope = Flexible Fiber-Optic Borescope** used for *in-situ* visual NDT of inaccessible internal surfaces.
- ✅ **Two-bundle architecture** is essential: **coherent** (image guide) + **incoherent** (light guide).
- ✅ **Working principle:** Cool light via incoherent bundle → reflected by target → objective lens → coherent bundle (TIR-relayed) → eyepiece → eye/CCD.
- ✅ **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** requires $n_1 > n_2$ and $\theta_i \geq \theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- ✅ **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$. Typical industrial value: **0.15 – 0.40**.
- ✅ **Resolving power:** $d_{\min} = 0.61\,\lambda / NA$. Higher NA → finer defect detection.
- ✅ **Light-gathering power:** Scales as $(NA)^2$.
- ✅ **Coherent bundle fiber count:** $N \approx \eta\,D_b^2 / d^2$. Typical: 5,000 – 40,000.
- ✅ **Bending radius:** Modern industrial fibroscopes tolerate ~30–50 mm bend radius; below this, *bend loss* rises sharply.
- ✅ **Standard governing Visual Testing:** **ASTM E1411**, **ASTM E1316**; classification as a Class A borescope.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 application hotspots:** Jet engines, power-plant boilers, petrochemical welds, automotive cylinders, castings.
- ✅ **Comparison memory aid:** *Rigid borescope* = straight, lens-relay, limited length; *Fibroscope* = flexible, fiber-relay, long, articulated tip. Use a fibroscope whenever the geometry is curved.
- ✅ **Always include the packing-efficiency factor** (≈ 0.6 – 0.9) when calculating the number of fibers in a coherent bundle — examiners reward this.

> **End of Module 1 — Fibroscopes | PESFT521 — Non-Destructive Testing Methods | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Circuit Television
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Closed Circuit Television

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) in NDT** is a *Remote Visual Inspection (RVI)* technique in which a miniature video camera, coupled to a high-intensity illumination source, is inserted into an enclosed, hazardous, or inaccessible industrial component. The optical image captured by the camera is transmitted via a sealed **coaxial or fibre-optic cable** to a remote display monitor and recording device, enabling the operator to perform a real-time, non-intrusive visual assessment of the internal surface condition without dismantling the equipment.

The closed-loop nature of the signal path (Camera → Cable → Monitor → Recorder → Camera) is the reason the technique is called **"closed circuit"**, as opposed to broadcast television where signals are transmitted over open airwaves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Industrial Endoscope":**
> Imagine a medical doctor performing a colonoscopy on a patient. The doctor cannot see inside the body directly, so a tiny camera mounted on a flexible tube is inserted, and a live video feed is displayed on a screen. CCTV in industry does **exactly the same job**, but for machines — pipelines, pressure vessels, storage tanks, reactor cores, jet engine turbine blades, and boiler tubes. The doctor becomes the **NDT Inspector**, the patient's body becomes the **industrial component**, and the screen in the operation theatre becomes the **engineer's monitor**.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Spatial Resolution (Limiting):** typically **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm** at standard working distance of 25 mm – 100 mm.
- **Luminance (Working):** internal cavity lighting of **≥ 10,000 lux** is recommended for crevice and pit detection.
- **System Frame Rate:** **25 fps (PAL)** or **30 fps (NTSC)** for smooth motion capture.
- **Minimum Borescope Diameter (Industrial):** **6 mm** (micro-borescopes down to **1.9 mm** exist for turbine blade inspection).

## 1.4 Distinction from Related NDT Visual Methods

| Feature | Direct Visual Testing (DVT) | Borescope | **CCTV / RVI Camera** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access | Surface must be visible | Curved flexible tube | Long, often **rigid push-rod or self-propelled crawler** |
| Reach | Few centimeters | 1 m – 8 m | **Up to 300 m** (with cable reels) |
| Image Transmission | Naked eye | Eyepiece / optical fibre | **Electronic video signal** |
| Recording | Manual notes / sketches | Photo via adapter | **Continuous video, time-stamped** |
| Typical Use | External weld / corrosion | Engine cylinders, turbine blades | **Pipelines, sewers, large tanks** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an NDT-CCTV System

The CCTV inspection chain is composed of **six sequential sub-systems**, each performing a non-trivial engineering function:

1. **Optical Probe (Camera Head)**
   - Miniature **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** or **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor)** image sensor.
   - Sensor sizes range from **1/4" to 1/3"** format.
   - **Lux rating (sensitivity)** typically **0.01 lux to 0.1 lux** for low-light cavities.

2. **Illumination Subsystem**
   - High-output **LED ring light** (preferred — solid state, long life ≥ 10,000 h).
   - Alternative: **fibre-optic light guide** coupled to an external **metal-halide or xenon arc lamp** (≥ 150 W).
   - Cool-light LEDs prevent thermal damage to polymeric linings (e.g., epoxy-coated tank walls).

3. **Push-Rod / Cable Assembly**
   - **Semi-rigid fibreglass push-rod** (for 30 m – 80 m runs) wrapped with **kevlar braid** for tensile strength.
   - Coaxial video cable (**75 Ω impedance**) carrying baseband composite video.
   - For very long runs, **fibre-optic multiplexing** is used to avoid signal attenuation.

4. **Central Control & Reel Unit**
   - Contains the **cable reel with metered counter** (measures distance inserted in metres).
   - Push-button controls for **focus, illumination intensity, pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ)**, and still capture.

5. **Display Console**
   - High-brightness **LCD / TFT monitor** of **17" – 22"** diagonal.
   - On-screen display (OSD) shows **cable distance, time, date, and operator ID**.

6. **Digital Recording Unit**
   - **DVR (Digital Video Recorder)** with **H.264 / H.265 compression**.
   - Storage on **HDD / SSD**; outputs in **MPEG-4** or **MP4** formats for archival.

## 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Principle of "Why CCTV Works":**
> Light from the illumination source falls on the internal surface → reflects with characteristic intensity → enters the camera lens → focuses on the photo-sensitive sensor array → sensor converts optical energy to an analog electrical signal (voltage proportional to luminance) → signal is amplified, encoded (PAL/NTSC), and sent via cable → monitor decodes the signal into a raster scan of pixels → human eye integrates the raster into a meaningful image.

## 2.3 Camera Categorisation (High-Yield)

| Camera Type | Locomotion | Typical Length | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Push Camera** | Manual push by operator | up to **80 m** | Straight pipelines, drains |
| **Crawler / Robotic Camera** | Motorised tractor with rubber tracks | up to **500 m** | Large sewer mains, offshore risers |
| **Pan-Tilt-Zoom (PTZ) Camera** | Motorised head with **360° azimuth** & **270° tilt** | Tethered cable | Tank floor, vessel shells |
| **Floating / Boat Camera** | Buoyancy-stabilised | Tethered | Water-filled tanks, large reservoirs |
| **Magnetically-Coupled Camera** | External magnet controls internal camera | Tethered, dual magnets | Thin-walled vessels, boiler tubes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in Part-A derivations. Memorise the symbols and units.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | FOV = Field of View (mm); d = working distance (mm); $\theta$ = lens cone angle (deg) | Determines **how much pipe wall** the camera sees at a time. |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o} = \dfrac{d_i}{d_o}$ | M = magnification; $h_i, h_o$ = image, object height; $d_i, d_o$ = image, object distance | Calculates **image scale** of a defect. |
| 3 | $\text{Res}_{\text{lim}} = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot d}{D}$ | $\text{Res}_{\text{lim}}$ = limiting resolution (mm); $\lambda$ = wavelength (mm); $D$ = lens aperture (mm) | **Rayleigh criterion** — minimum crack length visible. |
| 4 | $\text{DoF} \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot N \cdot c}{M^2}$ | DoF = Depth of Field; N = f-number; c = circle of confusion; M = magnification | **Range of distances** that appear sharp. |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{1}{L}$ in lines per mm | $f$ = spatial frequency; $L$ = line-pair width | **System MTF characterisation**. |
| 6 | $v_{\text{insp}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{pipeline}}}{T_{\text{insp}}}$ | $v$ = inspection speed; $L$ = pipe length; $T$ = inspection time | **Productivity planning** in pipeline surveys. |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{SNR}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ dB | $\eta_{\text{SNR}}$ = signal-to-noise ratio; $V_s$ = signal; $V_n$ = noise | **Image clarity metric**. |
| 8 | $P_{\text{LED}} = n \cdot I_f \cdot V_f$ | $P_{\text{LED}}$ = total LED power (W); n = number of LEDs; $I_f$ = forward current; $V_f$ = forward voltage | **Illumination design** for cavity. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View (FOV) at Pipe Wall

**Problem:** A CCTV push camera with a lens cone angle of $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ is positioned at the centre of a pipe of internal diameter $D_p = 200 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the field of view width on the pipe wall.

**Given:** $\theta = 60^{\circ}$, working distance $d = D_p / 2 = 100 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The camera sits at the centre of the pipe. The half-cone angle is $\theta/2 = 30^{\circ}$. The pipe wall lies at a perpendicular distance of $d = 100 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the standard FOV formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV} &= 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 100 \times \tan(30^{\circ}) \\
&= 200 \times 0.57735 \\
\text{FOV} &= 115.47 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**
At a pipe diameter of 200 mm, the camera covers approximately **115.5 mm** of the wall surface in a single frame. Therefore, the inspector must rotate the camera head, or rely on the camera's own **pan mechanism**, to view the remaining circumference of $C = \pi D_p = 628.32 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 4 — Coverage fraction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coverage} &= \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{C} = \dfrac{115.47}{628.32} = 0.1837 \\
\text{Coverage \%} &= 18.37\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the camera must be **rotated through at least 5 equal angular positions** to achieve full circumferential coverage (5 × 18.37% ≈ 91.85%, plus overlap = 100%).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Limiting Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion)

**Problem:** A CCTV camera uses a lens of aperture $D = 4 \text{ mm}$ with a green LED illumination of dominant wavelength $\lambda = 550 \text{ nm}$. The lens is focused at a working distance of $d = 80 \text{ mm}$. Determine the **smallest crack width** that can theoretically be resolved.

**Step 1 — Convert wavelength to millimetres (for unit consistency with $D$ and $d$).**

$$
\lambda = 550 \text{ nm} = 550 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm} = 5.50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rayleigh resolution formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Res}_{\text{lim}} &= \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot d}{D} \\
&= \dfrac{1.22 \times 5.50 \times 10^{-4} \times 80}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{0.05368}{4} \\
\text{Res}_{\text{lim}} &= 0.01342 \text{ mm} \approx 13.4 \text{ \mu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.**
The camera can theoretically distinguish features as small as **~13.4 µm**. However, this is the **diffraction-limited** value. In practical NDT, factors such as vibration, surface roughness, and electronic noise degrade this to a *realistic* limit of **0.1 mm to 0.2 mm**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth of Field (DoF)

**Problem:** A CCTV camera has magnification $M = 0.05\times$, f-number $N = 4$, and acceptable circle of confusion $c = 0.03 \text{ mm}$. Compute the depth of field.

**Step 1 — Apply the standard DoF approximation for small-magnification optics.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoF} &\approx \dfrac{2 \cdot N \cdot c}{M^2} \\
&= \dfrac{2 \times 4 \times 0.03}{(0.05)^2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.24}{0.0025} \\
\text{DoF} &= 96 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret.**
A 96 mm depth of field means that surface features within ±48 mm of the focal plane will appear acceptably sharp. This is **extremely useful** in pipes with diameter variation, weld crowns, and internal branch connections.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect Detection Capability

| Defect Type | CCTV Detectability | Detection Confidence | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Through-wall perforation** | Excellent | ≥ 95% | Light leak false positives |
| **General wall thinning** | Good (if visible bulge) | 70 – 80% | Requires visual texture change |
| **Pitting corrosion** | Excellent | ≥ 90% | Shadowing at deep pits |
| **Cracks (open)** | Good | 60 – 75% | Crack must be ≥ 0.1 mm wide |
| **Cracks (tight)** | Poor | < 30% | Below optical resolution |
| **Weld undercut** | Excellent | ≥ 90% | Direct line-of-sight needed |
| **Lamination** | Poor | < 20% | Subsurface, not visible |
| **Erosion patterns** | Excellent | ≥ 90% | Distinctive "scallop" texture |
| **Biological fouling** | Excellent | ≥ 95% | Colour contrast with bare metal |

## 3.5 Coverage Speed Derivation

**Problem:** A crawler CCTV is to inspect 1.2 km of sewer main. The crawler's average advance speed is $v = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$. Setup time at each manhole is $T_s = 25 \text{ min}$. There are 8 manholes. Calculate the total inspection time.

**Step 1 — Compute the active crawling time.**

$$
T_{\text{crawl}} = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{1200 \text{ m}}{0.15 \text{ m/s}} = 8000 \text{ s} = 133.33 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total setup time.**

$$
T_{\text{setup}} = N_{\text{mh}} \times T_s = 8 \times 25 = 200 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total inspection time.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{crawl}} + T_{\text{setup}} = 133.33 + 200 = 333.33 \text{ min} \approx 5 \text{ hours } 33 \text{ min}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NDT-CCTV System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete signal and data flow** of an industrial CCTV inspection system — from the inspection environment to the archival output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Inspection Environment]
        CAV[Internal Cavity / Pipe Wall]:::envNode
        LED[LED Ring Light Source]:::envNode
    end

    subgraph OPT[Optical Probe Assembly]
        LENS[Camera Lens with Fixed Cone Angle]:::optNode
        SENSOR[CCD or CMOS Image Sensor]:::optNode
        PTZ[Motorised Pan Tilt Zoom Head]:::optNode
    end

    subgraph CAB[Cable and Reel Unit]
        COAX[Coaxial Video Cable 75 Ohm]:::cabNode
        REEL[Cable Reel with Metred Counter]:::cabNode
        CTRL[Push Button Control Box]:::cabNode
    end

    subgraph CON[Display and Control Console]
        MON[LCD or TFT Monitor with OSD]:::conNode
        DVR[Digital Video Recorder H264 H265]:::conNode
        HDD[Hard Disk Drive for Archival]:::conNode
    end

    subgraph OUT[Post Processing and Report]
        SW[Inspection Software for Defect Mapping]:::outNode
        PDF[Final PDF Report with Snapshots]:::outNode
    end

    CAV -->|Reflected Light| LENS
    LED -->|Illumination Beam| CAV
    LENS --> SENSOR
    SENSOR -->|Analog Video Signal| COAX
    COAX --> REEL
    REEL --> MON
    MON --> DVR
    DVR --> HDD
    MON --> SW
    SW --> PDF
    CTRL -.->|Focus Zoom Tilt Commands| PTZ
    PTZ -->|Mechanical Response| LENS

    classDef envNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef optNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cabNode fill:#b8860b,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef conNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Logical Workflow of an NDT-CCTV Inspection Job

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Inspection Request] --> B[Review Site Drawings and P&ID]
    B --> C[Identify Access Points and Safety Hazards]
    C --> D[Select Camera Type Push Crawler PTZ]
    D --> E[Calibrate System Metre Counter and Focus]
    E --> F[Insert Probe and Begin Crawling]
    F --> G{Is Defect Detected}
    G -- Yes --> H[Capture Snapshot and GPS Mark]
    H --> I[Log Defect Type Size and Location]
    I --> F
    G -- No --> F
    F --> J[End of Run Reached]
    J --> K[Retrieve Probe and Clean Equipment]
    K --> L[Generate Final Inspection Report]
    L --> M[Archive Video and Report on Server]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Camera Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Pipeline Length} -->|Up to 80 m| R1[Push Camera]
    Q1 -->|80 m to 500 m| R2[Crawler or Robotic Camera]
    Q1 -->|Above 500 m| R3[Fibre Optic Tethered System]
    Q2{Environment} -->|Water Filled| S1[Floating Boat Camera]
    Q2 -->|Thin Wall Magnetic| S2[Magnetically Coupled Camera]
    Q2 -->|Large Vessel Dome| S3[PTZ Dome Camera]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "Closed Circuit Television" as applied to Non-Destructive Testing. List its main components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** CCTV in NDT is a remote visual inspection (RVI) method in which a miniature camera is introduced into an enclosed or inaccessible component and the captured image is transmitted via a closed signal cable to a remote display and recording station, allowing inspection of internal surfaces without dismantling the structure.

> **Main Components (2 Marks):** (i) Camera head with CCD/CMOS sensor, (ii) illumination source (LED ring or fibre-optic), (iii) push-rod or crawler assembly, (iv) cable reel with metred counter, (v) control unit, (vi) display monitor, (vii) digital video recorder.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a borescope and a CCTV camera in NDT applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Borescope | CCTV Camera |
|---|---|---|
| Viewing | Eyepiece (direct optical) or via fibre-optic image bundle | Electronic video display |
| Length | 1 – 8 m (semi-rigid) | Up to 300 – 500 m with cable reel |
| Image Recording | Optional camera attachment | Inherent continuous digital recording |
| Locomotion | Manual push through bends | Manual, crawler, or PTZ |
| Best for | Engine cylinders, turbine blades | Pipelines, sewer mains, large tanks |

**(1 Mark)** for correct differentiation in 3 parameters and **(2 Marks)** for tabular clarity and application accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> * Common loss-of-marks zones for CCTV questions:
>   1. **Confusing CCTV with simple borescope** — examiners allocate marks for **electronic signal transmission** specifically. State it explicitly.
>   2. **Forgetting the metred counter** — the **distance-measuring reel** is a *signature* feature of NDT-CCTV (versus general-purpose CCTV). Always mention it.
>   3. **Skipping the lighting subsection** — examiners expect **LED ring / fibre-optic** lighting engineering, especially when working inside a low-reflectivity cavity.
>   4. **No mention of standards** — referencing **ASTM E1351** (visual testing of pipelines) and **ASME Section V, Article 9** earns 1 bonus mark consistently.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a CCTV inspection system used in NDT. List at least six components with their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pipeline of internal diameter **300 mm** is to be inspected using a CCTV camera with a lens cone angle of **70°**. The camera is positioned at the centre of the pipe. Calculate the (i) field of view on the wall, (ii) the percentage of circumference visible in a single frame, and (iii) the minimum number of rotational positions required to cover 100% of the wall (allowing 10% overlap). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Already illustrated in Section 4.1. Reproduce or describe: *Light source → pipe wall → camera lens → CCD/CMOS sensor → coaxial cable → reel → monitor → DVR → archival*.

**Component Functions (4 Marks — ½ mark each × 6):**
- **Camera head** with CCD/CMOS sensor — converts optical image to electrical signal.
- **Lens** — focuses reflected light onto the sensor with a defined cone angle.
- **LED ring light** — provides cool, high-intensity, shadow-free illumination.
- **Push-rod / crawler cable** — supports and translates the camera; carries the video signal.
- **Metred reel** — measures insertion distance for defect localisation.
- **Control unit** — adjusts focus, illumination, and PTZ movements.
- **Monitor with OSD** — displays live video, distance, time, and operator ID.
- **DVR** — records inspection video in compressed digital format for archival.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D_p = 300$ mm, $\theta = 70^{\circ}$, working distance $d = D_p / 2 = 150$ mm.

**Step 1 — Field of view (2 Marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOV} &= 2 \cdot d \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 150 \times \tan(35^{\circ}) \\
&= 300 \times 0.7002 \\
\text{FOV} &= 210.06 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Circumference (1 Mark).**

$$
C = \pi \cdot D_p = \pi \times 300 = 942.48 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Coverage percentage (2 Marks).**

$$
\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{C} = \dfrac{210.06}{942.48} = 0.2229 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 22.29\%
$$

**Step 4 — Number of rotational positions with 10% overlap (2 Marks).**

Effective coverage per position = 22.29% × (1 − 0.10) = **20.06%**.

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{100\%}{20.06\%} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.98 \rceil = 5 \text{ positions}
$$

**Final Answer:** FOV = **210.06 mm**, coverage = **22.29%**, minimum positions = **5**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**(a)** Classify the various types of CCTV cameras used in NDT. Discuss the operating principle of a **self-propelled crawler camera** with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the applications, advantages, and limitations of CCTV inspection in industry. State the relevant standards governing CCTV-based NDT. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (3 Marks — ½ mark each category):**
1. Push cameras
2. Crawler / robotic cameras
3. PTZ cameras
4. Floating / boat cameras
5. Magnetically-coupled cameras
6. Articulating tractor cameras

**Crawler Camera — Operating Principle (4 Marks):**

A crawler consists of a **chassis with two pairs of rubber-treaded wheels** (or tracks) driven by **DC servo motors** housed within a waterproof stainless-steel body. The camera and LED ring are mounted on a **central turret that can pan 360° and tilt ±135°**. The crawler is connected via a **tethered cable** carrying power, control signals, and the composite video return signal. The operator uses a **joystick console** to drive forward, reverse, steer, and adjust camera orientation. Wheel diameter and pressure are chosen to **grip the pipe wall** without damaging internal coatings. Inclinometers and odometers on board provide **pitch, roll, and distance telemetry** for accurate defect mapping.

**Diagram (2 Marks):** Use the crawler subgraph from Section 4 (block diagram acceptable).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Applications (2 Marks):**
- Sewer and stormwater pipeline surveys.
- Inspection of pressure vessels, reactors, and heat-exchanger shells.
- Storage tank floor and roof inspections (without draining for roof cases).
- Offshore riser and caisson inspections.
- Pre-service and in-service weld inspection of large-diameter pipes.

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
- No need to dismantle the equipment — saves cost and downtime.
- Real-time assessment with continuous digital recording.
- Long reach (up to 500 m) and ability to negotiate bends.
- Quantitative defect sizing using calibrated still images.
- Safer than manned entry into confined spaces (no human hazard).

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
- Only **line-of-sight** defects visible — cannot see hidden surfaces.
- Image quality degrades in **turbid, oily, or fouled** fluids.
- Lighting limitations in very large cavities.
- Cannot detect **subsurface defects** (cracks below paint, laminations).
- Crawler can get **stuck or lost** in long or damaged runs.

**Standards (1 Mark):**
- **ASTM E1351** — Standard Practice for Production and Evaluation of Field Metallographic Replicas (related visual).
- **ASTM C1163** — Standard Practice for Mounting Actinide Samples for Spectral Analysis.
- **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Article 9** — Visual Examination.
- **API 570** — Piping Inspection Code.
- **NACE SP0108** — Corrosion Control of Offshore Structures by Protective Coatings (inspection section).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Closed Circuit Television (NDT):**

- **Core Idea:** CCTV in NDT = *Remote Visual Inspection* using electronic video signal transmitted over a **closed-loop coaxial cable**.
- **Key Distinction:** Unlike a borescope (optical eyepiece), CCTV **always uses electronic image acquisition + cable transmission + monitor display + digital recording**.
- **Six Subsystems:** Light source, camera head (CCD/CMOS), lens, cable + reel, control unit, display + recorder.
- **Camera Types:** Push, Crawler, PTZ, Floating, Magnetically-coupled — pick by **length, environment, and access**.
- **Signature Hardware:** **Metred cable reel** (for distance/location) and **PTZ head** (for full 360° coverage).
- **Signature Lighting:** **LED ring** (cool, solid-state) preferred over halogen/arc lamps inside enclosed cavities.
- **Key Formulae (must memorise):** FOV = $2 d \tan(\theta/2)$; Coverage % = FOV / (π D); Rayleigh resolution = 1.22 λ d / D; DoF = 2 N c / M².
- **Detection Strengths:** Perforations, pitting, weld profile, general corrosion, fouling, foreign objects.
- **Detection Weaknesses:** Sub-surface defects, tight cracks (<0.1 mm), hidden surfaces, defects behind obstructions.
- **Practical Resolution Limit:** ~**0.1 – 0.2 mm** in field conditions (theoretical Rayleigh limit is far lower).
- **Standards to Quote in Exam:** **ASTM E1351**, **ASME Section V Article 9**, **API 570**.
- **Safety Note:** CCTV **reduces human entry into confined spaces**, complying with **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146** permit-required confined-space regulations.
- **Examiner Trap:** Do **not** confuse CCTV (closed-circuit TV) with **general industrial security CCTV** — the syllabus here is specifically the **NDT inspection** application.
- **Always mention in long answers:** Distance metering, digital archival, OSD overlay, lighting engineering, and relevant ASTM/ASME/API standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Sources and Special Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Light Sources and Special Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Non-Destructive Testing (NDT), **Light Sources** refer to controlled electromagnetic radiation emitters, typically operating in the **ultraviolet (UV), visible (VIS), and infrared (IR)** bands of the spectrum, used to illuminate a test specimen so that surface and near-surface discontinuities become detectable to a human observer or a calibrated instrument. **Special Lighting** denotes the engineering selection, filtering, and arrangement of these sources—including intensity, wavelength, beam geometry, and color temperature—to optimize the contrast between a flaw (crack, porosity, laps, cold shuts, etc.) and the surrounding sound material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Light sources form the *physical enabler* of every optical NDT method. Without the correct light source, methods like **Visual Testing (VT)**, **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT)**, and **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** simply fail to produce a visible indication. The examiner *will* test whether you can match a *light source* to a *specific NDT method*.

The relevant electromagnetic spectrum bands used in NDT are:

- **UV-A (320 nm – 400 nm)** — also called *Long-wave UV* or *Black Light*. Essential for fluorescent LPT and fluorescent MPT.
- **Visible (380 nm – 780 nm)** — used for direct visual examination and white-light LPT.
- **IR (780 nm – 1 mm)** — used in thermography and some specialized surface inspection setups.

### 1.2 The Physics of Light in NDT (One-Line Truth)

> **Light in NDT is not illumination alone — it is a *contrast-enhancing tool* whose wavelength, intensity, and directivity are engineered to maximize the visibility of flaws.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a hospital emergency room at night. The doctor does not use a single bare bulb — they use a focused, high-intensity, shadow-free surgical light to make every scratch and bruise visible. If the doctor used a candle, the wound would be invisible; if they used a flashbulb, the patient would be blinded and reflections would hide detail.

**Non-destructive testing is exactly this problem, but for metals and welds:**

- The "wound" = a crack, a porosity, an undercut, an inclusion.
- The "patient" = the test specimen.
- The "surgical light" = a properly engineered NDT light source.

Special lighting in NDT is the *art and science* of choosing the correct "surgical light" — for example, a **UV-A black light at 365 nm** to make a fluorescent penetrant glow inside a fatigue crack, or a **diffused white light at 1000 lux** to visually inspect a weld bead under ASNT/IS 1182 guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A good NDT technician does not just "switch on a light." They select the *wavelength*, the *intensity (lux)*, the *angle of incidence*, the *diffusion*, and often a *filter* so that the flaw screams out from the background. This is what "Special Lighting" means in KTU Module 1.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (Highlighted)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **Wavelength–frequency relation:** $c = \lambda \nu$ (universal for all EM waves)
- **Recommended white-light intensity for VT:** **≥ 1000 lux** on the inspection surface (per **ASTM E 1106 / IS 1182** for general visual testing)
- **Recommended UV-A intensity for fluorescent LPT:** **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** (per **ASTM E 1417 / ISO 3452-1**) at the inspection surface, peak wavelength **365 nm ± 5 nm**

These metrics will recur in every KTU numerical problem on this topic — memorize them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Light Source Selection in NDT

The act of choosing a light source for an NDT application follows a structured decision chain. Understanding this chain is what KTU examiners test:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the NDT method.** Is it VT, LPT, or MPT? Each method has a default preferred wavelength band.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the indication mechanism.** Does the flaw absorb light, reflect light, fluoresce, or change polarization? The physics of the indication determines the wavelength needed.
3. **Step 3 — Identify the substrate.** Rough castings require diffused, high-intensity light; polished welds require directional, low-glare light; ferromagnetic parts may interfere with electrical lighting.
4. **Step 4 — Identify the safety/environmental envelope.** Is there explosive gas (ATEX zones), confined space, or extreme temperature? This narrows the source family.
5. **Step 5 — Quantify intensity.** Compute the required lux at the inspection surface using the inverse-square law (see Section 3).
6. **Step 6 — Apply filters and shields.** Add band-pass filters, polarizers, or shrouds to reject stray light and ambient interference.

### 2.2 Classification of NDT Light Sources (Structured Logic)

Light sources used in Module 1 are classified along four orthogonal axes — *mechanism of emission*, *wavelength band*, *beam profile*, and *power source*:

- **By Mechanism of Emission**
  - *Incandescence* — thermal radiation from a hot filament (e.g., tungsten-halogen).
  - *Luminescence* — electronic transition in a gas or phosphor (e.g., mercury vapor, fluorescent tubes, xenon arc).
  - *Electroluminescence* — photon emission from a semiconductor junction (e.g., Light Emitting Diodes, LEDs).
  - *Black-body / Radiant* — controlled high-temperature emitter for IR/UV calibration.
- **By Wavelength Band**
  - *White-light sources* (broadband 400–700 nm) — tungsten-halogen, xenon arc, white LEDs.
  - *UV-A sources* (peak ~365 nm) — mercury-vapor black light, UV-A LEDs.
  - *IR sources* — heat lamps, IR LEDs, IR lasers (for thermography).
- **By Beam Profile**
  - *Diffused* — large-area, low-glare (used for general visual inspection of large surfaces).
  - *Collimated / Directional* — high-intensity spot (used for crack detection on welds, borescopic inspection).
  - *Fibre-optic coupled* — for inspecting cavities and inside complex geometry.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Source — High-Yield Theory

**Tungsten-Halogen (Quartz-Halogen) Lamps**
- *How it works:* A tungsten filament is heated to ~3000 K inside a quartz bulb containing a halogen gas (iodine or bromine). The halogen regenerates the filament via the *halogen cycle*, giving a longer life and stable color temperature.
- *Why for NDT:* High **Color Rendering Index (CRI ≈ 100)**, continuous spectrum from UV to IR, stable intensity, ideal for white-light VT and LPT.
- *Limitation:* Generates significant heat; cannot be used near temperature-sensitive penetrants or in flammable atmospheres.

**Mercury-Vapor Low-Pressure Lamps (Black Lights)**
- *How it works:* An electric arc through low-pressure mercury vapor emits strong lines at **254 nm, 365 nm, and 405 nm**. The bulb is made of special *Wood's glass* (a nickel-oxide-doped silicate) that filters out everything except the **365 nm** line — hence the term *black light* (the lamp itself looks dark purple, but emits near-UV).
- *Why for NDT:* Provides the **365 nm UV-A peak** required for fluorescent penetrant and magnetic particle inspection.
- *Limitation:* Needs a warm-up time of 3–5 minutes; contains mercury (disposal hazard).

**Xenon Arc Lamps**
- *How it works:* A high-pressure xenon arc produces a continuous, sunlight-like spectrum.
- *Why for NDT:* Excellent for high-speed photography, stroboscopic inspection, and large-area diffuse illumination.
- *Limitation:* Very high cost, requires high-voltage ballast, intense heat.

**LED Light Sources (Modern KTU-2024 Emphasis)**
- *How it works:* Electrons recombine with holes across a semiconductor band-gap, releasing photons of energy $E_g = h\nu$.
- *Why for NDT:* Long life (>50,000 h), instant on/off, no warm-up, mechanically robust, can be tuned to specific wavelengths (e.g., **365 nm UV-A LEDs**, **white LEDs**), low-voltage DC operation, safe in explosive atmospheres.
- *Limitation:* Narrower spectrum than halogen (matters for true color-critical VT).

**Fibre-Optic Light Sources**
- *How it works:* A remote lamp (often halogen or metal-halide) is coupled through a fibre-optic bundle to deliver cool, intense, flexible illumination into borescopes, endoscopes, and inside cavities.
- *Why for NDT:* Allows inspection of internal surfaces of turbine blades, engine cylinders, and heat-exchanger tubes — areas where no other source can reach.

### 2.4 The 'How' — Special Lighting Techniques

Special lighting is not just the source itself, but the *geometry and accessories*:

- **Diffuse Illumination** — Source placed behind a translucent diffuser (ground glass) to eliminate specular highlights. Used for rough cast surfaces and to detect laps, folds, and cracks on machined parts.
- **Directional / Oblique Illumination** — Light at a low angle (10°–30°) to the surface. This *casts a shadow* inside a crack, dramatically increasing visibility. The single most important technique for VT of welds and machined surfaces.
- **Coaxial Illumination** — Light travels along the same axis as the observer's line of sight (through a half-mirror). Used in microscopes and borescopes for inspecting holes and small features.
- **Dark-Field Illumination** — Source is arranged so that direct light does *not* enter the camera/eye; only light *scattered* by the flaw reaches the observer. The flaw appears bright on a dark background — excellent for fine surface cracks.
- **Filtered / Monochromatic Illumination** — Band-pass filters isolate a specific wavelength (e.g., 365 nm for LPT or 546 nm green for crack-tip contrast enhancement).
