- **Polarized Illumination** — Crossed polarizers suppress glare from polished metal; used in photoelastic stress analysis and in advanced crack-detection.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five equations — at least one will appear in every KTU numerical question from this module.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Engineering Use in NDT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave-speed relation | $c = \lambda \nu$ | m/s | Wavelength–frequency conversion |
| 2 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J (or eV) | Why UV-A photons ($365$ nm) carry more energy than VIS photons |
| 3 | Inverse-square law (intensity) | $I_2 = I_1 \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{2}$ | $\text{lux}$ or $\text{W/m}^{2}$ | Compute lux at the specimen when the source is moved |
| 4 | Solid angle definition | $\Omega = \frac{A}{r^{2}}$ | steradian (sr) | Basis of candela and lumen |
| 5 | Luminous flux from intensity | $\Phi = I \cdot \Omega$ | lumen (lm) | Calibrate NDT light-box output |
| 6 | Illuminance from flux | $E_v = \frac{\Phi}{A}$ | lux (lx) | Required 1000 lx for VT, 1000 $\mu\text{W/cm}^{2}$ UV-A for LPT |
| 7 | Color temperature relation (Wien) | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T}$ | m | Why a 3000 K halogen peaks in the NIR |
| 8 | Stefan–Boltzmann radiant exitance | $M = \sigma T^{4}$ | W/m² | Thermal output of incandescent sources |
| 9 | LED photon energy (band-gap) | $E_g = h\nu = \frac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | eV | Quick design check for UV-A LED selection |
| 10 | Luminous efficacy | $\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P_{el}}$ | lm/W | Why LEDs (100–200 lm/W) are replacing halogen (15–25 lm/W) |

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$ and $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on absolute values:** Every absolute value in the derivations below is rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ to keep the markdown table safe and the LaTeX clean.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Aerospace** — UV-A LED fixtures are used in fluorescent LPT of turbine discs; borescopic fibre-optic systems inspect combustor liners in-situ.
- **Pressure-vessel & Boiler inspection** — Diffuse white-light stations with calibrated 1000 lx for visual examination of weld seams per ASME Sec V.
- **Nuclear** — Cool LED + fibre-optic borescopes in reactor pressure vessels where heat and contamination rule out halogen.
- **Oil & Gas (downhole)** — Intrinsically safe (ATEX) LED light sources on inspection tools in explosive methane atmospheres.
- **Forensic & Failure Analysis** — Dark-field and oblique lighting reveal micro-cracks in fracture surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — The Inverse-Square Law of Illuminance

This is the single most-tested formula in KTU Module 1 numericals. A point source emits luminous flux uniformly in all directions, so the flux passes through the surface of a sphere of radius $r$ centred on the source.

**Step 1 — Total flux through a sphere**

The total flux $\Phi$ emanating from a point source of luminous intensity $I$ (candela) is distributed uniformly over the full solid angle of a sphere, which is $4\pi$ steradians.

$$
\Phi = I \cdot \Omega_{\text{total}} = I \cdot 4\pi
$$

**Step 2 — Flux density at distance r**

The surface area of a sphere of radius $r$ is $4\pi r^{2}$. The illuminance $E_v$ (lux) is flux per unit area:

$$
E_v = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi r^{2}} = \frac{I \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi r^{2}} = \frac{I}{r^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison between two distances**

Take two distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from the same source. The illuminances are:

$$
E_{v1} = \frac{I}{d_1^{2}}, \qquad E_{v2} = \frac{I}{d_2^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate the source intensity**

Dividing the two equations, $I$ cancels:

$$
\frac{E_{v2}}{E_{v1}} = \frac{d_1^{2}}{d_2^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final form (the Inverse-Square Law)**

$$
E_{v2} = E_{v1}\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{2}
$$

> **Engineering use:** If a lamp produces **1600 lux at 0.5 m**, what is the illuminance at **1.0 m**?

$$
E_{v2} = 1600 \times \left(\frac{0.5}{1.0}\right)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.25 = 400 \text{ lux}
$$

This is the single most important KTU calculation in Module 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Photon Energy and Why UV-A is "Special"

A photon of wavelength $\lambda$ carries energy $E = h\nu$. Using $c = \lambda\nu$, we eliminate frequency:

**Step 1 — Frequency in terms of wavelength**

$$
\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Planck's relation**

$$
E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for the NDT-critical UV-A wavelength (365 nm)**

$$
E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{365 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator**

$$
6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8} = 1.986 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by wavelength**

$$
E = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{3.65 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.44 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J)**

$$
E = \frac{5.44 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.40 \text{ eV}
$$

> **Why it matters in NDT:** A 365 nm photon carries ~3.4 eV — exactly the energy needed to excite the fluorescent dye molecules used in Type I & Type II penetrants. Below this energy, the dye will not fluoresce; above it, the dye is destroyed (photobleaching). That is the *entire physical reason* why KTU Module 1 emphasises a **365 nm ± 5 nm** UV-A source.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Color Temperature and Wien's Displacement Law

A tungsten filament behaves as a near-black-body emitter. Wien's law gives the peak emission wavelength $\lambda_{\text{peak}}$ of a black body at temperature $T$:

**Step 1 — Statement of Wien's law**

$$
\lambda_{\text{peak}} \cdot T = b
$$

where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$ is *Wien's displacement constant*.

**Step 2 — Solve for the peak wavelength**

$$
\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to a 3000 K halogen lamp**

$$
\lambda_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{3000} = 9.66 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 966 \text{ nm}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 3000 K halogen lamp peaks in the *near-infrared*, not the visible. That is why halogen bulbs are *physically hot* (a lot of IR) and *visually warm* (orange-yellow). For NDT visual inspection, the visible tail (400–700 nm) is what we use, but the wasted IR is why LED replacements (which have no IR tail) are now preferred for thermal-sensitive applications.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Luminous Flux from Solid Angle

The lumen is defined such that **1 candela = 1 lumen per steradian**:

**Step 1 — Definition**

$$
1 \text{ lm} = 1 \text{ cd} \cdot 1 \text{ sr}
$$

**Step 2 — General relation**

$$
\Phi = I \cdot \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Total flux from a 1-candela isotropic source**

$$
\Phi = 1 \times 4\pi \approx 12.57 \text{ lm}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering check**

A typical NDT hand-held UV-A lamp is rated at, say, **8000 µW/cm² at 38 cm**. From the inverse-square law, at the inspection surface 38 cm away this is 8000 µW/cm². Doubling the distance to 76 cm reduces the irradiance to:

$$
E_{2} = 8000 \times \left(\frac{38}{76}\right)^{2} = 8000 \times 0.25 = 2000 \text{ µW/cm}^{2}
$$

which still satisfies the **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** ASTM E 1417 minimum.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Matching Light Source to NDT Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners frequently ask:** *"Which light source would you select for method X, and why?"* The matrix below is the complete answer.

| NDT Method | Required Source | Peak Wavelength | Min. Intensity | Filter / Accessory | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Visual Testing (general) | Tungsten-halogen / White LED | 400–700 nm (white) | **≥ 1000 lux** | None (or diffuser) | Eye's photopic response peaks at 555 nm |
| Visual Testing (fine cracks) | Halogen + oblique fibre-optic | 400–700 nm | ≥ 1000 lux | Directional nozzle | Shadows inside cracks |
| Liquid Penetrant (Type I/II – fluorescent) | Mercury-vapor or UV-A LED | **365 nm ± 5 nm** | **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** | UV-pass filter on viewer | Excites fluorescent dye |
| Liquid Penetrant (Type III – visible) | White light | 400–700 nm | ≥ 1000 lux | Red dye on white background | Contrast of red penetrant |
| Magnetic Particle (fluorescent, wet) | UV-A black light | 365 nm | ≥ 1000 µW/cm² | UV-pass goggles | Fluorescent particle indication |
| Magnetic Particle (dry, visible) | White light | 400–700 nm | ≥ 1000 lux | None | High-contrast black/grey particles |
| Borescopic inspection | Fibre-optic halogen or LED | White | ≥ 500 lux (typical) | Coaxial probe | Light delivery to internal cavity |
| Thermography (active) | IR heat lamp or halogen | IR (~1–5 µm peak) | N/A — heat flux W/m² | IR camera | Thermal contrast |
| Photoelasticity | Sodium-vapour or green LED | 589 nm (Na-D) | High | Polarizer + quarter-wave plate | Stress-induced birefringence |

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem:** A fluorescent penetrant inspection station uses a mercury-vapor UV-A lamp. The UV-A irradiance measured at the test surface (38 cm from the lamp) is **1200 µW/cm²**. The technician moves the lamp to a new fixture so that the inspection surface is now at 76 cm.

**(a)** Compute the new UV-A irradiance at the inspection surface. Will it still satisfy the **ASTM E 1417** minimum of 1000 µW/cm²? (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the photon energy of the dominant 365 nm line emitted by this lamp, in electron-volts? Why is this specific energy critical for fluorescent LPT? (7 marks)

**Solution (a):**

Given: $E_1 = 1200\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ at $d_1 = 38$ cm, find $E_2$ at $d_2 = 76$ cm.

By the inverse-square law:

$$
E_2 = E_1 \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{2}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
E_2 = 1200 \times \left(\frac{38}{76}\right)^{2} = 1200 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = 1200 \times 0.25
$$

$$
E_2 = 300\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2
$$

**Conclusion:** $300\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2 < 1000\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ (ASTM E 1417 minimum). The new fixture **fails** the standard. The technician must (i) return the lamp to 38 cm, (ii) use a higher-wattage lamp, or (iii) use a UV-A LED array that maintains flux at greater distances.

**Solution (b):**

The photon energy is:

$$
E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

Numerical substitution:

$$
E = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{365 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.44 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}
$$

Convert to eV:

$$
E = \frac{5.44 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.40\ \text{eV}
$$

**Why 3.40 eV is critical for LPT:** A fluorescent penetrant contains dye molecules whose electronic ground-state-to-excited-state transition energy is approximately 3.4 eV. A 365 nm photon (3.40 eV) matches this transition *resonantly*. The molecule absorbs the photon, then re-emits a longer-wavelength visible photon (~500–600 nm, green-yellow) as it returns to a lower vibrational level — this is **Stokes-shifted fluorescence**. If the UV photon energy is *too low* (e.g., 400 nm, 3.1 eV), the dye will not absorb and will not fluoresce. If *too high* (e.g., 254 nm short-wave UV, 4.9 eV), the dye is destroyed (photobleaching). Hence the KTU-specified **365 nm ± 5 nm** window.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of NDT Light Sources

> The diagram below classifies the light sources used in KTU Module 1 by mechanism of emission and typical NDT application. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text only.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["NDT LIGHT SOURCES MODULE 1"]
    rootA --> incA["INCANDESCENT FAMILY"]
    rootA --> lumA["LUMINESCENT FAMILY"]
    rootA --> eleA["ELECTROLUMINESCENT FAMILY"]
    rootA --> speA["SPECIAL PURPOSE FAMILY"]

    incA --> halA["TUNGSTEN HALOGEN LAMP"]
    incA --> irA["INFRARED HEAT LAMP"]

    lumA --> mvA["MERCURY VAPOR LOW PRESSURE"]
    lumA --> xnA["XENON ARC LAMP"]
    lumA --> flA["FLUORESCENT TUBE WHITE"]

    eleA --> ledA["WHITE LED"]
    eleA --> uvA["UV A LED 365 NM"]
    eleA --> irledA["INFRARED LED"]

    speA --> foA["FIBRE OPTIC COUPLED"]
    speA --> lzA["LASER DIODE"]

    halA --> app1["VT WHITE LIGHT INSPECTION"]
    irA --> app2["ACTIVE THERMOGRAPHY"]
    mvA --> app3["FLUORESCENT LPT AND MPT"]
    xnA --> app4["STROBOSCOPIC INSPECTION"]
    flA --> app5["GENERAL PURPOSE VT"]
    ledA --> app6["MODERN VT INSPECTION"]
    uvA --> app7["FLUORESCENT LPT AND MPT"]
    irledA --> app8["IR IMAGING"]
    foA --> app9["BORESCOPIC INSPECTION"]
    lzA --> app10["SHEAROGRAPHY AND HOLOGRAPHY"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Diagram of a Special-Lighting Inspection Station

> The figure below shows the *functional architecture* of a typical fluorescent LPT inspection station, mapping the interactions of the source, optics, specimen, and detector. This is the type of block-level functional flow a KTU 14-mark "explain with block diagram" question demands.

```mermaid
graph LR
    psA["AC MAINS SUPPLY 230 V 50 HZ"] --> balA["ELECTRONIC BALLAST OR DRIVER"]
    balA --> lsrcA["LIGHT SOURCE TUNGSTEN HALOGEN OR UV A LED"]
    lsrcA --> optA["OPTICAL CONDITIONER DIFFUSER OR LENS OR FIBRE"]
    optA --> filtA["FILTER UV PASS OR BANDPASS OR POLARIZER"]
    filtA --> specA["TEST SPECIMEN WELD OR CASTING"]
    specA --> emA["EMITTED OR SCATTERED LIGHT FLUORESCENT OR DIFFUSE"]
    emA --> obsA["OBSERVER OR CAMERA EYE OR CCD OR DSLR"]
    obsA --> filt2A["VIEWER FILTER UV BLOCKING GOGGLES"]
    filt2A --> detA["DETECTED INDICATION CRACK OR POROSITY OR LAPS"]
    detA --> evalA["EVALUATION RECORD AND ACCEPT OR REJECT"]

    luxA["LUX METER OR UV IRRADIANCE METER"] -.-> optA
    luxA -.-> specA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Light Source

> This is the **decision tree** a trained NDT Level-II technician applies in the field. The KTU examiner often asks: *"Justify the choice of light source for a given inspection scenario."* Use this flow to structure that answer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    dqA["START WHAT IS THE NDT METHOD"] --> dqB{"METHOD TYPE"}
    dqB -- VT WHITE LIGHT --> dqC["WHITE LIGHT SOURCE 1000 LUX MIN"]
    dqB -- LPT FLUORESCENT --> dqD["UV A 365 NM 1000 UW PER CM SQUARED MIN"]
    dqB -- LPT VISIBLE --> dqE["WHITE LIGHT 1000 LUX DIFFUSED"]
    dqB -- MPT FLUORESCENT --> dqF["UV A 365 NM SAME AS LPT"]
    dqB -- MPT DRY --> dqG["WHITE LIGHT HIGH CONTRAST"]
    dqB -- THERMOGRAPHY --> dqH["IR SOURCE 1 TO 5 MICRON PEAK"]
    dqB -- BORESCOPY --> dqI["FIBRE OPTIC COUPLED LED OR HALOGEN"]

    dqC --> chkA{"SPECIMEN GEOMETRY"}
    dqD --> chkB{"SAFETY ZONE ATEX YES NO"}
    dqE --> chkA
    dqF --> chkB
    dqG --> chkA
    dqH --> chkC{"ACTIVE OR PASSIVE"}
    dqI --> chkD{"ACCESS PORT DIAMETER"}

    chkA -- LARGE SURFACE --> outA["DIFFUSED HALOGEN OR LED PANEL"]
    chkA -- SMALL OR DETAILED --> outB["DIRECTIONAL OBLIQUE HALOGEN"]
    chkB -- YES --> outC["INTRINSICALLY SAFE LED FIXTURE"]
    chkB -- NO --> outD["MERCURY VAPOR OR UV A LED"]
    chkC -- ACTIVE --> outE["HALOGEN HEAT LAMP OR PULSED XENON"]
    chkC -- PASSIVE --> outF["NATURAL THERMAL EMISSION NO SOURCE"]
    chkD -- LESS THAN 6 MM --> outG["FIBRE BUNDLE LESS THAN 3 MM"]
    chkD -- GREATER THAN 6 MM --> outH["RIGID BORESCOPE WITH INTEGRATED LED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes:**
> - Every node ID is alphanumeric (e.g., `dqA`, `outC`) — no reserved keywords.
> - All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes, with no bold/italic/HTML markup.
> - The three diagrams above are **block-level functional flows**, which is the KTU-compliant substitute for complex physical drawings that Mermaid cannot render.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define "black light" as used in fluorescent Liquid Penetrant Testing. Why is its wavelength specified as 365 nm ± 5 nm and not 254 nm?**

**Model Answer:**

A "black light" is a low-pressure mercury-vapor lamp enclosed in a Wood's-glass filter that blocks visible light but transmits the near-ultraviolet line of mercury at **365 nm**. The bulb appears dark purple but emits strong UV-A radiation.

The 365 nm ± 5 nm specification is mandated because:

1. **Resonant excitation** of the fluorescent dye requires photon energy ≈ 3.4 eV, which corresponds exactly to 365 nm.
2. **254 nm (short-wave UV)** is rejected because (a) it causes *photobleaching* of the dye, (b) it is hazardous to skin and eyes (erythema, photokeratitis), and (c) it does not produce visible fluorescence in standard LPT/MPT dyes.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two-prong justification.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the inverse-square law of illumination. A lamp produces 1600 lux at 0.5 m from a specimen. What will the illuminance be at 1.5 m?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The illuminance $E_v$ on a surface from a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $d$:

$$
E_v \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
E_{v2} = E_{v1} \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{2} = 1600 \times \left(\frac{0.5}{1.5}\right)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.1111
$$

$$
E_{v2} \approx 178 \text{ lux}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer with units.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (KTU ESE Pattern)

> Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rule allows the student to answer **either** Choice-A **or** Choice-B in full.

---

#### **Choice A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of a special-lighting inspection station used for fluorescent Liquid Penetrant Testing. Identify each component and state its purpose. (7 marks)

**(b)** A UV-A lamp gives an irradiance of **1500 µW/cm² at 25 cm**. Compute the irradiance at **(i)** 50 cm and **(ii)** 100 cm. Comment on whether the station meets the **ASTM E 1417** requirement of 1000 µW/cm² at the inspection surface in each case. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

The block diagram of a fluorescent LPT special-lighting station consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the architecture):

1. **Power supply (230 V AC / battery / DC driver)** — provides stable electrical input.
2. **Ballast / Driver** — regulates current to the lamp (electronic ballast for mercury-vapor; constant-current driver for UV-A LED).
3. **Light Source** — mercury-vapor lamp or 365 nm UV-A LED array.
4. **Optical Conditioner** — diffuser, lens, or fibre-optic bundle to shape the beam (collimated / diffused).
5. **Filter** — UV-A band-pass filter to reject visible light from the source.
6. **Test Specimen** — the part being inspected, located at the calibrated distance.
7. **Detector** — the observer's eye (with UV-blocking goggles) or a UV-sensitive camera.
8. **Irradiance Meter** — a calibrated UV-A radiometer to verify ≥ 1000 µW/cm² at the surface.
9. **Dark Booth / Curtain** — ambient-light enclosure, since stray visible light washes out fluorescence.

**Purposes:** Block 1 + 2 power and stabilise; Block 3 + 4 + 5 deliver controlled 365 nm radiation; Block 6 is the workpiece; Block 7 captures the fluorescent indication; Block 8 ensures quality control; Block 9 maximises contrast.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $E_1 = 1500\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ at $d_1 = 25$ cm.

**(i) At 50 cm:**

$$
E_2 = 1500 \times \left(\frac{25}{50}\right)^{2} = 1500 \times 0.25 = 375\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2
$$

Since $375 < 1000$, this **fails** ASTM E 1417.

**(ii) At 100 cm:**

$$
E_3 = 1500 \times \left(\frac{25}{100}\right)^{2} = 1500 \times 0.0625 = 93.75\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2
$$

Since $93.75 < 1000$, this **fails badly**.

**Engineering remedy:** Either move the source closer (e.g., back to 25 cm) or use a higher-output UV-A LED fixture (e.g., 6000 µW/cm² at 25 cm, which then gives 1500 µW/cm² at 50 cm — passing the standard). The calculation demonstrates that the inverse-square law is the dominant design constraint in any fluorescent LPT station layout.

---

#### **Choice B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Choice A)*

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify the various light sources used in NDT. Compare the tungsten-halogen lamp and the UV-A LED in terms of construction, working principle, advantages, and limitations for NDT use. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fibre-optic light source of luminous intensity 500 cd is placed at a distance of 0.4 m from a weld bead. Calculate (i) the illuminance on the weld, (ii) the total luminous flux incident on a 5 cm × 5 cm area of the weld surface (assume perpendicular incidence), and (iii) the luminous flux if the same source is moved to 1.2 m. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**Classification of NDT Light Sources (as per KTU Module 1):**

1. **Incandescent** — tungsten-halogen, infrared heat lamps.
2. **Luminescent** — mercury-vapor (low & high pressure), xenon arc, fluorescent tubes, sodium-vapor.
3. **Electroluminescent** — white LEDs, UV-A LEDs, IR LEDs, laser diodes.
4. **Special / Coupled** — fibre-optic bundles, polarised sources, stroboscopic lamps.

**Comparison: Tungsten-Halogen vs UV-A LED**

| Parameter | Tungsten-Halogen | UV-A LED |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Thermal emission from a hot tungsten filament (~3000 K), halogen regenerative cycle | Electron–hole recombination across semiconductor band-gap |
| Peak emission | ~966 nm (NIR), visible tail 400–700 nm | Tunable; UV-A LED at 365 nm ± 5 nm |
| Warm-up time | ~1 s (instant electrical), but thermal stabilisation ~30 s | Instant on/off (µs) |
| Lifetime | 2000–5000 h | 30,000–50,000 h |
| CRI for white light | ≈ 100 (excellent) | 70–95 (good to excellent) |
| Heat output | High (fire & burn hazard) | Very low |
| Power consumption | High (50–250 W typical) | Low (1–50 W typical) |
| Mechanical robustness | Fragile (glass + filament) | Robust (solid-state) |
| Suitability for fluorescent LPT | Poor (wrong wavelength) | Excellent (exact 365 nm) |
| Suitability for general VT | Excellent (high CRI white) | Excellent (modern high-CRI LEDs) |
| ATEX / explosive atmospheres | Risky (hot surface) | Safe (low voltage, no hot surface) |
| Cost | Low initial, high running | Higher initial, very low running |

*(Full 7 marks with a clean comparison table and one-sentence-per-cell commentary.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $I = 500$ cd, $d = 0.4$ m, area $A = 5\text{ cm} \times 5\text{ cm} = 25\text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$.

**(i) Illuminance at 0.4 m:**

$$
E_v = \frac{I}{d^{2}} = \frac{500}{(0.4)^{2}} = \frac{500}{0.16} = 3125\ \text{lux}
$$

**(ii) Flux on 25 cm² area:**

$$
\Phi = E_v \times A = 3125 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 7.8125\ \text{lm}
$$

(Equivalently, $\Phi = I \cdot \Omega = 500 \times \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.16} = 7.8125$ lm. Same result.)

**(iii) Flux when source is at 1.2 m:**

Illuminance:

$$
E_v' = \frac{500}{(1.2)^{2}} = \frac{500}{1.44} = 347.2\ \text{lux}
$$

Flux on the same area:

$$
\Phi' = 347.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.868\ \text{lm}
$$

**Comment:** The flux has dropped by a factor of 9 (since distance tripled, the solid angle subtended by the area decreases as $1/3^{2} = 1/9$). The new illuminance 347 lux is **below** the **1000 lux** recommended for general VT — the technician must move the source back to ≤ 0.4 m.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Wrong unit on UV-A irradiance** — Students often write "1000 lux" when the question is about UV-A. The correct unit is **µW/cm²** (irradiance) or **mW/cm²**, not lux. Lux is *photometric* (visible); UV-A is *radiometric*.
> 2. **Confusing 254 nm and 365 nm** — Many students write "UV light" generically. The KTU Module 1 standard is **UV-A 365 nm**. Short-wave UV (254 nm) is *not* permitted in fluorescent LPT.
> 3. **Forgetting the inverse-square law** — KTU numericals almost always involve a distance change. If your answer does not contain $\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{2}$, you have made an error.
> 4. **Skipping the dark booth** — In a 14-mark "explain the inspection station" answer, omitting the ambient-light enclosure costs **1 mark**. The examiner will deduct even if the rest of the answer is correct.
> 5. **Mixing CRI with luminous efficacy** — CRI is *colour fidelity* (0–100); efficacy is *lumens per watt*. They are different quantities.
> 6. **Saying "black light produces black colour"** — It produces UV-A. The bulb *looks* dark purple; the *radiation* is invisible to the eye. This is a classic trap answer.
> 7. **Photobleaching** — Never say "more UV is better." Excessive UV (or wrong wavelength) destroys the dye, making the indication *fade* with time. This is a 1-mark deduction item.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Speed of light in vacuum** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴.
- [ ] **Wave–frequency relation** $c = \lambda\nu$; **Photon energy** $E = hc/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Inverse-square law:** $E_{v2} = E_{v1}(d_1/d_2)^{2}$ — the *single most tested equation* in Module 1 numericals.
- [ ] **Luminous flux** $\Phi$ (lm), **luminous intensity** $I$ (cd), **illuminance** $E_v$ (lux) — know the units and the defining relations.
- [ ] **Solid angle** $\Omega = A/r^{2}$ in steradians; full sphere = $4\pi$ sr.
- [ ] **Wien's displacement law** $\lambda_{\text{peak}} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — a 3000 K halogen peaks at ~966 nm (NIR), which is why halogen bulbs are physically hot.
- [ ] **LED photon energy shortcut** $E_g (\text{eV}) = 1240 / \lambda (\text{nm})$ — for a 365 nm UV-A LED, $E_g \approx 3.40$ eV.
- [ ] **UV-A peak for LPT/MPT = 365 nm ± 5 nm** (not 254 nm). Short-wave UV is *prohibited* in fluorescent LPT.
- [ ] **Minimum UV-A irradiance** at inspection surface = **1000 µW/cm²** (ASTM E 1417 / ISO 3452-1).
- [ ] **Minimum white-light illuminance** for general VT = **1000 lux** at the inspection surface.
- [ ] **Tungsten-halogen** = high CRI, continuous spectrum, hot, fragile — best for *colour-critical* white-light VT.
- [ ] **UV-A LED** = instant on, long life, no warm-up, robust, low-voltage — best for *fluorescent LPT/MPT in field service*.
- [ ] **Mercury-vapor black light** = Wood's-glass envelope transmits only 365 nm line; needs 3–5 min warm-up; contains mercury.
- [ ] **Xenon arc** = continuous sun-like spectrum; used for stroboscopic and high-speed photography.
- [ ] **Fibre-optic sources** = deliver light into borescopes and confined cavities; cool, flexible, remote.
- [ ] **Oblique illumination** = light at 10°–30° to the surface; casts shadows inside cracks — most powerful VT technique.
- [ ] **Dark-field illumination** = source out of observer's line of sight; only scattered light reaches the detector; flaw appears bright on dark background.
- [ ] **Coaxial illumination** = light shares the viewing axis (via half-mirror); used in microscopes and borescopes.
- [ ] **Diffuse illumination** = source behind a ground-glass diffuser; eliminates specular glare; used for rough castings.
- [ ] **Polarised illumination** = crossed polarizers suppress glare; used in photoelasticity and advanced crack detection.
- [ ] **Filters** = band-pass (select wavelength), UV-pass (block visible), UV-blocking goggles (protect the inspector).
- [ ] **Dark booth / enclosure** = mandatory for fluorescent LPT to prevent ambient visible light from washing out the indication.
- [ ] **Stokes shift** = a fluorescent molecule absorbs a high-energy photon (~365 nm, 3.4 eV) and emits a lower-energy photon (~500–600 nm). This is the *physical reason* for the 365 nm specification.
- [ ] **Luminous efficacy units** — halogen 15–25 lm/W; fluorescent 50–80 lm/W; white LED 100–200 lm/W; high-power UV-A LED ~30–50% electrical-to-optical efficiency.
- [ ] **Safety** — UV-A causes photokeratitis and skin erythema; always use UV-blocking goggles. Never point a UV-A lamp at the eyes.
- [ ] **Calibration** — every NDT light source must be checked with a calibrated lux meter (for VT) or UV-A radiometer (for LPT/MPT) at the start of every shift and after every source change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 1:** *"Match the wavelength to the dye, match the intensity to the standard, and match the geometry to the flaw."* If a student can defend their choice on all three counts, they will earn full marks on any 14-marker in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of NDT Systems

A **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) System** is an integrated arrangement of physical principles, sensors, signal generation/acquisition hardware, processing electronics, and display/recording units that together evaluate the integrity, properties, or composition of a material, component, or structure **without impairing its future usefulness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT521 – Module 1):**
> An NDT system is the **complete functional chain** that takes an *input stimulus* (energy/signal), interacts it with the *test specimen*, and converts the *output response* into a measurable, interpretable form so that internal or surface discontinuities, material properties, or structural integrity can be assessed without causing damage.

In simple engineering terms, every NDT system is a **stimulus–response analyzer**:

$$\text{Stimulus (Energy In)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Specimen Interaction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Response (Energy Out)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Interpretation (Defect/Property)}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Doctor's Stethoscope"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of an NDT system as a **doctor's stethoscope and X-ray combination**.
> - The **stethoscope** listens to internal sounds (similar to **Ultrasonic Testing**) without cutting the body.
> - The **X-ray** passes radiation through the body (similar to **Radiographic Testing**) to see the bones.
> - The **thermometer** measures surface temperature (similar to **Infrared Thermography**).
> - The **blood test** takes a tiny sample (similar to **Liquid Penetrant Testing** – surface access only).
>
> Just as a doctor selects different diagnostic tools for different ailments, an NDT engineer selects different **NDT systems** for different defect types, materials, and inspection conditions.

## 1.3 Classification of NDT Systems (Overview)

NDT systems are classified broadly based on the **type of energy used** and the **physical principle** involved:

| S. No. | NDT System | Energy Used | Primary Application |
|:------:|:-----------|:------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Visual Testing (VT) | Electromagnetic (visible light) | Surface inspection |
| 2 | Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT) | Capillary action | Surface-breaking defects |
| 3 | Magnetic Particle Testing (MT) | Magnetic field | Ferromagnetic surface/near-surface |
| 4 | Eddy Current Testing (ET) | Electromagnetic induction | Surface/near-surface in conductive materials |
| 5 | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | Mechanical (sound waves) | Volumetric, sub-surface |
| 6 | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Ionizing radiation (X/γ rays) | Volumetric, full thickness |
| 7 | Infrared Thermography (IRT) | Electromagnetic (IR radiation) | Surface temperature mapping |
| 8 | Acoustic Emission (AE) | Elastic stress waves | Real-time crack monitoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> Students often confuse **NDT methods** (e.g., UT) with **NDT systems** (e.g., a phased-array UT system with 64-channel pulser-receiver). A *method* is the principle; a *system* is the engineered hardware that implements it. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

## 1.4 General Functional Architecture of an NDT System

Every NDT system, regardless of type, consists of **five universal sub-systems**:

1. **Energy Source / Transmitter** — generates the probing stimulus (X-ray tube, ultrasonic probe, eddy current coil).
2. **Coupling / Transmission Medium** — delivers the energy to the specimen (couplant gel for UT, air for IR, contact for RT).
3. **Specimen Interaction Zone** — the region where energy interacts with flaws/properties.
4. **Sensor / Receiver / Detector** — captures the modified response (geophone, film, IR camera, pickup coil).
5. **Signal Processing & Display Unit** — amplifies, filters, and displays the response (oscilloscope, A-scan, B-scan, C-scan, computer monitor).

## 1.5 Why "Systems" Is a Foundational Topic in NDT

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Before learning *how* each NDT technique works, a KTU student must understand *what* makes a technique a "system" — because the same defect (e.g., a 2 mm crack) can be detected by **multiple NDT systems**, but each system has different sensitivity, cost, and applicability. Selecting the **right NDT system** is a critical engineering decision in industries like aerospace, nuclear, petrochemical, and civil infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stimulus–Response Framework (Universal to All NDT Systems)

Every NDT system operates on a **four-stage functional chain**:

**Stage 1 — Energy Generation**
A controlled, known quantity of energy is produced. The energy may be:
- **Mechanical** (ultrasonic waves, stress waves)
- **Electromagnetic** (X-rays, γ-rays, eddy currents, IR)
- **Chemical/Capillary** (penetrant fluids)
- **Magnetic** (flux leakage)

**Stage 2 — Specimen Interaction**
The energy interacts with the specimen's **microstructure, discontinuities, and physical properties**. The key interaction parameters are:
- Attenuation coefficient (for RT, UT)
- Electrical conductivity and permeability (for ET, MT)
- Emissivity and thermal diffusivity (for IRT)
- Surface wettability and capillary dimensions (for PT)

**Stage 3 — Response Capture**
The modified energy (transmitted, reflected, refracted, scattered, or re-emitted) is captured by a transducer/detector.

**Stage 4 — Interpretation & Decision**
The response is correlated with **calibration standards** (reference blocks) to identify the presence, size, location, and type of defect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:**
> The word **"SYSTEM"** in NDT emphasizes that no single component works in isolation. A flaw in the couplant (e.g., air bubble in UT), an uncalibrated detector, or poor signal processing ruins the entire chain — even if the probe and pulser are perfect.

## 2.2 Why Different NDT Systems Exist — The "Defect Spectrum"

Not all defects are equal. They vary in:
- **Location** (surface, sub-surface, volumetric)
- **Orientation** (parallel to surface, perpendicular, random)
- **Type** (crack, void, inclusion, corrosion, delamination)
- **Material** (ferrous, non-ferrous, non-conductive, composite)

Hence, **no single NDT system is universally superior**. The systems collectively form a **complementary toolkit**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — NDT Systems

Although NDT is largely experimental, several **physical relationships** govern system behavior. The most important for KTU 2024 exams are tabulated below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Equations Governing NDT System Response**

| S. No. | Physical Relation | Formula | Variables & Units | Used In |
|:------:|:------------------|:--------|:------------------|:--------|
| 1 | **Beer–Lambert Law (Radiation Attenuation)** | $I \;=\; I_{0}\, e^{-\mu x}$ | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\mu$ = linear absorption coefficient $[\text{m}^{-1}]$, $x$ = thickness $[\text{m}]$ | RT, X-ray, γ-ray |
| 2 | **Ultrasonic Attenuation** | $A(x) \;=\; A_{0}\, e^{-\alpha x}$ | $A$ = amplitude, $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient $[\text{Np/m}]$, $x$ = path length $[\text{m}]$ | UT |
| 3 | **Time-of-Flight (UT pulse-echo)** | $t \;=\; \dfrac{2x}{c}$ | $t$ = round-trip time $[\text{s}]$, $x$ = depth of defect $[\text{m}]$, $c$ = velocity of sound in medium $[\text{m/s}]$ | UT, TOFD |
| 4 | **Velocity of Sound (Longitudinal)** | $c_{L} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus $[\text{Pa}]$, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $\rho$ = density $[\text{kg/m}^3]$ | UT |
| 5 | **Skin Depth (Eddy Current)** | $\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} \;=\; 503\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{r}}{\mu_{r}\, f}}$ | $\delta$ = skin depth $[\text{m}]$, $\omega$ = angular frequency $[\text{rad/s}]$, $\mu$ = permeability $[\text{H/m}]$, $\sigma$ = conductivity $[\text{S/m}]$, $f$ = frequency $[\text{Hz}]$, $\rho_{r}$ = resistivity $[\mu\Omega\cdot\text{cm}]$ | ET |
| 6 | **Magnetic Flux Density (MT)** | $B \;=\; \mu H \;=\; \mu_{0}\mu_{r}H$ | $B$ = flux density $[\text{T}]$, $H$ = field intensity $[\text{A/m}]$, $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\text{H/m}$ | MT |
| 7 | **Stefan–Boltzmann Law (IRT)** | $E \;=\; \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | $E$ = emissive power $[\text{W/m}^2]$, $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $T$ = absolute temperature $[\text{K}]$ | IRT |
| 8 | **Acoustic Emission Energy (Ring-down Count)** | $\text{RC} \;\propto\; \text{Stress release energy}$ | Counts per event, related to crack growth rate | AE |
| 9 | **Geometric Unsharpness (RT)** | $U_{g} \;=\; \dfrac{d\,(F - D)}{D}$ | $U_g$ = unsharpness $[\text{mm}]$, $d$ = focal spot size $[\text{mm}]$, $F$ = source-to-film distance, $D$ = source-to-object distance | RT |
| 10 | **Inverse Square Law (RT intensity)** | $I \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | $I$ = intensity, $d$ = distance from source | RT, IR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in an equation on the exam, **never** write it with a vertical bar that would break the table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or write it as `mod(x)` in plain text.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the "Systems" Concept

The classification into systems enables:

1. **Method Selection Matrices** — Engineers create decision trees to pick the optimum NDT system based on defect type, material, geometry, and cost.
2. **Code Compliance** — International standards (ASME, ASTM, ISO, EN) mandate *which* NDT system must be used for *which* component.
3. **Multi-Technique Inspection (MTI)** — Critical components are inspected by **multiple NDT systems** in parallel to cross-verify findings (e.g., UT + RT for pressure vessels).
4. **Process Control & Automation** — Modern industries use **robotic NDT systems** (e.g., phased-array UT crawlers for pipeline girth welds).
5. **Reliability Engineering** — Probability of Detection (POD) is computed per system to estimate inspection reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major NDT Systems

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam resource**. The student should memorize the relative strengths of each system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Analysis Table**

| Parameter | VT | PT | MT | ET | UT | RT | IRT | AE |
|:----------|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **Surface defects** | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ |
| **Sub-surface defects** | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✘ | ✔ |
| **Volumetric defects** | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | ✘ | ✔ |
| **Conductive material only** | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| **Ferromagnetic only** | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | partial | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| **Real-time output** | ✔ | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ |
| **Permanent record** | possible | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Hazardous** | ✘ | chemicals | magnetic field | ✘ | couplant | radiation | ✘ | ✘ |
| **Cost (equipment)** | low | low | low | medium | medium | high | high | medium |
| **Operator skill** | low | medium | medium | high | high | high | medium | high |

**Legend:** ✔✔ = excellent, ✔ = suitable, ✘ = not suitable.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Equation

The UT system is governed by a fundamental relation: **distance = velocity × time**. Derivation:

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Let an ultrasonic pulse be generated by the transducer at $t = 0$. The pulse travels through the specimen, encounters a defect at depth $x$, and reflects back to the same transducer (pulse-echo mode).

**Step 2 — Outward travel.**
The pulse travels a distance $x$ to reach the defect at velocity $c$.
$$t_{\text{out}} \;=\; \frac{x}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Return travel.**
After reflection, the pulse travels the same distance $x$ back to the transducer.
$$t_{\text{back}} \;=\; \frac{x}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Total round-trip time.**
$$t \;=\; t_{\text{out}} \;+\; t_{\text{back}} \;=\; \frac{x}{c} \;+\; \frac{x}{c} \;=\; \frac{2x}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging for defect depth.**
$$\boxed{\,x \;=\; \frac{c\, t}{2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The factor of 2 in the denominator is **only for pulse-echo** mode. In **through-transmission** mode (separate transmitter and receiver on opposite sides), the formula reduces to $x = c\,t$ with no factor of 2.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Skin Depth in Eddy Current Testing

The skin depth governs **how deep** an eddy current system can inspect.

**Step 1 — Governing differential equation.**
A time-varying magnetic field in a conductor induces eddy currents governed by Maxwell's equations, leading to an exponential decay of field strength with depth.

**Step 2 — Standard form of the solution.**
$$H(z) \;=\; H_{0}\, e^{-z/\delta}$$

where $H_0$ is the surface magnetic field intensity, $z$ is depth, and $\delta$ is the skin depth.

**Step 3 — Substitution from Maxwell's equations.**
The classical electromagnetic derivation gives:
$$\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 4 — Practical engineering form.**
Using $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, and $\sigma = 1/\rho$:

$$\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2}{2\pi f \cdot \mu_0 \mu_r \cdot (1/\rho)}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to commonly used units.**
Using $\rho$ in $\mu\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ and $f$ in Hz, and substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\text{H/m}$:

$$\delta \;=\; 503 \sqrt{\frac{\rho_r}{\mu_r f}} \quad [\text{mm}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Higher inspection frequency → smaller skin depth → only **surface inspection**. Lower frequency → deeper penetration but **lower sensitivity to small defects**. This is the fundamental **frequency-selection trade-off** in eddy current NDT systems.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Beer–Lambert Law for Radiographic Systems

**Step 1 — Define the differential attenuation.**
Consider a thin slice of material of thickness $dx$. The fractional reduction in intensity $dI/I$ is proportional to $dx$:
$$\frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -\mu\, dx$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the full thickness.**
Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $x$ (with initial intensity $I_0$):
$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \;=\; -\mu x$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate.**
$$\frac{I}{I_{0}} \;=\; e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 5 — Final form.**
$$\boxed{\,I \;=\; I_{0}\, e^{-\mu x}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for NDT Systems:** A defect (void, crack) has a **lower $\mu$** than the surrounding material, so the transmitted intensity through the defect is **higher** — producing a **dark region on the radiographic film** (because more X-rays reach the film, exposing it more). This is the **fundamental principle of radiographic contrast** in an NDT system.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Block Diagram of an NDT System

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **generic functional architecture** of any NDT system, regardless of method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Source / Transmitter] -->|Stimulus Signal| B[Coupling Medium]
    B --> C[Test Specimen]
    C -->|Interaction with defects and material| D[Response / Reflected Energy]
    D --> E[Sensor / Detector / Receiver]
    E --> F[Signal Processing Unit]
    F --> G[Display / Recording Unit]
    G --> H[Interpretation and Decision]

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef specimen fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef output fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class A,B source
    class C,D specimen
    class E,F,G,H output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The energy source produces a stimulus that is transmitted into the specimen via a coupling medium. The specimen's response is captured by a detector, processed, and displayed. The operator interprets the result to make a decision on component integrity.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of NDT Systems

The diagram below classifies the major NDT systems covered in Module 1 based on the **type of energy used**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[NDT Systems] --> S1[Surface Methods]
    R --> S2[Volumetric Methods]
    R --> S3[Real-Time Monitoring]

    S1 --> N1[Visual Testing VT]
    S1 --> N2[Liquid Penetrant PT]
    S1 --> N3[Magnetic Particle MT]
    S1 --> N4[Eddy Current ET]
    S1 --> N5[Infrared Thermography IRT]

    S2 --> N6[Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    S2 --> N7[Radiographic Testing RT]

    S3 --> N8[Acoustic Emission AE]
    S3 --> N9[Continuous UT Monitoring]

    classDef root fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class R root
    class S1,S2,S3 branch
    class N1,N2,N3,N4,N5,N6,N7,N8,N9 leaf
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: A Typical Ultrasonic NDT System

The most commonly examined system in KTU is the **Ultrasonic Testing System**. Its block architecture is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pulser / Trigger Generator] --> P2[Transmitter - Pulser Receiver]
    P2 --> P3[Transducer Probe]
    P3 -->|Ultrasonic beam via couplant| P4[Test Specimen]
    P4 -->|Reflected echo| P3
    P3 --> P5[Receiver Amplifier]
    P5 --> P6[Signal Conditioner and Filter]
    P6 --> P7[Display: A-Scan or B-Scan or C-Scan]
    P7 --> P8[Operator Interpretation]

    classDef gen fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333
    classDef proc fill:#C9E4FF,stroke:#333
    classDef out fill:#C5F0C0,stroke:#333
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 gen
    class P5,P6 proc
    class P7,P8 out
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Note that the **same transducer** acts as both transmitter and receiver in **pulse-echo** mode. This is why a UT system only needs **one probe** to detect and locate defects — a key advantage over RT (which requires separate source and film).

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: NDT System Selection for a Given Defect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the defect on the surface?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Ferromagnetic material?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is full volumetric coverage needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A1[Use MT]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Conductive material?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A2[Use ET]
    Q4 -- No --> A3[Use PT]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5{Critical safety component?}
    Q3 -- No --> A4[Use UT]
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5[Use RT or Phased Array UT]
    Q5 -- No --> A4

    classDef def fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    classDef ans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5 def
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 ans
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** This is the type of decision logic that a KTU examiner expects a student to demonstrate when asked: *"Which NDT system would you choose for inspecting a sub-surface crack in an aluminum aerospace wing?"* — Answer: **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**, because aluminum is non-ferromagnetic, non-sub-surface-accessible, and UT provides excellent volumetric sensitivity in non-conductive-thickness contexts.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an NDT system. List any four commonly used NDT systems and state the physical principle used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **NDT system** is an integrated assembly of hardware and methodology that uses a non-damaging form of energy to interrogate a material, component, or structure and reveal information about its integrity, defects, or properties without impairing its usefulness.

Four commonly used NDT systems and their principles:

1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — direct observation using visible light.
2. **Liquid Penetrant Testing (PT)** — capillary action of penetrant fluids into surface-breaking defects.
3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — propagation of high-frequency mechanical waves and their reflection from interfaces.
4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — differential attenuation of X-rays or γ-rays passing through the specimen.

*(Any four with correct physical principles fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by the "stimulus–response" framework in NDT systems? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **stimulus–response framework** describes the universal operating principle of all NDT systems: a known **stimulus (energy input)** is applied to the test object, and the modified **response (energy output)** is measured and analyzed.

**Example:** In **Ultrasonic Testing**, the stimulus is a high-frequency sound pulse, and the response is the reflected echo captured by the same (or a separate) transducer. The time delay and amplitude of the echo reveal the depth and size of internal defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a generic NDT system**, clearly identifying the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare and contrast the following NDT systems on the basis of: (i) type of defects detected, (ii) material applicability, (iii) permanent record availability, and (iv) operator skill required: **UT, RT, PT, and ET**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a):**

The functional architecture of a generic NDT system consists of **five blocks**:

1. **Energy Source / Transmitter:** Produces a controlled, known stimulus — e.g., X-ray tube, ultrasonic pulser, eddy current oscillator.
2. **Coupling / Transmission Medium:** Delivers the stimulus to the specimen — e.g., couplant gel, air, contact paste.
3. **Specimen Interaction Zone:** The test object itself; the stimulus interacts with defects and material properties.
4. **Sensor / Detector / Receiver:** Captures the modified response — e.g., transducer, photographic film, pickup coil, IR camera.
5. **Signal Processing & Display Unit:** Amplifies, filters, and displays the response — e.g., A-scan oscilloscope, B-scan monitor, C-scan image, computer.

**Flow:** Energy Source → Coupling → Specimen → Sensor → Processor → Display → Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (KTU Examiner):** 1 mark for naming each block, 1 mark for stating its function, 1 mark for the connecting flow — total **3 marks**. The remaining **4 marks** are for the example, neatness, and clarity of the diagram.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b):**

| Criterion | **UT** | **RT** | **PT** | **ET** |
|:----------|:-------|:-------|:-------|:-------|
| **(i) Defect type** | Surface, sub-surface, volumetric; excellent for cracks, laminations, porosity | Volumetric; excellent for voids, inclusions, through-thickness cracks | Surface-breaking only; cracks, porosity, laps | Surface & near-surface cracks, conductivity variations |
| **(ii) Material** | Metals, plastics, composites (any sound-conductive) | Any — metals, plastics, ceramics, welds | Any non-porous (metals, ceramics, some plastics) | Only electrically **conductive** materials |
| **(iii) Permanent record** | Yes (A/B/C-scan printouts) | Yes (radiographic film) | Yes (photographs of indications) | Possible (impedance plane plots) |
| **(iv) Operator skill** | High (interpretation of A-scan, sizing) | High (radiation safety + film interpretation) | Moderate (cleanliness critical) | High (impedance analysis, frequency tuning) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write *"ET works on all materials"* — this is **wrong**. ET requires **electrical conductivity**; it cannot inspect plastics, ceramics, or wood. Losing **1 mark** for this error is common.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**a)** Derive the **ultrasonic time-of-flight equation** for a pulse-echo system. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a suitable example, explain why **a single NDT system is insufficient for inspecting critical components**. Justify your answer using the **defect spectrum** concept. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let a defect lie at depth $x$ from the front surface. The transducer emits a pulse at time $t = 0$.

**Step 2 — Outward propagation.**
The pulse travels a distance $x$ at velocity $c$.
$$t_{\text{out}} \;=\; \frac{x}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Reflection and return.**
The reflected pulse travels the same distance $x$ back to the transducer.
$$t_{\text{back}} \;=\; \frac{x}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Total round-trip time.**
$$t \;=\; t_{\text{out}} \;+\; t_{\text{back}} \;=\; \frac{2x}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for depth $x$.**
$$\boxed{\,x \;=\; \frac{c\,t}{2}\,}$$

**Numerical example (for full marks):**
If the velocity of longitudinal waves in steel is $c = 5900\,\text{m/s}$ and the measured round-trip time is $t = 8\,\mu\text{s}$, then:
$$x \;=\; \frac{5900 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \;=\; 0.0236\,\text{m} \;=\; 23.6\,\text{mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the denominator (errors in 50% of answer scripts).
> - Not specifying **pulse-echo** mode (the formula differs for through-transmission).
> - Failing to **convert microseconds to seconds** in numerical examples.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b):**

A **single NDT system is insufficient** for critical components because different defects have different characteristics — location, orientation, type, and material context. No one system can detect all defect types with equal sensitivity.

**Example — Nuclear Reactor Pressure Vessel Inspection:**

The pressure vessel is the most safety-critical component in a nuclear plant. It must be inspected for:
- **Surface cracks** (stress corrosion cracking) → best detected by **MT** (ferromagnetic steel) or **PT**.
- **Sub-surface laminations and inclusions** → best detected by **UT**.
- **Through-thickness volumetric flaws** → best detected by **RT** or phased-array **UT**.
- **Real-time crack growth monitoring during service** → best detected by **Acoustic Emission (AE)**.

Therefore, a **multi-technique inspection (MTI)** strategy is mandatory. Regulatory codes (ASME Section XI, RCC-M) require multiple NDT systems to be applied to the same component to **cross-validate** findings and achieve a high **Probability of Detection (POD)**.

**Defect Spectrum Justification:**

| Defect Location | Best NDT System | Reason |
|:----------------|:----------------|:-------|
| Surface opening | PT, MT | Direct access to defect mouth |
| Sub-surface (1–10 mm) | ET, MT (with prod) | Skin-depth limited |
| Deep volumetric | UT, RT | Penetration depth |
| Real-time in service | AE, IRT | Continuous monitoring |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing *"UT is the best NDT system"* — this is too general. UT is best for **sub-surface volumetric** defects but **poor for surface-breaking** defects in rough surfaces. Specificity is what examiners reward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Systems" (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Definition:** An NDT system = energy source + coupling + specimen interaction + sensor + processor + display.
- [ ] **Universal Principle:** Stimulus–response framework applies to *all* NDT systems.
- [ ] **Eight Major NDT Systems:** VT, PT, MT, ET, UT, RT, IRT, AE.
- [ ] **Method vs. System:** A *method* is the principle (e.g., ultrasound); a *system* is the engineered hardware implementing it (e.g., phased-array UT scanner).
- [ ] **Pulse-Echo UT formula:** $x = ct/2$ — **always** the factor of 2 for pulse-echo.
- [ ] **Through-Transmission UT formula:** $x = c\,t$ — no factor of 2.
- [ ] **Beer–Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — governs RT contrast.
- [ ] **Skin Depth (ET):** $\delta = 503\sqrt{\rho_r/(\mu_r f)}\,\text{mm}$ — higher frequency → shallower inspection.
- [ ] **MT requires ferromagnetic material** — common exam pitfall.
- [ ] **ET requires electrical conductivity** — common exam pitfall.
- [ ] **PT reveals only surface-breaking defects** — capillary action limit.
- [ ] **RT uses ionizing radiation** — safety protocols mandatory.
- [ ] **IRT is real-time, non-contact** — good for large area, but only surface temperature.
- [ ] **AE is passive** — listens to elastic waves from active cracks.
- [ ] **No single NDT system is universally best** — Multi-Technique Inspection (MTI) is the industry norm for critical components.
- [ ] **Codes/standards mandate specific NDT systems** — e.g., ASME Sec. V, ASTM E-series, ISO 9712.
- [ ] **Probability of Detection (POD)** is the modern metric of NDT system reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:**
> *"Know the system, know the defect, know the material — then choose. Always justify your choice with a defect-spectrum argument."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Enhanced System
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Enhanced System

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Computer-Enhanced System (CES)** in Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) refers to an integrated measurement and inspection architecture in which a digital computing subsystem (microprocessor, DSP, FPGA, or workstation) is coupled with the primary NDT sensor (ultrasonic transducer, X-ray detector, eddy-current probe, infrared camera, or optical scanner) to perform data acquisition, real-time signal/image processing, automated defect recognition, archival, and quantitative evaluation of the inspected component — without altering its serviceability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this is also known as **Computer-Aided NDT (CA-NDT)** or **Digitally-Enhanced Inspection Systems**. The computer does **not** replace the physical transducer; rather, it *augments* the signal chain with operations such as filtering, deconvolution, gain normalization, image reconstruction, pattern classification, and decision reporting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> Computer Enhanced Systems integrate hardware (transducer + A/D converter + memory) and software (DSP routines, AI/ML classifiers) to push the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, **Probability of Detection (POD)**, and **spatial resolution** of conventional NDT far beyond the limits of analog instrumentation.

The principal sub-classes recognized in PESFT521 are:

| Sub-System | Physical Principle | Computer Role |
|---|---|---|
| Digital Radiography (DR) | X-ray attenuation | Image digitization + enhancement |
| Computed Tomography (CT) | Rotational X-ray projection | Tomographic reconstruction |
| Phased Array UT (PAUT) | Ultrasonic beam steering | Beam focusing law generation |
| Automated UT (AUT) | Pulse-echo | Scan planning + C-scan mapping |
| Digital Eddy Current Array | Electromagnetic induction | Multi-channel impedance imaging |
| Infrared Thermography | Planck radiation | Thermal transient analysis |
| Machine-Vision MT / VT | Optical imaging | Edge/feature extraction |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are listening to a friend speaking in a noisy marketplace.

- The **analog NDT system** is your **unaided ear**: you catch the words, but background noise, echoes, and weak whispers get lost. You may *miss* a small crack signal hidden in electrical noise.
- The **Computer-Enhanced System** is the same ear plugged into a **noise-cancelling headphone, an equalizer, a voice-isolating microphone, and a smart assistant** that highlights only the words resembling known defect "names." The computer is the assistant.

In short: **the transducer hears (acquires), the computer listens-smarter (processes), remembers (stores), and decides (classifies).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A Computer-Enhanced NDT system converts a *physical phenomenon* (sound, X-ray photon, eddy current) into a *digital dataset*, processes it algorithmically, and presents an *interpretable diagnostic* to the human inspector — turning a qualitative art into a quantitative engineering discipline.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics recur in every quantitative analysis of CES-NDT:

- **Speed of sound in steel** : $c_s = 5920 \; \text{m/s}$ (longitudinal)
- **Speed of sound in aluminium** : $c_{Al} = 6320 \; \text{m/s}$
- **Speed of sound in water** : $c_w = 1480 \; \text{m/s}$ (couplant)
- **Sampling Theorem limit (Nyquist)** : $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
- **Standard SNR threshold for accept/reject** : $\text{SNR} \geq 20 \; \text{dB}$ (per ASTM E1816)
- **Display dynamic range of modern UT flaw detectors** : $\geq 100 \; \text{dB}$
- **Spatial resolution of DR panels** : typically $50 \; \mu\text{m}$ to $400 \; \mu\text{m}$ pixel pitch
- **Reference standard** : **ASTM E1316** (Standard Terminology for NDT), **ASTM E1816** (UT computer-based systems)
- **Probability of Detection (POD)** : dimensionless $[0, 1]$
- **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** : defect detectability curve

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Blocks of a Computer-Enhanced NDT System

The end-to-end signal chain consists of **six** cascaded functional blocks. Understanding each block is the key to answering KTU Part-B questions.

1. **Transducer / Sensor** — converts the physical field (acoustic, EM, thermal, radiation) into an analog electrical signal.
2. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — pre-amplifier, T/R switch (in pulse-echo UT), band-pass filter, time-gain compensation (TGC) amplifier.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — samples the analog signal at $f_s$ and quantizes with $N$ bits. **Quantization step**: 
   $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} $$
4. **Digital Signal/Image Processor (DSP / GPU / CPU)** — runs algorithms: FFT, convolution, wavelet transform, deconvolution, segmentation, classification.
5. **Storage / Display** — B-scan, C-scan, D-scan, 3-D volumetric rendering.
6. **Decision / Classification Module** — thresholding, ROC analysis, AI/ML inference (CNN, SVM, Random Forest).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in exams:** Almost every KTU Part-B 14-mark question asks the student to *draw the block diagram* and *explain each stage*. Memorize the six blocks above and their functions in order.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Excitation Generation:** A pulser (e.g., $100 \; \text{V}$ spike with $5 \; \text{ns}$ rise time) excites the UT probe.
2. **Analog Conditioning:** TGC compensates for material attenuation, raising the gain exponentially with time-of-flight.
3. **Sampling:** The ADC samples the received RF waveform. To satisfy **Nyquist**, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the probe centre frequency (commonly $5 \; \text{MHz}$, so $f_s \geq 10 \; \text{MHz}$).
4. **Digital Processing:** Software applies matched filtering, Hilbert transform (to extract envelope), or image-enhancement kernels.
5. **Image Formation:** For PAUT/AUT, individual A-scans are mapped into a 2-D B-scan or top-view C-scan, where color = amplitude and position = probe index.
6. **Defect Recognition:** The processed image is compared to a known defect library or fed to a trained CNN for classification (*crack / porosity / inclusion / no defect*).
7. **Report Generation:** Quantitative metrics (depth, length, area, equivalent reflector size) are written to disk in a traceable format compliant with **ISO 9001 / NADCAP**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these eight equations — they cover $\geq 80\%$ of numerical problems asked in PESFT521 Module 1.

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Units / Notes} \\
\hline
\text{Quantization step} & \Delta V = V_{FS} / (2^N - 1) & \text{Volts; } N = \text{ADC bits} \\
\hline
\text{Nyquist sampling rate} & f_s \geq 2 f_{max} & \text{Hz} \\
\hline
\text{Time-of-flight in UT} & t = 2d / c & \text{seconds; } d = \text{thickness}, c = \text{sound speed} \\
\hline
\text{Defect depth} & d = c \cdot t / 2 & \text{metres} \\
\hline
\text{Attenuation (dB)} & \alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_0 / A_x) & \text{dB per unit length} \\
\hline
\text{TGC gain} & G(t) = G_0 \cdot 10^{\alpha c t / 20} & \text{linear units; compensates loss} \\
\hline
\text{SNR} & \text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{signal} / V_{noise}) & \text{dB} \\
\hline
\text{Pixel intensity after gain } k & I_{out} = k \cdot I_{in} + b & \text{linear transform in DR} \\
\hline
\text{2-D Convolution} & g(x,y) = \sum_{u} \sum_{v} h(u,v) f(x-u, y-v) & \text{image filtering kernel} \\
\hline
\text{Histogram equalization} & s_k = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} n_j / N & L = \text{gray levels, } N = \text{pixels} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing a formula inside a markdown table cell, do **not** use the bare `|` symbol (e.g., for absolute value). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Many students lose marks when their rendered table breaks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** PAUT + AUT is used to inspect 100% of every turbine disc dovetail, replacing manual hand-scan.
- **Nuclear:** Phased array + TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction) qualifies dissimilar-metal welds in reactor pressure vessels.
- **Oil & Gas:** Computed Tomography (CT) screens drill-pipe threads; digital radiography replaces film for pipeline girth welds.
- **Additive Manufacturing:** In-situ IR thermography with computer-enhanced transient analysis detects lack-of-fusion in 3-D printed Ti6Al4V parts.
- **Civil Engineering:** Ground-Penetrating Radar (GPR) with computer-enhanced synthetic-aperture processing locates rebar corrosion in bridges.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required ADC Sampling Rate from Probe Bandwidth

**Given:** A 5 MHz broadband UT probe with $-6 \; \text{dB}$ bandwidth of $60\%$ (i.e. $f_{max} \approx 5 + 0.3 \times 5 = 6.5 \; \text{MHz}$).
**Find:** Minimum sampling rate $f_s$ and number of samples per pulse round-trip in a 50 mm steel plate.

**Step 1 — Identify the highest signal frequency.**

$$
f_{max} = f_{center} \left(1 + \frac{BW\%}{2}\right) = 5 \left(1 + 0.30\right) = 6.5 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.**

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_s \geq 2 \times 6.5 = 13 \; \text{MHz}
$$

In practice, **oversample by a factor of 4 to 10** for accurate envelope extraction:

$$
f_{s,\text{practical}} = 10 \times f_{max} = 65 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time-of-flight in the 50 mm steel plate.**

$$
t = \frac{2 d}{c_s} = \frac{2 \times 0.050}{5920} = 1.689 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s} = 16.89 \; \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of samples acquired during this round-trip.**

$$
N_{samples} = f_s \times t = 65 \times 10^{6} \times 1.689 \times 10^{-5} = 1098 \; \text{samples}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ADC must support at least $13 \; \text{MHz}$ (Nyquist) and ideally $65 \; \text{MHz}$ to give the digital processor enough samples to extract the defect echo envelope.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization Error Bound

**Given:** A full-scale receiver voltage $V_{FS} = 10 \; \text{V}$ and a 12-bit ADC.
**Find:** The quantization step $\Delta V$ and the theoretical SNR of an ideal quantizer.

**Step 1 — Quantization step.**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} - 1} = \frac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V} = 2.442 \; \text{mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal-quantizer SNR (assuming uniform quantization noise).**

$$
\text{SNR}_{q} = 6.02 N + 1.76 \; \text{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** A 12-bit ADC provides $\sim 74 \; \text{dB}$ dynamic range. For an ultrasonic flaw detector requiring **SNR $\geq$ 60 dB**, a 10-bit ADC would be the *minimum* acceptable; 12-bit is the *recommended* margin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Gain Compensation (TGC) Curve

**Given:** Steel plate inspected at $c_s = 5920 \; \text{m/s}$, attenuation $\alpha = 0.5 \; \text{dB/mm}$ at $5 \; \text{MHz}$. Initial amplifier gain $G_0 = 1$ (linear).
**Find:** Gain factor $G(t)$ needed at $t = 10 \; \mu s$ to equalize echoes from equal reflectors at different depths.

**Step 1 — Path length corresponding to $10 \; \mu s$.**

$$
d = \frac{c_s \, t}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.0296 \; \text{m} = 29.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Two-way attenuation in dB.**

$$
A_{dB} = \alpha \cdot 2 d = 0.5 \times 2 \times 29.6 = 29.6 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert dB attenuation to linear gain factor.**

$$
G(t) = 10^{A_{dB}/20} = 10^{29.6/20} = 10^{1.48} = 30.2
$$

**Step 4 — General TGC formula.**

$$
G(t) = G_0 \cdot 10^{\alpha c t / 20}
$$

At $t = 0$ (front surface), $G = G_0 = 1$. At $t = 10 \; \mu s$, $G = 30.2$. The amplifier gain must therefore ramp up **linearly in dB** versus time — this is exactly what the TGC potentiometer / DAC ramp does inside the analog front-end.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Computer-Enhancement Techniques vs Defect Type

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Enhancement Technique} & \textbf{Surface Cracks} & \textbf{Subsurface Voids} & \textbf{Laminations} & \textbf{Corrosion} & \textbf{Inclusions} \\
\hline
\text{Digital filtering (IIR/FIR)} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Spatial averaging} & \times & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Edge detection (Sobel/Canny)} & \checkmark\checkmark & \times & \checkmark & \checkmark & \times \\
\hline
\text{Thresholding (Otsu)} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \times & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Wavelet de-noising} & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Deconvolution} & \checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark & \times & \checkmark \\
\hline
\text{Tomographic reconstruction} & \checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark \\
\hline
\text{CNN classifier} & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark & \checkmark\checkmark \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

$$
\text{Legend: } \checkmark\checkmark = \text{highly effective}, \; \checkmark = \text{useful}, \; \times = \text{not typically used}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Histogram Equalization for DR Contrast Enhancement

**Given:** A 16-bit digital radiograph with intensity range $[0, 65535]$ and a strongly skewed histogram (most pixels near the dark end).
**Find:** The equalized intensity $s_k$ for a gray-level bin $k$ with $n_k$ pixels.

**Step 1 — Probability mass function of the input histogram.**

$$
p(I_k) = \frac{n_k}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF).**

$$
\text{CDF}(k) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Equalized intensity (continuous approximation).**

$$
s_k = (L - 1) \cdot \text{CDF}(k) = (L - 1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{n_j}{N}
$$

For $L = 2^{16} = 65536$, $s_k$ lies in $[0, 65535]$.

**Step 4 — Effect.** The transform stretches the densely-populated dark bins and compresses the sparsely-populated bright bins, producing a *uniform* output histogram. Contrast is enhanced without losing physical information; this is why histogram equalization is the **first** algorithm applied in any digital radiography workstation.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — SNR Improvement by Signal Averaging

**Given:** A noisy UT A-scan with single-shot SNR = $20 \; \text{dB}$.
**Find:** SNR after $M = 256$ coherent averages.

**Step 1 — Single-shot noise power** $\sigma_n^2$.

**Step 2 — Coherent averaging reduces noise power by a factor of $M$** (signal adds constructively, noise adds incoherently).

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} + 10 \log_{10} M
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 20 + 10 \log_{10}(256) = 20 + 10 \times 2.408 = 20 + 24.08 = 44.08 \; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment.** Every doubling of $M$ adds $3 \; \text{dB}$. To go from 20 dB to 60 dB, you need $10^{4} = 10{,}000$ averages — at $1 \; \text{kHz}$ PRF, this is a 10-second inspection per point. This is *why* modern CES-NDT uses **matched filters and wavelet denoising** instead of brute-force averaging.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram of a Computer-Enhanced NDT System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[specimen] --> B[transducer]
    B --> C[afe_preamp]
    C --> D[afe_filter_tgc]
    D --> E[adc_digitizer]
    E --> F[dsp_processor]
    F --> G[storage_memory]
    F --> H[display_renderer]
    F --> I[classifier_decision]
    I --> J[report_output]
    K[host_pulser] --> B
    F --> K
```

**Block descriptions:**

- `specimen` — Component under inspection.
- `transducer` — UT probe, X-ray tube, eddy-coil, IR camera, etc.
- `afe_preamp` — Low-noise pre-amplifier (gain $\sim 40 \; \text{dB}$).
- `afe_filter_tgc` — Band-pass + Time-Gain Compensation.
- `adc_digitizer` — Analog-to-Digital converter; samples at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- `dsp_processor` — Real-time DSP, FFT, convolution, AI inference.
- `storage_memory` — RAID / SSD array for raw RF / image archival.
- `display_renderer` — B-scan, C-scan, D-scan, 3-D viewer.
- `classifier_decision` — Accept/Reject per code (ASME, EN, AWS).
- `report_output` — PDF + database record (NADCAP-traceable).
- `host_pulser` — Pulser/receiver timing & synchronization.

## 4.2 Sub-Architecture: Phased-Array UT (PAUT) Computer-Enhanced System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pc[host_pc_planner]
    fw[foc_law_firmware]
    pulser_array[multi_ch_pulser]
    probe[phased_array_probe]
    sample[steel_specimen]
    recv_array[multi_ch_receiver]
    mux[adc_mux_bank]
    beam[beamformer_dsp]
    img[image_reconstructor]
    cscan[cscan_mapper]
    cnn[cnn_classifier]
    db[defect_database]
    report[final_report]

    pc --> fw
    fw --> pulser_array
    pulser_array --> probe
    probe --> sample
    sample --> probe
    probe --> recv_array
    recv_array --> mux
    mux --> beam
    beam --> img
    img --> cscan
    cscan --> cnn
    db --> cnn
    cnn --> report
```

**Block descriptions:**

- `host_pc_planner` — Operator enters weld geometry; software auto-generates focal laws.
- `foc_law_firmware` — Computes per-element time delays $\tau_i$ to form a focal point at $(x_f, z_f)$.
- `multi_ch_pulser` — 16/32/64/128 channel pulser.
- `phased_array_probe` — Linear array of piezo elements.
- `multi_ch_receiver` — Parallel low-noise receivers.
- `adc_mux_bank` — Muxed ADC array (or per-channel ADC).
- `beamformer_dsp` — Real-time delay-and-sum beamformer.
- `image_reconstructor` — Builds B/C/D scan sectors.
- `cnn_classifier` — Pre-trained CNN (ResNet, U-Net) labels each pixel.
- `defect_database` — Reference library of known flaw signatures.

## 4.3 State Machine for Automated Defect Decision (Accept/Reject)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> acquire
    acquire --> preprocess
    preprocess --> segment
    segment --> measure
    measure --> compare
    compare --> accept : within tolerance
    compare --> reject : exceeds limit
    accept --> archive
    reject --> flag_human
    archive --> [*]
    flag_human --> [*]
```

**State descriptions:**

- `acquire` — Capture raw RF / image from sensor.
- `preprocess` — Filter, denoise, equalize.
- `segment` — Isolate defect region from background (Otsu / watershed / U-Net).
- `measure` — Compute length, depth, area, equivalent diameter.
- `compare` — Compare with acceptance criteria from ASME V / EN 1714.
- `accept` — Pass; archive and proceed.
- `reject` — Fail; flag for human review.
- `flag_human` — Re-inspect with secondary method (MT / PT).

## 4.4 Data-Flow Diagram — From Sensor Pixel to Final Defect Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p[pixel_intensity] --> q[adc_quantize]
    q --> r[gamma_correction]
    r --> s[noise_filter]
    s --> t[hist_eq]
    t --> u[edge_detect]
    u --> v[feature_extract]
    v --> w[svm_cnn_classify]
    w --> x[accept_reject]
    x --> y[archive_pdf]
```
