### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Magnetization** is the process of inducing a magnetic field within a ferromagnetic test specimen to detect surface and sub-surface discontinuities using the **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** method. The intensity and direction of the magnetizing field must be sufficient to create a **leakage field** at any flaw that intercepts the magnetic flux path.

The **Methods of Magnetization** refer to the various techniques employed to introduce a controlled magnetic flux into a specimen. These methods are broadly classified as:

1. **Direct Magnetization** – Current is passed directly through the test piece.
2. **Indirect Magnetization** – Current is passed through a conductor (central conductor or coil) that induces a magnetic field in the specimen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two fundamental directions of magnetization that govern ALL MPT inspection are **Longitudinal Magnetization** (flux parallel to the current-carrying coil's axis) and **Circular Magnetization** (flux circles around the current-carrying conductor).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a stretched rubber band representing the magnetic flux lines inside a piece of steel. If you press a pin into the rubber band (the "flaw"), the rubber band deforms and bulges out at the point of the pin. In MPT, the "rubber band" is the **magnetic flux**, and the "pin" is a **crack**. Iron particles sprinkled on the surface behave like dust settling on the bulge, making the invisible flaw visible.

Now, how do you "stretch" the flux? You can:
- Push electricity **through** the part (like pushing water through a pipe) → **Direct Method**.
- Wrap a wire around the part and push electricity through the **coil** (like winding a solenoid) → **Indirect Method using a Coil**.
- Place a conductor **inside** a hollow part and push electricity through the conductor (like threading a heating element through a tube) → **Indirect Method using a Central Conductor**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants

- **Required Flux Density for Steels:** approximately **$\mathbf{0.7 \text{ to } 0.9 \text{ Tesla}}$** at the surface.
- **Standard Magnetizing Current Frequencies:** **AC (50/60 Hz)**, **DC**, **Half-Wave Rectified AC (HWAC)**, **Full-Wave Rectified AC (FWAC)**, and **Three-Phase FWAC**.
- **Key Governing Standard (International):** **ASTM E709** – *Standard Guide for Magnetic Particle Testing* and **ASTM E1444** for equipment specifications.
- **Right-Hand Rule (Fleming's):** Used to determine the direction of the magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box – Leakage Field:** A leakage field is a localized magnetic flux that escapes from the surface of a magnetized ferromagnetic material at the location of a discontinuity. This leakage field is what attracts the magnetic particles and creates the visible indication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Magnetization Methods

#### **A. Direct Methods of Magnetization**
The test current is passed **directly through the specimen** using prods, contact heads, or clamps.

| Method | Equipment | Field Direction | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prod Method** | Two prods pressed on the part | Circular, localized between prods | Localized inspection of welds, large forgings |
| **Contact Head Method** | Heavy-duty clamps gripping the ends | Circular along the length | Bars, billets, long shafts |
| **Induced Current Method** | No physical contact (high-frequency AC induces eddy currents) | Circular, controllable | Inspection of surface defects in tubes |
| **Permanent Magnet Method** | Permanent magnets (Alnico, NdFeB) | Bipolar, fixed field | Field inspections, portable, no power needed |

#### **B. Indirect Methods of Magnetization**
Current is passed through an auxiliary conductor; the specimen is magnetized by induction.

| Method | Equipment | Field Direction | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coil (Solenoid) Method** | Multi-turn helical coil | Longitudinal (along coil axis) | Bars, shafts, long components |
| **Central Conductor Method** | Solid bar or cable passed through hollow part | Circular around the conductor | Tubular parts, ring-shaped parts |
| **Yoke Method (AC or DC)** | Electromagnetic yoke (horseshoe) | Field across the poles (longitudinal to the part) | Welds, large structures, in-field testing |

### 2.2 Directional Logic – The Two Fundamental Field Geometries

**1. Circular Magnetization:**
- Achieved by passing current **through** the part or through a **central conductor**.
- Field lines form **concentric circles** around the current path.
- Governed by: **Right-Hand Rule for a straight conductor** → Thumb = current, Fingers = magnetic field direction.
- Best for detecting **longitudinal defects** (cracks running along the length of the part).

**2. Longitudinal Magnetization:**
- Achieved by passing current through a **multi-turn coil** wrapped around the part.
- Field lines run **parallel to the axis** of the coil.
- Governed by: **Right-Hand Rule for a solenoid** → Fingers curl in current direction, Thumb = North pole (field direction).
- Best for detecting **transverse defects** (cracks running circumferentially around the part).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of MPT:** The magnetic flux must be oriented **as close to 90° (perpendicular) to the expected defect** as possible. If the defect is parallel to the flux, it will NOT produce a leakage field and will go undetected. This is why most specifications require **MULTI-DIRECTIONAL MAGNETIZATION** (e.g., wet fluorescent particles with two shots at 90° to each other).

### 2.3 Types of Magnetizing Current and Their Effect

| Current Type | Source | Effective Skin Depth | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC (Alternating Current)** | Transformer secondary, 50/60 Hz | Surface only (approx. 1–2 mm) | Surface-breaking defects |
| **DC (Direct Current)** | Rectified battery banks, motor-generators | **Full penetration** of the part | Subsurface defects |
| **HWAC (Half-Wave Rectified AC)** | Half-wave rectifier | Surface to ~3 mm with strong particle mobility | Most common industrial MPT current |
| **FWAC (Full-Wave Rectified AC)** | Full-wave bridge rectifier | Deep penetration, smoother DC-like field | Subsurface and dry particles |
| **3-Phase FWAC** | Three-phase bridge rectifier | Lowest ripple, deepest penetration | Heavy forgings, critical aerospace parts |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | Magnetizing field strength inside a solenoid (Ampere-turns per metre) | A-turns/m |
| 2 | $B = \mu_0 \cdot (H + M)$ | Magnetic flux density (where $M$ = magnetization, $H$ = field strength) | Tesla (T) |
| 3 | $B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H$ | For a linear material, $\mu_r$ = relative permeability | Tesla (T) |
| 4 | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Magnetic flux through area $A$ | Weber (Wb) |
| 5 | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | Permeability of free space (constant) | H/m |
| 6 | $I_{\text{circ}} \approx \dfrac{25 \times D}{L} \times k$ | Circular magnetization current rule of thumb (varies with standard; $k$ = correction factor) | Amperes |
| 7 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\mu \omega}}$ | Skin depth $\delta$ for AC in a conductor | metres (m) |
| 8 | $F = N \cdot I$ | Ampere-turns (magnetomotive force, MMF) | Ampere-turns (At) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in engineering practice:** The Ampere-turns equation ($F = N \cdot I$) is the workhorse of MPT setup. A KTU problem will frequently ask: *"Given a coil of 5 turns and a current of 800 A, find the MMF."* The answer is $F = 5 \times 800 = 4000$ At. You must remember that 1 Ampere-turn per metre is the SI unit of magnetizing field, but in practice, MPT specifications use **Ampere-turns** as a single quantity.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Engine shafts, landing gear, turbine discs – inspected with longitudinal + circular magnetization using 3-phase FWAC.
- **Railways:** Axles and wheel sets are magnetized with contact heads running full-length DC.
- **Oil & Gas:** Drill pipes and sucker rods use prod magnetization in the field.
- **Automotive:** Forged crankshafts are 100% inspected with a multi-station MPT bench that combines a coil + central conductor in one cycle.
- **Welding Inspection:** Field welds are tested with portable AC yokes (most common NDT method in the world for welds).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Coil MMF, Field Strength, and Required Current

**Problem Setup:** A ferromagnetic shaft of length $L = 0.6$ m and diameter $D = 50$ mm must be magnetized longitudinally using a 6-turn coil. Calculate the current required to produce a peak flux density of $B = 1.2$ T inside the shaft, given that the relative permeability of the steel is approximately $\mu_r \approx 500$ (assume linearity in the operating region, and end effects are negligible so the field $H$ inside the coil is uniform and equal to $H$ inside the steel).

**Step 1: Recall the governing equation for a long solenoid.**

$$
H_{\text{coil}} = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}
$$

**Step 2: Relate $H$ to $B$ via the material's permeability.**

$$
B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $H$ from Step 1 into Step 2.**

$$
B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange the equation to solve for $I$ (the required current).**

$$
I = \frac{B \cdot L}{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
I = \frac{(1.2) \cdot (0.6)}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (500) \cdot (6)}
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the denominator first.**

$$
\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 500 \cdot 6
$$

$$
= 4 \cdot 3.14159 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 3000
$$

$$
= 12.5664 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 3000 = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 7: Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
B \cdot L = 1.2 \cdot 0.6 = 0.72
$$

**Step 8: Final calculation.**

$$
I = \frac{0.72}{3.7699 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 190.98 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 9: Engineering rounding.**

Since practical MPT equipment uses standardized current settings, we round up to **$I \approx 191$ A** (or use the next standard tap of 200 A on the magnetizing console).

> **Engineering Insight:** Note how a small relative permeability (just 500) and a modest coil turn count (6) require a current of nearly 200 A. This is why MPT machines are rated in the **kilo-Ampere** range – the resistance of the magnetic path is very low, and steel does NOT amplify the field indefinitely; it merely channels it.

### 3.2 Derivation: Circular Magnetization Using the $25 \times D$ Rule

**Problem Setup:** A solid steel shaft of diameter $D = 80$ mm and length $L = 1.5$ m must be magnetized circularly using contact heads. Using the empirical rule of thumb $I \approx \dfrac{25 \times D}{L}$ (in inches for imperial standard – we will use SI adaptation below), find the magnetizing current. Assume the formula is to be applied directly with $D$ and $L$ in mm for this problem.

**Step 1: State the empirical formula.**

$$
I_{\text{circ}} = \frac{25 \times D}{L}
$$

where $D$ and $L$ are in consistent units (here, mm).

**Step 2: Substitute $D = 80$ mm and $L = 1500$ mm.**

$$
I_{\text{circ}} = \frac{25 \times 80}{1500}
$$

**Step 3: Numerator evaluation.**

$$
25 \times 80 = 2000
$$

**Step 4: Final calculation.**

$$
I_{\text{circ}} = \frac{2000}{1500} = 1.333 \text{ A} \text{ (per mm equivalent)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note (KTU Board Examiner Caveat):** The above is a normalized form. In real practice, the rule is $I = k \cdot D$ where $D$ is in inches and $I$ is in amperes, and $k$ ranges from 600 to 1000. For SI adaptation, the **ASTM E709** general guide uses a peak magnetizing force of approximately **$H \approx 2400$ to $4800$ A/m** for most carbon steels, and the required current is then computed from the part's cross-sectional area. Always cite the standard in your KTU answer.

**Step 5: Recompute using the actual ASTM E709 method.**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $D = 80$ mm, the circumference is:

$$
C = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 80 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2513 \text{ m}
$$

For a target tangential field strength of $H = 3600$ A/m (a common mid-range value):

$$
I = H \cdot C = 3600 \cdot 0.2513 = 904.7 \text{ A}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This huge difference (1.33 A vs. 904 A) is exactly why KTU students must be careful with the formula's basis. **Always use $I = H \cdot C$ for circular magnetization, NOT the oversimplified $25D/L$ rule** in board examinations unless explicitly asked.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Inspection Scenario | Recommended Method | Current Type | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long shaft, transverse cracks suspected | Coil magnetization (longitudinal) | FWAC or DC | Flux runs along the axis, perpendicular to circumferential cracks |
| Pressure vessel, longitudinal weld seams | Prod magnetization (circular) | AC or HWAC | Flux circles around the current path, perpendicular to axial cracks |
| Hollow tube, internal surface | Central conductor | HWAC | Induces circular flux throughout the wall thickness |
| Field weld inspection, no power source | Permanent magnet or DC yoke | N/A | Portability is the priority; AC yokes are lighter |
| Aerospace turbine disc, both surface and sub-surface | Multi-directional (coil + central conductor in one shot) | 3-Phase FWAC | Demands complete coverage with minimum handling |
| Small castings, high volume | Bench-type MPT with contact pads | AC | Fast, repeatable, automated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Magnetization Methods (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Methods of Magnetization] --> B[Direct Magnetization]
    A --> C[Indirect Magnetization]

    B --> D[Prod Method]
    B --> E[Contact Head Clamp Method]
    B --> F[Induced Current Method]
    B --> G[Permanent Magnet Method]

    D --> D1[Circular Field Between Prods]
    E --> E1[Long Length Circular Field]
    F --> F1[Surface Only High Frequency AC]
    G --> G1[Fixed Bipolar Field]

    C --> H[Coil or Solenoid Method]
    C --> I[Central Conductor Method]
    C --> J[Yoke Method]

    H --> H1[Longitudinal Field Along Coil Axis]
    I --> I1[Circular Field Around Conductor]
    J --> J1[Field Between Yoke Poles]

    D1 --> K[Field Direction Logic]
    E1 --> K
    F1 --> K
    G1 --> K
    H1 --> K
    I1 --> K
    J1 --> K

    K --> L[Right Hand Rule for Straight Conductor gives Circular Field]
    K --> M[Right Hand Rule for Solenoid gives Longitudinal Field]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Selecting Magnetization Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Start: Choose Magnetization Method] --> Q{Defect Orientation Known?}
    Q -- Yes Longitudinal or Axial --> R[Use Circular Magnetization]
    Q -- Yes Transverse or Circumferential --> S[Use Longitudinal Magnetization]
    Q -- No or Unknown --> T[Use Multi Directional Magnetization]

    R --> U{Part Geometry?}
    S --> U
    T --> U

    U --> V[Solid Part]
    U --> W[Hollow Part]
    U --> X[Long Shaft]

    V --> Y[Prods or Contact Heads]
    W --> Z[Central Conductor]
    X --> AA[Coil Magnetization]

    Y --> AB[Set Current from H times Circumference]
    Z --> AB
    AA --> AC[Set Current from N times I divided by L]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Magnetic Field Geometries (Text-Based)

**A) Circular Magnetization (Prod Method):**

```
       Current I (flowing through the part)
              |
              v
        +-------------+
       /  ____________  \
      |  /            \  |
      | |   F L A W   | |
      |  \____________/  |
       \               /
        +-------------+
    <--- Magnetic flux forms CLOSED CIRCLES around the current path --->
```

**B) Longitudinal Magnetization (Coil Method):**

```
    +-----------------------------+
    |  Current enters coil here ->|
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |   N (North Pole) ---> S (South Pole)
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |   Magnetic flux runs ALONG the part
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |   axis, from N to S INSIDE the steel
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |
    |  ++++++++++++++++++++++++  |
    +-----------------------------+
    Internal Part Axis:  ===================>  (Field Direction)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Aid Tip:** In your answer sheets, always draw the **direction of current** with arrows and the **direction of magnetic field** with curved arrows or dashed lines. Examiners explicitly look for the **right-hand rule** application. A common mistake is to draw the field lines inside the coil as loops *outside*; remember, the field INSIDE a solenoid is **uniform and parallel to the axis**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define magnetization. List any four methods of magnetization used in Magnetic Particle Testing.*

**Model Answer:**

**Magnetization** is the process of inducing a magnetic field within a ferromagnetic material to detect surface and near-surface discontinuities by creating a leakage field at the location of any flaw.

The four methods of magnetization are:
1. **Prod Method** (Direct circular magnetization)
2. **Contact Head / Clamp Method** (Direct circular magnetization for long parts)
3. **Coil / Solenoid Method** (Indirect longitudinal magnetization)
4. **Central Conductor Method** (Indirect circular magnetization for hollow parts)

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** A definition is 1 mark, and listing 4 methods is 2 marks. Do not exceed 4 – you waste time. Use single-line bullet points.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Right-Hand Rule for a solenoid. What is its significance in MPT?*

**Model Answer:**

**Right-Hand Rule for a Solenoid:** When the fingers of the right hand are curled in the direction of the current flowing through the turns of the coil, the extended thumb points in the direction of the magnetic field (North Pole) inside the solenoid.

**Significance in MPT:**
- It determines the **direction of the longitudinal magnetic field** induced in the part.
- It helps the inspector orient the coil so that the resulting flux is **perpendicular to the expected defect direction** for maximum defect detection.
- It enables the user to identify the **North and South poles** of the magnetized part.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Provided)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) – Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **direct methods of magnetization** with neat sketches. Mention the current type best suited for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **5-turn coil** is used to magnetize a steel bar of length **0.5 m** and relative permeability **$\mu_r = 600$**. If a current of **400 A** is passed through the coil, calculate the **magnetic flux density $B$** inside the bar. Assume $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Direct Methods of Magnetization** are those techniques in which the magnetizing current is passed **directly through the test specimen** itself.

**1. Prod Method:**
- Two prods (copper or brass contact electrodes) are pressed against the cleaned surface of the part, separated by a distance of 75–200 mm.
- Current flows from one prod, through the metal, and out the other prod.
- The magnetic flux forms **closed circular loops** in the metal between the two prods.
- **Best for:** Localized inspection of welds and large non-cylindrical parts.
- **Current Type:** AC, DC, or HWAC. AC gives sharp surface indications; HWAC gives good particle mobility.

**2. Contact Head / Clamp Method:**
- Heavy copper jaws are clamped permanently to the two ends of the part.
- The entire length of the part is magnetized **circularly** in a single operation.
- **Best for:** Bars, billets, long shafts, and tubular components in production-line MPT benches.
- **Current Type:** DC, FWAC, or 3-Phase FWAC for deep penetration.

**3. Induced Current Method:**
- A high-frequency AC (typically 1–10 kHz) is passed through a **surrounding coil**, which induces eddy currents in the part.
- The eddy currents flow **circularly** in the part, producing a circular magnetic field.
- **Best for:** Localized surface inspection of tubes, pipes, and special geometries where prod contact is undesirable.
- **Current Type:** High-frequency AC only (for skin-effect concentration at the surface).

**4. Permanent Magnet Method:**
- Strong permanent magnets (Alnico, NdFeB) are placed on the surface, creating a localized bipolar field.
- **Best for:** Field inspections, very remote locations, and safety-critical in-service checks where no power is available.
- **Current Type:** Not applicable (no current used).

**Sketch (as expected in the answer sheet):**

```
     (Prod Method)
     PROD (+) ----------->  [    TEST PART    ]  <----------- PROD (-)
                                  |
                                  v
                  Circular magnetic flux lines between prods
                  (concentric circles around current axis)
```

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation for a solenoid's magnetic field.**

$$
H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the relationship between $B$ and $H$ inside the steel.**

$$
B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot H
$$

**Step 3: Combine the two equations.**

$$
B = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot \frac{N \cdot I}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the given values.**

- $N = 5$ turns
- $I = 400$ A
- $L = 0.5$ m
- $\mu_r = 600$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

$$
B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (600) \cdot \frac{5 \cdot 400}{0.5}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify the numerator and denominator.**

- $N \cdot I = 5 \times 400 = 2000$ A-turns
- $N \cdot I / L = 2000 / 0.5 = 4000$ A-turns/m

$$
B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 600 \cdot 4000
$$

**Step 6: Multiply step by step.**

- $600 \times 4000 = 2.4 \times 10^{6}$
- $4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}$

$$
B = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.4 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 7: Final calculation.**

$$
B = 1.2566 \cdot 2.4 = 3.016 \text{ T}
$$

> **Engineering Verdict:** This value ($B \approx 3.0$ T) is **above the saturation flux density of typical carbon steel** (which is around 1.6–2.0 T). In real practice, the steel would saturate, and the actual $B$ would plateau. The KTU answer, however, requires this calculated value using the linear assumption: **$\boxed{B \approx 3.02 \text{ Tesla}}$**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **central conductor method** and the **coil method** of indirect magnetization. Compare their applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hollow steel cylinder of **outer diameter 100 mm**, **inner diameter 80 mm**, and **length 600 mm** is to be magnetized **circularly** using a central conductor carrying a peak current such that the **tangential field strength $H$ is 3000 A/m**. Calculate the **magnetizing current** required. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Central Conductor Method:**
- A solid copper bar or heavy flexible cable is passed **through the hollow bore** of the part.
- Current from a magnetizing console is passed through this central conductor.
- The current-carrying conductor produces a **magnetic field in the form of concentric circles** in the surrounding steel (governed by the right-hand rule for a straight conductor).
- This produces **circular magnetization** in the cylindrical wall of the part, which detects **axial (longitudinal) defects**.
- **Best for:** Rings, sleeves, cylinders, and hollow shafts.
- **Diagram:**

```
    Cross-sectional view:
    +---------------------------+
    |  Steel Wall               |
    |   +-------------------+   |
    |   |                   |   |
    |   |  Central Conductor|<-- I (current)
    |   |       (Cu bar)    |   |
    |   |                   |   |
    |   +-------------------+   |
    |   <-- Magnetic flux lines |
    |   (concentric circles)    |
    +---------------------------+
```

**Coil Method (Solenoid Method):**
- The part is placed **inside a multi-turn helical coil**.
- When current flows through the coil, a **longitudinal magnetic field** is produced inside the coil along its axis (right-hand rule for solenoid).
- The part placed inside the coil experiences a magnetic field **parallel to its long axis**, magnetizing it longitudinally.
- **Best for:** Bars, shafts, and elongated components.
- **Diagram:**

```
    +-----------------------------+
    |  Coil turns carrying current|
    |  + + + + + + + + + + + +   |
    |  + + + + + + + + + + + +   | ---> Magnetic flux N -> S
    |  + + + + + + + + + + + +   |     (longitudinal)
    |  + + + + + + + + + + + +   |
    +-----------------------------+
       Test part inside:  ======>  (Field direction along axis)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Central Conductor | Coil Method |
|---|---|---|
| Field Direction | Circular | Longitudinal |
| Best for Defect Type | Longitudinal / axial cracks | Transverse / circumferential cracks |
| Suitable Part Geometry | Hollow parts | Solid elongated parts |
| Equipment | Heavy bar or cable | Multi-turn wound coil |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the geometry.** The magnetic flux lines are circular in the steel wall. The effective path length for the field in a cylindrical part is its **circumference at the mean radius**.

**Step 2: Calculate the mean diameter.**

$$
D_{\text{mean}} = \frac{D_{\text{outer}} + D_{\text{inner}}}{2} = \frac{100 + 80}{2} = 90 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the mean circumference.**

$$
C = \pi \cdot D_{\text{mean}} = \pi \cdot 90 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.2827 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Use the field-current relationship for circular magnetization.**

$$
H = \frac{I}{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = H \cdot C
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the given values.**

- $H = 3000$ A/m
- $C = 0.2827$ m

$$
I = 3000 \cdot 0.2827
$$

**Step 6: Final calculation.**

$$
I = 848.2 \text{ A}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The required magnetizing current is **$\boxed{I \approx 848 \text{ A}}$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use the outer or inner diameter directly.** You MUST use the **mean diameter** to find the mean circumference, because the magnetic field exists in the steel wall, not at the bore or the outer skin alone. This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit consistency is critical.** If $D$ is in mm, convert to m by multiplying by $10^{-3}$ before using $H$ in A/m. Students who forget this get an answer in the wrong order of magnitude (e.g., 0.848 A instead of 848 A) and lose all 7 marks.
> 3. **Never confuse the field equation:** For a solenoid, $H = NI/L$. For a single straight conductor or central conductor, $H = I/C$. Mixing these two will give completely wrong answers.
> 4. **Sketches must include arrows** for current direction and the resulting field direction using the right-hand rule. A sketch without arrows is treated as incomplete and costs 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Magnetization** is the induction of a magnetic field in a ferromagnetic part to detect flaws via leakage fields.
- The two **fundamental field directions** are **Circular** (current through the part or central conductor) and **Longitudinal** (current through a coil).
- The **Right-Hand Rule** for a straight conductor gives the **circular field** direction; for a solenoid, it gives the **longitudinal field** direction.
- The **Golden Rule:** Magnetic flux must be **perpendicular to the expected defect** for the defect to be detected.
- **Direct methods** include Prods, Contact Heads, Induced Currents, and Permanent Magnets.
- **Indirect methods** include Coils, Central Conductors, and Yokes.
- **Current types:** AC (surface), DC (deep), HWAC (general purpose), FWAC (deep, smooth), 3-Phase FWAC (critical parts).
- **Key formula for a solenoid:** $H = NI/L$.
- **Key formula for circular magnetization:** $H = I/C$, where $C$ is the mean circumference.
- **Key formula for flux density:** $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$.
- **Ampere-turns (MMF):** $F = N \cdot I$.
- **Standards to remember for KTU answers:** **ASTM E709**, **ASTM E1444**, **ASME Section V Article 7**, and **IS 5334** (Indian Standard for MPT).
- **Multi-directional magnetization** is mandatory when defect orientation is unknown.
- **Always cite the standard** and justify the current value with a calculation in your board answer.
- **Permanent magnets** are used when no power is available; **yokes** are the most common portable MPT tool for field welds.
- **Prod spacing** should be **75–200 mm** for localized inspection; too wide a spacing reduces the field strength below detection levels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetization Techniques: Head Shot Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Magnetization Techniques: Head Shot Technique # 🔍 Head Shot Technique — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition The **Head Shot Technique** (also called the **Prod Technique** or **Two-Probe Method**) is a *direct-contact* magnetization method used in **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**. In this technique, two prods (electrode contacts) are pressed firmly against the surface of a ferromagnetic test piece, and a heavy **low-voltage, high-amperage alternating (or rectified) current** is passed between them *through the part itself*. The current flowing through the localized region between the prods generates a **circulating magnetic field** that lies in a plane perpendicular to the direction of current flow, as dictated by the **Right-Hand Grip Rule**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Head Shot method belongs to the family of **Direct Magnetization Techniques**, alongside *Current Flow Technique (Through magnetization)* and *Coil Technique (Indirect magnetization)*. It is specifically designed for *localized inspection* of inaccessible zones, large castings, weld joints, and irregular geometries. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are holding a garden hose that is leaking at several spots. The water sprays out sideways, perpendicular to the hose's flow. Now imagine the hose is the **electric current** and the water sprays are the **magnetic field lines**. Wherever the hose passes close to a defect (a "kink" or "weak spot"), the magnetic field "leaks" out and forms poles — **N** and **S** — on the surface. Sprinkle iron filings (magnetic particles) over the hose, and they will pile up exactly at the leak points. In the **Head Shot Technique**, the *two prods* are like your two hands pinching a section of the hose, forcing current through a small zone. The "leak" is the **defect** (crack, inclusion, or void) and the *iron filings* are the dry magnetic powder or wet fluorescent particles sprayed on the surface. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot > [!NOTE] > **Head Shot at a Glance** > - **Magnetization type:** Direct (current passes *through* the part) > - **Field direction:** Circular around the current path > - **Defects detected best:** Transverse to current flow (perpendicular to the prod-prod line) > - **Typical current range:** 100 A – 1250 A > - **Prod spacing (S):** 75 mm to 200 mm > - **Standard reference:** **ASTM E709**, **ASTM E1444**, **ASME Section V Article 7** ## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used - **Magnetic permeability of free space (μ₀)** = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** = **1.256637 × 10⁻⁶ H/m** - **Magnetic field strength (H)** — measured in **A/m (SI)** or **Oersted (CGS)** (1 Oe = 79.577 A/m) - **Magnetic flux density (B)** — measured in **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (G)** (1 T = 10⁴ G) - **Conversion:** **B (T) = μ₀ · H (A/m)** for free space # 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The Head Shot technique follows a precise sequence: 1. **Surface Preparation:** The test surface is cleaned, degreased, and dried to remove oil, paint, rust, or scale. The surface must have a roughness that does not impede particle mobility (typically Ra ≤ 25 μm). 2. **Prod Placement:** Two copper or aluminum prods (with replaceable soft-metal tips to prevent arcing) are pressed firmly against the surface at a defined spacing **S**. 3. **Current Application:** A low-voltage (~5–10 V), high-amperage AC, half-wave DC, or full-wave DC current is applied between the prods via a **magnetic particle power pack**. 4. **Field Generation:** The current creates a **circular magnetic field** in the plane of the surface, with field lines forming concentric circles around the current path. 5. **Particle Application:** While the current is flowing, **dry magnetic powder** (or **wet fluorescent particles**) is dusted/sprayed on the surface. 6. **Defect Indication:** At any **discontinuity** (crack, porosity, lap, seam, etc.), the magnetic field "leaks out" of the part forming local **N–S poles**. The particles are attracted to these poles and form a visible **indication**. 7. **Demagnetization:** After inspection, the part is demagnetized (since residual magnetism may interfere with subsequent machining or service). 8. **Evaluation:** The indication is observed under white light (visible particles) or **UV-A light (365 nm)** for fluorescent particles. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Physics | Phenomenon | Engineering Reason | |---|---| | **Why circular field?** | Because current passes between two point contacts, the current spreads radially in all directions within the local zone. By the *Right-Hand Grip Rule*, the magnetic field wraps around each current streamline. | | **Why detect transverse defects?** | A defect running *across* (transverse to) the field lines causes maximum flux leakage. Field lines prefer to travel through the high-permeability steel; when they hit a low-permeability air-filled crack, they exit the surface → **N–S leakage poles** form. | | **Why AC vs DC?** | AC gives **surface indications only** (skin effect), DC (especially **half-wave DC**) gives **subsurface indications** up to ~6 mm deep. | | **Why use prods with soft tips?** | To prevent **arc burns**, **spitting**, and **local heating** at the contact zone. Lead, copper braid, or aluminum strips are used as contact tips. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All key formulas required for solving numerical problems on this topic.** | # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Magnetic field strength at distance *r* from current | $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ | A/m (for straight current element) | | 2 | Magnetic flux density in steel | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) | | 3 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | | 4 | Magnetizing current rule (prod spacing) | $I = (3.5 \text{ to } 4.5) \times S$ | **A** with **S in mm** (typical: 3.5 A/mm) | | 5 | Alternative rule (ASTM) | $I = 1.25 \text{ A/mm of prod spacing}$ | Conservative for AC | | 6 | Field intensity in CGS (Oersted) | $H_{Oe} = \dfrac{0.2 \cdot I}{r}$ | Oe with I in A, r in cm | | 7 | Prod spacing limitation | $S \geq 75 \text{ mm}$ (min) , $S \leq 200 \text{ mm}$ (max) | To avoid contact burns | | 8 | Maximum prod current | $I_{max} \approx 1250 \text{ A}$ | Standard MP power pack | | 9 | Sensitivity for subsurface (DC) | Penetration $\approx 25 \text{ mm}$ | For half-wave DC at 1000 A | | 10 | Particle size (dry) | $10 \text{ – } 60 \text{ μm}$ | Visible black or red powder | | 11 | Particle size (wet) | $1 \text{ – } 10 \text{ μm}$ | Fluorescent under UV-A (365 nm) | | 12 | Skid spacing (for wheel inspection) | $S = 2 \times \text{ wheel flange width}$ | For railway applications | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall:** Always remember that in Head Shot, the **magnetic field is parallel to the surface and perpendicular to the prod-prod line**. Therefore, the **technique detects defects that lie *perpendicular* to the prod-prod line**, NOT defects running parallel to it. This is the most common conceptual error in KTU answers. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Head Shot technique is the **workhorse of in-service inspection** for: - 🚂 **Railway axles and wheels** — detection of fatigue cracks near bearing seats - ⚙️ **Large turbine shafts and rotors** — where coil magnetization is impractical - 🛢️ **Pressure vessel nozzle welds** — localized examination - 🌉 **Bridge girders and weldments** — field inspection of large structures - 🏗️ **Cast iron pump housings** — surface-breaking cracks - ✈️ **Aircraft landing gear** — for detecting surface fatigue # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Magnetic Field Strength Around a Prod Contact The Head Shot method can be modeled as **current diverging radially from a point source** (the prod contact) into a semi-infinite steel medium. Consider a single prod injecting current **I** into a conducting half-space. By symmetry, the current density at a distance **r** from the prod spreads over a hemispherical surface of area $2\pi r^2$. The current density vector is: $$J(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$ The magnetic field intensity at radius **r** is derived from **Ampère's Circuital Law**: $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enclosed}$$ For a circular path of radius **r** lying in a plane perpendicular to **J**, symmetry gives constant **H** along the path: $$H \cdot (2\pi r) = I$$ Solving for **H**: $$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$ This is the foundational relation. The field falls off as **1/r**, meaning the field is strongest *close* to the prod and weakens with distance. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Problem > **[Example]** A ferromagnetic plate of thickness 25 mm is to be inspected using the Head Shot method. The prod spacing is **S = 150 mm**. Calculate: > (a) The recommended magnetizing current using ASTM E709. > (b) The magnetic field strength at a distance of **50 mm** from one prod, assuming the other prod is far away. > (c) Whether the chosen current is sufficient to magnetize the full 25 mm thickness. ### **Part (a): Recommended Current** Using the ASTM rule: $I = 1.25 \text{ A/mm} \times \text{prod spacing}$ $$I = 1.25 \text{ A/mm} \times 150 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{I = 187.5 \text{ A}}$$ ### **Part (b): Field Strength at r = 50 mm** Using the formula $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$: $$H = \frac{187.5 \text{ A}}{2\pi \times 0.05 \text{ m}}$$ $$H = \frac{187.5}{0.3142} \text{ A/m}$$ $$\boxed{H \approx 596.8 \text{ A/m}}$$ Converting to Oersted: $$H_{Oe} = \frac{596.8}{79.577} \approx 7.50 \text{ Oe}$$ ### **Part (c): Sufficiency Check** For full 25 mm thickness magnetization in low-carbon steel, a minimum of **≈ 2400 A/m (≈ 30 Oe)** is recommended. The calculated 596.8 A/m at 50 mm is sufficient, but the field *near* the prods may be excessively high (causing false indications or burns). Therefore, the inspector should: - Use **AC** for surface-only inspection (skin effect limits penetration to ~2 mm) - Use **half-wave DC** for full-thickness inspection - Ensure good prod contact to avoid localized overheating ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Head Shot | Application Scenario | Head Shot Suitable? | Reason | |---|---|---| | Large forging with localized defect suspicion | ✅ **Yes** | Pinpoints specific zones | | Small bolt inspection | ❌ No | Use coil/yoke instead | | Subsurface defect (5 mm deep) | ✅ **Yes (HWDC)** | DC penetrates deeper | | Non-ferromagnetic (Al, SS austenitic) | ❌ No | Material not magnetizable | | Welded plate — HAZ inspection | ✅ **Yes** | Prods can straddle weld | | Crowded component with no access | ❌ No | Cannot place prods | | Field/in-service inspection | ✅ **Yes** | Portable power packs available | ## 3.4 Sensitivity Limits & Sources of Error > [!WARNING] > **Common Loss-of-Mark Areas in KTU Valuation:** > - Forgetting to mention **Right-Hand Rule** (mandatory for explaining field direction). > - Confusing "transverse" and "longitudinal" defect orientation. > - Omitting the **3.5 A/mm** or **1.25 A/mm** rule when asked for current. > - Not specifying **AC vs HWDC** based on defect depth. > - Missing the **demagnetization** step. | Error Source | Effect | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | Poor prod contact | Arc burns, false indications | Use soft-metal tips, clean surface | | Insufficient current | Missed fine defects | Verify with pie gauge or known defect | | Excessive current | Burn marks, magnetism at prod tips | Reduce current, use larger spacing | | Surface contamination | Particles stick to dirt, not defects | Pre-clean to SA 2.5 finish | | Wrong field orientation | Misses parallel defects | Rotate prods 90° and re-inspect | | Demagnetization omitted | Residual magnetism in service | Use demag coil after inspection | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Process Flow of the Head Shot Technique ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Test Piece Received] --> B[Surface Preparation
Clean, Degrease, Dry] B --> C[Visual Pre-Inspection
Identify Suspect Zones] C --> D[Select Prod Spacing S
75 mm to 200 mm] D --> E[Position Two Prods
Firm Contact on Surface] E --> F[Select Current Type
AC / HWDC / FWDC] F --> G[Apply Magnetizing Current
I = 3.5 x S A] G --> H[Apply Magnetic Particles
Dry or Wet Fluorescent] H --> I[Observe Indications
White Light or UV-A 365 nm] I --> J{Defect Indication
Found?} J -->|Yes| K[Mark, Record, Photograph] J -->|No| L[Rotate Prods 90°
Re-inspect for Orthogonal Defects] L --> I K --> M[Demagnetize Part] M --> N[Final Report] N --> O[End] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Magnetization System ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Mains Supply
415 V 3-phase AC] --> Rect[Rectifier Unit
AC to DC Conversion] Rect --> Control[Current Control Panel
0 to 1250 A] Control --> Switch[Contactor Switch
Foot or Hand Operated] Switch --> Prods1[Prod 1
Copper with Soft Tip] Switch --> Prods2[Prod 2
Copper with Soft Tip] Prods1 --> Part[Test Piece
Ferromagnetic Material] Prods2 --> Part Part --> Field[Circular Magnetic Field
H = I over 2 pi r] Field --> Part Part --> Indic[Flux Leakage
at Defects] Indic --> PartApp[Magnetic Particle
Application Nozzle] PartApp --> Eval[Visual Evaluation
UV-A Lamp Optional] Eval --> Demag[Demagnetization Coil] Demag --> Output[Accepted or Rejected] ``` ## 4.3 Field Orientation & Defect Detection Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[Test Piece Surface View - Plan] P1A[Prod A]:::prodStyle P2A[Prod B]:::prodStyle P1A -->|Current I| P2A P2A -.->|Circular Field Lines| P1A Defect1[Defect T1
TRANSVERSE to prod line
DETECTABLE]:::detectStyle Defect2[Defect T2
PARALLEL to prod line
NOT DETECTABLE]:::notDetectStyle end classDef prodStyle fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef detectStyle fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef notDetectStyle fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Head Shot vs Other Direct Techniques ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Magnetic Particle Testing
Direct Magnetization Family] Start --> A1[Current Flow Through
Entire Part] Start --> A2[Head Shot / Prod
Local Current Path] Start --> A3[Central Conductor
Hollow Parts] A1 --> B1[Whole Part Magnetized
Detects All Orientations] A2 --> B2[Local Zone Only
Detects Transverse to Prods] A3 --> B3[Circumferential Field
Detects Longitudinal Defects] B1 --> End[Selection Based on
Part Geometry] B2 --> End B3 --> End ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** What is the **Head Shot technique** in magnetic particle testing? State any two advantages. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** The Head Shot technique is a localized direct magnetization method in which two electrode prods are placed on the surface of a ferromagnetic part and a heavy current is passed through the part between the prods, generating a circular magnetic field in the plane of the surface. - **Advantages (1 mark):** 1. Suitable for inspecting **large parts** that cannot be magnetized in a coil. 2. Allows **selective inspection** of suspected localized areas without magnetizing the whole component. 3. Portable and ideal for **in-service field inspections**. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Why is the **Right-Hand Rule** essential in the Head Shot method? Mention the typical current magnitude range. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Right-Hand Rule (2 marks):** The Right-Hand Rule determines the **direction of the magnetic field** generated by the current. When the thumb points in the direction of conventional current flow (from one prod to the other), the curled fingers indicate the direction of the circular magnetic field. This helps the inspector **orient the prods** such that the field is perpendicular to the suspected defect, ensuring maximum flux leakage and visible indication. - **Current range (1 mark):** Typically **100 A to 1250 A**, depending on prod spacing and part thickness. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Do **not** confuse the Head Shot field direction (in-plane, circular) with the Yoke technique (out-of-plane, between poles). > - Always state the **ASTM E709 current rule** explicitly when numerical values are involved. > - Drawing the field pattern with arrows is **mandatory** for full marks — verbal explanation alone fetches only 60% credit. > - Mentioning **demagnetization** is a free 1 mark — examiners often use it as a "hidden" differentiation. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the principle, procedure, and applications of the **Head Shot technique** in magnetic particle testing with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel plate of thickness **20 mm** is to be inspected using the Head Shot method with a prod spacing of **120 mm**. Calculate: 1. The recommended magnetizing current. 2. The magnetic field strength at **40 mm** from one prod. 3. Comment on whether **AC** or **HWDC** should be used for detecting a defect **3 mm below the surface**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) Principle, Procedure, and Applications (7 Marks):** - **Principle (2 marks):** When current passes between two prods through a ferromagnetic part, a **circular magnetic field** is generated in the plane of the surface, perpendicular to the current direction. Any discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion) lying transverse to the field causes **flux leakage**, which attracts magnetic particles and forms a visible indication. - **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Clean the surface to remove contaminants. 2. Place two prods firmly on the surface, separated by the required spacing **S**. 3. Apply the magnetizing current (AC, HWDC, or FWDC) using a power pack. 4. Sprinkle dry magnetic powder or spray wet fluorescent particles. 5. Observe indications under white light (or UV-A 365 nm for fluorescent). 6. Demagnetize the part. 7. Record and report the indications. - **Applications (2 marks):** 1. Inspection of **large castings** for surface cracks. 2. Examination of **welded joints** in bridges, pressure vessels, and pipelines. 3. In-service inspection of **railway axles** and **turbine shafts**. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** Prod spacing **S = 120 mm**, thickness = 20 mm, r = 40 mm. **Part 1: Recommended Current (2 marks)** Using ASTM E709 rule: $I = 1.25 \text{ A/mm} \times S$ $$I = 1.25 \times 120 = \boxed{150 \text{ A}}$$ **Part 2: Magnetic Field Strength (3 marks)** $$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r} = \frac{150}{2\pi \times 0.04}$$ $$H = \frac{150}{0.2513}$$ $$\boxed{H \approx 596.8 \text{ A/m} \approx 7.5 \text{ Oe}}$$ **Part 3: AC vs HWDC Selection (2 marks)** A defect **3 mm below the surface** is a **subsurface** defect. AC provides only **surface indications** (skin effect ~1–2 mm). **Half-wave DC (HWDC)** should be used because it penetrates deeper (up to ~6 mm) and can detect sub-surface defects effectively. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **field pattern and defect detection orientation** in the Head Shot technique. Discuss why certain defects are missed and how to overcome this. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Head Shot** and **Yoke** techniques of magnetization in terms of (i) field type, (ii) field direction, (iii) part accessibility, (iv) depth of inspection, and (v) typical application. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) Field Pattern and Defect Orientation (7 Marks):** - **Field Pattern (3 marks):** The current enters at Prod A and exits at Prod B, spreading radially through the steel. By the Right-Hand Grip Rule, the magnetic field forms **concentric circles** in the plane of the surface around the current path. The field strength is maximum near each prod and decreases with radial distance. - **Sketch:** (A circular loop with prod points, current flow arrow, magnetic field arrows tangential, and a transverse defect showing flux leakage.) - **Defect Detection Orientation (2 marks):** Defects that are **transverse** (perpendicular) to the prod-prod line cut the maximum number of field lines and produce the strongest indications. Defects running **parallel** to the prod-prod line lie *along* the field lines and produce **no flux leakage** — they are missed. - **Overcoming Missed Defects (2 marks):** To detect orthogonal defects, the **prods are repositioned 90°** and the inspection is repeated. Two orthogonal Head Shot passes ensure complete coverage in the inspected zone. This is called **"double prodding"** or **"rotational inspection."** --- **(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Head Shot Technique | Yoke Technique | |---|---|---| | (i) **Field type** | Circular field, in-plane of surface | Longitudinal field, between poles | | (ii) **Field direction** | Tangential, around current path | Perpendicular to surface, N–S between poles | | (iii) **Part accessibility** | Needs two accessible contact points | Needs access on opposite faces | | (iv) **Depth of inspection** | Surface (AC) to ~6 mm (HWDC) | Surface only (~2–3 mm) | | (v) **Typical application** | Large castings, weld joints, in-service shafts | Small parts, field weld inspection, local spot checks | | **Portability** | Requires power pack (heavy) | Highly portable, handheld | | **Demagnetization** | Usually needed | Sometimes not needed (open circuit) | *(1.4 marks per correct row × 5 rows = 7 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2** - ✅ Head Shot = **Direct, localized magnetization** using **two prods**. - ✅ Current = **3.5 A/mm to 4.5 A/mm** of prod spacing (practical rule) or **1.25 A/mm** (ASTM conservative). - ✅ Prod spacing range: **75 mm to 200 mm** (minimum to avoid arcing). - ✅ Field is **circular and in-plane**, direction given by **Right-Hand Grip Rule**. - ✅ Detects **transverse defects** (perpendicular to prod-prod line). - ✅ **AC → surface defects only** (skin effect). **HWDC → subsurface up to 6 mm**. - ✅ Use **soft-metal prod tips** (lead, aluminum) to prevent arc burns. - ✅ Always **demagnetize** after inspection. - ✅ Prods must be rotated **90°** and re-applied to catch orthogonal defects. - ✅ Field strength: $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ (A/m). - ✅ Conversion: **1 Oe = 79.577 A/m**; **1 T = 10⁴ Gauss**. - ✅ Standards: **ASTM E709, ASTM E1444, ASME Section V Article 7**. - ✅ **Limitations:** Contact burns, false indications near prods, not suitable for non-magnetic materials, requires clean surface access. - ✅ **Application sweet spots:** Railway axles, large castings, welded bridges, turbine rotors, pressure vessel nozzle welds. - ✅ **Examiner's hot keywords:** "Right-Hand Rule," "3.5 A/mm," "transverse defect," "demagnetization," "ASTM E709."

topics.title: Cold Shot Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Shot Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cold Shot Technique** is a specialized non-destructive testing (NDT) method primarily employed in **welding inspection** to evaluate the **fit-up quality, alignment accuracy, and geometric integrity** of joints prior to the actual welding operation. It is a low-cost, preliminary visual and dimensional verification technique used to detect misalignments, root gaps, root face heights, and angular distortions in the preparation stage of welded fabrications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Cold Shot Technique is classified under **Visual Testing (VT)** and **Dimensional Inspection** in Module 2 of PESFT521. It is a *pre-weld* quality control method that simulates the weld bead geometry using a temporary consumable or measurement gauge to verify joint preparation conformance against design tolerances.

The technique derives its name from the practice of depositing a short, temporary "cold" bead (a tack weld or temporary filler) at strategic points along the joint to verify dimensional parameters without committing to the full weld. The bead cools rapidly (hence "cold") and is later removed or incorporated into the final weld.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to assemble a complex piece of furniture. Before driving any screws permanently, you would first hold the pieces together, check if the edges line up properly, measure the gaps with a ruler, and verify the angles with a square. Only when you are confident about the fit would you commit to permanent assembly.

The **Cold Shot Technique works exactly like this "dry run" for welding**. Before laying down an expensive, time-consuming weld pass, the inspector performs a temporary placement check to verify that:
- The two pieces to be joined are properly aligned
- The gap between them matches the design specification
- The angle of preparation is correct
- There are no visible defects in the joint preparation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a tailor pinning fabric together before sewing. The pins (cold shots/tack welds) hold pieces in place temporarily to verify the fit, drape, and alignment. The final stitching (actual weld) is done only after the tailor is satisfied. Similarly, a cold shot is the "pin" that confirms the joint geometry before the "seam" is welded permanently.

## 1.3 Classification in NDT Hierarchy

The Cold Shot Technique falls within the following NDT classification framework:

| NDT Branch | Cold Shot Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Visual Testing (VT)** | Primary category - relies on direct observation |
| **Dimensional Inspection** | Secondary category - uses gauges and templates |
| **Pre-Weld Inspection** | Tertiary category - applied before actual welding |
| **Qualitative NDT** | Pass/Fail type evaluation, no quantitative imaging |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Inspection Standard:** **ASME Section V, Article 9** (Visual Examination)
- **Acceptance Criteria:** **AWS D1.1, Clause 5** (Fabrication Tolerances)
- **Typical Root Gap Tolerance:** **±1.5 mm** for plate thickness up to 25 mm
- **Angular Misalignment Limit:** **≤ 5% of plate thickness** (t = plate thickness)
- **Root Face Height Range:** **1 mm to 3 mm** depending on welding process
- **Cold Shot Length (typical):** **25 mm to 50 mm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These standard metrics (**bolded values above**) form the numerical backbone of KTU examination questions. Memorize the typical root gap tolerance and angular misalignment limit formula, as they appear frequently in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic - Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Cold Shot Technique follows a structured seven-step operational procedure. Each step is explained with the underlying **"Why"** and **"How"** for clarity.

### Step 1: Joint Preparation Review
- **What:** The inspector examines the prepared joint surfaces for cleanliness, absence of contaminants, and proper machining.
- **Why:** Contaminants (oil, rust, paint) can affect the cold shot's weldability and the accuracy of fit-up measurement.
- **How:** Visual inspection under adequate lighting (≥ **500 lux** for general surfaces).

### Step 2: Dimensional Measurement of Joint Geometry
- **What:** Measure the root gap, root face, included angle, and plate thickness.
- **Why:** Establish a baseline against which the cold shot's behavior will be evaluated.
- **How:** Use specialized welding gauges (e.g., **Hi-Lo Gauge, Fillet Weld Gauge, Bridge Cam Gauge**).

### Step 3: Trial Fit-Up Assembly
- **What:** The two components are temporarily clamped or tacked together using small temporary welds.
- **Why:** To simulate the actual welding condition without committing to the full weld.
- **How:** Use clamps, wedges, or short tack welds placed at intervals of **200-300 mm**.

### Step 4: Cold Shot Deposition
- **What:** A short, controlled weld bead is deposited at a strategic location (typically at a non-critical zone or test coupon).
- **Why:** To verify that the joint geometry supports proper weld metal flow and fusion.
- **How:** Using the same welding process parameters intended for the production weld, but for a short duration (typically **3-5 seconds** of arc time).

### Step 5: Visual and Dimensional Evaluation
- **What:** The cold shot is inspected for bead shape, penetration profile, and absence of defects.
- **Why:** Any irregularity in the cold shot indicates a problem with the joint preparation.
- **How:** Visual examination, dimensional measurement, and sometimes **dye penetrant testing** of the removed cold shot.

### Step 6: Decision Point - Accept or Reject
- **What:** Based on the cold shot's performance, the joint is either approved for full welding or sent back for rework.
- **Why:** Prevents costly repair of full welds by catching issues at the preparation stage.
- **How:** Comparison against acceptance criteria from applicable codes (ASME, AWS, EN).

### Step 7: Documentation and Traceability
- **What:** The results are recorded in inspection logs with photographs and measurements.
- **Why:** Quality assurance and regulatory compliance require documented evidence.
- **How:** Use standardized inspection report forms (e.g., **ASME Form Q-1** for weld mapping).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential mathematical relationships and tolerances used in the Cold Shot Technique.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Angular Misalignment Limit** | $\theta_{max} \leq 0.05 \cdot t$ | Maximum allowed misalignment as fraction of thickness | degrees |
| **Root Gap Tolerance** | $G_{actual} - G_{specified} \leq 1.5$ | Deviation from specified root gap | mm |
| **Root Face Height** | $1 \leq h_{rf} \leq 3$ | Typical range for most welding processes | mm |
| **Included Angle (V-groove)** | $45 \leq \alpha \leq 60$ | Standard V-groove preparation angle | degrees |
| **Tack Weld Spacing** | $200 \leq s_{tack} \leq 300$ | Distance between temporary tacks | mm |
| **Cold Shot Length** | $L_{cs} = 25 \text{ to } 50$ | Typical deposited length for verification | mm |
| **Inspection Lighting** | $I_{min} = 500$ | Minimum required illuminance | lux |
| **Penetration Depth Check** | $P_{actual} \geq 0.75 \cdot t_{throat}$ | Minimum penetration for full-strength joint | mm |
| **Weld Bead Width Ratio** | $R_{bw} = \frac{W_{bead}}{t_{plate}}$ | Typically 1.5 to 2.5 for good profiles | dimensionless |
| **Heat Input Verification** | $H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | To ensure cold shot mimics production heat input | kJ/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** The angular misalignment formula $\theta_{max} \leq 0.05 \cdot t$ (where $t$ is in mm) gives the result in radians, not degrees. For conversion: $\theta_{degrees} = \theta_{radians} \times \frac{180}{\pi}$. A common exam trap is to forget this conversion and lose **2 marks** in Part B calculations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Cold Shot Technique finds critical applications in several high-stakes engineering sectors:

- **Pressure Vessel Manufacturing:** Nuclear and petrochemical industries use cold shots to verify circumferential and longitudinal weld fit-up before final welding. A misalignment of even **2 mm** in a **100 mm thick** pressure vessel shell can cause stress concentrations that reduce fatigue life by **30-40%**.

- **Pipeline Construction:** In cross-country pipelines (API 5L), cold shot verification is mandatory at every field joint to ensure girth weld integrity.

- **Shipbuilding:** Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register) require cold shot trials for critical structural welds in hull and superstructure.

- **Aerospace Welding:** Turbine casing and engine mount welds undergo cold shot trials with **0.1 mm** tolerance levels.

- **Bridge Construction:** Box girder and orthotropic deck welds are pre-qualified using cold shot mock-ups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost-Benefit Insight:** The Cold Shot Technique, despite its simplicity, can save industries **$50,000 to $500,000 per avoided weld rework** in heavy fabrication. It is considered one of the highest ROI (Return on Investment) NDT methods in the pre-weld phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Angular Misalignment Tolerance for Butt Joints

**Problem Statement:** A butt joint between two plates of thickness $t = 20$ mm is to be welded. Calculate the maximum permissible angular misalignment according to standard cold shot acceptance criteria.

### Given Data
- Plate thickness: $t = 20$ mm
- Standard formula: $\theta_{max} \leq 0.05 \cdot t$ (result in radians)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the standard misalignment formula**

$$\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times t$$

This is the empirically derived limit where stress concentration factors remain within acceptable bounds (typically $K_t \leq 1.3$).

**Step 2: Substitute the plate thickness value**

$$\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times 20$$

Multiplying the dimensionless coefficient by the thickness value gives the maximum angular deviation in radians.

**Step 3: Calculate the result**

$$\theta_{max} = 1.0 \text{ rad}$$

This result is in radians and must be converted to degrees for practical interpretation.

**Step 4: Convert to degrees for engineering interpretation**

$$\theta_{max}^{\circ} = 1.0 \times \frac{180}{\pi}$$

Using the standard conversion factor where $\pi \approx 3.14159$, we perform the division.

**Step 5: Compute the final value**

$$\theta_{max}^{\circ} = \frac{180}{3.14159} \approx 57.3^{\circ}$$

### Final Result

The maximum permissible angular misalignment is $\theta_{max} \approx 57.3°$, but for practical cold shot inspections, the threshold is typically capped at **5°** regardless of calculation, making the 0.05t rule a theoretical upper bound.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cold Shot Heat Input Equivalence

**Problem Statement:** A production weld requires voltage $V = 25$ V, current $I = 200$ A, and travel speed $v = 5$ mm/s. Determine the heat input per unit length that the cold shot must replicate.

### Given Data
- Arc Voltage: $V = 25$ V
- Welding Current: $I = 200$ A
- Travel Speed: $v = 5$ mm/s

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the heat input formula**

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{v \times 1000}$$

The factor 60 converts mm/s to mm/min, and 1000 converts J to kJ.

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values**

$$H = \frac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{5 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the numerator**

$$25 \times 200 = 5000 \text{ W}$$

$$5000 \times 60 = 300{,}000 \text{ J/min}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the denominator**

$$5 \times 1000 = 5000 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 5: Divide to obtain heat input per unit length**

$$H = \frac{300{,}000}{5000} = 60 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Final Result

The cold shot must be deposited with a heat input of **60 kJ/mm** to accurately simulate production welding conditions. Deviation beyond **±10%** of this value invalidates the cold shot's representativeness.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Root Gap Deviation Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A design specifies a root gap of $G_{spec} = 2.5$ mm. Three measurements during cold shot trial give $G_1 = 2.8$ mm, $G_2 = 2.3$ mm, $G_3 = 3.1$ mm. Determine if the joint passes cold shot acceptance.

### Given Data
- Specified root gap: $G_{spec} = 2.5$ mm
- Tolerance: $\Delta G_{max} = \pm 1.5$ mm
- Measurements: $G_1 = 2.8$ mm, $G_2 = 2.3$ mm, $G_3 = 3.1$ mm

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate deviation for each measurement**

$$\Delta G_1 = G_1 - G_{spec} = 2.8 - 2.5 = +0.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta G_2 = G_2 - G_{spec} = 2.3 - 2.5 = -0.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$\Delta G_3 = G_3 - G_{spec} = 3.1 - 2.5 = +0.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compare each deviation against the tolerance**

$$\vert \Delta G_1 \vert = 0.3 \text{ mm} \leq 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\vert \Delta G_2 \vert = 0.2 \text{ mm} \leq 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\vert \Delta G_3 \vert = 0.6 \text{ mm} \leq 1.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Calculate the mean deviation**

$$\overline{\Delta G} = \frac{0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.6}{3} = \frac{0.7}{3} \approx 0.233 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since all individual deviations are within tolerance and the mean deviation is well below **1.5 mm**, the joint **passes** the cold shot acceptance criteria.

### Final Result

The joint is **APPROVED** for production welding. All measurements fall within the **±1.5 mm** tolerance band.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

The following matrix maps defect types to their cold shot indicators and corrective actions:

| Defect Type | Cold Shot Indicator | Severity | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Excessive Root Gap** | Excessive penetration, sagging bead | High | Reset to specification, re-tack |
| **Insufficient Root Gap** | Lack of penetration, unfused root | Critical | Rework joint preparation |
| **High-Low Misalignment** | Asymmetric bead, undercut on one side | High | Grind and re-align plates |
| **Incorrect Root Face** | Inconsistent bead width | Medium | Re-machine root face |
| **Contaminated Surface** | Porosity, slag inclusion in bead | Critical | Clean and re-prepare |
| **Wrong Included Angle** | Poor fusion at groove walls | High | Re-grind groove to spec |
| **Inadequate Tack Quality** | Tack failure during cold shot | Medium | Replace tacks with qualified welder |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cold Shot Process Flow Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the complete workflow of the Cold Shot Technique from initial inspection to final approval:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Joint Preparation Complete] --> B[Visual Examination of Groove Geometry]
    B --> C[Measure Root Gap with Hi-Lo Gauge]
    C --> D[Measure Root Face and Included Angle]
    D --> E{Meets Dimensional Specifications?}
    E -- No --> F[Issue Non-Conformance Report]
    F --> G[Rework Joint Preparation]
    G --> B
    E -- Yes --> H[Apply Temporary Tack Welds]
    H --> I[Deposit Cold Shot at Test Zone]
    I --> J[Inspect Cold Shot Bead Profile]
    J --> K{Defects Detected in Cold Shot?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Identify Root Cause]
    L --> M[Apply Corrective Action]
    M --> I
    K -- No --> N[Document Inspection Results]
    N --> O[Approve Joint for Production Welding]
    O --> P[End: Production Welding Authorized]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Cold Shot Technique in the context of non-destructive testing of welds. List any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The Cold Shot Technique is a pre-weld non-destructive testing method used to verify the dimensional accuracy and fit-up quality of welded joints before actual welding operations commence. It involves depositing a short, controlled weld bead (the "cold shot") at a strategic location to simulate production welding conditions and validate joint geometry.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Butt joint fit-up verification** in pressure vessel and pipeline fabrication
2. **Angular misalignment detection** in thick plate welding for shipbuilding and structural engineering

---

### Question 2: Tolerance Parameter `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the standard angular misalignment tolerance formula used in Cold Shot Technique? Explain the significance of the coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

The standard angular misalignment tolerance formula is:

$$\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times t$$

where $t$ is the plate thickness in mm and $\theta_{max}$ is in radians.

**Significance of the coefficient 0.05:** This empirical coefficient represents the maximum angular deviation that keeps the stress concentration factor ($K_t$) below the critical threshold of approximately **1.3**. Beyond this limit, fatigue life of the welded joint decreases exponentially. The coefficient ensures that the misalignment-induced secondary bending stresses remain within **10%** of the primary weld stresses, preserving structural integrity under cyclic loading.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Detailed Analysis of Cold Shot Application `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: A circumferential butt joint in a pressure vessel shell is to be welded. The plate thickness is 30 mm, the specified root gap is 3 mm, and the included angle of the V-groove is 60°.**

**(a) Calculate the maximum permissible angular misalignment for this joint and convert it to degrees. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Cold Shot trial on this joint, including the acceptance criteria and possible defects that may be detected. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Angular Misalignment Calculation**

**Step 1: State the given values**
- Plate thickness: $t = 30$ mm
- Formula: $\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times t$

**Step 2: Calculate the angular misalignment in radians**

$$\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times 30 = 1.5 \text{ radians}$$

**Step 3: Convert radians to degrees**

$$\theta_{max}^{\circ} = 1.5 \times \frac{180}{\pi}$$

$$\theta_{max}^{\circ} = 1.5 \times 57.2958$$

$$\theta_{max}^{\circ} \approx 85.94^{\circ}$$

**Step 4: Apply practical engineering limitation**

While the theoretical limit is approximately **85.94°**, practical cold shot acceptance criteria cap the maximum misalignment at **5°** for thick-section welds, as the 0.05t formula represents an absolute upper bound that is rarely achieved in practice.

**Final Answer:** The maximum permissible angular misalignment is **1.5 rad (≈ 85.94°)**, but practically limited to **5°** for this application. (7 marks)

**Part (b): Cold Shot Trial Procedure**

**Step 1: Pre-Inspection Preparation**
- Clean the joint surfaces to remove oil, rust, and contaminants
- Verify adequate lighting (≥ 500 lux)
- Calibrate measurement instruments (Hi-Lo gauge, vernier caliper)

**Step 2: Initial Dimensional Verification**
- Measure root gap: $G_{actual}$ vs $G_{spec} = 3$ mm (tolerance ±1.5 mm)
- Measure root face height: 1-3 mm acceptable
- Measure included angle: $60° \pm 2°$

**Step 3: Trial Assembly**
- Clamp plates in position
- Apply tack welds at 200-300 mm intervals
- Verify plate alignment using straightedge and feeler gauges

**Step 4: Cold Shot Deposition**
- Use the same welding process (e.g., SMAW) and parameters as production
- Deposit a 25-50 mm bead at a non-critical location
- Maintain heat input equivalent to production (within ±10%)

**Step 5: Cold Shot Evaluation**
- Inspect bead for:
  - Uniform penetration profile
  - Absence of porosity or cracks
  - Symmetric bead shape (indicating proper alignment)
  - Adequate fusion with base metal

**Step 6: Acceptance Decision**

| Parameter | Measured | Criteria | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Angular Misalignment | $\theta_{measured}$ | $\leq 5°$ practical | Pass/Fail |
| Root Gap Deviation | $\vert G_{actual} - 3 \vert$ | $\leq 1.5$ mm | Pass/Fail |
| Bead Defects | Visual | None | Pass/Fail |

**Step 7: Documentation and Approval**
- Record all measurements in inspection log
- Photograph cold shot for archival
- Issue acceptance certificate or non-conformance report

**Possible Defects Detected:**
1. **Excessive penetration** indicating oversized root gap
2. **Lack of penetration** indicating insufficient root gap or contaminated surface
3. **Undercut on one side** indicating high-low misalignment
4. **Porosity** indicating surface contamination or moisture
5. **Inconsistent bead width** indicating variable root face height

(7 marks)

---

### **Question B: Comparative and Analytical Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: (a) Compare the Cold Shot Technique with Dye Penetrant Testing and Radiographic Testing in terms of principle, application stage, cost, and defect detection capability. (7 marks)**

**(b) A fabricator performs Cold Shot trials on three different joints with the following data: Joint 1: misalignment = 0.8°, root gap deviation = +0.5 mm; Joint 2: misalignment = 4.5°, root gap deviation = +1.8 mm; Joint 3: misalignment = 6°, root gap deviation = -0.3 mm. Classify each joint as ACCEPT, CONDITIONAL ACCEPT, or REJECT with justification. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Comparative Analysis**

| Parameter | Cold Shot Technique | Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) | Radiographic Testing (RT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Deposits temporary weld bead to verify fit-up geometry | Capillary action draws penetrant into surface-breaking defects | Differential X-ray absorption reveals internal density variations |
| **Application Stage** | **Pre-weld** (before production welding) | **Post-weld** (after welding complete) | **Post-weld** (after welding complete) |
| **Cost** | **Very Low** (only consumables and labor) | Low (penetrant kit, ~$100-500) | **Very High** (radiation source, shielding, certified personnel) |
| **Defect Type** | Geometric/dimensional issues (gap, alignment, angle) | Surface-breaking defects (cracks, porosity, laps) | Internal defects (porosity, inclusions, lack of fusion) |
| **Defect Depth** | N/A (geometry only) | Surface only | Through-thickness |
| **Equipment** | Welding machine, gauges | Penetrant, cleaner, developer | X-ray/Ir-192 source, film, darkroom |
| **Time Required** | 10-30 minutes per joint | 30-60 minutes per joint | 2-4 hours per joint (including exposure and processing) |
| **Operator Skill** | Welding inspector | Trained NDT Level II | NDT Level II/III with radiation safety certification |
| **Quantitative Output** | Yes (dimensional measurements) | No (qualitative only) | Yes (with densitometry and IQI) |
| **Safety Concerns** | Minimal (welding hazards) | Chemical exposure | **High** (ionizing radiation) |

(7 marks)

**Part (b): Joint Classification**

**Given Criteria:**
- Angular misalignment limit: $\leq 5°$ (practical limit)
- Root gap tolerance: $\pm 1.5$ mm

**Joint 1 Analysis:**
- Misalignment: $0.8° \leq 5°$ ✓ **PASS**
- Root gap deviation: $\vert +0.5 \vert = 0.5$ mm $\leq 1.5$ mm ✓ **PASS**
- **Classification:** **ACCEPT** — Both parameters are well within tolerance. The joint is approved for production welding without conditions.

**Joint 2 Analysis:**
- Misalignment: $4.5° \leq 5°$ ✓ **PASS** (marginally)
- Root gap deviation: $\vert +1.8 \vert = 1.8$ mm $> 1.5$ mm ✗ **FAIL**
- **Classification:** **CONDITIONAL ACCEPT** — The angular misalignment is within limit, but the root gap exceeds tolerance by 0.3 mm. Conditional acceptance is possible if the root gap can be adjusted (e.g., by adding a spacer shim) before final welding, subject to a re-trial.

**Joint 3 Analysis:**
- Misalignment: $6° > 5°$ ✗ **FAIL** (exceeds by 1°)
- Root gap deviation: $\vert -0.3 \vert = 0.3$ mm $\leq 1.5$ mm ✓ **PASS**
- **Classification:** **REJECT** — The angular misalignment exceeds the practical limit of 5°. The joint must be re-aligned by grinding, re-machining, or re-clamping, followed by a complete re-trial. Root gap alone being within tolerance is insufficient for approval.

**Summary Table:**

| Joint | Misalignment | Root Gap Deviation | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint 1** | 0.8° (Pass) | +0.5 mm (Pass) | **ACCEPT** | All parameters within limits |
| **Joint 2** | 4.5° (Pass) | +1.8 mm (Fail) | **CONDITIONAL ACCEPT** | Root gap marginally exceeds; remediable |
| **Joint 3** | 6° (Fail) | -0.3 mm (Pass) | **REJECT** | Misalignment exceeds 5° limit; rework required |

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert radians to degrees in the angular misalignment formula. Always state the final answer in degrees for engineering interpretation.
> 2. **Misinterpreting the 0.05t Formula (–3 marks):** The formula gives radians, NOT degrees. Additionally, the 5° practical cap is NOT a formula output but an engineering judgment based on stress concentration. Do not present 5° as derived from 0.05t without clarification.
> 3. **Confusing Cold Shot with Tack Welding (–2 marks):** Cold shots are temporary verification beads, NOT production tack welds. Students often conflate the two, losing marks on definition questions.
> 4. **Ignoring Conditional Acceptance Logic (–2 marks):** In classification problems, students often give binary Pass/Fail answers. KTU expects nuanced judgment: ACCEPT, CONDITIONAL ACCEPT (with remedial action), or REJECT.
> 5. **Omitting Safety and Documentation (–1-2 marks):** Part B answers on procedures must include safety precautions and documentation steps. Omitting these loses easy marks.
> 6. **Formula Presentation (–1 mark):** Always write formulas using proper LaTeX formatting. Handwritten or verbally described formulas in exam scripts receive partial credit only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Definition:** Cold Shot Technique = Pre-weld NDT method for fit-up verification using temporary weld bead deposition
- ✅ **Primary Purpose:** Detect geometric defects (gap, alignment, angle) before committing to production welding
- ✅ **Classification:** Visual Testing (VT) + Dimensional Inspection
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $\theta_{max} = 0.05 \times t$ (radians), practically capped at **5°**
- ✅ **Root Gap Tolerance:** **±1.5 mm** from specification
- ✅ **Root Face Range:** **1-3 mm**
- ✅ **Standard V-Groove Angle:** **45-60°**
- ✅ **Tack Spacing:** **200-300 mm**
