- ✅ **Cold Shot Length:** **25-50 mm**
- ✅ **Inspection Lighting:** **≥ 500 lux**
- ✅ **Cost:** Very low (consumables + labor only)
- ✅ **Application Stage:** **Pre-weld** (unique among major NDT methods)
- ✅ **Defect Types Detected:** Misalignment, incorrect gap, wrong angle, surface contamination, inadequate tack quality
- ✅ **Standards Reference:** ASME Section V Article 9, AWS D1.1 Clause 5
- ✅ **Decision Categories:** ACCEPT / CONDITIONAL ACCEPT / REJECT
- ✅ **Documentation:** ASME Form Q-1 or equivalent inspection log

### 🔑 High-Yield Exam Triggers

Watch for these question patterns in KTU exams:
- "Calculate maximum angular misalignment for plate thickness X mm" → Use $\theta = 0.05t$, convert to degrees
- "Determine if joint passes/fails given measurements" → Apply both angular AND gap criteria
- "Compare Cold Shot with [other NDT method]" → Use the comparative table structure
- "Explain procedure with diagram" → Include all 7 steps with safety and documentation
- "State acceptance criteria" → Mention both angular (5° or 0.05t) and gap (±1.5 mm) limits

### 💡 Memory Aid: **COLD SHOT Acronym**

- **C** - Check joint geometry first
- **O** - Observe with adequate lighting (500 lux)
- **L** - Lay temporary tacks at 200-300 mm
- **D** - Deposit cold shot (25-50 mm bead)
- **S** - Study bead profile for defects
- **H** - Heat input must match production (±10%)
- **O** - Observe acceptance criteria (5° angle, ±1.5 mm gap)
- **T** - Take photographs and document

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Central Conductor Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Central Conductor Testing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Central Conductor Testing (CCT)**, also referred to as the **Central Conductor Method (CCM)**, is a specialized variant of **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** used primarily for the volumetric inspection of **non-ferromagnetic tubes** (austenitic stainless steel, copper alloy, brass, titanium, and Inconel tubes). The technique employs a centrally located current-carrying conductor that is drawn through the bore of the tube under test, generating a uniform primary alternating magnetic field that links the entire circumference of the tube wall, thereby inducing eddy currents whose perturbations are measured by an external probe. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT521, Module 2):** > Central Conductor Testing belongs to the family of *Eddy Current NDT Methods* and is conventionally grouped with **bobbin coil testing**, **remote field testing**, and **multi-frequency testing** as a tube-inspection protocol. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to articulate the **principle, instrumentation, and comparative advantage** of this method over conventional bobbin probes. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Hose Inside a Pipe Imagine you are inspecting a long metal garden pipe for hidden cracks. You cannot see the inner wall from outside, and the pipe is too narrow to thread a camera through. So, you do something clever — you slide a **flexible copper wire (the central conductor)** through the entire length of the pipe, and then pump a strong, rapidly alternating electrical current through it. This current, just like water rushing through a hose, creates a powerful, invisible "magnetic ripple" in the surrounding metal wall of the pipe. Now you walk a **magnetic sensor** slowly along the outside of the pipe. Where the wall is solid and uniform, the magnetic ripple bounces back predictably. But where there is a **crack, thinning, or corrosion pit**, the ripple is distorted — and your sensor records this distortion on a graph. That is essentially how Central Conductor Testing works. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Numerical Anchors Used in CCT:** > - **Excitation Frequency (f):** typically between **10 Hz and 1 MHz**, with most industrial CCT systems operating in the **1 kHz to 500 kHz** band. > - **Central Conductor Current (I):** of the order of **100 A to 1000 A** (high current is mandatory to energize the full tube wall). > - **Inspection Speed (v):** typically **0.5 m/s to 5 m/s** during in-service inspection campaigns. > - **Depth of Penetration (δ):** governed by the standard **skin-effect** equation. > - **Reference Standard:** ASTM E977 – *Standard Guide for Eddy Current Examination of Magnetic Saturation and Electromagnetic Acoustic Transducer Applications* (referenced by KTU 2024 NDT syllabus as a related protocol). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics Step-by-Step The Central Conductor Method follows a four-stage physical cascade: 1. **Primary Field Generation:** A high-magnitude alternating current (typically sinusoidal) is passed through a single straight conductor (a flexible copper cable or rigid rod) that is mechanically centered along the axis of the tube under inspection. By **Ampère's Circuital Law**, this current generates a concentric alternating magnetic field around the conductor. 2. **Coupling into the Tube Wall:** Because the conductor lies concentric with the tube, the magnetic flux lines link the tube wall **circumferentially** with near-perfect rotational symmetry. This couples energy into the conductive tube wall through the entire 360° perimeter — something a bobbin probe cannot achieve for tubes with internal features. 3. **Induction of Eddy Currents:** The time-varying primary flux induces **eddy currents** in the tube wall (per **Faraday's Law of Induction**). These eddy currents are circular and flow in closed loops within the wall material. 4. **Defect Detection via Secondary Field Sensing:** A search probe (Hall-effect sensor, encircling coil, or differential coil) moved along the outer surface of the tube detects the **resultant magnetic field**, which is the vector sum of the primary field and the secondary field generated by the eddy currents. Any discontinuity (crack, void, OD/ID pitting, wall thinning) locally alters the eddy current distribution, perturbing the secondary field and producing a measurable signal. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Central Conductor Conventional bobbin probes work well only for *symmetric* defects. The reason CCT is so powerful is that the **axial symmetry of the central conductor guarantees that the primary field is uniform around the tube's circumference**. This means a defect anywhere on either the ID or the OD surface will distort the field, and the external probe will sense it. Furthermore, because the field penetrates the entire wall thickness, CCT is **volumetric**, not merely surface, making it sensitive to subsurface flaws. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** > In Central Conductor Testing, the depth of inspection is determined by the **skin depth (δ)** of the material. By selecting an appropriate test frequency, the inspector can choose to be sensitive to **OD defects only**, **ID defects only**, or to the **entire wall** — a unique tuning capability not offered by simple bobbin probes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Primary Magnetic Field Intensity (at radius r) | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ | A/m | From Ampère's Law for infinite straight conductor | | Magnetic Flux Density | $B$ | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | | Depth of Penetration (Skin Depth) | $\delta$ | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | metres (m) | $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m) | | Skin Depth (Engineering form) | $\delta$ | $\delta = 503.3\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | millimetres (mm) | $\rho$ in $\mu\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}$, $f$ in Hz | | Resistivity Conversion | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ | $\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ | Required to convert from conductivity | | Induced EMF in Tube Wall | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = -N\,\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Volts (V) | Faraday's Law — $N$ turns normalised to 1 | | Frequency – Skin Depth Relationship | $f$ | $f \propto \dfrac{1}{\delta^{2}}$ | Hz | Halving the skin depth requires 4× the frequency | | Magnetic Field Strength in Tube Wall | $H_z$ | $H_z = H_0\,e^{-x/\delta}$ | A/m | Exponential decay with depth $x$ into conductor | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The absolute value notation in the magnetic field magnitude has been written as $\lvert H \rvert$ using LaTeX vertical bars — this is to **prevent breaking the markdown table** in PDF/HTML rendering pipelines. Always use $\lvert ... \rvert$ in KTU answer scripts when mathematical clarity is required. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Central Conductor Testing is the inspection method of choice in the following KTU-relevant industrial sectors: - **Power Generation:** Inspection of **condenser tubes**, **feedwater heater tubes**, and **boiler tubes** in thermal and nuclear power plants. - **Petrochemical Industry:** In-service inspection of **heat-exchanger tubes** in crude preheaters and overhead condensers. - **Aerospace:** Quality control of **hydraulic lines**, **fuel lines**, and **aircraft hydraulic tubing**. - **Nuclear Steam Generators:** Critical for the detection of **Outside Diameter Stress Corrosion Cracking (ODSCC)** in Inconel steam generator tubes, where conventional bobbin probes are blind to certain axial cracks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnetic Field of an Infinite Straight Conductor (Ampère's Law) **Statement:** The magnetic field intensity $H$ at a radial distance $r$ from a long straight conductor carrying current $I$ is given by $H = I / (2\pi r)$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1 — Apply Ampère's Circuital Law.** For any closed Amperian loop in a magnetic field, Ampère's Law states: $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enclosed}$$ **Step 2 — Choose a Symmetric Amperian Loop.** By symmetry, the magnetic field lines around an infinite straight conductor are concentric circles. We therefore choose a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$ coaxial with the conductor. By symmetry, $\lvert H \rvert$ is constant in magnitude along this loop and tangential to it. $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \oint dl = H \cdot 2\pi r$$ **Step 3 — Equate and Solve.** Since the enclosed current is simply $I$: $$H \cdot 2\pi r = I$$ $$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to Flux Density.** Multiplying by the permeability of the medium ($\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$): $$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2\pi r}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in CCT:** In Central Conductor Testing, the probe is positioned at a fixed radius (just outside the tube's OD). Therefore, the *primary* field strength at the probe location is a direct function of the current $I$ in the central conductor. Calibrating $I$ is equivalent to calibrating the *excitation baseline* of the entire test. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depth of Penetration (Skin Depth) **Statement:** The depth of penetration $\delta$ at which the eddy current density falls to $1/e$ ($\approx 36.8\%$) of its surface value is given by $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. **Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1 — Maxwell's Equation for Conductor Interior.** Inside a conductor, the time-varying magnetic field satisfies the diffusion equation: $$\nabla^{2} \vec{H} = \mu \sigma \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 2 — Assume Sinusoidal Steady-State Excitation.** Let the field vary as $H(x,t) = H_0(x)\,e^{j\omega t}$. Substituting: $$\frac{d^{2} H_0}{dx^{2}} = j\omega \mu \sigma \, H_0$$ **Step 3 — Solve the Ordinary Differential Equation.** The general solution of this linear ODE is: $$H_0(x) = A\,e^{kx} + B\,e^{-kx}$$ where $k = \sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma}$. Using Euler's identity, $k = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. **Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition at $x \to \infty$.** For a semi-infinite conductor, $H_0 \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$, so $A = 0$. Thus: $$H_0(x) = H_s\,e^{-kx} = H_s\,e^{-(1+j)x\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$ **Step 5 — Define Depth of Penetration.** The amplitude decays as $e^{-x/\delta}$ where $\delta$ is the *characteristic decay length*: $$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$ Therefore the magnetic field amplitude in the tube wall is: $$H(x) = H_s\,e^{-x/\delta}$$ and the corresponding eddy current density $J(x) = \sigma E(x) = \sigma (\partial B/\partial t \cdot \text{const})$ follows the identical exponential decay profile. **Step 6 — Engineering Form.** Substituting $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \mu_r$ H/m, $\sigma = 1/\rho$, and converting to mm: $$\delta \,(\mathrm{mm}) = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho \,(\mu\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm})}{\mu_r \cdot f \,(\mathrm{Hz})}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Worked Numerical Example (Mandatory KTU Calculation):** > A copper tube of resistivity $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ $\Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$ = $1.724$ $\mu\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}$, $\mu_r = 1$, is inspected at $f = 100$ kHz. Compute the skin depth. $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.724}{1 \times 100000}} \, \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{1.724 \times 10^{-5}} \, \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot 4.152 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\delta \approx 2.09 \, \mathrm{mm}}$$ **Interpretation:** Since typical tube walls are 1–3 mm thick, a 100 kHz excitation in copper makes the inspection **fully volumetric** — eddy currents penetrate the entire wall and a defect on either surface is detectable. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CCT vs. Other Tube-Inspection ECT Methods | **Parameter** | **Central Conductor Test (CCT)** | **Bobbin Coil Test** | **Remote Field Test (RFT)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Field Geometry** | Circumferential (azimuthal) from axial conductor | Localised through-coil flux | Diffuse double-loop coupling | | **Best Sensitivity** | OD and ID defects of all orientations | Severe localised wall loss | Deep ID defects, uniform wall loss | | **Speed** | High (whole tube wall energised at once) | Moderate | Slow (low frequency) | | **Frequency Range** | 1 kHz – 500 kHz | 10 kHz – 1 MHz | 5 Hz – 500 Hz | | **Material** | Non-ferromagnetic only | Ferromagnetic & non-ferro | Both | | **Key Limitation** | Cannot inspect ferromagnetic tubes | Blind to some axial defects | Slow; signal interpretation complex | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CCT System The diagram below maps the complete signal and energy flow of a Central Conductor Testing setup, from the high-current oscillator to the defect-indication display. ```mermaid flowchart TD OSC[AC Oscillator
High Power Signal Source] -->|High current| CC[Central Conductor
Copper Rod inside Tube] CC -->|Primary alternating field| TW[Tube Wall
Eddy Current Induction Zone] TW -->|Secondary perturbed field| PR[Search Probe
Hall Sensor or Coil] PR -->|Raw voltage signal| AMP[Preamplifier and Filter] AMP -->|Conditioned signal| PHS[Phase Sensitive Detector] PHS -->|X and Y components| DSP[Signal Processor and Comparator] DSP -->|Alarm logic| ALM[Defect Alarm Buzzer] DSP -->|Analog output| CRT[CRT or XY Recorder Display] PR -->|Mechanical pull| MECH[Probe Drive Mechanism
Linear Puller] MECH -->|Position feedback| DSP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the flow from the oscillator to the central conductor (power path), then from the tube wall back through the probe to the display (signal path). The mechanical puller is the **synchronisation link** — its position signal is used to correlate defect indications with axial position along the tube. ## 4.2 Equivalent Magnetic Circuit Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR I1[Central Conductor Current I] -->|Ampere's Law| H1[Primary Field H_p around conductor] H1 -->|Induction| E1[Induced EMF and Eddy Currents in Tube Wall] E1 -->|Opposes primary| H2[Secondary Field H_s from eddy currents] H2 -->|Vector sum| H3[Resultant Field H_res = H_p + H_s] H3 -->|Detection| P1[External Search Probe Voltage] P1 -->|Output| D1[Display Signal] D1 -.->|Defect present| F1[Perturbation in H_res causes Signal Deflection] D1 -.->|No defect| N1[Baseline flat-line output] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The vector sum $\vec{H}_{res} = \vec{H}_p + \vec{H}_s$ is the **fundamental detection principle**. The phase angle between $\vec{H}_p$ and $\vec{H}_s$ encodes defect type information — a trained KTU student should remember that *lift-off, conductivity changes, and permeability changes* all produce distinct phase signatures on the impedance plane. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is Central Conductor Testing? Mention any two of its industrial applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Central Conductor Testing is a non-destructive eddy current method in which a high alternating current is passed through a conductor positioned along the axis of a tube, generating a circumferential magnetic field that induces eddy currents in the tube wall. A search probe on the outer surface detects any perturbations in the secondary field caused by defects. *(1.5 marks)* **Applications:** *(1 mark each, any two)* - Inspection of **steam generator tubes** in nuclear power plants for ODSCC (Outside Diameter Stress Corrosion Cracking). - In-service inspection of **condenser and heat-exchanger tubes** in thermal power stations. - Quality control of **aerospace hydraulic and fuel lines**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List any four advantages of Central Conductor Testing over the conventional bobbin-coil eddy current method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Volumetric inspection:** CCT energises the entire tube wall circumferentially, allowing detection of defects on both inner and outer surfaces. *(0.75 mark)* 2. **Uniform sensitivity around circumference:** The axial symmetry of the central conductor provides a 360° uniform primary field, eliminating the directional blind spots of bobbin probes. *(0.75 mark)* 3. **High inspection speed:** Since the entire wall is energised simultaneously, the probe can be pulled rapidly without loss of coverage. *(0.75 mark)* 4. **Tunable depth of inspection:** By varying the excitation frequency, the operator can choose to inspect OD, ID, or full wall thickness. *(0.75 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]` **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and working of Central Conductor Testing. Discuss the role of the central conductor in generating the primary magnetic field.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Principle:** Central Conductor Testing is based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a high alternating current passes through a conductor placed coaxially inside a tube, it produces a time-varying circumferential magnetic field. This field induces eddy currents in the tube wall. Any defect alters the eddy current pattern, which is detected as a change in the impedance of a search probe placed on the outer surface. *(2 marks)* **Block Diagram:** *(1.5 marks — Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the expected schematic)* **Role of the Central Conductor:** - **Primary field generator:** By Ampère's Law, $H = I/(2\pi r)$, the conductor acts as a uniform source of circumferential flux. *(1 mark)* - **Symmetry provider:** Its axial placement guarantees rotational symmetry, ensuring that the primary field is identical in all radial directions. *(1 mark)* - **Coupling medium:** The conductor must be made of high-conductivity, low-resistance material (copper) to minimise its own voltage drop and ensure most of the energy couples into the tube wall. *(0.75 mark)* - **Tuning parameter:** The current magnitude $I$ and frequency $f$ of the conductor's excitation are the two principal test variables. *(0.75 mark)* --- **(b) Derive the expression for the depth of penetration in eddy current testing. A copper tube (resistivity $1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m}$, $\mu_r = 1$) is tested at a frequency of $400\ \mathrm{kHz}$. Calculate the skin depth. State its significance in CCT.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** *(4 marks — Refer to Section 3.2, Steps 1–6)* The depth of penetration is defined as the depth at which the eddy current density falls to $1/e$ of its surface value: $$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}\ \mathrm{mm}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** *(2 marks)* Given: $\rho = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot \mathrm{m} = 1.724\ \mu\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}$, $\mu_r = 1$, $f = 400\ \mathrm{kHz} = 4 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{Hz}$. $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.724}{1 \times 4 \times 10^{5}}}\ \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{4.31 \times 10^{-6}}\ \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\delta = 503.3 \cdot 2.076 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{mm}$$ $$\boxed{\delta \approx 1.045\ \mathrm{mm}}$$ **Significance in CCT:** *(1 mark)* - If the tube wall thickness $t \ll \delta$, the inspection is **volumetric** (full-wall coverage). - If $t \gg \delta$, the inspection is **surface-only** (OD-side sensitive). - By selecting $f$ such that $\delta \approx t$, the operator ensures maximum sensitivity to through-wall defects. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Supplementary Paper]` **(a) Compare Central Conductor Testing with Bobbin Coil and Remote Field Testing methods. Bring out the relative advantages and limitations of each.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** *(4 marks — Refer to Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix)* | **Aspect** | **CCT** | **Bobbin Coil** | **RFT** | |---|---|---|---| | Field orientation | Circumferential | Through-coil | Double-loop diffuse | | Best for | ID & OD defects in non-ferro tubes | Localised wall loss in ferro & non-ferro tubes | Uniform wall loss, deep ID defects | | Frequency | 1 kHz – 500 kHz | 10 kHz – 1 MHz | 5 Hz – 500 Hz | | Speed | High | Moderate | Low | | Limitation | Non-ferromagnetic only | Limited defect orientation | Complex signal, slow | **Relative Advantages:** *(2 marks)* - CCT is **faster and more symmetric** than bobbin coil. - RFT penetrates deeper into ferromagnetic tubes, where CCT cannot operate. - Bobbin coil is **versatile and inexpensive**, suitable for routine screening. **Limitations:** *(1 mark)* - CCT: Cannot inspect ferromagnetic tubes. - Bobbin coil: Misses some axial defects; sensitive to probe wobble. - RFT: Slow, complex impedance plane interpretation. --- **(b) With the help of a block diagram, describe the instrumentation system of a Central Conductor Testing setup. Explain the function of the phase-sensitive detector.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** *(2 marks — Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram)* **Functional Description of Each Block:** *(3.5 marks)* 1. **AC Oscillator (High-Power Source):** Generates the high-current AC excitation (typically 100–1000 A) at the chosen test frequency. 2. **Central Conductor:** Carries the current through the tube; may be a flexible cable or rigid copper rod. 3. **Tube Wall:** The region where eddy currents are induced; the inspection medium. 4. **Search Probe:** Hall-effect sensor or pickup coil placed on the OD surface; converts magnetic field into voltage. 5. **Preamplifier and Filter:** Boosts the weak probe signal and rejects high-frequency noise. 6. **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD):** Compares the probe signal with a reference from the oscillator; extracts the in-phase (X) and quadrature (Y) components. 7. **Signal Processor and Display:** Renders the X–Y impedance plane trace or the strip-chart recording; triggers alarms when signals exceed calibrated thresholds. **Function of the Phase-Sensitive Detector:** *(1.5 marks)* The PSD is the **heart of the signal processing chain**. It performs synchronous demodulation of the probe signal against a reference derived from the oscillator. This allows separation of the received signal into two orthogonal components: - **X-component (in-phase):** Sensitive primarily to *defect size* and *lift-off*. - **Y-component (quadrature):** Sensitive primarily to *material property changes* (conductivity, permeability). By analysing the X–Y trajectory, an experienced operator can classify the type of defect — for example, distinguishing between a crack, a corrosion pit, and a ferromagnetic inclusion. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > 1. **Do not confuse CCT with Remote Field Testing (RFT).** Examiners specifically deduct marks if a student writes "Remote Field" terminology while answering a CCT question. CCT uses a *single central conductor*; RFT uses *two coils* (exciter + detector) with an *annular spacing*. > 2. **Do not apply CCT to ferromagnetic tubes.** If a student claims CCT is suitable for carbon steel tubes, the examiner will mark it wrong — CCT is fundamentally a *non-ferromagnetic* technique because in ferromagnetic materials the high permeability shields the eddy currents from the central field. > 3. **Always show units in numerical skin-depth calculations.** Writing "δ = 2.09" without "mm" is a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction. > 4. **For derivation questions, you must explicitly mention Faraday's Law and Ampère's Law by name.** Examiners look for these named laws as anchoring points in your solution. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **CCT belongs to Eddy Current NDT** and is specifically designed for **non-ferromagnetic tubes** (austenitic SS, copper alloys, titanium, Inconel). - The **central conductor** is an axially placed current-carrying rod/cable; it does **not** itself sense defects. - The **primary magnetic field** is circumferential and obeys $H = I/(2\pi r)$. - **Eddy currents** are induced in the tube wall per Faraday's Law; their pattern is perturbed by defects. - The **depth of penetration (skin depth)** is $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ metres, or $\delta = 503.3 \sqrt{\rho/(\mu_r f)}$ mm in engineering units. - **Higher frequency** $\Rightarrow$ **smaller skin depth** $\Rightarrow$ **surface-sensitive** inspection. - **Lower frequency** $\Rightarrow$ **larger skin depth** $\Rightarrow$ **volumetric / through-wall** inspection. - **Applications:** Steam generator tubes, condenser tubes, heat-exchanger tubes, aerospace hydraulic lines. - **Cannot be used on ferromagnetic tubes** because the high permeability of the tube material short-circuits the central field before it can induce usable eddy currents. - **Phase-sensitive detection** is the key signal-processing step that allows defect classification via X–Y impedance plane analysis. - **Comparison Anchors:** CCT ≠ Bobbin Coil (single probe, volumetric) ≠ RFT (two-coil, double coupling, low frequency). - **ASTM E977** and related eddy current standards govern the calibration and procedure. - **KTU-typical exam traps:** mixing up CCT and RFT; forgetting units; not naming Faraday's and Ampère's Laws in derivations; claiming CCT works on ferromagnetic materials.

topics.title: Magnetization Using Yokes; Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Magnetization Using Yokes; Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Magnetization in NDT – The Foundation In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, magnetization is the deliberate process of generating a strong magnetic field within a ferromagnetic test piece so that surface and sub-surface discontinuities (cracks, inclusions, laps, porosity) can be detected using **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** or **Magnetic Flux Leakage (MFL)** techniques. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PESFT521 – Module 2):** > Magnetization is the *primary enabling step* of all magnetic NDT methods. Without proper magnetization, defect indications cannot be generated. The *direction* and *strength* of the magnetic field determine whether longitudinal or transverse defects are revealed. ### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Direct Magnetization:** A magnetization technique in which *electric current is passed directly through the test piece* itself, thereby generating the magnetic field inside the part. > *(Sources: ASME Section V Article 7; ASTM E709)* > [!NOTE] > **Indirect Magnetization:** A magnetization technique in which *no current passes through the test piece*; instead, the magnetic field is generated by a separate source (coil, yoke, central conductor) and coupled into the part magnetically. > [!NOTE] > **Yoke (in MPI):** A portable, hand-held magnetization device that establishes a magnetic flux path between two pole pieces through the test piece, used primarily for local magnetization of ferromagnetic components. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "Magnetization = Making Iron Talk" Imagine a quiet classroom of iron atoms. They are all sleeping in random directions. When you bring a strong magnet (or send a current) close to them, they all **stand up in a line** (align their domains), creating an orderly magnetic "column." If there is a hidden crack inside this column, the students cannot pass the line properly — they must "leak" out sideways. This sideways leakage is the **flux leakage** that we detect with iron particles or magnetic sensors. - **Direct magnetization** = telling the students directly to align (current through them). - **Indirect magnetization** = standing a big magnet outside the room and making them align by the field that flows in. - **Yoke** = the big handheld magnet that you can move around the room (portable magnetization). ### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Highlighted) - **Magnetic Field Strength (H)** = **A/m** (Amperes per meter) or Oersted (Oe); **1 Oe ≈ 79.58 A/m** - **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** = **Tesla (T)** or Gauss (G); **1 T = 10,000 G** - **Required magnetization for steels:** ≈ **2,400 A/m (≈ 30 Oe)** for surface indications; ≈ **6,400 A/m (≈ 80 Oe)** for sub-surface indications. - **Permeability (μ)** of ferromagnetic steel: typically **100 – 500 μ₀** (where μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Magnetization Using Yokes ### 2.1.1 What is a Yoke? A **magnetizing yoke** is a portable U-shaped (or articulated) device that establishes a closed magnetic circuit between its two pole tips and the test piece placed between them. It is the most versatile **local magnetization** tool, especially for in-service weld inspection, castings, and large structures where fixed benches cannot be used. ### 2.1.2 Types of Yokes **A. Permanent Magnet Yoke (PM Yoke)** - Uses **Alnico or rare-earth (NdFeB)** permanent magnets. - Requires **no external power** → ideal for hazardous / explosive / field environments. - **Drawback:** Fixed field strength, cannot be switched off (particle removal after inspection is difficult). **B. Electromagnetic (AC) Yoke** - Operates on **Alternating Current (mains 230 V / 50 Hz)** through a coil wound on a laminated iron core. - Provides a **pulsating AC magnetic field** → strong surface sensitivity, good particle mobility. - **Drawback:** Limited penetration (~1–2 mm); skin effect limits sub-surface defect detection. **C. Electromagnetic (DC) Yoke** - Uses a **rectified DC / Half-Wave DC** supply. - Better sub-surface penetration than AC; fewer skin-effect losses. - Yokes are typically rated **2,000 – 4,500 Ampere-Turns (AT)**; KTU-recommended minimum ≈ **40 AT per pole gap of 25 mm** (≈ 1,600 AT/m). > [!IMPORTANT] > **ASME / ASTM Lift-Power Verification (Mandatory KTU Concept):** > A yoke must be capable of lifting a **4.5 kg (10 lb)** test bar (or, per ASTM E709: 4.5 kg or 18 N pull) when the pole spacing is **≤ 150 mm (6 in)**. This is a *quantitative* performance check frequently asked in KTU exams. ### 2.1.3 Operating Principle of a Yoke 1. Place the **two pole tips** of the yoke on a clean, dry surface of the test piece (≈ 50–150 mm apart). 2. Energize the internal coil (for EM yoke) — or rely on the permanent flux (PM yoke). 3. Flux lines travel: **Pole 1 → Test piece → Pole 2 → Yoke core → Pole 1** (closed loop). 4. Flux is **perpendicular to the line joining the pole tips** → detects defects oriented *parallel* to the pole line. ### 2.1.4 Advantages & Limitations of Yokes | Feature | Yoke-Based Magnetization | |---|---| | Portability | Excellent (handheld) | | Power dependency | PM = none; EM = mains | | Field strength | Moderate (~2–4 kA/m at surface) | | Penetration depth | AC: ≤ 2 mm; DC: ≤ 6 mm | | Demagnetization | AC: easy (decay field); PM: difficult | | Best for | Welds, large structures, field NDT | ## 2.2 Direct Methods of Magnetization In **direct magnetization**, the test piece is made part of the electrical circuit. Current enters at one contact and exits at another; the resulting circular magnetic field surrounds the current path. ### 2.2.1 Classification of Direct Methods **1. Prods (Contact Prods / Hand Prods)** - Two pointed copper or aluminium electrodes ("prods") are pressed onto the part. - Current flows from prod 1 → through the part → to prod 2. - Field lines form **circles around the line connecting the two prods**. - **Field strength:** $$H = \frac{I}{2R} \quad \text{(in A/m at center between prods of radius R in metres)}$$ *(this is a standard KTU exam formula derived from Ampère's circuital law).* - **Polarity reversal** is achieved by switching leads. - **Disadvantage:** Burns the contact surface → arcing → pitting. Generally **not allowed by ASME Section V** for aerospace; accepted in general industry (ASTM E709) provided prod tips are clean and contact pressure ≥ 9 kg. **2. Clamps / Direct Contact Heads** - Heavy copper clamps grip both ends of the part. - Used on **bars, billets, tubes** of fixed length and cross-section. - Suitable for **longitudinal magnetization** of axis-symmetric parts. **3. Magnetic Conductor / Threading Bar** - A copper bar is passed through a hollow part; current through the bar creates a circumferential field *inside* the hollow part. - Used for **tubular components** (pipes, cylinders, gun barrels). ### 2.2.2 Why "Direct" Produces a Circular Field Ampère's Right-Hand Rule: when current **I** flows through a conductor, the magnetic field **H** circulates around the conductor in concentric circles. For current flowing through the bulk of a part (between two prods), **H** loops around the current path, making it ideal for detecting **longitudinal (axial) defects**, since the flux must cross any crack that lies perpendicular to the current flow. ## 2.3 Indirect Methods of Magnetization In **indirect magnetization**, the current never passes through the test piece. The field is generated externally and coupled magnetically (or via a central conductor / yoke) into the part. ### 2.3.1 Coil Magnetization (Solenoid Method) — Indirect - The part is placed **inside a multi-turn coil**. - Current through the coil produces a longitudinal field along the part's axis. - **Field inside a long solenoid:** $$H = \frac{NI}{L} \quad (\text{A/m})$$ where N = number of turns, I = current in Amperes, L = length of coil in metres. - **Total Ampere-Turns (NI):** determines the magnetizing force. KTU typical requirement: **NI ≥ 45,000 AT/m** for full longitudinal magnetization of long parts. - For parts whose length > 3× diameter, the **effective magnetization is reduced** by a factor k (Bishop's correction factor): $$H_{\text{effective}} = k \cdot \frac{NI}{L}, \qquad k = \frac{L}{D} \text{ (empirical table, KTU uses L/D ratio correction)}$$ **Two coil arrangements:** - **Through-coil magnetization:** part is entirely inside the coil — flux is longitudinal. - **Coil-shot (encircling coil):** most common indirect method for bars, billets, tubes, axles. ### 2.3.2 Central Conductor Method — Indirect - A heavy copper bar (central conductor) is passed through a hollow part. - The part itself is **not** carrying current. - Current in the central conductor induces a **circumferential magnetic field** in the part. - Used for hollow cylinders, pressure vessels, and pipe ends. ### 2.3.3 Yoke Method — Indirect - The yoke is **not** part of the electrical circuit passing through the piece — current is in the yoke's coil only. - Flux is transferred **magnetically** through the part between pole tips. - Hence, yoke magnetization is classified as an **indirect** method (per ASTM E709 classification). ### 2.3.4 Permanent Magnetization — Indirect (Residual Field) - The part is magnetized by any of the above methods, then **withdrawn from the field source**. - The **residual flux density (Bᵣ)** in the part is sufficient for MPI using dry powder or fluorescent particles. - Suitable only for **high-retentivity materials** (e.g., hardened steels). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Expectation:** Memorize the units and the physical meaning of every formula. Marks are awarded for *correct units* and *correct application to a defect-detection scenario*. | # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = \dfrac{NI}{L}$ | Magnetic field inside a long solenoid (coil magnetization) | A/m | | 2 | $H = \dfrac{I}{2R}$ | Field at centre between two prods separated by 2R (direct, prods) | A/m | | 3 | $H = \dfrac{NI}{5r}$ | Field inside a circular coil of mean radius r (encircling coil, KTU short-form) | A/m | | 4 | $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Flux density in the test piece | Tesla (T) | | 5 | $NI \geq 45{,}000$ | Min. AT for longitudinal magnetization (ASTM E709 long-part rule) | Ampere-Turns | | 6 | $NI \geq 35{,}000 \, d / L$ | Min. AT for shorter parts (d = diameter, L = length) | Ampere-Turns | | 7 | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | Permeability of free space | H/m | | 8 | $F_{\text{lift}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Maxwell stress (yoke lift-power derivation) | Newtons | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (Table-Cell Pipe Escape):** In KTU exam answers, when writing `\vert x \vert` or absolute-value expressions inline with formulas in a table, use **\vert** or **\mid** — *not* the raw pipe `|` — to prevent breaking the markdown table layout. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (ASME Section V):** All ferromagnetic landing-gear, turbine shafts, and wing fittings are 100 % MPI tested using **coil magnetization** and **prods** (with controlled current). - **Welding inspection (on-site):** **Electromagnetic yokes** are the de-facto tool for inspecting weld toes, HAZ, and parent metal of pressure vessels, bridges, and storage tanks. - **Pipeline girth welds:** **Central-conductor indirect magnetization** is the only practical method for circumferential defect detection in the field. - **Automotive forgings:** Production-line magnetizers use **prods or clamps** for 100 % defect screening. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field at the Centre Between Two Prods (Direct Magnetization) We treat the current flowing between the two prods as a **straight current-carrying conductor of finite length**, and apply **Ampère's Circuital Law**: $$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enc}}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1.** Consider the two prods separated by a distance $2R$ (so $R$ is the half-distance). Current $I$ flows from prod A to prod B, and the magnetic field $\vec{H}$ at the midpoint is perpendicular to the line AB (by right-hand rule). **Step 2.** By symmetry, take an Amperian circular loop of radius $R$ centred on the line AB, passing through the midpoint. The enclosed current is $I$, and $\vec{H}$ is tangent to the loop and constant in magnitude (by symmetry). **Step 3.** Evaluate the line integral: $$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \cdot (2\pi R)$$ **Step 4.** Apply Ampère's law: $$H \cdot 2\pi R = I$$ **Step 5.** Solve for $H$: $$H = \frac{I}{2\pi R} \cdot \pi = \frac{I}{2R}$$ $$\boxed{H = \frac{I}{2R} \;\; (\text{A/m})}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Note the factor $\pi$ cancels because the Amperian loop here is a *full circle* (2πR) — this is the textbook result students must reproduce. *Always state the assumption that the field is uniform along the loop.* ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Field Inside a Long Solenoid (Indirect Coil Magnetization) Consider a solenoid of $N$ turns, length $L$, carrying current $I$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1.** Apply Ampère's law to a rectangular Amperian loop *abcd* with one side inside the solenoid (length $\ell$, parallel to the axis) and the other side outside. **Step 2.** The field outside the solenoid is approximately zero (for an infinitely long solenoid). The field inside is parallel to the axis. Therefore the line integral reduces to: $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \cdot \ell$$ **Step 3.** The total current enclosed by the loop equals the number of turns *per unit length* times the length of the inside segment: $$I_{\text{enc}} = \frac{N}{L} \cdot \ell \cdot I = \frac{NI\ell}{L}$$ **Step 4.** Equate and solve: $$H \cdot \ell = \frac{NI\ell}{L}$$ $$\boxed{H = \frac{NI}{L} \;\; (\text{A/m})}$$ **Step 5.** The corresponding magnetic flux density inside the (air-core) solenoid is: $$B = \mu_0 H = \mu_0 \frac{NI}{L}$$ **Step 6.** When the test piece (steel, $\mu_r \gg 1$) is placed inside, the effective flux density becomes: $$B = \mu_0 \mu_r \frac{NI}{L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** The formula $H = NI/L$ applies only to a *long* solenoid. For finite-length parts, the demagnetization factor reduces the effective $H$, and the **Bishop correction factor** $k$ must be used. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lift-Power of an Electromagnetic Yoke The yoke is magnetized by Ampere-turns $NI$. The flux crossing the air gap between the pole tip and the test piece is $\Phi$. The Maxwell stress (force per unit area) on the contact surface is: $$p = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1.** The total attractive force between the pole tip and the test piece is: $$F = p \cdot A = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$ **Step 2.** Substituting $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$: $$F = \frac{\Phi^2}{2\mu_0 A}$$ **Step 3.** Relate $\Phi$ to the magnetomotive force (mmf) and the magnetic circuit reluctance $\mathcal{R}$: $$\Phi = \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}, \qquad \mathcal{R} = \frac{\ell}{\mu_0 A}$$ **Step 4.** Substitute back: $$F = \frac{(NI)^2}{2\mu_0 A \, \mathcal{R}^2}$$ **Step 5.** Eliminate $\mathcal{R}$ using $\mathcal{R} = \ell/(\mu_0 A)$: $$F = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2\ell^2}$$ $$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 A (NI)^2}{2\ell^2} \;\; (\text{Newtons})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The lift force is **proportional to the square of the Ampere-Turns** — doubling the AT quadruples the pull-off force. This is why KTU / ASTM lift tests are an excellent indirect indicator of yoke health. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Direct vs Indirect Magnetization | Parameter | Direct Method (Prods / Clamp) | Indirect Method (Coil / Yoke) | |---|---|---| | Current path | Through the test piece | External (coil / yoke / central conductor) | | Field geometry | Circular (around current path) | Longitudinal (along coil axis) or via pole line | | Defect orientation detected | Transverse to current flow | Transverse to coil axis / pole line | | Contact required? | Yes (burn marks possible) | No (yoke/coil only) | | Suitable for | Massive parts, irregular shapes | Bars, tubes, ring-shaped parts | | Demagnetization needed? | Usually yes | Usually yes | | Risk to part | Arcing, pitting at prods | Low (no contact) | | KTU example | Prod method on welded plate | Encircling coil on a shaft | ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A 1.2 m long steel shaft is magnetized longitudinally using a 600-turn coil carrying 75 A. Calculate (a) the magnetizing force $H$ produced, and (b) the flux density $B$ inside the shaft, given $\mu_r = 500$. ### Solution **(a)** Apply $H = NI / L$: $$H = \frac{600 \times 75}{1.2} = \frac{45{,}000}{1.2} = 37{,}500 \;\; \text{A/m}$$ **(b)** Apply $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$: $$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 500 \times 37{,}500$$ $$B = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \times 37{,}500$$ $$\boxed{B \approx 23.6 \;\; \text{T}}$$ Wait — this exceeds saturation of steel (~1.6 T). In practice, the relative permeability is **not constant**; it drops sharply as $B$ approaches saturation. The correct B is ≈ **1.5 – 1.7 T** for medium-carbon steel. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trap:** Students often forget that $\mu_r$ is *non-linear* for ferromagnetic materials. Always cross-check your $B$ against the material's $B$–$H$ curve before reporting. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Magnetization Process for MPI ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Source
AC Mains or DC Rectifier] --> B[Magnetizing Device
Yoke or Coil or Prods] B --> C[Flux Coupling
Direct or Indirect] C --> D[Ferromagnetic Test Piece] D --> E{Defect Present?} E -- Yes --> F[Flux Leakage
at Crack] F --> G[Particle Accumulation
MPI Indication] E -- No --> H[Uniform Flux Lines
No Indication] G --> I[Inspection Report] H --> I ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Direct vs Indirect Magnetization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph direct1[Direct Magnetization Path] D1[Current Source] --> D2[Prod or Clamp] D2 --> D3[Test Piece
Current passes through it] D3 --> D4[Circular Field
around current path] end subgraph indirect1[Indirect Magnetization Path] I1[Current Source] --> I2[Coil or Yoke or Central Conductor] I2 --> I3[Field generated externally] I3 --> I4[Field coupled into Test Piece
no current through piece] I4 --> I5[Longitudinal or Pole-Line Field] end D4 --> OUT1[Defects transverse to current] I5 --> OUT2[Defects transverse to field] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Yoke Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Power Switch] --> P2[Step Down Transformer] P2 --> P3[Rectifier AC to DC or Half Wave] P3 --> P4[Energizing Coil
wound on laminated core] P4 --> P5[U Shaped Laminated Iron Core] P5 --> P6[Pole Tip 1] P5 --> P7[Pole Tip 2] P6 --> PT[Test Piece between poles] P7 --> PT PT --> P8[Flux Loop Closed] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Magnetization Method ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Start: Select Magnetization Method] --> S1{Part geometry?} S1 -- Solid bar or shaft --> S2{Defect orientation?} S1 -- Hollow tube --> S3[Use Central Conductor Method] S1 -- Weld on large structure --> S4[Use Electromagnetic Yoke] S1 -- Irregular casting --> S5[Use Prod Method] S2 -- Longitudinal defects --> S6[Encircling Coil Longitudinal] S2 -- Transverse defects --> S7[Prod or Clamp Method] S3 --> S8[Circumferential field inside tube] S4 --> S9[Local field between poles] S5 --> S10[Circular field between prods] S6 --> Z[End] S7 --> Z S8 --> Z S9 --> Z S10 --> Z ``` ## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Defect Detection Physics ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Test Piece Magnetized] --> B[Flux Lines align with field] B --> C{Discontinuity encountered?} C -- No --> D[Flux continues straight] C -- Yes --> E[Flux lines bulge out at defect] E --> F[Leakage field above surface] F --> G[Magnetic particles attracted] G --> H[Visible indication formed] H --> I[Inspector evaluates] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between direct and indirect methods of magnetization with two examples each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Direct Method | Indirect Method | |---|---|---| | Current path | Current passes *through* the test piece | Current does *not* pass through the piece | | Field geometry | Circular around the current path | Longitudinal (coil) or pole-line (yoke) | | Contact required | Yes (prods / clamps) | No (coil / yoke) | | Examples | Prod method, Clamp method | Encircling coil, Yoke, Central conductor | *(Examiner awards 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for field geometry, 1 mark for examples.)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the lift-power verification test for an electromagnetic yoke as per ASTM E709. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - The yoke must be capable of lifting a **4.5 kg (10 lb)** ferrous test bar. - The pole spacing must not exceed **150 mm (6 inches)** during the test. - The test is repeated **before each shift** or after any yoke maintenance. - Failure to lift the bar = yoke is *condemned* and withdrawn from service. *(3 marks: 1 each for lift weight, pole spacing, frequency.)* ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **ESE Instruction (KTU 2024):** *Answer ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.* --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **a)** Explain the **construction and working of an electromagnetic AC yoke** used in magnetic particle inspection, with a neat sketch. List its advantages over a permanent magnet yoke. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction (3 marks):** - A **U-shaped laminated silicon-steel core** to minimise eddy-current loss. - An **energizing coil** of insulated copper wire wound on the central limb. - A **230 V / 50 Hz AC mains supply** stepped down to a safe low-voltage, high-current output. - **Two articulated pole tips** (often rotatable through 90°) to adapt to curved surfaces. - A **push-button or toggle switch** to energize / de-energize. - A **fuse** for short-circuit protection. **Working (2 marks):** - When energised, AC current in the coil produces an alternating magnetic flux in the laminated core. - Flux exits from one pole, passes **through the test piece**, re-enters the other pole, completing the magnetic circuit. - The alternating field causes **magnetic particle agitation** (good for wet MPI), producing sharp, mobile indications. - Demagnetization is automatic once the yoke is switched off (flux collapses). **Advantages over PM Yoke (2 marks):** - Adjustable / stronger magnetizing force (current controlled). - Can be **switched off** — easy demagnetization, easier particle removal. - Better **particle mobility** due to AC pulsating field. - Easier to verify quantitatively (lift-power test). **b)** A shaft of length 800 mm and diameter 60 mm is magnetized using a 400-turn solenoid carrying 60 A. Calculate (i) the magnetizing force H, and (ii) the flux density B if the relative permeability of steel is 600. Comment on whether the calculated B is physically realistic. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Magnetizing Force:** $$H = \frac{NI}{L} = \frac{400 \times 60}{0.8} = \frac{24{,}000}{0.8}$$ $$H = 30{,}000 \;\; \text{A/m}$$ **(ii) Flux Density:** $$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 600 \times 30{,}000$$ $$B = 7.5398 \times 10^{-4} \times 30{,}000$$ $$B \approx 22.6 \;\; \text{T}$$ **Comment on physical reality (2 marks):** - The calculated B ≈ 22.6 T is **physically impossible** for medium-carbon steel, since the saturation flux density of steel is only **~1.6 – 2.0 T**. - This discrepancy arises because **μᵣ is NOT constant** — it falls sharply as the material approaches magnetic saturation. - In practice, B would saturate near **1.5 – 1.7 T** and the assumption of constant μᵣ is invalid. - The correct H remains 30,000 A/m, but the **actual B must be read off the B–H curve** of the specific steel grade. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **a)** With a diagram, explain the **prod method of direct magnetization**. State its limitations and one industrial application. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Diagram description (2 marks):** Two prods pressed on a test plate; current I flows from prod 1 → through plate → to prod 2; circular field lines between the prods; defect indicated perpendicular to the line joining the prods. **Working (2 marks):** - Two **prods** (copper/aluminium electrodes with handles and insulation) are pressed onto a pre-cleaned ferromagnetic surface, separated by 50–200 mm. - A low-voltage, high-current source (typically 750–1500 A AC or half-wave DC) sends current through the prods and the part. - A **circular magnetic field** is established around the line joining the prods (right-hand rule). - Defects **perpendicular to the prod line** interrupt the flux and produce leakage indications. **Limitations (2 marks):** - **Arcing / burning** at the contact points damages the part's surface. - **Pitting** may occur at prod contact spots — *prohibited* in aerospace per ASME Section V. - Localized heating; not suitable for thin or heat-treated parts. - Requires accessible contact surfaces on both sides of the part. **Industrial application (1 mark):** - Inspection of **girth welds in pressure vessels, storage tank shells, and bridge components** where on-site MPI is required. **b)** Compare the **yoke method, encircling coil method, and prod method** with respect to (i) field direction, (ii) current path, (iii) best defect orientation detected, and (iv) typical industrial use. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (Tabular format — 7 marks distributed across rows):** | Parameter | Yoke Method | Encircling Coil | Prod Method | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Field direction | Between pole tips (transverse to part) | Longitudinal along the axis | Circular around the prod line | | (ii) Current path | Inside yoke coil only (not through part) | Inside coil only (not through part) | **Through the part** | | (iii) Defect orientation | Defects **perpendicular to pole line** | Defects **transverse to coil axis** (longitudinal cracks) | Defects **perpendicular to prod line** (transverse cracks) | | (iv) Industrial use | On-site weld inspection, in-service NDT | Bars, billets, shafts (longitudinal magnetization) | Heavy castings, large welds | | Classification | Indirect | Indirect | Direct | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Mark Deduction:** > 1. **Misclassifying the yoke as a "direct" method.** The yoke coil carries the current, not the part. The current is *induced* into the part magnetically. So the yoke is **indirect**. Many students lose 2 marks here. > 2. **Confusing the field geometry.** Prods produce *circular* fields; coils produce *longitudinal* fields; yokes produce *transverse* (pole-line) fields. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting units.** Always state H in A/m and B in Tesla. Omitting units is a **0.5–1 mark penalty** per formula. > 4. **Ignoring the Bishop correction factor** for short parts in coil magnetization. Always check if L/D < 3. > 5. **Using μᵣ as a constant** beyond the knee of the B–H curve. This is a **2-mark conceptual error** in numericals. > 6. **Not mentioning the lift-power test (4.5 kg / 150 mm)** when asked about yoke performance. This is a favourite 3-mark KTU question. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Magnetization Using Yokes; Direct & Indirect Methods** - [x] **Yoke = INDIRECT method** (current in yoke coil, NOT in the part). - [x] **Prods & clamps = DIRECT methods** (current passes through the part). - [x] **Coil (solenoid) = INDIRECT method** (longitudinal field). - [x] **Central conductor = INDIRECT method** (for hollow parts). - [x] **Lift-power test:** 4.5 kg (10 lb) at ≤ 150 mm pole spacing (ASTM E709). - [x] **Yoke minimum AT:** ~1,600 AT/m; typical 2,000–4,500 AT. - [x] **Longitudinal magnetization requirement:** NI ≥ 45,000 AT for long parts. - [x] **Key formulas:** $H = NI/L$, $H = I/(2R)$, $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$, $F = \mu_0 A (NI)^2 / (2\ell^2)$. - [x] **Prod field is circular; coil field is longitudinal; yoke field is transverse.** - [x] **Surface defects → AC magnetization; sub-surface defects → DC or Half-Wave DC.** - [x] **PM yokes** = no power, fixed field, hard to demagnetize. - [x] **EM yokes (AC)** = pulsating field, strong particle mobility, ≤ 2 mm penetration. - [x] **EM yokes (DC / HWDC)** = better penetration (≤ 6 mm), preferred for sub-surface defects. - [x] **μᵣ is NOT constant** — always check B–H curve near saturation. - [x] **Always state units** in KTU numericals (A/m for H, T for B, N for F). - [x] **Bishop's correction factor** is mandatory for short parts (L/D < 3) in coil magnetization. - [x] **Arcing / pitting** = prod method's main drawback; **prohibited in aerospace per ASME Section V**. - [x] **Demagnetization** is needed after MPI to prevent remnant flux interference with downstream service (especially bearings, instruments). - [x] **Demagnetization methods:** (i) Reversing DC with decaying amplitude, (ii) AC with gradual withdrawal from coil, (iii) Thermal (above Curie temperature). - [x] **A yoke is portable, can be used on vertical / overhead surfaces** — ideal for in-service weld inspection of tanks, bridges, ship hulls. - [x] **Curie temperature of steel** ≈ 770 °C; loss of ferromagnetism above this.

topics.title: Continuous Testing of MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Testing of MPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Testing (Continuous Magnetization Method)** in Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI) is a magnetization procedure in which the **magnetic particles are applied to the surface of the specimen while the magnetizing current is still flowing through it**. The particle suspension (wet) or dry powder is present on the part *during* the active application of the magnetic field, allowing particles to be drawn to active leakage fields in real time.

This method is also called the **Wet Continuous Method** when using a liquid suspension of ferromagnetic particles, or the **Dry Continuous Method** when using dry ferromagnetic powder. It is universally recognized in **ASTM E709** and **ASME Section V, Article 7** as the standard reference technique for detecting fine, tight surface and slightly sub-surface discontinuities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnet in Action"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a magnet attracting iron filings while it is still switched on.**

Imagine sprinkling iron filings on a *live* bar magnet. The filings instantly jump and align along the magnetic field lines, and more importantly, they cluster heavily at the **poles** (where the field "leaks" out). If a tiny crack existed on the magnet's surface, filings would pile up exactly over that crack — *while* the magnet is active.

- **Continuous MPI** = the filings are sprinkled *while* the magnet is ON.
- **Residual MPI** = the filings are sprinkled *after* the magnet is switched OFF (using leftover magnetism).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Magnetizing Current** | The current (AC, DC, or HWDC) flowing through the part during inspection. |
| **Leakage Field** | Magnetic flux that escapes the part at a defect, attracting particles. |
| **Indication** | The visible accumulation of magnetic particles revealing a discontinuity. |
| **Wet Suspension** | Fluorescent or non-fluorescent particles in a liquid carrier (water or oil). |
| **Demagnetization** | Removing residual magnetism post-inspection. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Continuous testing is mandated by ASME V Article 7 and ASTM E709 for detection of **fine, tight surface cracks** that produce weak leakage fields.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The continuous method follows a strict sequence of events. Each step is governed by electromagnetism principles.

- **Step 1 — Pre-Cleaning of the Specimen**
  - Oil, grease, paint, or scale must be removed.
  - *Why:* Surface contaminants block leakage fields and prevent particle adherence at defect sites.
  - *How:* Solvent cleaning, alkaline cleaning, or vapor degreasing per ASTM E709.

- **Step 2 — Application of Magnetic Particles (Concurrent with Current)**
  - In the **wet method**, the particle suspension is gently flowed/poured over the surface.
  - In the **dry method**, the powder is dusted using a powder blower.
  - *Why:* Particles must be physically present when the field is active to be attracted.

- **Step 3 — Application of Magnetizing Current**
  - Current is passed through the part using prod contacts, coils, or central conductors.
  - Field strength is computed using the **right-hand rule** and ampere-turns formula.
  - Current remains **ON** during particle application and inspection.

- **Step 4 — Particle Migration and Indication Formation**
  - Particles are drawn toward **north–south magnetic poles** formed at defect edges.
  - The indication is **visible immediately** — no waiting for residual decay.

- **Step 5 — Current Cut-off**
  - The current is switched off ONLY after inspection is complete and indications are recorded.

- **Step 6 — Post-Inspection Demagnetization**
  - Critical step: continuous testing leaves **strong residual magnetism**.
  - Demagnetization is performed by subjecting the part to a decaying AC field.

- **Step 7 — Post-Cleaning**
  - Particles are removed to prevent contamination, especially in aerospace and nuclear applications.

### 2.2 The Underlying Physics — Why It Works

The continuous method exploits the **active tangential component** of the magnetic leakage field $H_L$ above a surface crack.

The field at a defect can be approximated as:

$$
H_L \approx \frac{d \, J}{2}
$$

where $d$ is the depth of the crack and $J$ is the magnetizing current density. During continuous testing, the magnetizing force $H$ is at its **maximum** value:

$$
H = H_{\text{applied}} + H_{\text{residual}}
$$

This is why continuous testing is roughly **2× more sensitive** than the residual method for surface defects.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $NI = \frac{H \cdot L}{0.4\pi}$ | Ampere-turns required for magnetization | A·turns |
| $B = \mu_0 (H + M)$ | Flux density inside the part | Tesla (T) |
| $H_L = k \cdot \frac{I}{d}$ | Leakage field strength at crack | A/m |
| $F_m = V \cdot \chi_m \cdot H \cdot \frac{dH}{dx}$ | Magnetic force on a particle | Newtons (N) |
| $I_{\text{cont}} \approx 0.8 \times I_{\text{resid}}$ | Continuous method current is lower than residual | Amperes (A) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** Never use absolute value bars `\|x\|` notation inside a markdown table — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Turbine disks, landing gear, and engine shafts.
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, gears, and axles.
- **Welding Industry:** Detection of weld surface cracks (toe cracks, crater cracks).
- **Railways:** Axle and wheel inspection.
- **Power Generation:** Rotors, bolting, and high-pressure piping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ampere-Turns Requirement

The fundamental relation governing the continuous method is derived from **Ampere's Circuital Law**.

**Starting Point — Ampere's Law:**

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I
$$

For a long ferromagnetic specimen of length $L$ and permeability $\mu$:

$$
H \cdot L = N I
$$

Solving for the magnetomotive force (MMF):

$$
NI = H \cdot L
$$

Converting from CGS to SI using the factor $0.4\pi$ (since $1 \text{ Oe} = \frac{1000}{4\pi} \text{ A/m}$):

$$
NI = \frac{H_{\text{CGS}} \cdot L}{0.4\pi}
$$

This gives the **minimum ampere-turns** required to achieve a tangential field $H$ along a part of length $L$.

> **Logic:** The applied magnetizing current must overcome the demagnetizing effect of the part's geometry. A higher $L$ (longer part) requires proportionally more ampere-turns.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Continuous vs. Residual Current Relationship

In the residual method, only the **retained flux density** $B_r$ acts on the particles. In the continuous method, the total flux density is the sum of applied and residual contributions:

$$
B_{\text{total}} = B_{\text{applied}} + B_{\text{residual}}
$$

Since the leakage field $H_L \propto B$, and typical carbon steel has $B_r \approx 0.4 B_{\max}$:

$$
H_{L,\text{cont}} = H_{L,\text{applied}} + 0.4 \cdot H_{L,\text{applied}}
$$

$$
H_{L,\text{cont}} = 1.4 \cdot H_{L,\text{resid}}
$$

Because the continuous method already supplies the active field, the **required current is approximately 70–80% of the residual method current**:

$$
I_{\text{continuous}} \approx 0.75 \cdot I_{\text{residual}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Continuous Method | Residual Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Particle Application | **During** magnetization | **After** magnetization |
| Sensitivity to Surface Defects | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Sensitivity to Sub-surface | Slight (0.5–1 mm) | Slight |
| Magnetizing Current | Lower | Higher |
| Demagnetization Required | **Mandatory** | Recommended |
| Inspection of Complex Shapes | Easier | Difficult |
| Standards Body | ASTM E709, ASME V Art. 7 | ASTM E709 |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Worked Out Problem

> **Problem:** A steel shaft of length **300 mm** is to be magnetized using a circular magnetization coil. The required tangential field strength is **2400 A/m**. If the coil has **5 turns**, calculate the required magnetizing current using the continuous method.

**Solution:**

$$
NI = \frac{H \cdot L}{0.4\pi}
$$

$$
NI = \frac{2400 \times 0.3}{0.4\pi}
$$

$$
NI = \frac{720}{1.2566} \approx 573 \text{ A·turns}
$$

Since $N = 5$:

$$
I = \frac{NI}{N} = \frac{573}{5}
$$

$$
I \approx 114.6 \text{ A}
$$

> **Continuous method current (75% rule):**

$$
I_{\text{cont}} = 0.75 \times 114.6
$$

$$
I_{\text{cont}} \approx 85.95 \text{ A}
$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **86 A** of magnetizing current is required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Block Diagram — Continuous MPI Testing

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete workflow of the continuous method. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric prefixes, and labels are kept clean of markdown formatting to ensure valid Mermaid parsing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START Inspection] --> A2[Pre Cleaning of Specimen]
    A2 --> A3[Position Part on Magnetizer]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Wet Particle Suspension OR Dry Powder]
    A4 --> A5[Switch ON Magnetizing Current]
    A5 --> A6[Particles Migrate to Active Leakage Fields]
    A6 --> A7{Indications Visible}
    A7 -- YES --> A8[Record and Mark Indication]
    A7 -- NO --> A9[Switch OFF Current]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Demagnetize the Specimen]
    A10 --> A11[Post Cleaning to Remove Particles]
    A11 --> A12[END Inspection Report]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Magnetic Circuit in Continuous Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Power Source AC DC HWDC] --> B2[Magnetizing Coil or Prod Contacts]
    B2 --> B3[Ferromagnetic Specimen]
    B3 -- Magnetic Flux Lines --> B4[Internal Flux B inside Specimen]
    B3 -- Leakage Flux at Defect --> B5[Leakage Field HL above Crack]
    B5 --> B6[Attraction of Ferromagnetic Particles]
    B6 --> B7[Visible Indication on Surface]
    B1 -. AC Decay Field .- B8[Demagnetization Stage]
    B8 --> B9[Residual Field Reduced to Near Zero]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Continuous vs. Residual Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S_CONT[CONTINUOUS METHOD]
        C1[Apply Particles] --> C2[Switch ON Current]
        C2 --> C3[Inspect Indications]
        C3 --> C4[Switch OFF Current]
        C4 --> C5[Demagnetize]
    end

    subgraph S_RES[RESIDUAL METHOD]
        R1[Switch ON Current] --> R2[Switch OFF Current]
        R2 --> R3[Apply Particles]
        R3 --> R4[Inspect Indications]
        R4 --> R5[Demagnetize Optional]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the continuous method, the **Apply Particles** block occurs *before* and *during* the magnetization event — this is the defining temporal sequence. In the residual method, particle application occurs *after* current cut-off.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the continuous method of magnetic particle inspection. State two of its advantages.*

**Model Answer:**

> The continuous method of magnetic particle inspection is a magnetization procedure in which **the magnetic particles are applied to the surface of the specimen while the magnetizing current is flowing** through it. The particles are drawn to active leakage fields generated at surface and sub-surface discontinuities.
>
> **Two advantages:**
> 1. Higher sensitivity to fine, tight surface cracks compared to the residual method.
> 2. Lower magnetizing current is sufficient to produce indications.
> 3. Allows real-time inspection of indications while current is active.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Why is demagnetization mandatory after the continuous method of MPI?*

**Model Answer:**

> During the continuous method, the part is subjected to a strong magnetizing field. Even after the current is switched off, the part retains a **residual magnetic field** due to the hysteresis of ferromagnetic material. This retained magnetism can:
> 1. Attract iron filings and cause **false indications** in subsequent service life.
> 2. Interfere with **electronic instruments** and sensitive measuring devices.
> 3. Cause **arc damage** during subsequent welding.
>
> Hence, demagnetization is performed using a decaying AC field to bring the residual field close to zero.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, procedure, and particle application methods** used in continuous MPI. *\[7 Marks\]*

**(b)** Compare the **continuous method and residual method** of MPI in terms of sensitivity, current requirement, particle application timing, and demagnetization. *\[7 Marks\]*

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Principle:** When a ferromagnetic specimen is magnetized, magnetic flux lines flow uniformly through it. At the site of any surface or sub-surface discontinuity, the flux lines **leak out into the air** (leakage field). Ferromagnetic particles applied during magnetization are attracted to these leakage fields and form a **visible indication** outlining the defect.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Pre-clean the specimen.
  2. Place the part in the magnetizing fixture.
  3. Apply wet suspension or dry powder.
  4. Switch ON the magnetizing current.
  5. Observe indications under proper lighting (UV-A for fluorescent).
  6. Switch OFF the current.
  7. Demagnetize the part.
  8. Post-clean and report.
- **Particle Application Methods:**
  - **Wet Method:** Particles suspended in water or oil carrier (typically 0.1–0.4% by volume for fluorescent, 1–3% for non-fluorescent). Applied by spraying, flowing, or dipping.
  - **Dry Method:** Fine dry powder dusted over the surface using a powder bulb or shaker, with gentle air agitation.

**Model Solution (b):** Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.3 for the full table. Students are expected to list at least **4–5 comparative points** with technical justification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A **circular magnetization coil** of 4 turns is used to magnetize a steel component of length **250 mm**. If the required tangential field is **3200 A/m**, calculate the magnetizing current required for the **continuous method**. *\[7 Marks\]*

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations** of the continuous method and state the safety precautions to be observed during inspection. *\[7 Marks\]*

**Model Solution (a):**

Using the formula:

$$
NI = \frac{H \cdot L}{0.4\pi}
$$

$$
NI = \frac{3200 \times 0.25}{0.4\pi} = \frac{800}{1.2566} \approx 636.6 \text{ A·turns}
$$

For continuous method (75% of residual current requirement):

$$
I_{\text{cont}} = 0.75 \times \frac{NI}{N} = 0.75 \times \frac{636.6}{4}
$$

$$
I_{\text{cont}} = 0.75 \times 159.15 \approx 119.4 \text{ A}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Approximately **119 A**.

**Model Solution (b):**

- **Limitations:**
  1. Requires **demagnetization** post-inspection.
  2. Cannot detect **deeply buried** sub-surface defects.
  3. Inspection of **large parts** requires heavy-duty magnetizing equipment.
  4. **Heat generation** in prod contact method may damage the surface.
  5. The **part orientation** must ensure proper flow of particle suspension.

- **Safety Precautions:**
  1. Use **insulated gloves** and **arc-flash PPE** during prod contact method.
  2. Ensure **electrical isolation** of the magnetizing equipment.
  3. Provide **UV-A protective eyewear** during fluorescent inspection.
  4. Maintain proper **ventilation** for dry powder to avoid inhalation.
  5. Apply **lockout/tagout** before demagnetization cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing particle application timing:** Students often write "particles are applied *after* current is switched off" — this is the *residual* method, not continuous. Marks deducted for incorrect sequence.
> 2. **Forgetting demagnetization:** Marks are lost when the demagnetization step is omitted in procedure questions.
> 3. **Wrong unit in formula:** Using $NI = H \cdot L$ without the $0.4\pi$ factor when the field is in **A/m** (SI units) loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the 75% current rule:** Continuous method uses *lower* current than residual — stating equal current loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE:**

- **Definition:** Continuous MPI = particles applied **during** magnetization.
- **Sensitivity:** Highest for **fine surface cracks**; moderate for sub-surface (0.5–1 mm depth).
- **Current Rule:** $I_{\text{continuous}} \approx 0.75 \times I_{\text{residual}}$ (continuous uses **lower** current).
- **Ampere-Turns Formula:** $NI = \dfrac{H \cdot L}{0.4\pi}$ (SI units).
- **Demagnetization:** **Mandatory** after continuous method — use decaying AC.
- **Particle Methods:** Wet (fluorescent + non-fluorescent) and Dry (powder dusted).
- **Standards:** ASTM E709, ASME Section V Article 7, ISO 9712.
- **Defect Types Detected:** Surface cracks, laps, seams, cold shuts, weld crater cracks, grinding cracks.
- **Limitations:** Cannot detect deep sub-surface defects; requires demagnetization; heat at prod contacts.
- **Safety:** Insulated gloves, UV goggles, proper grounding, lockout/tagout.
- **Indications Form:** During current flow — **real-time observation** possible.
- **Equipment:** Prod contacts, coils, central conductors, yokes, wet benches, UV-A lamps (365 nm).
- **Difference from Residual:** Timing of particle application; sensitivity level; current magnitude.
- **Typical Industries:** Aerospace, automotive, railways, power, pressure vessel fabrication.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Continuous Testing of MPI**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residual Technique of MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Residual Technique of MPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Residual Technique of Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI):** A magnetization method in which the test specimen is **first magnetized** by passing an energizing current (AC or DC) through it, and the **magnetic particles are applied AFTER the magnetizing current has been switched off**, exploiting the **remanent (residual) magnetism** retained within the part to indicate the presence of surface and near-surface discontinuities.

The residual technique relies on a fundamental ferromagnetic property called **retentivity (remanence, $B_r$)** — the ability of a ferromagnetic material to retain a portion of magnetic flux density after the external magnetizing field is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite Concept — Retentivity ($B_r$):** When a ferromagnetic material is placed in a magnetic field $H$ and then removed, the flux density does not drop to zero immediately. It settles at a residual value $B_r$ on the $B$–$H$ hysteresis loop. This residual flux is what reveals flaws in the **Residual Technique**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **permanent magnet** (the bar magnet you played with in school). You rub it against an iron nail several times (magnetization step) and then pull it away. Even after you remove the magnet, the nail itself behaves like a small magnet and can pick up tiny paper clips or iron filings. This "leftover magnetism" in the nail is exactly what **residual magnetism** is.

In the **Residual Technique of MPI**, the engineer does something similar:
1. A strong current is passed through the steel component to "charge" it magnetically (like rubbing the bar magnet on the nail).
2. The current is switched OFF.
3. Even though the current is gone, the steel component is still slightly magnetic (just like the nail).
4. Now, dry iron particles or wet fluorescent particles are sprinkled/applied on the surface.
5. Where there is a **crack or flaw**, the residual magnetic flux leaks out into the air and pulls the particles into a visible indication — forming a dark line or a fluorescent pattern under UV light.

> [!TIP]
> **In one line:** *"Charge the metal magnetically → switch off the current → inspect using leftover magnetism."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Material Constants (Highlighted)

The following standard metrics govern the residual technique:

- **Retentivity / Remanence ($B_r$):** Residual flux density, typically expressed in **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (G)** where $1 \text{ T} = 10{,}000 \text{ G}$. Hard steels: $B_r \approx 0.8 - 1.4 \text{ T}$.
- **Coercivity ($H_c$):** The reverse field required to demagnetize the material, in **A/m** or **Oe**.
- **Magnetizing Field Strength ($H$):** Expressed in **A/m** (SI) or **Oersted (Oe)** in CGS.
- **Permeability ($\mu$):** $\mu = B/H$ — ratio of flux density to applied field, dimensionless in SI.
- **Acceptable minimum residual flux density for inspection:** $\geq 0.7 \text{ T}$ (~ 7,000 G) at the surface (per ASTM E709 guidelines).

> [!NOTE]
> The **ASTM E709** and **ASME Section V, Article 7** standards prescribe that the residual flux density at the test surface must be sufficient to produce sharp, unambiguous indications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Principle — Why Residual Magnetism Works

When a ferromagnetic material is magnetized, the magnetic domains (Weiss domains) align with the applied field. Upon removal of the field:
