1. In **soft magnetic materials** (e.g., low-carbon steel, iron), domains largely return to random orientation — **low retentivity**. The residual technique is **ineffective**.
2. In **hard magnetic materials** (e.g., high-carbon steel, alloy steels, martensitic stainless steels), domain wall motion is impeded by impurities, dislocations, and grain boundaries — **high retentivity**. Domains remain partially aligned, sustaining a residual field that is strong enough for MPI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU Board Favorite):** Residual technique is suitable only for materials with **Carbon content ≥ 0.4%** or hardness ≥ **~HRC 30 / 30 HRC equivalent** (i.e., materials that respond well to permanent magnetization).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Bulleted)

**Stage 1 — Pre-Cleaning**
- Remove oil, grease, paint, scale, rust from the test surface.
- Methods: solvent cleaning, vapor degreasing, grit blasting, acid pickling.
- *Why:* Contaminants mask indications and block particle mobility.

**Stage 2 — Magnetization (with current ON)**
- Apply magnetizing current (AC, HWDC, FWDC, or DC from a permanent magnet) to the part.
- The part becomes magnetized to near-saturation or to a value well above the knee of the B-H curve.
- The flux density reaches $B_{max}$.

**Stage 3 — Current Cut-Off (THE CRITICAL STEP)**
- Switch off the magnetizing current.
- Due to hysteresis, the operating point on the B-H loop drops from $B_{max}$ down to the residual value $B_r$ (at $H = 0$).
- *Residual field still exists in the part.*

**Stage 4 — Application of Magnetic Particles (with current OFF)**
- Apply dry powder (often red/black iron oxide, $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$) or wet fluorescent particles.
- Particles are attracted to flux leakage fields at discontinuities.
- Indications form as **crisp, well-defined lines**.

**Stage 5 — Inspection & Evaluation**
- View under white light (visible particles) or UV-A light (fluorescent particles, wavelength 320–400 nm, peak ~365 nm).
- Compare against acceptance standards (e.g., ASME, EN, IS standards).

**Stage 6 — Demagnetization**
- Slowly reduce an alternating field to zero (or heat above Curie temperature).
- *Why:* Residual magnetism can interfere with downstream machining, welding, or with sensitive instruments (compasses, electronic gauges).

**Stage 7 — Post-Cleaning**
- Remove particles to prevent contamination or corrosion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Symbol / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Magnetic Flux Density | $B = \mu H = \mu_0 (H + M)$ | Tesla (T) | $B$ in SI |
| 2 | Magnetic Field Intensity | $H = \dfrac{NI}{l}$ | A/m | Solenoid field |
| 3 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | **Constant** |
| 4 | Retentivity / Remanence | $B_r$ (read from B-H loop at $H=0$) | Tesla | Residual flux |
| 5 | Coercivity | $H_c$ (read from B-H loop at $B=0$) | A/m | Reverse field to erase |
| 6 | Magnetizing Force (Circular Magnetization) | $H = \dfrac{NI}{\pi D}$ | A/m | $N$=turns, $D$=part diameter |
| 7 | Magnetizing Force (Longitudinal) | $H = \dfrac{NI}{l}$ | A/m | Solenoid/coil |
| 8 | Required magnetizing current (FWDC for residual) | $I_{FWDC} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0 \times$ AC equivalent | A | For deep flaw detection |
| 9 | Sensitivity rule of thumb | $B_{residual} \geq 0.7$ T (7000 G) | T | ASTM E709 minimum |
| 10 | Demagnetizing factor for long rod | $N_d \approx \dfrac{D^2}{L^2} \left[\ln\left(\frac{2L}{D}\right) - 1\right]$ | dimensionless | For rod geometry |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation Rule (from system policy):** In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ style notation has been replaced with $\mu_0 (H + M)$ parentheses to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. Use `\vert x \vert` in raw LaTeX if absolute value is required.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component Inspected Using Residual MPI | Why Residual? |
|----------|---------------------------------------|----------------|
| Aerospace | Landing gear, turbine shafts | Critical fatigue-prone parts made of high-strength alloy steel |
| Railway | Axles, wheel sets | High-carbon steel with high retentivity |
| Heavy Engineering | Forged shafts, crankshafts | Material naturally retains flux |
| Pressure Vessel | Welded joints in ferromagnetic steels | Often used in field where power-source mobility is limited |
| Automotive | Cast iron components, forged stubs | High-carbon content supports residual technique |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Required Magnetizing Current for Residual Technique

The **Lizzio's Rule** and the **ASTM E709 empirical relationships** form the foundation of magnetizing current selection. Below is a step-by-step analytical derivation of the magnetizing force required for a cylindrical bar magnetized longitudinally using a coil.

### Given Data (Worked Example)
- Test part: cylindrical shaft, diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Length $L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- Material: medium-carbon steel (AISI 1045)
- Number of turns in magnetizing coil: $N = 5$ turns
- Goal: Determine current $I$ to achieve $H = 32{,}000 \text{ A/m}$ (a typical longitudinal magnetization value for detecting transverse flaws).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the magnetomotive force (mmf) equation.**

The magnetic field intensity inside a long solenoid (or an encircling coil) carrying current $I$ with $N$ turns over a length $L$ is:

$$
H = \frac{NI}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for current $I$.**

Rearranging the equation to isolate the unknown current $I$:

$$
I = \frac{H \cdot L}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

Insert $H = 32{,}000 \text{ A/m}$, $L = 0.40 \text{ m}$, and $N = 5$:

$$
I = \frac{32{,}000 \times 0.40}{5}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$
I = \frac{12{,}800}{5} = 2{,}560 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

A peak magnetizing current of approximately **2,560 A** is required. This is why residual MPI systems use **high-amperage, low-voltage** magnetizers (typically 6 V, 2,000–4,000 A).

### Conversion to a Circular Magnetization (Yoke/Prod) Equivalent

If we instead use **circular magnetization** (central conductor) on a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D_o = 50$ mm, the field at the bore is given by:

$$
H = \frac{NI}{\pi D_{mean}}
$$

For a single-turn central conductor ($N = 1$) and a bore diameter of $D = 50$ mm:

$$
H = \frac{I}{\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{I}{0.1571} \text{ A/m}
$$

For the same $H = 32{,}000$ A/m:

$$
I = 32{,}000 \times 0.1571 = 5{,}027 \text{ A}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Continuous vs Residual Technique

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** and frequently appears in board exams.

| Parameter | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Current status during particle application | **ON** | **OFF** |
| Source of magnetic field | Applied H-field | Remanence $B_r$ |
| Material requirement | Any ferromagnetic | High-retentivity (hard) ferromagnetic |
| Sensitivity to surface flaws | **High** | Moderate |
| Sensitivity to **subsurface** flaws | Low (AC: skin effect) | **Higher (DC-based residual field penetrates deeper)** |
| Indication sharpness | Can be washed away by strong fields | **Crisp, stable indications** |
| Power consumption during inspection | High (current held) | Low (current off) |
| Portability | Limited | **Highly portable** (permanent magnet versions) |
| Susceptibility to particle blow-off | High (especially AC) | **None** |
| Inspection of large parts | Difficult | Easier (magnetize at one station, inspect at another) |
| Demagnetization required | Yes | **Strongly recommended** |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Verifying Residual Flux Density

**Given:** A toroidal sample of AISI 4140 steel (high-carbon, hard steel) is magnetized to a peak flux density $B_{max} = 1.5$ T using a DC current. After current cutoff, the residual flux density $B_r = 1.0$ T. The coercivity $H_c$ for this material is $1{,}200$ A/m.

**Find:** (a) Whether the residual flux meets the ASTM minimum (0.7 T). (b) The relative permeability at the residual point if $H = 0$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Comparison with minimum standard:**

$$
B_r = 1.0 \text{ T} \geq B_{min} = 0.7 \text{ T} \quad \checkmark
$$

Therefore the residual technique is applicable, and the indications will be sharp.

**(b) Relative permeability at the residual point:**

At the residual point, $H = 0$. Technically $\mu_r = B_r / (\mu_0 H)$ is undefined at $H = 0$. However, we can compute the apparent permeability for a small increment $\Delta H$:

$$
\mu_{apparent} = \frac{\Delta B}{\mu_0 \Delta H}
$$

For practical engineering, the **differential permeability** near $H = 0$ on a typical steel B-H curve is:

$$
\mu_{differential} \approx \frac{B_r - (-B_r)}{2\mu_0 H_c} = \frac{2 B_r}{2 \mu_0 H_c} = \frac{B_r}{\mu_0 H_c}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\mu_{differential} = \frac{1.0}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1{,}200}
$$

$$
\mu_{differential} = \frac{1.0}{1.508 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 663
$$

**Interpretation:** A relative differential permeability of ~663 at the residual point is typical for a hard steel at the remanence operating point. This high effective permeability enables strong flux leakage at defect sites, producing visible indications.

## 3.4 Decision Flow — When to Use the Residual Technique

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exams:**
> 1. Material is hard ferromagnetic ($B_r$ high) → **Use Residual**.
> 2. Subsurface defect detection required → **Use Residual with FWDC**.
> 3. Inspection of large immobile parts (railway axles, forgings) → **Use Residual** (separate magnetization & inspection).
> 4. Light demagnetization critical → use **Continuous** with AC (no significant residual).
> 5. Soft material (low-carbon steel, pure iron) → **Residual is ineffective, use Continuous**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Residual Technique of MPI (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Test Specimen] --> B[Pre-Cleaning: Remove oil, scale, paint]
    B --> C{Is material ferromagnetic with high retentivity?}
    C -- No --> X1[STOP: Use Continuous Technique or alternative NDT]
    C -- Yes --> D[Stage 2: Magnetization - Apply FWDC or DC current]
    D --> E[Part reaches peak flux density Bmax]
    E --> F[Stage 3: Cut-off Magnetizing Current]
    F --> G[Operating point on B-H loop drops to Br at H=0]
    G --> H[Stage 4: Apply Dry Powder or Wet Fluorescent Particles]
    H --> I[Particles migrate to flux leakage zones at defects]
    I --> J[Stage 5: Inspect under white light or UV-A 365 nm]
    J --> K{Defect indication observed?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Mark, record, evaluate against acceptance criteria]
    K -- No --> M[Record as accepted]
    L --> N[Stage 6: Demagnetization - Decaying AC field or thermal]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Stage 7: Post-Cleaning and Final Report]
    O --> P[End]
    X1 --> P
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Equipment Setup for Residual MPI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerModule[Power and Control Module]
        PM1[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz]
        PM2[Step-Down Transformer 6V output]
        PM3[High-Current Rectifier FWDC]
        PM4[Current Regulator 0 to 4000A]
        PM1 --> PM2 --> PM3 --> PM4
    end
    subgraph Magnetizer[Magnetization Station]
        MZ1[Contact Prods OR Encircling Coil OR Permanent Magnet Yoke]
        MZ2[Specimen held in fixture]
        PM4 --> MZ1
        MZ1 --> MZ2
    end
    subgraph Inspection[Inspection Station]
        IN1[Dry Powder Applicator or Wet Bath with Hose]
        IN2[UV-A Lamp 365nm for fluorescent particles]
        IN3[White Light Source for visible particles]
        MZ2 --> IN1 --> IN2
        MZ2 --> IN1 --> IN3
    end
    subgraph Demag[Demagnetization Station]
        DG1[Decaying AC Solenoid or Thermal Oven above Curie temperature]
        MZ2 --> DG1
    end
    subgraph QC[Quality Control Output]
        QC1[Visual Evaluation by Level II or Level III Inspector]
        QC2[Documentation: Indication Sketch, Location, Magnitude, Reject/Accept Decision]
        IN2 --> QC1
        IN3 --> QC1
        QC1 --> QC2
    end
```

## 4.3 Hysteresis Loop Schematic — Operating Point During Residual Technique

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BHLoop[Schematic B-H Hysteresis Loop for Hard Steel]
        A1[Origin O at H=0 B=0] --> A2[Initial magnetization: H increases, B rises to Bmax]
        A2 --> A3[At peak: B = Bmax, H = Hmax]
        A3 --> A4[Current switched OFF, H drops to 0]
        A4 --> A5[Operating point lands at Br on the B axis where H=0]
        A5 --> A6[Residual flux Br drives particle attraction at defects]
        A6 --> A7[Demag: apply reverse H, reach Hc at B=0]
        A7 --> A8[Demag complete: return to origin O]
    end
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Continuous vs Residual Magnetization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Continuous Technique]
        A1[Current ON] --> A2[Apply particles while current flows]
        A2 --> A3[Defect flux leakage draws particles live]
        A3 --> A4[Shut off current and inspect immediately]
    end
    subgraph B[Residual Technique]
        B1[Current ON to magnetize part to Bmax] --> B2[Shut off current H becomes 0]
        B2 --> B3[Apply particles using residual flux Br only]
        B3 --> B4[Inspect under light, evaluate, then demagnetize]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the residual technique of magnetic particle inspection. List any two materials suitable for this technique.**

**Model Answer:**

The residual technique of MPI is a method in which the part is **first magnetized** to a peak value, the **magnetizing current is switched off**, and the magnetic particles are then **applied using the residual (remanent) flux** retained in the part to detect surface and near-surface defects.

**Suitable materials (any two):**
- Medium-carbon steel (AISI 1040, 1045)
- High-carbon steel (AISI 1095, spring steel)
- Alloy steels (AISI 4140, 4340)
- Martensitic stainless steel (AISI 410, 420)
- Hardened and tempered forgings

*(Common 3-mark layout: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for materials.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the residual technique not suitable for low-carbon steels?**

**Model Answer:**

Low-carbon steels (e.g., AISI 1018, 1020) have a **low carbon content and a soft, coarse-grained microstructure**. Their hysteresis loop is narrow, meaning:
1. **Low retentivity ($B_r$):** They retain very little flux after the magnetizing field is removed.
2. **Low coercivity ($H_c$):** Domains snap back to random orientation easily.

The residual flux density is **insufficient to attract particles** at defect sites, leading to **weak, non-detectable indications**. Hence the continuous technique (with the current held ON) must be used for soft ferromagnetic materials.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **step-by-step procedure** of the residual technique of magnetic particle inspection. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **continuous and residual techniques** of MPI in a tabular form, listing at least **six points of difference**. Discuss why the residual technique is preferred for **field inspection of railway axles**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Pre-Cleaning (1 mark)**
The surface to be inspected is thoroughly cleaned using suitable solvents (e.g., acetone, kerosene) or mechanical methods (grit blasting, wire brushing). All coatings, oil, grease, scale, and rust are removed because they can mask indications.

**Step 2: Magnetization (1.5 marks)**
The component is placed in a magnetizing setup (yoke, prods, encircling coil, or central conductor). A high-magnitude current (typically 1,000–4,000 A) of **FWDC** (Full Wave Direct Current) is passed through the part to drive the magnetic domains into alignment, reaching a peak flux density $B_{max}$ well above the knee of the B-H curve.

**Step 3: Current Cut-Off (1 mark)**
The magnetizing current is switched OFF. The operating point on the B-H hysteresis loop drops from $B_{max}$ at $H_{max}$ down to the residual flux density $B_r$ at $H = 0$. This residual flux remains in the part.

**Step 4: Application of Magnetic Particles (1 mark)**
Dry iron oxide powder ($\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4}$, red or black) is dusted lightly, or a wet suspension of fluorescent particles is sprayed gently. Particles are attracted to **leakage flux fields** at surface and near-surface discontinuities, forming sharp linear indications.

**Step 5: Inspection (1 mark)**
The part is examined under suitable illumination: white light (~1,000 lux) for visible particles or UV-A lamp (365 nm) for fluorescent particles. Indications are evaluated against the relevant acceptance standard (e.g., ASME V Article 7).

**Step 6: Demagnetization (1 mark)**
The component is passed through a decaying AC field or heated above the Curie temperature (770 °C for steel) to remove residual magnetism — important to avoid interference in subsequent machining or instrument use.

**Step 7: Post-Cleaning (0.5 marks)**
Particles are removed by air blow-off, brushing, or rinsing to prevent corrosion and contamination.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (6 points × 1 mark each = 6 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Magnetizing current is **ON** while particles are applied. | Magnetizing current is **OFF**; particles use residual flux. |
| 2 | Suitable for **all ferromagnetic materials**. | Suitable only for materials with **high retentivity** (hard steels). |
| 3 | Better for **surface defects**. | Better for **subsurface defects** (up to ~6 mm deep). |
| 4 | Indications can be **blown off** by electromagnetic forces. | Indications are **stable and crisp**. |
| 5 | Requires power during inspection. | Allows **separate magnetization and inspection stations**. |
| 6 | Demagnetization is easier (less residual flux). | Demagnetization is **strongly required** (high residual flux). |

**Why preferred for railway axle field inspection (1 mark):**

Railway axles are made of **medium/high-carbon steel** with high retentivity, are **large, heavy, and difficult to move continuously**, and inspection is done in **field depots** where power availability is limited. The residual technique allows the operator to:
- Magnetize the axle at one station,
- Move it to a separate inspection bay,
- Apply particles and inspect without the heavy current supply,
- Use **portable permanent-magnet yokes** where mains power is unavailable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **concept of retentivity and coercivity** with the help of a B-H hysteresis loop. State their **SI units** and typical values for hard steel. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical shaft of **diameter 60 mm** and **length 500 mm** is magnetized longitudinally using a **5-turn coil**. Calculate the **magnetizing current required** to produce a field of **30,000 A/m**. State whether the **residual technique is applicable** if the material is **AISI 1045 steel**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**B-H Hysteresis Loop — Explanation (4 marks):**

A B-H hysteresis loop is a closed curve that traces the relationship between the magnetic flux density $B$ and the magnetizing field intensity $H$ in a ferromagnetic material as it is taken through a complete magnetization cycle.

1. Starting from the demagnetized state at origin O, as $H$ increases, $B$ rises along the **virgin magnetization curve** until it approaches saturation $B_{sat}$.
2. When $H$ is reduced to zero, the curve does not return to the origin — instead $B$ remains at the value $B_r$, called **retentivity or remanence**.
3. To reduce $B$ back to zero, a reverse field of magnitude $H_c$ must be applied — this is **coercivity**.
4. Further increase of the reverse field drives $B$ to $-B_{sat}$, and the cycle continues to complete the loop.

**Retentivity ($B_r$):** The residual magnetic flux density retained by the material when the applied magnetizing field is reduced to zero. **SI Unit: Tesla (T).** Typical value for hard steel: $B_r \approx 1.0$ to $1.4$ T.

**Coercivity ($H_c$):** The reverse magnetizing field intensity required to reduce the residual flux density to zero. **SI Unit: Ampere per meter (A/m).** Typical value for hard steel: $H_c \approx 800$ to $2{,}000$ A/m.

**Why both matter in residual MPI (1 mark):**
A material with high $B_r$ retains enough flux after the current is switched off to attract particles at defect sites, enabling the residual technique. A material with low $B_r$ cannot support residual MPI.

**(Include a labeled sketch of the hysteresis loop — 1 mark)**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hyst[B-H Hysteresis Loop]
        A1[Origin O] --> A2[Virgin curve to Bsat]
        A2 --> A3[Reduce H to 0, B falls to Br]
        A3 --> A4[Apply reverse H, B reaches 0 at Hc]
        A4 --> A5[Continue reverse to minus Bsat]
        A5 --> A6[Return H to 0, B reaches minus Br]
        A6 --> A7[Complete loop back to origin]
    end
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 60$ mm (not needed for longitudinal magnetization in a coil)
- Length $L = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Number of turns $N = 5$
- Required field $H = 30{,}000 \text{ A/m}$

**Step 1 — Write the longitudinal magnetization formula (1 mark):**

$$
H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $I$ (1 mark):**

$$
I = \frac{H \cdot L}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$
I = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.5}{5}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
I = \frac{15{,}000}{5} = 3{,}000 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (0.5 marks):**

A peak current of **3,000 A** is required, achievable with a standard 6 V, 3,000 A magnetizer.

**Step 6 — Applicability of residual technique (2.5 marks):**

AISI 1045 is a **medium-carbon steel** (carbon content ~0.45%) with a **pearlitic-ferritic microstructure** in the normalized condition, or **tempered martensite** in the quenched and tempered condition. It exhibits:
- Retentivity $B_r \approx 1.0$ to $1.1$ T
- Coercivity $H_c \approx 1{,}000$ to $1{,}400$ A/m

Since $B_r \geq 0.7$ T (the ASTM E709 minimum for sharp residual indications), the **residual technique is applicable** to AISI 1045 shafts. For best results, **Full Wave DC (FWDC)** is recommended as the magnetizing current, since it provides deep penetration of the residual field, suitable for detecting **sub-surface flaws** in railway and automotive shafts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Valuation (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing Continuous and Residual techniques** — Always state explicitly that the current is OFF during particle application in the residual method.
> 2. **Omitting units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit in numericals (e.g., writing "I = 3000" without "A" loses credit).
> 3. **Forgetting the pre-cleaning step** — Many students jump directly to magnetization; this loses a structural mark in 7-mark procedure questions.
> 4. **Not stating the demagnetization requirement** — The residual technique *mandates* demagnetization; skipping it loses 1 mark in any 7-mark answer.
> 5. **Wrong retentivity threshold** — Always cite $B_r \geq 0.7$ T or 7,000 G; using 0.5 T or 1 T as the threshold is incorrect.
> 6. **Skipping the hysteresis loop sketch** — A 7-mark question on retentivity/coercivity *requires* a labeled B-H loop diagram. Missing it loses 1.5–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute KTU revision:**

- [x] **Definition:** Residual MPI uses **remanent flux** $B_r$ **after** the magnetizing current is switched OFF.
- [x] **Key Property:** Relies on **high retentivity** of hard ferromagnetic materials.
- [x] **Suitable Materials:** Medium/high-carbon steel, alloy steel (AISI 4140, 4340), martensitic stainless steel.
- [x] **Unsuitable Materials:** Low-carbon steel, pure iron, austenitic stainless steel (paramagnetic), non-magnetic alloys.
- [x] **Standard Current:** **FWDC** (Full Wave DC) preferred for deep penetration in residual technique.
- [x] **Minimum Residual Flux:** $B_r \geq 0.7$ T (7,000 G) per ASTM E709.
- [x] **Steps:** Clean → Magnetize → **Switch OFF current** → Apply particles → Inspect → **Demagnetize** → Clean.
- [x] **Advantages:** Crisp indications, separate magnetize/inspect stations, deep sub-surface detection, portable (permanent magnet yokes), no particle blow-off.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Requires high-retentivity material, demagnetization mandatory, not suitable for soft steels.
- [x] **Formula to Memorize:**
   - $H = NI/L$ (longitudinal)
   - $H = NI/(\pi D)$ (circular)
   - $B = \mu_0 (H + M)$
   - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- [x] **Industry Use:** Railway axles, aircraft landing gear, forged shafts, automotive crankshafts.
- [x] **Standards to Cite:** ASTM E709, ASME Section V Article 7, EN 1290, IS 5334.
- [x] **Particle types:** Dry powder ($\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$ black/red) or wet fluorescent (UV-A 365 nm).
- [x] **Hysteresis Loop Key Points:** $B_r$ (at $H=0$), $H_c$ (at $B=0$), saturation $B_{sat}$.
- [x] **Coercivity unit:** A/m (SI), Oe (CGS); $1$ Oe $\approx 79.577$ A/m.
- [x] **Demagnetization Methods:** (1) Decaying AC solenoid, (2) Thermal above Curie temperature 770 °C for steel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Sensitivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Sensitivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Sensitivity** in Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is formally defined as the **ability of an NDT system to detect the smallest possible discontinuity** under specified test conditions, expressed as the minimum detectable flaw size, signal amplitude, or dimensional threshold relative to a calibrated reference standard.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 NDT syllabus**, *System Sensitivity* is a **figure-of-merit** that quantifies the **smallest input signal (flaw response) that the NDT system can reliably detect above the noise floor**, with the measurement referenced to a **standard reference block or artificial flaw** of known geometry.

Mathematically, it is commonly expressed in **three different domains**, depending on the NDT method:

$$
S_{sys} = \frac{\text{Useful Signal Output from Smallest Detectable Flaw}}{\text{Reference Signal Output from Standard Flaw}}
$$

For **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — sensitivity is expressed in **decibels (dB)** relative to a reference reflector (e.g., a 2 mm flat-bottom hole at a reference distance).

For **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — sensitivity is expressed as a **percentage of the specimen thickness** using an **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**.

For **Penetrant Testing (PT)** and **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — sensitivity is **qualitative**, expressed in terms of the **sensitivity level (Level ½, Level 1, 2, 3, 4)** of the consumable set as defined by **ASTM E1417 / ASTM E709**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Whisper in a Stadium**

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a noisy cricket stadium. A friend whispers your name from 50 metres away. Can you hear it? That depends on:
1. **How loud your friend's whisper is** (analogous to the **flaw size** — bigger flaw = stronger signal).
2. **How sensitive your ears are** (analogous to the **NDT system's sensitivity**).
3. **How loud the crowd noise is** (analogous to **background noise, grain noise, scatter**).

If your hearing is *highly sensitive*, you can detect even the tiniest whisper. Similarly, an NDT system with **high sensitivity** can detect **very small defects** (tiny cracks, small inclusions, shallow porosity). But there is a trade-off — *too much sensitivity* picks up *irrelevant noise* (grain boundary reflections, surface roughness, electronic noise), making interpretation difficult.

So **System Sensitivity = "How good is the NDT system's 'ears' at picking up the faintest 'whisper' of a defect?"**

---

## 1.3 Key Reference Standards and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Sensitivity Quantification**
> 1. **Reference Sensitivity (UT)** — the gain (in dB) required to bring a known reference reflector (e.g., a 2 mm FBH) to a specified screen height (commonly **Full Screen Height — FSH** or **80% FSH**).
> 2. **Percent Sensitivity (RT)** — the ratio of the thinnest visible IQI wire diameter to the specimen thickness, multiplied by 100.

The **two most cited benchmark sensitivities** in industry are:

| Reference Metric | Typical Value | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic reference sensitivity | **2 mm flat-bottom hole** at near-surface distance | **IIW V1 / V2 Block, ASTM E164** |
| Radiographic IQI sensitivity | **2% to 4%** of specimen thickness | **ASTM E747, ISO 19232-1** |

---

## 1.4 Why Sensitivity is NOT Resolution or Accuracy

This is a **classic KTU examiner trap** — students frequently confuse these three terms:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sensitivity** → *Smallest* detectable flaw (the lower limit of detection).
> - **Resolution** → Ability to *separate* two closely-spaced reflectors (the ability to distinguish).
> - **Accuracy** → How *close* the measured flaw size/position is to the true value.

A system can be **highly sensitive but poorly resolving** (e.g., a low-frequency UT probe that detects everything but cannot pinpoint the exact flaw position).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — Sensitivity Theory

In **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Testing**, sensitivity is governed by the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of the reflected echo from a flaw versus the electronic/acoustic noise floor:

$$
\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{A_{flaw}}{A_{noise}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $A_{flaw}$ = peak amplitude of the flaw echo (V)
- $A_{noise}$ = root-mean-square amplitude of the noise floor (V)

**A KTU-acceptable sensitivity requires SNR ≥ 6 dB** (i.e., the flaw signal is at least twice the noise amplitude).

### 2.1.1 The Reference Sensitivity Concept

The **reference sensitivity** is established by adjusting the **gain (dB)** of the UT instrument until the echo from a **standard reference reflector** reaches a specific screen height.

If the **reference sensitivity** is at gain $G_{ref}$ (in dB), and the **actual inspection gain** is $G_{insp}$, then the **sensitivity margin** for a flaw of given size is:

$$
\Delta G = G_{insp} - G_{ref} \quad (\text{in dB})
$$

A **positive $\Delta G$** means inspection is performed **above reference sensitivity** (more sensitive — smaller flaws detectable).
A **negative $\Delta G$** means inspection is performed **below reference sensitivity** (less sensitive).

### 2.1.2 Distance Amplitude Correction (DAC) and DGS

Two KTU-favourite concepts linked to sensitivity:

1. **DAC (Distance Amplitude Correction)** — A curve plotted on the UT screen that compensates for **attenuation** with depth. Flaw echoes that fall **above the DAC curve** are considered detectable above sensitivity.
2. **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size) Diagram** — A graphical tool (also called **AVG** — Abstand, Verstärkung, Größe in German) that relates **flaw distance, required gain, and flaw size** for a given transducer.

The **DGS relationship** for a flat-bottom hole (FBH) of equivalent diameter $d$ at distance $N$ (in terms of near-field length $N_0$) is:

$$
V(d, N) = V_0 - 20 \log_{10}(N) + 40 \log_{10}\left( \frac{d}{d_0} \right)
$$

Where:
- $V(d,N)$ = echo height from flaw (dB)
- $V_0$ = reference echo height from standard reflector
- $N$ = distance ratio (actual/near-field length)
- $d$ = flaw diameter
- $d_0$ = reference reflector diameter

---

## 2.2 Radiographic Testing (RT) — Sensitivity Theory

The **percent radiographic sensitivity** $S_{RT}$ is the **most important KTU formula** for this topic:

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{T_{wire}}{T_{total}} \times 100 \%
$$

Where:
- $T_{wire}$ = diameter of the **thinnest visible wire** in the IQI (mm)
- $T_{total}$ = total thickness of the specimen at the location of the IQI (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Criterion (KTU Standard):** A radiographic inspection is considered acceptably sensitive if $S_{RT} \geq 2\%$.

**Worked Quick Example (for memory):**
If a 50 mm thick steel weld is radiographed, and the thinnest visible IQI wire is **1.0 mm**, then:
$$S_{RT} = \frac{1.0}{50} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$
This meets the **2% minimum** — **inspection passes sensitivity check**.

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting System Sensitivity (Cross-Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive list — highly KTU-favoured for 7-mark questions.**

**A. Transducer/Source Factors:**
- **Frequency** (UT): Higher freq → higher sensitivity to small flaws but lower penetration.
- **Transducer diameter** (UT): Larger diameter → more energy, higher sensitivity, but poorer near-field resolution.
- **X-ray tube voltage** (RT): Higher kV → greater penetration but **decreased contrast** (lower sensitivity).
- **Focal spot size** (RT): Smaller focal spot → higher geometric sharpness → better flaw visibility.

**B. Material Factors:**
- **Attenuation coefficient** (UT) — high attenuation in coarse-grained metals reduces sensitivity.
- **Density × thickness** (RT) — higher density requires more energy, may reduce contrast sensitivity.
- **Grain size** — coarse grains scatter ultrasound and reduce sensitivity.

**C. Surface & Geometry Factors:**
- **Surface roughness** — rough surfaces scatter sound/radiation and reduce sensitivity.
- **Part geometry** — curved surfaces, fillets, and complex shapes reduce sensitivity.

**D. Equipment/Setting Factors:**
- **Gain setting** (UT) — directly controls sensitivity.
- **Pulse repetition frequency (PRF)** — affects display clarity.
- **Film type and density** (RT) — slow film + high contrast = higher sensitivity.
- **Penetrant type and dwell time** (PT).

**E. Environmental Factors:**
- **Temperature** affects couplant viscosity (UT) and penetrant performance (PT).
- **Magnetic field strength** (MT) — must be optimized for sensitivity to subsurface flaws.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Method | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UT — SNR | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}(A_{flaw}/A_{noise})$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 2 | UT — Sensitivity Margin | $\Delta G = G_{insp} - G_{ref}$ | Gain Difference | dB |
| 3 | UT — DGS / AVG | $V(d,N) = V_0 - 20\log(N) + 40\log(d/d_0)$ | Flaw Echo Height | dB |
| 4 | UT — Wavelength | $\lambda = c/f$ | c = sound velocity, f = frequency | m, m/s, Hz |
| 5 | RT — % Sensitivity | $S_{RT} = (T_{wire}/T_{total}) \times 100$ | IQI Sensitivity | % |
| 6 | RT — Geometric Unsharpness | $U_g = (F \cdot d / D - d)$ | F = focal-object, d = focal, D = object-film | mm |
| 7 | RT — Attenuation Law | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert Law | — |
| 8 | MT — Field Strength | $H = NI / L$ | Magnetomotive force / length | A/m |
| 9 | PT — Sensitivity Level | Level ½, 1, 2, 3, 4 | ASTM E1417 | — |
| 10 | General | $S_{sys} = A_{flaw,min} / A_{ref}$ | Minimum Detectable / Reference | — |

> **Units Convention:** All UT gains are in **dB**; all RT sensitivities are in **%**; all distances in **mm or m**; velocities in **m/s**; frequencies in **MHz**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

System sensitivity is **the gatekeeper of inspection reliability**. In practice:

- **Aerospace (UT of turbine discs)** — sensitivity is tuned to detect **0.4 mm flat-bottom holes** using **10 MHz focused probes** with **DAC curves**.
- **Nuclear pressure vessels (RT)** — sensitivity must be **≤ 2%** as per **ASME Section V Article 2**.
- **Weld inspection (MT/PT)** — sensitivity levels are specified as **Level 2 or Level 3** for **critical welds** per **AWS D1.1**.
- **Pipeline girth welds (AUT)** — sensitivity verification uses **calibration blocks with 1.5 mm SDH (Side-Drilled Holes)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Percent Radiographic Sensitivity from IQI Geometry

**Given:**
- An IQI with wires of diameters $T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n$ placed on the source side of the specimen.
- Specimen total thickness at the IQI location = $T_{total}$.

**Step 1:** Identify the diameter of the **thinnest wire** that is *clearly visible* on the developed radiograph.
Let this be $T_{wire}$.

**Step 2:** By definition of radiographic sensitivity (the ability of the radiation to image fine details), the **percent sensitivity** is the ratio of the smallest imaged detail to the total thickness, expressed as a percentage:

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{\text{Minimum Imaged Detail}}{\text{Total Thickness}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the symbols:

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{T_{wire}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Dimensionally verify:
- $T_{wire}$ has units of **mm**.
- $T_{total}$ has units of **mm**.
- The ratio is **dimensionless**; multiplied by 100, the result is in **%**. ✔

**Step 5:** Engineering Acceptance — for carbon steel welds:
- $S_{RT} \geq 2\%$ → **Acceptable** (general industrial).
- $S_{RT} \geq 1\%$ → **Acceptable** (nuclear, high-criticality).
- $S_{RT} < 1\%$ → **Not acceptable**, re-shoot with better technique.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ultrasonic Sensitivity Limit as a Function of Frequency

**Given:** A pulse-echo UT system with a transducer of frequency $f$ (Hz) and sound velocity $c$ (m/s) in the test material.

**Step 1:** The wavelength of ultrasound is:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f}
$$

**Step 2:** Theoretical lower limit of detectable flaw size (Rayleigh criterion) — a flaw must be at least **one-half wavelength** ($\lambda/2$) in dimension to produce a detectable echo:

$$
d_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{c}{2f}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the **sensitivity** (smallest detectable flaw size) is *inversely proportional to frequency*. Doubling the frequency halves the minimum detectable flaw size (improves sensitivity):

$$
d_{min} \propto \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 4:** Trade-off — Attenuation also increases with frequency. The **attenuation coefficient** in metals approximately follows:

$$
\alpha = a \cdot f^n
$$

Where $a$ is a material constant and $n \approx 1$ to 1.5 for most metals. So increasing $f$ to improve sensitivity reduces the **maximum inspectable thickness** $x_{max}$:

$$
x_{max} = \frac{\text{Maximum allowable pulse-echo loss}}{\alpha} \approx \frac{\text{Const}}{f^n}
$$

**Step 5:** Summary of the sensitivity-penetration trade-off in UT:

$$
S_{UT} \uparrow \text{ as } f \uparrow, \quad x_{max} \downarrow \text{ as } f \uparrow
$$

This is the **fundamental compromise** in ultrasonic probe selection — exactly the type of conceptual question KTU examiners love to ask.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relation Between Sensitivity and SNR in Decibel Form

**Step 1:** The decibel definition for a voltage or amplitude ratio is:

$$
\text{Ratio (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** The signal-to-noise ratio in dB is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the ratio of amplitudes:

$$
\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/20}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — to detect a flaw with **100% reliability** (i.e., signal = 2 × noise), the minimum SNR in dB is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{min} = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

This **6 dB minimum** is a **widely used KTU rule-of-thumb** for ultrasonic inspection.

**Step 5:** If a **12 dB SNR** is required (signal = 4 × noise, more conservative inspection), the equivalent linear ratio is:

$$
\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}} = 10^{12/20} = 10^{0.6} = 3.98 \approx 4
$$

**Step 6:** Conversely, an SNR of **20 dB** corresponds to a signal 10× the noise — typical of high-quality inspection.

$$
10^{20/20} = 10^{1} = 10
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Sensitivity Loss from Multiple Factors (UT)

In real inspections, sensitivity is reduced by multiple losses. The total sensitivity loss (in dB) is the **sum** of individual losses (dB addition rule):

$$
L_{total} = L_{couplant} + L_{attenuation} + L_{surface} + L_{geometry} + L_{scatter}
$$

Each $L_i$ is expressed in dB. The **effective sensitivity** is the reference sensitivity minus losses:

$$
S_{eff} = S_{ref} - L_{total} \quad (\text{dB})
$$

This is why **gain compensation (DAC/TCG)** is applied — to *add back* the attenuation and coupling losses so the flaw echo appears at its **true sensitivity height** on the screen.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components Influencing NDT System Sensitivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NDT System Sensitivity] --> B[Equipment Factors]
    A --> C[Material Factors]
    A --> D[Operator Factors]
    A --> E[Environmental Factors]
    A --> F[Calibration Factors]

    B --> B1[Transducer Frequency]
    B --> B2[Transducer Diameter]
    B --> B3[X-ray kV / mA]
    B --> B4[Gain Setting dB]
    B --> B5[Focal Spot Size]

    C --> C1[Attenuation Coefficient]
    C --> C2[Grain Size]
    C --> C3[Density and Thickness]
    C --> C4[Surface Roughness]
    C --> C5[Magnetic Permeability]

    D --> D1[Couplant Selection]
    D --> D2[Probe Angle and Alignment]
    D --> D3[Dwell Time PT]
    D --> D4[Film Density Choice]

    E --> E1[Temperature]
    E --> E2[Humidity]
    E --> E3[Lighting MT PT]
    E --> E4[Vibration]

    F --> F1[Reference Block IIW V1 V2]
    F --> F2[IQI Wire Type or Hole Type]
    F --> F3[DAC and DGS Curves]
    F --> F4[Sensitivity Level ASTM E1417]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The root node `A` is the overall concept. Five branches (`B`–`F`) list the **five categories** of factors affecting sensitivity. Each category then enumerates **specific physical variables**. In a KTU answer, the student should be able to draw a similar hierarchical diagram and discuss at least **two factors from each category**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Flow — UT Sensitivity Calibration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Select Reference Block] --> P2[Step 2 Couple Probe to Standard Reflector]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Adjust Gain to FSH at Reference Reflector]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Note Reference Gain in dB]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Set Inspection Gain at Reference plus Margin dB]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Scan Test Specimen with Same Couplant and Setup]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Compare Flaw Echo Amplitude to DAC Curve]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Record Sensitivity as dB above Reference or Flaw Size in mm]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the **calibration workflow** for setting ultrasonic sensitivity. KTU examiners frequently test this in the 7-mark Part-B questions. Memorize the sequence: **Reference Block → Couplant → FSH Adjustment → Reference Gain → Inspection Gain (with margin) → Scan → Compare to DAC → Document**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Sensitivity Definitions Across NDT Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UT [Ultrasonic Testing]
        UT_DEF[Defined in dB above reference reflector]
        UT_REF[2 mm FBH on IIW V2 Block]
        UT_UNIT[Unit dB]
    end

    subgraph RT [Radiographic Testing]
        RT_DEF[Defined as percent of thickness]
        RT_REF[IQI Wire or Hole]
        RT_UNIT[Unit percent]
    end

    subgraph MT [Magnetic Particle Testing]
        MT_DEF[Defined as sensitivity level]
        MT_REF[Standard Test Block ASTM E709]
        MT_UNIT[Unit Level 1 to 4]
    end

    subgraph PT [Liquid Penetrant Testing]
        PT_DEF[Defined as sensitivity level]
        PT_REF[ASTM E1417 Panel Cracks]
        PT_UNIT[Unit Level 1 to 4]
    end
```

> **Use in exam answer:** When asked *"Compare the method of expressing sensitivity in UT, RT, MT, and PT,"* refer to this matrix. UT and RT are **quantitative** (numeric dB or %); MT and PT are **semi-quantitative** (sensitivity levels only).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two-mark conceptual clarity + one-mark diagram/standard.

### **Q1. Define System Sensitivity in Non-Destructive Testing. Mention the unit of sensitivity for Ultrasonic Testing.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

System Sensitivity is the ability of an NDT system to detect the smallest possible discontinuity under specified test conditions, referenced to a calibrated standard. For **Ultrasonic Testing**, sensitivity is expressed in **decibels (dB)** above the reference sensitivity established using a standard reference block (e.g., **IIW V2 block with a 2 mm flat-bottom hole**). A **higher dB value** above reference indicates **higher sensitivity** to small flaws. *(3 marks: 2 marks definition + 1 mark UT unit)*

---

### **Q2. What is meant by Percent Sensitivity in Radiographic Testing? State the typical acceptance value.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Percent Sensitivity in Radiographic Testing is defined as the ratio of the diameter of the thinnest visible IQI (Image Quality Indicator) wire to the total thickness of the specimen, expressed as a percentage:

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{T_{wire}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Typical acceptance value:** $S_{RT} \geq 2\%$ for general industrial radiographic inspection of welds (as per **ASTM E747 and ISO 19232-1**). For nuclear and high-criticality components, $S_{RT} \geq 1\%$ is required. *(3 marks: 2 marks formula + 1 mark acceptance criterion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A (a) Explain in detail the factors affecting the sensitivity of an Ultrasonic Testing system. Discuss the role of transducer frequency on sensitivity and penetration. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The sensitivity of a UT system depends on the ability to detect small flaw echoes above the noise floor. The major factors are:

**(1) Transducer Frequency (Most Influential Factor):**
- Higher frequency → shorter wavelength → better resolution and higher sensitivity to small flaws.
- Relation: $d_{min} = \lambda/2 = c/(2f)$. Doubling frequency halves the minimum detectable flaw size.
- **Trade-off:** Higher frequency also increases attenuation ($\alpha \propto f^n$), reducing penetration.

**(2) Transducer Diameter:**
- Larger diameter → more acoustic energy transmitted → higher sensitivity, but poorer near-field resolution.
- Smaller diameter → better resolution, but lower sensitivity.

**(3) Couplant:**
- Poor couplant → high interface losses → reduced sensitivity. Water, glycerin, and high-viscosity gels are common couplants.

**(4) Pulse Voltage and Damping:**
- Higher pulse voltage → stronger transmitted signal → higher sensitivity.
- **Heavy damping** improves resolution but reduces sensitivity; **light damping** improves sensitivity but reduces resolution.

**(5) Material Properties:**
- **Grain size:** Coarse grains scatter ultrasound → increases noise → reduces sensitivity.
- **Attenuation:** High attenuation in materials like cast iron reduces sensitivity.

**(6) Surface Condition:**
- Rough or uneven surfaces scatter sound → reduce sensitivity. Smooth, parallel surfaces are required for high-sensitivity UT.

**(7) Equipment Settings (Gain, PRF, Filter):**
- Higher gain → higher sensitivity, but risk of noise pickup.
- Proper band-pass filtering improves SNR → improves effective sensitivity.

**Conclusion:** Selection of frequency involves balancing sensitivity and penetration. For fine-grained steel, **2–5 MHz** is typical; for coarse-grained austenitic stainless steel, **1–2 MHz** is preferred to overcome scatter.

---

#### **Q.A (b) With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to establish the reference sensitivity in Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Testing using the IIW V2 block. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**The IIW V2 Block** is a standard reference block containing a **2 mm flat-bottom hole (FBH)** at a depth of **15 mm** from the top surface, used to establish reference sensitivity in pulse-echo UT.

**Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Apply couplant (oil/glycerin) to the test surface of the IIW V2 block.

**Step 2:** Place the ultrasonic probe on the couplant above the **2 mm FBH** location. Ensure the probe is normal (perpendicular) to the block surface.

**Step 3:** Adjust the **Time Base (Range)** on the UT instrument to display the 2 mm FBH echo at the correct screen position (corresponding to 15 mm depth, after accounting for the couplant delay).

**Step 4:** Adjust the **Gain control** until the FBH echo amplitude reaches **Full Screen Height (FSH)** or **80% FSH** (as per the inspection code).

**Step 5:** Note the gain reading in dB. This is the **Reference Sensitivity** $G_{ref}$.

**Step 6:** During actual inspection, the gain is increased by a specified **sensitivity margin** (e.g., +6 dB or +12 dB above reference), depending on the inspection standard. The new gain is the **Inspection Sensitivity** $G_{insp} = G_{ref} + \Delta G$.

**Step 7:** Apply **Distance Amplitude Correction (DAC)** if required, by recording FBH echoes at multiple depths and plotting the curve.

**Step 8:** Flaw echoes that exceed the DAC curve at the inspection gain are **detectable** and must be evaluated.

**Sketch (described in text — student should draw):**
A rectangular block with the 2 mm FBH at 15 mm depth; a probe on the top surface with couplant; a UT screen showing the FBH echo as a tall peak at the 15 mm position; the gain knob and FSH line marked.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

#### **Q.B (a) Define Percent Sensitivity in Radiographic Testing. A 40 mm thick carbon steel weld is radiographed using a wire-type IQI. The thinnest wire visible on the radiograph has a diameter of 0.8 mm. Determine the percent sensitivity and state whether the inspection is acceptable. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Percent Radiographic Sensitivity $S_{RT}$ is defined as the ratio of the diameter of the thinnest visible IQI wire to the total thickness of the test specimen, expressed as a percentage:

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{T_{wire}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Given Data:**
- Total specimen thickness $T_{total} = 40$ mm.
- Thinnest visible IQI wire diameter $T_{wire} = 0.8$ mm.

**Calculation:**

$$
S_{RT} = \frac{0.8}{40} \times 100 = 2.0\%
$$

**Acceptance Check:**

The minimum acceptable percent sensitivity for industrial radiographic inspection of welds is **$S_{RT} \geq 2\%$** as per **ASTM E747 / ISO 19232-1**. Since the calculated value is **exactly 2%**, the inspection **meets the minimum acceptance criterion** and is **acceptable**.

**Conclusion:** The radiographic sensitivity is **2.0%**, which is acceptable for general industrial inspection per ASTM E747.

---

#### **Q.B (b) Explain the role of Image Quality Indicator (IQI) in establishing radiographic sensitivity. Differentiate between wire-type and hole-type IQIs. (7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of IQI:**
The **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**, also called a **penetrameter**, is a device placed on the **source side** of the test specimen during radiography. Its primary function is to **quantify the radiographic sensitivity** of the inspection by determining the **thinnest detail (wire diameter or hole diameter) that is clearly visible on the developed radiograph**. The IQI is manufactured from the **same material (or radiographically similar) as the test specimen** to ensure equivalent absorption.

**Wire-Type IQI (per ASTM E747):**
- Consists of a set of **parallel wires** of varying diameters (e.g., 0.1, 0.13, 0.16, 0.20, 0.25, 0.32, 0.40, 0.50, 0.63, 0.80, 1.00, 1.25, 1.60, 2.00 mm).
- Wires are mounted on a transparent plastic holder.
- The **thinnest visible wire** determines sensitivity.
- Most commonly used in **weld inspection**.

**Hole-Type IQI (per ASTM E1025):**
- Contains **three holes** of specified diameters (e.g., 1T, 2T, 4T — where T is the specimen thickness).
- The **smallest hole visible** on the radiograph indicates sensitivity.
- Used in **castings and thicker sections** (above 50 mm).

**Differences Table:**

| Feature | Wire-Type IQI | Hole-Type IQI |
|---|---|---|
| Indicator Element | Series of parallel wires | Three holes of different sizes |
| Standard | ASTM E747 | ASTM E1025 |
| Sensitivity Metric | Thinnest visible wire | Smallest visible hole |
| Typical Application | Welds, thin to medium sections | Castings, thick sections |
| Sensitivity Range | 0.5% to 4% | 1% to 4% |

**Conclusion:** IQIs are the **standardized means of verifying** that the radiographic technique provides sufficient sensitivity to detect flaws of engineering significance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Sensitivity Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Sensitivity with Resolution** — KTU examiners *deliberately* set questions where students interchange these. Memorize: *Sensitivity = smallest detectable; Resolution = separation capability.*
> 2. **Forgetting the % sign in RT Sensitivity Formula** — Writing $T_{wire}/T_{total}$ without multiplying by 100 loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping Units** — UT sensitivity **must** have **dB**; RT sensitivity **must** have **%**. Omitting units is a guaranteed 0.5-mark penalty.
> 4. **Not Mentioning the Reference Standard** — Saying "sensitivity is set using a reference block" without specifying *which* block (IIW V1/V2, ASTM test block, etc.) loses a mark.
> 5. **Mixing DAC and DGS** — DAC is a **curve** that compensates attenuation; DGS is a **diagram** that relates distance, gain, and size. Examiners will deduct marks if you interchange them.
> 6. **Not Drawing a Diagram** — In 7-mark Part-B questions, a missing/neat diagram costs **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — System Sensitivity**

- ✅ **Definition:** System Sensitivity = ability of NDT system to detect the smallest flaw, referenced to a standard.
- ✅ **UT Sensitivity Unit:** **decibels (dB)**; reference from **IIW V2 block (2 mm FBH at 15 mm depth)** or **V1 block**.
- ✅ **RT Sensitivity Unit:** **percent (%)**; calculated using **IQI wire diameter / total thickness × 100**.
- ✅ **Acceptance criterion (RT):** $S_{RT} \geq 2\%$ (industrial); $S_{RT} \geq 1\%$ (nuclear/critical).
- ✅ **Sensitivity vs Resolution:** Sensitivity = detection limit; Resolution = separation of two close reflectors.
- ✅ **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = c/f$ — sensitivity improves as $f \uparrow$, but penetration reduces.
- ✅ **Attenuation law:** $\alpha \propto f^n$ — higher frequency → more attenuation → less penetration.
- ✅ **6 dB Rule:** UT inspection requires **SNR $\geq$ 6 dB** for reliable detection (signal = 2 × noise).
- ✅ **DAC Curve:** Plots echo amplitude vs depth; compensates for attenuation.
- ✅ **DGS (AVG) Diagram:** Relates **D**istance, **G**ain, and **S**ize of reflector (or German: **A**bstand, **V**erstärkung, **G**röße).
- ✅ **MT/PT Sensitivity:** Expressed as **Sensitivity Levels (Level ½ to Level 4)** per ASTM E709 / E1417.
- ✅ **Five Factor Categories:** Equipment, Material, Operator, Environmental, Calibration.
- ✅ **Trade-off in UT:** Sensitivity ↔ Penetration — choose frequency based on grain size and thickness.
- ✅ **IQI Placement:** Always on the **source side** of the specimen (not the film side).
- ✅ **Reference Gain** $G_{ref}$ → **Inspection Gain** $G_{insp} = G_{ref} + \Delta G$ (with sensitivity margin).
- ✅ **Remember the standards:** IIW Block, ASTM E164 (UT), ASTM E747 / E1025 (RT), ASTM E709 (MT), ASTM E1417 (PT).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
