$$k = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} \, (1 - j)$$

So the **attenuation constant** is:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

The skin depth is defined as the reciprocal of the attenuation constant:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $\mu = \mu_{0} \mu_{r}$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2\pi f \cdot \mu_{0} \mu_{r} \cdot \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu_{0} \mu_{r} \sigma}}$$

Now, using the relation $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ and $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_{0} \mu_{r}}}$$

Plugging in $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{4\pi^{2} \times 10^{-7} \cdot f \cdot \mu_{r}}}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3.5}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_{r} f}}$$

$$\delta \approx 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_{r} f}} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

This is the **standard skin depth formula** used by KTU examiners.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A non-magnetic aluminum alloy ($\mu_{r} = 1$) has a resistivity of $\rho = 2.82 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m. Find the skin depth at a test frequency of 480 kHz.

**Step 1 — Identify known values:**

$$\rho = 2.82 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Ω·m}, \quad f = 480 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}, \quad \mu_{r} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Apply the skin depth formula:**

$$\delta = 503.3 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{1 \times 480 \times 10^{3}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the argument of the square root:**

$$\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{4.8 \times 10^{5}} = 5.875 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{5.875 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.424 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 503.3:**

$$\delta = 503.3 \times 2.424 \times 10^{-7} = 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.122 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** At 480 kHz, eddy currents in aluminum penetrate only about **0.12 mm** — confirming that ECT is fundamentally a **surface and near-surface** technique.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Conductivity–Impedance Relationship

For a circular coil of radius $r_{c}$ placed at lift-off $l_{0}$ above a thick conductor, the change in coil impedance due to eddy currents is given by the **Dodd-Deeds solution** (simplified form for the absolute probe):

$$\Delta Z = \omega^{2} \mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi r_{c}^{2}}{2} \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{e^{-2 \alpha l_{0}} \cdot \alpha}{\alpha + \alpha_{1}} \cdot J_{1}^{2}(\alpha r_{c}) \, d\alpha$$

Where:
- $\alpha_{1} = \sqrt{\alpha^{2} + j \omega \mu_{0} \mu_{r} \sigma}$
- $J_{1}$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1
- $N$ is the number of turns

While the full integral is complex, the **key insight** for KTU is that:

$$\Delta Z \propto \sigma^{n} \quad \text{where } n \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1$$

This means the **impedance change is a monotonic function of conductivity** — the physical basis for quantitative measurement.

### 3.4 Phase Angle–Conductivity Relation

The phase angle $\phi$ of the secondary field depends on the **ratio of inductive to resistive** components, which scales with:

$$\tan \phi \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{f \mu_{r}}{\sigma}}$$

This is why:
- **High-conductivity materials (copper, aluminum):** Phase angle is **small** (eddy currents dominate, in-phase with driving field).
- **Low-conductivity materials (titanium, Inconel):** Phase angle is **large** (reactive/inductive response dominates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This phase-vs-conductivity mapping is what allows the ECT instrument's "phase dial" to directly read % IACS after calibration.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Increases When… | ECT Response |
|----------|----------------|--------------|
| Conductivity $\sigma$ | Material is purer, less alloyed | Larger $\Delta Z$, smaller phase $\phi$ |
| Frequency $f$ | Probe operates at higher $f$ | Smaller skin depth, surface-sensitive |
| Permeability $\mu_{r}$ | Material is ferromagnetic (steel) | Induces magnetic noise; magnetic ECT required |
| Lift-off $l_{0}$ | Probe moves away from surface | Vector moves along lift-off curve |
| Specimen thickness $d$ | Part is thicker | Approaches "thick plate" limit when $d > 3\delta$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an ECT Conductivity Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator Unit 60 kHz to 1 MHz] --> B[Power Amplifier]
    B --> C[Excitation Coil Probe]
    C --> D[Eddy Currents Induced in Specimen]
    D --> E[Secondary Magnetic Field]
    E --> F[Probe Impedance Change]
    F --> G[Bridge Balance Network]
    G --> H[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    H --> I[Signal Processor Microcontroller]
    I --> J[Calibration Lookup Table]
    J --> K[Display: Percent IACS]
    I --> L[Data Logging Interface]
    L --> M[USB or RS-232 Output to PC]

    style A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000000
    style C fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    style D fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000000
    style H fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    style K fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#5B21B6,color:#000000

```

### 4.2 Conductivity Measurement Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Specimen of Unknown Conductivity] --> B{Is Surface Clean and Flat?}
    B -- No --> C[Prepare Surface Polish, Degrease]
    C --> B
    B -- Yes --> D[Place Calibration Standard on Probe]
    D --> E[Perform Air Calibration Zero]
    E --> F[Calibrate with Known %IACS Block e.g., 100%, 30%, 10%]
    F --> G[Place Probe on Test Specimen]
    G --> H[Acquire Phase and Amplitude]
    H --> I[Apply Lift-Off Correction Dual-Frequency]
    I --> J[Convert to %IACS via Calibration Curve]
    J --> K{Conductivity in Expected Range?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Accept Reading, Record]
    K -- No --> M[Flag as Anomalous - Verify Specimen]
    M --> N{Heat Treat or Alloy Mismatch?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Report to QC Engineering]
    N -- No --> P[Recheck Calibration and Re-measure]
```

```mermaid

    B --> C[Specimen Contact: Lift-off = 0]
    C --> D{Lift-off Increases}

    D -- No --> F[Vector stays at Material Point]
    F --> G{High Conductivity e.g., Copper}
    F --> H{Medium Conductivity e.g., Aluminum}
    F --> I[Low Conductivity e.g., Titanium]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define conductivity measurement using ECT. What is the significance of the IACS scale?**

**Model Answer:**

Conductivity measurement using Eddy Current Testing is a non-contact NDT technique that determines the electrical conductivity of a material by analyzing the response of an eddy current probe's impedance to the secondary field generated by induced currents in the specimen.

The **International Annealed Copper Standard (IACS)** sets **100% IACS = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m (= 58 MS/m)**, defined as the conductivity of pure annealed copper at 20°C. The IACS scale allows universal comparison of conductivities across materials, alloys, and heat-treated conditions, expressed as a percentage. *(3 marks: Definition 1.5 marks + IACS significance 1.5 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**List three factors that affect eddy current conductivity measurement and explain how each influences the result.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lift-off (probe-to-specimen gap):** Increases lift-off moves the impedance vector along the lift-off curve, producing false conductivity readings if not compensated. *(1 mark)*
2. **Test frequency:** Higher frequency reduces skin depth, making measurement more surface-sensitive; lower frequency penetrates deeper. Frequency must be chosen based on material and expected defect depth. *(1 mark)*
3. **Material permeability (μᵣ):** Ferromagnetic materials distort the response because both conductivity and permeability affect the impedance. For non-magnetic materials, μᵣ ≈ 1, simplifying analysis. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **skin depth** in a conductor as used in ECT. A non-magnetic aluminum specimen has resistivity $\rho = 2.82 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m. Calculate the skin depth at a test frequency of 480 kHz. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **lift-off effect** in ECT conductivity measurement. How is it suppressed in modern dual-frequency conductivity meters? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a)**

**Step 1:** Start from Maxwell's equation for a plane wave in a good conductor:

$$\nabla^{2} \vec{E} = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2:** Assume $E_{x}(z, t) = E_{0} e^{j(\omega t - kz)}$ and substitute:

$$-k^{2} = j\omega \mu \sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \sqrt{-j\omega \mu \sigma}$$

**Step 3:** Write the attenuation constant $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Define skin depth as $\delta = 1/\alpha$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$, $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, and $\sigma = 1/\rho$:

$$\delta = 503.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

**Step 6:** Plug in the values for aluminum at 480 kHz:

$$\delta = 503.3 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{1 \times 480 \times 10^{3}}}$$

$$\delta = 503.3 \times 2.424 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.122 \text{ mm}}$$

**Marks distribution:** (1) Wave equation setup – 1, (2) Solving for k – 1, (3) Attenuation constant – 1, (4) Final formula – 1, (5) Substitution for aluminum – 2, (6) Final numerical answer – 1.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b)**

The **lift-off effect** is the change in probe impedance caused by variations in the small air gap between the probe tip and the specimen surface. It causes the impedance vector on the impedance plane to move along a characteristic curve (the "lift-off curve") rather than along the conductivity axis. This is the dominant source of error in conductivity readings, because unwanted probe wobble or surface curvature mimics changes in conductivity.

**Suppression in Dual-Frequency Meters:**

- The instrument operates simultaneously at two frequencies: a **high frequency (f₁ ≈ 480 kHz)** and a **low frequency (f₂ ≈ 60 kHz)**.
- The **high frequency** response is highly sensitive to lift-off but also to conductivity.
- The **low frequency** response is less sensitive to lift-off but still tracks conductivity.
- The instrument **subtracts** or **takes the ratio** of the two responses. Because lift-off produces similar changes at both frequencies (in a fixed proportion), the difference/ratio cancels lift-off and isolates the conductivity signal.
- A **phase-locked loop** and **microcontroller** implement this in real-time, yielding lift-off-corrected % IACS readings accurate to ±0.5% IACS.

**Marks distribution:** (1) Definition of lift-off – 2, (2) Effect on impedance plane – 2, (3) Dual-frequency principle – 2, (4) Resulting accuracy – 1.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an ECT conductivity meter**. List four engineering applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A copper conductor ($\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$) is tested at 100 kHz. Calculate the **skin depth**. If the test frequency is increased to 1 MHz, what will be the new skin depth? Comment on the engineering significance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a)**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above; draw oscillator → coil → specimen → impedance change → phase detector → signal processor → display.)

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**

1. **Oscillator** generates a high-frequency AC signal (60 kHz – 1 MHz).
2. The **excitation coil** converts this into a primary alternating magnetic field.
3. The field **induces eddy currents** in the nearby specimen (Faraday's Law).
4. The eddy currents create a **secondary magnetic field** that opposes the primary (Lenz's Law).
5. The **impedance of the probe coil changes** (both resistance and reactance components shift).
6. A **bridge balance network** compares this shifted impedance with a reference.
7. A **phase-sensitive detector** extracts the in-phase and quadrature components.
8. The **microcontroller** applies calibration curves to compute conductivity in % IACS.
9. The **display** shows the value, and the result can be logged.

**Four Engineering Applications:**

1. **Heat treatment verification** of aluminum alloys in aerospace.
2. **Sorting of mixed scrap metals** in recycling.
3. **Verification of cladding thickness** on turbine blades.
4. **Detection of heat damage** in stainless steel reactor components.

**Marks distribution:** Diagram – 2, Principle steps – 3, Applications – 2.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b)**

**Given:** $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$, $f_1 = 100$ kHz, $f_2 = 1$ MHz.

**Step 1 — Apply skin depth formula:**

$$\delta = 503.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$$

Since $\rho = 1/\sigma = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m:

**At f₁ = 100 kHz:**

$$\delta_1 = 503.3 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8}}{1 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$\delta_1 = 503.3 \times \sqrt{1.724 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$\delta_1 = 503.3 \times 4.152 \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_1 \approx 0.209 \text{ mm}}$$

**At f₂ = 1 MHz:**

$$\delta_2 = 503.3 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8}}{1 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$\delta_2 = 503.3 \times \sqrt{1.724 \times 10^{-14}}$$

$$\delta_2 = 503.3 \times 1.313 \times 10^{-7} \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_2 \approx 0.066 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Significance:**

- Skin depth scales as $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$. A **10× increase in frequency** reduces skin depth by a factor of **√10 ≈ 3.16**.
- At 100 kHz, the probe examines a copper layer about **0.21 mm** deep; at 1 MHz, only the **outermost 0.066 mm**.
- This means **higher frequencies are used for surface defects**, while **lower frequencies probe deeper** — a critical design choice when inspecting thick sections.
- For copper (very high conductivity), even at 1 MHz the skin depth is shallow, making ECT a true **surface NDT method** for high-conductivity materials.

**Marks distribution:** Formula – 1, f₁ calculation – 2, f₂ calculation – 2, Engineering significance – 2.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** in skin depth answers — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if "mm" or "m" is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing % IACS with S/m** — always clarify which unit the answer is in. The conversion factor 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m is the most-asked constant in this module.
> 3. **Incorrect square-root handling in skin depth** — students often write $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ instead of $\sqrt{1/(\pi f \mu \sigma)}$; these are equivalent, but show the full derivation to be safe.
> 4. **Ignoring lift-off in descriptive answers** — Q on ECT conductivity MUST mention lift-off suppression (dual-frequency or phase method) to score full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up the roles of frequency and conductivity in the skin depth formula** — frequency is in the denominator (higher f → smaller δ), conductivity is in the denominator (higher σ → smaller δ).
> 6. **Forgetting to mention μᵣ for non-magnetic materials** — always state $\mu_r = 1$ explicitly to avoid ambiguity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **100% IACS = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m = 58 MS/m** — the universal reference benchmark. *(Memorize!)*
- **Skin depth formula:** $\delta = 503.3 \sqrt{\rho / (\mu_r f)}$ meters — derived from Maxwell's equations, decays as 1/√f. *(Derive in exam!)*
- **ECT measures % IACS via impedance change** in the probe coil when eddy currents load it.
- **Lift-off effect** is the #1 source of error — modern meters use **dual-frequency** to suppress it.
- **Test frequencies** for conductivity meters: typically 60 kHz to 1 MHz; 480 kHz is a common standard.
- **Phase angle** decreases with increasing conductivity — high-σ materials give small phase shifts.
- **Skin depth at 480 kHz in aluminum** ≈ 0.12 mm — ECT is fundamentally a **surface technique** for high-conductivity materials.
- **Higher frequency** → shallower penetration → surface-sensitive measurement.
- **Higher conductivity** → smaller skin depth, larger eddy currents, smaller phase angle.
- **ECT applications** include heat-treatment verification, alloy sorting, corrosion detection, cold-work detection, and material verification (PMI).
- **Ferromagnetic materials** distort ECT readings due to high μᵣ — use **magnetic ECT probes** or surface magnetic saturation for steel inspection.
- **Calibration standards** of known % IACS (e.g., 100%, 30%, 10% IACS blocks) are mandatory before any quantitative measurement.
- **Phase-sensitive detection** extracts both amplitude and phase, allowing the instrument to separate conductivity from lift-off and other variables.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness** is defined as the **resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation**, typically caused by mechanical indentation, abrasion, or scratching. In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, hardness measurement is classified as a **mechanical surface NDT method** that evaluates a material's mechanical integrity by probing a small, localized region without destroying the component's serviceability.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hardness is the **measure of a material's ability to resist permanent (plastic) deformation** when subjected to a known applied force via a standard indenter of defined geometry and material.

The standard hardness unit is **kgf/mm²** (kilogram-force per square millimeter) for most indentation methods, while some methods (Rockwell) use **HR** (Hardness Rockwell) followed by a scale letter (e.g., **HRC**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thumbtack" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine pressing your thumb against a soft pillow versus a wooden table. The pillow deforms easily (low hardness), while the table resists deformation (high hardness). Now, in NDT, instead of a thumb, engineers press a **standardized geometric indenter** (like a hardened steel ball or a diamond pyramid) into the material with a **known force**, and measure the depth or size of the indentation. The smaller the indentation, the harder the material.

This is the entire philosophy of indentation hardness testing: **measure what resists deformation**.

## 1.3 Classification of Hardness Measurement Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Three Fundamental Approaches:**
> 1. **Indentation Hardness** — Resistance to permanent deformation by an indenter (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop).
> 2. **Rebound Hardness** — Measures elastic rebound (Shore Scleroscope, Leeb).
> 3. **Scratch Hardness** — Resistance to scratching (Mohs scale — qualitative only).

The **Indentation method** is the most widely used in industrial NDT and constitutes the bulk of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 assessment weightage.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Hardness correlates strongly with several mechanical properties:
- **Tensile Strength** — empirical relationship exists.
- **Wear Resistance** — directly proportional in most alloys.
- **Fatigue Strength** — surface hardness affects fatigue life.
- **Heat Treatment Verification** — surface hardness validates case hardening, tempering, annealing.
- **Material Identification & Homogeneity** — detects local variations, inclusions, phase changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical NDT Aspect:** Hardness testing is *semi-destructive* — it leaves a tiny indent, but the component is generally still serviceable. This places it at the boundary of destructive and non-destructive testing, and KTU explicitly classifies it under **NDT Module 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Indentation Hardness Testing

The general operational logic of any indentation hardness test follows a **four-stage sequence**:

1. **Surface Preparation** — The test surface is ground/polished to a defined roughness (typically $\leq 0.4 \, \mu m$ $R_a$ for Vickers, $\leq 0.8 \, \mu m$ $R_a$ for Brinell). A poorly prepared surface introduces scatter.
2. **Application of Pre-load (Major/Minor Load)** — A small initial load seats the indenter and removes surface effects, then the major load is applied for a defined **dwell time** (typically **10–15 seconds**).
3. **Indentation Formation** — The indenter penetrates the material plastically. The depth or projected/diagonal area is measured.
4. **Hardness Calculation** — A geometry-specific formula converts the measured dimension into a hardness number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dwell time matters:** The dwell allows **elastic recovery** to stabilize and **creep** to complete, ensuring the measured indentation size reflects only the plastic component — critical for repeatable results.

## 2.2 The Four Major Indentation Methods — Operational Comparison

### 2.2.1 Brinell Hardness Test (HB)

- **Indenter:** Hardened steel or tungsten carbide ball of diameter $D$ (commonly **10 mm**, also 5 mm, 2.5 mm, 1 mm).
- **Load $F$:** Standard loads of **500 kgf, 1500 kgf, 3000 kgf** chosen to keep the similarity index constant.
- **Measurement:** Diameter $d$ of the indentation left on the surface, measured using a portable microscope.
- **Applications:** Cast iron, forgings, rough surfaces, coarse-grained materials.

### 2.2.2 Vickers Hardness Test (HV)

- **Indenter:** Square-based diamond pyramid with face angle of **136°** between opposite faces.
- **Load $F$:** Range from **1 gf to 100 kgf** (micro to macro Vickers).
- **Measurement:** Mean diagonal $d = (d_1 + d_2) / 2$ of the square indentation.
- **Applications:** Hardened steels, thin sheets, case-hardened layers, small parts, research applications.

### 2.2.3 Rockwell Hardness Test (HR)

- **Indenter:** Either a **diamond cone (Brale)** with 120° apex angle, or a **steel ball** (1/16″, 1/8″, 1/4″, 1/2″).
- **Procedure:** Apply **minor load (10 kgf)**, then **major load (60, 100, or 150 kgf)**, measure residual depth $h$.
- **Measurement:** Depth-based, direct read on dial/display — **no optical measurement required**.
- **Applications:** Most widely used production-line test due to speed and simplicity.

### 2.2.4 Knoop Hardness Test (HK)

- **Indenter:** Elongated rhombic-based diamond pyramid with longitudinal edge angle **172°30′** and transverse edge angle **130°**.
- **Load $F$:** Typically **25 gf to 5 kgf** (microhardness regime).
- **Measurement:** Long diagonal $d$ of the elongated indentation.
- **Applications:** Microhardness, brittle materials, ceramic coatings, small regions, surface layers, fracture toughness evaluation.

## 2.3 Rebound Hardness — Leeb and Shore

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Rebound hardness does **not** measure plastic deformation; it measures **elastic energy loss** during impact. A hard material absorbs less energy and the indenter rebounds higher.

- **Shore Scleroscope:** A diamond-tipped hammer falls under gravity from a fixed height onto the test surface. The rebound height (1 to 140 scale divisions) is the hardness.
- **Leeb Hardness (HL):** A modern instrumented version. An impact body with a tungsten carbide ball is propelled toward the surface. The ratio of rebound velocity $v_r$ to impact velocity $v_i$ is measured electronically:
$$HL = \frac{v_r}{v_i} \times 1000$$

Leeb is the most widely used **portable rebound hardness** method and is essential for in-service inspection of large components (forgings, rolls, pipelines).

## 2.4 The Mohs Scratch Test

A **qualitative** mineralogical scale (1 = Talc, 10 = Diamond) based on scratch resistance. Used only for mineral identification; not for engineering NDT in the strict KTU sense.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — they appear every cycle.**

| **Method** | **Hardness Formula** | **Indenter Geometry** | **Typical Load** | **Key Parameter Measured** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brinell (HB)** | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$ | Hardened steel/Tungsten carbide ball, $D$ = 10 mm (std) | 500 / 1500 / 3000 kgf | Indent diameter $d$ (mm) |
| **Vickers (HV)** | $HV = \dfrac{2F \sin(136°/2)}{d^2} = \dfrac{1.8544 \, F}{d^2}$ | Square diamond pyramid, 136° face angle | 1 gf – 100 kgf | Mean diagonal $d$ (mm) |
| **Rockwell (HR)** | $HR = N - \dfrac{h}{s}$ (e.g., $N=100$, $s=0.002$ mm for C scale) | Diamond cone (120°) or steel ball | 60 / 100 / 150 kgf | Residual depth $h$ (mm) |
| **Knoop (HK)** | $HK = \dfrac{14.229 \, F}{d^2}$ | Elongated diamond pyramid, 172°30′ long edge | 25 gf – 5 kgf | Long diagonal $d$ (mm) |
| **Leeb (HL)** | $HL = \dfrac{v_r}{v_i} \times 1000$ | Tungsten carbide ball (impact) | Impact energy-based | Velocity ratio |
| **Shore** | $HS = \dfrac{h_{rebound}}{h_{drop}} \times 140$ (approx.) | Diamond hammer (drop) | Gravitational drop | Rebound height ratio |
| **Tensile-Hardness Correlation** | $UTS \approx (3.4 \text{ to } 3.5) \times HB$ (for steels) | — | — | Empirical constant |
| **Similarity Index (Brinell constraint)** | $\dfrac{F}{D^2} = \text{constant}$ | — | — | Ensures geometrically similar indents |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Constraints:**
> - **Brinell valid range:** $0.25 D < d < 0.6 D$ (otherwise readings are invalid — too small or too large indents produce scatter).
> - **Vickers valid range:** $d > 6 \, \mu m$ for micro Vickers; indent must be free from edge effects (distance from edge $\geq 2.5 d$).
> - **Rockwell C valid range:** $20 \leq HRC \leq 70$ (outside this, use other scales).
> - **Indent spacing:** Minimum **3 d** center-to-center and **2.5 d** from any edge for Vickers; **3 d** for Brinell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Vickers microhardness used to validate case depth of nitrided/tempered turbine components.
- **Power Generation:** Leeb portable testing on pressure vessels, rotors, and turbine blades in-situ.
- **Automotive:** Rockwell C for bulk hardness of crankshafts, camshafts, gears; Vickers for surface coatings.
- **Foundries & Forges:** Brinell preferred for rough, large castings/forgings.
- **Materials R&D:** Micro-Vickers and Knoop for thin films, welds, HAZ (heat-affected zone), fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ correlation via indentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

Brinell hardness is defined as the **applied load per unit curved (spherical) contact area** of the indentation on the surface of the material.

The contact surface is the **cap of a sphere** of radius $R = D/2$, with the indentation having depth $h$ and diameter $d$.

### 3.1.2 Geometry of the Spherical Cap

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric setup:** A ball of diameter $D$ creates a spherical indent of diameter $d$ and depth $h$. The relationship between $D$, $d$, and $h$ comes from the geometry of a circle.

Consider a circle of diameter $D$. A chord of length $d$ subtends a sagitta (height) of $h$. From the Pythagorean theorem applied to a radius $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 &= \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 \\
\frac{D^2}{4} - Dh + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4} &= \frac{D^2}{4} \\
h^2 - Dh + \frac{d^2}{4} &= 0 \\
h &= \frac{D \pm \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $h$ must be smaller than $D/2$, we take the **negative root**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Surface Area of the Spherical Cap

The curved surface area $A$ of a spherical cap of a sphere of diameter $D$ and sagitta $h$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \pi \, D \, h
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a standard result from solid geometry: the lateral surface area of a spherical cap of height $h$ cut from a sphere of diameter $D$ is $A = \pi D h$. Derivation comes from integrating circular strips, or equivalently using the theorem of Archimedes on surface area of a spherical segment.

Substituting the expression for $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \pi \, D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{\pi D}{2} \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Brinell Hardness Number

By definition, hardness is load per unit contact area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HB &= \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\frac{\pi D}{2} \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)} \\
\therefore \quad HB &= \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard Brinell formula** that KTU expects students to derive from first principles in ESE Module 2.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Number Formula

### 3.2.1 Indenter Geometry

A square-based diamond pyramid with face angle $\alpha = 136°$ between opposite faces. The **mean diagonal** of the square indentation is $d$.

### 3.2.2 Contact Area

The indentation is a square of side $a$, where the diagonal is $d = a\sqrt{2}$. The area of the square base is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = a^2 = \frac{d^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Slope Consideration

Because the indenter is a pyramid, the **projected contact area** at the surface (not the actual slanted face area) is used by convention in Vickers. The face angle is 136°, meaning each face makes an angle of $136°/2 = 68°$ with the vertical axis, or equivalently $22°$ with the horizontal surface. The projected area relates to the face area as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{projected} = A_{face} \times \sin(68°) = A_{face} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some texts use the actual contact area (slant area), but the **standard KTU Vickers formula** uses the projected area. We follow the projected-area convention.

With the standard derivation (using projected area on the indentation surface):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{d^2}{2} \\
HV &= \frac{F}{A} = \frac{2F}{d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Including the trigonometric correction for the pyramid face angle of 136° (each face makes 22° with the base, hence $\sin(22°)$ factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HV &= \frac{2F \sin(22°)}{d^2} = \frac{2F \times 0.9272}{d^2} \approx \frac{1.8544 \, F}{d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The constant 1.8544 = $2 \sin(22°)$ comes from the chosen face angle of 136°, which was deliberately selected so that Vickers and Brinell numbers agree for hardness values around 30 HB** — a historically important calibration consistency.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Rockwell Hardness Number

Rockwell uses a **differential depth measurement** under a two-stage loading sequence.

### 3.3.1 Procedure

1. Apply **minor (preliminary) load** $F_0 = 10$ kgf, seat indenter, establish reference depth $h_0$.
2. Apply **major load** $F_1$ (60, 100, or 150 kgf depending on scale). Total load = $F_0 + F_1$.
3. Hold major load for dwell time, then **remove major load** while keeping minor load.
4. Measure the **residual increment in depth** $\Delta h$ from the reference position.

### 3.3.2 Formula Derivation

By convention, the Rockwell number is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR = N - \frac{\Delta h}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N$ is a constant specific to the scale and $s$ is the scale unit (typically $s = 0.002$ mm = 2 μm).

**For Rockwell C scale (HRC):**
- Indenter: Diamond cone, 120° apex, rounded tip.
- $F_1 = 150$ kgf.
- $N = 100$, $s = 0.002$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRC = 100 - \frac{\Delta h}{0.002}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Rockwell B scale (HRB):**
- Indenter: 1/16″ steel ball.
- $F_1 = 90$ kgf.
- $N = 130$, $s = 0.002$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRB = 130 - \frac{\Delta h}{0.002}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The subtraction $N - \Delta h/s$ ensures that a **harder material** (smaller $\Delta h$) gives a **larger Rockwell number**, matching the intuitive sense.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 often asks:** *"Which hardness method would you select for the following application, and why?"* — This matrix is your answer.

| **Application Scenario** | **Recommended Method** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| Large rough cast iron forging | **Brinell (HB)** | Tolerates rough surface, large indent averages inhomogeneities |
| Hardened steel tool, surface case | **Vickers (HV)** | Precise, applicable to all hardness ranges, small indents |
| Production line, rapid QC | **Rockwell (HRB/HRC)** | Direct dial reading, no optics, fast (~5 sec/test) |
| Thin sheet, case depth profile | **Micro-Vickers (HV)** | Very low loads (10–1000 gf), small indents |
| In-situ rotor inspection | **Leeb (HL)** | Portable, no sample prep on-site |
| Ceramic coating, brittle material | **Knoop (HK)** | Low load, small indent, only one diagonal needed |
| Mineral identification | **Mohs** | Scratch resistance scale, qualitative |
| Heat-treated steel, quenched part | **Rockwell C (HRC)** | Fast, standard in heat-treatment QC |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Typical Question

**Problem:** A Brinell hardness test on a steel specimen used a $D = 10$ mm ball and a load $F = 3000$ kgf. The measured indentation diameter was $d = 4.5$ mm. Calculate the BHN.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 10 \text{ mm} \\
d &= 4.5 \text{ mm} \\
F &= 3000 \text{ kgf}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 1 — Compute $D^2 - d^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^2 - d^2 &= 100 - 20.25 = 79.75 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{79.75} &= 8.9306 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the denominator term $D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} &= 10 - 8.9306 = 1.0694 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Apply the Brinell formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HB &= \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times 1.0694} \\
HB &= \frac{6000}{33.591} = 178.6 \text{ kgf/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity check:** $d/D = 0.45$, which lies within the valid range $0.25 < 0.45 < 0.6$. ✓ Reading is valid.

Step 5 — Estimate ultimate tensile strength (for steel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
UTS &\approx 3.45 \times HB = 3.45 \times 178.6 \approx 616 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$
