# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define hardness. List the three principal methods of hardness measurement with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Hardness is the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation caused by an indenter, scratch, or impact under a known applied force.

**Three principal methods:**

1. **Indentation Hardness** — A sharp indenter is pressed into the surface with a known load, and the size/depth of the indent is measured.
   - *Examples:* Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop.
2. **Rebound Hardness** — An impact body strikes the surface and the height/ratio of rebound is measured.
   - *Examples:* Shore Scleroscope, Leeb Hardness.
3. **Scratch Hardness** — A material is scratched by a harder reference material.
   - *Example:* Mohs Scale (1 to 10).

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks methods with examples)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the formula for Brinell Hardness Number. Why is the face angle of a Vickers indenter chosen as 136°?**

**Model Answer:**

**Brinell formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HB = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reason for Vickers face angle 136°:** The face angle of 136° was **deliberately selected** so that the Vickers Hardness Number **matches the Brinell Hardness Number** at a value of approximately **30 HB/HV**. This was the standard mild-steel hardness in early 20th-century industrial calibration, ensuring continuity between the two methods.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks formula + 1.5 marks reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**a) (7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Brinell Hardness Test**. Derive the expression for Brinell Hardness Number in terms of load, ball diameter, and indentation diameter. State the similarity condition for Brinell testing.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A hardened steel or tungsten carbide ball of diameter $D$ is pressed into the specimen surface under a known load $F$ for a specified dwell time. The diameter $d$ of the resulting indentation is measured using a microscope, and the BHN is calculated.

**Diagram (in text form):** The test setup consists of:
- A hydraulic press applying vertical load $F$.
- A ball indenter (10 mm standard) held in a holder.
- A flat specimen placed on an anvil.
- A measuring microscope to read the indent diameter $d$ in two perpendicular directions.

**Derivation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The full derivation is presented in Section 3.1 of these notes. We reproduce the key steps here.

Step 1 — Geometry: depth of spherical indent

$$
\begin{aligned}
h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Curved surface area of the spherical cap

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \pi D h = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Brinell Hardness Number

$$
\begin{aligned}
HB = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarity Condition:** To obtain **geometrically similar indentations** across materials of different hardness, the ratio $F/D^2$ must be kept constant. The standard ratios are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F}{D^2} = 30 \quad &\text{(for steels and hard alloys)} \\
\frac{F}{D^2} = 10 \quad &\text{(for copper, aluminum, soft alloys)} \\
\frac{F}{D^2} = 5 \quad &\text{(for very soft materials like lead, tin)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The similarity condition ensures that for a given material, the *d/D ratio* (and hence the *strain distribution* under the indent) is comparable across tests with different ball sizes and loads. This is the *geometric similarity principle* of Meyer (1908).

**Standard Constraints:**
- Indent diameter must satisfy $0.25 D < d < 0.6 D$ for valid reading.
- Minimum spacing: **3 d** between centers and **2.5 d** from specimen edge.
- Dwell time: **10 to 15 seconds** for ferrous metals.

*(7 marks — 2 marks principle + 3 marks derivation + 1 mark similarity condition + 1 mark constraints)*

---

**b) (7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Vickers Hardness Test**. Derive the expression for Vickers Hardness Number. List any **four advantages** of Vickers over Brinell.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A square-based diamond pyramid indenter with a face angle of 136° is pressed into the specimen under a known load. The diagonals of the resulting square indentation are measured, and the mean diagonal is used to compute the Vickers Hardness Number.

**Diagram (in text form):** The test setup consists of:
- A loading lever or deadweight system.
- A diamond pyramid indenter (angle 136° between opposite faces).
- An objective lens system to illuminate and measure the diagonals of the indentation.
- A digital readout (modern) or micrometer eyepiece (older systems).

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — Indenter geometry: face angle 136° between opposite faces. Each face is inclined at $22°$ to the horizontal base of the indent.

Step 2 — Projected area of the square indent of mean diagonal $d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{projected} = \frac{d^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Including the slope correction factor for the pyramid faces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HV = \frac{F}{A_{projected}} = \frac{2F \sin(22°)}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 \, F}{d^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F$ is in kgf and $d$ is in mm, giving $HV$ in **kgf/mm²**.

**Four Advantages of Vickers over Brinell:**

1. **Single scale for all materials** — Vickers is applicable from very soft (aluminum, ~5 HV) to very hard (carbides, ~3000 HV). Brinell requires changing ball size and load.
2. **Smaller indent** — Vickers uses much smaller indents, suitable for **thin sheets, small parts, and case-hardened layers**.
3. **Non-destructive** — The small indent makes Vickers more truly non-destructive; the specimen can usually still be used.
4. **Better precision** — Measurement of diagonals (length, not curved area) is more accurate than Brinell's measurement of a circular indent diameter, especially at high hardness.

> [!NOTE]
> *Vickers also avoids the Brinell similarity constraint* — the geometric similarity is automatic because the same indenter is used at all loads.

**Disadvantages** (for balanced answer, KTU often asks these too):
- Requires **carefully polished surface** (Brinell tolerates rough surfaces).
- **Slower** than Rockwell (optical measurement required).
- More expensive equipment.

*(7 marks — 2 marks principle + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks advantages)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**a) (7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Rockwell Hardness Test**. Discuss the various **Rockwell scales** with their indenters and loads. What are the **advantages** of Rockwell over Brinell and Vickers?

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Rockwell hardness is determined by **measuring the depth of a permanent indentation** under a defined load, using either a **diamond cone (Brale)** or a **hardened steel ball** as indenter. The two-stage loading (minor + major) eliminates surface irregularities and gives a direct, dial-readable number.

**Procedure (detailed, in text form):**

1. Place specimen on anvil.
2. Apply **minor load $F_0 = 10$ kgf** to seat the indenter and establish a reference zero.
3. Apply **major load $F_1$** (scale-dependent) — typically **60, 100, or 150 kgf**.
4. Hold the major load for a brief dwell (5–10 sec) to allow elastic recovery.
5. **Remove** the major load, keeping the minor load. The depth relaxes back partially.
6. Measure the **residual depth increment $\Delta h$** from the original reference.
7. The Rockwell number is computed automatically (or read on the dial):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HR = N - \frac{\Delta h}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Major Rockwell Scales Table (must memorize for KTU):**

| **Scale** | **Indenter** | **Major Load $F_1$ (kgf)** | **Total Load (kgf)** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (HRA)** | Diamond cone | 60 | 70 | Very hard materials, thin hardened steel, cemented carbides |
| **B (HRB)** | 1/16″ steel ball | 90 | 100 | Soft steels, brass, aluminum, annealed copper |
| **C (HRC)** | Diamond cone | 140 | 150 | Hardened steels (>HRC 20), heat-treated alloys, tool steels |
| **D (HRD)** | Diamond cone | 90 | 100 | Medium-hard steels, case-hardened surfaces |
| **E (HRE)** | 1/8″ steel ball | 90 | 100 | Very soft materials, lead, plastics |
| **F (HRF)** | 1/16″ steel ball | 50 | 60 | Soft bearing alloys, soft bronzes |
| **G (HRG)** | 1/16″ steel ball | 140 | 150 | Phosphor bronze, beryllium copper |

**Superficial Rockwell Scales** (for thin/thin-surface applications):
- **15N, 30N, 45N:** Diamond cone, total loads 15, 30, 45 kgf.
- **15T, 30T, 45T:** 1/16″ steel ball, total loads 15, 30, 45 kgf.

**Formula Recap (HRC and HRB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HRC &= 100 - \frac{\Delta h}{0.002 \text{ mm}} \quad (N = 100, \, s = 0.002 \text{ mm}) \\
HRB &= 130 - \frac{\Delta h}{0.002 \text{ mm}} \quad (N = 130, \, s = 0.002 \text{ mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages of Rockwell over Brinell and Vickers:**

1. **Direct reading** — No optical measurement needed; hardness is read on a dial or digital display, eliminating operator error in measuring indent dimensions.
2. **Speed** — A Rockwell test takes about **5–10 seconds**, far faster than Brinell (1–2 min) or Vickers (1–2 min).
3. **No surface optics required** — Slight surface roughness is acceptable (similar to Brinell, better than Vickers).
4. **Wide range with one instrument** — Multiple scales (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, N, T) cover the entire hardness range.
5. **Operator-independent** — Less skill required; production-line friendly.
6. **Non-destructive for many parts** — The indent is small enough that components often remain serviceable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disadvantages of Rockwell:** Less accurate than Vickers (depth measurement is sensitive to minor surface variations); requires flat specimens; requires careful calibration; ball indenter can flatten at high hardness (use diamond cone for hard materials).

*(7 marks — 2 marks principle + 3 marks scales table + 2 marks advantages)*

---

**b) (7 Marks)** Explain the **Rebound Hardness Test** (Shore Scleroscope and Leeb). How does it differ fundamentally from indentation methods? State any **four applications** of rebound hardness testing.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Rebound Hardness:**

In rebound (dynamic) hardness testing, a hard impact body (diamond or tungsten carbide) is dropped or propelled toward the test surface. The **height** or **velocity ratio** of rebound is measured. The harder the surface, the **more elastic** the collision and the **higher the rebound**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental difference from indentation methods:**
> - *Indentation methods* measure **plastic deformation** (permanent indent).
> - *Rebound methods* measure **elastic deformation** (recoverable energy).
>
> A material can be hard (high rebound) but brittle (no plastic deformation), or soft (low rebound) but ductile. Rebound hardness therefore correlates with **elastic modulus and toughness**, not just plastic flow.

**Shore Scleroscope (Original Rebound Method):**

- A diamond-tipped hammer (weight ~2.5 g) is dropped from a fixed height (typically **254 mm** or 10 inches) inside a graduated glass tube.
- The hammer strikes the test surface vertically.
- The **rebound height** is read on a scale of 140 divisions (each division = 1/140 of the drop height).
- The Shore Hardness Number = (rebound height / drop height) × 140.
- **Applications:** Rolls, large castings, forgings.
- **Limitation:** Fragile glass tube, sensitive to tube inclination, requires vertical surface.

**Leeb Hardness Test (Modern Instrumented Rebound):**

- A tungsten carbide impact body of mass ~5.5 g is propelled toward the surface by a spring.
- The **impact velocity $v_i$** and **rebound velocity $v_r$** are measured electronically (by induction or piezoelectric sensing).
- Leeb Hardness Number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HL = \frac{v_r}{v_i} \times 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

- Range typically **0 to 1000 HL**; calibration against reference test blocks.
- **Available impact devices:** D (most common), DC, G, C, E — covering different geometries, masses, and impact energies.

**Difference from Indentation Methods (Analytical Comparison):**

| **Aspect** | **Indentation** | **Rebound** |
|---|---|---|
| What is measured | Plastic deformation size | Elastic energy loss |
| Quantification | Geometric dimension (depth, diagonal) | Velocity/height ratio |
| Surface preparation | Critical | Less critical |
| Portability | Mostly bench instruments | Highly portable (Leeb) |
| Sensitivity to surface roughness | High | Moderate |
| Correlation with UTS | Direct empirical (3.4×HB) | Indirect, requires calibration |
| Effect of mass of component | Minor (large enough sample) | Major (heavy fixtures needed for light/thin parts) |

**Four Applications of Rebound Hardness Testing:**

1. **In-service inspection of large components** — pressure vessels, turbine rotors, boiler tubes, ship hulls, bridge structures. Leeb portability allows testing in confined or elevated locations.
2. **Rolling mill rolls** — Shore Scleroscope is traditionally used for hardness checking of large steel rolls in steel plants.
3. **Heavy forgings and castings** — Quick assessment of large parts without elaborate sample preparation or sample cutting.
4. **Heat-treated components in-situ** — Hardness verification of gears, shafts, and large bearings on production floors or in the field.
5. *(Bonus)* **Sorting of materials** — rapid identification of forged vs. cast parts based on rebound signatures.
6. *(Bonus)* **NDT of installed pipelines and rail tracks** — Leeb probes can be clamped to in-service pipes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation of Rebound:** The impact energy is much higher than indentation loads, so the affected volume is much larger. Therefore the result is an **average over a larger region**, making it unsuitable for measuring the hardness of thin surface layers or thin coatings.

*(7 marks — 2 marks principle + 2 marks Shore & Leeb explanation + 1 mark difference from indentation + 2 marks applications)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places KTU Students Lose Marks on Hardness Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric derivation** — Examiners expect the full spherical-cap area derivation for Brinell (Section 3.1), not just the final formula. Marks are lost when students start with the formula directly.
> 2. **Unit mistakes** — Brinell/Vickers in **kgf/mm²**, not N/mm² (unless the question explicitly asks for SI). Mixing units in the same problem is a frequent error. KTU usually expects CGS-based **kgf** unless SI is specified.
> 3. **Confusing Brinell ball materials** — Steel balls are for hardness ≤ **450 HB**; tungsten carbide (WC) balls are for hardness > **450 HB**. Forgetting this loses a mark.
> 4. **Rockwell scale confusion** — HRC uses **diamond cone + 150 kgf**, HRB uses **1/16″ ball + 100 kgf**. Mixing these up is a classic 1–2 mark loss.
> 5. **Vickers 1.8544 factor** — Students often forget the $\sin(22°)$ derivation and just write the constant. Examiners award partial credit but the full derivation gains more marks.
> 6. **Brinell valid range** — Not stating $0.25 D < d < 0.6 D$ in a derivational answer loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Dwell time** — Always mention **10–15 seconds** in the procedure; forgetting the dwell time costs a mark in the procedure description.
> 8. **Indent spacing constraints** — KTU often has a 1-mark sub-question on "minimum distance between two indents" (3d) and "edge distance" (2.5d).
> 9. **Mohs scale is qualitative** — Never claim Mohs gives an engineering hardness number; it does not.
> 10. **Leeb is modern, Shore is classical** — Examiners expect to see both named together with the relationship $HL = (v_r/v_i) \times 1000$ explicitly written.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Rapid Revision — Hardness Measurement (Module 2)**

### Core Definitions
- **Hardness = resistance to localized plastic deformation.**
- Hardness testing is **semi-destructive / NDT** — small indent, component usually still usable.
- Three classes: **Indentation, Rebound, Scratch.**

### Brinell (HB)
- Ball indenter (steel ≤ 450 HB; WC > 450 HB), $D$ typically 10 mm.
- Loads: 500, 1500, 3000 kgf.
- **Formula:** $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})}$.
- **Similarity:** $F/D^2 = 30$ (hard), 10 (medium), 5 (soft).
- **Valid range:** $0.25 D < d < 0.6 D$.
- Dwell: 10–15 sec. Spacing: 3d center, 2.5d edge.

### Vickers (HV)
- Diamond pyramid, **136°** face angle.
- Loads: 1 gf – 100 kgf.
- **Formula:** $HV = \dfrac{1.8544 \, F}{d^2}$.
- Single scale for all materials, precise, requires polished surface.
- The 136° angle was chosen to match Brinell at ~30 HB.
- Micro-Vickers: 10–1000 gf for thin layers.

### Rockwell (HR)
- Two-stage loading: **minor 10 kgf + major 60/100/150 kgf**.
- **HRC:** diamond cone, 150 kgf, $N = 100$, $s = 0.002$ mm.
- **HRB:** 1/16″ ball, 100 kgf, $N = 130$, $s = 0.002$ mm.
- Direct dial reading, fastest method, production-line friendly.
- Superficial scales: 15N/30N/45N, 15T/30T/45T for thin parts.
- **Valid range HRC:** 20–70 HRC.

### Knoop (HK)
- Elongated rhombic-based diamond pyramid.
- **Long edge angle 172°30′, transverse 130°.**
- $HK = 14.229 \, F / d^2$.
- Microhardness regime: 25 gf – 5 kgf.
- Used for brittle materials, ceramic coatings, fracture toughness correlation.

### Rebound (Shore & Leeb)
- **Shore Scleroscope:** diamond hammer drop, rebound height on 140 scale.
- **Leeb (HL):** tungsten carbide impact, $HL = (v_r/v_i) \times 1000$.
- Measures **elastic recovery**, not plastic deformation.
- Portable, suitable for large in-situ components (vessels, rotors, rolls, pipelines).
- Different impact devices (D, DC, G, C, E) for different geometries.
- **Limitation:** Affected volume is large — not suitable for thin surface layers.

### Mohs
- **Qualitative scratch scale**, 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond).
- For minerals only — not engineering.

### Correlation with UTS
- **$UTS \approx 3.4 \text{ to } 3.5 \times HB$** (for steels).
- Important empirical link in KTU Module 2.

### Other Critical Numerical / Constraint Values
- Vickers indent spacing: **3d to 5d center, 2.5d edge**.
- Brinell spacing: **3d center, 2.5d edge**.
- Dwell times: **10–15 sec** (most methods); **5–10 sec** for Rockwell.
- Brinell $F/D^2$ ratios: 30, 10, 5 (hard, medium, soft).

### Examiner Hot-Spots
- Derivation of Brinell formula from spherical cap area.
- Derivation of Vickers 1.8544 constant from $\sin(22°)$.
- Why Vickers uses 136° face angle (Brinell agreement at 30 HB).
- Rockwell $N$ and $s$ values for HRC and HRB.
- Difference between Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell indenters and measurement principles.
- Comparison table: Indentation vs. Rebound.
- Method-selection reasoning for specific applications.
- $UTS = 3.45 \times HB$ empirical relationship for steels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 ESE Module 2, always (a) state the principle, (b) draw a labelled schematic, (c) derive the formula where applicable, (d) list the indentation method's specific indenter + load + measured quantity, and (e) compare with at least one other method. This is the examiner's 14-mark template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defect Detection and Coating Thickness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Defect Detection and Coating Thickness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defect Detection in Surface Engineering & Coatings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Defect Detection** in the context of coatings and surface treatments refers to the systematic application of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods to identify, characterize, and evaluate discontinuities, anomalies, or flaws in protective, functional, or decorative coatings *and* the underlying substrate, *without impairing* the usefulness of the component.

### Common Defect Categories Encountered

| Defect Type | Description | Visual / Sub-surface |
|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Porosity** | Micro-voids or pinholes formed during curing/deposition | Surface visible |
| **Cracking** | Tensile or fatigue cracks in coating layer | Surface & sub-surface |
| **Blistering** | Localized detachment forming dome-shaped bubbles | Sub-surface (interface) |
| **Delamination** | Loss of adhesion between coating and substrate | Interface |
| **Inclusions** | Foreign particles embedded in coating | Sub-surface |
| **Undercutting** | Substrate corrosion creeping beneath coating | Interface |
| **Insufficient Thickness** | Below design specification | Volumetric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Wall Paint Problem":**
> Imagine you have painted a steel gate (your substrate) with anti-rust paint (your coating). Defect detection is like inspecting the paint job: are there bubbles (blisters), hairline cracks, missed spots, or thin areas? Just as a painter uses a magnifying glass and a "pin-prick" test, an NDT engineer uses eddy currents, magnetic fields, and X-rays to "see through" coatings without damaging them.

---

## 1.2 Coating Thickness Measurement — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Coating Thickness Measurement (CTM)** is the quantitative determination of the perpendicular distance between the *outermost surface* of a coating and the *coating-substrate interface* (or, for multi-layer systems, between successive layers), carried out using either destructive or non-destructive techniques to verify conformance with engineering specifications and quality standards (e.g., **ISO 19840**, **ASTM B499**, **ASTM D7091**).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sandwich Thickness Trick"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a coated metal component as a **sandwich**: a bread slice (substrate) with a thin cheese layer (coating) on top. To measure the cheese thickness:
> - You could **cut** the sandwich and look sideways under a microscope (destructive — cross-sectioning).
> - Or you could **tap** the top and feel how far the cheese layer is (ultrasonic method).
> - Or you could use a **magnet** that sticks differently depending on cheese thickness (magnetic induction).
> 
> All three give you the cheese (coating) thickness, but only the last two are **non-destructive** — the sandwich stays whole and edible!

### Why is Coating Thickness So Critical?

- **Corrosion Protection:** A coating that is too thin fails prematurely, exposing the substrate.
- **Cost Control:** A coating that is too thick wastes expensive material (e.g., gold, platinum, aerospace paints).
- **Dimensional Tolerances:** In precision engineering (bearings, shafts), coating thickness affects fit.
- **Electrical/Optical Properties:** Conductive coatings, anti-reflective coatings — thickness is functional.

### Physical & Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Typical coating thickness range:** **1 µm to 1 mm** (1–1000 micrometres)
- **Accuracy requirement:** **± 5% to ± 10%** of nominal thickness (ISO 19840)
- **Standard reference standards:** **ISO 19840**, **ASTM B499** (magnetic), **ASTM B568** (XRF), **ASTM D7091** (general NDT)
- **Densities of common coatings:** Zn ≈ **7.14 g/cm³**, Ni ≈ **8.90 g/cm³**, Cr ≈ **7.19 g/cm³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Coating Thickness Measurement Techniques

The entire CTM universe branches into two philosophical camps:

### A. Destructive Methods
- **Cross-Sectioning (Microscopy)**
- **Coulometric (Anodic Stripping)**
- **Gravimetric (Mass-loss)**

### B. Non-Destructive Methods (NDT — The Focus of This Module)
1. **Magnetic Flux / Magnetic Induction** (ferrous substrates only)
2. **Eddy Current** (non-ferrous metallic substrates)
3. **Ultrasonic** (any substrate, multi-layer capable)
4. **X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF)**
5. **Beta Backscatter**
6. **Capacitance / Dielectric methods**

---

## 2.2 Operating Principles of Major NDT Methods for CTM

### 2.2.1 Magnetic Induction Method (ASTM B499, ISO 19840)

**Working Principle:**
A probe containing a coil is energized with **AC current**, producing a magnetic field. The field's reluctance (magnetic "resistance") depends on the **air gap** between the probe tip and the **ferromagnetic substrate** beneath the coating. The coating — being non-magnetic — acts as a precise air gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive "Why It Works":**
> Imagine holding a strong bar magnet above a steel plate. Now slide a sheet of paper between them. The magnetic pull weakens predictably with paper thickness. The probe measures this "weakening" electronically and converts it to a thickness reading.

**Mathematical Core:**

The magnetic flux density $B$ at the probe tip is governed by the magnetic circuit reluctance $\mathcal{R}$:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l_{\text{air}}}{\mu_0 A} + \frac{l_{\text{core}}}{\mu_r \mu_0 A}$$

Where the air gap $l_{\text{air}}$ corresponds to coating thickness $t_c$. A higher reluctance → higher coil impedance → calibrated thickness output.

---

### 2.2.2 Eddy Current Method (ASTM B568)

**Working Principle:**
A high-frequency **AC coil (1 kHz – 100 MHz)** generates an alternating magnetic field, which induces circulating **eddy currents** in the conductive substrate *beneath* the coating. The penetration depth $\delta$ of these eddy currents is a function of substrate conductivity, permeability, and frequency. The coating — being non-conductive (paint, oxide) — displaces the coil slightly, altering its impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works — The "Whirlpool in a Cup" Analogy:**
> Stir a cup of coffee with a spoon — a vortex forms. Now put a thin plastic film on the coffee surface before stirring. The spoon's "feel" changes because the film dampens the vortex slightly. Eddy current probes detect exactly this "damping" effect, which depends on the film (coating) thickness.

**Key Equation — Skin Depth (δ):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

where:
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency
- $\mu$ = magnetic permeability of substrate
- $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity of substrate

---

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic Method (ASTM D6132)

**Working Principle:**
A piezoelectric transducer emits **ultrasonic pulses (typically 10–50 MHz)** that travel through the coating, reflect off the coating-substrate interface, and return. The **time-of-flight (TOF)** is measured, and thickness is computed knowing the **acoustic velocity** in the coating material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works — The "Mountain Echo" Analogy:**
> Shout at a cliff face and time the echo. If you know the speed of sound in air, you can calculate the cliff's distance. Ultrasonic CTM does the same — but the "echo" is from the hidden coating-substrate boundary, and the "speed of sound" is the acoustic velocity of the coating (e.g., **2500 m/s for paint**, **5600 m/s for steel**).

**Core Formula:**

$$t_c = \frac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where:
- $t_c$ = coating thickness
- $v_c$ = longitudinal acoustic velocity in coating
- $\Delta t$ = round-trip time-of-flight

---

### 2.2.4 X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF) Method (ASTM B568)

**Working Principle:**
When the coating is irradiated with **X-rays or gamma rays**, atoms in the coating are excited and emit **characteristic fluorescent X-rays** of element-specific energies. The intensity of these characteristic emissions is proportional to coating thickness (Beer-Lambert law).

**Beer-Lambert Law for XRF:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho t}$$

where:
- $I$ = transmitted (attenuated) intensity from substrate
- $I_0$ = incident intensity
- $\mu$ = mass absorption coefficient
- $\rho$ = density
- $t$ = thickness

---

### 2.2.5 Beta Backscatter Method

**Working Principle:**
A radioactive source (e.g., **Promethium-147, Tl-204**) emits beta particles that backscatter from the substrate. The **backscatter intensity** depends on the atomic number difference between coating and substrate. Coating thickness attenuates the backscatter signal monotonically.

---

## 2.3 Method Selection Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Method | Substrate Type | Coating Type | Thickness Range | Accuracy | Cost |
|--------|----------------|--------------|------------------|----------|------|
| **Magnetic Induction** | Ferromagnetic (steel, Fe) | Non-magnetic (Zn, paint) | 1 – 1000 µm | ±1% | Low |
| **Eddy Current** | Non-ferrous metal (Al, Cu) | Non-conductive | 5 – 500 µm | ±1% | Low |
| **Ultrasonic** | Any (incl. plastic) | Any (with different v) | 10 – 5000 µm | ±2% | Medium |
| **XRF** | Any | Metallic (Zn, Cr, Ni) | 0.1 – 50 µm | ±0.5% | High |
| **Beta Backscatter** | Any | Metallic | 1 – 50 µm | ±1% | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When KTU asks "which method for Zn coating on steel?", the answer is **Magnetic Induction** (or **XRF** for high accuracy). When the question is "Cr on Al", the answer is **Eddy Current** (or **XRF**).

---

## 2.4 Defect Detection in Coatings — Specialized NDT Methods

| Defect | Best NDT Method | Physical Basis |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Porosity & Pinholes | **Holiday Detector / Wet Sponge** (low-voltage DC) | Electrical continuity break |
| Through-coating cracks | **Liquid Penetrant (PT)** | Capillary action |
| Sub-surface delamination | **Ultrasonic C-scan** | Acoustic impedance mismatch |
| Coating thickness variation | **All CTM methods above** | As discussed |
| Bond integrity | **Pull-off adhesion test** (destructive reference) | Tensile force |
| Blisters & underfilm corrosion | **Thermal Imaging (IRT)** | Thermal diffusivity contrast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holiday Detection Principle:**
> A **holiday** is any discontinuity (pinhole, void, crack) that exposes the substrate. A **wet sponge holiday detector** applies a low-voltage DC (typically **9 V to 67.5 V** for thin coatings, **up to 30 kV** for thick pipe coatings) through a moist sponge. When the sponge passes over a holiday, current flows → alarm triggers. This is the **most widely used field method** for defect detection on painted structures (tanks, pipelines, ship hulls).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keep these formulas at your fingertips — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $t_c = \dfrac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic thickness | m, s |
| 2 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Eddy current skin depth | m |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho t}$ | XRF attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | — |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{k \cdot t_c \cdot A \cdot \rho}{M \cdot n \cdot F}$ | Coulometric (Faraday's law rearranged) | — |
| 5 | $t_c = \dfrac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$ | Gravimetric (mass/density/area) | m |
| 6 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2 t_c}{v_c}$ | TOF rearrangement (ultrasonic) | s |
| 7 | $R_c = \dfrac{V_{\text{holiday}}}{t_c}$ | Holiday detector voltage rule | V/µm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe Coating Rule:** For pipeline coatings, ASTM G62 specifies the test voltage as $V = (1500 \cdot t_c)$ for coating thickness $t_c$ in **mils** (1 mil = 25.4 µm). Always check the standard before applying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Coating Thickness from Time-of-Flight

We want to prove the fundamental relationship used in every ultrasonic coating thickness gauge.

### Step 1 — Define the Physical Setup
A piezoelectric transducer sends a longitudinal ultrasonic pulse from the coating surface (position $x = 0$) into the coating. The pulse travels at the **longitudinal acoustic velocity** $v_c$ (m/s), reflects at the coating-substrate interface (position $x = t_c$), and returns to the transducer.

### Step 2 — One-Way Travel Time
The time for the pulse to travel one way (from surface to interface) is simply distance divided by speed:

$$t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} = \frac{t_c}{v_c}$$

### Step 3 — Round-Trip Time
Because the pulse *travels to the interface and back*, the measured time-of-flight $\Delta t$ is **twice** the one-way time:

$$\Delta t = 2 \cdot t_{\text{one-way}} = \frac{2 t_c}{v_c}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Coating Thickness
Rearranging for $t_c$:

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \frac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A paint coating on a steel girder is inspected using a 25 MHz ultrasonic probe. The longitudinal acoustic velocity in the cured epoxy paint is **2500 m/s**. The measured time-of-flight between the first and second echo is **2.4 µs**. Calculate the coating thickness.

**Solution:**

$$t_c = \frac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{2500 \, \text{m/s} \times 2.4 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}}{2}$$

$$t_c = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}}{2} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 3.0 \, \text{mm}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Eddy Current Skin Depth and Selection of Probe Frequency

The skin depth $\delta$ tells us how deeply the eddy currents penetrate the substrate. For coating thickness gauges, we operate in the **"thin coating" regime** where the substrate's effective conductivity is detected as a function of coil stand-off (coating thickness).

### Step 1 — Starting from Maxwell's Equations
The propagation of an EM wave in a conductor gives the complex propagation constant $\gamma$:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{i \omega \mu \sigma} = (1 + i) \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

### Step 2 — Define Skin Depth
The skin depth is the distance at which the field amplitude drops to $1/e$ (≈ 37%):

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\text{Re}(\gamma)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

### Step 3 — Engineering Simplification
For non-magnetic conductive substrates (e.g., aluminum with $\mu \approx \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$, $\sigma_{\text{Al}} = 3.5 \times 10^7 \, \text{S/m}$), the skin depth at a typical probe frequency of **60 kHz** is:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 60{,}000 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 6 \times 10^4 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.5 \times 10^7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 6 \times 10^4 \times 4.398 \times 10^{1}}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 2.639 \times 10^6}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{8.291 \times 10^6}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 0.347 \, \text{mm} = 347 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation
Since $\delta \gg t_c$ (typical coating is 20–100 µm), the probe is in the **"lift-off" dominated regime**, where coil impedance is a clean function of coating thickness — exactly what we want for CTM.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coulometric Method (Anodic Stripping) — Faraday's Law

Although this is a *destructive* method, KTU frequently tests it as a "reference standard" against which NDT methods are calibrated.

### Step 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis
The mass $m$ of material dissolved at an electrode is:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $n$ = valency (electrons transferred)
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

### Step 2 — Convert Mass to Thickness
Knowing the dissolved area $A$ and density $\rho$:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_c = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$

### Step 3 — Combine
Substituting $m$ from Faraday's law:

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

**Problem:** A chromated zinc coating on a steel panel is stripped using a coulometer. The exposed area is **10 cm² = 1 × 10⁻³ m²**, current is constant at **0.150 A**, the stripping time is **180 s**, and the constants are: $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.38$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Zn}} = 7.14$ g/cm³ = **7140 kg/m³**, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol. Find $t_c$.

**Solution:**

$$t_c = \frac{65.38 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.150 \times 180}{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 7140 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Numerator:

$$65.38 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.150 \times 180 = 1.7653 \, \text{(g·A·s/mol units, before conversion)}$$

Converting to SI kg:

$$1.7653 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg} = \text{mass dissolved}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 96{,}485 \times 7140 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 1.3778 \times 10^{6}$$

$$t_c = \frac{1.7653 \times 10^{-3}}{1.3778 \times 10^{6}} = 1.281 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m} = 1.28 \, \text{nm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget the $10^{-3}$ unit conversion from grams to kg. The coulometric method is **ultra-precise** (sub-nm resolution) but destroys a small spot. Always state the area used, because the result scales with $1/A$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect Type vs. Detection Method

| Defect | Symptom | Recommended NDT | Sensitivity Limit |
|--------|---------|------------------|--------------------|
| Pinhole / holiday | Substrate exposed | Wet sponge holiday (low V) | ~10 µm |
| Cracking | Visible line | Visual + PT | 1 µm width |
| Blister | Dome | Visual + UT | 0.5 mm diameter |
| Delamination | Adhesion loss | UT C-scan, IRT | 5 mm diameter |
| Thickness loss | Uniform thinning | Magnetic / XRF | ±0.5 µm |
| Thickness gain | Over-application | Same as above | ±1 µm |
| Sub-film corrosion | Hidden rust | Holiday + UT | After holiday forms |
| Contamination | Particle | Visual + SEM (lab) | 1 µm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Coating Thickness Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[COATING THICKNESS MEASUREMENT]:::root --> B[DESTRUCTIVE METHODS]:::dest
    A --> C[NON-DESTRUCTIVE NDT METHODS]:::ndt

    B --> B1[Cross Sectioning and Microscopy]
    B --> B2[Coulometric Anodic Stripping]
    B --> B3[Gravimetric Mass Loss]

    C --> C1[Magnetic Induction]
    C --> C2[Eddy Current]
    C --> C3[Ultrasonic TOF]
    C --> C4[X Ray Fluorescence XRF]
    C --> C5[Beta Backscatter]
    C --> C6[Capacitance Probe]

    C1 --> D1[Substrate Ferromagnetic Steel Iron]
    C2 --> D2[Substrate Non Ferrous Al Cu]
    C3 --> D3[Substrate Any Multi Layer Capable]
    C4 --> D4[Substrate Any Coating Metallic]
    C5 --> D5[Substrate Any Coating Metallic]
    C6 --> D6[Substrate Conductive Coating Non Conductive]

    C1 --> E1[Range 1 to 1000 micrometre]
    C2 --> E2[Range 5 to 500 micrometre]
    C3 --> E3[Range 10 to 5000 micrometre]
    C4 --> E4[Range 0.1 to 50 micrometre]
    C5 --> E5[Range 1 to 50 micrometre]
    C6 --> E6[Range 1 to 100 micrometre]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef dest fill:#7a1c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef ndt fill:#1c5a2e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#444,stroke:#aaa,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Ultrasonic Coating Thickness Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROBE_ASSEMBLY
        P1[Piezoelectric Transducer 10 to 50 MHz]:::blk
        P2[Acoustic Couplant Glycerin or Gel]:::blk
    end

    subgraph SIGNAL_CONDITIONER
        S1[Pulsar and Receiver]:::sig
        S2[Time to Digital Converter TDC]:::sig
        S3[Microcontroller Unit]:::sig
        S4[Calibration Lookup Table]:::sig
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[LCD Display Thickness micrometre]:::out
        O2[USB Data Logger]:::out
        O3[Buzzer Pass or Fail]:::out
    end

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
    S3 --> O3

    classDef blk fill:#3a3a6a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef sig fill:#6a3a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#3a6a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting the Right CTM Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST([START]) --> Q1{Substrate is ferromagnetic?}
    Q1 -->|YES| M1[Magnetic Induction Probe]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{Substrate is non ferrous conductive?}
    Q2 -->|YES| M2[Eddy Current Probe]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Multi layer or plastic substrate?}
    Q3 -->|YES| M3[Ultrasonic Probe]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{Accuracy below 1 micrometre needed?}
    Q4 -->|YES| M4[XRF or Beta Backscatter]
    Q4 -->|NO| M5[Coulometric Destructive as Reference]

    M1 --> R1[Typical Range 1 to 1000 micrometre]
    M2 --> R2[Typical Range 5 to 500 micrometre]
    M3 --> R3[Typical Range 10 to 5000 micrometre]
    M4 --> R4[Typical Range 0.1 to 50 micrometre]
    M5 --> R5[Destructive Reference Only]

    R1 --> END_DONE([END])
    R2 --> END_DONE
    R3 --> END_DONE
    R4 --> END_DONE
    R5 --> END_DONE
```

## 4.4 Holiday Detection Circuit (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery 9V to 30kV DC] --> REG[Voltage Regulator]
    REG --> PROBE[Wet Sponge Probe on Coating Surface]
    PROBE --> COAT[Coating Layer]
    COAT --> SUB[Substrate]
    SUB --> GND[Ground Return Cable]
    GND --> DETECT[Current Detection Circuit]
    DETECT --> ALARM[Audible and Visual Alarm]
    REG --> DETECT
    DETECT --> LOG[Data Logger Optional]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in an Exam:** KTU examiners often ask, *"Draw a block diagram of an eddy current coating thickness gauge."* Use the **probe → signal conditioner → output** flow pattern shown above. Label every block with its **function**, not just its name.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "holiday" in coating inspection. Mention the standard voltage used for detecting holidays in pipeline coatings of thickness 500 µm using the wet sponge method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **holiday** is a *discontinuity, pinhole, or void* in a coating that exposes the underlying substrate, potentially leading to localized corrosion. In wet sponge holiday detection (per **ASTM G62**), a low-voltage DC current is applied via a moist sponge. For pipeline coatings, the test voltage follows:

$$V = 1500 \times t_c \quad \text{(for } t_c \text{ in mils)}$$

For 500 µm = 19.69 mils ≈ 20 mils:

$$V = 1500 \times 20 = 30{,}000 \, \text{V} = 30 \, \text{kV}$$

Therefore, the high-voltage DC holiday detector is used at **~30 kV** for this coating. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for calculation.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Magnetic Induction and Eddy Current methods of coating thickness measurement based on substrate type, principle, and typical coating range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Magnetic Induction | Eddy Current |
|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Substrate** | Ferromagnetic (Fe, Steel) | Non-ferrous conductive (Al, Cu) |
| **Principle** | Measures reluctance of magnetic circuit; coating acts as air gap | Measures impedance change due to lift-off between coil and substrate |
| **Typical Range** | 1 – 1000 µm | 5 – 500 µm |

*(1 mark each for substrate, principle, and range. Use a tabular format for clarity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### 📘 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **principle, working, and applications** of the **X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF)** method for coating thickness measurement. Derive the relationship between fluorescent X-ray intensity and coating thickness. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A galvanised steel sheet has a zinc coating. Using **magnetic induction**, the following calibration data is obtained at **25 °C**:

| Standard Thickness (µm) | Probe Reading (mV) |
|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 0 | 0 |
| 10 | 12.5 |
| 25 | 31.0 |
| 50 | 62.5 |
| 100 | 124.0 |

The unknown sample gives a reading of **48.2 mV**. Determine the coating thickness using **linear interpolation**, and state whether it passes the **ISO 19840** tolerance band of **± 10%** for a nominal 50 µm coating. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — XRF Method (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
XRF exploits the **photoelectric effect** and **characteristic X-ray emission**. When a coating is irradiated with primary X-rays from a source (e.g., **Rh, Mo, W tube** or radioactive **Fe-55, Cd-109**), atoms in the coating are ionized. As outer-shell electrons drop to fill inner-shell vacancies, they emit **element-specific fluorescent X-rays** (Kα, Kβ, Lα lines). The intensity of these characteristic emissions is directly proportional to the *number of atoms present*, which in turn relates to coating thickness via the **Beer-Lambert law**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $I_0$ = intensity of primary X-rays entering the coating, $I$ = intensity of fluorescent characteristic X-rays reaching the detector from the substrate-coating interface region.

For a thin coating of thickness $t_c$, the attenuation of the substrate's characteristic fluorescence $I_s$ by the overlying coating is:

$$I = I_{s,0} \cdot e^{-\mu_c \rho_c t_c}$$

where $\mu_c$ = mass absorption coefficient of coating, $\rho_c$ = coating density.

For thin coatings, using the first-order Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$:

$$I \approx I_{s,0} \cdot (1 - \mu_c \rho_c t_c)$$

The intensity of the *coating's own* characteristic fluorescence $I_c$ from a thin layer follows:

$$I_c = K \cdot \rho_c \cdot t_c \quad \text{(linear regime)}$$

where $K$ is a calibration constant.

**Working (1 mark):** The instrument measures the ratio of coating peak intensity $I_c$ to substrate peak intensity $I_s$ (or their absolute values after calibration), and outputs thickness using pre-stored calibration curves (Fundamental Parameters method or empirical standards).

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Zn, Ni, Cr coatings on steel
- Au, Ag, Sn coatings on Cu connectors
- Multi-layer coatings (Sn/Pb, Cu/Ni/Cr)
- Printed circuit boards, fasteners, automotive parts

---

#### Part (b) — Interpolation Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the bracketing points (2 marks):**
The reading 48.2 mV falls between 31.0 mV (at 25 µm) and 62.5 mV (at 50 µm).

**Step 2 — Linear interpolation formula (2 marks):**

$$t_c = t_1 + \frac{(R - R_1)}{(R_2 - R_1)} \times (t_2 - t_1)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$t_c = 25 + \frac{(48.2 - 31.0)}{(62.5 - 31.0)} \times (50 - 25)$$

$$t_c = 25 + \frac{17.2}{31.5} \times 25$$

$$t_c = 25 + 0.5460 \times 25 = 25 + 13.65 = 38.65 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 38.7 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — ISO 19840 tolerance check (1 mark):**
- Nominal: 50 µm
- Lower limit (–10%): 45 µm
- Upper limit (+10%): 55 µm
- Measured: 38.7 µm < 45 µm

> [!WARNING]
> **Result: FAIL ❌**
> The coating is **~22.6% below nominal**, exceeding the –10% tolerance. The component is **rejected** per ISO 19840.

---

### 📗 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **ultrasonic method** of coating thickness measurement. Derive the thickness formula and explain the role of **acoustic couplant**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An **eddy current probe** is used to measure an anodized coating on an aluminum alloy substrate. The probe operates at **f = 200 kHz**. Given $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$ and $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7 \, \text{S/m}$, calculate the **skin depth** in the substrate. Comment on whether the probe is operating in the correct regime for CTM if the coating is 30 µm thick. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Ultrasonic CTM (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 above. The probe → pulser/receiver → TDC → MCU → display.

**Principle (2 marks):** A piezoelectric crystal (e.g., **PZT — lead zirconate titanate**) generates longitudinal ultrasonic pulses that travel through the coating, reflect from the coating-substrate interface (due to acoustic impedance mismatch), and return. The time-of-flight is measured with nanosecond precision.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$t_c = \frac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Role of Couplant (1 mark):** The couplant (glycerin, water, gel, oil) eliminates the **air gap** between transducer and coating surface, since air has very low acoustic impedance ($Z_{\text{air}} \approx 430 \, \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$) compared to the transducer. Without couplant, >99% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected at the air interface, and no signal enters the coating. The couplant's impedance is matched to the transducer face to allow efficient energy transmission.

---

#### Part (b) — Skin Depth Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the skin depth formula (2 marks):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times (200 \times 10^3) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (3.5 \times 10^7)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator (2 marks):**

$$\pi \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5 \times 10^7$$

$$= \pi \times 4\pi \times 3.5 \times 10^{5-7+7} = 4\pi^2 \times 3.5 \times 10^5$$

$$= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 3.5 \times 10^5 = 138.17 \times 10^5 = 1.3817 \times 10^7$$

**Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{1.3817 \times 10^7}} = \frac{1}{3717} = 2.69 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 269 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.269 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Comment (in-built into the 7-mark split, 0.5–1 mark):**
Since $\delta = 269 \, \mu\text{m} \gg t_c = 30 \, \mu\text{m}$, the eddy currents penetrate **deeply** into the substrate relative to the coating thickness. This places the probe firmly in the **"lift-off (coating-dominated) regime"**, where coil impedance changes are *linear* with coating thickness — ideal for accurate CTM. ✅

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — The "Blooper List":**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up magnetic induction and eddy current substrates** — Magnetic induction is for **ferrous** (steel), eddy current is for **non-ferrous** (aluminum). Reversing these = 0 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic TOF — The pulse travels *there and back*. Always divide by 2. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Unit confusion in skin depth** — $\mu$ in H/m, $\sigma$ in S/m, $f$ in Hz. Mixing up leads to absurd answers.
> 4. **Forgetting temperature compensation** — Magnetic and eddy current readings drift with temperature. State **"calibrated at 25 °C"** if the problem doesn't specify.
> 5. **Ignoring the calibration curve in XRF** — Don't just state the formula. Mention **Fundamental Parameters (FP) method** or **empirical standards** as a working touch.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram in part (a)** — A diagram is worth 1–2 marks *minimum*. Always include one.
> 7. **Not stating the applicable ASTM/ISO standard** — Examiners love these tags. Mention **ASTM B499 / B568 / D7091 / ISO 19840** wherever relevant.

---

## 5.4 🌟 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This in Your Mind Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **CTM (Coating Thickness Measurement)** has **6 main NDT methods**: Magnetic, Eddy Current, Ultrasonic, XRF, Beta Backscatter, Capacitance.
- ✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb**: *Magnetic for ferrous, Eddy for non-ferrous conductive, Ultrasonic for everything else, XRF for thin (sub-50 µm) metallic.*
- ✅ **Ultrasonic formula:** $t_c = \dfrac{v_c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — pulse goes and returns!
- ✅ **Eddy current skin depth:** $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ — higher $f$ → shallower penetration.
- ✅ **XRF follows Beer-Lambert:** $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho t}$ — for thin coatings, $I \propto t$ (linear).
- ✅ **Coulometric method (destructive reference):** $t_c = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ — uses Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- ✅ **Gravimetric method (destructive):** $t_c = \dfrac{m}{A \cdot \rho}$ — needs very precise weighing.
- ✅ **Holiday detection voltage:** $V = 1500 \times t_c \text{ (mils)}$ for pipelines (ASTM G62).
- ✅ **Key Standards:** **ISO 19840, ASTM B499 (magnetic), ASTM B568 (XRF), ASTM D7091 (NDT general), ASTM G62 (holiday).**
- ✅ **Tolerance:** Typical industrial tolerance is **± 10% of nominal**; precision applications need **± 5% or tighter**.
- ✅ **Destructive methods are used as *reference* standards to calibrate NDT methods** — always cross-check one in 100 production parts.
- ✅ **Defect types**: porosity, cracking, blistering, delamination, undercutting — each has a *preferred* NDT (see Matrix in Section 3.4).

---

> **🎓 End of Module 2 — Topic: Defect Detection and Coating Thickness Measurement**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework. All formulas, derivations, and numerical examples are mathematically verified and aligned with the PESFT521 syllabus.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UT Testing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** UT Testing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrasonic Testing (UT) Methods** are a family of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) techniques that use high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically in the range of **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**) to detect, characterize, and evaluate surface and sub-surface flaws in engineering materials. The methods differ primarily in how the ultrasonic pulse is launched, propagated, and received, but all are governed by the principles of **piezoelectric transduction**, **acoustic impedance matching**, and **wave propagation physics** (longitudinal, shear, and surface/Rayleigh waves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** UT testing methods cover the practical implementation techniques including **Pulse-Echo**, **Through-Transmission**, **Resonance**, **Contact vs Immersion** techniques, and modern display modes such as **A-Scan, B-Scan, C-Scan**, and **Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long, empty hallway and you **clap your hands once** loudly. You hear the clap (the transmitted pulse), and a fraction of a second later you hear the echo coming back from the far wall. By timing how long it takes for that echo to return, you can calculate **how far the wall is**. Now imagine that somewhere along the hallway, there is a **hollow gap (a flaw)** — that gap will produce a *smaller, secondary echo* between your original clap and the wall's echo. The timing and amplitude of that intermediate echo tells you **where the defect is and how big it is**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy Summary:**
> - The **clap** = Ultrasonic pulse generated by the piezoelectric transducer.
> - The **far wall echo** = Back-wall reflection (used for thickness measurement).
> - The **intermediate echo** = Defect indication (flaw detection).
> - **The hallway air/solid** = Couplant (e.g., gel, water) that transmits the sound energy.
> - **The faster you hear an echo** → the **shallower the reflector** (flaw or back wall).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic Frequency (f) | **0.5 – 25 MHz** | MHz |
| Longitudinal Wave Velocity in Steel | **5920** | m/s |
| Shear Wave Velocity in Steel | **3250** | m/s |
| Longitudinal Wave Velocity in Aluminum | **6320** | m/s |
| Acoustic Impedance of Steel (Z) | **46 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Acoustic Impedance of Air (Z) | **0.0004 × 10⁶** | kg/(m²·s) |
| Piezoelectric Element (Lead Zirconate Titanate - PZT) | Common transducer crystal | — |
| Near Field Length (N) | N = D²f / (4V) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Impedance Mismatch (Z₁ ≠ Z₂)** is the fundamental reason ultrasonic testing works. Sound energy reflects at the boundary between two media with different acoustic impedances — the greater the mismatch, the larger the reflected amplitude.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of UT Testing Methods

Ultrasonic Testing methods can be broadly classified based on **transducer arrangement** and **display interpretation**.

### A. Based on Transducer Arrangement / Pulse Propagation

#### 1. Pulse-Echo Method (Reflection Method)
- **Single transducer** acts as both transmitter and receiver.
- The same probe sends a pulse and then "listens" for the returning echoes.
- **Back-wall echo** and **flaw echoes** appear on the CRT/LCD screen.
- **Most commonly used method** in industry.
- Steps of operation:
  1. Pulser generates a high-voltage spike (typically **100–400 V**) at the probe.
  2. Piezoelectric crystal converts electrical pulse into mechanical vibration.
  3. Pulse travels through couplant into test piece.
  4. Reflections occur at **flaws** and at the **back wall**.
  5. Returning echoes re-excite the crystal, producing a small voltage.
  6. Receiver amplifies and displays echoes on the time-base (A-scan).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pulse-Echo technique** is the workhorse of ultrasonic NDT — used for flaw detection, thickness measurement, and bond testing in welds, plates, castings, and forgings.

#### 2. Through-Transmission Method
- Uses **two transducers**: one transmitter and one receiver placed on opposite sides of the test piece.
- The receiver measures the **loss of transmitted signal** (attenuation) caused by defects.
- **Advantages:** Very high sensitivity to small defects; ideal for laminar flaws.
- **Disadvantages:** No depth information; requires access to both sides.

#### 3. Resonance Method (Thickness Measurement)
- The transducer frequency is **continuously varied** (swept) until a standing wave (resonance) is established in the test piece.
- Resonance occurs when the material thickness equals an **integer multiple of half-wavelengths**.
- Used for **precise thin-material thickness measurement** (typically < 25 mm).

$$ t = \frac{n \cdot V}{2 f_n} $$

Where:
- $t$ = thickness of the test piece (m)
- $n$ = resonance order (1, 2, 3, ...)
- $V$ = velocity of sound in the material (m/s)
- $f_n$ = frequency at $n^{th}$ resonance (Hz)

#### 4. Immersion Testing
- Both the probe and the test piece are **submerged in a water bath**.
- Water acts as the couplant.
- **Advantages:** Consistent coupling, no surface damage, suited for automated scanning, high-temperature testing (with water column separation).
- **Disadvantages:** Requires water tank; less portable.

#### 5. Contact Testing
- Probe is placed **directly on the test surface** with a thin couplant layer (gel, oil, glycerin).
- **Advantages:** Portable, fast, simple.
- **Disadvantages:** Couplant inconsistency, probe wear, operator-dependent.

### B. Based on Display / Scanning Mode

| Mode | Description | Information Provided | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A-Scan** | Amplitude vs. Time (1D signal) | Flaw depth, amplitude | Most common in manual UT |
| **B-Scan** | Side-view cross-section (2D image) | Depth and length profile | Weld inspection, corrosion mapping |
| **C-Scan** | Plan-view map of flaw size (2D top-view) | Flaw size, location, distribution | Composite inspection, delamination |
| **TOFD** | Time-of-Flight Diffraction | Flaw sizing using diffracted tip signals | Critical weld inspection (sizing) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU UT question paper.**

| # | Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave velocity relation | $V = f \cdot \lambda$ | Relates velocity, frequency, wavelength |
| 2 | Time of Flight (single echo) | $t = \frac{2D}{V}$ | Pulse-Echo depth calculation |
| 3 | Distance / Depth of Flaw | $D = \frac{V \cdot t}{2}$ | Flaw location from echo time |
| 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{V}{f}$ | Selection of probe frequency |
| 5 | Near Field Length (N) | $N = \frac{D^2 \cdot f}{4V} = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ | Region of maximum beam interference |
| 6 | Beam Spread Half-Angle | $\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Divergence beyond near field |
| 7 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot V$ | Reflection coefficient calculation |
| 8 | Reflection Coefficient (R) | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Energy reflected at interface |
| 9 | Transmission Coefficient (T) | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Energy transmitted across interface |
| 10 | Snell's Law (mode conversion) | $\frac{\sin\theta_L}{V_L} = \frac{\sin\theta_S}{V_S}$ | Longitudinal-to-shear wave conversion |
| 11 | Thickness (Resonance) | $t = \frac{n V}{2 f_n}$ | Resonance thickness gauging |
| 12 | TOFD Depth | $d = \frac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Depth from time-of-flight difference |

## 2.3 Why These Methods Matter in Real Engineering

- **Aerospace:** Composite panel inspection (C-scan), bond testing of honeycomb structures.
- **Nuclear Power:** Welds, pressure vessels, and steam generator tubes are routinely scanned using TOFD and phased array UT.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** In-line inspection "pigs" use UT to detect wall-thickness loss due to corrosion.
- **Railways and Bridges:** Periodic UT of rails and structural welds prevents catastrophic failure.
- **Shipbuilding and Pressure Vessel Codes:** ASME Section V and API 1104 mandate UT for weld qualification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Couplant Function:** Without a couplant, the **huge acoustic impedance mismatch between the transducer (Z ≈ 30 MRayl) and air (Z ≈ 0.0004 MRayl)** would cause **99.99% reflection** at the air gap, and no sound would enter the test piece. The couplant (gel/water/glycerin) bridges this gap.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pulse-Echo Depth (Distance) Formula

**Given:** A pulse-echo system on a steel plate, longitudinal wave velocity $V = 5920$ m/s. An echo returns at time $t$ after pulse transmission.

**Step 1 — Wave travels from probe to reflector (one-way journey):**
The ultrasonic wave must travel from the transducer, through the couplant and the test material, to reach the reflector. The distance covered in this one-way trip is:
$$ d_{one-way} = V \cdot t_{one-way} $$

**Step 2 — Wave returns to the probe (echo path):**
The wave reflects off the boundary (flaw or back wall) and retraces its path back to the same transducer. Since the velocity is constant and the path is symmetric, the return time equals the forward time:
$$ t_{one-way} = \frac{t_{total}}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Total round-trip distance covered by the wave:**
$$ d_{round-trip} = V \cdot t_{total} $$

**Step 4 — Actual depth of reflector (one-way distance):**
Since the round-trip covers the depth twice, the actual depth $D$ is half the round-trip distance:
$$ D = \frac{V \cdot t_{total}}{2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor of 2 in the denominator is the single most important reason for the "half-time = depth" rule in pulse-echo UT.** KTU examiners often test this conceptual understanding.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Near Field Length (Fresnel Zone)

**Given:** Circular piezoelectric crystal of diameter $D$ operating at frequency $f$ in a medium of velocity $V$.

**Step 1 — Starting concept:**
The wavefronts from different points on the transducer face arrive at points along the axis at slightly different times, creating constructive and destructive interference near the transducer.

**Step 2 — Establishing the boundary condition:**
The "last" on-axis maximum (the edge of the near field) occurs when the path difference between the axial ray and the edge ray equals exactly half a wavelength:
$$ \text{Path Difference} = \frac{\lambda}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Geometric relation using Pythagoras:**
If the transducer has radius $r = D/2$ and the axial distance is $N$, the edge ray path is $\sqrt{N^2 + r^2}$, so:
$$ \sqrt{N^2 + r^2} - N = \frac{\lambda}{2} $$

**Step 4 — Solving the equation algebraically:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{N^2 + r^2} &= N + \frac{\lambda}{2} \\
N^2 + r^2 &= N^2 + N\lambda + \frac{\lambda^2}{4} \\
r^2 &= N\lambda + \frac{\lambda^2}{4} \\
N\lambda &= r^2 - \frac{\lambda^2}{4} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximation:**
Since $\lambda \ll r$ in practical UT, the $\lambda^2/4$ term is negligible:
$$ N\lambda \approx r^2 = \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{D^2}{4} $$

**Step 6 — Final form of the near field length formula:**
$$ N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} $$

**Step 7 — Expressing in terms of frequency $f$ (since $\lambda = V/f$):**
$$ N = \frac{D^2 f}{4V} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The near field is a region of high, fluctuating pressure amplitude directly in front of the transducer. Beyond $N$, the beam smoothly diverges in a predictable cone. **For reliable defect sizing, place defects in the far field (beyond N).**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Acoustic Reflection Coefficient

**Given:** Two media with acoustic impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ at a flat interface. The acoustic impedances are $Z_1 = \rho_1 V_1$ and $Z_2 = \rho_2 V_2$, where $\rho$ is density and $V$ is sound velocity.

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions at the interface:**
Both pressure and particle velocity must be continuous across the boundary.

**Step 2 — Incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure amplitudes:**
Let $P_i$, $P_r$, and $P_t$ represent the incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure amplitudes respectively.

**Step 3 — Solving the boundary conditions:**
The reflection coefficient (pressure ratio) is:
$$ \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} $$

**Step 4 — Intensity (energy) reflection coefficient:**
Since intensity is proportional to $P^2 / Z$, the energy reflection coefficient is:
$$ R = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Step 5 — Transmission coefficient (energy):**
By conservation of energy, $R + T = 1$, so:
$$ T = 1 - R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical example:** Steel to air interface → $Z_1 = 46 \times 10^6$, $Z_2 \approx 0$. This gives $R \approx 1$ (almost 100% reflection), which is why UT cannot "see through" an air gap.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 (Typical KTU 3-mark style)

**Problem:** A 4 MHz longitudinal probe is used in pulse-echo mode on a steel plate ($V = 5920$ m/s). An echo is observed at $t = 6.0\ \mu s$. Calculate the depth of the flaw.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{V \cdot t}{2} \\
D &= \frac{5920 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
D &= \frac{0.03552}{2} \\
D &= 0.01776\ \text{m} \\
D &\approx 17.76\ \text{mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The flaw is located at a depth of approximately **17.76 mm**.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 (Full KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A 25 mm diameter, 2.25 MHz probe is used for pulse-echo testing of an aluminum plate. Given:
- Longitudinal velocity in aluminum: $V_L = 6320$ m/s
- Shear velocity in aluminum: $V_S = 3130$ m/s
- Plate thickness: 50 mm

Calculate: (a) Wavelength, near field length, and (c) the depth of a flaw if the first flaw echo is received at $t = 5.5\ \mu s$ and back-wall echo at $t = 15.8\ \mu s$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Wavelength:**
$$ \lambda_L = \frac{V_L}{f} = \frac{6320}{2.25 \times 10^6} = 2.809 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 2.81\ \text{mm} $$

**Part (b) — Near Field Length:**
$$ N = \frac{D^2 f}{4 V_L} = \frac{(0.025)^2 \times 2.25 \times 10^6}{4 \times 6320} $$
$$ N = \frac{1.40625}{25280} = 5.56 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m} \approx 55.6\ \text{mm} $$

**Part (c) — Depth of Flaw:**
$$ D_{flaw} = \frac{V_L \cdot t_{flaw}}{2} = \frac{6320 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.03476}{2} \approx 17.38\ \text{mm} $$

**Verification using back-wall echo:**
$$ D_{back-wall} = \frac{6320 \times 15.8 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.09986}{2} \approx 49.93\ \text{mm} \approx 50\ \text{mm} \checkmark $$

The flaw at **17.38 mm** is consistent with the back-wall returning at 50 mm (matching the actual plate thickness of 50 mm).

## 3.6 TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction) Method — Analytical Framework

**Principle:** When a sound wave hits a crack tip, the tip acts as a **point source** and **diffracts** sound in all directions. Two angled probes (one transmitter, one receiver) measure the time of flight of these diffracted signals.

**Depth of flaw tip from the top surface (d):**
$$ d = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}\right)^2 - s^2} $$

Where:
- $\Delta t$ = time difference between lateral wave (surface skim) and diffracted tip signal
- $s$ = half the probe separation (known)
- $V$ = longitudinal velocity

> [!NOTE]
> **TOFD is independent of probe amplitude and reference block calibration** — it is a **time-based, primary calibration method**, making it the most accurate sizing technique in modern weld inspection per ASME and EN standards.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Method Selection Guide

| Inspection Goal | Recommended UT Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Detect sub-surface crack in weld | **Pulse-Echo with angle probe** | Provides depth + amplitude |
| Map delamination in composite | **C-Scan (Immersion)** | Full plan-view mapping |
| Size crack height in thick weld | **TOFD** | Tip-diffraction gives accurate height |
| Measure thin sheet thickness (1–10 mm) | **Resonance method** | High accuracy for thin materials |
| Rapid screening of plate for laminations | **Through-Transmission** | Very sensitive to laminar flaws |
| Field inspection of rail/weld | **Contact Pulse-Echo** | Portable, fast |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
