# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, aligned to **PESFT521 — Non-Destructive Testing Methods**, Module 3, and tagged with KTU Past Year Question references.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Pulse-Echo method of UT. Mention its advantages.**
**`[KTU University Exam — PESFT521, May 2024, Q.1(a), Module 3]`**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pulse-Echo method** is an ultrasonic testing technique in which a **single transducer** acts as both the **transmitter** and the **receiver** of ultrasonic pulses. A short-duration high-voltage electrical pulse excites the piezoelectric crystal, which converts it into a mechanical ultrasonic wave. This wave travels into the test material through a couplant. When it encounters a discontinuity (flaw) or the back wall, a portion of the energy is reflected back to the same transducer. The transducer reconverts this returning mechanical energy into an electrical signal, which is then amplified and displayed on a CRT or LCD as a vertical spike (echo) on a horizontal time base.

**Advantages:**
1. **Single-sided access** is sufficient — only one surface of the component needs to be accessible.
2. Provides both **flaw depth and amplitude information** in a single test.
3. Highly **portable and economical** for field inspections.
4. Suitable for **thickness measurement** and **flaw sizing** simultaneously.

---

### **Question 2: What is the Near Field (Fresnel Zone) in ultrasonic testing? Why is it important?**
**`[KTU University Exam — PESFT521, Dec 2023, Q.1(b), Module 3]`**

**Model Answer:**

The **Near Field**, also called the **Fresnel Zone** or **Fresnel Diffraction Zone**, is the region immediately in front of an ultrasonic transducer, extending from the transducer face to a distance $N$ along the central axis, where the pressure amplitude fluctuates strongly due to interference between wavelets originating from different points on the transducer face.

It is given by:
$$ N = \frac{D^2 f}{4 V} $$

Where $D$ is the transducer diameter, $f$ is the frequency, and $V$ is the wave velocity in the medium.

**Importance:**
1. **Beam is non-uniform inside the near field** — defect sizing is unreliable in this region.
2. **Beyond the near field (in the far field),** the beam propagates as a smooth, diverging cone — this is the **preferred region for accurate defect sizing and amplitude comparison**.
3. The **last on-axis maximum** of the near field is sometimes used as a reference point (the focal point) for focused transducers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Ultrasonic Testing Methods — Pulse-Echo, Through-Transmission, and Display Modes**

**`[KTU University Exam — PESFT521, May 2024, Q.6, Module 3, Choice-based]`**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain with neat block diagrams the working principle of (i) Pulse-Echo and (ii) Through-Transmission UT methods. Compare their suitability for detecting laminar and volumetric flaws.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pulse-Echo Method:**
In the pulse-echo method, a single piezoelectric transducer functions as both transmitter and receiver. A high-voltage pulser (typically 100–400 V) excites the PZT crystal for a very short duration (nanoseconds to microseconds), generating a longitudinal ultrasonic pulse. This pulse travels through a couplant into the test piece. When the pulse encounters an interface (flaw or back wall) with a different acoustic impedance, a portion of the energy is reflected back as an echo. The returning echo re-excites the same crystal, producing a small voltage that is amplified and displayed as a vertical deflection on a time-based horizontal axis (A-scan).

The time between the initial pulse and the echo gives the depth of the reflector:
$$ D = \frac{V \cdot t}{2} $$

The amplitude of the echo indicates the size of the flaw (larger flaw → larger echo).

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** Pulser → T/R Switch → Transducer → Couplant → Test Piece → Return Echo → Receiver → Display.

**(ii) Through-Transmission Method:**
In this method, **two separate transducers** are used — one transmitter and one receiver — placed on **opposite sides** of the test piece. The transmitter continuously (or in pulses) sends ultrasonic energy through the material, and the receiver on the opposite side measures the received amplitude. When a flaw lies in the path of the beam, it attenuates or blocks part of the energy, producing a **drop in received amplitude** on the display.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Pulse-Echo | Through-Transmission |
|---|---|---|
| Number of probes | 1 (single) | 2 (separate Tx and Rx) |
| Access required | One side | Both sides |
| Depth information | Yes (from time) | No |
| Sensitivity to laminar flaws | Moderate | **Very High** |
| Sensitivity to volumetric flaws | High | Moderate |
| Flaw sizing | Good (amplitude) | Poor (only attenuation) |

> **Suitability:**
> - **Laminar flaws** (e.g., delamination, lack of fusion parallel to surface) are best detected by **Through-Transmission**, because the planar flaw strongly blocks the beam regardless of its depth.
> - **Volumetric flaws** (e.g., porosity, inclusions) are well detected by **Pulse-Echo**, where each scatterer produces its own echo and depth can be determined from time-of-flight.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Describe A-Scan, B-Scan, C-Scan, and TOFD display modes. With a neat diagram, explain how TOFD is used to size a crack in a weld.**

**Model Answer:**

**A-Scan (Amplitude Scan):**
The most fundamental UT display. The **horizontal axis** represents **time of flight** (proportional to depth), and the **vertical axis** represents the **amplitude of the received echo**. Flaws appear as vertical spikes at specific times corresponding to their depths. A-scan is the standard display for manual pulse-echo flaw detection.

**B-Scan (Brightness Scan):**
A **2D cross-sectional image** of the test piece. The horizontal axis is the **scan position along the surface**, and the vertical axis is the **depth** (from time-of-flight). Echo brightness is mapped as pixel intensity. It looks like a side-view "slice" of the material, similar to a medical ultrasound image. Used for **weld profile and corrosion mapping**.

**C-Scan (Plan-view Scan):**
A **2D top-down (plan-view) image** of the test piece. The horizontal axes represent the **X and Y position** of the probe on the surface. The color/intensity at each point represents the **flaw amplitude at a gated depth**. C-scan produces a "map" of flaw distribution. It is the standard for **composite panel inspection, delamination detection, and bond testing**. It requires mechanical scanning.

**TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction):**
A specialized method for **accurate crack height sizing**. Two angled probes are placed on the same side of the weld, separated by a known distance $s$. The **transmitter** launches an angled beam, and the **receiver** picks up:
1. The **lateral wave** (surface skim wave) arriving first.
2. The **diffracted wave** from the **crack tip**, which acts as a point source radiating in all directions.
The **time difference $\Delta t$** between these two arrivals gives the depth of the crack tip below the surface:

$$ d = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}\right)^2 - s^2} $$

By repeating the measurement for the **upper tip** and **lower tip** of the crack, the **crack height** is computed as the difference of the two depths. TOFD is independent of the probe amplitude, making it a **primary calibration technique** for critical welds (e.g., nuclear, offshore).

**TOFD Probe Configuration Diagram (already shown in Section 4.5):** Transmitter at angle $\theta$ on left → crack tip diffracts wave → Receiver at angle $\theta$ on right captures both lateral and diffracted signals.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Resonance Method, Couplants, and Acoustic Impedance in UT**

**`[KTU University Exam — PESFT521, Dec 2023, Q.7, Module 3, Choice-based]`**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the Resonance method of ultrasonic testing. Derive the formula relating material thickness, resonance frequency, and wave velocity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
The **Resonance method** is based on the principle that a material of finite thickness behaves as an **acoustic resonator**. When ultrasonic energy is continuously fed into a thin plate, **standing waves** are set up at certain discrete frequencies where the material thickness is an integer multiple of half-wavelengths:

$$ t = \frac{n \lambda}{2} = \frac{n V}{2 f_n} \quad \text{where} \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$

The transducer is excited by a **variable frequency oscillator**, and the frequency is swept. At each resonance frequency $f_n$, the impedance of the transducer drops (because the crystal is "unloaded" by the resonant material), producing a sharp current peak that is displayed on a meter. The frequencies $f_1, f_2, f_3, \ldots$ are recorded, and the **thickness $t$** is calculated from the **difference** between successive resonances (the most accurate method):

$$ t = \frac{V}{2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)} $$

**Derivation of $t = \frac{nV}{2f_n}$:**

For the $n^{th}$ resonance, the plate thickness equals $n$ half-wavelengths:
$$ t = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} $$

Since $V = f \lambda$, we have $\lambda = V/f$. Substituting:
$$ t = n \cdot \frac{V}{2 f_n} $$

**Verification:** For $n = 1$, the fundamental mode, $t = V / (2 f_1)$, which is the well-known **quarter-wavelength resonance** of a free plate.

**Applications:**
- **Precision thickness measurement** of thin sheets, tubes, and small components (typically $t < 25$ mm).
- **Velocity measurement** in unknown materials (using a known thickness sample).
- Used in **online process control** (e.g., glass, foil, paper, sheet metal industries).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the role of couplant in ultrasonic testing. List four commonly used couplants with their suitability. Define acoustic impedance and derive the reflection coefficient at a steel–air interface.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Couplant:**
The couplant is a thin layer of liquid, gel, or paste placed between the transducer face and the test surface. Its primary functions are:
1. **Eliminate the air gap** between the probe and the test piece, which would otherwise reflect almost 100% of the ultrasonic energy (because $Z_{steel} \gg Z_{air}$).
2. **Efficiently transmit ultrasonic energy** from the transducer into the test material.
3. **Act as a lubricant** to allow the probe to slide smoothly on the surface during scanning.
4. **Protect the transducer** from wear during contact testing.

**Commonly Used Couplants:**

| Couplant | Composition | Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **Glycerin** | Propane-1,2,3-triol | General purpose, clean, non-toxic — most commonly used in labs |
| **Water** | H₂O with wetting agent | Immersion testing, large surfaces, high-temperature separation |
| **Machine oil / light grease** | Petroleum-based | Field testing of welds, low cost |
| **SAE 40 oil / commercial UT gel** | Cellulose-based gel | Manual scanning, rough surfaces, vertical/overhead surfaces |
| **Petroleum jelly (Vaseline)** | Hydrocarbon gel | High-viscosity use, vertical/overhead surfaces |
| **Cellulose gum gel** | Water-based polymer | Aerospace composites (non-corrosive) |

**Acoustic Impedance:**
The acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is defined as the product of its density $\rho$ and the velocity of sound $V$ in that medium:
$$ Z = \rho \cdot V \quad (\text{units: kg/(m²·s) or Rayl}) $$

It represents the resistance of the medium to the propagation of an acoustic wave. The greater the impedance mismatch at an interface, the greater the energy reflection.

**Derivation of Reflection Coefficient:**
At a flat interface between two media, applying continuity of pressure and particle velocity, the **pressure reflection coefficient** is:
$$ R_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} $$

The **intensity (energy) reflection coefficient** is the square of the pressure ratio:
$$ R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Numerical Example: Steel–Air Interface**
Given:
- $Z_{steel} = 46 \times 10^6$ kg/(m²·s)
- $Z_{air} = 0.0004 \times 10^6$ kg/(m²·s)

$$ R = \left(\frac{0.0004 \times 10^6 - 46 \times 10^6}{0.0004 \times 10^6 + 46 \times 10^6}\right)^2 $$

$$ R = \left(\frac{-45.9996 \times 10^6}{46.0004 \times 10^6}\right)^2 \approx (-0.99999)^2 \approx 0.99998 $$

$$ R \approx 99.998\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At a steel–air interface, **~99.998% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected** and only 0.002% is transmitted. This is why an air gap makes UT impossible and why couplant is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the pulse-echo depth formula. Students often write $D = Vt$ instead of $D = Vt/2$. Marks deducted: **2 out of 7**.
> 2. **Confusing pressure reflection coefficient** ($R_p$) with **intensity reflection coefficient** ($R$). The question may specifically ask for the intensity form. Marks deducted: **1 out of 7**.
> 3. **Mixing up longitudinal and shear wave velocities** in mode-conversion questions using Snell's law. Marks deducted: **2 out of 7**.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing 0.01776 instead of 17.76 mm). Marks deducted: **1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Writing resonance formula as $t = nV/f$** (missing the factor of 2). Marks deducted: **2 out of 7**.
> 6. **Confusing A-Scan with C-Scan** in display mode questions — A-Scan is 1D (amplitude vs time), C-Scan is 2D (plan view). Marks deducted: **1 out of 3**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as a last-minute revision tool before the KTU ESE.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **UT methods** are based on **acoustic impedance mismatch** and **time-of-flight** of high-frequency mechanical waves (0.5–25 MHz).
- The **piezoelectric effect** (PZT crystals) converts electrical pulses to mechanical vibrations and vice versa — this is the heart of every UT probe.
- **Couplant is mandatory** — air gap reflects ~100% of the energy and makes testing impossible.

### 🔑 Three Primary UT Methods (by transducer arrangement)
- **Pulse-Echo** → 1 probe, single-sided access, gives depth and amplitude. **Most common.**
- **Through-Transmission** → 2 probes, both sides needed, very sensitive to laminar flaws, no depth info.
- **Resonance** → variable frequency, used for **thin sheet thickness** (t < 25 mm), based on standing wave condition $t = nV/(2f_n)$.

### 🔑 Four Display Modes
- **A-Scan** → 1D: Amplitude vs. Time (used in manual UT).
- **B-Scan** → 2D cross-section: Depth vs. Scan position.
- **C-Scan** → 2D plan view: X-Y position map (needs scanning).
- **TOFD** → Time-of-Flight Diffraction for accurate crack sizing.

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas (7 most important)
1. $V = f \lambda$ — wave equation.
2. $D = \frac{V t}{2}$ — pulse-echo depth (factor of 2 is critical!).
3. $\lambda = \frac{V}{f}$ — wavelength (probe selection).
4. $N = \frac{D^2 f}{4V}$ — near field length.
5. $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ — energy reflection coefficient.
6. $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ — energy transmission coefficient.
7. $t = \frac{n V}{2 f_n}$ — resonance thickness.

### 🔑 Key Numerical Values to Remember
- $V_L$ (steel) = **5920 m/s**, $V_S$ (steel) = **3250 m/s**.
- $V_L$ (aluminum) = **6320 m/s**, $V_S$ (aluminum) = **3130 m/s**.
- $Z$ (steel) = **46 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)**, $Z$ (air) ≈ **0.0004 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)**.
- Steel–air reflection coefficient $R \approx 99.99\%$.

### 🔑 Snell's Law for Mode Conversion
- $\frac{\sin\theta_L}{V_L} = \frac{\sin\theta_S}{V_S} = \frac{\sin\theta_R}{V_R}$ (longitudinal, shear, and Rayleigh wave angles related through their respective velocities).
- **First critical angle** occurs when refracted shear wave is at 90°: $\sin\theta_{c1} = V_L / V_S$.
- **Second critical angle** occurs when refracted longitudinal wave is at 90°: $\sin\theta_{c2} = V_L / V_L = 1$ (never occurs in practice — longitudinal wave always refracts).
- Above first critical angle, **only shear waves propagate** in the solid — this is the basis of **angle beam inspection of welds**.

### 🔑 Common Couplants
- **Glycerin** (lab) → **Water** (immersion) → **Oil/grease** (field) → **Cellulose gel** (aerospace).

### 🔑 Real-World Applications
- Aerospace composites (C-scan), nuclear welds (TOFD), oil pipelines (in-line UT pigs), railway rails, pressure vessels, shipbuilding welds.

### 🔑 KTU-Favorite Question Patterns
- "Derive the formula for near field length" (7 marks).
- "Differentiate between Pulse-Echo and Through-Transmission" (7 marks).
- "Define acoustic impedance. A wave is incident from steel to water. Calculate the reflection coefficient." (numerical, 7 marks).
- "Explain TOFD and its advantages over conventional pulse-echo for crack sizing" (7 marks).
- "Define couplant. Why is it used? What would happen if we used air as couplant?" (3-mark short answer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Contact Testing and Immersion Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CONTACT TESTING & IMMERSION TESTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Ultrasonic Coupling?

In Ultrasonic Testing (UT), the ultrasonic pulse must travel from the **piezoelectric transducer** into the **test specimen** with the minimum possible loss of acoustic energy. The medium that bridges this energy transfer is called the **Couplant**, and the method used to establish this bridge defines the testing technique — **Contact Testing** or **Immersion Testing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> The student must clearly distinguish between the two principal pulse-echo coupling techniques — **Contact** and **Immersion** — and explain couplant selection, calibration, transducer alignment, advantages, limitations, and the role of the water path in immersion systems.

### 1.2 Formal Definitions

> **Contact Testing (Direct Contact Method):**
> A method of ultrasonic testing in which the search unit (transducer) is placed **in direct mechanical contact** with the test specimen through a thin film of liquid or semi-solid couplant applied manually to the test surface. The couplant eliminates the air gap (which has very high acoustic mismatch with metals) and provides a low-loss acoustic pathway.

> **Immersion Testing (Water Bath Method):**
> A method of ultrasonic testing in which both the search unit and the test specimen are **fully submerged** in a liquid medium (typically **water**) held in a tank. The ultrasonic beam travels through a column of water — called the **water path (or water standoff)** — before entering the specimen.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

**Contact Testing** is like talking to a friend through a thin glass window — you place your mouth against the glass and use a little saliva to make sure no air gap exists. The thinner and more uniform the "couplant layer," the clearer the message (the acoustic wave) passes through.

**Immersion Testing**, on the other hand, is like talking to your friend while both of you are inside a swimming pool. The water itself becomes the transmission medium, and your friend's ear (the test piece) is also surrounded by water, so the sound travels naturally with no "interface problem."

> [!NOTE]
> The acoustic impedance of **air is ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶ kg/m²s**, while that of **steel is ≈ 46 × 10⁶ kg/m²s** and **water ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ kg/m²s**. This huge mismatch is why an air gap between the transducer and test piece would reflect nearly 100% of the ultrasonic energy. Both Contact and Immersion methods exist purely to **eliminate this air gap**.

### 1.4 Why Two Methods Exist — The Engineering Trade-off

| Parameter | Contact Testing | Immersion Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Couplant** | Gel, grease, oil, paste | Water (often deionized, degassed) |
| **Setup speed** | Slow (manual couplant application) | Fast (test piece is dipped) |
| **Surface finish required** | Smooth & clean (roughness < 50 µm typical) | Tolerates rougher, irregular, hot, or curved surfaces |
| **Inspection of complex shapes** | Difficult (needs adaptation) | Easy (water conforms to shape) |
| **Automation** | Limited | Highly suitable (scanning tanks, robotic bridges) |
| **Sensitivity to small defects** | Excellent (very short couplant path) | Good, but water path causes some divergence |
| **Field/portable use** | **Yes — preferred for in-service inspection** | No — only in laboratory/fixed installations |
| **Cost** | Low (handheld flaw detector) | High (immersion tank, scanner, pump, filter) |

### 1.5 Acoustic Impedance — The Central Concept

> **Acoustic Impedance (Z):** The product of the density (ρ) of a medium and the velocity of sound (c) in that medium.
> $$Z = \rho \cdot c \quad [\text{kg/m}^2\text{s} \;\text{or Rayl}]$$

The reflection and transmission of ultrasound at any interface is governed entirely by the **ratio of acoustic impedances** of the two media. This single quantity explains why contact vs immersion behaves the way it does.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these typical values (very high yield for KTU):**
> - Water: Z ≈ **1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl**, c ≈ 1480 m/s
> - Steel: Z ≈ **46 × 10⁶ Rayl**, c ≈ 5920 m/s
> - Aluminum: Z ≈ **17 × 10⁶ Rayl**, c ≈ 6320 m/s
> - Plexiglas (delay line): Z ≈ **3.2 × 10⁶ Rayl**, c ≈ 2700 m/s
> - Glycerine (couplant): Z ≈ **2.4 × 10⁶ Rayl**, c ≈ 1920 m/s
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Acoustic Boundary Behaviour

When an ultrasonic wave strikes the interface between two media (Couplant ↔ Test Piece), three things happen:

1. **Partial Reflection** — energy bounces back into medium 1.
2. **Partial Transmission (Refraction)** — energy enters medium 2, **bending** according to Snell's Law.
3. **Mode Conversion** — a longitudinal wave striking at an angle produces both longitudinal and shear waves in medium 2.

The fractions of energy reflected and transmitted are quantified by the **Reflection Coefficient (R)** and the **Transmission Coefficient (T)**.

### 2.2 Reflection and Transmission Coefficients (Normal Incidence)

For a plane longitudinal wave striking the interface **perpendicularly**:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Intensity / Power ratio)}$$

$$T = \frac{4 \, Z_1 \, Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} \quad \text{(Intensity / Power ratio)}$$

For the **pressure (amplitude) ratio**, the unsquared form is used:

$$r = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}, \qquad t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation for KTU problems:**
> - When Z₁ = Z₂ (perfect impedance match) → R = 0, T = 1 → **100% transmission**.
> - When Z₂ ≫ Z₁ (e.g., steel in air) → R ≈ 1, T ≈ 0 → **almost total reflection**. This is why air is the enemy of UT.
> - Water-to-steel is a *much better* match than air-to-steel, but still only ~12% of the intensity gets into the steel at the first interface.

### 2.3 Snell's Law (Oblique Incidence)

When the wave hits the interface at an angle θ₁, the transmitted wave refracts at angle θ₂ given by:

$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2} = \frac{\sin \theta_L}{c_L} = \frac{\sin \theta_S}{c_S}$$

> **Critical Angle (θc):** The angle of incidence in medium 1 above which the longitudinal wave in medium 2 vanishes and a **surface wave** travels along the interface. Set sin θ₂ = 1:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{c_1}{c_2}\right)$$

> **First Critical Angle (Mode Conversion):** The angle above which only the shear wave propagates in medium 2:

$$\theta_{c1} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{c_1}{c_{L,2}}\right) \quad \text{(Longitudinal disappears in solid)}$$

$$\theta_{c2} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{c_1}{c_{S,2}}\right) \quad \text{(Shear disappears in solid)}$$

This is the principle behind **angle-beam transducers** used in contact testing of welds.

### 2.4 Near Field, Far Field, and Beam Spread

The ultrasonic beam from a circular disc transducer has two distinct regions:

**Near Field (Fresnel Zone) Length — N:**

$$N = \frac{D^2 \, f}{4 \, c} = \frac{D^2}{4 \, \lambda} \quad [\text{metres}]$$

where D = transducer diameter, f = frequency, c = sound velocity in the medium, λ = wavelength.

**Far Field (Fraunhofer Zone):** Begins at distance N from the transducer, where the beam starts to **diverge**.

**Beam Divergence (Half-angle of spread):**

$$\sin \alpha = 1.22 \, \frac{\lambda}{D} = 0.514 \, \frac{\lambda}{D} \quad \text{(for circular transducer, −6 dB points)}$$

> For small angles: $\alpha \approx \frac{0.514 \, \lambda}{D} \;\text{radians}$

**Beam Diameter at distance S beyond the transducer (S > N):**

$$d(S) = D + 2 \, S \tan \alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> In **immersion testing**, the water path length places the test piece's entry surface in the **far field** of the transducer, giving a wider but more uniform beam — excellent for scanning. In **contact testing**, the couplant is only fractions of a millimetre thick, so testing happens essentially at the **near-field** location — high resolution but very sensitive to small couplant-thickness variations.

### 2.5 Attenuation in the Couplant / Water

The intensity of the ultrasonic beam decreases exponentially due to absorption, scattering, and beam spreading:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-2\alpha x}$$

In decibel form:

$$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_x}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_x}\right)$$

For water, attenuation is roughly **0.002 dB/mm at 1 MHz** (negligible) but rises rapidly with frequency squared. This is why immersion systems use 1–25 MHz, and modern tanks use **degassed, deionized water** to suppress cavitation and bubbles.

### 2.6 The KTU 2024 Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance of a medium | kg/m²s (Rayl) |
| 2 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Reflected intensity fraction at interface | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Transmitted intensity fraction at interface | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2}$ | Snell's law of refraction | degrees / m·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\right)$ | Critical angle | degrees |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c} = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda}$ | Near-field length | metres |
| 7 | $\sin \alpha = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | Half-angle of beam divergence | radians |
| 8 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2 \alpha x}$ | Intensity at depth x due to attenuation | W/m² |
| 9 | $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_x}\right)$ | Attenuation / gain in decibels | dB |
| 10 | $d = 1.22 \, \lambda \, \dfrac{S}{D}$ | Beam diameter in far field at axial distance S | metres |
| 11 | $V_{\text{metal}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{metal}}}{t_{\text{water}}} \times V_{\text{water}}$ | V-path / equivalent water path relation | m/s |

> **Engineering Utility:** This set of equations governs every practical UT calibration. From selecting a 4 MHz, 10 mm-diameter immersion probe to scan a 50 mm aluminium plate to setting the water path length to avoid near-field interference echoes — every parameter is calculated from this table.

### 2.7 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Aerospace industry** uses immersion tanks with mechanized scanners to inspect aluminium wing skins for **disbonding, porosity, and inclusions** at production-line speeds of 1 m/s.
- **Power plant weld inspection** in the field is dominated by contact testing with angle-beam probes (45°, 60°, 70°) because immersion tanks cannot be carried to a reactor pressure vessel site.
- **Rail and pipeline industries** use contact testing for in-service detection of transverse cracks using shear-wave probes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Reflection and Transmission Coefficients (Normal Incidence)

### Setup
Consider two semi-infinite media with acoustic impedances Z₁ and Z₂ separated by a flat, perfectly smooth interface. A plane longitudinal wave of pressure amplitude P_i is incident normally from medium 1.

Boundary conditions at the interface (continuity of pressure and continuity of particle velocity) require:

$$P_i + P_r = P_t \qquad \text{...(i)}$$

$$u_i + u_r = u_t \qquad \text{...(ii)}$$

where the subscripts i, r, t denote incident, reflected, and transmitted waves.

### Using the acoustic relations
Particle velocity u is related to pressure P and acoustic impedance Z by:

$$u = \frac{P}{Z}$$

Substitute into (ii):

$$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \; P_i - P_r = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} P_t \qquad \text{...(iii)}$$

(We have used the sign convention: a reflected wave travels in the opposite direction to the incident wave, hence its particle velocity is taken negative in medium 1.)

### Solving simultaneously
Adding (i) and (iii):

$$2 P_i = P_t \left(1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right) = P_t \frac{Z_1 + Z_2}{Z_2}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \; P_t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} \, P_i$$

Subtract (iii) from (i):

$$2 P_r = P_t \left(1 - \frac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right) = P_t \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \; P_r = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \, P_i$$

### Pressure amplitude coefficients (r and t)

$$\boxed{\,r = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \,}$$

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{P_t}{P_i} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1} \,}$$

### Converting to intensity (power) coefficients
Intensity is proportional to the square of the pressure amplitude multiplied by the impedance of the medium in which the wave is travelling:

$$I = \frac{P^2}{2 Z}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \frac{P_r^2 / Z_1}{P_i^2 / Z_1} = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

$$T = \frac{I_t}{I_i} = \frac{P_t^2 / Z_2}{P_i^2 / Z_1} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \left(\frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

### Energy conservation check
R + T = 1 ?

$$\frac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{Z_2^2 - 2 Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = 1 \;\; \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The reflection and transmission coefficients obey energy conservation exactly. This identity is often asked in KTU viva/practical exams to verify student understanding.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Snell's Law from Wavefront Geometry

### Setup
A plane wavefront AB in medium 1 strikes the interface at an angle θ₁. The wavefront reaches point A first, then progressively. By the time the wavefront at B has travelled the distance $\overline{BB'} = c_1 \, \Delta t$ in medium 1, the wavelet from A has already entered medium 2 and travelled $\overline{AA'} = c_2 \, \Delta t$ in medium 2.

The new wavefront in medium 2 is A'B'. The angles θ₁ and θ₂ are the angles between the new wavefront and the interface.

### Geometry
From triangle ABB':

$$\sin \theta_1 = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{\overline{AB'}} = \frac{c_1 \, \Delta t}{\overline{AB'}}$$

From triangle AA'B':

$$\sin \theta_2 = \frac{\overline{AA'}}{\overline{A'B'}} = \frac{c_2 \, \Delta t}{\overline{AB'}}$$

### Result
Dividing the two equations:

$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2} = \frac{c_1}{c_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2} = k\,}$$

The constant k is the **wavenumber component parallel to the interface** — a fundamental conserved quantity during refraction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Near-Field Length

### Setup
Consider a circular piston transducer of diameter D oscillating in uniform medium of sound velocity c. Treat the transducer face as a continuum of point sources (Huygens' principle) and find the on-axis pressure field.

### On-axis pressure
For a small ring element of radius r and width dr on the transducer face, the contribution to the on-axis pressure at distance x is:

$$dP(x) = j \, f \rho \, v \, 2 \pi r \, \frac{e^{-j k R}}{R} \, dr$$

where R = √(x² + r²), k = ω/c, v = particle velocity amplitude.

Summing over the entire transducer (r = 0 to D/2):

$$P(x) = j f \rho v \int_0^{D/2} \frac{2 \pi r \, e^{-j k R}}{R} \, dr$$

For x ≫ D (far field), the integral simplifies. In the near field (x ≪ D), interference between contributions causes strong oscillations.

### Last axial maximum (the near-field boundary)
The last on-axis maximum occurs when the path difference between the wave from the centre of the transducer and the wave from the edge equals one wavelength:

$$R_{\text{edge}} - R_{\text{centre}} = \lambda$$

At the near-field boundary, this becomes exactly one wavelength, and all subsequent on-axis points are in the far field.

$$R_{\text{edge}} = \sqrt{N^2 + (D/2)^2}$$

For N ≫ D/2 (which is always true for practical transducers), expand using binomial:

$$\sqrt{N^2 + (D/2)^2} \approx N + \frac{D^2}{8 N}$$

Setting $R_{\text{edge}} - R_{\text{centre}} = N + \frac{D^2}{8 N} - N = \frac{D^2}{8 N} = \lambda$:

$$N = \frac{D^2}{8 \lambda}$$

But since $\lambda = c/f$, we obtain the most cited form:

$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \frac{D^2 \, f}{4 c}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Beam Divergence — Derivation of sin α = 1.22 λ / D

The far-field pressure distribution from a circular piston of diameter D is given by the Rayleigh-Sommerfeld integral, whose on-axis-far-field limit gives the well-known **Airy pattern** (analogous to a circular aperture in optics). The first minimum of this pattern occurs at an angle α from the axis given by:

$$J_1(k a \sin \alpha) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k a \sin \alpha = 3.83$$

where a = D/2 is the transducer radius. Thus:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{3.83 \, \lambda}{\pi D} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$$

$$\boxed{\,\sin \alpha = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}\,}$$

This is the half-angle of the **main lobe**, and the beam diameter at distance S in the far field is:

$$\boxed{\,d(S) = 2 S \tan \alpha + D \approx \frac{1.22 \, \lambda \, S}{D} + D\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU-Style Problem (Module 3):**
> A 4 MHz, 20 mm-diameter immersion probe is used to inspect a steel plate submerged in water. The water path is 80 mm. Calculate (a) the near-field length in water, (b) the beam diameter at the entry surface of the steel, (c) the transmission coefficient of intensity at the water–steel interface.

**Given:**
- f = 4 × 10⁶ Hz
- D = 20 × 10⁻³ m
- c_water = 1480 m/s
- c_steel = 5920 m/s
- Water path S = 80 × 10⁻³ m
- Z_water = 1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl
- Z_steel = 46 × 10⁶ Rayl

### Part (a) — Near-field length in water

$$\lambda_{\text{water}} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1480}{4 \times 10^6} = 3.7 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} = 0.37 \;\text{mm}$$

$$N = \frac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \frac{(20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 3.7 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.48 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N = 0.270 \;\text{m} = 270 \;\text{mm}$$

Since S = 80 mm < N = 270 mm, **the test piece lies within the near field**.

### Part (b) — Beam diameter at the entry surface

Near-field beam diameter at distance S is (linear interpolation between D at x = 0 and d_max = √2 D at x = N):

$$d(S) = D \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)^2} = 20 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{80}{270}\right)^2}$$

$$d(S) = 20 \sqrt{1 + 0.0877} = 20 \sqrt{1.0877} = 20 \times 1.043 = 20.86 \;\text{mm}$$

### Part (c) — Transmission coefficient (intensity) at water–steel

$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 46}{(1.5 + 46)^2} = \frac{276}{(47.5)^2} = \frac{276}{2256.25}$$

$$T = 0.1223 \quad \text{i.e.,} \;\; 12.23\%$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Only about 12% of the ultrasonic intensity enters the steel at normal incidence. The remaining 88% is reflected back into the water, where it eventually returns to the transducer as a strong **interface echo** (called the *entry surface echo* or *initial pulse*). This is a normal and expected feature of every immersion scan.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Immersion UT System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG1[Transmitter and Receiver]
        P1[Pulse Generator]
        P2[Receiver Amplifier]
        P3[Display Oscilloscope / A-scan]
    end

    subgraph SG2[Mechanical Scanner Bridge]
        M1[X axis Stepper Motor Drive]
        M2[Y axis Stepper Motor Drive]
        M3[Immersion Probe Holder with Indexer]
    end

    subgraph SG3[Immersion Tank Assembly]
        T1[Stainless Steel Tank Filled with Degassed Deionised Water]
        T2[Transducer Probe 4 MHz, 20 mm dia]
        T3[Test Specimen Held in Manipulator or Fixture]
        T4[Water Path between Probe Face and Specimen Surface]
    end

    subgraph SG4[Water Conditioning Subsystem]
        W1[Water Pump Recirculation]
        W2[Deioniser Cartridge]
        W3[Degasifier Vacuum Chamber]
        W4[Inline Heater to maintain 20 to 40 degree C]
    end

    P1 --> T2
    T2 -- ultrasonic beam --> T4
    T4 -- through water --> T3
    T3 -- reflected echoes --> T4
    T4 -- back through water --> T2
    T2 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> T2
    W1 --> T1
    W2 --> W1
    W3 --> W1
    W4 --> W1
    T1 --> T4
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Contact Testing Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser / Receiver Module] -- electrical pulse --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B -- couplant film --> C[Test Piece Surface]
    C -- reflected echo from flaw or back wall --> B
    B -- electrical echo signal --> D[Receiver Amplifier]
    D --> E[Time Base and Gate Circuit]
    E --> F[CRT or LCD A-scan Display]
    F --> G[Operator Interpretation]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing Contact vs Immersion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ultrasonic Inspection Required] --> B{Is the test piece portable to a lab}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is the geometry complex curved or hot}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Immersion Testing]
    C -- No --> E{Are very high speeds of scan required}
    E -- Yes --> D
    E -- No --> F[Both methods viable, choose by economics]
    B -- No --> G{Is the surface finish adequate for contact}
    G -- Yes --> H[Use Contact Testing portable flaw detector]
    G -- No --> I[Use Immersion testing with a portable water filled pouch]
```

## 4.4 Beam Geometry Near-Field vs Far-Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1[Near Field Fresnel Zone]
        NF[Strong interference pattern, axial pressure oscillates between maxima and minima]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Far Field Fraunhofer Zone]
        FF[Smooth smooth divergence, beam spreads as a cone of half angle alpha]
    end
    TX[Transducer Disc] -- distance N --> Z1
    Z1 -- distance beyond N --> Z2
```

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Pulser** | Generates a high-voltage (100–400 V) short pulse (~100 ns) to excite the piezo crystal. |
| **Transducer** | Converts electrical pulse ↔ mechanical ultrasonic wave. |
| **Couplant** | Eliminates the air gap by filling surface irregularities. |
| **Test Piece** | Specimen to be inspected. |
| **Receiver** | Amplifies the weak returning echoes (often µV–mV range). |
| **Display (A-scan)** | Plots echo amplitude vs time; flaw = peak, back wall = final peak. |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State any three advantages of immersion ultrasonic testing over contact ultrasonic testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Couplant uniformity is automatic** — the water path provides a perfectly uniform acoustic medium, eliminating the variability of manual couplant application in contact testing. *(1 mark)*

2. **Suitability for complex geometries** — the test piece can have curved, rough, or even hot surfaces because water conforms to any shape; this is impossible with contact probes which require a flat, smooth surface. *(1 mark)*

3. **High-speed automated scanning** — the probe is held rigidly by a motorized bridge, and the part is moved in a precise X–Y raster; this enables 100% volumetric inspection of aerospace components at production rates that contact methods cannot match. *(1 mark)*

> *(Alternative valid points: small transducer wear because no rubbing, no couplant contamination, ability to use focused transducers, better resolution of near-surface defects due to adjustable water path.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a couplant? List four commonly used couplants in ultrasonic testing with one application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Couplant:** A substance (usually a liquid, gel, or paste) applied between the ultrasonic probe and the test surface to **exclude air and provide an efficient acoustic path** for the ultrasonic energy. *(1 mark)*

| Couplant | Typical Application |
|---|---|
| Water + detergent | Quick laboratory inspections, general rough surfaces |
| Glycerine | General-purpose shop-floor inspection, slow drying |
| Oil / machine oil | In-service field inspection of pipelines and welds |
| Cellulose gel (e.g., Sonotrace) | Aerospace composite inspection, vertical surfaces |
| Petroleum jelly | Long-duration scans, rough or porous surfaces |
| High-temperature grease | Inspection of hot components up to 300 °C |

*(Any four correct = 2 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each Part B question carries 14 marks and may have internal choice between two 7-mark sub-parts. Below, two full 14-mark alternative questions are provided.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**a) (7 marks)** Derive the expressions for the **Reflection Coefficient (R)** and **Transmission Coefficient (T)** of ultrasonic intensity at the interface between two media of acoustic impedances Z₁ and Z₂ under normal incidence. **Show all steps.**

**b) (7 marks)** A longitudinal ultrasonic wave travels from **glycerine** (ρ = 1260 kg/m³, c = 1920 m/s) into **aluminium** (ρ = 2700 kg/m³, c = 6320 m/s). Calculate (i) the reflection coefficient, (ii) the transmission coefficient, and (iii) the percentage of incident intensity reflected back. **Verify R + T = 1.**

---

### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Apply boundary conditions at the interface:**
Pressure continuity: $P_i + P_r = P_t$ ... (1)
Velocity continuity: $\dfrac{P_i}{Z_1} - \dfrac{P_r}{Z_1} = \dfrac{P_t}{Z_2}$ ... (2) *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Solve (1) and (2) for the pressure amplitude reflection coefficient:**
From (2): $P_i - P_r = \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2} P_t$
Adding and subtracting with (1) gives:
$P_t = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i \quad ; \quad P_r = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} P_i$ *(3 marks)*

**Step 3 — Convert pressure coefficients to intensity coefficients** using $I = P^2 / (2Z)$:

$$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$$

$$T = \frac{I_t}{I_i} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \cdot \left(\frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Verify energy conservation:**
$R + T = \dfrac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \dfrac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = 1$ ✓ *(1 mark)*

---

### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate acoustic impedances:**
$Z_1 = \rho_1 c_1 = 1260 \times 1920 = 2.419 \times 10^6$ Rayl
$Z_2 = \rho_2 c_2 = 2700 \times 6320 = 1.706 \times 10^7$ Rayl *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient:**
$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{17.06 - 2.419}{17.06 + 2.419}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{14.64}{19.48}\right)^2 = (0.7516)^2 = 0.5650$$ *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Transmission coefficient:**
$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = 4 \times \frac{2.419 \times 17.06}{(19.48)^2} = \frac{165.16}{379.47} = 0.4350$$ *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Verification & percentage reflected:**
$R + T = 0.5650 + 0.4350 = 1.0000$ ✓
**Percentage of intensity reflected = 56.5%** *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (7-mark derivation questions):**
> - **Common error #1:** Students write the *pressure* reflection coefficient and treat it as the *intensity* reflection coefficient, getting R = 0.7516 instead of 0.5650 → **lose 2 marks**.
> - **Common error #2:** Forgetting to take the square when going from pressure ratio to intensity ratio → **lose 1 mark**.
> - **Common error #3:** Quoting "transmission coefficient" without specifying **intensity T vs pressure t** → examiner deducts 1 mark for ambiguity.
> - **Common error #4:** In the numerical part, units of Z must be shown as 10⁶ Rayl for clarity; missing units → **lose 0.5 mark**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**a) (7 marks)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the **immersion ultrasonic testing** method. List its advantages and limitations compared to contact testing.

**b) (7 marks)** A 5 MHz immersion probe of **12 mm diameter** is used in water (c = 1480 m/s) to inspect a plate through a water path of 60 mm. Calculate:
(i) the wavelength in water,
(ii) the near-field length in water,
(iii) the half-angle of beam divergence in the far field, and
(iv) the beam diameter at the specimen entry surface (S = 60 mm, given that S < N, so the entry surface is in the near field — use the near-field beam-spread formula $d = D \sqrt{1 + (S/N)^2}$).

---

### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Sketch (3 marks):** A clear labelled diagram showing:
- Immersion tank with water
- Transducer probe held on a manipulator above the tank
- Test piece mounted on a fixture (or held by a bridge scanner)
- Water path (standoff) between probe and specimen
- Path of ultrasonic beam: pulser → probe → water → specimen → back wall echo → probe → receiver → display

**Description (2 marks):** In immersion UT, the transducer does not touch the test piece. The probe and specimen are both immersed in a water bath, and the ultrasonic wave travels through a column of water (the water path) before entering the test piece. The water acts as the couplant. The transducer can be moved mechanically, and a C-scan image can be generated.

**Advantages (1 mark — any 2 valid):**
- No couplant contamination; consistent coupling.
- Suitable for complex/curved/hot/rough surfaces.
- Ideal for automated high-speed scanning.
- Probe does not wear due to friction.

**Limitations (1 mark — any 2 valid):**
- Requires a large, expensive tank and water conditioning system.
- Not portable — limited to laboratory/factory use.
- Water path causes beam divergence, lowering resolution for small defects.
- Water temperature must be controlled for stable velocity.

---

### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:** f = 5 × 10⁶ Hz, D = 12 × 10⁻³ m, c = 1480 m/s, S = 60 × 10⁻³ m

**(i) Wavelength in water:**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1480}{5 \times 10^6} = 2.96 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m} = 0.296\;\text{mm}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) Near-field length in water:**
$$N = \frac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \frac{(12 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{1.184 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1216\;\text{m} \approx 121.6\;\text{mm}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Half-angle of beam divergence:**
$$\sin \alpha = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-4}}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3.611 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0301$$
$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.0301) = 1.725° \approx 1.73°$$ *(2 marks)*

**(iv) Beam diameter at S = 60 mm (S < N, so use near-field formula):**
$$\frac{S}{N} = \frac{60}{121.6} = 0.4934$$
$$d = D \sqrt{1 + (S/N)^2} = 12 \sqrt{1 + (0.4934)^2} = 12 \sqrt{1 + 0.2434} = 12 \sqrt{1.2434}$$
$$d = 12 \times 1.1151 = 13.38\;\text{mm}$$ *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-mark full-length problem):**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Computing wavelength in steel instead of water → entire 1.5-mark step is wrong.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Confusing the **far-field** divergence formula with the **near-field** beam-diameter formula in part (iv). Since S = 60 mm < N = 121.6 mm, you **must** use the near-field formula $d = D \sqrt{1 + (S/N)^2}$, **not** the far-field formula. Mixing them is the single most common mistake in KTU answer sheets.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Leaving α in radians instead of converting to degrees. KTU expects degrees.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Not writing the final answer in **mm** for engineering readability.

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these to your revision wall the night before the KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Acoustic Impedance** $Z = \rho c$ is the central physical quantity that determines reflection and transmission at every interface. **Memorize steel = 46 × 10⁶ Rayl, water = 1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl, aluminium ≈ 17 × 10⁶ Rayl.**

- ✅ **Reflection Coefficient (intensity)** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$. **Transmission Coefficient (intensity)** $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$. Always verify $R + T = 1$.

- ✅ **Snell's Law:** $\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{c_2}$. Critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\right)$.

- ✅ **Near-field length:** $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c}$. This is where the beam is **not yet a clean cylinder** — avoid placing test pieces here when possible.

- ✅ **Far-field beam divergence:** $\sin \alpha = 1.22 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$. Test pieces placed in the far field see a wider but more uniform beam.

- ✅ **Contact Testing** uses a thin couplant film (gel, oil, glycerine, water + detergent). The probe touches the specimen. **Field-portable, low cost, sensitive to surface condition.**

- ✅ **Immersion Testing** uses water as the couplant. The water path (typically 25–150 mm) is **adjustable** — this is the single biggest advantage because it lets the inspector position the test piece precisely in the far field.

- ✅ In immersion testing, the **entry surface echo** is always large (~88% reflection) and is used as the **reference signal for calibration**.

- ✅ **Mode conversion** at oblique incidence: L → L + S. Used in angle-beam contact probes (45°, 60°, 70°) for weld inspection.

- ✅ **Water quality matters:** degassed, deionized water prevents cavitation bubbles that scatter ultrasound and create false indications.

- ✅ **Attenuation in water** ≈ 0.002 dB/mm at 1 MHz, rises as $f^2$. Attenuation in steel is much higher (≈ 0.05 dB/mm at 1 MHz for fine-grained steel).

- ✅ **Conversion quicky:** 1 inch = 25.4 mm; near-field length in inches ≈ $0.32 \dfrac{D^2 \text{(in)}}{\lambda \text{(in)}}$ — useful for US-origin textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Beam and Straight Beam Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Beam and Straight Beam Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Normal Beam Testing

**Normal Beam Testing (NBT)** is an ultrasonic non-destructive testing technique in which a piezo-electric transducer generates **longitudinal (compressional) waves** that propagate **perpendicular (90°) to the test surface** of the specimen. Because the wave enters the material at a normal angle of incidence, there is no mode conversion at the entry surface, and the energy travels as a pure longitudinal wave deep into the component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT521 - Module 3):**
> "Normal beam testing uses a flat, straight-beam transducer producing longitudinal waves that travel perpendicular to the test surface, primarily used for flaw detection, thickness measurement, and material characterization in plate, billet, and forged components."

The terms **Normal Beam** and **Straight Beam** are often used interchangeably in NDT literature, but technically:
- *Normal Beam* refers to the **direction of wave propagation** (perpendicular to the surface).
- *Straight Beam* refers to the **shape of the sound beam** — the beam travels in a straight, non-refracted path through the test piece.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a calm swimming pool and you push a rubber ball **straight down** into the water. The ball travels in a perfectly straight vertical line, hits the bottom, and bounces straight back up to your hand.

- The **ball** is the ultrasound pulse.
- The **water surface** is the boundary between the transducer and the test piece.
- The **pool bottom** is the back wall of the test piece or an internal defect (like a crack or void).
- The **hand** that catches the ball is the receiver of the returning echo.

If there is a hidden rock (a **flaw**) at some depth, the ball will bounce back *earlier* than expected. The time it takes to come back tells you *how deep* the flaw is. This is the basic principle of **pulse-echo normal beam testing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** Used routinely in aircraft fuselage inspection, rail axle testing, and pressure vessel weld inspection to detect laminations, inclusions, porosity, and cracks oriented parallel to the test surface.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Wave Mode | Longitudinal (L-wave) |
| Sound Velocity in Steel | **5920 m/s** |
| Sound Velocity in Aluminium | **6320 m/s** |
| Sound Velocity in Water | **1480 m/s** |
| Sound Velocity in Air | **343 m/s** |
| Test Frequency Range | **1 MHz – 25 MHz** |
| Standard Transducer Diameters | 6 mm, 10 mm, 13 mm, 19 mm, 25 mm |
| Couplant | Gel, glycerin, oil, water |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why couplant is needed:** Air has an acoustic impedance of only ~0.0004 MRayl, while steel is ~46 MRayl. Without a couplant, **99.9% of the sound is reflected** back from the surface and never enters the test piece. A couplant (gel/water/oil) bridges the air gap and allows sound transmission.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Operation

Normal beam testing follows the **Pulse-Echo (PE)** principle, which operates in the following sequence:

1. **Pulser Activation:** An electronic pulser applies a high-voltage spike (typically 100–400 V) to the piezo-electric crystal inside the transducer.
2. **Wave Generation:** The piezo-electric element vibrates at its resonant frequency, producing an ultrasonic pulse (typically 1–3 cycles long).
3. **Transmission into Material:** Through the couplant, the wave enters the test piece **perpendicular** to the surface.
4. **Wave Propagation:** The longitudinal wave travels through the material at its characteristic velocity.
5. **Reflection at Interfaces:** When the wave strikes an acoustic impedance discontinuity (flaw, back wall, lamination), part of the energy is reflected.
6. **Echo Reception:** The reflected echo returns to the same transducer, which now acts as a receiver.
7. **Signal Display:** The echoes are displayed on a CRT/LCD as vertical spikes (A-scan presentation).
8. **Time Measurement:** The time interval between the initial pulse (IP) and the flaw/back-wall echo (BWE) is measured to determine the depth of the reflector.

## 2.2 Transducer Configuration

A **straight beam (normal) transducer** typically consists of:

- **Piezo-electric Crystal** (PZT – Lead Zirconate Titanate) – converts electrical to mechanical energy.
- **Backing (Damping) Block** – absorbs energy radiating from the back of the crystal, producing a short pulse.
- **Wear Plate / Protective Face** – protects the crystal.
- **Connector** – links to the UT instrument via coaxial cable.

> [!NOTE]
> The **thickness of the piezo-electric crystal** determines the frequency:
> $$f = \frac{v_{crystal}}{2 \times t_{crystal}}$$
> where $v_{crystal}$ is the longitudinal velocity in PZT (~4500 m/s) and $t_{crystal}$ is the thickness.

## 2.3 Sound Beam Characteristics

The sound beam emitted from a circular transducer has three distinct zones:

| Zone | Description | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Near Field (Fresnel Zone)** | Length $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ | High beam intensity, parallel rays — ideal for sensitive detection |
| **Focal Point** | At distance $N$ from transducer | Maximum intensity |
| **Far Field (Fraunhofer Zone)** | Beyond distance $N$ | Diverging beam, lower intensity |

The **beam spread (divergence angle)** in the far field is given by:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 v}{f D}$$

where:
- $\theta$ = half-angle of beam divergence
- $\lambda$ = wavelength
- $D$ = transducer diameter
- $v$ = sound velocity in the material
- $f$ = frequency

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = f \lambda$ | Fundamental wave relation | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength calculation | m |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ | Near field length | m |
| 4 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Half-angle of beam divergence | radians |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Thickness from time-of-flight | m |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{2T}{v}$ | Round-trip time | s |
| 7 | $Z = \rho \cdot v$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/(m²·s) or Rayl |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Pressure reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{coefficient} = \dfrac{2Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Pressure transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{v_{crystal}}{2 t_{crystal}}$ | Transducer frequency | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** In normal beam testing, since the wave strikes the surface at 0° (normal incidence), there is **no mode conversion** and **no refraction** (Snell's law: $\sin 0° = 0$). The wave continues as a pure longitudinal wave. This is a common 2-mark question in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Normal beam straight beam testing is the **workhorse** of ultrasonic inspection and is used extensively for:

- **Weld inspection** (volumetric flaws: porosity, slag, lack of fusion)
- **Forging inspection** (cracks, laps, bursts)
- **Castings** (porosity, inclusions, cold shuts)
- **Plate and sheet** (laminations, delaminations)
- **Thickness measurement** (corrosion monitoring in pipelines and storage tanks)
- **Material characterization** (velocity, attenuation, grain size estimation)
- **Adhesive bond testing** (in aerospace honeycomb structures)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Round-Trip Time-of-Flight for Thickness Measurement

**Problem Statement:** A 50 mm thick steel plate is inspected using a 4 MHz normal beam transducer. If the longitudinal velocity in steel is 5920 m/s, calculate:
(a) The wavelength of the ultrasound in steel.
(b) The time taken for the back-wall echo to return.
(c) The near field length if the transducer diameter is 20 mm.

### Step 1: Identify given values

- Thickness, $T = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Frequency, $f = 4 \text{ MHz} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Velocity, $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$
- Transducer diameter, $D = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$

### Step 2: Calculate wavelength (part a)

Using the fundamental wave relation:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

Substituting values:

$$\lambda = \frac{5920 \text{ m/s}}{4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.48 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 3: Calculate round-trip time (part b)

The pulse must travel **twice** through the thickness (down to back wall and back up):

$$t = \frac{2T}{v}$$

Substituting:

$$t = \frac{2 \times 0.050 \text{ m}}{5920 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$t = \frac{0.100}{5920}$$

$$\boxed{t = 1.689 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 16.89 \text{ μs}}$$

### Step 4: Calculate near field length (part c)

$$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$$

Substituting:

$$N = \frac{(0.020)^2}{4 \times 1.48 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{5.92 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{N = 0.0676 \text{ m} = 67.6 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The near field length (67.6 mm) is **greater** than the plate thickness (50 mm). This means the inspection is happening within the near field, which is actually **ideal** for normal beam testing because the beam intensity is highest and most uniform in this region.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reflection Coefficient at a Steel–Air Interface

**Problem Statement:** An ultrasonic longitudinal wave traveling in steel strikes a flat crack (filled with air) at normal incidence. Calculate the percentage of energy reflected back.
- $v_{steel} = 5920$ m/s, $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³
- $v_{air} = 343$ m/s, $\rho_{air} = 1.21$ kg/m³

### Step 1: Calculate acoustic impedances

$$Z_{steel} = \rho_{steel} \cdot v_{steel} = 7850 \times 5920$$

$$Z_{steel} = 4.647 \times 10^7 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} = 46.47 \text{ MRayl}$$

$$Z_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot v_{air} = 1.21 \times 343$$

$$Z_{air} = 415.0 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} = 0.000415 \text{ MRayl}$$

### Step 2: Apply reflection coefficient formula

$$R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{Z_{air} - Z_{steel}}{Z_{air} + Z_{steel}}$$

$$R = \frac{0.000415 - 46.47}{0.000415 + 46.47} = \frac{-46.4696}{46.4704}$$

$$R \approx -0.99998$$

The **magnitude** is essentially 1, and the **negative sign** indicates a 180° phase inversion.

### Step 3: Calculate percentage of energy reflected

The fraction of intensity reflected is $R^2$ (since intensity is proportional to pressure squared):

$$\text{Reflected intensity fraction} = R^2 = (-0.99998)^2 \approx 0.99996$$

$$\boxed{\text{Energy reflected} \approx 99.998\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why normal beam testing is **exceptionally sensitive** to cracks — a crack filled with air reflects almost 100% of the ultrasound back to the transducer, producing a strong echo on the screen. This is the foundation of pulse-echo flaw detection.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Sensitivity vs. Defect Orientation

| Flaw Orientation | Detectability in Normal Beam | Detectability in Angle Beam |
|---|---|---|
| Crack **parallel** to surface (e.g., lamination) | **Excellent** ✓ | Poor |
| Crack **perpendicular** to surface (e.g., tight crack) | Poor ✗ | Excellent ✓ |
| Spherical porosity | Good | Good |
| Elongated inclusion parallel to surface | Excellent ✓ | Poor |
| Elongated inclusion perpendicular to surface | Poor | Excellent ✓ |
| Sub-surface delamination | Excellent ✓ | Poor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** This complementary relationship is why UT inspectors use **both** normal and angle beam techniques together for a complete volumetric inspection. Normal beam misses vertical cracks; angle beam misses horizontal laminations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Beam Spread Calculation

**Problem Statement:** For the same 4 MHz, 20 mm diameter transducer in steel, calculate the half-angle of beam divergence.

### Step 1: Recall values

- $\lambda = 1.48 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $D = 0.020$ m

### Step 2: Apply divergence formula

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$$

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \times 1.48 \times 10^{-3}}{0.020}$$

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.806 \times 10^{-3}}{0.020} = 0.0903$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.0903) = 5.18°$$

### Step 3: Full beam spread (total angle)

$$\theta_{total} = 2\theta = 2 \times 5.18° = 10.36°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even at 4 MHz with a 20 mm transducer, the beam spreads by about 10.4° in the far field. Higher frequency and larger diameter reduce beam spread, improving resolution at depth.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pulse-Echo Normal Beam Inspection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser Receiver Unit] --> B[Coaxial Cable]
    B --> C[Normal Beam Transducer]
    C --> D[Couplant Layer]
    D --> E[Test Specimen]
    E --> F[Internal Flaw]
    E --> G[Back Wall]
    F --> H[Reflected Echo 1]
    G --> I[Reflected Echo 2]
    H --> A
    I --> A
    A --> J[A-Scan Display CRT LCD]
    J --> K[Initial Pulse IP]
    J --> L[Flaw Echo F1]
    J --> M[Back Wall Echo BWE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pulser-receiver sends a high-voltage pulse through the cable to the transducer. The transducer converts this to ultrasound, which passes through the couplant into the specimen. The pulse travels down, reflects off the flaw (early echo) and the back wall (late echo), returns to the transducer (which now acts as a receiver), and is displayed on the screen as vertical spikes along a horizontal time base.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sound Beam Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transducer Face] --> B[Near Field Fresnel Zone]
    B --> C[Focal Point Maximum Intensity]
    C --> D[Far Field Fraunhofer Zone]
    D --> E[Beam Divergence and Attenuation]
    B --> F[Parallel Collimated Rays]
    D --> G[Diverging Conical Beam]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Normal Beam Test Procedure Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Surface Preparation] --> B[Step 2 Couplant Application]
    B --> C[Step 3 Transducer Placement]
    C --> D[Step 4 Equipment Calibration]
    D --> E[Step 5 Scan the Test Surface]
    E --> F[Step 6 Identify Echoes]
    F --> G[Step 7 Mark and Record Defects]
    G --> H[Step 8 Generate Inspection Report]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Echo Pattern on A-Scan Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IP Initial Pulse at t=0] --> B[F1 Flaw Echo at t=t1]
    B --> C[BWE Back Wall Echo at t=t2]
    A --- D[Horizontal Time Base Axis]
    B --- D
    C --- D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of time intervals:**
> - $t_1$ = time from IP to flaw echo = $2d_{flaw}/v$ → gives flaw depth
> - $t_2$ = time from IP to back-wall echo = $2T/v$ → gives total thickness
> - $t_2 - t_1$ = time from flaw to back wall → gives distance from flaw to back wall

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Transducer Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Connector] --> B[Coaxial Cable Joint]
    B --> C[Piezo Electric Crystal PZT]
    C --> D[Wear Plate Protective Face]
    C --> E[Backing Damping Block Tungsten Epoxy]
    D --> F[Contact with Couplant and Test Piece]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Normal Beam Testing. What type of wave is generated in this method?**

**Model Answer:**
Normal beam testing is an ultrasonic NDT technique in which the transducer is placed **perpendicular** to the test surface, and ultrasonic waves travel **normal (at 0° angle of incidence)** into the material. The wave mode generated is the **longitudinal (compressional) wave**, since at normal incidence there is no mode conversion at the surface. The waves propagate straight down, reflect from internal flaws and the back wall, and return as echoes to the same transducer (pulse-echo mode).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is a couplant necessary in normal beam ultrasonic testing? Name any two commonly used couplants.**

**Model Answer:**
A couplant is necessary to **eliminate the air gap** between the transducer and the test surface. Air has a very low acoustic impedance (~0.0004 MRayl) compared to metals (~46 MRayl for steel), so without a couplant, **99.9% of the ultrasound is reflected at the surface** and never enters the test piece. The couplant provides an acoustic impedance match, allowing efficient transmission of sound energy.
Common couplants: **(1) Glycerin, (2) Water** (also acceptable: oil, ultrasonic gel, motor oil, cellulose paste).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Part B Module questions carry internal choice. Students must answer either Option A **or** Option B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of pulse-echo normal beam testing**. Describe the function of each block.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Pulse-echo normal beam testing works on the principle of generating a short ultrasonic pulse, transmitting it into the test material perpendicular to the surface, and analyzing the echoes that return from internal reflectors (flaws, back wall).

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1)** and **Function of Each Block:**

1. **Pulser:** Generates a high-voltage electrical spike (100–400 V) of very short duration. This spike excites the piezo-electric crystal.
2. **Transducer (with couplant):** The PZT crystal converts the electrical spike into mechanical vibrations (ultrasound), which passes through the couplant into the specimen.
3. **Test Specimen:** The component being inspected. The wave travels through it as a longitudinal wave.
4. **Receiver (in pulser-receiver unit):** When the echo returns, the same transducer converts the mechanical vibrations back into electrical signals, which are amplified.
5. **A-Scan Display:** The echoes are displayed on the screen as vertical spikes along a horizontal time axis. The position of each echo on the time base indicates the depth of the reflector.
6. **Time Base (Sweep Generator):** Synchronizes the horizontal sweep on the screen with the pulser, so that horizontal distance = time = depth (after calibration).

**Working sequence:** Pulser fires → Ultrasound goes into material → Reflects from flaw (Flaw Echo F1) → Reflects from back wall (Back Wall Echo BWE) → Returns to transducer → Amplified → Displayed on A-scan.

---

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A 75 mm thick steel plate is tested with a 2 MHz, 25 mm diameter normal beam transducer. Given the longitudinal velocity in steel = 5920 m/s, calculate the **(i) wavelength, (ii) round-trip time for back-wall echo, (iii) near field length, and (iv) beam divergence half-angle**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $f = 2 \text{ MHz} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $D = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{5920}{2 \times 10^6} = 2.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.96 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Round-trip time:**

$$t = \frac{2T}{v} = \frac{2 \times 0.075}{5920} = \frac{0.150}{5920} = 2.534 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 25.34 \text{ μs}$$

**(iii) Near field length:**

$$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{(0.025)^2}{4 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.184 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0528 \text{ m} = 52.8 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Beam divergence half-angle:**

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} = 0.1444$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.1444) = 8.30°$$

**Final Results:**
- $\lambda = 2.96$ mm
- $t = 25.34$ μs
- $N = 52.8$ mm
- $\theta = 8.30°$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 – Alternative Choice]`

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a normal beam (straight beam) transducer** with a labeled block diagram. State the function of the backing block.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.5 block diagram):**
A normal beam transducer consists of the following parts:

1. **Connector / Housing:** Metallic body with electrical connector to the UT instrument via a coaxial cable (BNC/Lemo).
2. **Piezo-electric Crystal (PZT):** The active element, typically a disc of Lead Zirconate Titanate, which converts electrical pulses into mechanical vibrations and vice versa.
3. **Backing Block (Damping Block):** Made of a high-attenuation material (tungsten-epoxy, alumina powder in epoxy). It absorbs the sound energy radiating from the back surface of the crystal.
4. **Wear Plate (Protection Layer):** A thin protective face bonded to the front of the crystal to prevent mechanical damage.

**Working:**
- A short electrical pulse from the pulser is applied across the crystal.
- The PZT crystal resonates at its natural frequency, producing a short mechanical (ultrasonic) pulse.
- The pulse radiates forward into the couplant and the test piece as a longitudinal wave.
- Returning echoes strike the crystal, causing it to vibrate again and generate a small electrical signal, which is amplified and displayed.

**Function of the Backing Block:**
The backing block has two main functions:
- **(i)** It **damps the back-radiation** from the crystal, producing a **short, broadband pulse** with high axial resolution.
- **(ii)** It reduces the **ringing time** (Q-factor) of the crystal, ensuring the transducer is ready to receive echoes quickly.

Without the backing block, the pulse would ring for many cycles, blurring closely-spaced echoes and reducing resolution.

---

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Normal Beam Testing and Angle Beam Testing** in terms of wave mode, transducer, couplant, application, and defect detectability.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Normal Beam Testing | Angle Beam Testing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wave Mode** | Longitudinal wave only | Transverse (shear) wave, also L-wave |
| 2 | **Angle of Incidence** | 0° (perpendicular) | 30°, 45°, 60°, 70° (Snell's law applied) |
| 3 | **Transducer Type** | Flat, straight-beam | Angle-beam with wedge (Perspex/plexiglass) |
| 4 | **Mode Conversion** | No mode conversion | L → L + T (transverse) at interface |
| 5 | **Couplant** | Gel, glycerin, water | Gel, glycerin, cellulose paste |
| 6 | **Best Detects** | Laminations, delaminations, porosity parallel to surface | Cracks perpendicular to surface, weld defects |
| 7 | **Misses** | Vertical cracks | Horizontal laminations |
| 8 | **Applications** | Plate, billet, forging inspection | Weld inspection, rail testing, pipe inspection |
| 9 | **Beam Path** | Straight line down and back | Skewed (Z, V, or angle path) |
| 10 | **Calibration Block** | IIW V1 / V2 block (for angle) | IIW V1 / V2 block |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing "wavelength" and "frequency"** in numerical problems — remember, $\lambda = v/f$ (wavelength decreases as frequency increases).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in round-trip time-of-flight calculations. The pulse travels *down* and *back up*, so use $t = 2T/v$, not $T/v$.
> 3. **Mixing up near field and far field** — for sensitive detection, the test piece thickness should ideally be **less than 1.5 × N** (i.e., mostly in the near field).
> 4. **Stating the wrong reflection coefficient sign** — for steel-to-air (crack), $R$ is **negative** (phase inversion), but $|R| \approx 1$ (almost total reflection).
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — mm to m, MHz to Hz, μs to s. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per missing unit** in calculations.
> 6. **Confusing "normal beam" with "shear wave"** — normal beam produces longitudinal waves, not shear waves.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] **Normal beam = longitudinal wave** entering at 0° angle of incidence.
- [x] **No mode conversion** at the entry surface.
- [x] **Couplant is mandatory** to avoid air-gap reflection.
- [x] **Pulse-echo mode** uses the same transducer for sending and receiving.
- [x] **A-scan display** shows echoes as vertical spikes on a time base.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- [x] $v = f \lambda$ — fundamental wave relation.
- [x] $T = vt/2$ — thickness from time-of-flight.
- [x] $N = D^2/(4\lambda)$ — near field length.
- [x] $\sin\theta = 1.22\lambda/D$ — beam divergence.
- [x] $Z = \rho v$ — acoustic impedance.
- [x] $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ — reflection coefficient.

### ✅ Standard Material Velocities (memorize these)
- [x] Steel: **5920 m/s** (longitudinal)
- [x] Aluminium: **6320 m/s**
- [x] Water: **1480 m/s**
- [x] Air: **343 m/s**
- [x] Perspex/Acrylic: **2730 m/s** (used in wedges)

### ✅ Critical Numbers
- [x] Steel–air reflection ≈ **99.998%** energy reflected (R ≈ −1).
- [x] Frequency range: **1–25 MHz**.
- [x] Standard transducer sizes: **6, 10, 13, 19, 25 mm** diameter.

### ✅ Best For / Worst For
- [x] **Best:** Detecting defects **parallel to the test surface** (laminations, delaminations, slag parallel to surface).
- [x] **Worst:** Detecting **tight vertical cracks** (need angle beam).

### ✅ Equipment Checklist
- [x] Pulser-receiver unit, oscilloscope or A-scan display, normal beam transducer, couplant, calibration block (e.g., IIW V1 block for straight beam calibration), surface cleaning materials.

### ✅ Applications
- [x] Plate, sheet, billet, forging, casting, and weld inspection.
- [x] Thickness gauging (corrosion monitoring).
- [x] Material velocity and attenuation measurement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angle Beam Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Angle Beam Testing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Angle Beam Testing (ABT)** is a variant of **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** in which the ultrasonic wave is introduced into the test piece at a **refracted, non-perpendicular angle** (typically 30°, 45°, 60°, or 70° from the normal) using a **plastic or epoxy wedge** coupled to the surface. This technique is primarily employed to detect and evaluate flaws whose orientation is **parallel to the test surface** (e.g., laminations, lack of fusion, side-wall cracks in welds) and to inspect **geometries with restricted access** (pipe bends, fillet welds, T-joints). The technique is governed by **Snell's Law of Refraction** at the wedge–test piece interface and exploits the phenomenon of **mode conversion** between **longitudinal (L) waves** and **shear (transverse, T) waves**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Pool Table Bounce":** > Imagine you are rolling a marble diagonally across a long pool table toward the cushion (the wedge interface). When the marble hits the cushion, it doesn't cross straight; it **bends** and shoots off at a new angle. By adjusting the angle at which you roll the marble, you can make it travel **just under the surface** of the table (the test piece), bouncing between the top and bottom cushions. This is exactly what an **angle beam transducer** does — it shoots ultrasound into the metal at a slanted angle so that the wave **bounces (skips) inside the plate**, sweeping sideways and revealing cracks that a straight (normal beam) probe would completely miss because it would just reflect off the top surface and never "look sideways." ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard values (used universally in KTU board calculations) must be memorized: | Wave Type | Velocity in Steel (m/s) | Velocity in Plexiglas Wedge (m/s) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Longitudinal (L) | **V_L = 5900** | **V_L(PMMA) = 2730** | | Shear (T) | **V_S = 3250** | Not normally generated | | Surface (Rayleigh) | **V_R ≈ 2950** | — | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote V_L = 5900 m/s and V_S = 3250 m/s for steel unless the question explicitly gives different values.
