The velocity ratio V_S/V_L = 0.55 is a frequently tested quantity. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Pulse Generation:** A piezoelectric crystal (usually PZT) inside the probe generates a short burst of high-frequency longitudinal ultrasound, typically **2–6 MHz**. 2. **Wedge Coupling:** The crystal is mounted in a plastic wedge (Perspex/Plexiglas, Rexolite, or epoxy). The wedge's angled face is paired with the test surface, with a thin film of **couplant** (gel, oil, water) to expel air. 3. **Refraction at Interface:** At the wedge–steel boundary, **Snell's Law** redirects the beam. Depending on the incidence angle (in the wedge), the wave in steel can be: - **Longitudinal only** (incidence angle θ < θ_c1) - **Longitudinal + Shear (mode-converted)** (θ_c1 < θ < θ_c2) - **Shear only** (θ > θ_c2) - **Surface (Rayleigh) wave** (θ ≈ θ_R) 4. **V-Path Travel (Skip Path):** The shear wave travels down to the bottom surface, reflects, returns to the top — this zig-zag path is called the **V-path** or **skip path**. 5. **Flaw Interaction:** If a flaw (crack, lack of fusion, porosity, slag) lies within the swept volume, a portion of the energy reflects back along the same path. 6. **Receiving the Echo:** The reflected echo is displayed on the **A-scan CRT/LCD** at a flight time corresponding to the V-path length. ## 2.2 Critical Angles (KTU Favourite Topic) The **critical angles** are the incidence angles (measured in the wedge) at which the refracted wave in the test piece grazes along the interface (refraction angle = 90°). Applying Snell's Law: $$\frac{\sin \theta_{\text{incidence}}}{V_{\text{wedge}}} = \frac{\sin 90°}{V_{\text{steel}}}$$ - **First Critical Angle (θ_c1):** For longitudinal wave in steel - **Second Critical Angle (θ_c2):** For shear wave in steel - **Rayleigh Critical Angle (θ_R):** For surface wave in steel > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Practice:** Most commercial angle probes are designed to operate **between θ_c1 and θ_c2** (i.e., at 45°, 60°, or 70° wedge angles) so that **only a pure shear wave** propagates in the steel. This is desirable because shear waves are slower, give higher resolution, and do not mode-convert at the back wall. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Snell's Law** | $\dfrac{\sin\theta_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{V_2}$ | Refraction at wedge–steel interface | | 2 | **First Critical Angle** | $\sin\theta_{c1} = \dfrac{V_{\text{wedge}}}{V_{L,\text{steel}}}$ | Angle above which L-wave is cut off | | 3 | **Second Critical Angle** | $\sin\theta_{c2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{wedge}}}{V_{S,\text{steel}}}$ | Angle above which S-wave is cut off | | 4 | **Rayleigh Critical Angle** | $\sin\theta_{R} = \dfrac{V_{\text{wedge}}}{V_{R,\text{steel}}}$ | Angle to generate surface wave | | 5 | **Refraction Angle in Steel** | $\sin\beta_S = \dfrac{V_{S,\text{steel}}}{V_{\text{wedge}}} \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Beam angle in test piece | | 6 | **V-Path Length** | $V\text{-path} = \dfrac{2t}{\cos\beta_S}$ | Total travel through the plate | | 7 | **Skip Distance (S)** | $S = 2t \cdot \tan\beta_S$ | Surface distance between successive skips | | 8 | **Depth of Reflector (d)** | $d = \dfrac{V\text{-path}}{2} \cdot \cos\beta_S = t$ | Vertical depth from entry surface | | 9 | **Horizontal Distance (h)** | $h = \dfrac{V\text{-path}}{2} \cdot \sin\beta_S$ | Surface distance from index point to reflector | | 10 | **Half-Angle Beam Spread** | $\sin^{-1}(0.5\lambda / D)$ | Geometric beam divergence | | 11 | **Beam Skew on Curved Surface** | $\sin\gamma = \dfrac{R}{R+t}\sin\beta$ | Effective beam angle change on pipes | | 12 | **Attenuation (dB)** | $\text{dB} = 20\log_{10}\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Sensitivity calibration | **Notation used in tables:** θ → incidence angle in wedge β → refraction angle in steel t → plate thickness D → transducer crystal diameter λ → wavelength in steel R → pipe outer radius ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Weld Inspection (ASME V Article 4 / API 1104):** Detection of sidewall cracks, lack of fusion, and root porosity in butt and fillet welds. - **Pipe and Tube Inspection:** Circumferential scanning of ERW and seamless pipes (longitudinal and transverse flaws). - **Aircraft Component Testing:** Wing spars, landing gear forgings, and composite laminates. - **Pressure Vessel and Boiler Shells:** Volumetric examination during in-service inspection. - **Railroad Axles and Axle Journals:** Where access is from a single curved surface. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Angles for a Plexiglas–Steel System **Given Data (standard KTU values):** - Velocity of longitudinal wave in Plexiglas wedge: $V_L(\text{PMMA}) = 2730 \text{ m/s}$ - Velocity of longitudinal wave in steel: $V_L(\text{steel}) = 5900 \text{ m/s}$ - Velocity of shear wave in steel: $V_S(\text{steel}) = 3250 \text{ m/s}$ **Step 1:** Apply Snell's Law for the **first critical angle** (longitudinal wave grazes along steel surface, so refraction angle = 90°). $$\frac{\sin\theta_{c1}}{V_L(\text{PMMA})} = \frac{\sin 90°}{V_L(\text{steel})}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $\sin 90° = 1$ and rearrange. $$\sin\theta_{c1} = \frac{V_L(\text{PMMA})}{V_L(\text{steel})} = \frac{2730}{5900}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the numerical value. $$\sin\theta_{c1} = 0.4627$$ **Step 4:** Take the inverse sine to obtain the critical angle. $$\theta_{c1} = \sin^{-1}(0.4627) = 27.56° \approx 27.6°$$ **Step 5:** Apply the same logic for the **second critical angle** (shear wave grazes along steel surface). $$\sin\theta_{c2} = \frac{V_L(\text{PMMA})}{V_S(\text{steel})} = \frac{2730}{3250}$$ **Step 6:** Calculate the numerical value. $$\sin\theta_{c2} = 0.8400$$ **Step 7:** Take the inverse sine. $$\theta_{c2} = \sin^{-1}(0.8400) = 57.14° \approx 57.1°$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The wedge angle on commercial angle probes is **always chosen between θ_c1 and θ_c2** (i.e., between 27.6° and 57.1°), so that a **pure shear wave** is generated in the steel. Common production angles are 45°, 60°, and 70° (note: 70° exceeds θ_c2, but is widely used in practice for certain mode-conversion-free inspection of surface-breaking cracks; pure shear is dominant when β_S < 35°). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refraction Angle Inside the Steel for a 60° Wedge **Given:** - Wedge angle (incidence angle from normal) α = 60° (in Plexiglas) - $V_L(\text{PMMA}) = 2730$ m/s, $V_S(\text{steel}) = 3250$ m/s **Step 1:** Write Snell's Law relating the incidence in the wedge to refraction in the steel. $$\frac{\sin 60°}{V_L(\text{PMMA})} = \frac{\sin\beta_S}{V_S(\text{steel})}$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $\sin\beta_S$. $$\sin\beta_S = \frac{V_S(\text{steel})}{V_L(\text{PMMA})} \cdot \sin 60°$$ **Step 3:** Substitute numerical values. $$\sin\beta_S = \frac{3250}{2730} \cdot \sin 60° = 1.1905 \cdot 0.8660 = 1.031$$ **Step 4:** Interpretation — since $\sin\beta_S > 1$, the shear wave **cannot exist** in the steel at this incidence angle. Therefore, the 60° Plexiglas wedge does **not** generate a shear wave in steel; it produces only a surface (Rayleigh) wave. > [!NOTE] > **Correction for KTU boards:** The wedge angle that produces a 45° shear wave in steel is found by setting β_S = 45° and back-calculating α. This explains why **commercial 45° probes have a wedge angle much smaller than 45°** (typically around 21°). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — V-Path, Skip Distance, and Depth in a Plate Consider a **60° shear-wave probe** (refraction angle in steel β_S = 60°) scanning a plate of thickness t = 25 mm. **Step 1:** Geometry of the V-path — the wave travels at angle β_S to the normal, hits the bottom surface, and reflects. $$V\text{-path} = \frac{2t}{\cos\beta_S} = \frac{2 \cdot 25}{\cos 60°} = \frac{50}{0.5} = 100 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Skip distance (the distance along the top surface from the index point to the first leg of the V). $$S = 2t \cdot \tan\beta_S = 2 \cdot 25 \cdot \tan 60° = 50 \cdot 1.732 = 86.6 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3:** Depth of a hypothetical reflector (lying on the bottom surface) below the entry point. $$d_{\text{reflector}} = \frac{V\text{-path}}{2} \cdot \cos\beta_S = \frac{100}{2} \cdot 0.5 = 25 \text{ mm} = t$$ This confirms the trivial result that the bottom surface is at depth t. **Step 4:** For a flaw located at the **mid-thickness (12.5 mm)** of the same plate, the V-path becomes: $$V\text{-path}_{\text{flaw}} = \frac{2 \cdot 12.5}{\cos 60°} = \frac{25}{0.5} = 50 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5:** Horizontal surface distance from the index point to the flaw: $$h = \frac{V\text{-path}_{\text{flaw}}}{2} \cdot \sin\beta_S = \frac{50}{2} \cdot \sin 60° = 25 \cdot 0.866 = 21.65 \text{ mm}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Beam Skew on a Curved (Pipe) Surface When a flat-faced probe is used on a curved surface, the effective angle of incidence changes. Let the pipe outer radius be R, and the plate (wall) thickness be t. **Step 1:** Apply the refraction relationship for a curved surface. $$\sin\beta_{\text{eff}} = \frac{R}{R+t} \cdot \sin\beta_{\text{flat}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute R = 100 mm, t = 20 mm, β_flat = 60°. $$\sin\beta_{\text{eff}} = \frac{100}{100+20} \cdot \sin 60° = 0.8333 \cdot 0.8660 = 0.7217$$ **Step 3:** Determine the new effective refraction angle. $$\beta_{\text{eff}} = \sin^{-1}(0.7217) = 46.17°$$ > [!WARNING] > **This is why a 60° probe does NOT give a 60° beam in a thin pipe wall.** Beam skew can be tens of degrees on small-diameter thin-wall tubes, and **the index point also shifts** (it is not on the centre of the wedge). Calibration on a curved calibration block (e.g., DSC block) is mandatory for pipe inspection. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Distance Amplitude Correction (DAC) Concept **Step 1:** The amplitude of an echo from a flat-bottomed hole (FBH) of the same diameter decreases with distance due to beam spread and material attenuation. **Step 2:** The loss in dB between two distances $x_1$ and $x_2$ is given by: $$\Delta\text{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right) + \alpha(x_2 - x_1)$$ where α is the **attenuation coefficient in dB/mm** (for steel at 4 MHz, α ≈ 0.002 dB/mm is typical). **Step 3:** For a 6 dB drop criterion, the distance ratio $x_2/x_1$ must equal: $$6 = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{x_2}{x_1} = 10^{6/20} = 2$$ **Step 4:** This means a 6 dB drop occurs when the **distance doubles** (for a 3 mm FBH reference in steel at 4 MHz) — a classic KTU board result. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Angle Beam Transducer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulse Generator
Pulser-Receiver] --> B[Piezoelectric Crystal
PZT 2-6 MHz] B --> C[Plastic Wedge
Plexiglas or Epoxy] C --> D[Couplant Layer
Gel or Oil] D --> E[Test Piece
Steel Plate] E -- V-Path Travel --> F[Reflector Flaw] F -- Echo Return --> E E --> D D --> C C --> B B --> A A --> G[A-Scan Display
CRT or LCD] G --> H{Analyst Decision
Accept / Reject} ``` ## 4.2 Schematic Flow — Beam Path Inside a Plate (V-Path Geometry) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Entry Point on Top Surface] --> P2[Refracted Shear Wave
at angle beta-S] P2 --> P3[Hits Bottom Surface
at depth t] P3 --> P4[Reflects to Top Surface] P4 --> P5[Returns to Transducer] P5 --> P6[Echo on A-Scan at
V-path flight time] subgraph SKIP_GEOMETRY [Skip Distance Geometry] P2 -.-> Q1[Horizontal skip distance S = 2t tan beta-S] P3 -.-> Q2[Vertical depth = t] end ``` ## 4.3 Mode Conversion Decision Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Wedge Incidence Angle alpha] --> check1{Is alpha less than
theta-c1 equals 27.6 deg?} check1 -- YES --> modeA[Pure Longitudinal Wave in Steel] check1 -- NO --> check2{Is alpha between
theta-c1 and theta-c2?} check2 -- YES --> modeB[Dual Mode: Longitudinal plus Shear
Energy splits at interface] check2 -- NO --> check3{Is alpha greater than
theta-c2 equals 57.1 deg?} check3 -- YES --> modeC[Pure Shear Wave in Steel
Most Common in Practice] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — ABT Inspection Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Select Probe
45 60 or 70 deg] --> S2[Step 2: Choose Calibration Block
IIW V1 or DSC] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Set Index Point
and Sensitivity] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Scan Weld or Component
with couplant] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Record Flaw Echoes
Distance and Amplitude] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compare with DAC Curve
or Reference Level] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Evaluate against
Acceptance Standard] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Mark and Report Defects] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each flowchart cell represents a **functional block** in the angle beam inspection chain. The V-path geometry block makes clear that a single transducer placement yields echoes from a line of reflectors beneath the surface — a fundamentally different mapping from a normal-beam probe. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Q1.** Define **angle beam testing**. Why is it preferred over normal beam testing for weld inspection? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Angle beam testing is an ultrasonic NDT method in which ultrasound is introduced into the test material at a refracted, oblique angle (commonly 45°, 60°, or 70°) using a **wedge**, so that the beam travels sideways within the material. - **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** Normal beam (0°) ultrasound reflects specularly off planar cracks parallel to the surface and produces no echo; angle beam shear waves strike such cracks at 90° and produce strong reflections. - **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** It allows inspection of welds from a single accessible side and can detect sidewall cracks, root cracks, and lack of fusion. --- **Q2.** What are the **first and second critical angles**? State their values for a Plexiglas–steel system. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The critical angle is the incidence angle in the wedge at which the refracted wave grazes along the wedge–steel interface (refraction angle = 90°). - **First critical angle (1 mark):** $\theta_{c1} = \sin^{-1}(2730/5900) = 27.6°$. Above this, longitudinal wave cannot propagate in steel. - **Second critical angle (1 mark):** $\theta_{c2} = \sin^{-1}(2730/3250) = 57.1°$. Above this, only shear wave exists in steel. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle of angle beam testing** and the phenomenon of **mode conversion**. Derive the expressions for the **first and second critical angles** for a Plexiglas–steel interface. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Sketch (2 marks):** Show the wedge, crystal, refracted L and S waves inside the steel, and the V-path bouncing between top and bottom surfaces. The mode-converted shear wave should be clearly labelled at an angle β_S to the normal. 2. **Principle (2 marks):** The piezoelectric crystal emits a longitudinal pulse. The pulse refracts at the Plexiglas–steel boundary per Snell's Law. For wedge angles between θ_c1 and θ_c2, mode conversion produces a **shear wave** in the steel, which travels in a V-path and detects flaws oriented parallel to the surface. 3. **Mode conversion (1 mark):** A longitudinal wave striking the boundary at an oblique angle partially converts into a **shear (transverse) wave** in the second medium, because shear waves can exist in solids. The energy splits in a ratio governed by acoustic impedances and the angle of incidence. 4. **Derivation of θ_c1 and θ_c2 (2 marks):** - From Snell's Law, with refraction angle = 90°: $\sin\theta_{c1} = V_{\text{PMMA}} / V_{L,\text{steel}} = 2730/5900$, giving θ_c1 = 27.6°. - $\sin\theta_{c2} = V_{\text{PMMA}} / V_{S,\text{steel}} = 2730/3250$, giving θ_c2 = 57.1°. --- **(b)** A **60° wedge-angle probe** is used on a 30 mm thick steel plate. The probe index point coincides with the leading edge of the wedge. Calculate **(i)** the V-path length, **(ii)** the skip distance, and **(iii)** the time-of-flight of the second back-wall echo. Use V_S = 3250 m/s and assume β_S = 60° in steel. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** t = 30 mm, β_S = 60°, V_S = 3250 m/s = 3.25 mm/μs. **(i) V-path length (2 marks):** $$V\text{-path} = \frac{2t}{\cos\beta_S} = \frac{2 \cdot 30}{\cos 60°} = \frac{60}{0.5} = 120 \text{ mm}$$ **(ii) Skip distance (2 marks):** $$S = 2t \tan\beta_S = 2 \cdot 30 \cdot \tan 60° = 60 \cdot 1.732 = 103.92 \text{ mm}$$ **(iii) Time-of-flight of the 2nd back-wall echo (3 marks):** Each V-path is 120 mm, so the 2nd back-wall echo travels 2 V-paths = 240 mm. $$T = \frac{240 \text{ mm}}{3.25 \text{ mm/}\mu s} = 73.85 \text{ μs}$$ --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **(a)** Explain the **IIW V1 calibration block** and the **DSC (Distance-Sensitivity-Correction) block**. How is the **index point** and **sensitivity** of an angle beam probe determined using these blocks? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **IIW V1 Block (2 marks):** The IIW V1 (International Institute of Welding, Type 1) block is a small reference block of known thickness (typically 25 mm) with a 50 mm radius curved surface and a 1.5 mm diameter side-drilled hole. It is used to verify the **beam angle** and the **index point** of an angle beam probe. 2. **DSC Block (2 marks):** The DSC block contains a series of side-drilled holes or flat-bottomed holes at different depths and distances. It is used to plot the **Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC) curve**, which compensates for the natural decrease in echo amplitude with distance. 3. **Index Point Determination (1.5 marks):** The probe is moved along the IIW V1 block until the maximum echo from the 1.5 mm SDH is obtained. The point on the probe face directly above this hole is the **index point** (usually marked on the wedge). 4. **Sensitivity Setting (1.5 marks):** With the probe on the SDH at a known depth, the gain is adjusted to bring the echo peak to a fixed reference height (e.g., 80% of full screen height). This becomes the **reference sensitivity** for subsequent scans. --- **(b)** A **45° shear-wave probe** scans a plate of thickness 40 mm. A flaw is detected when the probe index point is **35 mm** away from the leading edge of the wedge, and the echo appears at a **V-path of 90 mm**. Calculate the **depth and horizontal distance** of the flaw from the index point. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** β_S = 45°, V-path = 90 mm. **Step 1: Depth of the flaw (3 marks):** $$d = \frac{V\text{-path}}{2} \cdot \cos\beta_S = \frac{90}{2} \cdot \cos 45° = 45 \cdot 0.7071 = 31.82 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Horizontal distance from index point (2 marks):** $$h = \frac{V\text{-path}}{2} \cdot \sin\beta_S = \frac{90}{2} \cdot \sin 45° = 45 \cdot 0.7071 = 31.82 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Verification with the surface position of 35 mm (2 marks):** The horizontal distance h = 31.82 mm is **less than** the 35 mm index-point-to-leading-edge distance, indicating the flaw lies within the plate (depth 31.82 mm < 40 mm). Hence the flaw is geometrically valid. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing wedge angle α with refraction angle β_S.** The wedge angle is in Plexiglas; the beam angle in steel is a different value. Many students write "60° probe" when they mean β_S = 60° — verify which medium the angle refers to. > 2. **Forgetting to double for V-path.** A single "bounce" in the plate corresponds to a 2t thickness travel, not 1t. > 3. **Using V_L instead of V_S in time-of-flight.** Once a pure shear wave is generated, use 3250 m/s, not 5900 m/s. > 4. **Neglecting beam skew on curved surfaces.** On pipes, the effective beam angle is *not* the nominal wedge angle — always apply the curvature correction formula. > 5. **Mixing up index point and leading edge.** The index point is a specific reference marker on the probe; the leading edge is the front face of the wedge. These are not the same on most probes. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Angle Beam Testing:** - **Definition:** Ultrasonic testing at a non-perpendicular refracted angle using a wedge; detects flaws parallel to the surface. - **Standard velocities (steel):** V_L = 5900 m/s; V_S = 3250 m/s; V_R ≈ 2950 m/s. Wedge: V_L(PMMA) = 2730 m/s. - **Snell's Law** governs refraction: $\sin\theta_1 / V_1 = \sin\theta_2 / V_2$. - **Critical angles for Plexiglas–steel:** θ_c1 = 27.6°, θ_c2 = 57.1°, θ_R ≈ 67.7°. - **Mode conversion** occurs for θ_c1 < α < θ_c2; pure shear wave is desired for most inspections. - **Common probe angles:** 45°, 60°, 70° — all generate shear waves in steel. - **V-path length:** $V = 2t / \cos\beta_S$. **Skip distance:** $S = 2t \tan\beta_S$. - **Depth and horizontal distance** of a flaw can be calculated from the V-path and β_S. - **Beam skew** on curved surfaces: $\sin\beta_{\text{eff}} = [R/(R+t)] \sin\beta_{\text{flat}}$. - **Calibration blocks:** IIW V1 (index point & angle), DSC block (DAC curve). - **TR probes** (separate transmit & receive crystals) reduce near-surface noise; SE probes (single crystal) are simpler. - **Couplants** (gel, oil, water, paste) are mandatory to exclude air at the wedge–test piece interface. - **Sensitivity** is set using reference reflectors (SDH or FBH) and adjusted with dB pads. - **Surface waves (Rayleigh)** travel along the surface and detect tight surface cracks; generated when α ≈ θ_R. - **TOFD (Time of Flight Diffraction)** is a related but distinct technique using diffraction from crack tips.

topics.title: Dual Crystal Probe
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Dual Crystal Probe — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dual Crystal Probe

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dual Crystal Probe** (also called a **Transmit-Receive (TR) Probe** or **TR Probe**) is a specialized ultrasonic transducer that contains **two piezoelectric crystals** housed within a single probe case — one crystal dedicated to **transmitting** the ultrasonic pulse and the other dedicated to **receiving** the reflected echo. A critical mechanical-acoustic component called an **acoustic barrier** (also called a "delay wedge" or "sound damper") is placed between the two crystals to prevent the transmit signal from directly coupling into the receive crystal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> A Dual Crystal Probe is a contact-type ultrasonic probe that uses separate transmitter and receiver elements with an acoustic isolator in between, designed primarily to overcome the **dead zone / near-surface resolution** problem associated with single-crystal (normal) probes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍎 The "Two-Ear Apple Test" Analogy**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to determine if a freshly bitten apple is fresh or rotten — but you can only tap it ONCE and listen. If you tap with the *same hand* that is also holding the apple, the tapping sound is masked by the squishy noise of your own fingers, and you might miss the subtle difference. However, if you tap with your **left hand** and listen with your **right hand**, you get a clean "tap" and a clean "listen" — a much clearer signal.
>
> A **Dual Crystal Probe** works exactly like this. In a normal single-crystal probe, the same crystal is asked to "tap" (transmit) and "listen" (receive), creating a noisy "ringing" period called the **dead zone** during which near-surface defects are invisible. The Dual Crystal Probe has a dedicated **"tapper"** (transmitter) and a dedicated **"listener"** (receiver), separated by a barrier that prevents the tapping noise from reaching the listener.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Operating Frequency Range:** Typically **1 MHz to 10 MHz** (commonly **2.25 MHz and 5 MHz** in industrial applications)
- **Typical Probe Diameter:** **10 mm to 25 mm**
- **Standard Beam Angle in Test Material:** The acoustic barrier creates a fixed **V-shaped (or X-shaped)** sound path; the crossing angle is fixed by probe design.
- **Focal Point / Intersection Point:** Determined by geometry — the point where transmit beam and receive beam cross.
- **Sound Velocity Standards (at 25°C):**
  - Steel: **5920 m/s**
  - Aluminium: **6320 m/s**
  - Water: **1480 m/s**

## 1.4 Classification of Dual Crystal Probes

| Type | Description | Common Use |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **Contact Type TR Probe** | Sits directly on the test surface; uses a small delay line | Plate, billet, thickness gauging |
| **Immersion Type TR Probe** | Operates submerged in water column | High-speed scanning, curved surfaces |
| **Angle-Beam TR Probe** | Uses wedges to introduce a shear/refracted beam | Weld inspection |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction & Working Principle

The Dual Crystal Probe consists of the following structured elements:

1. **Transmitter Crystal (T)** — A piezoelectric element that converts electrical pulses from the flaw detector into mechanical (ultrasonic) vibrations. Typically damped to produce a **short pulse** for high near-surface resolution.
2. **Receiver Crystal (R)** — A second piezoelectric element optimized to detect the returning echoes with high sensitivity. Usually has a slightly different resonance or is matched for reception only.
3. **Acoustic Barrier / Isolator** — A physical, highly attenuating block (often a dense, lossy material) placed between T and R. Its job is to absorb any direct acoustic leakage from T to R along the probe body.
4. **Delay Line / Wear Plate** (optional) — A solid wedge (often Perspex / acrylic / Rexolite) that introduces a known time delay so the initial receiver gating can be extended, allowing inspection to start *very close* to the surface.
5. **Common Housing** — Both crystals sit in a single ergonomic case, wired to the flaw detector via a **T-R switch** or directly via a TR cable.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is a Dual Crystal Probe Used?**
> To solve the **Dead Zone Problem** of normal (single-element) probes, and to enable inspection of **thin sections, near-surface defects, and corroded back-walls**.

**Step 1 — Pulser Activation:**
The flaw detector sends a high-voltage spike (typically 100–400 V) to the **transmitter crystal (T)**.

**Step 2 — Transmission into the Test Piece:**
The transmitter emits an ultrasonic pulse that travels through the delay line (if any) and enters the test material at a fixed angle dictated by the probe geometry.

**Step 3 — Sound Field Geometry:**
The transmitted beam travels at a fixed angle θ_T from the normal, and the receiver "listens" along a fixed angle θ_R. The two beams **intersect at a focal crossover point (F)** in the material — this forms a characteristic **"X" or "V" sound field**.

**Step 4 — Echo Reception:**
Any reflector (flaw, back wall, lamination) within the overlap region scatters ultrasonic energy. A portion of this scattered energy returns along the receive path to the **receiver crystal (R)**, which is electrically and acoustically isolated from the transmitter.

**Step 5 — Display & Interpretation:**
The receiver converts the mechanical echo back into a voltage, which is amplified, gated, and displayed on the flaw detector screen as an A-scan signal.

**Step 6 — The Acoustic Barrier's Role:**
Without the barrier, the loud transmit pulse would "leak" around the housing and saturate the receiver, creating a giant false signal and a long dead zone. The barrier **traps this energy inside the probe body**, ensuring clean transmit-receive separation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formulas for Dual Crystal Probe Analysis**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $V = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation linking velocity, frequency, wavelength | $V$ = velocity (m/s), $f$ = frequency (Hz), $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) |
| 2 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}} \cdot \sin\alpha}{V_{\text{wedge}}}$ | Snell's Law (refraction at delay line) | $\theta$ = refraction angle, $\alpha$ = incidence angle |
| 3 | $D_d = \dfrac{V \cdot t_d}{2}$ | Depth of a defect from a single echo | $D_d$ = defect depth, $t_d$ = echo time, $V$ = velocity |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{V \cdot t_b}{2}$ | Thickness from back-wall echo | $T$ = thickness, $t_b$ = back-wall time |
| 5 | $d_{\text{min}} \approx \dfrac{V}{2 \cdot f}$ | Approximate minimum resolvable defect depth (lower limit) | $d_{\text{min}}$ = minimum resolvable depth |
| 6 | $X_f = \dfrac{D}{\tan\theta_T + \tan\theta_R}$ | Crossover (focal) depth of TR probe sound field | $X_f$ = focal depth, $D$ = crystal separation, $\theta$ = beam angles |
| 7 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{V}$ | Round-trip time for a defect at depth $d$ | $\Delta t$ = time-of-flight, $d$ = depth |
| 8 | $\text{Resolution} \propto \dfrac{1}{f \cdot BW}$ | Resolution is inversely proportional to frequency-bandwidth product | $BW$ = bandwidth of the crystal |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dual Crystal Probes are the **workhorse inspection tool** in the following critical applications:

- **Aerospace Industry:** Inspection of thin aluminum wing skins, turbine blade leading edges, and composite laminates.
- **Petrochemical / Pipeline Inspection:** Corrosion mapping on pipe walls (typically 4–25 mm thick) where back-wall echoes are faint and near-surface pitting must be detected.
- **Railway Axle & Wheel Inspection:** Detecting sub-surface fatigue cracks initiated at the surface.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Inspection of thin-walled heat exchanger tubes.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Thickness gauging of sheet metal, glass, and polymer panels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Focal Crossover Depth ($X_f$) of a Dual Crystal Probe

The geometry of a TR probe is such that the transmit beam leaves one crystal at angle $\theta_T$ and the receive beam enters the other crystal at angle $\theta_R$. The two beams **intersect at a focal point** at a depth $X_f$ below the probe face.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**
Let the horizontal separation between the two crystal centers be $D$ (along the probe face). The transmit beam travels downward and to the right at angle $\theta_T$, while the receive beam travels downward and to the left at angle $\theta_R$, meeting at the crossover point F.

**Step 2 — Express Horizontal Distances:**
From the transmit crystal to directly below F:
$$D_T = X_f \cdot \tan\theta_T$$

From the receive crystal to directly below F:
$$D_R = X_f \cdot \tan\theta_R$$

**Step 3 — Apply Constraint $D_T + D_R = D$:**
$$X_f \cdot \tan\theta_T + X_f \cdot \tan\theta_R = D$$

$$X_f \cdot (\tan\theta_T + \tan\theta_R) = D$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X_f$:**
$$X_f = \dfrac{D}{\tan\theta_T + \tan\theta_R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The focal point is **closer to the surface** when the angles are steep (e.g., $\theta = 45°$) and **deeper** when the angles are shallow. Probe designers choose $D$ and $\theta$ based on the inspection depth needed.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Given $D = 20$ mm, $\theta_T = \theta_R = 30°$:

$$X_f = \dfrac{20 \text{ mm}}{\tan 30° + \tan 30°} = \dfrac{20}{0.5774 + 0.5774} = \dfrac{20}{1.1547}$$

$$X_f \approx 17.32 \text{ mm}$$

So the focal crossover sits at a depth of about **17.3 mm** below the surface.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dead Zone Reduction Using a Delay Line

**Step 1 — The Dead Zone in a Single Probe:**
In a normal contact probe, the crystal "rings" for a duration $t_r$ after excitation. During this time, any returning echo is masked. The dead zone depth $D_{dz}$ is given by:
$$D_{dz} = \dfrac{V_{\text{material}} \cdot t_r}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Insert a Delay Line of Thickness $L_d$ and Velocity $V_d$:**
The transmit pulse must first travel through the delay line, taking time:
$$t_d = \dfrac{L_d}{V_d}$$

By the time the pulse enters the test material, the crystal has already stopped ringing. Any surface echo (from the test piece surface) takes a finite time to return through the delay line, and any near-surface flaw echo at depth $d$ returns through the delay line too.

**Step 3 — Time of Flight for a Near-Surface Flaw at Depth $d$:**
$$t_{\text{flaw}} = \dfrac{L_d}{V_d} + \dfrac{d}{V_m} + \dfrac{L_d}{V_d} + \dfrac{d}{V_m} = \dfrac{2L_d}{V_d} + \dfrac{2d}{V_m}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for Flaw to Be Detectable (not masked by initial pulse):**
$$\dfrac{2L_d}{V_d} > t_r$$

If this condition is met, the initial transmit pulse has decayed before the first returning echo arrives at the receiver, **eliminating the dead zone**.

**Step 5 — Effective Near-Surface Resolution:**
With a dual crystal probe and a properly chosen delay line, **near-surface defects as shallow as 1–2 mm from the surface** can be reliably detected — something a normal probe cannot achieve.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single vs. Dual Crystal Probe

| Parameter | Single Crystal (Normal) Probe | Dual Crystal (TR) Probe |
|-----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Number of crystals** | 1 (acts as both T and R) | 2 (separate T and R) |
| **Acoustic barrier** | Not required | Essential |
| **Sound field** | Concentrated, single-beam | V or X-shaped overlap region |
| **Dead zone** | Significant (3–10 mm typical) | Greatly reduced (≤ 1 mm with delay line) |
| **Near-surface defect detection** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Thin-section inspection** | Difficult | Ideal |
| **Couplant requirement** | Yes (between probe and surface) | Yes |
| **Pulse-echo operation** | Yes | No (uses through-transmission or pitch-catch) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Sensitivity to small flaws** | Moderate | Very high (focused overlap region) |
| **Typical applications** | General flaw detection, large forgings | Thin plates, corrosion, surface cracks |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Approximate Minimum Resolvable Defect Depth

The minimum flaw depth $d_{\text{min}}$ that can be detected is fundamentally limited by the **pulse length** in the material, which in turn depends on the **frequency** $f$ and the **damping** of the crystal.

**Step 1 — Spatial Pulse Length in the Material:**
$$L_p = n \cdot \lambda = n \cdot \dfrac{V}{f}$$
where $n$ is the number of cycles in the pulse (typically $n \approx 1$ to $3$ for a well-damped probe).

**Step 2 — Two echoes are resolvable only if separated by at least one pulse length:**
Two defects at depths $d_1$ and $d_2$ are resolvable if:
$$2 \cdot \vert d_2 - d_1 \vert \geq L_p$$

**Step 3 — For a single small defect to produce a distinct echo, its depth must exceed half a pulse length:**
$$d_{\text{min}} \approx \dfrac{L_p}{2} = \dfrac{n \cdot V}{2 f}$$

**Step 4 — For $n = 1$, $V = 5920$ m/s (steel), $f = 5$ MHz:**
$$d_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 5920}{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{5920}{10^7}$$

$$d_{\text{min}} = 5.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.592 \text{ mm}$$

So with a **5 MHz dual crystal probe**, defects as small as **~0.6 mm** in steel can be resolved.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dual Crystal Probe System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords used, labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLAN DETECTOR PULSER UNIT] --> B[T R SWITCH AND TIMING CONTROL]
    B --> C[TRANSMITTER CRYSTAL PIEZOELECTRIC ELEMENT]
    C --> D[ACOUSTIC BARRIER ISOLATION BLOCK]
    D --> E[RECEIVER CRYSTAL PIEZOELECTRIC ELEMENT]
    E --> F[AMPLIFIER AND SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    F --> G[FLAN DETECTOR DISPLAY A SCAN]
    H[COUPLANT LAYER] --> C
    H --> E
    I[DELAY LINE WEDGE] --> H
    J[TEST MATERIAL SPECIMEN] --> H
    C --> K[ULTRASONIC BEAM TRANSMIT PATH]
    K --> L[DEFECT OR BACK WALL REFLECTOR]
    L --> M[ULTRASONIC BEAM RECEIVE PATH]
    M --> E
    D -.BLOCKS DIRECT ACOUSTIC COUPLING BETWEEN T AND R.-> C
    D -.BLOCKS DIRECT ACOUSTIC COUPLING BETWEEN T AND R.-> E
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Sound Field Geometry — V-Shaped (X-Shaped) Overlap Region

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[TRANSMITTER CRYSTAL T] -->|BEAM PATH AT ANGLE THETA T| F[FOCAL CROSSOVER POINT]
    P2[RECEIVER CRYSTAL R] -->|BEAM PATH AT ANGLE THETA R| F
    F --> BW[BACK WALL OF TEST MATERIAL]
    F --> DF[NEAR SURFACE DEFECT IN OVERLAP ZONE]
    S1[PROBE HOUSING FACE] --> P1
    S1 --> P2
    M1[TEST MATERIAL MEDIUM] --> DF
    M1 --> BW
    style F fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:3px
    style DF fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow & Functional Stages

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[STAGE 1 EXCITATION] --> B[STAGE 2 TRANSMISSION]
    B --> C[STAGE 3 PROPAGATION IN MATERIAL]
    C --> D[STAGE 4 REFLECTION AT DEFECT]
    D --> E[STAGE 5 RECEPTION AT R CRYSTAL]
    E --> F[STAGE 6 AMPLIFICATION]
    F --> G[STAGE 7 DISPLAY AND INTERPRETATION]
    style A fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Dead Zone Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    N[SURFACE OF TEST MATERIAL] --> DZ1[DEAD ZONE NORMAL PROBE 3 TO 10 MM]
    N --> DZ2[DEAD ZONE DUAL CRYSTAL PROBE LESS THAN 1 MM WITH DELAY LINE]
    DZ1 --> S1[FLAWS IN THIS REGION ARE MASKED]
    DZ2 --> S2[FLAWS IN THIS REGION ARE DETECTABLE]
    style DZ1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style DZ2 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style S2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define a Dual Crystal Probe and state its primary advantage over a normal single-crystal probe.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Dual Crystal Probe (Transmit-Receive Probe)** is an ultrasonic transducer that contains two separate piezoelectric crystals — one for **transmission** and the other for **reception** — housed in a single case and separated by an **acoustic barrier**.

**Primary Advantage:** It **drastically reduces the dead zone**, enabling detection of **near-surface defects** and inspection of **thin sections** that a normal probe cannot resolve. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is the function of the acoustic barrier in a Dual Crystal Probe?**

**Model Answer:**
The **acoustic barrier** (or sound damper) is a highly attenuating block placed between the transmitter and receiver crystals. Its function is to **absorb and prevent any direct acoustic leakage** of the transmit pulse from reaching the receiver crystal, thereby:
1. Eliminating false cross-talk signals.
2. Reducing the **dead zone**.
3. Improving the **signal-to-noise ratio** for near-surface echoes. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a Dual Crystal Probe. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **focal crossover depth** $X_f$ of a Dual Crystal Probe and compute its value for $D = 25$ mm, $\theta_T = \theta_R = 45°$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction & Working:**

A Dual Crystal Probe (see figure below) consists of the following components:

1. **Transmitter Crystal (T):** A piezoelectric disc that converts the high-voltage pulse from the flaw detector into a mechanical ultrasonic wave. Damped to produce a short pulse for high resolution.
2. **Receiver Crystal (R):** A second piezoelectric disc optimized for high sensitivity in receiving returning echoes.
3. **Acoustic Barrier:** A lossy, dense material (e.g., cork-rubber composite) placed between T and R to prevent direct acoustic cross-talk.
4. **Delay Line (optional):** A solid wedge of known thickness and velocity (Perspex / Rexolite) to provide a time delay and reduce the dead zone.
5. **Common Housing & Cable:** Both crystals are wired via a TR cable; the cable may carry both T and R signals, or use a T-R switch.

**Working Principle:**
- The flaw detector's pulser excites the **transmitter (T)**, which emits an ultrasonic beam into the test material at a fixed angle.
- The beam propagates, hits a reflector (defect or back wall), and a portion of the energy scatters back along a separate path to the **receiver (R)**.
- The receiver converts this mechanical echo into an electrical signal, which is amplified and displayed.
- The acoustic barrier ensures the loud transmit pulse does not directly reach the receiver, enabling **clean separation of transmit and receive events** and a **much smaller dead zone**. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Let $D$ be the horizontal separation between the two crystal centers. The transmit beam leaves T at angle $\theta_T$ and the receive beam enters R at angle $\theta_R$, crossing at focal point F at depth $X_f$.

**Step 2:** Horizontal distance from T to point directly below F:
$$D_T = X_f \cdot \tan\theta_T$$

**Step 3:** Horizontal distance from R to point directly below F:
$$D_R = X_f \cdot \tan\theta_R$$

**Step 4:** Geometric constraint:
$$D_T + D_R = D \implies X_f \cdot \tan\theta_T + X_f \cdot \tan\theta_R = D$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $X_f$:
$$X_f = \dfrac{D}{\tan\theta_T + \tan\theta_R}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Computation:**
Given $D = 25$ mm, $\theta_T = \theta_R = 45°$:
$$X_f = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{\tan 45° + \tan 45°} = \dfrac{25}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{25}{2}$$

$$\boxed{X_f = 12.5 \text{ mm}}$$

The focal crossover point lies at a depth of **12.5 mm** below the probe face. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — ALTERNATIVE**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **dead zone problem** in a normal ultrasonic probe and describe how a Dual Crystal Probe with a delay line overcomes it. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 5 MHz Dual Crystal Probe is used to inspect a steel plate (V = 5920 m/s). Calculate the **approximate minimum resolvable defect depth** if the pulse has 1.5 cycles. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Dead Zone & Delay Line:**

**Dead Zone in Normal Probe:**
After excitation, the piezoelectric crystal continues to "ring" (vibrate) for a finite time $t_r$. During this period, the receiver cannot distinguish returning echoes from the residual vibration. The **dead zone depth** is:
$$D_{dz} = \dfrac{V \cdot t_r}{2}$$
This typically ranges from 3 to 10 mm, making near-surface defects invisible.

**How Dual Crystal Probe with Delay Line Solves It:**
- A **delay line** (a solid wedge of thickness $L_d$ and velocity $V_d$) is interposed between the crystal and the test surface.
- The transmit pulse must traverse the delay line, taking time $t_d = L_d / V_d$.
- By the time the pulse enters the test material, the crystal has stopped ringing.
- Any near-surface echo returns through the delay line and arrives at the receiver **after** the ringing has decayed.
- Additionally, the **acoustic barrier** between the transmit and receive crystals prevents direct electrical/acoustic cross-talk, further reducing the dead zone.
- Net effect: **near-surface defects as shallow as 1–2 mm** can be reliably detected. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Minimum Resolvable Defect Depth:**

**Step 1 — Formula for spatial pulse length:**
$$L_p = n \cdot \lambda = n \cdot \dfrac{V}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting values:**
$$L_p = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{5920 \text{ m/s}}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 1.5 \cdot 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$L_p = 1.776 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.776 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum resolvable defect depth (half pulse length):**
$$d_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{L_p}{2} = \dfrac{1.776}{2}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{min}} \approx 0.888 \text{ mm} \approx 0.89 \text{ mm}}$$

So a defect of approximately **0.89 mm** in depth is the lower detection limit for this configuration. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing TR Probe with Dual-Element Probe terminology:** Some students incorrectly use "transducer" and "probe" interchangeably. Remember: a **probe** includes the housing, delay line, cable, etc., while a **crystal/transducer** is just the piezoelectric element.
> 2. **Forgetting the acoustic barrier in diagrams:** When asked for a sketch, **always draw and label the acoustic barrier** between the two crystals. Examiners specifically test for this.
> 3. **Units error in $X_f$ derivation:** Students often forget to keep $D$ and $X_f$ in the **same units** (both in mm or both in m). A common mistake is mixing mm and m, leading to a 1000× error.
> 4. **Mixing up pitch-catch and pulse-echo:** A Dual Crystal Probe operates in **pitch-catch (or through-transmission) mode**, not pulse-echo. Do not describe a single crystal acting as both T and R.
> 5. **Skipping the delay line explanation:** When the question asks about dead zone reduction, you **must** mention the delay line — it is the second key mechanism (the first being the acoustic barrier).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Dual Crystal Probe**

- ✅ **Two crystals:** One **transmit (T)**, one **receive (R)**, housed in a single case.
- ✅ **Acoustic barrier is mandatory** — it blocks direct T-to-R cross-talk.
- ✅ **Primary purpose:** Eliminate the **dead zone** and detect **near-surface defects**.
- ✅ **Sound field is V or X-shaped** with a **focal crossover point** at depth $X_f$.
- ✅ **Focal depth formula:** $X_f = \dfrac{D}{\tan\theta_T + \tan\theta_R}$
- ✅ **Delay line** is an optional but very common feature that further reduces the dead zone by introducing a time delay $t_d = L_d / V_d$.
- ✅ **Operating mode:** **Pitch-catch** (not pulse-echo).
- ✅ **Frequencies commonly used:** 2.25 MHz, 5 MHz, 10 MHz.
- ✅ **Best suited for:** Thin plates, corrosion mapping, surface-breaking cracks, near-surface laminations.
- ✅ **Key formula — minimum resolvable defect:** $d_{\text{min}} \approx \dfrac{n \cdot V}{2 f}$
- ✅ **Standard velocities to remember (25°C):** Steel = 5920 m/s, Aluminium = 6320 m/s, Water = 1480 m/s, Perspex (delay line) ≈ 2730 m/s.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Higher cost, more complex electronics (T-R switch or dual channel), sensitive to couplant condition.
- ✅ **KEEP THIS DISTINCT:** *Single crystal probe* uses one crystal in pulse-echo; *Dual crystal probe* uses two crystals in pitch-catch.
- ✅ **Couplant** is still required because ultrasound does not travel efficiently through air gaps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Testing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Testing Techniques # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** is a non-destructive evaluation (NDE) technique that employs **high-frequency mechanical vibrations (sound waves)** in the frequency range of **0.1 MHz to 25 MHz** to detect, characterize, and evaluate surface and sub-surface flaws in engineering materials. The technique relies on the **principle of acoustic impedance mismatch** at the interface of two media, causing partial reflection, transmission, or mode conversion of the propagating wave. In formal KTU terminology, Ultrasonic Testing is classified as a **Volumetric NDT method** under the ASME Section V / ASTM E114 standards framework. It is widely used for **detecting internal defects** such as cracks, voids, delaminations, porosity, inclusions, and measuring **thickness** in metallic and non-metallic components. ### Governing Physical Principle The technique is governed by the **Piezoelectric Effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880), wherein certain crystalline materials (e.g., Quartz, Lead Zirconate Titanate – PZT, Lithium Niobate) generate an electric charge when subjected to mechanical stress, and conversely, deform mechanically when an electric field is applied. ### Key Frequency Spectrum > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Infrasound:** < **20 Hz** > - **Audible Range:** **20 Hz to 20 kHz** > - **Ultrasonic Range:** > **20 kHz** (typically 0.1–25 MHz for NDT) > - **Hypersound:** > **1 GHz** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at one end of a long, empty concrete tunnel and you shout loudly. The sound wave travels down the tunnel, hits the far wall, and the **echo** returns to you. By measuring the time it takes for the echo to return, you can estimate the length of the tunnel. If there is a large boulder (defect) inside the tunnel, you will hear a **softer, earlier echo** before the wall echo arrives. **This is exactly how Ultrasonic Testing works:** | Human Shout Analogy | Ultrasonic Testing Equivalent | |---------------------|-------------------------------| | Human voice | High-frequency pulse from transducer | | Tunnel walls | Material boundaries / back surface | | Echo return time | Time-of-Flight measurement | | Boulder in tunnel | Internal defect (crack, void) | | Ear listening | Receiving transducer / display screen | The key difference is that the "shout" is at a frequency **thousands of times higher** than human hearing, allowing very fine, short-wavelength waves to "see" tiny defects with **millimeter precision**. ## 1.3 Why Ultrasonic Testing is Critical in Engineering > [!NOTE] > - **Speed of sound in steel ≈ 5920 m/s** (a critical constant). > - Wavelength at **2.25 MHz in steel ≈ 2.6 mm** — small enough to resolve fine defects. > - UT can detect flaws as small as **0.5 mm** in heavy forgings, welds, castings, and composites. It is the **preferred method** for in-service inspection of pipelines, pressure vessels, aircraft components, railway axles, and nuclear reactor components due to its depth of penetration, sensitivity, and instantaneous results. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework The entire discipline of Ultrasonic Testing is built upon the physics of **wave propagation in elastic media**. The operational logic can be broken down into five sequential steps: ### Step 1: Wave Generation via Piezoelectric Effect A **piezoelectric crystal** (commonly PZT – Lead Zirconate Titanate) is excited by a high-voltage, short-duration **electrical pulse** (typically 100–400 V, lasting nanoseconds). The crystal undergoes rapid mechanical deformation, producing an **ultrasonic wave** that is coupled into the test material. ### Step 2: Wave Propagation Through the Test Medium The ultrasonic wave travels through the material at a velocity determined by the material's **elastic moduli and density**. As it propagates, it interacts with the grain structure (causing some **attenuation** due to scattering and absorption). ### Step 3: Interaction with a Defect or Boundary When the wave strikes an interface (defect, back wall, or material change), three phenomena occur based on the **acoustic impedance (Z)** of the media: - **Partial Reflection:** Wave bounces back toward the transducer. - **Partial Transmission:** Wave continues through the interface. - **Mode Conversion:** At oblique incidence, the longitudinal wave may convert to shear or surface waves. ### Step 4: Echo Detection and Time-of-Flight Measurement The reflected wave returns to the transducer (which now acts as a receiver), reconverts the mechanical vibration into an electrical signal, and the instrument measures the **time elapsed (Δt)** between transmission and reception. ### Step 5: Flaw Localization and Characterization Using the known velocity of sound in the material and the measured time-of-flight, the **depth of the flaw** is calculated. The **amplitude** of the reflected signal indicates the flaw's size, orientation, and nature. ## 2.2 Acoustic Impedance – The Foundation of UT > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is the product of material density (ρ) and ultrasonic wave velocity (c). It represents the resistance of a medium to the propagation of ultrasonic waves. $$Z = \rho \times c \quad \text{[Rayl or kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)]}$$ where: - $\rho$ = density of the medium (kg/m³) - $c$ = velocity of sound in that medium (m/s) ### Reflection Coefficient (R) When an ultrasonic wave strikes an interface between two media, the fraction of energy reflected is given by: $$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ ### Transmission Coefficient (T) $$T = 1 - R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A larger mismatch in acoustic impedance produces a **stronger echo**. For example, a void (Z₂ ≈ 0) in steel (Z₁ ≈ 46 × 10⁶ Rayl) gives R ≈ 1, meaning almost 100% reflection — making UT extremely sensitive to gas-filled cracks or porosity. ## 2.3 Types of Ultrasonic Waves | Wave Type | Particle Motion | Velocity in Steel | Penetration Depth | Primary Application | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Longitudinal (L-wave)** | Parallel to propagation | **5920 m/s** | Highest | Bulk flaw detection, thickness gauging | | **Shear / Transverse (S-wave)** | Perpendicular to propagation | **3250 m/s** | Moderate | Flaw detection at oblique angles, insensitive to liquids | | **Surface (Rayleigh) Wave** | Elliptical orbit on surface | **2960 m/s** | ~1 wavelength depth | Surface-breaking crack detection | | **Lamb Wave** | Complex through-thickness | Mode-dependent | Full plate thickness | Thin plate, composite inspection | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Rayl (kg/m²·s) | | 2 | Reflection Coefficient | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 3 | Transmission Coefficient | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 4 | Wave Velocity (Longitudinal in solid) | $c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | m/s; E=Young's modulus, $\nu$=Poisson's ratio | | 5 | Wave Velocity (Shear in solid) | $c_S = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$ | m/s; G=Shear modulus | | 6 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | m | | 7 | Depth of Flaw (Pulse-Echo) | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m; factor 2 due to round trip | | 8 | Thickness (Through-Transmission) | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | m; one-way travel | | 9 | Near Field Length (N) | $N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ | m; D=transducer diameter | | 10 | Beam Divergence (Half-angle) | $\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Radians | | 11 | Attenuation Coefficient | $A = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | dB/m; $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades, composite airframes, and landing gear for fatigue cracks. - **Oil & Gas:** In-line inspection (ILI) of pipelines for corrosion and weld defects using "smart pigs" with UT sensors. - **Nuclear:** Reactor pressure vessel integrity monitoring, fuel rod inspection. - **Manufacturing:** Real-time thickness gauging in rolled steel, aluminum sheet, and polymer films. - **Railways:** Continuous testing of rails and axles for transverse defects using phased-array UT. - **Marine:** Hull plate thickness surveys to assess corrosion wastage. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Acoustic Impedance and Reflection at an Interface Consider a longitudinal ultrasonic wave traveling through Medium 1 (acoustic impedance $Z_1 = \rho_1 c_1$) and striking an interface with Medium 2 (acoustic impedance $Z_2 = \rho_2 c_2$) at normal incidence. ### Step 1: Pressure Continuity at the Interface The total pressure on the incident side must equal the total pressure on the transmitted side at the boundary ($x = 0$). $$P_i + P_r = P_t$$ where $P_i$ = incident pressure amplitude, $P_r$ = reflected pressure amplitude, $P_t$ = transmitted pressure amplitude. ### Step 2: Particle Velocity Continuity The particle velocity must also be continuous at the interface (no separation of media): $$v_i - v_r = v_t$$ (The negative sign on $v_r$ accounts for the reversal of direction upon reflection.) ### Step 3: Relate Pressure to Velocity via Acoustic Impedance By definition, the pressure amplitude is related to particle velocity by acoustic impedance: $$P = Z \cdot v \implies v = \frac{P}{Z}$$ Substituting into the velocity continuity equation: $$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$ ### Step 4: Solve the System of Equations From Step 1: $P_t = P_i + P_r$. Substituting into the velocity equation: $$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_i + P_r}{Z_2}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$Z_2 (P_i - P_r) = Z_1 (P_i + P_r)$$ $$Z_2 P_i - Z_2 P_r = Z_1 P_i + Z_1 P_r$$ $$P_i (Z_2 - Z_1) = P_r (Z_2 + Z_1)$$ Therefore: $$\frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$ ### Step 5: Define the Pressure Reflection Coefficient (r) $$r = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$ ### Step 6: Derive the Energy Reflection Coefficient (R) Since intensity is proportional to the square of pressure amplitude: $$R = r^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ ### Step 7: Apply Conservation of Energy The transmitted energy fraction must equal $1 - R$: $$T = 1 - R = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** For a steel–air interface, $Z_1 = 46 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_2 \approx 0$ (air), giving $R \approx 1$. This is why UT easily detects gas-filled voids and cracks in metals. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Depth of Flaw from Pulse-Echo Time-of-Flight ### Step 1: Identify the Wave Path In pulse-echo mode, the ultrasonic wave travels from the transducer to the flaw (depth = $d$) and reflects back to the transducer, covering a total path length of $2d$. ### Step 2: Apply the Fundamental Wave Equation Distance = Velocity × Time: $$2d = c \cdot \Delta t$$ ### Step 3: Solve for Depth $$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ The factor of **2** accounts for the round-trip path (down and back). ### Step 4: Substitute Known Values (Worked Numerical Example) **Problem:** In a steel plate, a flaw echo is received at $\Delta t = 12 \,\mu s$ after the initial pulse. The longitudinal wave velocity in steel is $c_L = 5920$ m/s. Find the depth of the flaw. $$d = \frac{5920 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.07104}{2} = 0.03552 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d = 35.52 \text{ mm}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Longitudinal Wave Velocity from Elastic Moduli Starting from the 1-D wave equation applied to a long rod, the longitudinal velocity is: $$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$ However, for a 3-D solid medium (infinite in lateral extent), the lateral inertia must be considered, introducing the Poisson's ratio correction: ### Step 1: Begin with 3-D Stress-Strain Relations For a thin bar, lateral contraction is unrestricted, giving $c_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$. For a bulk solid, lateral inertia adds a correction. ### Step 2: Apply Bulk Modulus Concept The relevant modulus for longitudinal waves in an unbounded medium is: $$M = \frac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$$ This $M$ is called the **P-wave modulus** or **constrained modulus**. ### Step 3: Express Velocity $$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{M}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$ ### Step 4: Express Shear Wave Velocity For shear waves, the restoring force is governed by the shear modulus $G$: $$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$ $$c_S = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$ ### Step 5: Verification with Steel For steel: $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.29$, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³. $$M = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times (1 - 0.29)}{(1 + 0.29)(1 - 0.58)} = \frac{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.71}{1.29 \times 0.42}$$ $$M = \frac{142 \times 10^9}{0.5418} \approx 262 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$ $$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{262 \times 10^9}{7850}} = \sqrt{33.375 \times 10^6} \approx 5777 \text{ m/s}$$ (The slight difference from the standard 5920 m/s is due to the use of approximate modulus values; the formula is correct.) ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Near-Field Length (Fresnel Zone) The acoustic field in front of a circular, unfocused transducer is divided into two regions: the **near field (Fresnel zone)** and the **far field (Fraunhofer zone)**. ### Step 1: Origin of the Near Field In the near field, multiple wavefronts from different points on the transducer face interfere constructively and destructively, producing a complex pressure distribution. The maximum interference occurs at distance $N$. ### Step 2: Geometric Derivation For a transducer of diameter $D$ vibrating at frequency $f$ in a medium of wavelength $\lambda$, the path difference between the wave from the edge and the center of the transducer at the last axial maximum equals $\lambda$: $$N = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}$$ ### Step 3: Engineering Implication - Within $N$: Beam is **collimated** (parallel), best for sensitive scanning. - Beyond $N$: Beam **diverges** at half-angle $\sin\theta = 1.22\lambda/D$. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: UT Testing Methods | Method | Transducers Used | Signal Source | Best For | Limitation | |--------|------------------|---------------|----------|------------| | **Pulse-Echo (Reflection)** | Single | Reflected echoes from flaws & back wall | General flaw detection, thickness | Dead zone near surface | | **Through-Transmission** | Two (Tx + Rx) | Loss of transmitted signal | Large area, automation | No depth info; two-side access needed | | **Resonance (Pitch-Catch)** | One or two | Resonance at half-wavelength multiples | Thin wall thickness, coating thickness | Only for uniform thickness | | **Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD)** | Two angled | Diffraction from flaw tips | Sizing of cracks | Complex signal interpretation | ## 3.6 Display Modes in Ultrasonic Testing | Display Mode | Plot Type | Information Provided | |--------------|-----------|----------------------| | **A-Scan** | Amplitude vs. Time | 1-D; shows echo heights and positions; primary inspection mode | | **B-Scan** | Echo position vs. Transducer position | 2-D cross-sectional image (depth view) | | **C-Scan** | Echo amplitude vs. X-Y position | 2-D plan view of flaw distribution at set depth | > [!NOTE] > **A-Scan is the fundamental display.** All other scan modes (B, C, D) are derived from A-scan data acquired at multiple positions, typically by automated scanners. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Ultrasonic Testing System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulse Generator
High Voltage Pulser] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer
UT Probe] B -->|Ultrasonic Wave via Couplant| C[Test Piece
Material Under Inspection] C -->|Reflected Echoes from Flaws and Back Wall| B B -->|Electrical Signal| D[Receiver Amplifier
Time-Gated Gain Control] D --> E[Signal Processing Unit
Rectification, Filtering, A/D] E --> F[Display System
A-Scan, B-Scan, C-Scan] E --> G[Data Acquisition and Storage] F --> H[Operator Interpretation
and Decision] G --> H ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Pulse-Echo Testing Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Pulser Fires High-Voltage Pulse] --> A2[Transducer Converts Pulse to US Wave] A2 --> A3[Couplant Transmits Wave into Test Piece] A3 --> A4{Wave Hits Interface?} A4 -->|Defect or Back Wall| A5[Partial Reflection Occurs] A4 -->|No Interface Detected| A6[Wave Attenuates Completely] A5 --> A7[Reflected Wave Returns to Transducer] A7 --> A8[Transducer Acts as Receiver] A8 --> A9[Signal Amplified and Displayed on A-Scan] A9 --> A10[Time of Flight Measured] A10 --> A11[Depth of Flaw Calculated] A11 --> A12[Accept or Reject Decision] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Through-Transmission Method ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Transmitter Probe
On Surface A] -->|Continuous or Pulsed US Wave| B[Test Piece] B -->|Transmitted Energy| C[Receiver Probe
On Surface B] C --> D[Amplifier and Comparator] D -->|Loss of Signal Indicates Flaw| E[Alarm or Reject Signal] D -->|Full Signal Indicates Sound Material| F[Accept Signal] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Types of UT Probes and Their Applications ```mermaid flowchart TD UT[Ultrasonic Transducers] --> N[Normal Probe
Straight Beam Longitudinal] UT --> A[Angle Probe
Shear Wave at Oblique Angle] UT --> D[Delay Line Probe
Short Dead Zone] UT --> T[Dual Element Probe
Separate Tx and Rx] UT --> PA[Phased Array Probe
Electronic Beam Steering] UT --> IM[Immersion Probe
Used in Water Bath] N --> N1[Thickness Gauging
Straight Beam Testing] A --> A1[Weld Inspection
Oblique Flaw Detection] D --> D1[Near-Surface Defect Detection] T --> T1[Surface and Near-Surface Inspection] PA --> PA1[Complex Geometry Scanning
Weld and Corrosion Mapping] IM --> IM1[Automated C-Scan Systems
Aerospace Composite Inspection] ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram: Role of Couplant in UT ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Transducer Face] -->|Air Gap Blocks Wave
99.9% Reflected| X[Without Couplant: Fail] X --> B[Apply Couplant Layer] B --> C[Couplant: Water, Glycerin, Oil, Gel] C -->|Eliminates Air Gap
Enables Efficient Wave Transfer| D[Sound Transmission Achieved] D --> E[Reliable Inspection Possible] ``` ## 4.6 Functional Flow: UT Inspection Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Surface Preparation
Cleaning, Smooth Finish] --> S2[Step 2: Couplant Application] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Calibration using Reference Block
IIW V1 or V2 Block] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Probe Placement and Scanning] S4 --> S5{Defect Detected?} S5 -->|Yes| S6[Step 5: Mark, Measure Depth, Evaluate Size] S5 -->|No| S7[Continue Scan and Record] S6 --> S8[Accept or Reject per Acceptance Criteria] S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[Generate Inspection Report] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** **Define acoustic impedance. What is its role in ultrasonic testing?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Acoustic impedance (Z) is the product of material density (ρ) and ultrasonic wave velocity (c): **Z = ρ × c** (1 mark). It is measured in Rayl (kg/m²·s) (0.5 marks). In ultrasonic testing, acoustic impedance governs the **reflection and transmission** of ultrasonic waves at material interfaces. A large mismatch in Z between two media produces a **strong reflected echo**, enabling the detection of internal defects such as cracks, voids, and delaminations (1.5 marks). ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]** **List any three types of ultrasonic waves used in NDT. State one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Longitudinal (L-wave):** Particle motion parallel to propagation; velocity ~5920 m/s in steel; used for **general flaw detection and thickness measurement** (1 mark). 2. **Shear/Transverse (S-wave):** Particle motion perpendicular to propagation; velocity ~3250 m/s in steel; used for **weld inspection and detection of flaws oriented parallel to surface** (1 mark). 3. **Surface (Rayleigh) Wave:** Travels along surface with elliptical particle motion; used for **detection of surface-breaking cracks** (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **pulse-echo method** of ultrasonic testing. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ultrasonic pulse is sent into a steel test piece. A defect echo is received **8 microseconds** after the initial pulse, and the back-wall echo is received at **20 microseconds**. Calculate: - (i) The depth of the defect from the search surface - (ii) The thickness of the test piece - (iii) The wavelength of the 4 MHz ultrasonic wave in steel Take longitudinal velocity in steel = **5920 m/s**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Pulse-Echo Method – Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):** **Working Principle:** In the pulse-echo method, a **single transducer** acts as both transmitter and receiver. A short, high-voltage electrical pulse is applied to the piezoelectric crystal, generating an ultrasonic pulse that is coupled into the test material. (1 mark) **Procedure:** - The pulse travels through the material and encounters a defect or the back wall. - A portion of the wave energy is **reflected** back as an echo. - The same transducer receives the echo and converts it back into an electrical signal. (1 mark) - The time elapsed between transmission and reception ($\Delta t$) is measured. (1 mark) - The depth of the reflecting interface is calculated as $d = c \Delta t / 2$. (1 mark) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` [Pulser] → [T/R Switch] → [Transducer] ⇄ [Test Piece with Flaw] ↓ (Echo) [T/R Switch] ← [Amplifier] ← [Display A-Scan] ``` **Advantages:** (1 mark) - Single-side access to the test piece. - Provides depth information of the flaw. - Portable and suitable for in-service inspection. **Limitations:** (1 mark) - **Dead zone** near the surface due to transducer ringing. - Requires skilled operator for interpretation. - Couplant is essential; rough surfaces reduce sensitivity. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $\Delta t_{flaw} = 8 \,\mu s$, $\Delta t_{back} = 20 \,\mu s$, $c_L = 5920$ m/s, $f = 4$ MHz. **(i) Depth of the defect (3 marks):** $$d_{flaw} = \frac{c_L \cdot \Delta t_{flaw}}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$d_{flaw} = \frac{0.04736}{2} = 0.02368 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d_{flaw} = 23.68 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Thickness of the test piece (2 marks):** $$t = \frac{c_L \cdot \Delta t_{back}}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$t = \frac{0.1184}{2} = 0.0592 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{t = 59.2 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iii) Wavelength of the 4 MHz wave (2 marks):** $$\lambda = \frac{c_L}{f} = \frac{5920}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda = 1.48 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part (b):** A very common mistake students make is **forgetting the factor of 2** in the depth formula. Remember, in pulse-echo, the wave travels **down and back**, so the round-trip time must be halved. Also, ensure unit conversion: **microseconds (μs) = 10⁻⁶ s**, not 10⁻³ s. Marks are routinely deducted for such errors. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a piezoelectric ultrasonic transducer**. What is the piezoelectric effect? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two steel plates are to be bonded together. A UT inspection using a **5 MHz** probe reveals that the echo from the bond-line interface arrives at **6 μs**, and the echo from the back wall arrives at **18 μs**. Determine: - (i) The thickness of the first plate. - (ii) The thickness of the second plate. - (iii) Whether the bond is sound (state the criterion used). Take $c_L$ in steel = **5900 m/s**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Piezoelectric Transducer – Principle, Construction, and Working (7 marks):** **Piezoelectric Effect (2 marks):** The **direct piezoelectric effect** (Curie brothers, 1880) is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., Quartz, PZT, Lithium Niobate) to generate an electric charge across their faces when subjected to mechanical stress. The **converse piezoelectric effect** is the deformation of such crystals when an electric field is applied. **Construction (3 marks):** - **Piezoelectric Crystal (Active Element):** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) is most common. Typical dimensions: 10–25 mm diameter, 0.5–2 mm thick. - **Backing Block (Damping Material):** High-density tungsten-epoxy composite absorbs backward-traveling waves, **shortening the pulse width** and improving resolution. - **Electrodes:** Thin silver or nickel coatings on both faces of the crystal, connected to the coaxial cable. - **Wear Plate / Faceplate:** Protects the crystal; may also act as a delay line. - **Housing:** Metal casing for mechanical protection and EMI shielding. **Working (2 marks):** - A high-voltage pulse (100–400 V, ~50 ns duration) is applied to the electrodes. - The crystal undergoes **rapid mechanical deformation** (converse piezoelectric effect), generating an ultrasonic wave. - The wave exits through the front face into the test material (via couplant). - In pulse-echo mode, the same crystal later receives the echo and converts it to an electrical signal (direct effect) via the **T/R switch** (transmit/receive switch). --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $\Delta t_1 = 6 \,\mu s$ (bond-line echo), $\Delta t_2 = 18 \,\mu s$ (back wall echo), $c_L = 5900$ m/s, $f = 5$ MHz. **(i) Thickness of the first plate (3 marks):** $$t_1 = \frac{c_L \cdot \Delta t_1}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$t_1 = \frac{0.0354}{2} = 0.0177 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{t_1 = 17.7 \text{ mm}}$$ **(ii) Thickness of the second plate (2 marks):** The difference in time-of-flight between the bond-line echo and the back wall echo represents the round-trip travel within the second plate: $$\Delta t_{2nd} = 18 - 6 = 12 \,\mu s$$ $$t_2 = \frac{c_L \cdot \Delta t_{2nd}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$t_2 = \frac{0.0708}{2} = 0.0354 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{t_2 = 35.4 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iii) Bond integrity assessment (2 marks):** For a **sound (perfect) bond**, the bond-line interface must have **negligible acoustic impedance mismatch**, meaning the echo amplitude at the bond line should be **≤ 6 dB above the back-wall echo (reference level)** (a typical acceptance criterion per ASTM E114). Since the problem provides both echoes with similar arrival characteristics and no amplitude data indicating abnormal reflection, the bond can be considered **sound**, provided the bond-line echo amplitude is within the acceptable threshold. In practical inspection, the operator would compare the bond-line echo height with the calibration echo and apply the **6 dB drop criterion** for acceptance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part (a):** Students often describe the construction of the transducer but **forget to explain the working with a clear cause-effect chain**: voltage pulse → crystal deformation → ultrasonic wave → couplant → material. Marks are awarded for **logical flow**, not just listing components. Also, do not confuse the **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects — they are reciprocal but different in direction. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Warning for Ultrasonic Testing Questions > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Across All UT Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in pulse-echo depth calculations (round-trip travel). > 2. **Mixing up longitudinal and shear wave velocities** — shear wave velocity in steel is ~3250 m/s, NOT 5920 m/s. > 3. **Wrong unit conversions** — μs = 10⁻⁶ s, MHz = 10⁶ Hz. Marks are lost for unit errors even when the formula is correct. > 4. **Confusing A-scan, B-scan, and C-scan** — A-scan is amplitude vs. time, B-scan is depth vs. position (cross-section), C-scan is plan view at set depth. > 5. **Ignoring the role of couplant** — a question asking "why couplant is needed?" expects the answer that **air has very low acoustic impedance (Z ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶ Rayl) vs. steel (Z ≈ 46 × 10⁶ Rayl)**, causing >99.9% reflection at any air gap. > 6. **Not stating the formula's underlying assumption** — e.g., in $d = c\Delta t/2$, the assumption is **normal incidence** and **homogeneous, isotropic medium**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Ultrasonic Testing Techniques** - ✅ **Definition:** UT uses high-frequency mechanical waves (0.1–25 MHz) to detect internal flaws based on **acoustic impedance mismatch** and **time-of-flight** measurement. - ✅ **Core Equation:** $Z = \rho \cdot c$; $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$; $d = \frac{c \Delta t}{2}$ (pulse-echo). - ✅ **Standard Velocities in Steel:** $c_L = 5920$ m/s, $c_S = 3250$ m/s, $c_R = 2960$ m/s. - ✅ **Piezoelectric Effect:** Direct (mechanical → electrical) and Converse (electrical → mechanical). **PZT is the most common transducer material**. - ✅ **Wave Types:** Longitudinal (fastest, most used), Shear (oblique inspection, doesn't propagate in liquids), Surface (Rayleigh), Lamb (plate waves). - ✅ **Testing Methods:** Pulse-Echo (single probe), Through-Transmission (two probes), Resonance, TOFD. - ✅ **Display Modes:** A-scan (1-D amplitude vs. time), B-scan (2-D cross-section), C-scan (2-D plan view at depth). - ✅ **Couplant:** Mandatory to eliminate air gap. Common couplants: water, glycerin, oil, gel, cellulose paste. Function: acoustic impedance matching. - ✅ **Near Field Length:** $N = D^2 f / (4c)$ — critical for transducer selection. - ✅ **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c / f$ — determines minimum detectable flaw size (~λ/2 for direct detection). - ✅ **Applications:** Weld inspection, thickness gauging, corrosion mapping, composite inspection, pipeline integrity, aerospace and nuclear components. - ✅ **Advantages:** High sensitivity, depth penetration, instantaneous results, single-side access, no radiation hazard. - ✅ **Limitations:** Requires skilled operator, couplant needed, dead zone near surface, difficult on rough/coarse-grained materials (e.g., cast iron, austenitic stainless steel welds). - ✅ **Calibration Standards:** IIW V1/V2 block, ASTM E114 reference block, AWS calibration block. - ✅ **Acceptance Criteria (KTU frequently tested):** 6 dB drop rule for bond inspection; DAC (Distance-Amplitude Correction) for flaw sizing.

topics.title: Resonance Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resonance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Resonance Testing** is a non-destructive testing (NDT) technique based on the principle of **mechanical resonance**, in which a test specimen is excited into vibration at one or more of its natural (resonant) frequencies. The corresponding resonant response—characterized by maximum amplitude and a sharp phase transition—is monitored to evaluate the specimen's **geometric dimensions, elastic properties, density, and internal integrity** (such as delaminations, cracks, or voids).

The method exploits the fact that the natural frequency of a body is a direct function of its **stiffness (k)**, **mass (m)**, and **boundary conditions**. Any internal flaw alters the local effective stiffness or mass distribution, producing a measurable shift in the resonant frequency, which forms the basis of defect detection.
