- $L_1$ = source-to-object distance in mm
- $U_g$ = geometric unsharpness in mm

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Big source, big blur — push object close to film"*. To reduce $U_g$, decrease $d$ (use small focal-spot tube), decrease $t$ (place object against film), or increase $L_1$ (move source far away).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect Detection Capability of Each NDT Type

| NDT Type | Surface Defect | Subsurface Defect | Volumetric Defect | Material Restriction |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| VT | ✔✔✔ | ✘ | ✘ | All materials |
| LPT | ✔✔✔ | ✘ (only if open to surface) | ✘ | All non-porous |
| MPT | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔ (near-surface) | ✘ | Ferromagnetic only |
| ECT | ✔✔ | ✔ (within skin depth) | ✘ | Conductive only |
| UT | ✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔ | Most engineering materials |
| RT | ✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | Most materials |
| AET | ✔ | ✔✔ | ✔✔ | Most materials |
| IRT | ✔✔ | ✔ (delamination) | ✔ | All materials |

Legend: ✔✔✔ Excellent | ✔✔ Good | ✔ Limited | ✘ Not Applicable
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: UT Pulse-Echo Working Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pulser
    participant Transducer
    participant TestPiece
    participant Receiver
    participant Display

    Pulser->>Transducer: Apply high voltage pulse
    Transducer->>TestPiece: Emit ultrasonic wave into material
    Note over Transducer,TestPiece: Couplant transmits acoustic energy
    TestPiece->>TestPiece: Wave encounters defect or back wall
    TestPiece->>Transducer: Reflected wave returns to probe
    Transducer->>Receiver: Convert mechanical echo to electrical signal
    Receiver->>Display: Amplify and rectify signal
    Display->>Display: Plot A-scan amplitude vs time
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1.** List any **three surface NDT methods** and state one limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Visual Testing (VT):** Limitation — only detects defects visible to the eye; cannot locate subsurface flaws.
2. **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT):** Limitation — works only on **non-porous** materials and detects only **surface-breaking** defects.
3. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT):** Limitation — applicable **only to ferromagnetic materials** (cannot inspect aluminium, copper, austenitic stainless steel).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2.** What is the difference between **Type I** and **Type II** penetrants in LPT?

**Model Answer:**
- **Type I — Fluorescent Penetrants:** Contain dyes that fluoresce under UV-A light (365 nm), producing bright yellow-green indications. Higher sensitivity, used in critical aerospace and nuclear inspections.
- **Type II — Visible Dye Penetrants:** Contain red dye visible in white light. Cheaper, used in field inspections and lower-criticality components.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify the different types of Non-Destructive Testing methods. Discuss in detail the principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic transducer operating at **4 MHz** is used to inspect a steel plate. The velocity of longitudinal waves in steel is **5900 m/s**.
  - (i) Calculate the wavelength of the ultrasonic wave.
  - (ii) Calculate the acoustic impedance of steel, given density = **7850 kg/m³**.
  - (iii) Determine the near-field length for a transducer of diameter **20 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Classification of NDT types** (write in tabular form for 2 marks):
- Surface methods — VT, LPT, MPT, ECT
- Volumetric methods — UT, RT, NRT
- Special methods — AET, IRT, LT

**UT — Principle (2 marks):**
High-frequency mechanical waves (0.5 to 25 MHz) are introduced into the material via a piezoelectric transducer through a couplant. Reflections occur at discontinuities and at the back wall due to acoustic impedance mismatch. These reflections are captured and analyzed.

**UT — Procedure (1 mark):**
Surface preparation → application of couplant → probe placement → pulse transmission → receiving echo → interpretation via A-scan, B-scan, or C-scan display.

**UT — Advantages (1 mark):**
- Detects both surface and subsurface defects
- High sensitivity (down to fractions of a millimetre)
- Portable, instantaneous results
- No ionizing radiation hazard

**UT — Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires skilled operator
- Difficult on rough, irregular, or very thin surfaces
- Couplant is mandatory
- Reference standards required for calibration

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**(i) Wavelength calculation:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{5900}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.475 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.475 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Acoustic impedance:**

$$Z = \rho \cdot v = 7850 \times 5900 = 4.6315 \times 10^7 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s} = 46.3 \text{ MRayl}$$

**(iii) Near-field length:**

$$N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 1.475 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{5.9 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0678 \text{ m} = 67.8 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of **Radiographic Testing (RT)**. Compare the use of **X-rays** and **Gamma rays** as radiation sources, listing at least three points of difference. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radiographic exposure uses an X-ray source of focal-spot size **3 mm**. The source-to-object distance is **500 mm**, and the object thickness (object-to-film distance) is **25 mm**.
  - (i) Calculate the geometric unsharpness $U_g$.
  - (ii) If the same source is moved to **1000 mm**, what is the new $U_g$?
  - (iii) State whether the new $U_g$ is acceptable as per **ASTM E94** if the limit is **0.5 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
Ionizing electromagnetic radiation (X-ray or Gamma-ray) is passed through the test object. Differential absorption occurs based on material density and thickness. Defects (voids, cracks, porosity) absorb less radiation than sound material, so they appear as **dark regions** on the radiographic film after development. Digital detectors may also be used.

**Schematic (description, 1 mark):**
Source → Collimator → Test Object → Film / Detector → Processing → Interpretation. Mention **IQI (penetrameter)** placed on the source side of the object.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | X-rays | Gamma rays |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Source | X-ray tube (electrical) | Radioactive isotope (Ir-192, Co-60) |
| Energy control | Adjustable (kV / MeV) | Fixed (isotope-specific) |
| Wavelength | Continuous spectrum | Discrete lines |
| Portability | Needs power supply | Truly portable |
| On/Off control | Can be switched off | Cannot be switched off |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Initial geometric unsharpness:**

$$U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{L_1} = \dfrac{3 \times 25}{500} = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) New unsharpness at $L_1 = 1000$ mm:**

$$U_{g,\text{new}} = \dfrac{3 \times 25}{1000} = 0.075 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Acceptability:**
Since $0.075 \text{ mm} < 0.5 \text{ mm}$, **the new $U_g$ is acceptable** as per ASTM E94.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Density must be in $\text{kg/m}^3$, not $\text{g/cm}^3$. Marks deducted for unit mismatch.
> 2. **Forgetting the square** in the intensity reflection coefficient (you will lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Sign error in reflection coefficient:** Acoustic impedance *always positive*; the formula is $(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 / (Z_2 + Z_1)^2$ — never negative.
> 4. **Writing "Gamma rays are always better":** Wrong! X-rays have *adjustable energy*; gamma rays are *fixed energy*. KTU examiners want comparative understanding.
> 5. **Omitting IQI mention:** Whenever RT is asked, mention **ASTM E747 penetrameter** at least once.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 "Types"**

- ✔ There are **10 major NDT types** recognized in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- ✔ **VT is the cheapest; RT and NRT are the most expensive** but provide permanent records.
- ✔ **MP works only on ferromagnetic materials** — never on austenitic SS or aluminium.
- ✔ **ECT works only on conductive materials** — cannot inspect plastics or ceramics.
- ✔ **LPT detects only surface-breaking defects** — the defect must be open to the surface.
- ✔ **UT uses piezoelectric effect** — PZT (lead zirconate titanate) is the common crystal.
- ✔ **Frequency range:** UT = 0.5 to 25 MHz; ECT = 100 kHz to 5 MHz; AET = 20 kHz to 1 MHz.
- ✔ **Standard speeds:** Longitudinal in steel ≈ 5900 m/s; Longitudinal in aluminium ≈ 6320 m/s; Shear in steel ≈ 3200 m/s.
- ✔ **Acoustic impedance units:** $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ or **Rayl** ($1 \text{ MRayl} = 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$).
- ✔ **Steel Z ≈ 46 MRayl; Aluminium Z ≈ 17 MRayl; Water Z ≈ 1.5 MRayl; Air Z ≈ 0.0004 MRayl.**
- ✔ **Curie temperature of iron ≈ 770 °C** — above this, MPT becomes impossible.
- ✔ **ASTM standards to memorize:** E165 (LPT), E709 (MPT), E309 (ECT), E114 (UT), E94 (RT).
- ✔ **Indian Standards:** IS 13805 (LPT), IS 5334 (MPT), IS 2417 (UT), IS 3658 (RT).
- ✔ **Geometric unsharpness formula:** $U_g = d t / L_1$ — always express in **mm** for KTU board exam.
- ✔ **Personnel qualification** is governed by **ISO 9712** (international) and **ASNT SNT-TC-1A** (American).
- ✔ **Three NDT levels:** Level I (operator), Level II (inspector), Level III (trainer/auditor).
- ✔ **Selection rule of thumb:** *Surface → LPT/MPT; Subsurface → UT; Through-thickness → RT; In-service monitoring → AET/IRT.*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions in KTU Module 3, always draw a labelled schematic, write the formula in standard notation, substitute with units, and conclude with a real-world application sentence. This scoring pattern consistently fetches full marks under the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT), **Frequency** is defined as the number of complete cycles of a periodic wave (mechanical, electromagnetic, or acoustic) that occur per unit time, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. It is the fundamental parameter governing wave–matter interaction in ultrasonic, eddy current, and acoustic emission testing methods.

Mathematically, for a sinusoidal wave propagating through a medium, frequency $f$ is related to the angular frequency $\omega$ and the time period $T$ by:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In NDT, the term "Frequencies" specifically refers to the operating frequency range of the test signal, its relationship with wavelength, velocity, penetration depth, and the criteria used by the NDT engineer to select the correct frequency for a given inspection scenario.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Radio Tuner:**
> Imagine you are tuning a radio. Low-frequency stations (like AM) can travel long distances but carry less detail. High-frequency stations (like FM) carry rich detail but fade quickly over distance. Now imagine NDT frequencies as "radio channels" sent *into* a material. **Low frequencies** are like deep-voiced sounds that travel deep into a thick steel forging but cannot "see" small hairline cracks. **High frequencies** are like a high-pitched whistle that can detect a tiny crack on a turbine blade surface, but they get absorbed before reaching deep defects. Choosing the right frequency in NDT is exactly like picking the right radio channel — you must balance **penetration** against **resolution (detail detection)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants in NDT Frequency Analysis

| Medium | Wave Type | Propagation Velocity (v) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Steel (Longitudinal) | Ultrasonic | **5920 m/s** |
| Steel (Shear) | Ultrasonic | **3250 m/s** |
| Aluminum (Longitudinal) | Ultrasonic | **6320 m/s** |
| Water | Ultrasonic (Couplant) | **1480 m/s** |
| Air | Ultrasonic | **343 m/s** |
| Vacuum / Free Space | Electromagnetic | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why velocity matters:** Frequency alone is meaningless without the medium's wave velocity, because wavelength $\lambda = v/f$ determines the physical scale of interaction. A 5 MHz wave behaves very differently in steel than in aluminum.

## 1.4 Classification of NDT Methods by Operating Frequency

| NDT Method | Typical Frequency Range | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | **0.5 MHz – 25 MHz** | Flaw detection, thickness gauging |
| Eddy Current Testing (ET) | **100 Hz – 10 MHz** | Surface/subsurface crack detection |
| Acoustic Emission (AE) | **20 kHz – 1 MHz** | Real-time defect monitoring |
| Radiography (X-ray) | **10¹⁶ – 10¹⁹ Hz** | Internal volumetric inspection |
| Infrared Thermography | **10¹³ – 10¹⁴ Hz** | Surface heat pattern analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Frequency Selection in NDT

When an NDT engineer selects a test frequency, they balance three competing requirements:

### Pillar 1: **Wavelength (Resolution)**
- Resolution ≈ wavelength. To detect a flaw of size $d$, we need $\lambda \leq d/3$.
- Higher frequency → smaller wavelength → finer resolution.

### Pillar 2: **Penetration Depth (Energy Loss)**
- Attenuation $\alpha$ in a medium generally increases with frequency: $\alpha \propto f^n$ (where $n = 1$ to $2$).
- Higher frequency → more energy absorbed → less penetration.
- Standard rule: **doubling frequency roughly doubles the attenuation.**

### Pillar 3: **Beam Characteristics (Directivity)**
- Beam divergence angle $\theta \approx \sin^{-1}(1.22 \lambda / D)$ for a circular transducer of diameter $D$.
- Higher frequency → smaller beam spread → better defect localization.

## 2.2 Standard Frequency Bands Used in Ultrasonic NDT

| Band | Frequency | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Low Frequency | **0.5 – 1 MHz** | Coarse-grained austenitic steel, cast iron, thick forgings, concrete |
| Medium Frequency | **2 – 5 MHz** | General-purpose inspection of carbon steel, weld testing |
| High Frequency | **10 – 25 MHz** | Thin sheets, precision parts, laboratory microstructure studies |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least one formula is guaranteed in every KTU University Exam.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $v = f \lambda$ | Fundamental wave equation | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $\lambda = v / f$ | Wavelength from frequency | m |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 v}$ | Near-field length of circular transducer | m |
| 4 | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}\right)$ | Half-angle of beam divergence | radians |
| 5 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Standard depth of penetration (eddy current) | m |
| 6 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 f$ | Attenuation coefficient (linear approx.) | Np/m |
| 7 | $P(x) = P_0 \, e^{-2\alpha x}$ | Intensity remaining after depth $x$ | W/m² |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Acoustic impedance reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Z = \rho \, v$ | Acoustic impedance | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 10 | $f_{AE}^{min} = \dfrac{v}{\pi \, d_{min}}$ | Resonant frequency of an acoustic emission source | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations allow the NDT engineer to mathematically determine the correct transducer frequency *before* approaching a real component, ensuring compliance with codes like ASME Section V and ASTM E164.

## 2.4 Frequency Selection Decision Logic

The selection process follows a strict logical hierarchy used in KTU board problems:

1. **Identify the material** → Look up velocity $v$ and grain structure.
2. **Identify the defect type and minimum detectable size** → Determine required wavelength $\lambda \leq d_{min}/3$.
3. **Compute the required frequency** → $f = v / \lambda$.
4. **Check penetration requirement** → Ensure $\alpha f x$ is small enough that signal is still detectable.
5. **Adjust frequency downward** if attenuation is too high, or **upward** if resolution is insufficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wavelength from the One-Dimensional Wave Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the general 1D wave equation** for a disturbance $y(x,t)$ propagating in the $+x$ direction:

$$y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

where:
- $A$ = amplitude of the wave
- $k$ = angular wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $\phi$ = phase constant

**Step 2 — Identify the spatial and temporal periods.**
A full cycle in space corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$, giving wavelength $\lambda = 2\pi / k$.
A full cycle in time corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$, giving time period $T = 2\pi / \omega$.

**Step 3 — Express the velocity** by tracking a point of constant phase (say $\omega t - kx = \text{const}$). Differentiating:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate the NDT-critical parameter, frequency:**

$$f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This shows that in a *given* material (fixed $v$), doubling the frequency halves the wavelength — directly doubling the inspection resolution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Near-Field Length of a Circular Ultrasonic Transducer

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**
A circular piezoelectric disc of diameter $D$ radiates sound. The wavefronts from every point on the disc interfere constructively and destructively, producing an interference pattern.

**Step 2 — On-axis pressure equation.**
The acoustic pressure $P$ on the transducer axis at distance $x$ from the disc is:

$$P(x) = 2 P_0 \sin\left[\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left(\sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x\right)\right]$$

where $a = D/2$ is the radius.

**Step 3 — Apply the far-field approximation.**
When $x \gg a$, the term $\sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x$ can be expanded using the binomial series:

$$\sqrt{x^2 + a^2} - x = x\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{a^2}{x^2}} - 1\right) \approx \frac{a^2}{2x}$$

**Step 4 — The last axial maximum** (transition from near field to far field) occurs when the argument of the sine equals $\pi/2$, i.e.:

$$\frac{\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{a^2}{2x} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x = N$ (the near-field length):**

$$\frac{a^2}{2N \lambda} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$N = \frac{a^2}{\lambda} = \frac{(D/2)^2}{\lambda} = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\lambda = v/f$ to express in terms of frequency:**

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4v}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the foundation of the rule: *higher frequency transducers produce longer near fields*, meaning the focal-like interference zone extends deeper — useful for inspecting thin sections at a standoff distance.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem Type)

> **Problem:** A 10 mm diameter ultrasonic transducer operates at 4 MHz in a steel plate. The longitudinal velocity in steel is **5920 m/s**.
> Find: (a) Wavelength, (b) Near-field length, (c) Minimum detectable flaw size, (d) Beam divergence half-angle.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- $f = 4 \text{ MHz} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$

### (a) Wavelength

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{5920}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.48 \text{ mm}$$

### (b) Near-Field Length

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4v} = \frac{(0.01)^2 \times 4 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5920}$$

$$N = \frac{0.0001 \times 4 \times 10^6}{23680} = \frac{400}{23680} = 0.01689 \text{ m} \approx 16.9 \text{ mm}$$

### (c) Minimum Detectable Flaw Size (using $\lambda \leq d/3$)

$$d_{min} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 1.48 = 4.44 \text{ mm}$$

### (d) Beam Divergence Half-Angle

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}\right) = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \cdot \frac{1.48 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01}\right)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.1806) = 10.4^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This 4 MHz, 10 mm transducer can resolve flaws of about **4.4 mm or larger** in steel, with the useful focused beam extending to **~17 mm** from the transducer face. Beyond that, the beam begins to spread at a 10.4° half-angle.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Frequency on Key NDT Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Trend with Increasing $f$ | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **Decreases** (inversely) | Better resolution |
| Near-field length | $N$ | **Increases** | Longer interference zone |
| Beam divergence | $\theta$ | **Decreases** | Better directivity |
| Attenuation | $\alpha$ | **Increases** | Reduced penetration |
| Sensitivity to small flaws | — | **Increases** | Better micro-crack detection |
| Couplant requirement | — | **Stricter** (smaller gaps visible) | Needs low-viscosity couplant |
| Grain noise | — | **Increases sharply** in coarse grain | Limits use in austenitic steel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Frequency Selection Architecture for NDT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Material Identified] --> B{Determine Min. Defect Size}
    B --> C[Compute Required Wavelength lambda = d_min / 3]
    C --> D[Compute Required Frequency f = v / lambda]
    D --> E{Is Attenuation Acceptable?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Select Standard Transducer at f]
    E -->|No - Too High| G[Reduce Frequency to Increase Penetration]
    G --> H{Re-check Resolution OK?}
    H -->|Yes| F
    H -->|No| I[Use Dual Transducer Method: Low f for Detection, High f for Sizing]
    I --> F
    F --> J[End: Transducer Selected]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: NDT Frequency Spectrum Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOWF["Low Frequency Band"]
        A1[0.5 to 1 MHz: Thick Forgings]
        A2[100 Hz to 10 kHz: Deep Eddy Current]
    end
    subgraph MIDF["Medium Frequency Band"]
        B1[2 to 5 MHz: General UT]
        B2[10 kHz to 1 MHz: Surface ET]
        B3[20 kHz to 1 MHz: Acoustic Emission]
    end
    subgraph HIGHF["High Frequency Band"]
        C1[10 to 25 MHz: Thin Section UT]
        C2[1 to 10 MHz: Surface Micro-crack ET]
    end
    subgraph EMR["Electromagnetic Spectrum"]
        D1[10 to the 13 Hz: Infrared Thermography]
        D2[10 to the 18 Hz: X-ray Radiography]
    end
    LOWF --> MIDF --> HIGHF --> EMR
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Frequency vs. Penetration Trade-off

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Increase Frequency f] --> node2[Wavelength lambda decreases]
    node1 --> node3[Resolution improves]
    node1 --> node4[Attenuation alpha increases]
    node1 --> node5[Penetration depth decreases]
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node6[Better defect detection of small flaws]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node7[Signal-to-noise ratio drops in thick parts]
    node6 --> node8[OPTIMAL FREQUENCY BALANCE POINT]
    node7 --> node8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define frequency in the context of ultrasonic NDT. State the typical frequency range used for general-purpose ultrasonic inspection of carbon steel.**

**Model Answer:**
Frequency is the number of complete oscillations of an acoustic wave per second, measured in Hertz (Hz). It is the primary parameter that determines the wavelength of the ultrasonic beam inside a test material, given by $\lambda = v / f$, where $v$ is the longitudinal wave velocity in the material.

The typical frequency range used for general-purpose ultrasonic inspection of carbon steel is **2 MHz to 5 MHz**, which provides a practical balance between penetration depth (sufficient for plates up to several inches thick) and flaw resolution (capable of detecting flaws down to a few millimetres).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1), Formula linkage (1), Frequency range with justification (1).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why are low frequencies (around 1 MHz) preferred for inspecting coarse-grained materials like austenitic stainless steel and cast iron?**

**Model Answer:**
Low frequencies (typically **0.5 to 1 MHz**) are preferred for inspecting coarse-grained materials because:

1. The grain boundaries in austenitic steel and cast iron scatter high-frequency ultrasound, producing severe **grain noise (grass)** that masks genuine flaw signals.
2. Attenuation in these materials increases sharply with frequency, so lower frequencies suffer less energy loss and achieve **greater penetration depth**.
3. The longer wavelength at low frequency is less affected by diffraction at grain interfaces.

> **Trade-off accepted:** Resolution is sacrificed, but in these materials the alternative (high frequency) is unusable due to overwhelming background noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Mention of grain scattering (1), Attenuation-frequency relation (1), Wavelength/resolution trade-off (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between frequency, wavelength, and velocity of an ultrasonic wave in a solid medium. Explain how this relationship governs the selection of transducer frequency in NDT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 20 mm diameter ultrasonic transducer operating at 2 MHz is used to inspect an aluminium plate. Given the longitudinal velocity in aluminium is **6320 m/s**, calculate:
   (i) The wavelength of the ultrasonic wave.
   (ii) The near-field length.
   (iii) The minimum detectable flaw size.
   (iv) The half-angle of beam divergence.
   **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation and Concept (7 marks)

**Step 1:** The 1D wave equation solution is $y(x,t) = A\sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$.

**Step 2:** Spatial period gives $\lambda = 2\pi/k$; temporal period gives $T = 2\pi/\omega$.

**Step 3:** Velocity of constant phase: $v = \omega/k$.

**Step 4:** Substituting: $v = f \lambda$, hence $f = v/\lambda$. *(Derivation carries 4 marks)*

**Step 5 — Application to NDT transducer selection:** *(3 marks)*
- The required minimum flaw size $d_{min}$ dictates the wavelength: $\lambda \leq d_{min}/3$.
- The frequency is then $f = v/\lambda \geq 3v/d_{min}$.
- The chosen frequency must also satisfy the penetration constraint: $2\alpha x < 1$ (signal must not be fully attenuated before reaching the back wall of the test piece).
- The engineer therefore selects the *highest* frequency that still satisfies the penetration requirement — this is the **optimal inspection frequency**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $f = 2 \text{ MHz} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $v = 6320 \text{ m/s}$.

**(i) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{6320}{2 \times 10^6} = 3.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.16 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(ii) Near-Field Length:**

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4v} = \frac{(0.02)^2 \times 2 \times 10^6}{4 \times 6320} = \frac{800}{25280} = 0.0316 \text{ m} = 31.6 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Minimum Detectable Flaw Size:**

$$d_{min} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 3.16 = 9.48 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Beam Divergence Half-Angle:**

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}\right) = \sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \cdot \frac{3.16 \times 10^{-3}}{0.02}\right)$$

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.1928) = 11.1^\circ \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the factors that govern the selection of test frequency in ultrasonic NDT. Explain the effect of frequency on near-field length, beam divergence, attenuation, and resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 mm thick steel plate is to be inspected for sub-surface flaws. The longitudinal velocity in steel is **5920 m/s** and the attenuation coefficient is $\alpha_0 = 0.5 \text{ Np/(m·MHz)}$. Determine:
   (i) The maximum usable frequency such that at least **1% of the transmitted intensity** reaches the back wall.
   (ii) The corresponding minimum detectable flaw size at this frequency.
   **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Discussion (7 marks)

**Four governing factors — each carries ~1.5 marks, plus 1 mark for synthesis:**

1. **Material grain structure:** Coarse grain → lower frequency to avoid grain scattering noise.
2. **Required resolution (minimum flaw size):** Smaller flaw → higher frequency (smaller $\lambda$).
3. **Penetration thickness:** Greater thickness → lower frequency (lower $\alpha$).
4. **Surface condition / couplant capability:** Rough surfaces and viscous couplant limits → lower frequency (longer wavelength tolerates air gaps).

**Effect of frequency on each parameter:**

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing $f$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Near-field length $N = D^2f/4v$ | **Increases** linearly with $f$ |
| Beam divergence $\theta \propto \lambda/D = v/fD$ | **Decreases** with $f$ (better directivity) |
| Attenuation $\alpha = \alpha_0 f$ | **Increases** linearly with $f$ (less penetration) |
| Resolution $\lambda = v/f$ | **Improves** (smaller $\lambda$ detects smaller flaws) |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $x = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$, $\alpha_0 = 0.5 \text{ Np/(m·MHz)}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the intensity equation.** We require $P(x)/P_0 \geq 0.01$:

$$\frac{P(x)}{P_0} = e^{-2\alpha x} \geq 0.01$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$-2\alpha x \geq \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$\alpha x \leq 2.3026$$

**Step 3 — Express $\alpha$ in terms of $f$:**

$$\alpha = \alpha_0 f = 0.5 f \quad \text{where } f \text{ is in MHz}$$

$$0.5 f \times 0.025 \leq 2.3026$$

$$0.0125 f \leq 2.3026$$

$$f \leq 184.2 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{(3 marks for this part)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply engineering constraint.** A maximum of 184 MHz is the theoretical ceiling, but practical NDT transducers top out at ~25 MHz. We therefore select the highest *practical* standard frequency: **$f = 25 \text{ MHz}$** *(state this engineering judgment — 1 mark)*.

**Step 5 — Calculate the wavelength at the selected frequency:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{5920}{25 \times 10^6} = 2.368 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.237 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Minimum detectable flaw size:**

$$d_{min} = 3\lambda = 3 \times 0.237 = 0.71 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of choice:** At 25 MHz, $\alpha = 0.5 \times 25 = 12.5 \text{ Np/m}$, and the intensity ratio at 25 mm is $e^{-2 \times 12.5 \times 0.025} = e^{-0.625} = 0.535$, i.e. 53.5% — well above 1%. So 25 MHz is feasible and gives the best resolution possible. *(1 mark for verification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the intensity law** — The intensity ratio uses $e^{-2\alpha x}$ (two-way travel), not $e^{-\alpha x}$. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing units** — Attenuation coefficient is in Np/m but frequency is in MHz. Convert consistently before multiplying: either convert MHz to Hz or treat $\alpha_0$ as 0.5 Np/(m·MHz) with $f$ in MHz.
> 3. **Skipping the engineering judgment step** — Simply writing "f ≤ 184 MHz" without recognizing the practical transducer limit will lose the synthesis mark.
> 4. **Not stating the $\lambda \leq d/3$ criterion** explicitly in derivations — the examiner awards a mark for naming the underlying physical principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frequencies in NDT**

- **Fundamental equation:** $v = f \lambda$ — non-negotiable in every problem.
- **Resolution rule of thumb:** Flaw must be at least $\lambda / 3$ in size to be reliably detected.
- **Penetration rule:** Attenuation $\alpha \propto f$; doubling $f$ roughly halves usable penetration.
- **Standard UT frequencies to memorize:** 1 MHz, 2.25 MHz, 4 MHz, 5 MHz, 10 MHz.
- **Near-field length formula:** $N = D^2 f / (4v)$ — increases with frequency and transducer diameter.
- **Beam divergence formula:** $\theta = \sin^{-1}(1.22 \lambda / D)$ — decreases with frequency.
- **Eddy current standard depth of penetration:** $\delta = 1 / \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ — decreases with $\sqrt{f}$.
- **Acoustic Emission frequency range:** 20 kHz to 1 MHz (above human hearing but below UT).
- **Always convert units consistently:** Wavelength in metres, frequency in Hz, velocity in m/s.
- **For thin sections:** Use high frequency (10–25 MHz).
- **For thick/coarse-grained sections:** Use low frequency (0.5–1 MHz).
- **In two-way intensity calculations:** Use $e^{-2\alpha x}$, not $e^{-\alpha x}$.
- **Codes referencing frequency:** ASME Section V Article 4, ASTM E164, ASTM E317 — these are the standards KTU expects you to be aware of.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizes Commonly Used; Reference Blocks with Artificially Created Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizes Commonly Used; Reference Blocks with Artificially Created Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Blocks (Test Blocks / Calibration Blocks)**: Standardized specimens of known geometry, material, and artificially induced defects (reflectors) used to calibrate, verify performance, and establish sensitivity settings of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) equipment — particularly **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**, **Radiographic Testing (RT)**, and **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** systems.

A **Reference Standard** is any physical object possessing one or more **known, reproducible reflectors** that can be used to verify equipment performance and to standardize test sensitivity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a weighing scale. Before measuring a sack of rice, you place a **calibration weight** (say, exactly 1 kg) on it to confirm it reads correctly. That weight is your "reference block."

In NDT, the **reference block** plays the same role:
- The **UT flaw detector** is your "scale."
- The **artificially created defect** inside the block is your "known weight."
- You tune your instrument (Time Base, Sensitivity, DAC) using the reference block, then proceed to test the real component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Reference blocks are required by international standards (ASTM E127, ASTM E428, IIW, AWS D1.1, ISO 2400, ISO 7963) for **periodic calibration** and **verification of UT/RT/ECT equipment accuracy**.

### 1.3 Categories of Reference Standards (Master vs. Working)

| Type | Description | Purpose |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Master Reference Block** | Highest-grade certified block, traceable to National/International standards | Calibrates the test procedure and secondary blocks |
| **Working Reference Block** | Field-use block, calibrated against the master | Daily calibration by the NDT operator |

### 1.4 Common Reference Standards and Governing Codes

| Standard | Issuing Body | Typical Application |
|----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **ASTM E127** | ASTM International | UT reference blocks for longitudinal wave testing |
| **ASTM E428** | ASTM International | Standard practice for fabrication and control of steel reference blocks |
| **IIW Type 1 & Type 2 (V1, V2)** | International Institute of Welding | UT transducer evaluation & calibration |
| **ISO 2400** | ISO | Ultrasonic testing — IIW V1 block specifications |
| **ISO 7963** | ISO | Ultrasonic testing — IIW V2 block specifications |
| **AWS D1.1** | American Welding Society | UT of welds (DS plates, DSC blocks) |
| **ASTM E493 / E494** | ASTM | Through-transmission calibration |

### 1.5 Typical Material of Construction

Reference blocks are fabricated from materials **acoustically and metallurgically similar** to the test piece:

- **Low-carbon steel** (most common, e.g., **AISI 1018** or **AISI 1020**) — sound velocity ≈ **5920 m/s** (longitudinal), density ≈ **7850 kg/m³**.
- **Aluminum alloys** (e.g., **AA 7075-T6** for aerospace) — sound velocity ≈ **6320 m/s**.
- **Stainless steel (Austenitic 304/316)** for nuclear/aerospace applications.
- **Titanium, Inconel, Brass** for specialized industrial applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reference block material **must have a known, certified grain size, hardness, and sound velocity** to ensure calibration accuracy. For coarse-grained materials (e.g., austenitic SS, cast stainless), special **fine-grained reference blocks** are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standard Sizes Commonly Used

#### 2.1.1 IIW Type 1 Block (V1 Block) — ISO 2400

- **Dimensions**: **25 mm thickness × 100 mm width × 305 mm length** (or 150 mm length in compact variants).
- **Key Features**:
  - **50 mm radius** cylindrical surface (curvature) for transducer evaluation, beam profile, and index point verification.
  - **Engraved 25 mm and 50 mm scales** on the top surface.
  - Contains a **1.5 mm diameter side-drilled hole (SDH)** at approximately **44 mm depth**.
  - Includes a **Φ5 mm × 1.5 mm deep slot** for shear wave mode evaluation.
- **Application**: Verification of longitudinal and shear wave probes, index point location, beam angle, and time-base linearity.

#### 2.1.2 IIW Type 2 Block (V2 Block) — ISO 7963

- **Dimensions**: **25 mm thickness × 100 mm width × 250 mm length**.
- **Key Features**: A single **Φ5 mm side-drilled hole** located at a precise distance from one end (typically **100 mm**) — used for **time-base calibration** at a fixed known depth.

#### 2.1.3 DSC Block (Distance, Sensitivity, Calibration) — AWS D1.1

- **Dimensions**: **25 mm thickness × 75 mm width × 300 mm length**.
- **Five SDHs** of **Φ3 mm** at distances of **25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 mm** from one end.
- **Application**: Construction of **Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC)** and **Distance-Gain-Size (DGS)** curves, plus sensitivity calibration.

#### 2.1.4 AWS Resolution Block (DS Plate)

- **Dimensions**: Typically **25 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm**.
- Contains **3 SDHs of Φ3 mm at 25, 50, and 75 mm**.
- Used for **sensitivity and resolution verification**.

#### 2.1.5 Miniature / Small Reference Blocks

- Used for **thin sections, small-diameter pipes, and curved components**.
- Dimensions as small as **5 mm × 25 mm × 50 mm**.
- Often feature **flat-bottom holes (FBH) of Φ1.0 to Φ1.5 mm** for high-sensitivity calibration.

#### 2.1.6 Area Amplitude Blocks (ASTM E127 Series)

- **Standard sizes**: **25 mm (1") thick × 75 mm (3") wide × 250 mm (10") long**.
- **Five flat-bottom holes** of incremental diameters (e.g., **1.6, 3.2, 4.8, 6.4, 9.5 mm**) at fixed distances (e.g., **25, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm**).

### 2.2 Sizes of Artificially Created Defects (Reflectors)

#### 2.2.1 Side-Drilled Holes (SDH)

- **Most common** in IIW, AWS, and V2 blocks.
- **Standard diameters**: **Φ0.8 mm, Φ1.5 mm, Φ2.0 mm, Φ3.0 mm, Φ5.0 mm**.
- Drilled **half-thickness** (T/2) of the block.
- Typical choice: **Φ3 mm** — represents a "small" defect equivalent in size for general calibration.

#### 2.2.2 Flat-Bottom Holes (FBH)

- **ASTM E127 standard FBH diameters**:
  - **1.6 mm (1/16")**
  - **3.2 mm (1/8")**
  - **4.8 mm (3/16")**
  - **6.4 mm (1/4")**
  - **9.5 mm (3/8")**
- Drilled from the **back surface** to a precise depth (e.g., T/4, T/2, 3T/4).

#### 2.2.3 EDM (Electro-Discharge Machined) Notches

- **Rectangular or U-shaped** notches of varying depth: **0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mm** and widths of **0.5 to 1.0 mm**.
- Used in **weld inspection** as a substitute for cracks.
- **Tip radius**: typically ≤ **0.1 mm**.

#### 2.2.4 Saw Cuts / Slits

- Lengths of **5, 10, 15, 20 mm** with depth of **0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mm**.
- Used in **NDT of tubular products** and **composite laminate inspection**.

#### 2.2.5 Tungsten Carbide Ball (TCB) Reflectors

- Spheres of **Φ1.0, Φ1.5, Φ2.0 mm**.
- Embedded in the block by diffusion bonding or epoxy.
- Used as **geometric "point" reflectors** for DGS (Avignon Diagram) calibration.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Sound Velocity (Longitudinal)** $v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | Velocity in a solid medium | m/s |
| **Wavelength** $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Relationship between sound velocity and frequency | mm |
| **Near Field Length (N)** $N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 v}$ or $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda}$ | Distance from transducer to the end of Fresnel zone | mm |
| **Beam Spread (Half-angle)** $\sin^{-1}\left(1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}\right)$ | Diffraction divergence | degrees |
| **Refraction (Snell's) Law** $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{v_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_t}{v_2}$ | Mode conversion at an interface | dimensionless |
| **Time-of-Flight in Block** $t = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ | Round-trip time to a flat reflector at depth *d* | µs |
| **Attenuation Coefficient** $\alpha = \dfrac{20}{2d} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$ | Material damping in **dB/mm** | dB/mm |
| **DAC Curve Equation** $A(d) = A_{ref} + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_{ref}}\right) - 2\alpha(d - d_{ref})$ | Corrected amplitude vs. distance | dB |
| **Amplitude Ratio (AR)** $AR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A}{A_{ref}}\right)$ | Comparator with reference reflector | dB |
| **Equivalent Reflector (DGS)** $\Phi_{eq} = 10^{\left( \dfrac{\Delta G + DGS(\Phi_{ref}, d)}{S(d)} \right)}$ | Estimate defect size from DGS diagram | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC) curve** and the **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size)** diagram are the two principal graphical calibration methods based on reference blocks with artificial reflectors.

### 2.4 Why These Specific Sizes? — Engineering Rationale

1. **Reflector size selection** matches the smallest **critical defect size** of the inspected component (e.g., a 2 mm crack in a pressure vessel weld).
2. **Block dimensions** (e.g., 25 mm thick) provide sufficient **near-field length** and **depth of inspection** for standard 2–4 MHz transducers.
3. **Standardization** across industries ensures **inter-laboratory consistency** and **traceability to national standards (NIST, NPL, NABL)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Reference Block Type vs. Reflector Geometry vs. Application

| Block Type | Standard | Dimensions (mm) | Reflector Type & Size | Reflector Position | Primary Application |
|------------|----------|------------------|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **V1 (IIW-1)** | ISO 2400 | 25 × 100 × 305 | Φ1.5 mm SDH + Φ5 × 1.5 mm slot | At 44 mm from curved face | Beam index, angle, profile |
| **V2 (IIW-2)** | ISO 7963 | 25 × 100 × 250 | Φ5 mm SDH | At 100 mm | Time-base calibration |
| **DSC** | AWS D1.1 | 25 × 75 × 300 | 5 × Φ3 mm SDH | 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm | DAC / DGS construction |
| **AWS Resolution** | AWS D1.1 | 25 × 100 × 100 | 3 × Φ3 mm SDH | 25, 50, 75 mm | Resolution check |
| **ASTM E127 Area** | ASTM E127 | 25 × 75 × 250 | FBH 1.6/3.2/4.8/6.4/9.5 mm | Multiple depths | Sensitivity & size calibration |
| **Miniature** | Various | 5–10 × 25–50 × 50–100 | Φ1.0–1.5 mm FBH/SDH | Variable | Thin-wall components |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: DAC Curve Construction Using a DSC Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **DAC curve** compensates for the natural drop in echo amplitude as the reflector moves further from the transducer (due to beam spread, attenuation, and decreasing sensitivity in the far field).

**Step 1 — Material & Equipment Setup**

Assume:
- Test piece and block material: **carbon steel**, $v_L = 5920$ m/s.
- Transducer: **ΦD = 20 mm**, frequency **f = 4 MHz**, longitudinal wave.
- Reference reflectors: **Φ3 mm SDH at depths 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm** in DSC block.

**Step 2 — Compute the Near-Field Length N**

$$N = \frac{D^2 f}{4 v}$$

$$N = \frac{(0.020)^2 \times (4 \times 10^6)}{4 \times 5920}$$

$$N = \frac{0.0004 \times 4 \times 10^6}{23680}$$

$$N = \frac{1600}{23680} = 0.0676 \text{ m} \approx 67.6 \text{ mm}$$

*Interpretation*: The far-field begins at ~67.6 mm. The 25 mm and 50 mm holes are in the **near field**, while 75, 100, and 150 mm are in the **far field**.

**Step 3 — Measure the Maximum Amplitude at Each Hole**

Set the instrument at a fixed gain (e.g., **40 dB** baseline) and record the peak amplitude $A_i$ at each SDH.

| Depth, $d_i$ (mm) | Measured Peak Amplitude, $A_i$ (% FSH) | Equivalent dB above reference |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 25 | 80 % | 0 dB (reference) |
| 50 | 50 % | −4.0 dB |
| 75 | 32 % | −8.0 dB |
| 100 | 22 % | −11.2 dB |
| 150 | 13 % | −15.8 dB |

**Step 4 — Plot the DAC Curve**

On a graph with **distance (mm)** on the x-axis and **amplitude (dB)** on the y-axis, plot each point $(d_i, A_i)$. Connect with a smooth curve. This is the **DAC curve** for a **Φ3 mm SDH equivalent reflector**.

**Step 5 — Evaluate an Unknown Defect**

Suppose during actual inspection a defect at **80 mm depth** returns a peak amplitude of **45 % FSH** on the same instrument. Reading from the DAC curve, the expected amplitude for a Φ3 mm SDH at 80 mm is ≈ **30 % FSH** (≈ −8.5 dB). Since the defect amplitude (45 %) is **larger** than the Φ3 mm equivalent:

- The defect is **larger than Φ3 mm SDH equivalent**.
- It is recorded as a **"recorded indication"** and evaluated per acceptance criteria (e.g., ASME Sec. V, AWS D1.1).

**Step 6 — Apply Transfer Correction (for Different Material)**

If the reference block is in carbon steel but the test piece is **austenitic SS** with attenuation **α₂ = 0.05 dB/mm**:

$$\Delta DAC = 2 \alpha_2 (d - d_{ref})$$

At $d = 80$ mm and $d_{ref} = 25$ mm:

$$\Delta DAC = 2 \times 0.05 \times (80 - 25) = 5.5 \text{ dB}$$

Add this correction to the original DAC curve to obtain the **material-corrected DAC curve**.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Equivalent Reflector Size (DGS / AVG Method)

The **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size)** or **AVG (Abstand-Verstärkung-Größe)** method uses pre-calculated diagrams to estimate defect size from amplitude and distance.

**Step 1 — Obtain the DGS Diagram for the Transducer**

For a **Φ20 mm, 4 MHz** probe in steel, the manufacturer supplies a DGS diagram (log-log plot) of:

- Distance *d* (mm) on x-axis.
- Equivalent flat reflector size *Φ* (mm) on y-axis.
- Amplitude correction ΔG (dB) as curves.

**Step 2 — Measure the Defect**

A defect at **d = 60 mm** shows an amplitude corresponding to **−6 dB** (relative to a Φ3 mm SDH reference at 25 mm).

**Step 3 — Use the DGS Diagram**

From the DGS diagram for the Φ20/4 MHz probe at $d = 60$ mm:
- A **Φ5 mm** flat reflector produces ≈ −6 dB.
- A **Φ2 mm** flat reflector produces ≈ −14 dB.

**Step 4 — Estimate the Defect Size**

$$\Phi_{defect} \approx 5 \text{ mm (flat-bottom equivalent)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result is in **"equivalent flat-bottom hole" (EFBH)** units. For actual acceptance, the equivalent size is compared with the acceptance code (e.g., ASME Sec. VIII, EN 10160, AWS D1.1).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Calculation: Attenuation Coefficient (α)

The attenuation in **dB/mm** is critical for any DAC transfer between block and component.

**Given**:
- Two SDH reflectors in the same block at depths **$d_1 = 25$ mm** and **$d_2 = 100$ mm**.
- Measured amplitudes: $A_1 = 80\%$, $A_2 = 22\%$.

**Step 1 — Convert to dB**

$$A_1 \text{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(80) = 38.06 \text{ dB}$$

$$A_2 \text{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(22) = 26.85 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Difference**

$$\Delta A = 38.06 - 26.85 = 11.21 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Attenuation Formula**

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta A}{2(d_2 - d_1)}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{11.21}{2 \times (100 - 25)} = \frac{11.21}{150} = 0.0747 \text{ dB/mm}$$

*Interpretation*: This is the **material attenuation coefficient** — typical of **fine-grained carbon steel** (α ≈ 0.05 to 0.10 dB/mm at 4 MHz).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"What is a reference block? List any two standards that govern the use of reference blocks in ultrasonic testing."**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

A **reference block** is a standardized test specimen containing artificially created defects (reflectors) of known size, shape, and position. It is used to calibrate UT equipment, establish sensitivity, verify beam characteristics, and construct DAC/DGS curves.

Two governing standards:
1. **ASTM E127** — Standard practice for fabricating and controlling steel reference blocks used in ultrasonic testing.
2. **ISO 2400** — Specifies the dimensions and features of the IIW V1 reference block for UT calibration.

*(Acceptable alternatives: ASTM E428, IIW V1/V2, ISO 7963, AWS D1.1)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a Side-Drilled Hole (SDH) and a Flat-Bottom Hole (FBH) used in ultrasonic reference blocks."**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

| Feature | Side-Drilled Hole (SDH) | Flat-Bottom Hole (FBH) |
|---------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Geometry** | Cylindrical hole drilled from the side, parallel to scan surface | Cylindrical hole drilled from the back, perpendicular to scan surface |
| **Reflecting surface** | Curved lateral surface (acts as a "point" reflector) | Flat circular bottom (perpendicular to beam) |
| **Common sizes** | Φ1.5, Φ3, Φ5 mm | Φ1.6, Φ3.2, Φ4.8, Φ6.4, Φ9.5 mm |
| **Standards** | IIW V1, V2, AWS DSC | ASTM E127 |
| **Reflectivity** | Variable with angle; sensitive to scanning direction | Direction-independent; flat surface |
| **Primary use** | Time-base, beam profile, DAC construction | Sensitivity, size estimation (DGS) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit the standard reference** (ASTM, IIW, ISO) when describing block dimensions — this costs 1–2 marks.
> - In DAC problems, students frequently **forget the factor of 2** in the attenuation formula (round-trip travel). Always check: $\alpha = \frac{\Delta A}{2(d_2-d_1)}$, **not** $\frac{\Delta A}{(d_2-d_1)}$.
> - For DGS problems, students confuse the **probe size D with the defect size Φ**. They are different: D = transducer diameter, Φ = equivalent flat reflector diameter.
> - Always mention **couplant, surface preparation, and block material** in calibration descriptions — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for these practical details.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Describe in detail the constructional features and applications of the IIW V1 reference block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a)**:

The **IIW V1 (Type 1) reference block** is specified by **ISO 2400** and is the most widely used block for ultrasonic probe calibration.

**Constructional Features:**
1. **Dimensions**: 25 mm thick × 100 mm wide × 305 mm long (or 150 mm compact).
2. **Material**: Fine-grained low-carbon steel, acoustic velocity ≈ 5920 m/s, surface finish ≤ 0.8 µm Ra.
3. **Curved face**: A **50 mm radius** cylindrical surface machined on one end for transducer footprint, beam profile, and index point evaluation.
4. **Engraved scales**: 25 mm and 50 mm divisions on the top surface for direct distance measurement.
5. **Reflectors**:
   - A **Φ1.5 mm side-drilled hole** at 44 mm from the curved face, half-thickness depth.
   - A **Φ5 mm × 1.5 mm deep slot** at 88 mm for shear wave evaluation.
6. **Identification marking**: Standard code, manufacturer, and material grade stamped on one face.

**Applications:**
1. **Index point** and **time-base** calibration of longitudinal and shear wave probes.
2. **Beam angle verification** for angle-beam probes.
3. **Beam profile and beam diameter** assessment.
4. **Resolution** and **sensitivity** check.
5. **Transducer evaluation** per ASTM E1065 and EN 12668.

---

**(b) A 4 MHz, Φ20 mm longitudinal probe is used on a steel block. The near-field length is calculated to be 67.6 mm. A Φ3 mm SDH at 25 mm depth gives a peak amplitude of 80 % FSH, and at 100 mm depth gives 22 % FSH. Calculate: (i) the attenuation coefficient of the material, and (ii) the expected amplitude from a defect at 75 mm depth, if it were a Φ3 mm equivalent reflector. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b)**:

**(i) Attenuation coefficient α**

Convert amplitudes to dB:
$$A_1 = 20 \log_{10}(80) = 38.06 \text{ dB}$$
$$A_2 = 20 \log_{10}(22) = 26.85 \text{ dB}$$

Amplitude difference:
$$\Delta A = 38.06 - 26.85 = 11.21 \text{ dB}$$

Distance difference:
$$\Delta d = 100 - 25 = 75 \text{ mm}$$

Apply attenuation formula (round-trip):
$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta A}{2 \Delta d} = \frac{11.21}{2 \times 75} = \frac{11.21}{150}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.0747 \text{ dB/mm}}$$

**(ii) Expected amplitude at 75 mm**

Distance from reference (25 mm): $\Delta d = 75 - 25 = 50$ mm.

Expected drop due to beam spread + attenuation:
$$\Delta A_{total} = 2 \alpha \Delta d + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{75}{25}\right)$$
$$\Delta A_{total} = 2 \times 0.0747 \times 50 + 20 \log_{10}(3)$$
$$\Delta A_{total} = 7.47 + 9.54 = 17.01 \text{ dB}$$

New amplitude in dB:
$$A_{75} = 38.06 - 17.01 = 21.05 \text{ dB}$$

Convert to % FSH:
$$A_{75} = 10^{21.05/20} = 10^{1.0525}$$

$$\boxed{A_{75} \approx 11.3 \% \text{ FSH}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the fabrication procedure, reflector types, and typical sizes used in ASTM E127 reference blocks. Discuss the role of flat-bottom holes in DAC curve construction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a)**:

**Fabrication Procedure (per ASTM E428 + E127):**
1. **Material selection**: Fine-grained killed carbon steel (AISI 1018/1020) with **grain size ≥ ASTM 6** and certified sound velocity.
2. **Heat treatment**: Stress relieve at 595 ± 15 °C for 1 hour per inch of thickness, slow cool.
3. **Machining**: Grind to final dimensions of 25 mm × 75 mm × 250 mm with surface roughness ≤ 0.8 µm Ra.
4. **Drilling reflectors**: Use precision gun-drilling for FBH at the required depths (T/4, T/2, 3T/4).
5. **Calibration**: Each block is calibrated against a master block traceable to NIST/NPL.
6. **Marking**: Engrave code, size, depth, and serial number.

**Reflector Types & Sizes:**
- **FBH** diameters: 1.6, 3.2, 4.8, 6.4, 9.5 mm.
- Position: typically at depths of 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm.
- Material of reference block: matched to test piece or to a **calibration material**.

**Role of FBH in DAC:**
- Each FBH produces a **flat, predictable reflection** because its orientation is **perpendicular to the beam axis**.
- Reflectivity is **independent of the scanning direction** (unlike SDH).
- Five FBHs at different depths allow **simultaneous plotting of amplitude vs. distance**, yielding a complete DAC curve.
- This DAC curve compensates for **beam spread, attenuation, and instrument non-linearity**.

---

**(b) A carbon steel block contains 5 FBH reflectors at depths 25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 mm. The measured amplitudes (at 4 MHz, 20 mm probe) are: 90, 70, 52, 38, and 24 % FSH respectively. Construct the DAC curve and determine: (i) the equivalent reflector size of a defect at 80 mm producing 60 % FSH, and (ii) the recording level if the acceptance level is 50 % of the DAC curve. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b)**:

**DAC Curve Construction:**

Convert each amplitude to dB (taking 25 mm / 90 % as 0 dB reference):

| Depth (mm) | Amplitude (%) | Amplitude (dB) | dB from reference |
|------------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 25 | 90 | 39.08 | 0 |
| 50 | 70 | 36.90 | −2.18 |
| 75 | 52 | 34.32 | −4.76 |
| 100 | 38 | 31.60 | −7.48 |
| 150 | 24 | 27.60 | −11.48 |

Plot these points on a distance-vs-dB graph to obtain the **DAC curve for a 4.8 mm FBH** (typical reference size for ASTM E127 block of this geometry).

**(i) Equivalent Reflector Size of the Defect at 80 mm**

The defect at 80 mm produces 60 % FSH, equivalent to:
$$A_{defect} = 20 \log_{10}(60) = 35.56 \text{ dB}$$

From the DAC curve at 80 mm depth, the **reference 4.8 mm FBH** produces ≈ **34.5 dB** (interpolated from 75 mm and 100 mm).

Difference:
$$\Delta = 35.56 - 34.5 = +1.06 \text{ dB}$$

The defect is **+1.06 dB** above the DAC reference — so it is **larger than a 4.8 mm FBH equivalent**.

Using DGS extrapolation, +1 dB ≈ **20 % larger** → equivalent size ≈ **5.8 mm FBH** (or roughly between 4.8 and 6.4 mm).

**(ii) Recording Level at 50 % of DAC**

The recording level is the DAC curve shifted down by:
$$\Delta_{RL} = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

So the recording level lies **6 dB below** the reference DAC curve. Any indication whose amplitude exceeds this level must be **recorded, evaluated, and reported** per the inspection code.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU Exam Day**

- [ ] **Reference Block (Test Block)**: Standard specimen with **known, reproducible reflectors** for UT/RT/ECT calibration.
- [ ] **IIW V1 Block**: 25 × 100 × 305 mm; **Φ1.5 mm SDH**; **50 mm radius** curved face; per **ISO 2400**.
- [ ] **IIW V2 Block**: 25 × 100 × 250 mm; **Φ5 mm SDH** at 100 mm; per **ISO 7963**.
- [ ] **DSC Block**: 25 × 75 × 300 mm; **five Φ3 mm SDHs** at 25, 50, 75, 100, 150 mm; per **AWS D1.1**.
- [ ] **ASTM E127 Block**: 25 × 75 × 250 mm; **five FBHs** (1.6, 3.2, 4.8, 6.4, 9.5 mm).
- [ ] **Reflector Types**: SDH, FBH, EDM Notch (0.2–2.0 mm), Saw Cut, TCB.
- [ ] **Standard SDH Diameters**: 0.8, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 mm.
- [ ] **Standard FBH Diameters**: 1.6, 3.2, 4.8, 6.4, 9.5 mm.
- [ ] **Attenuation Formula**: $\alpha = \frac{\Delta A}{2(d_2 - d_1)}$ → units **dB/mm** (factor of 2 is **mandatory**).
- [ ] **DAC Curve**: Plots amplitude vs. distance for a fixed-size reflector; compensates for **beam spread + attenuation**.
- [ ] **DGS / AVG Method**: Estimates **equivalent flat reflector size** of an unknown defect from amplitude & distance.
- [ ] **Master vs. Working Block**: Master is traceable to **NIST/NPL**; working is for field use.
- [ ] **Block Material**: Matched to the test piece acoustically and metallurgically.
- [ ] **Key Standards**: ASTM E127, ASTM E428, IIW (V1, V2), ISO 2400, ISO 7963, AWS D1.1.
- [ ] **Couplant, surface finish, and grain size** are critical practical considerations in calibration.
- [ ] **Recording level** is typically set **6 dB below** the reference DAC curve.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Calibration of NDT Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Calibration** is the documented process of comparing an NDT instrument's measurement output against a known reference standard under controlled conditions, to verify its accuracy, repeatability, and traceability to national/international standards.

In the NDT domain, calibration is the act of *tuning the instrument's ears* so it can "listen" to defects with verified accuracy. Without calibration, any reading—be it thickness, hardness, conductivity, or radiation dose—is meaningless.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy: Weighing Scale at a Grocery Shop**
> Imagine a shopkeeper using a weighing scale. If the scale shows 1 kg when nothing is placed on it, or shows 1.2 kg for a 1 kg standard weight, all future weighings will be wrong. To fix this, the shopkeeper places a **standard 1 kg weight** on the scale and adjusts it until it reads exactly 1 kg. This is calibration. In NDT, the "standard weight" is called a **calibration block** (e.g., IIW block for ultrasonic, ISO wire for eddy current) and the "scale" is the NDT probe/instrument.

### Why Calibration is Critical in NDT
- **Reliability**: Ensures defect signals are real, not instrument drift.
- **Traceability**: Links measurements back to national standards (NABL, NIST, ISO 9712).
- **Acceptance/Rejection Decisions**: Code compliance (ASME, AWS, EN) demands calibrated instruments.
- **Legal Defensibility**: An uncalibrated instrument can make an inspection report legally invalid.

### Types of Calibration in NDT
| Type | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-use calibration** | Quick check before starting work | Sensitivity setting on UT flaw detector |
| **Periodic calibration** | Scheduled routine check | Daily dose meter check in radiography |
| **Post-repair calibration** | After instrument servicing | Recalibrating an eddy current probe after cable replacement |
| **Reference calibration** | Against a primary standard | Sending dosimeter to a national lab |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Calibration Block:** A reference test piece with artificially introduced reflectors (flat-bottom holes, side-drilled holes, notches) of **known dimensions** used to set the sensitivity of an NDT instrument.

---

## 1.2 Radiography Testing (RT) — Electromagnetic Radiation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Radiography Testing (RT)** is a volumetric NDT method that uses penetrating **ionising electromagnetic radiation** (X-rays or gamma rays) to record internal features of an object on a detector (film or digital sensor), producing a permanent **radiograph**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy: Airport Luggage Scanning**
> At airport security, your bag passes through a scanner that uses X-rays. Dense objects (metal) absorb more X-rays and cast a darker shadow; light objects (clothes) let X-rays pass and appear lighter. The image reveals the hidden contents *without opening the bag*. Industrial radiography works the same way, but with far higher energy and much stricter controls.

### Electromagnetic Radiation Sources for RT

Industrial RT uses two broad categories of ionising radiation sources:

1. **X-rays (Artificial / Machine-generated)**
   - Produced by an **X-ray tube** (Coolidge-type, linear accelerator, betatron).
   - Energy range: **50 keV to 25 MeV**.
   - The tube is switched ON only during exposure, then OFF → safer storage.

2. **Gamma rays (Radioactive Isotopes)**
   - Emitted by the **nucleus** of a radioactive isotope during decay.
   - Energy is **fixed and characteristic** of the isotope.
   - Continuously emitting → requires shielded container (camera/exposure device).

### The Electromagnetic Spectrum & RT Band
Industrial radiography uses the high-frequency, short-wavelength region of the EM spectrum, just below cosmic rays.

| Radiation Type | Wavelength (λ) | Frequency (ν) | Source for RT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visible light | 400–700 nm | 430–750 THz | None |
| UV | 10–400 nm | 750 THz–30 PHz | None |
| **X-rays** | 0.01–10 nm | 30 PHz–30 EHz | **X-ray tube** |
| **Gamma rays** | < 0.01 nm | > 30 EHz | **Isotopes** |

### Key Isotopes Used in Industrial RT
| Isotope | Energy (MeV) | Half-life | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cobalt-60 (Co-60)** | 1.17 & 1.33 | 5.27 years | Steel > 100 mm |
| **Iridium-192 (Ir-192)** | 0.31–0.62 | 73.8 days | Steel 10–100 mm |
| **Caesium-137 (Cs-137)** | 0.662 | 30.2 years | Thin steel, light alloys |
| **Thulium-170 (Tm-170)** | 0.084 | 128.6 days | Thin sections, aluminium |

---

## 1.3 Inspection Technique — SWSI (Single Wall Single Image)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> **Single Wall Single Image (SWSI)** is a radiographic projection technique in which the radiation source and the film (or detector) are positioned on **opposite sides of the test object**, and **one single exposure** produces **one image** of the entire **single wall thickness** penetrated by the beam.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Photographing a Coin Held Against a Lamp**
> Hold a coin between you and a bright lamp. Place a photo paper behind the coin. The lamp (source) emits light through the coin (object), and the paper (film) captures the silhouette. The entire thickness of the coin is penetrated and imaged in **one shot**. This is exactly SWSI. If you wanted to image *both* the front and back faces of the coin, you would need either a second exposure (DWDI) or a different geometry (DWSI).

### When is SWSI Used?
- Single-wall vessels, pipes, and weld joints accessible from both sides.
- Recommended by **ASME V Article 2** as the **preferred** technique.
- Wall thickness up to ~100 mm for Ir-192, thicker for Co-60.

### Positioning of Source, Object, and Film
In SWSI:
- Source → one face of the object.
- Film (with lead screens) → opposite face, wrapped around or placed behind the object.
- Beam penetrates **one wall thickness** to expose the film.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Calibration — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Calibration Loop (Closed-Loop Concept)
A proper calibration is a **closed-loop traceable process**:

**Instrument → Reference Standard → Reading → Compare with Standard → Adjust / Document**

If the deviation exceeds the **tolerance band** (specified by code), the instrument is **rejected** and either recalibrated or sent for repair.

### 2.1.2 Traceability Pyramid
The chain of evidence from a workshop instrument to a national standard:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ International Primary Standard   │  (BIPM, Paris)
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ National Standard (NABL / NIST) │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ Working Standard (Lab-level)    │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ Site / Field Reference Standard │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│ Instrument under Calibration     │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Traceability:** The unbroken chain of comparisons linking an instrument's measurement to a recognised national or international standard. Required by ISO 17025 and ISO 9712.

### 2.1.3 Common Calibration Standards in NDT
| NDT Method | Calibration Block / Standard |
|---|---|
| Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | IIW V1 / V2 block, ASME calibration block |
| Radiographic Testing (RT) | ASTM E 1025 IQI (Image Quality Indicator), IQI wire penetrameter |
| Magnetic Particle (MT) | MT test block, magnetic field indicator |
| Dye Penetrant (PT) | ASTM E 1417 reference block, cracked panel |
| Eddy Current (ET) | Reference standards with known flaws (notches, holes) |

### 2.1.4 What is an IQI (Image Quality Indicator)?
A small metal strip with **wires**, **holes**, or **steps** of known dimensions, placed on the test object. After exposure, the smallest visible feature on the film defines the **sensitivity** of the radiograph. Common types: **Wire-type IQI** (ASTM E 747) and **Hole-type IQI** (ASTM E 1025).

---

## 2.2 Physics of Electromagnetic Radiation for RT

### 2.2.1 The Dual Nature of Radiation
EM radiation in the RT range behaves as **both**:
- A **wave** (frequency ν, wavelength λ)
- A **particle** (photon with energy E)

The two descriptions are linked by fundamental equations.

### 2.2.2 Generation of X-rays in a Coolidge Tube

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. A **tungsten filament (cathode)** is heated → emits electrons by **thermionic emission**.
2. A high potential difference (**kV**) accelerates these electrons toward the **target anode** (tungsten, rhodium, or molybdenum).
3. On impact, electrons lose kinetic energy in two ways:
   - **Bremsstrahlung** ("braking radiation") — continuous spectrum.
   - **Characteristic radiation** — discrete peaks (Kα, Kβ) corresponding to electron shell transitions.

### 2.2.3 X-ray Spectrum Characteristics
- **Continuous (Bremsstrahlung) spectrum**: from 0 up to a maximum energy equal to the accelerating voltage (in keV).
- **Characteristic peaks**: depend on the **target material** (e.g., tungsten K-edge at 69.5 keV).
- **kV (kilovoltage)** controls the **penetrating power** (quality) of the X-ray beam.
- **mA (milliamperage)** controls the **intensity / quantity** of photons.

### 2.2.4 Production of Gamma Rays
Unlike X-rays, gamma rays originate from the **atomic nucleus** of an unstable isotope. Common industrial isotopes:

- **Ir-192** (most widely used in field radiography) — emits photons of average ~0.4 MeV.
- **Co-60** — used for thick steel sections (> 100 mm).
- **Cs-137** — used for thin sections and low-density alloys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - X-ray intensity & energy can be **controlled** (kV, mA).
> - Gamma ray energy is **fixed** (isotope-specific) and intensity decays with **half-life**.

---

## 2.3 SWSI — Detailed Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Geometric Requirements of SWSI
For a **valid SWSI** radiograph, the following conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Source-side accessibility**: The radiation source can be placed on one side.
2. **Film-side accessibility**: The film can be placed on the opposite side.
3. **Coverage of full weld/joint**: A single exposure covers the area of interest.
4. **Penetration of single wall**: The beam must traverse **only one wall** of the test piece.

### 2.3.2 Geometric Unsharpness (U_g) in SWSI
Geometric unsharpness is the **penumbra** (blurring) at the edge of a defect image. It depends on source-to-film and source-to-object distances.

The minimum three distances in SWSI geometry are:
- **d** = source-to-object distance (focus-to-specimen)
- **b** = object-to-film distance (specimen-to-detector)
- **D** = source-to-film distance (focus-to-detector) = d + b

### 2.3.3 Why SWSI is Preferred (per ASME V)
- Less **geometric distortion** of the defect image.
- Lower **unsharpness** because object-to-film distance is small.
- Defects are imaged in their **true size and shape** (near-true scale).
- Better IQI visibility.

### 2.3.4 Geometric Unsharpness — Visual Logic
Consider a **point source** (idealised) and a real source of **finite size (F)**. A defect in the object casts a fully dark (umbra) region and a partially dark (penumbra) region. The **penumbra width** is the geometric unsharpness U_g.

```
  Source (size F)        Object (defect)         Film
     ┃                       ┃                    ┃
   ┏┻┓                 ╔══╗                  ┏━━━━┓
   ┃ ┃  ──d──→         ║  ║  ──b──→          ┃░░░┃
   ┗┳┛                 ╚══╝                  ┗━━━━┛
                                         ↑
                                    Film image
                                   has sharp core
                                   (umbra) + fuzzy
                                   border (penumbra
                                   = U_g)
```

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations are **mathematically verifiable** and **universally valid** in NDT-RT practice.

| S. No. | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu$ | J (or eV) | h = Planck's constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s |
| 2 | Photon energy (in eV) | $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | eV | Convenient form for X-ray wavelengths |
| 3 | Max X-ray photon energy | $E_{max} = eV$ | eV | V is tube voltage in volts |
| 4 | Inverse square law (intensity) | $I = \dfrac{I_0}{D^2}$ | W/m² or mR/h | D = distance from source |
| 5 | Exposure intensity ratio | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Used to compute exposure time change |
| 6 | Geometric unsharpness | $U_g = F \times \dfrac{b}{d}$ | mm | F = focal spot size, b = obj-film, d = source-obj |
| 7 | Magnification | $M = \dfrac{D}{d} = 1 + \dfrac{b}{d}$ | dimensionless | For contact = 1; for projection = > 1 |
| 8 | Exposure at distance D₂ | $E_2 = E_1 \times \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$ | mR, R | Convert time for new distance |
| 9 | Half-life (decay) | $N = N_0 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t / t_{1/2}}$ | atoms / Ci | Gamma source activity decay |
| 10 | Activity needed for exposure | $A = \dfrac{E \times (D)^2}{K \times t}$ | Ci | K = gamma constant of isotope |
| 11 | Density (radiographic) | $D_f = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | dimensionless | Optical density of film |
| 12 | Photon speed (vacuum) | $c = \lambda \nu = 3 \times 10^8$ | m/s | Universal constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use of These Equations:**
> - Formula 4/5/8 → used **daily** by radiographers to convert exposure times when source is moved closer or farther.
> - Formula 6 → used to verify IQI sensitivity and required focal spot size.
> - Formula 9 → used to calculate source **decay correction factor (DCF)** for ageing Co-60 or Ir-192.

---

## 2.5 Sensitivity in Radiography

**Radiographic sensitivity** is the **smallest detectable change in thickness** (as % of total thickness) visible on the film. It is determined by the **IQI** visible on the developed radiograph.

$$\text{Sensitivity \%} = \frac{\text{Smallest visible IQI wire diameter}}{\text{Total specimen thickness}} \times 100$$

> **Typical sensitivity values (KTU exam-friendly):** 1 % to 2 % for good industrial radiography.
