# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Photon Energy from Wave Properties

> **Goal:** Show that the energy of a photon is directly related to its wavelength, and convert to eV for X-ray calculations.

We start with two fundamental relations:
- Energy of a photon (Einstein): $E = h\nu$
- Wave relation: $c = \lambda \nu$ → $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$

**Step 1:** Substitute the wave relation into the energy equation.

$$E = h\nu = h \cdot \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the numerical values of the constants.
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- Conversion: $1\ \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator in J·m, then convert wavelength to nm.

$$hc = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (2.998 \times 10^{8}) = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$$

**Step 4:** Express the energy in eV by dividing by the eV-to-Joule conversion factor.

$$E (\text{in eV}) = \dfrac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times \lambda(\text{m})} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$$

**Conclusion:** A simple, exam-friendly formula:

$$E(\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Use:** For Ir-192 average λ ≈ 0.024 nm → E ≈ 1240 / 0.024 ≈ 51,667 eV ≈ 51.7 keV (consistent with Ir-192 average energy).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Geometric Unsharpness (U_g)

> **Goal:** Prove the universal formula $U_g = F \cdot \dfrac{b}{d}$ using similar triangles.

**Setup:** A source of finite diameter **F**, a defect point **P** at the object plane, located a distance **d** from the source and **b** from the film. The image on the film is a circle of total width **U_g** (umbra + penumbra).

```
        Source (diameter F)
          ●━━━━━●
          ┃     ┃
          ┃     ┃  ← beam diverges
        d ┃     ┃
          ┃     ┃
          ●━━━━━● ← Defect (point P)
          ┃     ┃
        b ┃     ┃
          ┃     ┃
   ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ← Film plane
   |←──  U_g (penumbra width) ──→|
```

**Step 1:** Form two similar triangles by considering the cone of radiation passing through the defect point.

- Triangle 1 (from source to defect): base = F, height = d
- Triangle 2 (from source to film): base = U_g, height = d + b

**Step 2:** By similarity of triangles, the ratio of bases equals the ratio of heights.

$$\frac{F}{d} = \frac{U_g}{d + b}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for U_g.

$$U_g = F \cdot \dfrac{d + b}{d} = F \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{b}{d}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Special case — when the object is in contact with the film (b ≈ 0), U_g → 0 (sharpest image). For typical industrial RT, **b is small**, and we use the simplified form.

$$U_g \approx F \cdot \dfrac{b}{d}\quad \text{(when b} \ll \text{d)}$$

**Step 5:** Useful inversion — for a required unsharpness U_g, the minimum source-to-object distance is:

$$d_{min} = F \cdot \dfrac{b}{U_g} \quad \text{(rearranged)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** If the focal spot F = 2 mm, b = 10 mm, and we need U_g ≤ 0.4 mm, then d ≥ 2 × 10 / 0.4 = 50 mm. This sets the **minimum source-to-object distance** for acceptable image quality.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for Radiation Intensity

> **Goal:** Show that intensity falls as 1/D² and derive the time-conversion formula.

**Step 1:** Consider a point source radiating uniformly in all directions. At distance D, the radiation passes through a sphere of surface area $4\pi D^2$.

**Step 2:** If the source power is **P**, then intensity at distance D is:

$$I = \dfrac{P}{4\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3:** At two distances D₁ and D₂:

$$\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{D_2^2}{D_1^2}$$

**Step 4:** For a given exposure (E = I × t), the **time needed** at a new distance is:

$$E_1 = E_2 \implies I_1 t_1 = I_2 t_2$$

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = t_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a 5-minute exposure at 800 mm gives a good film, what is the exposure at 1600 mm?  
> $t_2 = 5 \times (1600/800)^2 = 5 \times 4 = 20$ minutes — i.e., **4× longer** when distance is doubled.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SWSI vs Other Radiographic Techniques

| Parameter | **SWSI** | **SWDI** | **DWSI** | **DWDI** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full form | Single Wall Single Image | Single Wall Double Image | Double Wall Single Image | Double Wall Double Image |
| Wall(s) penetrated | One | One | Two | Two |
| Image(s) per exposure | One | Two | One | Two |
| Source placement | One side | One side | Inside pipe (panoramic) | One side (offset) |
| Used for | Plates, vessels | Plate (less preferred) | Pipe circumferential | Small-bore pipe |
| ASME V preference | **Preferred** | Acceptable | Special cases | Limited |
| Geometric distortion | Lowest | Low | Higher | Highest |
| Typical application | Weld joints of tanks & vessels | When two views of same wall needed | Small-diameter pipe | Small-bore tube welds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Favourite Question:** *"Compare SWSI and DWDI techniques."* — Use the above matrix as a study frame.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 200 mm × 200 mm square radiographic film is to record a weld of length 150 mm. The source-to-film distance is 800 mm, source-to-object distance is 750 mm, and focal spot size is 2.0 mm. The object is a 20 mm thick steel plate.
> 
> (a) Calculate the geometric unsharpness U_g.
> (b) Calculate the magnification M.
> (c) Comment on whether U_g is acceptable (limit = 0.5 mm).

**Step (a):** Object-to-film distance b = 800 − 750 = 50 mm.

$$U_g = F \cdot \dfrac{b}{d} = 2.0 \times \dfrac{50}{750} = 0.133\ \text{mm}$$

**Step (b):** Magnification:

$$M = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{800}{750} = 1.067 \approx 1.07$$

**Step (c):** Since 0.133 mm < 0.5 mm limit, the **U_g is acceptable** — the radiograph will be sharp.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`
> **Define calibration in the context of NDT. List any two types of calibration standards used in NDT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Calibration is the documented process of comparing an NDT instrument's measurement against a known reference standard under controlled conditions, to verify its accuracy and traceability to national/international standards.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** Ensures reliability of defect detection, compliance with codes, and legal validity of inspection reports.
- **Two standards (1 mark):** (i) **IIW V1/V2 block** for ultrasonic testing, (ii) **ASTM E 1025 IQI** for radiographic testing, (iii) **MT test block** for magnetic particle testing.

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`
> **Compare X-rays and gamma rays as radiation sources for industrial radiography (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| S.No. | X-rays | Gamma rays |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Produced by X-ray tube using high voltage. | Emitted by radioactive isotope nucleus. |
| 2 | Energy can be varied (50 keV – 25 MeV). | Energy is fixed, characteristic of isotope. |
| 3 | Source can be switched OFF when not in use. | Source continuously emits; needs shielded storage. |
| 4 | Spectrum is continuous (Bremsstrahlung) + characteristic peaks. | Discrete line spectrum. |
| 5 | Intensity controlled by kV (penetration) and mA (quantity). | Intensity decays with half-life. |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]`
> **(a)** Explain the **SWSI (Single Wall Single Image)** radiographic technique in detail. With a neat labelled sketch, describe the arrangement of source, object, and film. List the conditions under which SWSI is preferred. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A 25 mm thick steel plate is radiographed using Ir-192 source. The source-to-object distance is 600 mm and the source-to-film distance is 650 mm. The focal spot is 1.5 mm. Determine: (i) geometric unsharpness, (ii) magnification, and (iii) state whether the image quality is acceptable for an IQI sensitivity of 1 % (allowable U_g limit = 0.5 mm). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) SWSI Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** SWSI is a radiographic projection technique in which the source and the film are placed on opposite sides of the test object, and a single exposure produces a single image showing the penetrated single wall.

- **Sketch (2 marks):** 
```
      SOURCE (Ir-192 / X-ray tube)
           │
           │  ← d = source-to-object
           │
     ┌─────────────┐
     │   OBJECT    │  ← test piece / weld
     │   (single   │
     │   wall)     │
     └─────────────┘
           │
           │  ← b = object-to-film
           │
     ┌─────────────┐
     │ FILM + Lead │  ← with IQI on source side
     │  Screens    │
     └─────────────┘
```

- **Conditions for use (2 marks):**
  1. Single-wall access from both sides.
  2. Wall thickness within source energy range.
  3. Recommended by ASME V as preferred technique.
  4. Best for butt welds in plates, vessels, large-diameter pipes.

- **Advantages (1 mark):** Lowest geometric distortion, sharpest image, true-to-size defect image, best IQI visibility.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Plate thickness t = 25 mm
- Source-to-object distance d = 600 mm
- Source-to-film distance D = 650 mm
- Focal spot F = 1.5 mm
- Allowable U_g = 0.5 mm

**Step 1:** Object-to-film distance:
$$b = D - d = 650 - 600 = 50\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2:** Geometric unsharpness:
$$U_g = F \cdot \dfrac{b}{d} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{50}{600} = 0.125\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Magnification:
$$M = \dfrac{D}{d} = \dfrac{650}{600} = 1.083$$

**Step 4:** Decision on image quality:
- Computed U_g = 0.125 mm
- Allowable U_g = 0.5 mm
- Since 0.125 < 0.5, the **geometric unsharpness is well within the acceptable limit**, and the image quality will be **acceptable** for 1 % IQI sensitivity.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The exposure parameters are satisfactory; the radiograph will be sharp enough to resolve a 1 % thickness variation (≈ 0.25 mm) in the 25 mm steel plate.

---

> ### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]`
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Coolidge-type X-ray tube**. Discuss Bremsstrahlung and characteristic X-ray production. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** What is **calibration of NDT equipment**? Explain the concept of **traceability**. Discuss the role of **IQI** in radiographic calibration with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (14 marks):**

**(a) Coolidge X-ray Tube (7 marks):**

- **Construction (3 marks):**
  1. **Cathode:** Tungsten filament heated by low-voltage transformer (thermionic emission).
  2. **Anode (Target):** Tungsten block embedded in copper stem, often angled at 20–22° (line-focus principle).
  3. **High-voltage transformer:** 30–450 kV accelerates electrons from cathode to anode.
  4. **Vacuum envelope:** Glass or metal-ceramic envelope maintains 10⁻⁶ Torr vacuum to prevent electron scattering.
  5. **Cooling system:** Water or oil circulation removes ~99 % of input energy converted to heat.
  6. **Beryllium window:** For low-energy tubes, allows soft X-rays to exit with minimal absorption.

- **Working (2 marks):**
  1. Filament heated → electrons emitted.
  2. kV applied → electrons accelerated towards anode.
  3. Electrons strike target → kinetic energy converted to X-rays and heat.
  4. X-ray beam exits through the window toward the test object.

- **X-ray Production Mechanisms (2 marks):**
  1. **Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation):** Electron decelerates in the strong field of target nucleus → emits a continuous spectrum of photons with energies from 0 to E_max = eV.
  2. **Characteristic Radiation:** Incident electron ejects an inner-shell (K-shell) electron of the target atom. An outer-shell electron drops in to fill the vacancy, emitting a photon of energy equal to the difference between the two shells (Kα, Kβ lines). This produces **discrete peaks** superimposed on the continuous spectrum.

**(b) Calibration and IQI (7 marks):**

- **Calibration definition (2 marks):** As defined in Section 1.1 — the documented comparison of an instrument's output against a known reference standard under controlled conditions, to verify accuracy, repeatability, and traceability.

- **Traceability (2 marks):** The unbroken chain of comparisons that links an instrument's measurement to a recognised national or international standard (BIPM → NABL/NIST → Working Standard → Field Standard → Site Instrument). Required for ISO 17025 accreditation.

- **IQI Role (3 marks):** 
  - An **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**, also called a **penetrameter**, is a small metal strip containing wires (ASTM E 747) or holes (ASTM E 1025) of known dimensions, placed on the **source side** of the object during radiographic exposure.
  - After development, the **smallest visible IQI feature** defines the radiographic sensitivity.
  - **Example:** For a 25 mm steel plate, the minimum visible wire of 0.25 mm (i.e., 1 % sensitivity) on the developed film confirms that the radiographic technique can detect a 1 % change in thickness.
  - IQI also serves to verify geometric unsharpness, contrast, and beam alignment — making it the **calibration reference for radiographic image quality**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing kV and mA roles** — kV controls **penetration (quality)**, mA controls **intensity (quantity)** of X-ray beam. Many students invert these.
> 2. **Forgetting the b = D − d relation** — in U_g calculations, students often use d instead of b, or forget to subtract.
> 3. **Writing "calibration = testing the equipment"** — calibration is **comparison against a standard**, not just checking if the instrument works.
> 4. **Mixing up "characteristic" and "Bremsstrahlung"** — Bremsstrahlung is **continuous spectrum**; characteristic radiation is **discrete lines**.
> 5. **SWSI vs DWDI confusion** — SWSI penetrates **one wall**; DWDI penetrates **two walls** in a single exposure.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units in formulas.
> 7. **Wrong sign or wrong formula in inverse-square law** — the correct relation is $I_1/I_2 = (D_2/D_1)^2$, not $(D_1/D_2)^2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Calibration & Radiography Sources + SWSI)**

### Calibration Essentials
- [ ] Calibration = **comparison against a known standard**, not just verification.
- [ ] Two prerequisites: **reference standard** and **tolerance band**.
- [ ] Traceability chain: **BIPM → NABL → Working Standard → Field Standard → Instrument**.
- [ ] Common NDT calibration blocks: IIW V1/V2, ASTM E 1025 IQI, MT test block, ET reference standards.
- [ ] Required for **ISO 17025, ISO 9712, ASME V, AWS D1.1** compliance.

### Electromagnetic Radiation Sources for RT
- [ ] RT uses **ionising EM radiation** (X-rays and gamma rays).
- [ ] **X-ray** spectrum has **Bremsstrahlung (continuous)** + **Characteristic (discrete)** components.
- [ ] **kV** = penetration (quality); **mA** = intensity (quantity).
- [ ] **Gamma sources**: Co-60, Ir-192, Cs-137, Tm-170 — each with fixed energy and half-life.
- [ ] X-rays **can be switched off**; gamma sources **cannot** (need shielded camera).
- [ ] Photon energy: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.

### SWSI Essentials
- [ ] **SWSI** = Source and film on **opposite sides**; **one** exposure; **one wall** penetrated.
- [ ] **Preferred** technique per **ASME V Article 2**.
- [ ] Lowest **geometric distortion** and **unsharpness**.
- [ ] Best suited for plate, vessel, and large-diameter pipe welds.
- [ ] Geometric unsharpness: $U_g = F \cdot b / d$; **must be < code limit** (typically 0.5 mm).

### Key Formulas to Memorise
- [ ] $E = hc / \lambda = 1240 / \lambda(\text{nm})$ in eV
- [ ] $U_g = F \cdot b / d$
- [ ] $M = D / d$
- [ ] $t_2 = t_1 \cdot (D_2 / D_1)^2$
- [ ] Sensitivity % = (Smallest visible IQI / Total thickness) × 100
- [ ] $N = N_0 \cdot (1/2)^{t/t_{1/2}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024 Exam:**  
> *"Calibrate before you inspect. Choose your source by energy and half-life. Always prefer SWSI unless geometry forces you otherwise. Check U_g before exposing the film."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

# DWSI — Dye Washable Surface Inspection (Direct Wetting/Solvent Surface Inspection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> **DWSI (Dye Washable Surface Inspection)** is a variant of the Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT) family that falls under **Surface NDT Methods**. It is a **post-penetrant-removal inspection technique** in which the excess penetrant from the test surface is removed using a controlled **dry wiping or solvent-wetting procedure** (rather than a water-wash), followed by developer application and visual interpretation of indications. DWSI combines principles of **dye penetrant chemistry** with **controlled surface preparation**, allowing detection of surface-breaking defects such as **cracks, laps, porosity, cold shuts, and fatigue cracks** in non-porous materials (metals, alloys, ceramics, and some polymers).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Lipstick Test":**
> Imagine a smooth white shirt that has tiny, almost invisible tears. You dab a **colored lipstick** (the **dye penetrant**) over the shirt. The lipstick seeps into every tiny tear but stays on the surface elsewhere. Now, instead of throwing the shirt in the washing machine (which would remove everything), you carefully **wipe it with a damp tissue** (the **DWSI solvent removal**). The lipstick inside the tears stays trapped, while the surface lipstick comes off. Finally, you spray a thin white powder (the **developer**) which acts like a "reverse sponge" — it pulls the colored dye *back out* of the tears, leaving vivid red/colored streaks on top. These streaks = **defect indications**. That, in essence, is DWSI.

### Why DWSI Matters in NDT

| Attribute | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Inspection Type | **Surface-breaking defect detection** |
| Material Suitability | **Ferrous & non-ferrous metals, alloys, ceramics, glass, plastics** |
| Defect Orientation | **Open to surface** (any orientation) |
| Sensitivity Range | **5 µm to 25 µm** typical width detection |
| Standards Governing | **ASTM E165, ASTM E1417, ISO 3452, IS 3658** |
| Cost Rating | **Low to Moderate** |
| Field Applicability | **High** (portable, no electricity required) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> DWSI is a *hybrid* technique — it is neither purely **Water-Washable (Type 1)** nor purely **Post-Emulsifiable (Type 2)** penetrant testing. It uses a **solvent-based controlled wiping** action, making it ideal for **field inspections** where water is unavailable or undesirable (e.g., cold-environment sites, in-service pipelines, aerospace field repairs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

# Working Principle & Process Flow of DWSI

The DWSI method is governed by **capillary action** and **surface tension physics**. The entire process rests on three physical phenomena:

1. **Capillary Penetration** — the ability of low-viscosity liquid to enter narrow cracks.
2. **Reverse Capillary Action (Bleed-out)** — the developer draws the trapped dye back to the surface.
3. **Selective Removal** — controlled dry/solvent wipe removes surface penetrant *without* extracting penetrant from defects.

## Step-by-Step Process Logic

### Step 1 — Pre-Cleaning (Surface Preparation)
- The test surface must be **free of oil, grease, paint, scale, dirt, and moisture**.
- Cleaning methods: solvent wipe, alkaline cleaning, vapor degreasing, or ultrasonic cleaning.
- **Why?** Contaminants block penetrant entry into real defects → false negatives.

### Step 2 — Penetrant Application
- A **low-viscosity, high-wetting dye penetrant** (red or fluorescent) is applied by spraying, brushing, or dipping.
- **Dwell time**: typically **10 to 30 minutes** (per ASTM E165).
- Capillary action pulls the penetrant into surface-breaking discontinuities.

### Step 3 — Controlled Removal (The "DW" in DWSI)
> [!NOTE]
> This is the **defining step** of DWSI. The excess penetrant on the surface is removed using:
> - A **clean, dry, lint-free cloth**, followed by
> - A **cloth lightly moistened with approved solvent remover**.
> 
> The wiping motion is **single-pass, unidirectional** (not circular) to avoid spreading penetrant back into the cleaned area.

### Step 4 — Drying
- Surface is **air-dried** or wiped dry with clean cloth before developer application.
- Critical because moisture interferes with developer adhesion and bleed-out.

### Step 5 — Developer Application
- A **thin, even layer of dry powder developer** (e.g., talc, chalk, or silica-based powder) or **wet developer suspension** is applied.
- Developer acts as a **blotter**, drawing penetrant *out* of defects by reverse capillary action.

### Step 6 — Inspection (Bleed-out Observation)
- After a **development time of 10 to 30 minutes**, indications appear as colored marks.
- Inspection under **white light (≥1000 lux)** for visible dye, or **UV-A (365 nm)** for fluorescent penetrant.

### Step 7 — Post-Cleaning
- The part is cleaned after inspection and evaluation to prevent corrosion from developer residue.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical formulas and physical parameters for DWSI analysis (use `\vert` notation, NOT `\|` to preserve markdown table integrity):**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)** | $$h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$ | Height of penetrant rising into a crack of radius $r$ | meters (m) |
| 2 | **Penetration Depth (Lucas-Washburn)** | $$L^2 = \dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}$$ | Depth of penetrant into a crack in time $t$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | **Surface Tension Force Balance** | $$F_{\text{cap}} = 2 \pi r \gamma \cos\theta$$ | Capillary force drawing penetrant into defect | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | **Bleed-out Width (indications)** | $$w_b = k \cdot \sqrt{D_p \cdot t_d}$$ | Width of bleed-out as function of developer time $t_d$ | mm |
| 5 | **Viscosity Effect (Washburn-eq)** | $$\eta < \eta_{\max} = \dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{v_{\text{pen}} L}$$ | Max allowable penetrant viscosity for given flow | Pa·s |
| 6 | **Inspection Sensitivity Index** | $$S_i = \dfrac{w_b}{w_{\text{defect}}}$$ | Ratio of bleed-out width to actual defect width | dimensionless |
| 7 | **UV-A Irradiance Requirement** | $$I_{UV} \geq 1000\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$$ | Minimum for fluorescent inspection (ASTM E1417) | $\mu$W/cm² |
| 8 | **Minimum Visible-Light Intensity** | $$I_{\text{vis}} \geq 1000\ \text{lux}$$ | For visible dye (red) inspection | lux |
| 9 | **Developer Particle Size (D50)** | $$d_p = 1\text{ to }50\ \mu\text{m}$$ | Typical range for dry powder developer | µm |
| 10 | **Rejection Crack Width Threshold** | $$w_{\text{crit}} \geq 5\ \mu\text{m}$$ | Smallest reliably detectable crack width | µm |

### Symbol Legend
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (radians or degrees)
- $\rho$ = density of penetrant (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $r$ = effective capillary radius (m) — half of crack width
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of penetrant (Pa·s)
- $t$ = dwell time (s)
- $L$ = penetration depth (m)
- $k$ = empirical bleed-out constant (depends on developer chemistry)

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> DWSI is extensively used in:
> - **Aerospace**: turbine blade crack detection during in-service maintenance
> - **Oil & Gas**: weld inspection of cross-country pipelines
> - **Automotive**: fatigue crack detection in crankshafts and connecting rods
> - **Power Generation**: blade root and disc inspection of steam/gas turbines
> - **Foundries**: pre-machining inspection of cast components
> - **Forgings**: surface defect mapping of large forged shafts

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

# Exhaustive Derivations and Numerical Analysis for DWSI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped transitions.

---

## Derivation 1: Jurin's Law (Capillary Penetration Mechanics)

### Starting Premise
A liquid penetrant with surface tension $\gamma$ contacts the wall of a cylindrical surface-breaking defect at contact angle $\theta$. The defect has effective capillary radius $r$ and is oriented vertically. The penetrant rises to a height $h$ due to the balance of **surface tension force** and **gravitational force**.

### Step 1 — Surface Tension Force (Vertical Component)

The liquid-air interface forms a meniscus around the circumference of the capillary. The total surface tension force acts along the contact line of length $2\pi r$ and pulls the liquid upward at angle $\theta$ from the wall.

$$F_{\gamma} = 2 \pi r \gamma \cos\theta$$

*Explanation:* The factor $\cos\theta$ is the projection of the surface tension vector onto the vertical axis. For a perfectly wetting liquid, $\theta = 0°$ and $\cos\theta = 1$, giving maximum upward force.

### Step 2 — Gravitational Force (Opposing)

The column of liquid lifted has volume $V = \pi r^2 h$, mass $m = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h$, and weight:

$$F_g = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 h \cdot g$$

*Explanation:* This is the standard hydrostatic weight expression. The fluid mass is density times volume, and weight is mass times gravitational acceleration.

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition

At equilibrium (penetrant no longer rises), the upward surface tension force equals the downward gravitational force:

$$F_{\gamma} = F_g$$

$$2 \pi r \gamma \cos\theta = \rho \pi r^2 h g$$

### Step 4 — Solving for h

Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides:

$$2 \gamma \cos\theta = \rho r h g$$

Solving for the capillary rise height $h$:

$$h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

*Explanation:* This is **Jurin's Law**. The result shows that narrower cracks (smaller $r$) yield *greater* rise height — counterintuitive but physically correct because smaller capillaries concentrate the surface tension force over a smaller liquid weight.

---

## Derivation 2: Lucas-Washburn Equation (Time-Dependent Penetration Depth)

### Starting Premise
Jurin's Law gives the *static* equilibrium height, but DWSI uses a *dynamic* time-dependent penetration. The **Lucas-Washburn equation** describes how the depth of penetration $L(t)$ grows with dwell time $t$.

### Step 1 — Force Balance with Viscous Drag

In dynamic penetration, the system must also overcome the **viscous resistance** of the fluid flowing through the capillary. Using Hagen-Poiseuille flow analysis, the viscous drag force on a fluid column moving at average velocity $v$ through a tube of radius $r$ and length $L$ is:

$$F_{\text{visc}} = 8 \pi \eta L v$$

*Explanation:* This follows from the Hagen-Poiseuille law for laminar flow in cylindrical tubes. The viscosity $\eta$ resists flow, and the resistance grows with the length of fluid column already in the tube.

### Step 2 — Relating Velocity to Position

The instantaneous velocity of the liquid front equals the time derivative of the penetration depth:

$$v = \dfrac{dL}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Dynamic Equilibrium

In dynamic conditions, the net driving force (capillary minus gravity) is balanced by the viscous drag. Ignoring gravity for a horizontal defect (worst case — minimum driving force):

$$2 \pi r \gamma \cos\theta = 8 \pi \eta L \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dt}$$

### Step 4 — Simplification

Cancel $2\pi$:

$$r \gamma \cos\theta = 4 \eta L \cdot \dfrac{dL}{dt}$$

Rearrange to separate variables:

$$L \, dL = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

### Step 5 — Integration

Integrating both sides from $L = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $L = L$ at time $t$:

$$\int_0^L L \, dL = \int_0^t \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

$$\dfrac{L^2}{2} = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \cdot t$$

### Step 6 — Final Form

$$L^2 = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}$$

Therefore:

$$L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **Lucas-Washburn equation** governing DWSI dwell time. The depth grows as the **square root of time**, which means doubling the dwell time increases penetration depth by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is why DWSI specifies minimum dwell times of 10–30 minutes but excessive dwell times give diminishing returns.

---

## Analytical Worked Example

### Problem (KTU 2024 Style)
> A surface-breaking crack in an aluminum alloy has an effective half-width $r = 5\ \mu\text{m}$. A dye penetrant with surface tension $\gamma = 0.030\ \text{N/m}$, contact angle $\theta = 15°$, and viscosity $\eta = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$ is applied. Calculate:
> (a) The capillary rise height $h$ (assuming vertical orientation, penetrant density $\rho = 920\ \text{kg/m}^3$).
> (b) The penetration depth $L$ after a dwell time of $t = 20$ minutes (horizontal crack — ignore gravity).

### Solution (a) — Jurin's Law

Given values:
- $r = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- $\gamma = 0.030\ \text{N/m}$
- $\theta = 15°$ → $\cos 15° = 0.9659$
- $\rho = 920\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

$$h = \dfrac{2 \times 0.030 \times 0.9659}{920 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$2 \times 0.030 \times 0.9659 = 0.05795\ \text{N/m}$$

Compute denominator:

$$920 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 920 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-5} = 0.04513\ \text{N/m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$h = \dfrac{0.05795}{0.04513} = 1.284\ \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The capillary rise height is **1.28 m**! This is why even microscopic cracks can be completely filled — the capillary force in sub-millimeter cracks is enormous due to the inverse proportionality to $r$.

### Solution (b) — Lucas-Washburn Depth

Given:
- $t = 20\ \text{min} = 1200\ \text{s}$
- $\eta = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$

$$L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}}$$

Numerator:

$$r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t = (5 \times 10^{-6})(0.030)(0.9659)(1200)$$

$$= (5 \times 10^{-6})(0.02898)(1200) = 1.7386 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \eta = 2 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$$

Ratio:

$$\dfrac{1.7386 \times 10^{-4}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01932\ \text{m}^2$$

$$L = \sqrt{0.01932} = 0.1390\ \text{m} = 139.0\ \text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The penetrant theoretically reaches **139 mm** depth in 20 minutes along a horizontal crack. In practice, surface roughness, defect tortuosity, and contamination reduce this by 50–80%, but the result confirms DWSI is highly sensitive to deep surface-breaking defects.

---

## Comparison Matrix: DWSI vs. Other Penetrant Methods

| Parameter | **DWSI (Dry/Solvent)** | **Water-Washable (Type 1)** | **Post-Emulsifiable (Type 2)** | **Lipophilic Removal** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Removal Medium | Dry cloth + solvent | Water rinse | Water + emulsifier | Oil-based solvent |
| Sensitivity | Medium-High | Medium | Very High | High |
| Field Suitability | **Excellent** | Limited (needs water) | Limited | Good |
| Process Complexity | **Low** | Low | High (extra emulsifier step) | Medium |
| Reusability | Single-use consumables | Good | Good | Limited |
| Cost per Inspection | **Low** | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Crack Detection Limit | ~5 µm | ~10 µm | ~2 µm | ~5 µm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of DWSI

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams represent the **process flow architecture** and **defect-detection logic** of the DWSI method. Mermaid syntax follows the strict alphabetic node-ID rule (no reserved keywords as node IDs).

## Diagram 1: DWSI Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Component Received] --> B1[Pre-Cleaning Stage]
    B1 --> B2{Surface Clean?}
    B2 -- No --> B1
    B2 -- Yes --> C1[Apply Dye Penetrant]
    C1 --> D1[Dwell Time 10 to 30 min]
    D1 --> E1[Controlled Dry Wipe Step 1]
    E1 --> F1[Solvent Dampened Wipe Step 2]
    F1 --> G1{Surface Free of Excess Penetrant?}
    G1 -- No --> F1
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Air Drying Stage]
    H1 --> I1[Apply Developer Layer]
    I1 --> J1[Development Time 10 to 30 min]
    J1 --> K1[Visual Inspection White Light 1000 lux]
    K1 --> L1{Indications Found?}
    L1 -- No --> M1[Mark Acceptable Part]
    L1 -- Yes --> N1[Record Defect Map]
    N1 --> O1[Evaluate Against Acceptance Criteria]
    O1 --> P1[Post Cleaning Stage]
    M1 --> P1
    P1 --> Q1[End: Final Report]
```

## Diagram 2: DWSI Defect Detection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Surface Defect Open to Air] --> B2[Capillary Action Pulls Penetrant In]
    B2 --> C2[Penetrant Trapped in Defect]
    C2 --> D2[Surface Penetrant Removed by DWSI Wipe]
    D2 --> E2[Developer Applied as Blotter]
    E2 --> F2[Reverse Capillary Bleed Out]
    F2 --> G2[Colored Mark Forms on Surface]
    G2 --> H2[Inspector Identifies Indication]
    H2 --> I2{Linear or Rounded?}
    I2 -- Linear --> J2[Crack Lapping or Cold Shut]
    I2 -- Rounded --> K2[Porosity or Pits]
    J2 --> L2[Reject or Rework]
    K2 --> L2
```

## Diagram 3: System-Level Block Architecture of DWSI Inspection Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PREP[Pre Cleaning Module]
        P1[Degreasing Tank]
        P2[Drying Oven]
    end
    subgraph APPLY[Penetrant Application Module]
        PA1[Penetrant Spray Gun]
        PA2[Dwell Timer 10 to 30 min]
    end
    subgraph REMOVE[Removal Module DW Core]
        R1[Dry Lint Free Cloth Station]
        R2[Solvent Moisten Station]
        R3[Wipe Inspection Booth]
    end
    subgraph DEV[Developer Application Module]
        D1[Dry Powder Developer Spray Booth]
        D2[Development Timer 10 to 30 min]
    end
    subgraph INSP[Inspection Module]
        I1[White Light Source 1000 lux]
        I2[UV A Source 365 nm if Fluorescent]
        I3[Magnifier 10x]
        I4[Recording Camera]
    end
    subgraph POST[Post Cleaning Module]
        PO1[Final Wash Station]
        PO2[Anti Corrosion Spray]
    end
    PREP --> APPLY --> REMOVE --> DEV --> INSP --> POST
```

## Diagram 4: Decision Tree for Indication Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Indication Detected] --> S2{Indication Color Matches Penetrant?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[False Indication: Reject and Reclean]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4{Sharp and Linear?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Probable Crack: Reject]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Cluster of Rounded Marks?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Probable Porosity: Evaluate per Standard]
    S6 -- No --> S8{Single Rounded Indication?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Probable Pit: Evaluate Size]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Unclear: Reinspect at Higher Magnification]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

# KTU 2024 Scheme Question Bank — DWSI

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of DWSI inspection. List any three advantages of DWSI over conventional water-washable penetrant testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The principle of DWSI (Dye Washable/Dry-Wipe Surface Inspection) is based on **capillary action**: a low-viscosity dye penetrant is drawn into surface-breaking defects, the excess is removed by controlled **dry-wipe + solvent-wipe** (instead of water washing), and a developer is applied that draws the trapped dye back out, forming visible indications.
> 
> **Three advantages over water-washable PT:**
> 1. **No water dependency** — usable in desert, arctic, and remote field sites.
> 2. **Lower background noise** — controlled wipe prevents over-washing which can extract penetrant from shallow defects.
> 3. **Reduced risk of corrosion** — no water residue on ferrous components.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define contact angle and surface tension. State their roles in DWSI penetrant selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Surface tension ($\gamma$)** is the cohesive force per unit length at a liquid surface, measured in **N/m**. It drives the penetrant to wet the crack walls.
> 
> **Contact angle ($\theta$)** is the angle between the liquid-air interface and the solid surface at the contact line, measured in **degrees**.
> 
> **Role in DWSI:** For maximum capillary penetration, the penetrant must have **low $\theta$** (high wettability, ideally $\theta < 30°$) and **appropriate $\gamma$** (typically 0.025–0.035 N/m). Lower $\theta$ and higher $\gamma$ maximize the capillary rise $h = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ per Jurin's Law.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure of DWSI** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the importance of dwell time and developer time. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Lucas-Washburn equation** for time-dependent penetration of a dye penetrant into a surface-breaking crack. **(7 marks)**

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Discuss the **physical principles** governing DWSI, with reference to capillary action, surface tension, and reverse capillary bleed-out. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A surface-breaking crack in a steel component has an effective half-width $r = 4\ \mu\text{m}$. A penetrant with $\gamma = 0.028\ \text{N/m}$, $\theta = 20°$, $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$ is used. Calculate the **penetration depth after 30 minutes** (horizontal crack). **(7 marks)**

---

## MODEL SOLUTIONS

### Question A — Solution

#### Part (a) — DWSI Procedure (7 marks)

1. **Pre-Cleaning (1 mark):** Remove all contaminants (oil, grease, scale, paint) by solvent cleaning, vapor degreasing, or alkaline washing. Purpose: ensure penetrant can enter real defects without obstruction.

2. **Penetrant Application (1 mark):** Apply a thin, even layer of dye penetrant by spray, brush, or dip. Ensure complete coverage of the test region.

3. **Dwell Time (1 mark):** Allow 10–30 minutes for capillary penetration. Importance: longer dwell time = deeper penetration (per Lucas-Washburn, $L \propto \sqrt{t}$), but diminishing returns after ~30 minutes.

4. **DWSI Removal (1.5 marks):** 
   - First wipe: clean, dry, lint-free cloth in single unidirectional strokes.
   - Second wipe: cloth lightly moistened with approved solvent.
   - Critical: avoid over-removal (extracts defect penetrant) or under-removal (high background).

5. **Drying (0.5 marks):** Air-dry or wipe dry; moisture must be absent before developer.

6. **Developer Application (1 mark):** Apply a thin, uniform layer of dry or wet developer. Developer draws penetrant *out* of defects by reverse capillary action. **Development time** = 10–30 minutes; shorter times may miss slow bleed-out, longer times cause indication spreading.

7. **Inspection and Evaluation (1 mark):** View under white light (≥1000 lux) or UV-A (≥1000 µW/cm²). Record and interpret indications per ASTM E165 / E1417.

#### Part (b) — Lucas-Washburn Derivation (7 marks)

Full derivation as given in **Section 3, Derivation 2** of these notes:

Starting from force balance on a fluid column penetrating a horizontal capillary of radius $r$:

$$2 \pi r \gamma \cos\theta = 8 \pi \eta L \dfrac{dL}{dt}$$

Rearranging and integrating from 0 to $L$ and 0 to $t$:

$$\int_0^L L \, dL = \int_0^t \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

$$\dfrac{L^2}{2} = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{L^2 = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}}$$

**Engineering interpretation (1 mark within the 7):**
- $L \propto \sqrt{t}$: time has diminishing returns
- $L \propto \sqrt{r}$: wider cracks penetrate faster
- $L \propto 1/\sqrt{\eta}$: lower viscosity = better penetration
- Higher $\gamma$ and lower $\theta$ improve penetration

---

### Question B — Solution

#### Part (a) — Physical Principles (7 marks)

1. **Capillary Action (2 marks):** Defined by Jurin's Law. Surface tension $\gamma$ at the liquid-air interface creates a net upward (or inward) force on the liquid in a narrow space. The driving force is $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta$. This is what pulls penetrant *into* the defect.

2. **Surface Tension (2 marks):** The intermolecular cohesion at a liquid surface. For DWSI, penetrants are formulated with $\gamma \approx 25\text{–}35\ \text{mN/m}$, balancing fluidity with cohesion.

3. **Reverse Capillary Bleed-out (2 marks):** When developer (a porous powder) contacts the dried penetrant in a defect, the developer's fine pores create a new capillary network with extremely small effective $r$. By Jurin's Law, smaller $r$ means stronger capillary pull, so the penetrant migrates *from* the defect *into* the developer, forming a visible colored mark on the developer surface.

4. **Wetting and Contact Angle (1 mark):** Low contact angle $\theta < 30°$ ensures the penetrant wets defect walls and enters freely. DWSI formulations include surfactants to reduce $\theta$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $r = 4\ \mu\text{m} = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- $\gamma = 0.028\ \text{N/m}$
- $\theta = 20°$ → $\cos 20° = 0.9397$
- $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$
- $t = 30\ \text{min} = 1800\ \text{s}$

**Using Lucas-Washburn:**

$$L = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t}{2 \eta}}$$

**Numerator computation (2 marks):**

$$r \gamma \cos\theta \cdot t = (4 \times 10^{-6})(0.028)(0.9397)(1800)$$

$$= (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.028 \times 0.9397 \times 1800)$$

$$= (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (47.35)$$

$$= 1.894 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Denominator computation (1 mark):**

$$2 \eta = 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.010\ \text{Pa·s}$$

**Division (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1.894 \times 10^{-4}}{0.010} = 0.01894\ \text{m}^2$$

**Square root (1 mark):**

$$L = \sqrt{0.01894} = 0.1376\ \text{m} = 137.6\ \text{mm}$$

**Final answer with engineering comment (1 mark):**

> $$\boxed{L \approx 137.6\ \text{mm}}$$
> 
> **Engineering comment:** In practice, due to surface roughness, contamination, and defect tortuosity, the *effective* penetration is typically **40–60%** of this theoretical value, giving a realistic depth of ~55–82 mm. DWSI is therefore excellent for detecting cracks of depths up to ~80 mm in steel components.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** $r$ must be in meters, not µm, in SI unit calculations. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Using $\theta$ in radians:** The contact angle in the formula $\cos\theta$ is in **degrees** for trigonometric computation. Students often mistakenly use radian mode.
> 3. **Confusing Jurin's Law with Lucas-Washburn:** Jurin's gives *static* rise $h$; Washburn gives *dynamic* depth $L(t)$. Examiners expect students to know which to use based on "vertical/horizontal" and "dwell time" context.
> 4. **Ignoring developer time:** Many students describe procedure but skip the *importance* of developer time (10–30 min) — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Missing standards reference:** Always cite **ASTM E165 / E1417** or **IS 3658** in procedure questions for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DWSI (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **DWSI = Dye Washable / Dry-Wipe Surface Inspection** — a variant of Liquid Penetrant Testing using controlled dry + solvent wiping.
- **Core physics:** Capillary action (Jurin's Law) + Reverse capillary bleed-out.
- **Detection capability:** Surface-breaking defects ≥ 5 µm width.
- **Dwell time:** 10–30 minutes. **Development time:** 10–30 minutes.
- **Removal step is the defining feature:** single-pass unidirectional dry wipe → solvent-moistened wipe.
- **Inspection lighting:** ≥ 1000 lux (white light) or ≥ 1000 µW/cm² (UV-A at 365 nm).
- **Key formulas:**
  - Jurin's Law: $h = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$
  - Lucas-Washburn: $L^2 = \frac{r\gamma\cos\theta \cdot t}{2\eta}$
- **Applicable to:** All non-porous materials — ferrous, non-ferrous, ceramics, glass, polymers.
- **NOT applicable to:** Porous materials (castings with surface porosity, sintered parts, concrete).
- **Advantages:** No water needed, field-portable, low background, fast cycle time.
- **Limitations:** Operator-skill dependent in removal step; cannot detect subsurface defects.
- **Standards:** ASTM E165, ASTM E1417, ISO 3452-1, IS 3658, ASME Section V Article 6.
- **Sensitivity ranking:** Post-emulsifiable > DWSI ≈ Lipophilic > Water-washable.
- **Accept/Reject criteria:** Defined per applicable code (e.g., ASME, AWS D1.1 for welds).
- **Common defects detected:** Cracks, laps, cold shuts, porosity, fatigue cracks, grinding cracks, stress-corrosion cracks.
- **In KTU 2024 Scheme:** DWSI is a high-weightage topic under Module 3 (Surface NDT); expect 7–14 mark questions combining theory and derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DWDI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DWDI** stands for **Dry and Wet Developer Inspection**, a critical sub-topic within **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT)** — also known as **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)**. It specifically addresses the two principal developer application philosophies used to draw the entrapped penetrant out of surface-breaking discontinuities and render them visible to the inspector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> *Dry and Wet Developer Inspection (DWDI) refers to the methodologies by which a developer medium—either in dry powder form or suspended in a liquid carrier—is applied over a test surface following penetrant application and excess penetrant removal, in order to reverse the capillary action, extract the penetrant from discontinuities, and produce a visible indication of the defect.*

In the **dry developer method (Method A – Type I & II per ASME V Art. 6)**, a fine, dry, absorbent powder is dusted or sprayed onto the part. In the **wet developer method (Method B – Type I & II)**, the developer is supplied as a suspension in water (aqueous) or a volatile solvent (non-aqueous) and applied as a slurry or spray.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Sponge on a Sweating Wall"**
>
> Imagine a **plaster wall** with hidden hairline cracks. The wall has been **soaked with water** from a leak inside. You cannot see the cracks, but the wall feels damp.
>
> - **Step 1 (Penetrant):** The wall is the test piece; the water that seeped into the cracks is the **penetrant**.
> - **Step 2 (Cleaning):** You wipe the surface clean so no water remains on the outside.
> - **Step 3a (Dry Developer — DWDI Dry):** You press a **dry powder puff (talc/chalk)** against the wall. The powder acts like a sponge — capillary forces wick the trapped water back out, and you see a dark line where the crack is.
> - **Step 3b (Wet Developer — DWDI Wet):** You paint a **thin white paste (developer in solvent)** on the wall. As the solvent evaporates, the white powder draws the water out, leaving a clear white streak against the wall.
>
> In both cases, the **developer does NOT find the crack**; the **penetrant inside the crack does**. The developer simply **reverse-pumps** the penetrant outward and provides a contrasting background.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric / Constant | Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Particle size of dry developer | **1 µm to 75 µm** | ASTM E 165 / ASME V Art. 6 |
| Drying oven temperature (post-developer) | **50 °C – 80 °C** | ASTM E 165 |
| Developing time (minimum dwell) | **10 minutes** (Type I) / **2 minutes** (Type II) | ASME V Art. 6 |
| Penetrant dwell time | **5 – 60 minutes** | ASTM E 165 |
| Inspection temperature range | **15 °C – 50 °C** | ASTM E 165 |
| Surface roughness (pre-cleaning) | Ra ≤ **2 µm** preferred | ASME V Art. 6 |
| Water content in dry developer | **≤ 0.5 %** | ASTM E 165 |
| Developer film thickness (dry) | **uniform, thin, matt finish** | ASME V Art. 6 |

---

## 1.4 Why DWDI Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT521), Module 3 typically covers **Liquid Penetrant Testing in depth**. DWDI is a **high-weightage topic** because it directly relates to the *practical interpretation* of indications, the *physics of capillary reversal*, and *standards-based procedure selection* — all of which are favorite ESE (End Semester Examination) question stems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of DWDI — Capillary Reversal

The entire DWDI process is governed by a **reversal of capillary dynamics**. Let us dissect the underlying physics step by step.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1: Penetrant Entrapment (Capillary Intake)

When low-surface-tension penetrant contacts a surface-breaking crack, **capillary pressure** drives it inward:

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P_{cap}$ = Capillary pressure differential across the meniscus
- $\gamma$ = Surface tension of the penetrant (typically **0.025 – 0.035 N/m**)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle between penetrant and crack wall
- $r$ = Effective crack radius (typical: **1 µm – 50 µm**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A smaller $r$ (tighter crack) produces **higher** $\Delta P_{cap}$, meaning the penetrant is sucked in more aggressively — but it is also harder to extract later. This is why **DWDI (the developer step) must generate an even stronger reverse capillary force**.

---

### 2.1.2 Stage 2: Developer Action (Capillary Reversal)

The dry or wet developer particles act as a **porous, absorbent membrane**. When applied to the surface, the developer forms millions of micro-capillaries of radius $r_{dev}$, which exert a **reverse capillary pull** on the trapped penetrant.

For the developer to extract the penetrant:

$$\frac{2 \cdot \gamma_{dev} \cdot \cos\theta_{dev}}{r_{dev}} > \frac{2 \cdot \gamma_{pen} \cdot \cos\theta_{pen}}{r_{crack}}$$

The developer **wins** because:
- $r_{dev}$ (developer inter-particle gap) is **larger** than $r_{crack}$, BUT
- $\gamma_{dev}$ is much higher (developer is hygroscopic/porous, acting like blotting paper), AND
- The developer **provides surface area and absorption**, plus in wet systems, the **evaporation of solvent** creates a pressure differential that further pumps penetrant out.

---

## 2.2 The Six-Step DWDI Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Sequence — Memorize This!**

1. **Pre-cleaning** — Degrease, etch, or use ultrasonic cleaning (Ra ≤ 2 µm).
2. **Penetrant application** — Spray, dip, or brush. Dwell: **5–60 min**.
3. **Excess penetrant removal** — Emulsify (lipophilic/hydrophilic) or solvent wipe. **CRITICAL STEP — over-cleaning destroys indications.**
4. **Drying** — Warm air dry at **50–80 °C** (for aqueous systems).
5. **Developer application** — **DWDI STEP** (dry dust or wet slurry).
6. **Inspection** — Allow **≥ 10 min** developing time; inspect under white light (visible) or UV-A (fluorescent) at **≥ 1000 µW/cm²**.

---

## 2.3 Types of Developers — Comparative Table

| Developer Form | Type | Carrier | Application Method | Drying Required | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Powder** | Form **a** | None (airborne) | Dusting chamber / electrostatic spray | No | Field inspections, rough surfaces, large parts |
| **Water-Soluble (Aqueous)** | Form **b** | Water | Dip or spray | Yes (50–80 °C) | High-volume production, intricate parts |
| **Water-Suspendible** | Form **c** | Water | Spray | Yes | General shop use |
| **Non-Aqueous (Solvent-Based)** | Form **d / e** | Volatile solvent (e.g., isopropanol, acetone) | Aerosol spray | No (air-dry 30 s) | Field use, rapid inspection, weld inspection |
| **Film Developer** | Form **f** | Pre-coated plastic film | Stick-on | No | Smooth surfaces, critical aerospace parts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Type I* (fluorescent) and *Type II* (visible) with developer forms. **Type I/II refers to penetrant type**, NOT developer form. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Critical Equations for DWDI**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure driving penetrant into crack | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $v_{pen} = \dfrac{r^{2} \cdot \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille velocity of penetrant flow | m/s |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | Volumetric flow rate of penetrant extraction | m³/s |
| 4 | $A_{ind} \propto \dfrac{W_{dev} \cdot t_{dev}}{d_{part}}$ | Indication area ∝ developer weight × dwell ÷ particle size | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_{a} \leq 2\,\mu m$ | Maximum permissible surface roughness | µm |
| 6 | $I_{UV} \geq 1000\,\mu W/cm^{2}$ | Minimum UV-A intensity for fluorescent inspection | µW/cm² |
| 7 | $T_{dev} \geq 10\,\text{min}$ (Type I) | Minimum developing time | minutes |
| 8 | $\gamma_{pen} < \gamma_{dev\_effective}$ | Condition for reverse capillary action | N/m |

Where:
- $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity of penetrant (Pa·s)
- $L$ = Crack depth (m)
- $W_{dev}$ = Developer mass applied per unit area (g/m²)
- $t_{dev}$ = Developer dwell time (s)
- $d_{part}$ = Mean developer particle diameter (µm)
- $I_{UV}$ = UV-A irradiance on inspection surface

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where DWDI is Used in Industry:**

- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade crack detection (fluorescent penetrant + dry/wet developer)
- **Nuclear:** Steam generator tube inspection (aqueous developer for water-submerged parts)
- **Welding:** Weld seam inspection on bridges, pressure vessels (non-aqueous aerosol developer — most common field method)
- **Automotive:** Crankshaft, connecting rod inspection (water-suspendible wet developer for high throughput)
- **Foundry:** Casting crack detection (dry powder developer for rough as-cast surfaces)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Penetration Depth of Penetrant into a Crack

We derive the **Lucas–Washburn equation** for capillary penetration, which is fundamental to understanding how deep a penetrant travels into a defect — and therefore how effective the subsequent DWDI developer step will be.

### Starting Principle

The driving force is the **capillary pressure** $\Delta P_{cap}$, and the resisting force is the **viscous drag** in the crack.

**Step 1 — Capillary Pressure:**

The pressure drop across a curved meniscus in a cylindrical crack of radius $r$ is given by the Young–Laplace equation:

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Viscous Flow in the Crack (Hagen–Poiseuille):**

For a Newtonian fluid penetrating a circular capillary of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$\Delta P_{visc} = \frac{8 \eta L v}{r^{2}}$$

where $v$ is the average velocity of the penetrant front.

**Step 3 — Force Balance:**

At the advancing meniscus, the **capillary pressure** must overcome the **viscous pressure drop**:

$$\frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{8 \eta L v}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Velocity:**

Rearranging for $v$:

$$v = \frac{r \cdot \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta L}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Depth–Time Relation:**

Since $v = \dfrac{dL}{dt}$, we have:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta L}$$

**Step 6 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$L \cdot dL = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

$$\int_{0}^{L} L\, dL = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 7 — Obtain the Lucas–Washburn Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,L^{2}(t) = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2 \eta}\, t\,}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Penetration Time $t_{pen}$:**

If a crack of depth $L_{crack}$ is to be fully penetrated:

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \eta L_{crack}^{2}}{r \gamma \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation (KTU-Exam Ready):**
> - $t_{pen} \propto L_{crack}^{2}$ → **doubling crack depth quadruples penetration time**
> - $t_{pen} \propto \eta$ → **higher viscosity penetrants need longer dwell**
> - $t_{pen} \propto 1/r$ → **wider cracks fill faster**
> - $t_{pen} \propto 1/\gamma$ → **lower surface tension (better wetting) fills faster**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reverse Extraction Efficiency of the Developer

In the DWDI step, the developer draws penetrant **out** of the crack. We model the developer as a porous slab of thickness $h_{dev}$ and porosity $\phi$, with inter-particle capillary radius $r_{dev}$.

**Step 1 — Total Penetrant Mass in Crack:**

$$m_{pen} = \rho_{pen} \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot L_{crack}$$

**Step 2 — Reverse Capillary Suction by Developer:**

The developer absorbs penetrant until the capillary pressure in the developer equals that in the crack. The extracted volume is:

$$V_{ext} = \phi \cdot A_{dev} \cdot h_{dev} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_{dev}/\tau_{dev}}\right)$$

where the time constant is:

$$\tau_{dev} = \frac{8 \eta_{pen} \cdot h_{dev}^{2}}{r_{dev}^{2} \cdot \Delta P_{dev}}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Extraction Limit (as $t_{dev} \to \infty$):**

$$V_{ext}^{\infty} = \phi \cdot A_{dev} \cdot h_{dev}$$

**Step 4 — Indication Visibility Condition:**

An indication is **visible** when the extracted penetrant spreads to an area:

$$A_{ind} = \frac{V_{ext}}{h_{film}} \geq A_{min}$$

where $A_{min}$ is the minimum resolvable indication area (typically $\approx 0.5\,\text{mm}^{2}$ for unaided eye under white light, and $\approx 0.1\,\text{mm}^{2}$ under UV-A for fluorescent systems).

**Step 5 — Substituting:**

$$\frac{\phi \cdot A_{dev} \cdot h_{dev} \left(1 - e^{-t_{dev}/\tau_{dev}}\right)}{h_{film}} \geq A_{min}$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Developing Time:**

Solving for $t_{dev}$:

$$\boxed{\,t_{dev}^{min} = -\tau_{dev} \cdot \ln\left(1 - \frac{A_{min} \cdot h_{film}}{\phi \cdot A_{dev} \cdot h_{dev}}\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is the **analytical justification** for the 10-minute minimum developing time prescribed by ASME V Art. 6 — it ensures $t_{dev} \geq 3\tau_{dev}$, achieving $\geq 95\%$ of the asymptotic extraction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A turbine blade has a fatigue crack of width $r = 5\,\mu m$ and depth $L_{crack} = 2\,\text{mm}$. The penetrant has surface tension $\gamma = 0.028\,\text{N/m}$, viscosity $\eta = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, and contact angle $\theta = 0°$.

**(a) Calculate the penetration time.**

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \eta L_{crack}^{2}}{r \gamma \cos\theta}$$

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \times (4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.028 \times \cos 0°}$$

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{pen} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.2286\,\text{s}$$

> **Answer: $t_{pen} \approx 0.23\,\text{s}$** — Capillary action is extremely fast in micro-cracks.

**(b) If the prescribed minimum penetrant dwell is 10 min, by what factor is the dwell time excessive?**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{600\,\text{s}}{0.2286\,\text{s}} \approx 2625$$

> **Interpretation:** The 10-minute dwell is a **safety and uniformity margin**, not a kinetic minimum. It ensures complete filling of all defects regardless of orientation, contamination, or local surface condition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: The DWDI Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **block-level functional flow diagram** of the complete DWDI process, including both the dry and wet developer branches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Test Piece Received] --> A1[Stage 1: Pre-Cleaning]
    A1 --> A2{Part State OK?}
    A2 -- No --> A1a[Re-clean / Etch] --> A1
    A2 -- Yes --> B1[Stage 2: Penetrant Application]
    B1 --> B2[Penetrant Dwell 5 to 60 min]
    B2 --> C1[Stage 3: Excess Penetrant Removal]
    C1 --> C2[Emulsifier Application if Required]
    C2 --> C3[Water Rinse or Solvent Wipe]
    C3 --> D1[Stage 4: Drying]
    D1 --> D2[Dry at 50 to 80 C]
    D2 --> E1{Stage 5: Developer Selection}
    E1 -- Dry Method --> E1a[Apply Dry Powder Developer]
    E1a --> E1b[Dust Chamber or Electrostatic Spray]
    E1 -- Wet Method --> E1c[Aqueous Suspension]
    E1c --> E1d[Dip or Spray Application]
    E1 -- Wet Method Alt --> E1e[Non-Aqueous Aerosol]
    E1e --> E1f[Air Dry 30 seconds]
    E1b --> F1[Stage 6: Developer Dwell]
    E1d --> F1
    E1f --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Wait Minimum 10 min]
    F2 --> G1[Stage 7: Inspection]
    G1 --> G2{Type I Fluorescent?}
    G2 -- Yes --> G2a[Inspect under UV-A greater than 1000 uW per cm2]
    G2 -- No --> G2b[Inspect under White Light greater than 1000 lux]
    G2a --> H1[Record and Evaluate Indications]
    G2b --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Stage 8: Post-Cleaning]
    I1 --> I2[Remove Developer with Water or Solvent]
    I2 --> J1[End: Report Generated]
```

---

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Dry vs. Wet Developer Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DryDeveloperPath[DWDI Dry Developer Branch]
        D1[Cleaned and Dried Part] --> D2[Penetrant Already Removed]
        D2 --> D3[Developer Powder in Hopper]
        D3 --> D4[Dusting Chamber]
        D4 --> D5[Airborne Cloud Settles on Part]
        D5 --> D6[Excess Powder Blown Off Gently]
        D6 --> D7[Indications Appear as White or Pink Lines]
    end
    subgraph WetDeveloperPath[DWDI Wet Developer Branch]
        W1[Cleaned Part] --> W2[Choose Carrier]
        W2 --> W3[Water Based Aqueous Slurry]
        W2 --> W4[Solvent Based Non-Aqueous Spray]
        W3 --> W5[Dip or Flow On]
        W4 --> W6[Aerosol Spray]
        W5 --> W7[Hot Air Dry 50 to 80 C]
        W6 --> W8[Air Dry 30 seconds]
        W7 --> W9[Indications Appear as White Blotches on Red or Fluorescent Background]
        W8 --> W9
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Indications and Evaluation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Developer Applied Part] --> P2[Penetrant Bleeds Out of Crack]
    P2 --> P3[Forms Visible Indication on Developer Layer]
    P3 --> P4{Indication Shape}
    P4 -- Linear --> P5[Likely Crack or Cold Shut]
    P4 -- Round --> P6[Likely Porosity]
    P4 -- Aligned --> P7[Likely Linear Laps or Forging Defects]
    P5 --> P8[Mark and Report]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Accept or Reject per Acceptance Standard]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define DWDI. List the two main categories of developers used in Liquid Penetrant Testing with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
DWDI — **Dry and Wet Developer Inspection** — refers to the two methodologies of applying a developer medium (dry powder or liquid-carried suspension) onto a test surface after penetrant application and excess removal, in order to **reverse the capillary action**, draw the entrapped penetrant back out of surface-breaking discontinuities, and **produce a visible indication** of the defect.

**Two Categories (1.5 marks):**

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dry Developer** | Talc-based / chalk-based powder applied via dusting chamber or electrostatic spray |
| 2 | **Wet Developer** | (a) Aqueous suspension in water (dip/spray), or (b) Non-aqueous suspension in volatile solvent (aerosol) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the minimum developing time prescribed by ASME V Article 6 for Type I and Type II penetrant systems, and explain why this dwell is necessary.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Minimum Times (1.5 marks):**
- **Type I (Fluorescent):** $\geq$ **10 minutes**
- **Type II (Visible / Color Contrast):** $\geq$ **2 minutes** (typically 10 min recommended for best results)

**Why Necessary (1.5 marks):**
The dwell allows the **reverse capillary extraction** of the penetrant from the crack into the developer layer to reach a **steady-state (95% asymptotic extraction)**, corresponding to $t_{dev} \geq 3\tau_{dev}$. This produces an indication of sufficient **size, contrast, and color saturation** to be reliably detected by the inspector.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*(a) Explain the physics of capillary action governing the entry and exit of penetrant in a surface-breaking crack, with reference to the Young–Laplace equation.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) Compare Dry Developer and Wet Developer methods in LPT, listing advantages, limitations, and typical applications of each.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Capillary Action Physics (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Young–Laplace Equation:**

For a cylindrical crack of radius $r$ and a wetting penetrant with surface tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$:

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Penetrant Entry:**

Capillary pressure is **positive** when $\theta < 90°$ (wetting), pulling penetrant into the crack. From the Lucas–Washburn analysis:

$$L^{2}(t) = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2 \eta} t$$

Penetrant penetration depth grows as the **square root of time** — initially fast, then slowing as viscous drag builds.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Cleaning and Drying:**

After the dwell, **excess surface penetrant is removed** by emulsification or solvent wiping. The penetrant **inside the crack is retained** by capillary forces, while the surface penetrant is eliminated.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Reverse Extraction by Developer:**

The developer provides a **porous absorbent layer** with effective inter-particle capillary radius $r_{dev}$. The reverse capillary pressure exerted by the developer:

$$\Delta P_{rev} = \frac{2 \gamma_{dev} \cos\theta_{dev}}{r_{dev}}$$

For successful extraction: $\Delta P_{rev} > \Delta P_{crack}$. In wet systems, **solvent evaporation** additionally creates a negative pressure that aids extraction.

---

#### **Solution (b) — Dry vs. Wet Developer Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | **Dry Developer** | **Wet Developer** |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Fine powder (1–75 µm) | Powder suspended in water or solvent |
| **Application** | Dusting chamber / electrostatic spray | Dip, flow-on, or aerosol spray |
| **Drying** | Not required | Required (50–80 °C for aqueous; 30 s air-dry for non-aqueous) |
| **Film Quality** | Thin, matt, may be uneven | Uniform, controlled thickness |
| **Sensitivity** | Lower (50–70% of wet) | Higher (especially non-aqueous) |
| **Indications** | Wider, more diffuse, white/pink on contrasting background | Sharper, smaller, more accurate |
| **Best For** | Rough surfaces, large parts, field use on hot/cold surfaces | Smooth surfaces, high-sensitivity, production lines |
| **Limitations** | Powder inhalation hazard; sensitive to humidity; over-application masks indications | Requires drying equipment; aqueous may wash out shallow defects |
| **Examples** | Talc, magnesium carbonate, silica | Sodium silicate, kaolin, titanium dioxide in water/isopropanol |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate to high |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Wet (non-aqueous) developers are preferred for **high-sensitivity, precision inspections**; dry developers are preferred for **field conditions, rough surfaces, and rapid turnaround**.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*(a) Describe the step-by-step procedure of Liquid Penetrant Testing using the Dry Developer method, with the relevant standards, parameters, and quality control checks.* **(7 marks)**

*(b) A surface-breaking crack of width 10 µm and depth 3 mm is to be inspected. Given: $\gamma = 0.030\,\text{N/m}$, $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\theta = 0°$. Compute the theoretical penetration time. Comment on the practical dwell time prescribed in ASME V.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — LPT Procedure with Dry Developer (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Pre-Cleaning:**
Remove oil, grease, paint, scale by solvent cleaning, alkaline degreasing, or ultrasonic cleaning. Surface roughness **Ra ≤ 2 µm**. Dry thoroughly.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Penetrant Application:**
Apply penetrant (Type I fluorescent or Type II visible) by **spray, dip, or brush**. Ensure full coverage. Dwell time as per code: typically **5–60 minutes** (10 min default).

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Excess Penetrant Removal:**
- **Water-washable:** Rinse with water spray.
- **Post-emulsifiable:** Apply lipophilic emulsifier, then rinse.
- **Solvent-removable:** Wipe with solvent-dampened cloth.
**Do not over-clean.** Surface must appear free of penetrant, but crack remains full.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Drying:**
Warm air dry at **50–80 °C** until surface is fully dry. Do not overheat (would evaporate penetrant in crack).

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Dry Developer Application (DWDI Step):**
- Place part in **dusting chamber** with developer powder (talc/kaolin, particle size 1–75 µm).
- Apply for **30–60 seconds** by gentle dusting or **electrostatic spray**.
- Ensure **uniform, thin, matt coating**. Avoid thick layers (mask indications).
- **Gently blow off excess** with low-pressure air.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Developing Time & Inspection:**
- Wait **≥ 10 min** (Type I) or **≥ 2 min** (Type II).
- Inspect under **UV-A (365 nm) at ≥ 1000 µW/cm²** (Type I) or **white light at ≥ 1000 lux** (Type II).
- Record indications, mark, photograph, and compare with reference standards.

**Step 7 (0.5 marks) — Post-Cleaning:**
Remove developer with water rinse or solvent wipe per requirements. Apply **post-emulsifiable developer** if part is to be put into service or further processed.

**Standards:** ASME V Art. 6, ASTM E 165, ASTM E 1417, ISO 3452.

---

#### **Solution (b) — Penetration Time Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $r = 10\,\mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $L_{crack} = 3\,\text{mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $\gamma = 0.030\,\text{N/m}$
- $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1$

**Formula:**

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \eta L_{crack}^{2}}{r \gamma \cos\theta}$$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Substitute:**

$$t_{pen} = \frac{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{(10 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.030 \times 1}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute Numerator:**

$$2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$(3 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 9.0 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute Denominator:**

$$10 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.030 = 3.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final Result:**

$$t_{pen} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-8}}{3.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.30\,\text{s}$$

**Theoretical penetration time: $t_{pen} = 0.30\,\text{s}$**

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Comment on Practical Dwell:**

The ASME V Article 6 prescribed dwell of **10 minutes (600 s)** is approximately **2000× the theoretical minimum**. This large margin is justified by:
- **Defect variability:** Not all cracks are straight cylindrical capillaries — tortuous, branching, or partially blocked cracks require longer.
- **Surface tension reduction by contamination:** Real surfaces are not perfectly clean, lowering $\cos\theta$ effectively.
- **Wetting kinetics:** Initial wetting on contamination-free surfaces takes time.
- **Inspection reliability:** A longer, standardized dwell ensures **process uniformity and repeatability**, which is the bedrock of any qualified NDT procedure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in DWDI Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Type I/II" with "Dry/Wet"** — Type I/II refers to *penetrant* (fluorescent/visible), not developer. (−2 marks typical)
> 2. **Omitting the 10-minute developing time** — Always state it explicitly; it is a code-mandated parameter. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the pre-cleaning step** — LPT is invalid without proper pre-cleaning. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Writing "developer finds the crack"** — The developer does NOT find the crack; the **penetrant** does. The developer only **extracts and renders it visible**. (−2 marks — conceptual error)
> 5. **Wrong units in derivation** — Use SI units consistently (Pa, m, N/m, s). (−0.5 mark per error)
> 6. **Skipping the cleaning step in the procedure** — LPT has a strict 6-step sequence. Missing "excess penetrant removal" is a serious procedural omission. (−1.5 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DWDI**

- ☐ **DWDI = Dry and Wet Developer Inspection** — a sub-process within Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT/DPI).
- ☐ **Purpose of developer:** Reverse capillary extraction + provide contrasting background for visibility.
- ☐ **Two main categories:** **Dry powder developer** and **Wet developer** (aqueous or non-aqueous).
- ☐ **Six standard LPT steps:** Pre-clean → Penetrant → Dwell → Remove excess → Develop → Inspect.
- ☐ **Young–Laplace equation** governs capillary pressure: $\Delta P_{cap} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r$.
- ☐ **Lucas–Washburn equation** governs penetration depth vs. time: $L^{2} \propto t$.
- ☐ **Minimum penetrant dwell:** 5–60 min (10 min default).
- ☐ **Minimum developing time:** 10 min (Type I fluorescent), 2 min (Type II visible).
- ☐ **UV-A intensity required:** $\geq 1000\,\mu W/cm^2$ for Type I.
- ☐ **Surface roughness:** Ra ≤ **2 µm** for reliable inspection.
- ☐ **Dry developer particle size:** 1–75 µm; **water content ≤ 0.5%**.
- ☐ **Wet developer forms:** Aqueous (b), water-suspendible (c), non-aqueous (d/e), film (f).
- ☐ **Aqueous developers need drying** (50–80 °C); **non-aqueous dry in ~30 s**.
- ☐ **Wet (non-aqueous) developers give sharper, higher-sensitivity indications** than dry.
- ☐ **Dry developers are best for rough, hot, or field conditions.**
- ☐ **Standards to cite:** ASME V Art. 6, ASTM E 165, ASTM E 1417, ISO 3452.
- ☐ **The developer does NOT find the crack — the penetrant does. The developer only extracts it.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Panoramic Exposure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Panoramic Exposure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Panoramic Exposure (Panoramic Radiography):** A specialized variant of *internal-exposure radiography* in which a small, essentially **point-type radiation source** is positioned precisely on the central longitudinal axis of a hollow, cylindrical (or near-cylindrical) component. The recording medium (industrial X-ray / radiographic film, or a digital detector array) is wrapped around the **outer circumference** of the component, allowing the **entire 360° perimeter** of the wall — and all transverse features such as circumferential welds — to be imaged in a **single, simultaneous exposure**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this technique belongs to the family of **"Inside-Source Radiographic Techniques"** and is specifically classified under **Module 3: Special Radiographic Exposure Techniques.** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Donut Camera" Analogy:** > Imagine placing a tiny camera in the **hollow center of a doughnut**, with the lens pointing outward, and wrapping a continuous strip of light-sensitive film around the *outside* of the doughnut. When you take a single photograph, the camera simultaneously captures the image of the **entire inner surface of the doughnut wall** on the surrounding film. The film unrolled becomes a complete 360° "panorama" of the doughnut's interior wall. That is exactly what panoramic exposure does — except the "camera" is a radioactive source (Ir-192, Co-60) or a micro-focus X-ray tube, and the "film" is a radiographic film wrapped around a pipe, tube, or cylindrical vessel. ## 1.3 Why This Technique Exists — The Engineering Motivation Conventional radiography places the source **outside** the object. For a long pipe, this means: - A pipe of length **L** requires the source to be moved along the axis, - Or the film is moved along the pipe in a sequence of overlapping shots, - Each shot exposes only a small angular segment. **Panoramic exposure solves this in a single shot**, which is why it is the preferred KTU-recommended technique for: - Circumferential welds in pipe spools, - Heat-exchanger / boiler tubes, - Valve bodies, nozzle necks, gun barrels. > [!TIP] > **Standard Panoramic Metric:** A typical setup uses a source of size **f ≤ 1.5 mm** placed concentrically using a **centring jig / spider**, with **SFD (Source-to-Film Distance) = Outer Radius R** of the component being inspected. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Object Geometry Restriction:** The component **must be hollow and approximately cylindrical** (or spherical in rare variants) and **symmetric about a central axis** through which the source can be introduced. 2. **Source Insertion:** A small, near-point **gamma source** (Ir-192, Co-60, Tm-170, Se-75) or **micro-focus X-ray tube** is positioned on the central axis using a **centring spider / positioning rod** so that divergence of the beam is radial. 3. **Film Placement:** A flexible, light-tight **radiographic film cassette** (often a "strip film") is wrapped tightly against the **outer surface** of the component over the region of interest (typically a circumferential weld zone plus parent metal reference). 4. **Exposure:** Radiation diverges radially outward in **all 360° directions**. Every elemental area of the wall is exposed simultaneously.
