The film records a complete annular image that, when unwrapped, forms a flat panoramic radiograph. 5. **Centring Markers:** Lead "V" or arrow markers are placed on the film (or attached to the outside of the component) to mark **0°, 90°, 180°, 270°** positions — vital for **flaw-location correlation** after the film is developed. 6. **Image Interpretation:** The developed film is interpreted for volumetric flaws (porosity, slag, lack of fusion, cracks) with the help of **IQI (Image Quality Indicator)** sensitivity verification. ## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' of the Geometry Because the source is **inside** and the film is **outside**, every ray passing through the wall has a **different Source-to-Object Distance (SOD)** and the **same Source-to-Film Distance (SFD = R)**. | Ray Position | SOD | SFD | Geometric Magnification, **M** | |---|---|---|---| | Through OD (outer fibre) | R | R | $M = 1$ | | Through mid-wall | $(R+T)/2$ | R | $M = \dfrac{2R}{2R+T}$ | | Through ID (inner fibre) | $r$ | R | $M = \dfrac{R}{r}$ | where **T** = wall thickness, **R** = outer radius, **r** = inner radius, and $R = r + T$. > [!NOTE] > **The critical insight:** Radiographic geometric unsharpness $U_g$ depends on $M - 1$. Hence, the **image is sharpest at the OD** and **least sharp at the ID**. This is the single most-tested KTU concept under panoramic exposure. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Outer radius | $R = r + T$ | mm | Sets SFD | | 2 | Inner radius | $r = R - T$ | mm | Sets minimum SOD | | 3 | Geometric magnification at ID | $M_{ID} = R/r$ | dimensionless | Maximum magnification (worst) | | 4 | Geometric magnification at OD | $M_{OD} = 1$ | dimensionless | Best image sharpness | | 5 | Geometric unsharpness | $U_g = f \cdot (M - 1)$ | mm | Must be < 0.4 mm typically | | 6 | Unsharpness at ID | $U_{g,ID} = f \cdot (R - r)/r = f \cdot T/r$ | mm | **Limiting case for IQI design** | | 7 | Unsharpness at OD | $U_{g,OD} = 0$ | mm | Ideal — film in contact | | 8 | Source-size constraint | $f \le \dfrac{U_{g,\text{allow}} \cdot r}{T}$ | mm | Drives selection of source | | 9 | Exposure (inverse square) | $X \propto \dfrac{1}{(SFD)^2}$ for fixed SSD-style setup, but SFD = R constant for ID/OD points | — | Dose at film uniform if wall thickness uniform | | 10 | Total radiographic density | $D = K \cdot I \cdot t^{n}$ (Hurter–Driffield) | — | Optical density at film | | 11 | Penumbra / umbra boundary | $U_g = f \cdot \dfrac{d}{SOD}$ | mm | Equivalent form | | 12 | Recommended f for panoramic | $f \le 1.5$ mm (gamma), $f \le 0.4$ mm (X-ray tube) | mm | KTU practical guidance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, examiners **always** expect the student to identify the **ID-side unsharpness** as the controlling design parameter. Marking $M = 1$ everywhere is a common error that costs 2–3 marks. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Panoramic exposure is **the standard** for: - **ASME Section V / ISO 17636** qualified welds in **process piping** (refineries, petrochemical plants), - **Boiler tube circumferential seams** in thermal power stations, - **Heat-exchanger U-tubes** and headers, - **Aerospace tubular components** (rocket motor casings, hydraulic lines), - **Nuclear fuel cladding tubes** (specialised micro-focus variant). It eliminates **multiple exposures**, reduces **scatter overlap**, and gives a **single, geometrically consistent image** for the entire weld circumference. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Geometric Magnification Across the Wall **Given:** A pipe of outer radius **R**, inner radius **r**, wall thickness **T = R − r**, and a point source of focal-spot size **f** placed on the central axis. **Step 1 — Define the generic setup** Let a flaw (or material point) of interest be located at radial distance **d** from the central axis, where $r \le d \le R$. The film is wrapped on the outer surface, so the ray from the source to the film at the angular position of the flaw travels a Source-to-Object Distance (SOD) = d and a Source-to-Film Distance (SFD) = R. **Step 2 — Write the magnification definition** Geometric magnification is the ratio of the image size on the film to the actual (object) size of the feature: $$M(d) \;=\; \dfrac{\text{SFD}}{\text{SOD}} \;=\; \dfrac{R}{d}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate at the boundary radii** At the **outer fibre** $d = R$: $$M(R) \;=\; \dfrac{R}{R} \;=\; 1$$ At the **inner fibre** $d = r$: $$M(r) \;=\; \dfrac{R}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{r+T}{r} \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{T}{r}$$ **Step 4 — Express magnification in terms of wall geometry** Using $T = R - r$: $$M_{ID} \;=\; 1 + \dfrac{T}{R-T}$$ This shows the magnification grows **hyperbolically** as the pipe becomes thinner relative to its inner radius — a critical KTU design point. **Step 5 — Derive the geometric unsharpness formula** From the standard radiography definition: $$U_g \;=\; f \cdot (M - 1)$$ Substitute $M(d) = R/d$: $$U_g(d) \;=\; f \cdot \left( \dfrac{R}{d} - 1 \right) \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{R - d}{d}$$ **Step 6 — Apply at the two extremes** At the **OD** ($d = R$): $$U_{g,OD} \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{R - R}{R} \;=\; 0 \; \text{mm}$$ At the **ID** ($d = r$): $$U_{g,ID} \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{R - r}{r} \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{T}{r}$$ This is the **controlling unsharpness** for panoramic IQI selection. **Step 7 — Invert the unsharpness equation to get the source-size design rule** Given an allowable maximum unsharpness $U_{g,\text{allow}}$ (typically **0.4 mm** per ASME / ISO), solve for **f**: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{g,\text{allow}} &\ge f \cdot \dfrac{T}{r} \\[4pt] f &\le U_{g,\text{allow}} \cdot \dfrac{r}{T} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **source-size selection criterion** every KTU student must memorise. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem:** A pipe of outer diameter **120 mm** and wall thickness **10 mm** is to be inspected by panoramic exposure using Ir-192 (focal spot $f = 1.2$ mm). Find the geometric unsharpness at the ID and OD, and verify whether the source size meets the ASME limit of $U_g \le 0.4$ mm. **Step 1 — Compute geometric parameters** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{120}{2} = 60 \; \text{mm} \\ r &= R - T = 60 - 10 = 50 \; \text{mm} \\ T &= 10 \; \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Magnification at the ID** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{ID} &= \dfrac{R}{r} = \dfrac{60}{50} = 1.20 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Unsharpness at the ID (limiting case)** $$ \begin{aligned} U_{g,ID} &= f \cdot (M_{ID} - 1) = 1.2 \times (1.20 - 1) \\ U_{g,ID} &= 1.2 \times 0.20 = 0.24 \; \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Unsharpness at the OD** $$ \begin{aligned} U_{g,OD} &= f \cdot (M_{OD} - 1) = 1.2 \times (1 - 1) \\ U_{g,OD} &= 0 \; \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — ASME Compliance Check** $$ \begin{aligned} U_{g,ID} = 0.24 \; \text{mm} &\le U_{g,\text{allow}} = 0.40 \; \text{mm} \quad \checkmark \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The source is acceptable. If the pipe wall had been **T = 20 mm** instead, then $r = 40$ mm, $M_{ID} = 1.5$, and $U_{g,ID} = 0.60$ mm → **fails the limit**, requiring a smaller source (Se-75 with $f \approx 0.6$ mm). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Source-Size Selection Table (KTU Quick-Reference) | Pipe OD (mm) | T (mm) | r (mm) | $M_{ID}$ | $U_{g,ID}$ with f = 1.5 mm (mm) | Suitable Source? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 60 | 5 | 25 | 1.20 | 0.30 | Yes | | 60 | 10 | 20 | 1.50 | 0.75 | **No — too thick** | | 100 | 8 | 42 | 1.19 | 0.29 | Yes | | 100 | 15 | 35 | 1.43 | 0.64 | Marginal — use Se-75 | | 200 | 20 | 80 | 1.25 | 0.375 | Yes | | 200 | 35 | 65 | 1.54 | 0.81 | **No — use X-ray tube** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Panoramic Exposure — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pipe / Tube Component
Outer Radius R, Inner Radius r, Wall T] --> B[Clean Surface
Remove scale, paint, oil] B --> C[Mount Centring Jig
Source Spider along axis] C --> D[Insert Radiation Source
Ir-192 / Co-60 / X-ray Tube
focal spot f] D --> E[Place Lead Centring Markers
0°, 90°, 180°, 270°] E --> F[Wrap Film Cassette
around OD circumference] F --> G[Place IQI Wire Set
on source side of object] G --> H[Expose for calculated Time
based on I, SFD, screen speed] H --> I[Retrieve Film
Process in Darkroom] I --> J[Interpret Panoramic Image
Identify flaws, mark angular position] J --> K[Compare with IQI Sensitivity
Accept / Reject Weld] style A fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366 style D fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#990000 style F fill:#fff5e0,stroke:#996600 style K fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Source–Object–Film Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source
f = focal spot] -->|radial beam
360°| OBJ[Pipe Wall
r → R] OBJ -->|transmitted beam| FILM[Film on OD
SFD = R] SRC -.->|SOD = r| IDP[Inner Diameter Point
M = R/r, max Ug] SRC -.->|SOD = R| ODP[Outer Diameter Point
M = 1, Ug = 0] FILM --> DEV[Processing
Darkroom] DEV --> RES[Developed Panoramic Image
360° unwrapped] RES --> INT[Interpretation
+ IQI Verification] style SRC fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#990000 style FILM fill:#fff5e0,stroke:#996600 style IDP fill:#ffe0f0,stroke:#990066 style ODP fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600 style RES fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366 ``` ## 4.3 Geometric Schematic of Panoramic Exposure (Mermaid Block Description) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AXIAL[Central Axis of Pipe] S1[● Radiation Source] end S1 -->|Ray to ID| ID1[ID Point
SOD = r] S1 -->|Ray to MW| MW1[Mid-wall Point
SOD = (R+r)/2] S1 -->|Ray to OD| OD1[OD Point
SOD = R] ID1 -.->|SFD = R| F1[Film Element] MW1 -.->|SFD = R| F2[Film Element] OD1 -.->|SFD = R| F3[Film Element] F1 --- F2 --- F3 style S1 fill:#ff0000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000000 style F2 fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000000 style F3 fill:#ffff00,stroke:#000000 style ID1 fill:#ffccff,stroke:#000000 style MW1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#000000 style OD1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the schematic:** Each ray diverges radially from the central source. The **ID-point ray** travels the *longest* SOD and therefore produces the *largest* projected image on the film (largest $M$, largest $U_g$). The **OD-point ray** travels the *shortest* SOD (= R) and produces a *1:1* shadow (sharpest image). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define panoramic exposure in radiographic testing. State any two of its principal applications.** **Model Answer:** Panoramic exposure is a radiographic technique in which a small, near-point radiation source (Ir-192, Co-60, or micro-focus X-ray tube) is placed on the **central longitudinal axis** of a hollow cylindrical component, with the **film wrapped around the outer circumference**. A single exposure produces a complete **360° image** of the entire wall in the region of interest. **Two applications:** 1. Inspection of **circumferential welds** in pipes, headers, and pressure-vessel nozzles. 2. Inspection of **boiler tubes, heat-exchanger U-tubes**, gun barrels, and small-diameter cylindrical aerospace components where a single-shot 360° image is more efficient than multiple external exposures. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **In panoramic radiography, why is the geometric unsharpness maximum at the inner diameter (ID) of the pipe?** **Model Answer:** In panoramic radiography, the source is on the central axis and the film is on the OD, so SFD = R (constant). The Source-to-Object Distance (SOD) at the ID is the **inner radius r**, while at the OD it is R. The geometric magnification is: $$M = \dfrac{\text{SFD}}{\text{SOD}} = \dfrac{R}{d}$$ At the **ID** ($d = r$): $M_{ID} = R/r$ — this is the **largest** magnification value. At the **OD** ($d = R$): $M_{OD} = 1$ — the **smallest**. Since geometric unsharpness is $U_g = f(M-1)$, and the source size $f$ is constant, the unsharpness scales linearly with $(M-1)$. Hence, $U_{g,ID} = f(T/r)$ is the **maximum** and is the controlling parameter for IQI / source-size selection. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **principle, setup, and procedure of panoramic radiographic exposure**. List the major sources used and the **advantages and limitations** of the technique. **(b) [7 Marks]** A pipe of **outer diameter 100 mm** and **wall thickness 12 mm** is inspected by panoramic exposure using an Ir-192 source of focal-spot size **1.2 mm**. Determine the **geometric unsharpness at the ID and at the OD**, and state which one is critical for IQI design. #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Principle:** A point source of radiation is placed concentrically inside a hollow cylindrical component; the film on the OD records a 360° image in one shot. **Setup (mermaid block — see SECTION 4):** Source on central axis, film wrapped externally, centring jig/spider for source positioning, lead V-markers at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°, and IQI placed on the source side. **Procedure:** 1. Clean the pipe surface (remove scale, paint, oil). 2. Mount the centring jig and verify the source is on the geometric axis using a plumb or laser aligner. 3. Insert the source (Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75, or X-ray tube). 4. Place the IQI on the source side of the weld region. 5. Wrap the film cassette tightly and tape securely. 6. Set the exposure time using the radiographic exposure chart for the chosen source, SFD (= R), and material thickness (= T). 7. Expose, retrieve the film, develop, fix, wash, and dry. 8. Interpret for flaws, correlating angular position with the centring markers. **Common sources:** Ir-192 (most popular, $f \le 1.5$ mm), Co-60 (thick walls), Se-75 (thin walls, lower f), Tm-170 (very thin), micro-focus X-ray tubes (small-diameter tubes, $f \le 0.4$ mm). **Advantages:** - One exposure covers 360° — high productivity. - Constant SFD = R — consistent exposure at all angular positions. - Excellent for circumferential welds and small-diameter tubes. - Reduced scatter from surroundings because source is internal. **Limitations:** - Applicable only to hollow cylindrical/spherical components. - ID-side unsharpness is inherently higher than OD-side — limits wall thickness range. - Source centring is critical; mis-centring causes non-uniform exposure and distortion. - Radiation safety: source is inside an enclosed component — additional retrieval procedures required. #### Model Answer — Part (b) **Given:** OD = 100 mm, T = 12 mm, f = 1.2 mm. **Step 1 — Geometric quantities** $$R = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad r = R - T = 38 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Magnification at ID** $$M_{ID} = \dfrac{R}{r} = \dfrac{50}{38} = 1.316$$ **Step 3 — Unsharpness at ID** $$U_{g,ID} = f \cdot (M_{ID} - 1) = 1.2 \times (1.316 - 1) = 1.2 \times 0.316 = 0.379 \; \text{mm}$$ **Step 4 — Unsharpness at OD** $$U_{g,OD} = f \cdot (M_{OD} - 1) = 1.2 \times (1 - 1) = 0 \; \text{mm}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** The **ID-side unsharpness (0.38 mm)** is the **controlling value** for IQI design and source selection. The OD side is essentially perfectly sharp because the film is in contact with the outer surface (1:1 projection). Since $U_{g,ID} = 0.38$ mm is below the typical ASME limit of 0.4 mm, the exposure is acceptable. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for **geometric unsharpness in panoramic radiography** as a function of the source focal-spot size **f**, wall thickness **T**, and inner radius **r**. Comment on how this expression is used to **select the appropriate radiation source** for a given pipe size. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare panoramic exposure with **conventional external-source radiography** for circumferential welds. Discuss geometric, image-quality, productivity, and safety aspects. #### Model Answer — Part (a) **Derivation (re-stated for completeness):** The source is on the central axis, the film is on the outer surface, and SFD = R. For a feature at radial distance $d$ from the axis: $$M(d) = \dfrac{R}{d}, \quad U_g(d) = f \cdot (M - 1) = f \cdot \dfrac{R - d}{d}$$ At the **ID** ($d = r$), since $R - r = T$: $$\boxed{\,U_{g,ID} \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{T}{r}\,}$$ **Inverting for source-size selection:** $$f \;\le\; U_{g,\text{allow}} \cdot \dfrac{r}{T}$$ **Application:** For a pipe with $r = 30$ mm and $T = 10$ mm, and a target $U_{g,\text{allow}} = 0.4$ mm: $$f \le 0.4 \times \dfrac{30}{10} = 1.2 \; \text{mm}$$ So an **Ir-192 source** ($f \le 1.5$ mm) is acceptable. If the wall were thicker, say $T = 20$ mm, the limit becomes $f \le 0.6$ mm, forcing the use of **Se-75** ($f \le 0.6$ mm) or a **micro-focus X-ray tube**. This single formula drives practical source selection in KTU-relevant problems. #### Model Answer — Part (b) — Comparison Table | Aspect | Panoramic Exposure | Conventional External-Source | |---|---|---| | **Source position** | Inside the component, on axis | Outside the component | | **Number of exposures per weld** | **One** (360° coverage) | **Two or more** (with offset angles) | | **SFD** | Constant = R | Variable, decreases as source moves closer | | **Magnification** | Variable (1 at OD, R/r at ID) | $M = SFD/SOD$ (controlled by setup) | | **Limiting unsharpness** | ID-side, $U_g = f \cdot T/r$ | $U_g = f \cdot (M-1)$ everywhere | | **Image quality** | Variable around circumference | More uniform if geometry is controlled | | **Productivity** | Very high (one shot) | Lower (multiple shots, repositioning) | | **Source centring** | Critical — needs jig/spider | Less critical | | **Geometry restriction** | Hollow cylindrical / spherical | Any geometry | | **Radiation safety** | Source inside an enclosed object — extra retrieval protocol | Standard external shielding | | **Typical use** | Pipes, tubes, headers, gun barrels | Plates, vessels, complex geometries | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps on Panoramic Exposure Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the ID is the worst case.** Students often quote $M = 1$ for the whole wall and conclude the technique is perfectly sharp everywhere. **Lose 2–3 marks.** > 2. **Mixing SFD and SOD.** Always state which is which. In panoramic, **SFD = R** and **SOD varies** from $r$ to $R$. > 3. **Wrong source-selection formula.** The right expression is $f \le U_{g,\text{allow}} \cdot (r/T)$, **not** $f \le U_{g,\text{allow}}$. > 4. **Skipping the centring-mark convention.** Examiners expect explicit mention of **0°, 90°, 180°, 270° markers** for flaw location. Omitting this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing panoramic with contact / projection radiography.** Panoramic is a *form* of projection radiography but with the source **inside**. The "contact" reference is the film-on-OD, not a true contact technique. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Panoramic Exposure** > - **Definition:** Single-shot, 360° internal-source radiography for hollow cylindrical/spherical components. > - **Source on axis, film on OD** — this is the **defining geometry**. > - **SFD = R** (constant); **SOD = d** varies from $r$ to $R$. > - **Magnification:** $M(d) = R/d$. Min = 1 (OD), Max = $R/r$ (ID). > - **Geometric unsharpness:** $U_g = f \cdot (M - 1)$. **Worst at ID:** $U_{g,ID} = f \cdot T/r$. > - **Source-size rule:** $f \le U_{g,\text{allow}} \cdot (r/T)$. > - **Common sources:** Ir-192, Co-60, Se-75, Tm-170, micro-focus X-ray tube. > - **Centring jig / spider** is mandatory; use **plumb or laser** to verify. > - **Lead V-markers at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°** for angular flaw location on the developed film. > - **IQI** is placed on the **source side** (i.e., on the ID) of the object — the worst-case side. > - **Applications:** Circumferential welds in pipes/tubes, boiler tubes, headers, gun barrels, aerospace tubes, nuclear fuel cladding. > - **Advantages:** One shot, constant SFD, high productivity, reduced scatter. > - **Limitations:** Hollow-cylinder restriction, ID-side unsharpness dominates, source centring critical, retrieval-safety overhead. > - **ASME / ISO limit:** Typical $U_{g,\text{allow}} \le 0.4$ mm for general welds; **0.2 mm** for high-criticality components (e.g., nuclear). > - **Exam golden line:** "The unsharpness is **minimum at the OD** and **maximum at the ID** because of the diverging geometry from a centrally placed source."

topics.title: Real-Time Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Real-Time Radiography (RTR)** is a non-destructive testing (NDT) modality in which penetrating radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) is used to produce **dynamic, instantaneously viewable, two-dimensional projection images** of an object's internal structure on an electronic display monitor, with image acquisition, processing, and display occurring at frame rates (typically **≥ 15 fps**, with modern systems achieving **30–60 fps**) that approach real human visual perception.

As per ASTM E2698 (Standard Practice for Radiographic Examination Using Digital Detector Arrays) and ASME Section V, Article 22, RTR is formally defined as:

> *"The process of capturing, digitizing, and displaying radiographic images at a frame rate sufficient to permit the operator to observe internal features of a test object essentially as they occur or as the test object is manipulated."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RTR is classified as a *dynamic digital imaging* technique under Module 3 (Advanced Radiographic Methods). It is the bridge between conventional Film Radiography (FR) and Computed Radiography (CR)/Digital Radiography (DR).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Live X-Ray Stream"

Imagine you are watching a **live television broadcast** of a cooking show. The chef is cutting vegetables on camera, and you see the chopping motion *as it happens* — no waiting, no developing of a film negative. Real-Time Radiography works exactly the same way for industrial inspection:

- 🎥 The **X-ray source** is the "broadcaster," continuously emitting radiation.
- 🥗 The **test specimen (welded joint, casting, pipe)** is the "stage" where the action unfolds.
- 📷 The **digital detector (Image Intensifier or Flat Panel)** acts as the "high-speed camera," capturing radiation that has passed through the object.
- 🖥️ The **monitor** is your "television screen," showing you the inside of the object live.
- 🕹️ The **operator** can rotate, tilt, or push the object and immediately see defects (cracks, porosity, inclusions) appear or disappear.

Unlike Film Radiography, where you shoot, develop in a darkroom, fix, wash, dry, and then interpret (often **30–60 minutes per shot**), RTR delivers inspection feedback in **milliseconds**.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Terminology

| Term | Definition (per ASTM E1316) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Frame Rate (fps)** | Number of complete images displayed per second; minimum **15 fps** for "real-time" |
| **Spatial Resolution** | Smallest detail distinguishable, often measured in **line-pairs per mm (lp/mm)** |
| **Contrast Sensitivity** | Minimum density difference (ΔD) detectable, expressed as a percentage |
| **Detector Lag** | Residual signal remaining in detector after radiation is removed |
| **Image Intensifier (II)** | Vacuum tube device that converts X-rays to visible light with brightness gain |
| **DDA** | Digital Detector Array — a flat-panel solid-state X-ray sensor |
| **SNR** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio — a measure of image cleanliness |
| **DQE** | Detective Quantum Efficiency — overall measure of detector performance |
| **MTF** | Modulation Transfer Function — how well contrast is preserved across spatial frequencies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in RTR Calculations:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - X-ray photon energy range for industrial RTR: **20 keV to 10 MeV**
> - Visible light wavelength (monitor output): **400–700 nm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

RTR operates on the same fundamental physics as conventional radiography — **differential attenuation of penetrating radiation** — but with a critical modification: the image receptor is **electronic, not chemical**.

### Step 1: Generation of Penetrating Radiation
An X-ray tube (or radioisotope source such as Ir-192 or Co-60) emits a continuous or pulsed beam of photons. The tube voltage (**kV** for low-energy, **MeV** for high-energy applications) determines beam penetration.

### Step 2: Differential Attenuation Through the Test Object
As photons traverse the specimen, they interact via **photoelectric absorption**, **Compton scattering**, and (at high energies) **pair production**. The Beer-Lambert law governs intensity reduction:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity (photons/cm²·s)
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), a function of material and photon energy
- $x$ = material thickness

### Step 3: Detection and Conversion
The transmitted radiation strikes a detector:
- **Image Intensifier (II):** X-rays → electrons (photocathode) → accelerated electrons → visible light on output phosphor (brightness gain of **5,000–30,000×**).
- **Flat Panel Detector (FPD):** Direct conversion (amorphous Se) or indirect conversion (scintillator + a-Si photodiode array).

### Step 4: Digitization
The light image is captured by a **CCD (Charged Coupled Device)** or **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor)** sensor, with pixels typically **100–400 μm** in size. The analog signal is digitized by an **ADC** (Analog-to-Digital Converter) with bit depths of **10, 12, 14, or 16 bits** (yielding **1024 to 65,536 gray levels**).

### Step 5: Image Processing
Real-time software applies:
- **Frame averaging** (noise reduction)
- **Contrast/brightness enhancement** (windowing)
- **Edge enhancement** (sharpening filters)
- **Digital zoom** (magnification without loss in some systems)
- **Defect recognition algorithms** (AI-assisted in modern systems)

### Step 6: Display and Interpretation
Images appear on a calibrated monitor (typically **≥ 19 inch**, **2 MP to 8 MP** resolution) at the operator's workstation. The operator can manipulate the object in real time.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| Differential Attenuation | Creates the image contrast that reveals flaws |
| Electronic Detection | Eliminates film chemistry; allows instant display |
| Digitization | Enables post-processing, archiving, and quantitative analysis |
| Real-time Display | Allows the operator to scan large areas and find orientation-dependent defects (e.g., planar cracks) |
| High Frame Rate | Permits observation of dynamic processes (fluid flow, moving parts) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert attenuation law | $I$ in photons/cm²·s |
| 2 | $\mu = \rho \, (\sigma_{pe} + \sigma_c + \sigma_{pp})$ | Mass attenuation = density × cross-section | cm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $DQE(\nu) = \frac{SNR_{out}^2(\nu)}{SNR_{in}^2(\nu)}$ | Detective Quantum Efficiency at spatial frequency $\nu$ | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 4 | $MTF(\nu) = \frac{Contrast_{out}(\nu)}{Contrast_{in}(\nu)}$ | Modulation Transfer Function | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| 5 | $R_{spatial} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot p}$ | Spatial resolution limit (Rayleigh/Nyquist) where $p$ is pixel pitch | lp/mm |
| 6 | $SNR = \frac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t_{acq} = \frac{1}{f_{fps}}$ | Time per frame; e.g., for 30 fps, $t_{acq} = 33.3$ ms | seconds |
| 8 | $G_{brightness} = G_{flux} \times G_{minification}$ | Image intensifier brightness gain (5,000–30,000×) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{min} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{max}}$ | Minimum photon energy (for cut-off wavelength) | eV or J |
| 10 | $T_{eq} = 0.693 \cdot t_{1/2}^{-1}$ | Decay constant for radioactive source | s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER** use the pipe symbol `\|` inside a KTU table cell. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to denote absolute values or conditional logic in LaTeX. This is the #1 table-breaking error in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | RTR Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Aerospace** | Inspection of turbine blades, composite panels, rocket motor casings |
| **Nuclear** | Spent fuel rod examination, reactor vessel welds |
| **Pipeline & Pressure Vessels** | In-service weld inspection, circumferential scanning |
| **Foundry & Castings** | Detection of shrinkage porosity, sand inclusions in engine blocks |
| **Electronics** | BGA solder joint inspection, PCB trace verification |
| **Security** | Baggage and cargo screening at airports |
| **Additive Manufacturing** | Layer-by-layer inspection of 3D-printed metal parts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Detective Quantum Efficiency (DQE) — The "Holy Grail" of Detector Performance

The DQE characterizes how efficiently a detector converts incoming X-ray quanta into usable image information. It is the **ratio of output SNR² to input SNR²** as a function of spatial frequency.

### Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1: Input SNR**

The input to the detector is a Poisson-distributed X-ray photon flux $\bar{N}$ (mean number of photons per unit area). For a Poisson process:

$$\sigma_{in} = \sqrt{\bar{N}}$$

Therefore:

$$SNR_{in} = \frac{\bar{N}}{\sqrt{\bar{N}}} = \sqrt{\bar{N}}$$

Squaring gives:

$$SNR_{in}^2 = \bar{N}$$

> **Logic:** Poisson statistics of X-ray photon emission means the noise floor is set by the square root of the signal itself — you cannot have lower noise than this quantum limit.

**Step 2: Output SNR**

The detector adds its own noise. Let the output signal (e.g., digital counts) be $S_{out}$ and the output noise be $\sigma_{out}$. Then:

$$SNR_{out}^2 = \left(\frac{S_{out}}{\sigma_{out}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Definition of DQE**

By formal definition (IEC 62220-1):

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{SNR_{out}^2(\nu)}{SNR_{in}^2(\nu)}$$

Substituting from Step 1 and Step 2:

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{SNR_{out}^2(\nu)}{\bar{N}}$$

**Step 4: Expanded Form (IEC 62220-1)**

For a digital detector with measured MTF, NPS (Noise Power Spectrum), and exposure $X$:

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{MTF^2(\nu)}{\bar{N} \cdot NPS(\nu) \cdot \Delta x \cdot \Delta y}$$

Where:
- $MTF(\nu)$ = modulation transfer function at spatial frequency $\nu$
- $NPS(\nu)$ = noise power spectrum at frequency $\nu$ (in variance units)
- $\Delta x, \Delta y$ = pixel pitch in x and y directions (mm)
- $\bar{N}$ = mean number of incident photons per mm²

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose:
- Pixel pitch: $\Delta x = \Delta y = 0.2 \text{ mm}$
- Mean incident photons: $\bar{N} = 10{,}000 \text{ photons/mm}^2$
- At spatial frequency $\nu = 1 \text{ lp/mm}$: $MTF = 0.7$
- $NPS = 0.0005 \text{ (digital units)}^2 \cdot \text{mm}^2$

Compute:

$$DQE = \frac{(0.7)^2}{10{,}000 \times 0.0005 \times 0.2 \times 0.2}$$

$$DQE = \frac{0.49}{10{,}000 \times 0.0005 \times 0.04}$$

$$DQE = \frac{0.49}{20}$$

$$DQE = 0.0245 \quad \text{or} \quad 2.45\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A DQE of 2.45% is low — typical of older image intensifiers. Modern FPDs achieve **60–80% DQE** at low spatial frequencies.

## 3.2 Derivation: Spatial Resolution from Pixel Pitch

The maximum resolvable spatial frequency by a digital sampling system is given by the **Nyquist limit**:

$$\nu_{Nyquist} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot p}$$

Where $p$ = pixel pitch in mm.

For an FPD with 200 μm pixels:

$$\nu_{Nyquist} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.2 \text{ mm}} = 2.5 \text{ lp/mm}$$

**Why?** At the Nyquist frequency, you need at least 2 samples per cycle to reconstruct a sinusoid. A 200 μm pixel can just barely resolve a 400 μm line pair (200 μm line + 200 μm space).

## 3.3 Derivation: Contrast Sensitivity for a Digital Detector

The contrast sensitivity $C_s$ is the smallest fractional change in intensity the system can detect:

$$C_s = \frac{\Delta I_{min}}{I_{background}} = \frac{1}{SNR}$$

If the system has an SNR of 100:1, then $C_s = 0.01$ or **1%** — meaning it can detect a 1% change in transmitted intensity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RTR vs. Other Radiographic Methods

| Parameter | Film Radiography (FR) | Computed Radiography (CR) | Real-Time Radiography (RTR) | Digital Radiography (DR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Image Receptor** | Film + screens | Imaging Plate (PSP) | II/FPD + CCD/CMOS | FPD (direct) |
| **Image Availability** | 30–60 min | 2–5 min | **< 1 second** | < 1 second |
| **Dynamic Imaging** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** | Limited (pulsed) |
| **Re-usable Receptor** | ❌ No (consumable) | ✅ Yes (erasable) | ✅ Yes (continuous) | ✅ Yes |
| **DQE** | ~1–2% | 25–50% | 30–80% | 60–85% |
| **Spatial Resolution** | 8–20 lp/mm | 5–10 lp/mm | 2–5 lp/mm | 3–8 lp/mm |
| **Frame Rate** | 1 (single shot) | 1 (single shot) | **15–60 fps** | 1–10 fps |
| **Cost per Image** | ₹50–150 | ₹5–20 | **₹1–5** | ₹2–10 |
| **Storage Format** | Physical film | DICONDE | **DICONDE / AVI** | DICONDE |
| **Digital Processing** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Real-time** | ✅ Yes |
| **Operator Skill** | High (darkroom) | Moderate | Moderate (with IT) | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** The line between RTR and DR is sometimes blurred. By strict KTU syllabus definition, **RTR implies continuous live display** (≥ 15 fps), while **DR may involve static or single-shot digital capture**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Real-Time Radiography System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOURCE ["RADIATION SOURCE MODULE"]
        XRayTube["X-Ray Tube\nor Ir-192/Co-60\nIsotope Source"]
        HVGen["High Voltage\nGenerator\n20 kV to 450 kV"]
        Collimator["Collimator\nBeam Shaper"]
    end

    subgraph SPECIMEN ["TEST OBJECT STAGE"]
        Manip["Manipulator /\nRotating Table\n(2-axis / 3-axis)"]
        Sample["Specimen\nWeld, Casting,\nPipe, Composite"]
        Shielding["Lead Shielding\nCollimator Aperture"]
    end

    subgraph DETECTOR ["DETECTION & CONVERSION MODULE"]
        Scint["Scintillator\nCsI or Gd2O2S"]
        FPD["Flat Panel Detector\nAmorphous Si\nor a-Se"]
        IInt["Image Intensifier\nII Tube"]
        CCD["CCD / CMOS\nSensor Array"]
    end

    subgraph PROC ["DIGITAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        ADC["Analog-to-Digital\nConverter (12-16 bit)"]
        FPGA["FPGA / GPU\nReal-Time\nFrame Buffer"]
        CPU["Image Processing\nCPU + RAM"]
    end

    subgraph DISPLAY ["OPERATOR CONSOLE"]
        Monitor["LCD / LED Monitor\n2 MP to 8 MP"]
        Keyboard["Operator Controls\nJoystick / Keyboard"]
        Archive["DICONDE / DICOM\nStorage Server"]
    end

    HVGen -.energizes.-> XRayTube
    XRayTube --> Collimator
    Collimator --> Shielding
    Shielding --> Manip
    Manip --> Sample
    Sample -->|Transmitted Photons| Scint
    Scint --> FPD
    Sample -->|Transmitted Photons| IInt
    IInt --> CCD
    FPD --> ADC
    CCD --> ADC
    ADC --> FPGA
    FPGA --> CPU
    CPU --> Monitor
    Keyboard -->|Commands| Manip
    Keyboard -->|Commands| CPU
    CPU --> Archive
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Real-Time Image Acquisition Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A(["Operator Starts\nInspection"]) --> B["Position Specimen\nin Beam Path"]
    B --> C["Activate X-Ray\nSource"]
    C --> D["Detector Captures\nTransmitted Photons"]
    D --> E["Photons Convert\nto Electrical Signal"]
    E --> F["Analog Signal\nDigitized by ADC"]
    F --> G["Frame Stored in\nGPU Memory"]
    G --> H["Apply Real-Time\nFilters and Windowing"]
    H --> I["Display on\nMonitor at 30 fps"]
    I --> J{"Defect\nDetected?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Mark, Capture Still\nand Log to Database"]
    J -- No --> L["Continue Scanning\nor Reposition Object"]
    L --> I
    K --> M(["Inspection\nComplete"])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Detector Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph II ["IMAGE INTENSIFIER (II) TUBE"]
        InputWin["Input Window\nAl or Ti"]
        Photocat["Photocathode\nSb-Cs or S-20"]
        ElecOpt["Electron Optics\nElectrostatic Lenses"]
        OutputPhos["Output Phosphor\nZnCdS or Gd2O2S"]
    end

    subgraph FPD ["FLAT PANEL DETECTOR (FPD)"]
        ScintF["Scintillator Layer\nCsI:Tl or Gd2O2S:Tb"]
        Photodiode["a-Si Photodiode\nMatrix Array"]
        TFT["TFT Switching\nArray"]
    end

    subgraph CMOS ["LINE-SCAN CMOS DETECTOR"]
        LinScint["Linear Scintillator\nStrip"]
        LinArray["Linear CMOS\nPixel Array"]
    end

    InputWin --> Photocat --> ElecOpt --> OutputPhos
    ScintF --> Photodiode --> TFT
    LinScint --> LinArray
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selection of RTR Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start(["Begin: Material\nand Thickness?"]) --> Q1{"Thickness\n< 50 mm?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LowE["Use Low Energy\n50 to 150 kV\nFPD with CsI"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Thickness\n50 to 200 mm?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MidE["Use Medium Energy\n150 to 400 kV\nFPD with Gd2O2S"]
    Q2 -- No --> HighE["Use High Energy\n1 to 10 MeV\nLinear Accelerator\nor Ir-192 / Co-60"]
    LowE --> Q3{"Static or\nDynamic?"}
    MidE --> Q3
    HighE --> Q3
    Q3 -- Dynamic --> RTR["RTR: II or High-Speed\nFPD at 30 fps"]
    Q3 -- Static --> DR["DR: Single-Shot FPD\nor Imaging Plate CR"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Real-Time Radiography (RTR). List any two advantages over conventional Film Radiography.**

**Model Answer:**

Real-Time Radiography is a non-destructive testing technique in which penetrating radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) is used to produce a **continuously viewable, dynamic image** of the internal structure of a test object on a display monitor, with image acquisition and display occurring at frame rates of **15 fps or higher**.

**Two advantages over Film Radiography:**
1. **Instant image availability** — no chemical processing required; inspection results in < 1 second compared to 30–60 minutes for film.
2. **Dynamic imaging capability** — defects can be viewed from multiple angles in real time by rotating the object, increasing probability of detection for **orientation-dependent flaws** like cracks and lack of fusion.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is an Image Intensifier? State its function in RTR.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Image Intensifier (II)** is a vacuum-tube device that converts a low-intensity X-ray image into a bright, visible-light image through a multi-stage process: X-ray photons → electrons (at photocathode) → accelerated electrons → light photons (at output phosphor).

**Functions in RTR:**
1. **Brightness amplification** — typical gain of **5,000 to 30,000×**, allowing imaging at very low X-ray dose rates.
2. **Image size reduction** — minification of input screen (~40 cm dia) to output screen (~2.5 cm dia), enabling coupling to a small CCD sensor.
3. **Real-time compatibility** — minimal lag time, suitable for live display at standard video frame rates (25/30 fps).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Real-Time Radiography system. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

In RTR, X-rays (or gamma rays) from a source pass through the test object and are differentially attenuated based on material density and thickness. The transmitted radiation is captured by an electronic detector (Image Intensifier or Flat Panel Detector), converted into an electrical signal, digitized, processed, and displayed on a monitor in real time.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   [X-Ray / Gamma Source]
            |
            v
   [Collimator & Shielding]
            |
            v
   [Test Specimen on Manipulator]
            |
            v
   [Image Intensifier or FPD]
            |
            v
   [CCD / CMOS Sensor + ADC]
            |
            v
   [Digital Image Processor (FPGA / CPU)]
            |
            v
   [Display Monitor + Archive]
```

**Main Components:**

1. **Radiation Source** — X-ray tube (20 kV–450 kV) or radioisotope (Ir-192, Co-60).
2. **Collimator & Shielding** — Shapes the beam and protects personnel.
3. **Manipulator** — 2-axis or 3-axis positioning system for the specimen.
4. **Detector** — Image Intensifier (II) tube or Flat Panel Detector (FPD) with CsI or Gd₂O₂S scintillator.
5. **Readout Electronics** — CCD/CMOS sensor array and Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), typically 12–16 bit.
6. **Image Processing Unit** — FPGA / GPU for real-time filtering, contrast enhancement, frame averaging.
7. **Display & Archive** — Calibrated monitor (≥ 2 MP), DICONDE/DICOM storage server.
8. **Control Console** — Operator interface with joystick, keyboard, exposure controls.

**Working — Stepwise:**
- The source emits penetrating radiation continuously or in pulses.
- Radiation passes through the specimen; flaws cause localized intensity variations.
- The detector converts these variations into light (II) or directly into charge (FPD).
- ADC digitizes the signal at 10–16 bits per pixel.
- Real-time processor applies filtering and enhancement.
- Live image appears on monitor at 30 fps.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare Real-Time Radiography (RTR) with Film Radiography (FR) and Computed Radiography (CR) on the basis of: (i) image receptor, (ii) image acquisition time, (iii) reusability of receptor, (iv) dynamic imaging capability, and (v) storage format.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Film Radiography (FR) | Computed Radiography (CR) | Real-Time Radiography (RTR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Image Receptor** | Silver-halide film + intensifying screens | Photostimulable Phosphor Plate (PSP) | Image Intensifier (II) or Flat Panel Detector (FPD) |
| **(ii) Image Acquisition Time** | 30–60 minutes (incl. development) | 2–5 minutes (laser scan + display) | < 1 second (real-time) |
| **(iii) Reusability** | No — film is single-use consumable | Yes — plate erased and reused ~1000 times | Yes — solid-state, indefinitely reusable |
| **(iv) Dynamic Imaging** | Not possible | Not possible | **Yes — 15 to 60 fps** |
| **(v) Storage Format** | Physical film archive (large space) | DICONDE digital file | DICONDE, DICOM, AVI video stream |

**Conclusion:** RTR offers significant advantages in speed, dynamic capability, and reusability, making it ideal for high-throughput industrial inspection lines, while FR remains the gold standard for archival legal records and CR serves as a cost-effective digital transition technology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Detective Quantum Efficiency (DQE) in Real-Time Radiography. Derive its expression and discuss the factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:**

The **Detective Quantum Efficiency (DQE)** is the single most important metric for evaluating the performance of a digital X-ray detector. It quantifies how efficiently the detector converts the input X-ray quantum signal into a usable output image signal, taking into account the noise added by the detector itself.

**Formal Definition (IEC 62220-1):**

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{SNR_{out}^2(\nu)}{SNR_{in}^2(\nu)}$$

**Derivation:**

For a Poisson-distributed X-ray photon flux with mean $\bar{N}$:

$$SNR_{in}^2 = \bar{N}$$

Let the output signal (in digital counts) be $S_{out}$ and the output noise standard deviation be $\sigma_{out}$. Then:

$$SNR_{out}^2 = \left(\frac{S_{out}}{\sigma_{out}}\right)^2$$

Therefore:

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{SNR_{out}^2(\nu)}{\bar{N}}$$

In practice, the full IEC 62220-1 form is used:

$$DQE(\nu) = \frac{MTF^2(\nu)}{\bar{N} \cdot NPS(\nu) \cdot \Delta x \cdot \Delta y}$$

**Factors Affecting DQE:**

1. **Scintillator Material** — Higher light yield and stopping power = higher DQE. CsI:Tl outperforms Gd₂O₂S:Tb.
2. **Photon Energy** — DQE peaks at the optimal energy for a given detector thickness; falls off at very low or very high energies.
3. **Pixel Pitch** — Smaller pixels can yield higher spatial resolution but may reduce DQE per pixel (less sensitive area per pixel).
4. **Electronic Noise** — Read-out noise from the TFT/CMOS array adds noise and reduces DQE, especially at low exposures.
5. **Detector Thickness** — Thicker scintillators absorb more photons (higher $\bar{N}_{absorbed}$) but introduce more optical blur, reducing high-frequency DQE.
6. **Scintillator Columnar Structure** — CsI needles channel light directly to photodiodes, reducing lateral spread and improving DQE at high spatial frequencies.

**Typical DQE Values:**
- Film-screen: 1–2%
- Image Intensifier: 20–40%
- Modern FPD (CsI + a-Si): **60–80%** at low spatial frequencies

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the application of Real-Time Radiography in (i) weld inspection and (ii) pipeline inspection. Mention two specific advantages in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Weld Inspection:**

In welded joints (butt welds, fillet welds, pipe girth welds), RTR is used to detect volumetric and planar flaws such as porosity, slag inclusions, lack of penetration, lack of fusion, and cracks.

**Working Setup:**
- A panoramic X-ray tube or directional beam is aimed at the weld region.
- The detector (FPD or II) is placed on the opposite side.
- The weld is scanned in real time, often as the source-detector assembly traverses along the weld length (scanning RTR).
- The operator views a live image stream, looking for indications.

**Two Specific Advantages:**
1. **Real-time defect identification** — operators can immediately stop the scan to investigate suspicious features, increasing probability of detection (POD) for critical defects like cracks.
2. **Cost-effective for long welds** — scanning RTR is **5–10× faster** than film-based techniques for long seam welds (e.g., pressure vessel circumferential welds), reducing inspection cost per meter.

**(ii) Pipeline Inspection:**

RTR is used for inspection of circumferential girth welds in oil & gas pipelines, as well as for in-service inspection of pipe walls (corrosion, erosion).

**Working Setup:**
- Crawler-mounted X-ray systems ("pipeline pigs") carry the source and detector through the pipe.
- Real-time images are transmitted via cable or stored locally.
- The pipe wall thickness can be measured in real time using **digital densitometry**.

**Two Specific Advantages:**
1. **In-line inspection** — modern "digital radiography crawlers" can inspect girth welds in **< 2 minutes per weld** during pipeline lay, enabling continuous production.
2. **In-service corrosion mapping** — RTR combined with computed tomography can measure remaining wall thickness without taking the pipeline out of service, supporting fitness-for-service (FFS) assessments per ASME B31G or API 1163.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing RTR with DR** — many students define both as "digital X-ray" without highlighting the **dynamic/real-time** aspect. Always emphasize the **frame rate (≥ 15 fps)** in your definition.
> 2. **Forgetting units in DQE** — DQE is **dimensionless**; do not write units of % in derivation steps (only in final results).
> 3. **Skipping the ADC** — a common omission; the digitization step (analog → digital) is a key part of the RTR chain, distinct from the detector itself.
> 4. **Writing the formula with a pipe symbol `\|`** — this breaks the KTU answer booklet's table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values.
> 5. **No mention of safety** — in 14-mark answers on RTR, you MUST mention **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) and **personnel shielding** (lead barriers, dosimeters) to score full marks.
> 6. **Confusing MTF and DQE** — MTF is a measure of **spatial resolution/contrast transfer**, while DQE is a measure of **signal-to-noise efficiency**. They are NOT the same thing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **RTR Definition:** Dynamic, real-time (≥ 15 fps) radiographic imaging on an electronic display.
- ✅ **Two Main Detector Types:** Image Intensifier (II) and Flat Panel Detector (FPD).
- ✅ **Brightness Gain of II:** 5,000–30,000×.
- ✅ **Bit Depth:** 10–16 bits per pixel → 1024 to 65,536 gray levels.
- ✅ **Standard Frame Rate:** 30 fps (modern), 25 fps (PAL), 15 fps (minimum for "real-time").
- ✅ **Pixel Pitch:** 100–400 μm typical; determines spatial resolution.
- ✅ **Nyquist Limit:** $\nu_{Nyquist} = \frac{1}{2p}$ lp/mm.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — fundamental attenuation equation.
- ✅ **DQE Definition:** Ratio of output SNR² to input SNR²; 60–80% for modern FPDs.
- ✅ **MTF Definition:** Ratio of output contrast to input contrast at a given spatial frequency.
- ✅ **DICONDE:** Digital Imaging and Communication in Nondestructive Evaluation — standard storage format.
- ✅ **ALARA Principle:** Always mandatory in RTR answer discussions.
- ✅ **Industries:** Aerospace, nuclear, pipeline, foundry, electronics, security, additive manufacturing.
- ✅ **Advantages of RTR over FR:** Speed, dynamic imaging, no chemicals, reusability, digital processing, lower cost per image.
- ✅ **Limitations of RTR:** Lower spatial resolution than film, expensive initial setup, requires trained operators, lower dynamic range than film (typically 1000:1 vs. film's 10,000:1).
- ✅ **Key Standards:** ASTM E2698, ASTM E2737, ASME Section V Article 22, IEC 62220-1.
- ✅ **Typical Applications in KTU Context:** Weld inspection (RTR crawlers), casting inspection, in-service pipeline inspection, security screening.

### 🎯 High-Yield Memory Hooks

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **"S-D-D-P-D-A"** | Source → Differential Attenuation → Detector → Digitize → Process → Display |
| **"II or FPD"** | The two detector families in RTR |
| **"DQE = SNR² ratio"** | Never forget the squaring! |
| **"30 fps = 33.3 ms"** | Per-frame time = 1 / frame rate |
| **"CsI > Gd₂O₂S"** | Cesium Iodide gives higher DQE than Gadolinium Oxysulfide |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Films Used in Industrial Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Films Used in Industrial Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Industrial Radiography**, the **radiographic film** is a specially manufactured, light-tight, flexible sheet that acts as the primary **image receptor (recording medium)**. It permanently captures the spatial distribution of penetrating radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) that has passed through the object under inspection, producing a **radiograph** — a two-dimensional shadow image of internal features.

The film consists of a thin, transparent **polyester (PET) base** coated on one or both sides with a radiation-sensitive **emulsion** containing **silver halide crystals** (primarily silver bromide, AgBr) suspended in gelatin. When the film is exposed to radiation and subsequently chemically processed, the silver halide grains that absorbed energy are reduced to **metallic silver**, producing dark regions on the developed film. Areas receiving less radiation remain light, yielding the characteristic **shadow image** of density variations inside the test object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT521 / Module 3):**
> Films used in industrial radiography are classified, characterized, and selected based on their **speed, contrast, granularity, and base density**. The *characteristic curve (H & D curve)* is the central graphical tool used to evaluate film performance and is a guaranteed KTU high-yield topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Photograph" Mental Model

Think of an industrial X-ray film exactly like an **extremely sensitive, black-and-white "solar camera film"** that records invisible light (X-rays/gamma rays) instead of visible sunlight.

- **Passing of X-rays** through a thick steel plate is analogous to sunlight passing through a **stained-glass window** — clean areas (no defects) let most of the energy through (producing dark film), while dense defects (slag, cracks, porosity) **block** the energy (producing lighter regions on the film).
- The **silver halide grains** behave like **"miniature photon counters"** — each grain that absorbs sufficient radiation energy is "marked" and is later developed into a visible black speck of metallic silver.
- The film's **speed** is like the **ISO rating of a camera film** — a high-speed film needs less radiation (shorter exposure) to produce the same image darkness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** Industrial radiographic films are used universally in **weld inspection (ASME Section V, AWS D1.1)**, **castings and forgings evaluation**, **pipeline girth weld inspection**, **aircraft structural components**, and **nuclear fuel rod verification**.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Film Speed (S):** Reciprocal of the exposure required to produce a specified density, typically measured at **Net Density = 2.0** above base + fog. Higher speed ⇒ shorter exposure time.
- **Film Contrast (Ḡ or gradient):** Slope of the characteristic curve in its **useful straight-line region** (typically between Net Density 2.0 and 4.0). Higher contrast ⇒ sharper image, narrower exposure latitude.
- **Base Density (D₀):** Inherent optical density of the unprocessed film base plus emulsion (typically **0.05 to 0.20** for a polyester base of ~0.18 mm thickness).
- **Fog Density (D_f):** Unwanted density arising from ageing, storage, or processing, **added to base density** to give **Base + Fog**.
- **Granularity (σ_D):** Statistical fluctuation in density measured with a microdensitometer having a **48 µm diameter circular aperture**. Indicates image noise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Construction of an Industrial Radiographic Film

An industrial X-ray film is a **sandwich** of multiple precision-engineered layers, manufactured under strict clean-room conditions to ensure uniform response and low base density.

| Layer | Approximate Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Protective Overcoat (Supercoat)** | ~1 µm | Tough, scratch-resistant gelatin layer to protect emulsion from abrasion. |
| **Emulsion (Radiation-Sensitive Layer)** | 10–20 µm (each side) | Contains **silver halide crystals (AgBr/AgI)** dispersed in gelatin. Double-coated films have emulsion on **both** sides. |
| **Subbing (Adhesion) Layer** | ~1 µm | Ensures firm bonding of emulsion to the base; prevents delamination during processing. |
| **Film Base (Polyester / PET)** | 0.10 – 0.20 mm | Dimensionally stable, transparent support that provides mechanical strength. |
| **Anti-halation Backing (Optional)** | 1–5 µm | Absorbs radiation reflected from the back of the base to prevent **halation** (image blurring). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial films are double-coated (emulsion on both sides)** — this is a key distinction from medical films, which are often single-coated. Double-coating doubles the silver halide content per unit area, which significantly **increases speed** and **reduces exposure time**.

## 2.2 ASTM E1815 Classification of Industrial Radiographic Films

ASTM International classifies films into **five principal types** based on their speed, contrast, and intended use:

| Film Type | ASTM Class | Relative Speed | Contrast (Ḡ) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** | Class I Special | Very Slow (~10) | Very High | Critical aerospace components, high-precision light-alloy castings. |
| **Type 2** | Class I | Slow (~40) | High | Standard radiography of light alloys, thin sections. |
| **Type 3** | Class II | Medium (~80) | Medium-High | General-purpose inspection of castings and welds. |
| **Type 4** | Class III | Fast (~200) | High | Field radiography of welds, thicker sections. |
| **Type 5** | Class IV | Very Fast (~400) | Medium | High-energy radiography, use with **lead screens** for thick steel. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Always pair a film with **intensifying screens** (lead foil screens, typically 0.02–0.25 mm thick) for energies above ~120 kV. Lead screens (a) absorb back-scattered low-energy radiation, (b) emit secondary electrons/characteristic X-rays that enhance film exposure (especially at higher energies), and (c) extend the useful life of the cassette.

## 2.3 The Characteristic Curve (Hurter & Driffield, H & D Curve)

The **characteristic curve** is the most important graphical tool in industrial radiography. It plots **Optical Density (D)** on the y-axis against **Log Relative Exposure (log₁₀ E)** on the x-axis. The shape of this curve dictates the practical utility of the film.

### 2.3.1 Four Distinct Regions of the H & D Curve

1. **Base + Fog Region:** The film density at zero useful exposure — equal to the inherent base density plus chemical fog. For a typical industrial film, this is between **0.05 and 0.25**.
2. **Toe (Under-exposure) Region:** Slowly rising portion where the film response is non-linear. Avoid using this region in industrial radiography because small density differences are not distinguishable.
3. **Straight-Line (Useful) Region:** Linear portion between Net Density **~2.0 and ~4.0** — the **operative range for evaluation**. The slope of this line is the **average gradient (Ḡ)** and represents the film's contrast.
4. **Shoulder (Over-exposure) Region:** Curve flattens as all silver halide grains are converted to metallic silver (saturation).

## 2.4 The Exposure Equation — The Heart of Radiographic Sensitivity

The **exposure** at the film plane is determined by the source strength, distance, and attenuation through the specimen. KTU students must internalize the following fundamental relationships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Inverse Square Law of Radiation Intensity** states that intensity falls off with the square of the distance from a point source. The exposure time is therefore proportional to the square of the source-to-film distance (SFD).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **definitive numerical reference** for solving all KTU numerical problems on radiographic film selection and exposure calculation.

| Quantity | Formula | Definition of Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Density** | $D = \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)$ | $I_0$ = incident light intensity; $I$ = transmitted light intensity | Dimensionless |
| **Net Density** | $D_n = D - (D_b + D_f)$ | $D_b$ = base density; $D_f$ = fog density | Dimensionless |
| **Film Speed (ASTM)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{E_{2.0}}$ | $E_{2.0}$ = exposure (in Röntgens) required to produce Net Density = 2.0 | Röntgens⁻¹ |
| **Average Gradient** | $\bar{G} = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}$ | Slope of straight-line portion (typically between $D_n = 2.0$ and $4.0$) | Dimensionless |
| **Inverse Square Law** | $\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \left( \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2$ | $I_1, I_2$ = intensities at distances $d_1, d_2$ | W/m² or mR/h |
| **Exposure Time Scaling** | $\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \left( \dfrac{d_1}{d_2} \right)^2$ | $t_1, t_2$ = exposure times at distances $d_1, d_2$ | Seconds (s) or minutes (min) |
| **Relative Exposure Factor** | $E \propto \dfrac{C \cdot t}{d^2}$ | $C$ = source activity or tube current·time; $t$ = time; $d$ = SFD | mR or R |
| **Characteristic Curve Equation (Linear Region)** | $D = \bar{G} \cdot (\log E - \log E_i) + D_i$ | $E_i$ = exposure at which straight line intercepts the curve; $D_i$ = density at $E_i$ | Dimensionless |
| **Reciprocity Law (Bunsen–Roscoe)** | $I \cdot t = \text{constant for fixed } D$ | Holds only when intensity-time product is constant | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** The *reciprocity law* **fails** in industrial radiography for very long exposures (> ~ 1 hour) — known as the **Schwarzschild-Villiger effect**. For low-activity Ir-192 sources, exposure must be increased by a factor of 1.3–1.5 to compensate. This is a frequently tested KTU concept.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The characteristic curve is the engineer's **calibration chart** for film selection:
- **Aerospace inspection (Type 1/2):** High contrast, low granularity required to detect hairline cracks in turbine blades.
- **Pipeline girth weld (Type 3/4 + lead screens):** Field-portable, fast films with sufficient contrast to reveal weld defects in thick-wall carbon steel.
- **Nuclear component inspection (Type 1):** Maximum image quality to satisfy ASME Section III nuclear code acceptance criteria.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Optical Density from Transmitted Light

The **optical density** $D$ of a processed film is defined in terms of the ratio of incident light to transmitted light, measured with a **densitometer**. The derivation proceeds as follows.

Let $I_0$ be the intensity of the calibrated light source incident on the film, and $I$ the intensity transmitted through the film to the photodetector. By definition of the **base-10 logarithm**, the optical density is:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right)$$

**Step 1 — Express $D$ in terms of transmittance.**

The transmittance $T$ of the film is the ratio $\frac{I}{I_0}$. Therefore:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{T} \right) = -\log_{10}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Convert density to percent transmittance for practical interpretation.**

Since $T = \frac{I}{I_0}$ is a fraction, multiplying by 100 gives percent transmittance (%T). Hence:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{100}{\%T} \right) = 2 - \log_{10}(\%T)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical interpretation (useful in KTU problems).**

| % Transmittance | Optical Density $D$ |
|---|---|
| 100 % | 0.00 |
| 50 % | 0.30 |
| 10 % | 1.00 |
| 1 % | 2.00 |
| 0.1 % | 3.00 |
| 0.01 % | 4.00 |

This explains why a film density of **2.0 corresponds to 1 % transmittance** — a critical benchmark for radiographic viewing conditions per ASTM E1742.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Film Speed (S) from the Characteristic Curve

**Given:** A radiographic film is exposed such that the straight-line portion of its H & D curve is observed between Net Density 2.0 and 4.0. The exposure required to achieve a Net Density of 2.0 (above base + fog) is measured to be $E_{2.0}$.

**Definition:** Speed $S$ is inversely proportional to the exposure required to reach a specified density:

$$S = \frac{1}{E_{2.0}}$$

**Step 1 — Tabulate the straight-line data.**

Suppose the curve is anchored at $(E_1, D_1) = (0.10 \text{ mR}, \, 2.0)$ and $(E_2, D_2) = (0.40 \text{ mR}, \, 4.0)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the average gradient.**

$$\bar{G} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10}(0.40) - \log_{10}(0.10)}$$

$$\bar{G} = \frac{2.0}{-0.398 - (-1.000)} = \frac{2.0}{0.602} \approx 3.32$$

**Step 3 — Compute the speed.**

$$S = \frac{1}{E_{2.0}} = \frac{1}{0.10 \text{ mR}} = 10 \, \text{mR}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A film with $S = 10$ is **very slow** (Type 1). A Type 4 film with the same gradient but $E_{2.0} = 0.005$ mR has $S = 200$ — twenty times faster. This explains why Type 4 films dominate **field weld inspection**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Exposure Time from the Inverse Square Law

**Given:** A radiographer achieves acceptable density at a source-to-film distance (SFD) of $d_1 = 700$ mm using an exposure time of $t_1 = 4$ minutes. The job requires a longer SFD, $d_2 = 1000$ mm. Find the new exposure time $t_2$ assuming the reciprocity law holds.

**Step 1 — Write the inverse square law for intensity.**

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Invoke the reciprocity law** ($I \cdot t = \text{constant}$):

$$I_1 \cdot t_1 = I_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two relations.**

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \frac{I_1}{I_2} = t_1 \cdot \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$t_2 = 4 \text{ min} \times \left( \frac{1000}{700} \right)^2 = 4 \times 2.041 = 8.16 \text{ min}$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing the SFD by 43 % requires **104 %** more exposure time. This is a routine KTU numerical problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Film for the Job

The following decision matrix is a **KTU exam favourite** — students are often asked to justify the choice of film for a given application.

| Application | Wall Thickness | Energy Range | Recommended Film | Screen Pair | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thin aluminium casting | 5–15 mm | 50–100 kV | **Type 1 or 2** | None or 0.02 mm lead front | Low energy, slow film, high contrast, no screens needed. |
| Steel weld | 10–25 mm | 150–250 kV (X-ray) or Ir-192 | **Type 3 or 4** | 0.10 mm lead (front & back) | Moderate speed, balanced contrast. |
| Thick pipe girth weld | 25–60 mm | Ir-192 / Co-60 | **Type 4** | 0.20 mm lead (front & back) | High speed essential; screens boost effective sensitivity. |
| Heavy steel forging | 60–150 mm | Co-60 or 1–4 MeV X-ray | **Type 4 or 5** | 0.25–0.40 mm lead (front & back) | Maximum speed; coarser granularity acceptable. |
| Aerospace turbine blade | 2–10 mm | 40–80 kV | **Type 1** | None (direct exposure) | Lowest granularity, maximum contrast, finest detail. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important SFD Rule of Thumb (ASTM E1742):**
> The minimum SFD is governed by $d_{\min} = 7.2 \cdot \sqrt{D_s \cdot t^{2/3}}$, where $D_s$ is the source size in mm and $t$ is the specimen thickness in mm. This ensures geometric unsharpness $U_g$ remains below the tolerance limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Double-Coated Industrial Film

The following Mermaid **block diagram** depicts the layered construction of a typical double-coated industrial radiographic film. Each block represents a functional layer, with arrows showing the direction of radiation penetration.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INCIDENT RADIATION Beam Direction Top to Bottom"] --> B["PROTECTIVE OVERCOAT Scratch resistant gelatin layer 1 micrometre"]
    B --> C["EMULSION LAYER FRONT AgBr AgI crystals in gelatin 10 to 20 micrometre"]
    C --> D["SUBBING LAYER Adhesion promoter 1 micrometre"]
    D --> E["POLYESTER BASE PET support 0.10 to 0.20 mm"]
    E --> F["SUBBING LAYER Adhesion promoter 1 micrometre"]
    F --> G["EMULSION LAYER BACK AgBr AgI crystals in gelatin 10 to 20 micrometre"]
    G --> H["ANTI HALATION BACKING Absorbs back reflected radiation 1 to 5 micrometre"]
    H --> I["PROCESSED FILM Radiograph with shadow image"]

    classDef radiation fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef structure fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    class A,I radiation
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H structure
```

## 4.2 The Characteristic (H & D) Curve — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **four regions** of the H & D curve to their respective operating regimes in industrial radiography.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["Exposure Axis log10 E in mR"] --> B0["Zero Exposure"]
    B0 --> C0["REGION 1 BASE PLUS FOG Density 0.05 to 0.25 No useful image"]
    C0 --> D0["REGION 2 TOE Under exposure Non linear avoid in practice"]
    D0 --> E0["REGION 3 STRAIGHT LINE Useful range Net Density 2.0 to 4.0 Slope gives Gradient"]
    E0 --> F0["REGION 4 SHOULDER Over exposure Saturation all grains reduced"]
    F0 --> G0["Maximum Density typically 4.5 to 5.0"]

    H0["Reading the Curve"] --> I0["Film Speed S = 1 divided by E at Net D equal 2.0"]
    I0 --> J0["Average Gradient G bar = slope between Net D 2.0 and 4.0"]
    J0 --> K0["Latitude = log E range over straight line = inverse of contrast"]

    classDef region fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef metric fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000

    class C0,D0,E0,F0,G0 region
    class I0,J0,K0 metric
```

## 4.3 Film Processing Workflow — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **complete wet-processing cycle** of an industrial radiographic film, with each block representing a process stage, the chemistry involved, and its functional outcome.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["EXPOSED FILM Carries latent image of AgX grains"] --> B0["STAGE 1 DEVELOPMENT 20 to 25 degree C for 3 to 8 minutes"]
    B0 --> C0["Developer Solution Hydroquinone plus Phenidone plus Sodium Sulphite plus Potassium Bromide"]
    C0 --> D0["Function Reduces exposed AgBr to metallic silver Ag black image forms"]
    D0 --> E0["STAGE 2 RINSE OR STOP BATH Acetic acid solution 30 seconds"]
    E0 --> F0["STAGE 3 FIXING Sodium Thiosulphate Hypo at 20 to 25 degree C for 5 to 10 minutes"]
    F0 --> G0["Function Dissolves unexposed AgBr crystals and removes them from emulsion"]
    G0 --> H0["STAGE 4 WASHING Running water 15 to 30 minutes"]
    H0 --> I0["Function Removes residual thiosulphate to prevent image fading"]
    I0 --> J0["STAGE 5 DRYING Cabinet dryer at 30 to 40 degree C or forced air"]
    J0 --> K0["FINAL RADIOGRAPH Ready for interpretation per ASTM E1742 viewing conditions"]

    classDef stage fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#1A237E,color:#000
    classDef chem fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    class B0,E0,F0,H0,J0 stage
    class C0,D0,G0,I0 chem
    class A0,K0 output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The *latent image* on the exposed film is invisible to the eye. **Development** is the chemical amplification stage that converts the invisible latent image into a visible, permanent metallic silver image. The characteristic curve is generated by plotting densities of processed films against known exposures.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define optical density and base density of a radiographic film. State the typical values of base density for an industrial film.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Optical Density (D):** It is the logarithmic measure of the film blackening defined as the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of incident light intensity ($I_0$) to transmitted light intensity ($I$) through the film:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right)$$

A density of 2.0 means only **1 %** of the incident light is transmitted; density 3.0 corresponds to **0.1 %** transmittance.

**Base Density ($D_b$):** It is the inherent optical density of the **unprocessed, unexposed** film — contributed by the polyester base, the gelatin of the emulsion, and any anti-halation backing. For a 0.18 mm thick polyester-base industrial film, $D_b$ typically lies between **0.05 and 0.20**.

(1 mark — definition of D; 1 mark — formula and interpretation; 1 mark — base density definition and range.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by film contrast? How is the average gradient (Ḡ) of a film determined from its characteristic curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Film Contrast (Ḡ):** Film contrast is the ability of the film to **record and display small differences in radiation intensity as visible differences in optical density**. Higher contrast produces a sharper, more readable image but reduces the exposure latitude.

**Determination of Average Gradient:** The average gradient is the slope of the **straight-line portion** of the H & D curve, measured between Net Density 2.0 and Net Density 4.0:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10} E_4 - \log_{10} E_2}$$

Typical values: **Ḡ ≈ 4 to 5** for Type 1/2 (high contrast); **Ḡ ≈ 3 to 4** for Type 3/4 (medium contrast).

(1 mark — contrast definition; 1 mark — gradient formula; 1 mark — typical values and significance.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **characteristic (Hurter & Driffield) curve** of an industrial radiographic film. Explain clearly the four regions of the curve and indicate which region is used for practical radiography. State the significance of base + fog, speed, and average gradient.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The characteristic curve plots **optical density (D)** on the y-axis versus **log₁₀ relative exposure (log E)** on the x-axis. The four regions are:

1. **Region I — Base + Fog:** Flat starting region corresponding to the unexposed film. Base + fog density for a typical industrial film is between 0.10 and 0.25. This represents the minimum background blackening of the processed film.

2. **Region II — Toe (Under-exposure Region):** A slowly rising, non-linear region where small exposure differences are not adequately recorded. Image quality is poor; this region is **not used** in industrial radiography.

3. **Region III — Straight Line (Useful Region):** Linear region between Net Density ≈ 2.0 and Net Density ≈ 4.0. This is the **operative range** for evaluating radiographs. The slope of this line is the **average gradient (Ḡ)**, which is the film contrast. Industrial viewing conditions per ASTM E1742 require densities in this range.

4. **Region IV — Shoulder (Over-exposure Region):** Curve flattens as nearly all silver halide grains are reduced to metallic silver. The maximum achievable density is around 4.5–5.0. Films exposed in this region suffer loss of contrast and detail.

**Significance of key parameters:**
- **Base + Fog:** Sets the lower limit of useful density; subtracted from total density to obtain Net Density.
- **Speed (S):** Inverse of exposure at Net D = 2.0; higher speed means shorter exposure time.
- **Average Gradient (Ḡ):** Slope of the straight-line portion; quantifies image contrast.

(2 marks — sketch; 3 marks — region description; 2 marks — parameter significance.)

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A **Type 3** film is found to have an average gradient of **3.2**. If the exposure required to produce a Net Density of 2.0 is **0.025 mR** and the straight line extends to a Net Density of 4.0 at an exposure of **0.10 mR**, calculate the **film speed** and verify the **average gradient** value.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Film Speed.**

The film speed is defined as the reciprocal of the exposure required to produce a Net Density of 2.0:

$$S = \frac{1}{E_{2.0}} = \frac{1}{0.025 \text{ mR}} = 40 \, \text{mR}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the Average Gradient.**

The average gradient is the slope of the straight-line portion between Net D = 2.0 and Net D = 4.0:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1}$$

Substituting $D_1 = 2.0$, $E_1 = 0.025$ mR, $D_2 = 4.0$, $E_2 = 0.10$ mR:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10}(0.10) - \log_{10}(0.025)}$$

$$\bar{G} = \frac{2.0}{(-1.000) - (-1.602)} = \frac{2.0}{0.602} \approx 3.32$$

**Step 3 — Comparison.**

Calculated Ḡ ≈ 3.32 ≈ 3.2 (declared value, with rounding tolerance). ✓ Verified.

**Conclusion:** The film speed is **S = 40 mR⁻¹**, consistent with a Type 3 (medium-speed) film. The average gradient of approximately **3.3** is consistent with a medium-to-high contrast film suitable for general radiography.

(2 marks — speed formula and value; 3 marks — gradient calculation; 2 marks — conclusion and consistency check.)

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **construction of a typical double-coated industrial radiographic film** with a neat diagram. List the functions of the base, emulsion, subbing layer, and protective overcoat. Why are industrial films double-coated?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A double-coated industrial film consists of the following layers (from top to bottom):

1. **Protective Overcoat (Supercoat):** A thin (~1 µm) layer of hard, scratch-resistant gelatin on the top surface. It protects the soft emulsion from mechanical damage during handling, loading into cassettes, and processing.

2. **Top Emulsion Layer:** A 10–20 µm thick layer containing silver halide crystals (AgBr, often with AgI for finer grain) suspended in gelatin. This is the radiation-sensitive layer where the latent image is formed.

3. **Subbing Layer (Top):** A thin adhesion-promoter coating that bonds the emulsion to the base, preventing delamination in the developer bath.

4. **Polyester (PET) Base:** A 0.10–0.20 mm thick transparent, dimensionally stable support. Polyester is preferred over cellulose triacetate (used in medical films) for its superior mechanical strength, low moisture absorption, and lower base density.

5. **Subbing Layer (Bottom):** Mirror of the top subbing layer.

6. **Bottom Emulsion Layer:** Same composition as the top emulsion — for **double-sided exposure**.

7. **Anti-halation Backing:** An optional dye-containing layer that absorbs back-reflected radiation to prevent *halation*, the unwanted image blur caused by radiation reflecting off the back of the base.

**Why double-coated?**
- **Doubles the silver halide content per unit area** ⇒ higher film speed (less exposure time).
- **Wider exposure latitude** because the two emulsion layers saturate at different rates.
- **Reduced mottle (radiographic noise)** because of statistically averaged grain response from both sides.
- **Shorter inspection times in production environments** — a major economic driver.

(2 marks — diagram; 3 marks — layer functions; 2 marks — justification of double-coating.)

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **inverse square law of radiation** and apply it to determine the required exposure time when a 4-minute exposure at 600 mm SFD is to be doubled to 1200 mm SFD for the same film density. What practical safety and image-quality implications follow from doubling the SFD?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Inverse Square Law.**

The intensity of radiation from a point source decreases in proportion to the square of the distance:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Invoke the Reciprocity Law.**

For a constant film density, the product of intensity and exposure time is constant (Bunsen–Roscoe law):

$$I_1 \cdot t_1 = I_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Exposure Time Equation.**

Combining the two relations:

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2$$
