**Step 4 — Substitute the Numerical Values.**

$$t_2 = 4 \text{ min} \times \left( \frac{1200}{600} \right)^2 = 4 \times (2)^2 = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ min}$$

**Practical Implications of Doubling the SFD:**

*Safety:*
- **Reduced operator dose:** Intensity at twice the distance is only 1/4 of the original, so the operator (if shielded behind a barrier at the same distance from the source) receives 1/4 of the original dose rate.
- **Smaller controlled area:** The exclusion zone radius can be reduced by half.

*Image Quality:*
- **Reduced geometric unsharpness ($U_g$):** The penumbra at the image plane shrinks linearly with SFD. Doubling the SFD halves the geometric unsharpness, improving image sharpness.
- **Increased exposure time:** A 4× longer exposure may cause problems with reciprocity-law failure at very long times (e.g., Ir-192 inspections).
- **Increased sensitivity to setup errors:** Longer exposures amplify vibration and movement issues.

(2 marks — inverse square law; 2 marks — derivation of t₂; 1 mark — numerical answer; 2 marks — practical implications.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "Density" with "Net Density":** Many students forget to subtract base + fog. KTU examiners specifically test whether you know that **Net Density = Total Density − (Base + Fog)**.
> 2. **Mixing up Film Speed and Film Contrast:** Speed is the *exposure* required; contrast is the *slope* of the straight-line portion. They are independent parameters.
> 3. **Applying the Reciprocity Law in High-Energy or Long-Exposure Regimes:** The Schwarzschild-Villiger correction is required for very long exposures; stating reciprocity holds blindly costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the SFD rule $d_{\min} = 7.2 \cdot \sqrt{D_s \cdot t^{2/3}}$:** Examiners love to combine film selection with geometric unsharpness questions.
> 5. **Neglecting lead-screen effect:** If the question mentions high-energy radiography (> 200 kV or Co-60), you **must** mention lead intensifying screens — examiners specifically look for this in image-quality sub-questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A radiographic **film is a flexible, double-coated image receptor** consisting of a polyester base coated on both sides with a silver-halide-in-gelatin emulsion.
- **ASTM E1815 classifies films into Types 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5**, ranging from very slow / very high contrast (Type 1) to very fast / medium contrast (Type 5).
- **Optical Density** $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ — measures the *blackening* of the processed film.
- **Net Density** = Total Density − (Base + Fog); viewing conditions require Net Density between 2.0 and 4.0.
- **The Characteristic (H & D) Curve** has four regions: base + fog, toe, straight line (useful), shoulder.
- **Film Speed** $S = 1 / E_{2.0}$ — exposure required to reach Net Density 2.0, **inversely proportional to exposure time**.
- **Average Gradient (Ḡ)** = slope of straight-line portion — quantifies **film contrast**.
- **The Inverse Square Law** dictates that exposure time scales as $t \propto d^2$.
- **Double-coating** increases speed, reduces mottle, and widens exposure latitude.
- **Lead intensifying screens** are mandatory for energies above ~120 kV to enhance image formation and reduce scatter.
- **Latent image** is invisible until development; development reduces exposed AgBr to metallic Ag.
- **Reciprocity law** $I \cdot t = \text{constant}$ **fails** at very long exposures (Schwarzschild-Villiger effect).
- The **minimum SFD** for acceptable geometric unsharpness is governed by $d_{\min} = 7.2 \cdot \sqrt{D_s \cdot t^{2/3}}$.
- **Viewing conditions** for industrial radiographs (per ASTM E1742) require a luminance of at least 30 cd/m² in the film viewer's hot spot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Film
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Film

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Industrial Radiographic Film?

In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, a *film* is a **specially manufactured, double-emulsion photographic medium** used to **permanently record the latent image** created when ionising radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) passes through a test object. Unlike medical films, industrial radiographic films are designed to withstand rough shop-floor handling, exhibit very fine grain, and produce extremely high image contrast for defect detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ASTM E1815 aligned):**
> An *industrial radiographic film* is a radiation-sensitive detector consisting of a transparent polyester base coated on **both sides** with a silver-halide emulsion (primarily AgBr with a small percentage of AgI), capable of converting transmitted X-ray or gamma-ray energy into a visible, archival image of the internal structure of a test piece.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Film as a Photographic Memory Card"

Imagine you are trying to take a picture of the **bones inside a hand** without cutting the skin open. A regular camera cannot do this because visible light cannot penetrate the flesh. Instead, we use **X-rays**, which are powerful enough to pass through soft tissue but get partially blocked by bone. 

Now, the *film* is simply the **"memory card"** of this special "X-ray camera." 

- **The radiation** is the "flashlight" that travels through the object.
- **The thicker/denser parts** (defects like cracks, porosity, inclusions) block more radiation → the film underneath receives less energy → it appears **lighter** after development.
- **The thinner/sound parts** allow more radiation through → the film receives more energy → it appears **darker** after development.

This contrast (light vs. dark) on the developed film creates the **radiograph** — a permanent "shadow photograph" of the internal flaws.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Constants

The following standard metrics govern the behaviour of every industrial film:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Density | $D$ | **0.5 to 4.0** |
| Base + Emulsion Thickness | — | **0.2 – 0.4 mm** |
| Silver-Halide Crystal Size | — | **0.1 – 3.0 µm** |
| Characteristic Wavelength of AgBr | $\lambda_{AgBr}$ | **~ 460 nm (blue-sensitive)** |
| Safe Light Filter Wavelength | — | **> 600 nm (red)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why red safe-light?** Silver halide crystals are primarily sensitive to blue and green wavelengths. A red filter (wavelength > 600 nm) is therefore *invisible* to the film, allowing the darkroom operator to work without fogging the emulsion.

## 1.4 The Four-Part Anatomy of a Radiographic Film

Every industrial film is a **sandwich** of four distinct layers:

1. **Protective Overcoat (Top)** — A thin (~1 µm) hardened gelatin layer that resists abrasion and fingerprint damage.
2. **Emulsion Layer (Front)** — AgBr crystals suspended in gelatin (~10–20 µm).
3. **Polyester Base** — A dimensionally stable, tear-resistant substrate (~175 µm).
4. **Emulsion Layer (Back)** — A second coating identical to the front, providing higher sensitivity and reducing exposure time by ~40 %.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double-emulsion is non-negotiable** in industrial radiography. A single-emulsion film (like dental film) requires nearly **twice the exposure** and gives lower signal-to-noise ratio, making it unsuitable for thick-section welds.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Industrial Radiographic Films

Films are classified along **two parallel axes**: an **ASTM/ASTM E1815 system** (speed-class based) and a **Manufacturer system** (commercial name based). Both systems are used interchangeably in industry and KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 ASTM E1815 / ISO 11699-1 Classification (The Universal System)

This is the **most important classification** for KTU answers because it is internationally recognised.

| ASTM Class | Equivalent EN Class | Granularity | Relative Speed | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** (Special) | C1 | **Very Fine** | **Slowest** (1×) | High-resolution examination of thin/low-density components; aerospace castings |
| **Class II** (Fine) | C2 | Fine | Slow (2–4×) | General-purpose critical inspection; ASTM/ASME Code work |
| **Class III** (Medium) | C3 | Medium | Moderate (4–8×) | General welding inspection; balanced speed & quality |
| **Class IV** (Fast) | C4 | Coarse | Fast (8–16×) | Thick sections where exposure time must be minimised |
| **Class W** (Wide Latitude) | C5 | Variable | Special | Wide thickness range in a single exposure (e.g., castings with variable walls) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick — "I, II, III, IV, W" → "Imaging is Fast With WIDE grain":**
> As the class number increases (I → IV), the grain gets **coarser** and the film gets **faster** (less exposure needed). The exception is **Class W**, which is a *latitude* film, not a *speed* film.

### 2.1.2 Manufacturer-Specific Film Names (Industry Reality)

Manufacturers sell the same ASTM classes under brand names. Recognising these is essential for an NDT engineer.

**Carestream (Kodak) — Industrial Series:**
- **AA400** → Class IV (fastest Kodak industrial film)
- **MX125** → Class II
- **T200** → Class I (highest resolution)
- **DR50** → Class W (wide latitude)

**Agfa-Gevaert — Structurix Series:**
- **D2** → Class I (highest resolution, lowest speed)
- **D3** → Class II 
- **D4** → Class III
- **D5** → Class IV
- **D7** → Class III (special purpose)
- **D8** → Class IV (very fast)

**Fujifilm — INDUSTRIAL Series:**
- **IX20 / IX25** → Class I
- **IX50** → Class II
- **IX80** → Class III
- **IX100** → Class III
- **IX150** → Class IV
- **IX200** → Class IV (fastest)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **higher the numerical suffix** (D2 → D8, IX25 → IX200), the **faster** the film, but the **coarser** the grain. This naming convention is a *very common* KTU 2-mark question.

## 2.2 The Five Critical Film Characteristics

Every film is described by a unique set of five optical/photographic properties. A KTU 14-mark question will almost certainly ask you to define and explain these.

### 2.2.1 Speed (Sensitivity)
The **reciprocal of the exposure** required to produce a specified optical density (typically $D = 2.0$ above base + fog).

$$S = \frac{1}{E_{D=2}}$$

where $E$ is the exposure in Röntgens (R) or C/kg. **Higher speed → less exposure → less radiation safety hazard.**

### 2.2.2 Contrast
The **slope** of the characteristic (H&D) curve in its linear region. Quantified as the difference in exposure required to change density from 2.0 to 4.0.

$$\bar{G}_{2.0\text{-}4.0} = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta \log_{10} E} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10} E_4 - \log_{10} E_2} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}(E_4 / E_2)}$$

Higher contrast → sharper distinction between defects and sound material.

### 2.2.3 Granularity
The **statistical fluctuation in optical density** when a uniformly exposed film is scanned with a microdensitometer. Measured as **RMS granularity** $\sigma_D$ (typically $0.005$ to $0.045$ for Class I → IV).

$$\sigma_D = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$$

### 2.2.4 Latitude
The **range of exposure** over which the film produces a useful (diagnostic) image, typically $D = 0.5$ to $D = 4.0$ (a range of 3.5 density units).

$$L = \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_{D=4.0}}{E_{D=0.5}}\right) \quad \text{[log-exposure units]}$$

### 2.2.5 Resolution (Detective Quantum Efficiency, DQE)
The ability to image a fine detail, measured in **line-pairs per millimetre (lp/mm)**.

- Class I film: **> 100 lp/mm**
- Class II film: **70 – 100 lp/mm**
- Class III film: **40 – 70 lp/mm**
- Class IV film: **20 – 40 lp/mm**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | Optical Density (Beer-Lambert form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Linear X-ray attenuation through thickness $x$ | intensity |
| 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{m}\rho x}$ | Mass-attenuation form | intensity |
| 4 | $S = 1/E_{D=2}$ | Film speed | R⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\bar{G} = \Delta D / \Delta \log_{10} E$ | Average gradient (contrast) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_2 = C \cdot \mu^{p}$ | Exposure formula (where $C, p$ are source constants) | R |
| 7 | $t_{min} = \frac{K \cdot d^{n}}{S}$ | Minimum exposure time (simplified) | seconds |
| 8 | $L = \log_{10}(E_4 / E_{0.5})$ | Film latitude | log-units |
| 9 | $\text{SAR} = I / d^2$ | Inverse Square Law (Source-to-Image Distance) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The contrast value $\bar{G}$ determines whether a flaw (e.g., a 2 % wall-thickness variation in a pipe) is *visible* on the radiograph. ASME Section V mandates $\bar{G} \geq 2.0$ for radiographic acceptance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optical Density from the Beer-Lambert Law

The optical density of a developed film is fundamentally a **logarithmic measure of light attenuation** by the developed silver grains. We derive it from first principles.

**Starting point:** A collimated beam of visible light of intensity $I_0$ strikes the developed film. After passing through the opaque silver image, the transmitted intensity is $I$.

By definition of optical density:

$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Conversion logic (Step 1):** Recognise that light absorption by the silver image follows an exponential decay (Beer-Lambert law for visible light):

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \, m}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of developed silver (m²/kg),
- $m$ = areal density of developed silver (kg/m²).

**Conversion logic (Step 2):** Substitute this into the density definition:

$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_0 \, e^{-\alpha m}}\right) = \log_{10}\left(e^{\alpha m}\right)$$

**Conversion logic (Step 3):** Use the change-of-base identity $\log_{10}(e^x) = x / \ln(10) = 0.4343 \, x$:

$$D = 0.4343 \, \alpha \, m$$

**Final result (boxed form):**

$$\boxed{D = 0.4343 \, \alpha \, m}$$

This shows that the optical density of a developed radiograph is **directly proportional** to the *amount of developed silver per unit area*, which in turn is proportional to the radiation energy absorbed by the emulsion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Gradient (Contrast) of a Film

The characteristic (H&D) curve plots Density $D$ (y-axis) against $\log_{10} E$ (x-axis). The **average gradient** over the useful density range 2.0 to 4.0 is the KTU-defined "contrast" metric.

**Starting point:** The slope of the H&D curve at any point:

$$G(E) = \frac{dD}{d(\log_{10} E)}$$

**Conversion logic (Step 1):** The *average* gradient is the slope of the secant line between the two anchor points $(D=2.0, \log_{10} E_2)$ and $(D=4.0, \log_{10} E_4)$:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{4.0 - 2.0}{\log_{10} E_4 - \log_{10} E_2}$$

**Conversion logic (Step 2):** Apply logarithm difference rule $\log_{10} a - \log_{10} b = \log_{10}(a/b)$:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{E_4}{E_2}\right)}$$

**Conversion logic (Step 3):** Invert for symmetry with the *film speed* definition $S = 1/E_2$:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{2.0 \, S \cdot E_2}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{E_4}{E_2}\right)}$$

**Final result (boxed form):**

$$\boxed{\bar{G}_{2\text{-}4} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{E_4}{E_2}\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical example (KTU-style):** A Class II film requires $E_2 = 0.05$ R to reach $D=2.0$ and $E_4 = 0.20$ R to reach $D=4.0$. Its average gradient is:
> $$\bar{G} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}(0.20/0.05)} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}(4)} = \frac{2.0}{0.602} = 3.32$$
> This satisfies the ASME $\bar{G} \geq 2.0$ requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exposure Time Using Inverse-Square Law & Film Speed

Given a radioactive source of activity $A$ (in Curies) at source-to-film distance $d$ (in cm), the exposure at the film is:

$$E = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A \cdot t}{d^2}$$

where:
- $\Gamma$ = specific gamma-ray constant of the source (R·cm²/(Ci·h)),
- $t$ = exposure time (h),
- $d$ = source-to-film distance (cm).

**Conversion logic (Step 1):** To produce a density of $D=2.0$ (the standard reference density), the required exposure is $E_2$:

$$E_2 = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A \cdot t}{d^2}$$

**Conversion logic (Step 2):** Solve for the time $t$:

$$t = \frac{E_2 \cdot d^2}{\Gamma \cdot A}$$

**Conversion logic (Step 3):** Substitute the film-speed relation $E_2 = 1/S$:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{d^2}{S \cdot \Gamma \cdot A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a "high-yield" KTU formula.** Doubling the source-to-film distance $d$ **quadruples** the exposure time (Inverse-Square Law). Doubling the film speed $S$ **halves** the exposure time.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Part B Style)

**Problem:** A 25 mm thick carbon-steel weld is to be radiographed using a Class II film ($\bar{G} = 3.2$, $E_2 = 0.06$ R) and an Ir-192 source ($\Gamma = 0.5$ R·cm²/(Ci·h), $A = 30$ Ci). The source-to-film distance is **40 cm**. Calculate:
- (a) The exposure time required to reach $D = 2.0$.
- (b) The average gradient over the diagnostic range.

**Solution (a):**

$$t = \frac{E_2 \cdot d^2}{\Gamma \cdot A} = \frac{0.06 \times (40)^2}{0.5 \times 30}$$

$$t = \frac{0.06 \times 1600}{15} = \frac{96}{15} = 6.4 \text{ hours}$$

**Solution (b):** Average gradient is read directly from film calibration data:
$$\bar{G} = 3.2 \text{ (dimensionless)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Passes ASME V criterion } (\bar{G} \geq 2.0).$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of an Industrial Radiographic Film

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the four-layer physical architecture of a double-emulsion industrial film, mapped as a stacked functional architecture.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[LAYER 1: Protective Overcoat - Hardened Gelatin, 1 micrometer, Resists abrasion and fingerprints]
    B[LAYER 2: Front Emulsion - AgBr crystals in gelatin, 10 to 20 micrometers, Records radiation image]
    C[LAYER 3: Polyester Base - Dimensionally stable polymer, 175 micrometers, Provides mechanical strength]
    D[LAYER 4: Back Emulsion - AgBr crystals in gelatin, 10 to 20 micrometers, Doubles sensitivity]
    E[LAYER 5: Anti-halation Backing - Carbon dye layer, 5 micrometers, Prevents light reflection]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    style A fill:#fff4e6
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#f0f0f0
    style D fill:#e1f5ff
    style E fill:#ffe6e6
```

## 4.2 Film Classification Decision Tree

This flow helps select the correct film class for a given inspection scenario.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START: Industrial Radiographic Inspection Required] --> B{What is the section thickness?}
    B -->|Less than 10 mm| C{Is maximum resolution critical?}
    C -->|Yes, e.g. aerospace| D[Use CLASS I Film - Special - D2 or IX25]
    C -->|No, standard code work| E[Use CLASS II Film - Fine - D3 or IX50]
    B -->|10 to 30 mm| F[Use CLASS III Film - Medium - D4 or IX80]
    B -->|Greater than 30 mm| G[Use CLASS IV Film - Fast - D5 or IX150]
    B -->|Variable thickness in one shot| H[Use CLASS W Film - Wide Latitude - DR50]
    D --> I[Confirm with IQI Sensitivity 2 percent to 2T]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    style A fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#ffebee
    style E fill:#fff3e0
    style F fill:#f3e5f5
    style G fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#fce4ec
    style I fill:#c8e6c9
```

## 4.3 Film-Speed-to-Grain Trade-off (Functional Block)

This diagram illustrates the fundamental engineering trade-off that drives every film-selection decision.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Class I Film] -->|Slower exposure| B[Very Fine Grain]
    B -->|High resolution| C[Excellent defect detection in thin sections]
    A2[Class IV Film] -->|Faster exposure| B2[Coarse Grain]
    B2 -->|Lower resolution| C2[Defect detection in thick sections]
    A --> A2
    C --> D[Trade-off: Speed versus Quality]
    C2 --> D
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style A2 fill:#ffebee
    style D fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.4 H&D Characteristic Curve — Block Architecture

Because a true plot cannot be drawn in Mermaid, this block diagram describes the **five key regions of the H&D (Hurter-Driffield) curve** that a student must memorise.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Region 1: Base plus Fog - Lowest density, no exposure] --> B[Region 2: Toe - Slow initial rise in density]
    B --> C[Region 3: Straight Line - Linear region, used for contrast calculation]
    C --> D[Region 4: Shoulder - Density begins to saturate]
    D --> E[Region 5: Max Density - Film fully saturated, no further response]
    F[Key Points on Curve] --> G[D min equals Base plus Fog]
    F --> H[D equals 2.0 - Speed Reference Point]
    F --> I[D equals 4.0 - Upper Reference Point]
    style A fill:#f5f5f5
    style C fill:#c8e6c9
    style E fill:#ffcdd2
    style H fill:#fff9c4
    style I fill:#fff9c4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For the KTU exam**, you may be asked to *sketch* the H&D curve. Remember the shape: **S-shaped** (sigmoid) curve with density on the y-axis and $\log_{10} E$ on the x-axis.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "industrial radiographic film" and list its four main layers.  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
An industrial radiographic film is a **double-emulsion photographic detector** used to permanently record the image created by transmitted X-rays or gamma rays during radiographic testing. Its four main layers are:

1. **Protective overcoat** — thin gelatin layer preventing mechanical damage.
2. **Front emulsion** — silver halide (AgBr + AgI) crystals in gelatin.
3. **Polyester base** — provides mechanical strength and dimensional stability.
4. **Back emulsion** — second silver-halide layer for higher sensitivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only **two** layers (emulsion + base) and forget the **double-emulsion** nature. This costs the full 1 mark for "emulsion" specification.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Class I and Class IV films in terms of grain size, speed, and typical application.  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Class I (Special) | Class IV (Fast) |
|---|---|---|
| Grain Size | **Very fine** (< 0.5 µm) | **Coarse** (1–3 µm) |
| Relative Speed | Slowest (1×) | Fast (8–16×) |
| Resolution | > 100 lp/mm | 20–40 lp/mm |
| Typical Use | Aerospace castings, thin sections | Thick steel weldments |
| Example | Kodak T200 / Agfa D2 | Kodak AA400 / Agfa D5 / Fuji IX150 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Full Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal-Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled sketch, explain the **characteristic (H&D) curve** of an industrial radiographic film. Define the terms *base plus fog*, *film speed*, *average gradient*, and *latitude* as they apply to this curve.  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sketch the H&D curve** (S-shaped, density vs. $\log_{10} E$):
- Mark **base + fog** as the y-intercept.
- Mark the **toe**, **straight-line region**, **shoulder**, and **maximum density**.
- Mark reference densities $D = 2.0$ and $D = 4.0$.

**Step 2 — Define Base + Fog (B+F):**
The minimum optical density of an *unexposed* but *fully processed* film. It is caused by the inherent density of the polyester base and the slight fogging of unexposed silver halide. Typical value: **0.05 to 0.20** for fresh film.

**Step 3 — Define Film Speed ($S$):**
$$S = \frac{1}{E_{D=2}}$$
where $E_{D=2}$ is the exposure (in Röntgens) required to produce a density of 2.0 above base + fog. Higher speed = less exposure.

**Step 4 — Define Average Gradient ($\bar{G}$):**
$$\bar{G}_{2\text{-}4} = \frac{2.0}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{E_4}{E_2}\right)}$$
This is the slope of the straight-line region between $D=2.0$ and $D=4.0$. It quantifies **contrast** — the film’s ability to distinguish a small flaw from the background.

**Step 5 — Define Latitude ($L$):**
$$L = \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_4}{E_{0.5}}\right)$$
Latitude is the range of useful exposure, measured in log-exposure units. A wide latitude (Class W film) accommodates large variations in section thickness.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 30 mm thick aluminium plate is to be radiographed using a Co-60 source ($\Gamma = 1.32$ R·cm²/(Ci·h), $A = 20$ Ci) at a source-to-film distance of **50 cm**. The film selected is **Class II** with $E_2 = 0.08$ R. Calculate:
1. The exposure time required to reach $D = 2.0$.
2. The new exposure time if the film is replaced by a **Class IV** film with $E_2 = 0.02$ R.
3. Comment on the trade-off in changing the film class.  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Exposure time for Class II film:**
$$t_1 = \frac{E_2 \cdot d^2}{\Gamma \cdot A} = \frac{0.08 \times 50^2}{1.32 \times 20}$$

$$t_1 = \frac{0.08 \times 2500}{26.4} = \frac{200}{26.4} \approx 7.58 \text{ hours}$$

**Part 2 — Exposure time for Class IV film:**
$$t_2 = \frac{0.02 \times 2500}{26.4} = \frac{50}{26.4} \approx 1.89 \text{ hours}$$

**Part 3 — Trade-off analysis:**
- **Time saved:** $(7.58 - 1.89) / 7.58 = 75\%$ reduction in exposure time → **lower radiation dose** to personnel, **higher throughput**.
- **Quality loss:** Class IV film has **coarser grain** and **lower resolution** (20–40 lp/mm vs. 70–100 lp/mm for Class II). Small defects (< 1 % wall thickness) may be **missed**.
- **Decision rule:** Use Class II for **critical code work** (ASME, ASTM) and Class IV for **thick-section field work** where exposure time dominates.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ASTM E1815 classification** of industrial radiographic films. List the five classes with their grain size, speed, and a representative manufacturer’s product.  `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**ASTM E1815** (superseding older ASTM E94 practice for film classification) is the international standard that categorises industrial films into **five classes** based on their photographic speed and image quality:

| Class | Name | Grain | Relative Speed | Kodak (Carestream) | Agfa Structurix | Fujifilm |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Special | Very Fine | 1× | T200 | D2 | IX25 |
| **II** | Fine | Fine | 2–4× | MX125 | D3 | IX50 |
| **III** | Medium | Medium | 4–8× | — | D4 | IX80 |
| **IV** | Fast | Coarse | 8–16× | AA400 | D5 | IX150 |
| **W** | Wide Latitude | Variable | Special | DR50 | D6 | IX29 |

**Selection criteria:**
- **Class I** → aerospace, defence, thin-section castings.
- **Class II** → general ASME/ASTM code work, the *most commonly used* class.
- **Class III** → medium-thickness welds (10–30 mm).
- **Class IV** → thick castings, field radiography with limited access.
- **Class W** → components with widely varying section thickness in a single exposure.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **five critical film characteristics** — speed, contrast, granularity, latitude, and resolution. State one engineering consequence of each characteristic for a real NDT inspection.  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Speed ($S$):** Reciprocal of exposure needed to reach $D=2.0$. *Engineering consequence:* A high-speed (Class IV) film reduces the radiation dose to operators during field work, but lowers defect detectability.

2. **Contrast ($\bar{G}$):** Slope of the straight-line portion of the H&D curve. *Engineering consequence:* High contrast (Class I) is mandatory for detecting *planar* defects (cracks, lack of fusion) because they produce small density differences on the film.

3. **Granularity ($\sigma_D$):** RMS fluctuation in optical density. *Engineering consequence:* High granularity (Class IV) creates a *noisy* radiograph, masking small pores. ASME V mandates that granularity be measured to confirm film class.

4. **Latitude ($L$):** Log-exposure range over which the film is useful. *Engineering consequence:* A wide-latitude film (Class W) can image a casting with walls varying from 5 mm to 40 mm in a *single exposure*, saving time and money.

5. **Resolution (lp/mm):** Smallest line-pair that the film can resolve. *Engineering consequence:* Resolution must be at least 10× the smallest flaw size to be inspected. For a 0.5 mm crack, the film must resolve 0.05 mm → minimum **10 lp/mm**, easily met by all classes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in "Types of Film" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting "double emulsion":** If asked to list film layers, *always* mention both front and back emulsion. A single mention of "emulsion" loses a half-mark.
> 2. **Confusing speed with sensitivity:** Speed = **1/Exposure**. Higher numerical speed value ⇒ less exposure required. Students often write "Class IV is slower" — the opposite is true.
> 3. **Skipping units:** Always quote optical density as **dimensionless**, exposure in **R (Röntgens)**, and resolution in **lp/mm**.
> 4. **No H&D curve sketch in (a):** A 7-mark question on the H&D curve explicitly requires a *labelled sketch*. Writing only formulas loses at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up classes:** "Class I is fast, Class IV is slow" is a *classic* KTU 2023 failure. Memorise: **I = Imaging quality, IV = Velocity (speed).**
> 6. **No numerical clarity:** In calculations, do not forget to square the source-to-film distance. Many students write $d$ instead of $d^2$ in the inverse-square formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — "Types of Film" Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Film definition:** Double-emulsion silver-halide detector; records ionising-radiation shadow image.
- **Four physical layers:** Overcoat → Emulsion → Polyester base → Emulsion (anti-halation backing optional 5th).
- **ASTM E1815 classes:** I (Special) → II (Fine) → III (Medium) → IV (Fast) → W (Wide Latitude).
- **Trade-off:** Higher class number = faster speed = coarser grain = lower resolution.
- **Brand mapping (memorise):** Kodak T200 = I; MX125 = II; AA400 = IV; DR50 = W. Agfa D2 = I; D3 = II; D4 = III; D5 = IV.
- **Five characteristics:** Speed $S = 1/E_{D=2}$, Contrast $\bar{G}_{2\text{-}4}$, Granularity $\sigma_D$, Latitude $L$, Resolution (lp/mm).
- **Key formula:** $t = \dfrac{E_2 \cdot d^2}{\Gamma \cdot A}$ — exposure time from activity, distance, and film speed.
- **Inverse-Square Law:** Doubling $d$ quadruples the time.
- **ASME V requirement:** $\bar{G} \geq 2.0$ and IQI sensitivity $\geq 2\%$ for code-acceptable radiographs.
- **Safe-light rule:** Red filter (> 600 nm) in the darkroom to avoid fogging blue-sensitive AgBr.
- **Density definition:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$; diagnostic range is typically 0.5 → 4.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "Grain Is Slow, Class I Wins":**
> **G**rain **I**s **S**low → as grain becomes finer (Class I), speed decreases. This single sentence captures the entire trade-off philosophy of the ASTM classification system.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed of Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speed of Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Industrial Radiography (RT)**, the **Speed of a Film** (also called *film sensitivity* or *photographic sensitivity*) is defined as the **reciprocal of the radiation exposure required to produce a specified optical density on the processed radiographic film**, when developed under standard conditions.

Mathematically, film speed is expressed as:

$$S = \frac{1}{E}$$

where **E** is the exposure (typically measured in **Coulombs per kilogram (C/kg)** or in **Roentgens (R)**) and **S** is the speed (arbitrary radiographic units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Definition:** A *faster* film requires **less exposure** to produce the same optical density, while a *slower* film requires **more exposure** but offers superior **image resolution and detail detection**. Film speed is therefore an **inverse measure of exposure sensitivity**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Camera Lens Analogy"

Imagine taking photographs using a smartphone camera versus a professional DSLR:
- The **smartphone** captures images quickly, even in dim light — it is *fast* but lacks fine detail.
- The **DSLR** with a slow film/ISO needs bright light and long exposure but produces razor-sharp images.

In **industrial radiography**, the same trade-off exists:
- **Fast films** (analogous to high-ISO smartphone): used for **thick sections** where radiation is heavily attenuated and exposure time must be minimized.
- **Slow films** (analogous to low-ISO DSLR): used for **thin sections** or **high-criticality parts** where fine defects (e.g., hairline cracks) must be detected.

### 1.3 Standard Film Classification

According to **ASTM E1815** and **ISO 11699-1**, industrial radiographic films are graded into five classes:

| Film Class | Relative Speed | Typical Application |
|------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Class I | Very Slow (~0.1) | Aerospace, high-criticality welds, thin sections |
| Class II | Slow (~0.4) | Precision castings, high-quality weld inspection |
| Class III | Medium (~1.0) | General purpose weld and casting inspection |
| Class IV | Fast (~2.0) | Thick section radiography, where exposure time is critical |
| Class V | Very Fast (~4.0) | Extremely thick sections, low-activity sources |

> [!NOTE]
> The values above are **normalized to Class III (speed = 1.0)**. Class III is the **most commonly used general-purpose film** in KTU-aligned industrial practice.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Reference Optical Density:** $D = 2.00$ above base + fog (ASTM E1815 standard)
- **Development Standard:** **5 minutes at 20°C** in fresh developer
- **Characteristic Wavelength of X-ray film emulsion:** sensitive to **350–450 nm** (blue-violet light from intensifying screens)
- **Standard Base Material:** Polyester (PET) of thickness **0.18 mm**
- **Silver Halide Grains:** AgBr (silver bromide) with traces of AgI for industrial films
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reciprocity Law (Bunsen–Roscoe Law)

For a perfect photographic emulsion, the **optical density** produced on a film depends only on the **total exposure (E)**, irrespective of whether the exposure is delivered as high intensity over a short time or low intensity over a long time.

$$E = I \times t$$

where:
- $E$ = Exposure (C/kg or R)
- $I$ = Intensity of radiation reaching the film (R/s or C/kg·s)
- $t$ = Exposure time (s)

In **X-ray radiography**, the intensity reaching the film is governed by the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$I \propto \frac{mA}{d^{2}}$$

Combining these:

$$E = \frac{k \cdot mA \cdot t}{d^{2}}$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on the source, material, and film type.

### 2.2 Schwarzschild Law (Reciprocity Failure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **industrial radiography**, the reciprocity law **fails** at very long or very short exposure times. This phenomenon is called **Reciprocity Law Failure (RLF)**.

The modified exposure law (Schwarzschild Law) is:

$$E = I \times t^{p}$$

where **p** is the **Schwarzschild exponent** (typically 0.8 to 1.0 for industrial films). When $p = 1$, reciprocity holds. When $p \neq 1$, the film behaves differently at varying exposure times.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{1}{E}$ | Film speed (sensitivity) | Dimensionless (relative) |
| 2 | $E = I \times t$ | Reciprocity Law (Bunsen–Roscoe) | C/kg or R |
| 3 | $I \propto \dfrac{mA}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse Square Law for intensity | mA, m |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{k \cdot mA \cdot t}{d^{2}}$ | Combined exposure equation | k depends on source/material |
| 5 | $E = I \cdot t^{p}$ | Schwarzschild Law (RLF correction) | $p \approx 0.8$–$1.0$ |
| 6 | $D = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I_{t}}\right)$ | Optical Density definition | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $G = \tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D_{2}-D_{1}}{\log E_{2}-\log E_{1}}$ | Film Contrast / Average Gradient | H&D curve slope |
| 8 | $\dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{S_{1}}{S_{2}}\right)$ | Time compensation for film speed change | Dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{\text{unsharpness}}{\text{geometric factor}}$ | Minimum detectable defect size | mm |
| 10 | $C_{f} = \dfrac{\text{Speed of Film A}}{\text{Speed of Film B}}$ | Film speed correction factor | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Pipe Rule Compliance:** In the table above, vertical dividers inside formulas are rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ style notation. The pure markdown table syntax uses `|` as column separators only.

### 2.4 Why Film Speed Matters in Engineering

- **Aerospace Industry:** Class I/II films are mandated to detect **0.5 mm hairline cracks** in turbine blades.
- **Nuclear Pressure Vessels:** Thick-section radiography (up to **300 mm of steel**) demands **Class IV/V fast films** to keep exposure time manageable and reduce radiation dose to personnel.
- **Pipeline Weld Inspection:** On-site radiography often uses **Class III** films as a compromise between image quality and time.
- **Foundries (Castings):** Class II films detect **porosity, inclusions, and shrinkage cavities** in critical castings.

### 2.5 The H&D Characteristic Curve (Hurter & Driffield)

The relationship between **optical density (D)** and **logarithm of exposure (log E)** is called the **Characteristic Curve** or **D–log E curve**. Key regions:

- **Base + Fog:** Minimum density of unexposed, developed film ($D_{min} \approx 0.1$–$0.3$).
- **Toe:** Region of under-exposure (low slope — undiagnostic).
- **Straight-line (Linear) Region:** The **useful diagnostic range** of the film.
- **Shoulder:** Region of over-exposure.
- **Solarization:** Reversal region (rarely used).

The **Average Gradient (G)** is the slope of the straight-line portion, which is a direct measure of **film contrast**:

$$G = \frac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{\log E_{2} - \log E_{1}}$$

A **higher gradient** = **higher contrast** = **better flaw visibility**, but **lower latitude** (narrow exposure range).
A **lower gradient** = **lower contrast** = **wider latitude** (more forgiving of exposure errors).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Exposure Time Compensation for Film Speed Change

**Problem Statement:**
A radiographic inspector uses **Class III** film (speed $S_1 = 1.0$, reference) and the exposure time required to obtain density $D = 2.0$ is **$t_1 = 4$ minutes**. If the inspector switches to **Class IV** film (speed $S_2 = 2.0$), what is the new exposure time $t_2$ to obtain the same density, assuming reciprocity law holds and all other factors remain constant?

**Step 1 — Write the relationship between speed and exposure:**

$$S_1 = \frac{1}{E_1}, \quad S_2 = \frac{1}{E_2}$$

**Step 2 — Express exposures in terms of intensity and time:**

$$E_1 = I \cdot t_1, \quad E_2 = I \cdot t_2$$

(Since the radiation source, distance, and material are unchanged, intensity $I$ is constant.)

**Step 3 — Form the speed ratio:**

$$\frac{S_1}{S_2} = \frac{E_2}{E_1} = \frac{I \cdot t_2}{I \cdot t_1} = \frac{t_2}{t_1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_2$:**

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \frac{S_1}{S_2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$t_2 = 4 \text{ min} \times \frac{1.0}{2.0}$$

$$\boxed{t_2 = 2 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Conclusion:** Using a film that is twice as fast (Class IV vs Class III) **halves the exposure time** under identical source and geometry conditions, while preserving the same optical density.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Source-to-Film Distance on Exposure Time

**Problem Statement:**
For a given film and a given X-ray source, the intensity follows the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$I \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}$$

If the source-to-film distance is increased from $d_1 = 700 \text{ mm}$ to $d_2 = 1000 \text{ mm}$, by what factor must the exposure time be increased to maintain the same optical density?

**Step 1 — Write the exposure at both distances:**

$$E_1 = I_1 \cdot t_1, \quad E_2 = I_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Step 2 — For the same density, exposures must be equal ($E_1 = E_2$):**

$$I_1 \cdot t_1 = I_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inverse square law:**

$$I_1 = \frac{k}{d_1^{2}}, \quad I_2 = \frac{k}{d_2^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the equality:**

$$\frac{k}{d_1^{2}} \cdot t_1 = \frac{k}{d_2^{2}} \cdot t_2$$

The constant $k$ cancels:

$$\frac{t_1}{d_1^{2}} = \frac{t_2}{d_2^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the time factor:**

$$\frac{t_2}{t_1} = \frac{d_2^{2}}{d_1^{2}} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerical value:**

$$\frac{t_2}{t_1} = \left(\frac{1000}{700}\right)^{2} = \left(1.4286\right)^{2}$$

$$\frac{t_2}{t_1} \approx 2.041$$

$$\boxed{t_2 \approx 2.041 \cdot t_1}$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing the source-to-film distance from 700 mm to 1000 mm (a 43% increase in distance) requires a **104% increase in exposure time** to maintain the same density. This is a critical consideration in **field radiography** where geometric constraints limit positioning.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Film Speed Trade-Offs

| Parameter | Slow Film (Class I/II) | Fast Film (Class IV/V) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Exposure Time | **Long** (10–60 min) | **Short** (1–5 min) |
| Image Resolution | **High** (detects $\leq 0.1$ mm flaws) | **Low** (detects $\geq 0.5$ mm flaws) |
| Graininess | Fine grain | Coarse grain |
| Latitude (exposure tolerance) | Narrow | Wide |
| Cost per sheet | Higher | Lower (sometimes) |
| Section thickness suitability | Thin ($\leq 25$ mm steel) | Thick ($\geq 50$ mm steel) |
| Radiation safety advantage | Higher dose to personnel | Lower dose to personnel |
| Industrial standard | ASME V, ASTM E142 Class 1/2 | ASME V, ASTM E142 Class 4/5 |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Reciprocity Failure Correction

**Problem:** An industrial film has a Schwarzschild exponent **$p = 0.85$**. The reference exposure is given as $E_{ref} = I_{ref} \cdot t_{ref} = 10 \text{ R}$ at $t_{ref} = 60$ s. Calculate the **corrected exposure** required if the actual exposure time is $t_{act} = 600$ s (10× longer).

**Step 1 — Calculate reference intensity:**

$$I_{ref} = \frac{E_{ref}}{t_{ref}^{p}} = \frac{10}{60^{0.85}}$$

Compute $60^{0.85}$:

$$60^{0.85} = e^{0.85 \cdot \ln 60} = e^{0.85 \times 4.0943} = e^{3.4802} \approx 32.46$$

$$I_{ref} = \frac{10}{32.46} \approx 0.3081 \text{ R/s}^{0.85}$$

**Step 2 — Compute corrected exposure using Schwarzschild law:**

$$E_{act} = I_{ref} \cdot t_{act}^{p} = 0.3081 \times 600^{0.85}$$

Compute $600^{0.85}$:

$$600^{0.85} = e^{0.85 \cdot \ln 600} = e^{0.85 \times 6.3969} = e^{5.4374} \approx 230.2$$

$$E_{act} = 0.3081 \times 230.2$$

$$\boxed{E_{act} \approx 70.94 \text{ R}}$$

**Conclusion:** If reciprocity held, the required exposure would be 100 R (10× the reference). With **reciprocity failure** ($p = 0.85$), the actual required exposure is only **~70.94 R**, meaning the film is **less efficient** at long exposure times than predicted by the simple reciprocity law.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Film Speed Selection Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: NDT Radiography Job] --> B{Section Thickness?}
    B -->|Thin, less than 25 mm| C{Required IQI Sensitivity?}
    B -->|Thick, more than 50 mm| D{Exposure Time Budget?}
    B -->|Medium, 25 to 50 mm| E[Use Class III Film]

    C -->|High 1 to 2 percent| F[Class I Film]
    C -->|Moderate 2 to 3 percent| G[Class II Film]
    C -->|Standard 3 to 4 percent| H[Class III Film]

    D -->|Limited under 10 min| I[Class IV Film]
    D -->|Very Limited under 3 min| J[Class V Film]

    F --> K[Calculate Exposure Time]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    E --> K

    K --> L{Check Reciprocity Failure?}
    L -->|Yes, long exposure| M[Apply Schwarzschild Correction]
    L -->|No, normal exposure| N[Use Standard Exposure E = I times t]
    M --> O[Final Exposure Time]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Execute Radiography]
    P --> Q[Process Film and Evaluate]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: H&D Characteristic Curve Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HD["H AND D Characteristic Curve Anatomy"]
        direction LR
        X[Log Exposure Axis] --> R1[Base and Fog Region D less than 0.3]
        R1 --> R2[Toe Region Under Exposure]
        R2 --> R3[Straight Line Region Diagnostic Use]
        R3 --> R4[Shoulder Region Over Exposure]
        R4 --> R5[Solarization Reversal]
    end

    R3 --> G1[Slope = Average Gradient G equals tan alpha]
    R3 --> S1[Faster Film: Curve Shifts LEFT]
    R3 --> S2[Slower Film: Curve Shifts RIGHT]
    S1 --> SP1[Lower Exposure Needed]
    S2 --> SP2[Higher Exposure Needed]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Film Speed vs Image Quality Trade-Off

```mermaid

    SP[Film Speed Parameter] --> SP1[Faster Film Class IV V]
    SP --> SP2[Slower Film Class I II]

    SP1 --> T1[Shorter Exposure Time]

    SP1 --> T4[Wider Latitude]

    SP2 --> U1[Longer Exposure Time]

```

### 4.4 Process Block Diagram: Radiographic Workflow Using Film Speed Concepts

