```mermaid
    S0[Source Selection Ir192 X-ray Co60] --> S1[Calculate Intensity I from mA and d]
    S1 --> S2[Select Film Class per Thickness and Code]
    S2 --> S3{Apply Speed Factor S}
    S3 --> S4[Compute Base Exposure E equals I times t]
    S4 --> S5{Check Exposure Duration}
    S5 -->|Normal| S6[Use Bunsen Roscoe Reciprocity]
    S5 -->|Very Long or Very Short| S7[Apply Schwarzschild p less than 1]
    S6 --> S8[Execute Exposure]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Film Processing 5 min at 20 degrees C]
    S9 --> S10[Read Optical Density D on Densitometer]
    S10 --> S11{D equals 2.0 plus or minus 0.2?}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Acceptable Radiograph]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Re shoot with Corrected Exposure]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Speed of a Film" as used in industrial radiography. How is it related to the exposure required to produce a given optical density?**

**Model Answer:**

The **speed of a radiographic film** is defined as the **reciprocal of the exposure (E)** required to produce a specified optical density (typically $D = 2.0$ above base + fog) under standard development conditions. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$S = \frac{1}{E}$$

A film that requires **less exposure** to achieve the same density is said to be a **faster** film (higher S), while one requiring **more exposure** is a **slower** film (lower S). Film speed is an inverse measure of radiation sensitivity and is critical in determining exposure time for a given source, distance, and material thickness. Faster films are preferred for thick sections; slower films are preferred for thin or critical sections.

**(3 Marks — Full credit for definition, formula, and one practical application.)**

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**What is Reciprocity Law Failure in radiographic films? State the Schwarzschild Law.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reciprocity Law Failure (RLF):** In an ideal photographic emulsion, the optical density produced depends only on the **total exposure (E = I × t)**, irrespective of how the exposure is distributed over time. In practice, **industrial radiographic films deviate from this behavior** at very long or very short exposure times — this deviation is called **Reciprocity Law Failure**.

**Schwarzschild Law** (corrected form):

$$E = I \times t^{p}$$

where **p** is the **Schwarzschild exponent** (typically 0.8 to 1.0 for industrial films). When $p = 1$, reciprocity holds; when $p < 1$, the film is **less efficient** at long exposure times, requiring more exposure than predicted by simple reciprocity.

**(3 Marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for Schwarzschild formula and meaning.)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing **film speed** with **film contrast (gradient)** — these are different concepts.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **standard reference density (D = 2.0)** when defining film speed.
> 3. Ignoring **reciprocity failure** in long-exposure industrial radiography (e.g., Ir-192 setups).
> 4. Not specifying **units** in formula-based answers.
> 5. Skipping the **Inverse Square Law** when explaining exposure calculations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the factors affecting the speed of radiographic films. How does film speed influence the choice of film for different industrial applications? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the characteristic (H&D) curve of a radiographic film. Explain the significance of the **average gradient** and **latitude** of the film. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Factors Affecting Film Speed and Industrial Application:**

**Factors affecting film speed:**

1. **Emulsion composition and thickness:** Heavier AgBr emulsions with finer grains → slower film with higher resolution.
2. **Type of intensifying screen:** Lead foil screens absorb backscattered radiation and intensify the image, effectively increasing perceived film speed.
3. **Wavelength of radiation:** Lower-energy (softer) radiation interacts more strongly with the emulsion, increasing effective speed.
4. **Development conditions:** Time, temperature, and developer activity directly influence final density and effective speed. **Over-development** can artificially "speed up" a film.
5. **Storage and age:** Aged or improperly stored films lose speed due to fogging.
6. **Reciprocity failure:** At non-standard exposure times, effective speed changes.

**Influence on industrial application:**

- **Class I/II (slow) films** are used in **aerospace, nuclear, and pressure-vessel welds** where sub-millimetre defect detection is mandatory.
- **Class III (medium) films** are the **general-purpose workhorse** of fabrication shops.
- **Class IV/V (fast) films** are chosen for **thick-section radiography** (e.g., pipelines, heavy castings) where source strength and exposure time are limiting factors.

The selection balances **image quality (resolution)** against **economic and radiation-safety constraints (exposure time)**.

---

**(b) H&D Curve, Average Gradient, and Latitude:**

**Characteristic Curve Description:**

The H&D (Hurter–Driffield) curve plots **optical density (D)** on the y-axis against the **logarithm of exposure (log E)** on the x-axis. The curve has five regions:

- **Base + Fog:** $D \approx 0.1$–$0.3$, representing unexposed, processed film.
- **Toe:** Curved, low-slope region of under-exposure.
- **Straight-line (linear) region:** The **diagnostic working range** of the film.
- **Shoulder:** Region of over-exposure, slope decreases.
- **Solarization:** Reversal region (not used in practice).

**Average Gradient (G):** It is the slope of the straight-line portion of the H&D curve:

$$G = \frac{D_{2} - D_{1}}{\log E_{2} - \log E_{1}}$$

A **higher G** means **higher contrast** and better flaw visibility, while a **lower G** means **lower contrast** but **wider latitude**.

**Latitude:** It is the **range of exposure** over which the film produces diagnostically useful densities (approximately the straight-line region). A film with a wide latitude is more forgiving of exposure errors and is preferred in field radiography where conditions are variable.

**Significance in NDT:**
- High-gradient films detect **small density differences** (good for tiny cracks).
- Wide-latitude films are used when exposure conditions cannot be tightly controlled.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — December 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between exposure time, source-to-film distance, and film speed for maintaining a constant optical density on a radiographic film. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A radiographic setup uses a **Class II film** (speed $S_1 = 0.4$, reference) at a source-to-film distance of **600 mm** with an exposure time of **8 minutes**. If the inspector switches to a **Class IV film** (speed $S_2 = 2.0$) and moves the source to **900 mm**, calculate the new exposure time required to maintain the same optical density. State any assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Derivation:**

The total exposure on the film is:

$$E = I \times t$$

The intensity of radiation reaching the film follows the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$I = \frac{k \cdot mA}{d^{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$E = \frac{k \cdot mA \cdot t}{d^{2}}$$

Film speed is defined as:

$$S = \frac{1}{E} \implies E = \frac{1}{S}$$

Equating the two expressions for exposure:

$$\frac{1}{S} = \frac{k \cdot mA \cdot t}{d^{2}}$$

Solving for time $t$:

$$t = \frac{d^{2}}{k \cdot mA \cdot S}$$

For a constant mA, constant k, and the requirement to maintain the same density (i.e., the same exposure $E$):

$$t \cdot S = \text{constant} \times d^{2}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{t_1 \cdot S_1}{d_1^{2}} = \frac{t_2 \cdot S_2}{d_2^{2}}$$

This is the **governing relationship** for exposure-time compensation when changing film type or geometry.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $S_1 = 0.4$, $d_1 = 600$ mm, $t_1 = 8$ min
- $S_2 = 2.0$, $d_2 = 900$ mm, $t_2 = ?$

**Using the derived relationship:**

$$\frac{t_1 \cdot S_1}{d_1^{2}} = \frac{t_2 \cdot S_2}{d_2^{2}}$$

Solving for $t_2$:

$$t_2 = t_1 \cdot \frac{S_1}{S_2} \cdot \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^{2}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$t_2 = 8 \times \frac{0.4}{2.0} \times \left(\frac{900}{600}\right)^{2}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- Speed ratio: $\dfrac{0.4}{2.0} = 0.20$
- Distance ratio squared: $\left(\dfrac{900}{600}\right)^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25$
- Product: $0.20 \times 2.25 = 0.45$

$$t_2 = 8 \times 0.45$$

$$\boxed{t_2 = 3.6 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Assumptions made:**
1. The **X-ray source output (mA, kV) and material being radiographed are unchanged.**
2. **Reciprocity law holds** (no Schwarzschild correction needed, exposure time is in the normal range).
3. **No reciprocity failure** during this 3.6-minute exposure.
4. The **intensifying screens and processing conditions** are identical for both films.
5. **Scatter radiation and geometric unsharpness** effects are neglected.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Speed of Films (Module 3, PESFT521)**

- **Film Speed Definition:** $S = 1/E$ — the **reciprocal of exposure** required to produce a reference density of **D = 2.0 above base + fog**.
- **Faster Film = Less Exposure Required** (inverse relationship).
- **ASTM E1815 Classes:** Class I (slowest, finest detail) → Class V (fastest, coarsest detail).
- **Class III** is the **general-purpose benchmark** film in industrial radiography.
- **Reciprocity Law (Bunsen–Roscoe):** $E = I \times t$ — holds for **normal exposure times**.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $I \propto 1/d^2$ — governs how intensity drops with source-to-film distance.
- **Combined Exposure Equation:** $E = (k \cdot mA \cdot t) / d^2$ — the workhorse of radiographic exposure calculation.
- **Schwarzschild Law (RLF):** $E = I \cdot t^p$ where $p \approx 0.8$–$1.0$; apply this for **long or very short exposures**.
- **Time Compensation Formula:** $t_2 = t_1 \cdot (S_1/S_2) \cdot (d_2/d_1)^2$.
- **H&D Curve Regions:** Base + Fog → Toe → Straight Line (diagnostic) → Shoulder → Solarization.
- **Average Gradient (G):** Slope of straight-line portion = measure of **film contrast**.
- **Latitude:** Width of useful exposure range — **wider for fast films, narrower for slow films**.
- **Trade-off Rule of Thumb:** Speed ↑ → Resolution ↓ (and vice-versa) — this is the **central engineering compromise** in film selection.
- **Industrial Application Mapping:** Aerospace/Nuclear → Slow film; Fabrication shops → Medium film; Thick-section/Pipeline → Fast film.
- **Optical Density Formula:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I_t)$ — remember this for densitometer-related problems.
- **ASTM Standards to Cite in Answers:** **E1815** (film classification), **E142** (image quality), **ISO 11699-1** (international equivalent).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Qualities of Film Screens Used in Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Qualities of Film Screens Used in Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Industrial Radiography**, a **radiographic film** is a precision photographic recording medium that captures, stores, and reproduces a latent image of the internal structure of an object exposed to penetrating ionizing radiation (X-rays or gamma rays). The film operates on the principle of **photolysis of silver halide crystals** suspended in a gelatin emulsion, where incident radiation reduces silver ions (Ag⁺) to metallic silver (Ag⁰), forming the latent image which is subsequently developed into a visible radiograph.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A radiographic film is a light-tight, flexible sheet consisting of a transparent polyester base coated on both sides with a radiation-sensitive silver halide emulsion, designed to permanently record the differential attenuation of X-ray or gamma radiation passing through the test object."

A **film screen** (specifically a **metal intensifying screen**) is a thin metallic foil—almost always **lead (Pb)**—placed in direct intimate contact with one or both surfaces of the film during exposure. These screens serve two principal functions:
1. To enhance the photographic effect of penetrating radiation through **secondary electron emission** and **fluorescent action**.
2. To reduce exposure time and improve radiographic contrast.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to classify film speeds (ASTM system), interpret the **H & D characteristic curve**, identify the qualities of a good radiographic film (speed, contrast, graininess, resolution, latitude, density, fog), and explain the role of intensifying screens in industrial radiography.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are standing behind a frosted glass window at night with someone shining a flashlight on the other side. The amount of light that passes through depends on what's between the light and the glass. Now, replace the frosted glass with **special photographic paper** (the **film**), the flashlight with an **X-ray machine** (the **source**), and the object in between as a **welded joint with hidden cracks**. The film "remembers" how much radiation reached each point—just like the paper darkens where light hits it. The **intensifying screen** is like putting a piece of **aluminum foil behind the paper** that amplifies the X-rays, so you need less exposure time. Just as a camera lens focuses light, the film's emulsion captures a permanent "shadow picture" of the internal flaws of the object.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board-Important)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **Optical Density (D)** | Dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 4.0** in industrial films |
| **Film Base Thickness** | **0.18 mm** polyester (polyethylene terephthalate) |
| **Emulsion Thickness** | **10–20 µm** per side |
| **Silver Halide Composition** | **AgBr** (95–98 %) + small percentage of **AgI** |
| **ASTM Film Speed Classes** | **Class 1, 2, 3, 4** (slowest to fastest) |
| **Standard Developing Temperature** | **20 °C – 24 °C** |
| **Standard Developing Time** | **5 – 8 minutes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture of a Radiographic Film

A modern industrial X-ray film is a **sandwich structure** consisting of **seven distinct layers**, applied symmetrically on both sides of a transparent base:

1. **Protective Overcoat (Top Coat):** A thin, hard gelatin layer (~1 µm) that protects the emulsion from physical abrasion, pressure marks, and chemical contamination during handling.
2. **Emulsion Layer (Upper):** Contains photosensitive **silver halide microcrystals** (typically AgBr with traces of AgI, size 0.1–3 µm) uniformly dispersed in a gelatin matrix. Crystals are ripened to grow to optimal size for the desired film speed.
3. **Subbing (Adhesion) Layer:** A thin sub-coat of gelatin-chrome alum that firmly bonds the emulsion to the base, preventing separation during processing.
4. **Film Base:** A **0.18 mm thick, dimensionally stable polyester (PET)** sheet that provides mechanical strength, flexibility, and optical transparency.
5. **Subbing Layer (Lower):** Mirror of the upper subbing layer.
6. **Emulsion Layer (Lower):** Symmetrical coating for double-sided sensitivity (improves speed and reduces exposure time).
7. **Protective Overcoat (Lower):** Final abrasion-resistant layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Double emulsion** is standard in industrial films because the cumulative effect nearly **doubles the speed** and improves signal-to-noise ratio, with no significant loss of resolution since X-ray images lack fine optical detail.

### 2.2 Qualities / Characteristics of a Good Radiographic Film

The performance of a radiographic film is evaluated using seven **interlinked qualities** that KTU examiners frequently test:

**A. Film Speed (Sensitivity)**
- **Definition:** The reciprocal of the exposure required to produce a specified density (typically D = 1.0) above base + fog.
- **High speed** = lower exposure required = **larger silver halide crystals** = coarser grain.
- Industrial films: speeds range from ASTM Class 1 (very slow) to Class 4 (very fast).

**B. Film Contrast (Gamma, γ)**
- **Definition:** The slope of the **straight-line portion** of the H & D (Hurter & Driffield) characteristic curve.
- **High contrast** = steep slope = short tonal range = better flaw detectability for **thickness variations** typical in welds.
- **Low contrast** = gradual slope = wide latitude = better for complex geometry or large thickness ranges.

**C. Graininess**
- **Definition:** The visual, non-uniform "salt-and-pepper" appearance of the developed image due to the random distribution and size of developed silver grains.
- Function of crystal size: larger grains = faster film but more graininess.
- Quantified using the **RMS (Root Mean Square)** microdensitometer method.

**D. Resolution (Definition)**
- **Definition:** The ability of the film to render fine detail distinctly, measured in **line pairs per millimetre (lp/mm)**.
- Class 1 film (fine grain): resolution ≈ **100 lp/mm**.
- Class 4 film (coarse grain): resolution ≈ **30–40 lp/mm**.

**E. Optical Density (D)**
- **Definition:** A logarithmic measure of film blackening.
- Typical acceptable radiographic density range: **2.0 – 4.0** (for steel/castings).

**F. Latitude**
- **Definition:** The total range of exposure (log E) over which the film produces **acceptable, useful density** (typically 0.5 to 2.0 above fog).
- Wide latitude = forgiving to exposure errors but lower contrast.
- Narrow latitude = demanding but superior flaw visibility.

**G. Fog (Base + Fog)**
- **Definition:** Unwanted density produced by causes other than the intended exposure—chemical fog from over-development, age fog, light leaks, or radiation fog.
- **Acceptable limit:** B + F should not exceed **0.30** for industrial radiography.

### 2.3 The H & D Characteristic Curve (Density vs Log Exposure)

The H & D curve is the **fingerprint** of a radiographic film and is the single most important graph in film-based NDT.

**Description of the curve regions:**
- **Region 1 – Base + Fog (D₀):** Density when no exposure is given; should be ≤ 0.30.
- **Region 2 – Toe (Under-exposure):** Curved section where density slowly rises; represents threshold sensitivity.
- **Region 3 – Straight Line (Useful region):** Linear relationship between D and log E; slope = gamma (γ).
- **Region 4 – Shoulder (Over-exposure):** Density approaches saturation.
- **Region 5 – Solarization (Reversal):** Density actually decreases with further exposure (rarely used in NDT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The slope of the straight-line portion defines contrast. A tangent drawn at any point on the curve gives the **local gradient**; the **average gradient** between two specified densities is what ASTM uses to classify film speed.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical Equations)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical Density | $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = -\log_{10}(T)$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Transmittance | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | $0 \le T \le 1$ |
| 3 | Opacity | $O = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{I_0}{I}$ | $O = 10^{D}$ |
| 4 | Gamma (Slope of H&D curve) | $\gamma = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10}(E_2) - \log_{10}(E_1)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Average Gradient | $\bar{\gamma} = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10}(E_2/E_1)}$ | ASTM-defined |
| 6 | Exposure (Beer–Lambert form) | $E = E_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | $E$ in Roentgen (R) or C/kg |
| 7 | Speed Number (relative) | $S = \dfrac{1}{E \,(\text{for } D = 1.0)}$ | Reciprocal of exposure |
| 8 | RMS Granularity | $G_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2}{n}}$ | × 1000 for standard reporting |
| 9 | Resolution (limit) | $R = \dfrac{1}{d_{min}}$ | line pairs / mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value note:** For modulus / magnitude expressions in KTU answer sheets, always write $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX form (using `\vert`) and **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In aerospace weld inspection, **Class 1 or Class 2 films** are mandatory because the **acceptance criteria** (per ASME Section V, ASTM E192) require detection of wire-sized (0.2 mm) cracks. The high contrast and fine grain of these films provide the **modulation transfer function (MTF)** needed to resolve such flaws. In contrast, the in-service inspection of thick nuclear reactor pressure vessels uses **Class 3 or 4 films** with **fluorescent screens** because exposure times must be minimized to limit worker dose, and the lower inherent resolution is acceptable for the **larger flaws (≥ 2 % of wall thickness)** being sought.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optical Density from Transmittance

**Given:** A beam of light of intensity $I_0$ falls on a developed radiographic film; the transmitted intensity is $I$.

**Step 1 — Define Transmittance.**
Transmittance is the fraction of incident light passing through the film:
$$T = \frac{I}{I_0}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Logarithmic Definition of Density.**
By international photographic convention, density is defined as the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of incident to transmitted intensity:
$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite Using Transmittance.**
Since $\dfrac{I_0}{I} = \dfrac{1}{T}$, we can substitute:
$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = -\log_{10}(T)$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Opacity.**
Opacity is defined as $O = 1/T$, therefore:
$$D = \log_{10}(O) \quad \text{or} \quad O = 10^{D}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**
If a film transmits only $1\,\%$ of incident light, $T = 0.01$:
$$D = -\log_{10}(0.01) = -(-2) = 2.0$$

This corresponds to a **moderate darkening**, ideal for general radiography.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gamma (Slope) of the Characteristic Curve

**Given:** Two points on the straight-line portion of the H & D curve, with corresponding densities $D_1, D_2$ and exposures $E_1, E_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Independent Variable.**
Because the H & D curve plots $D$ versus $\log_{10}(E)$, the abscissa is the logarithm of exposure.

**Step 2 — Compute the Slope.**
The slope (gradient) $\gamma$ of a straight line is rise over run:
$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta(\log_{10} E)} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10}(E_2) - \log_{10}(E_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Use Logarithm Property.**
Using $\log_{10}(E_2) - \log_{10}(E_1) = \log_{10}(E_2/E_1)$:
$$\gamma = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10}(E_2/E_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to a Numerical Case.**
Suppose $D_1 = 1.0$ at $E_1 = 1\,\text{R}$ and $D_2 = 2.5$ at $E_2 = 10\,\text{R}$:
$$\gamma = \frac{2.5 - 1.0}{\log_{10}(10/1)} = \frac{1.5}{1.0} = 1.5$$

This is a typical gamma for a **Class 1 industrial film**.

### 3.3 Derivation: ASTM Film Speed Classification

**Given:** The ASTM E1815 standard defines film speed by the exposure $E_s$ required to produce a density of $2.0$ above base + fog.

**Step 1 — Establish the Reference.**
For ASTM Class 1 films, the reference speed is established at $E_s = 0.08\,\text{R}$ (for direct X-ray exposure at 200 kV).

**Step 2 — Define Relative Speed.**
$$S_{rel} = \frac{E_{s,\,Class\,1}}{E_{s,\,Class\,n}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Comparison.**
If Class 3 needs $0.02\,\text{R}$ for the same density:
$$S_{rel} = \frac{0.08}{0.02} = 4.0$$

So the **Class 3 film is 4× faster** than the Class 1 film at the expense of coarser grain and lower resolution.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Between Film Qualities

| Film Quality | Speed ↑ | Contrast ↑ | Graininess ↑ | Resolution ↑ | Latitude ↑ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | — | Inversely related | Increases | Decreases | Often widens |
| **Contrast (γ)** | Inversely related | — | Independent | Independent | Inversely related |
| **Graininess** | Increases | Independent | — | Inversely related | Independent |
| **Resolution** | Decreases | Independent | Inversely related | — | Independent |
| **Latitude** | Often widens | Inversely related | Independent | Independent | — |
| **Density (for fixed E)** | Increases | Higher γ → higher D | Independent | Independent | Independent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic (GSSRLD):** "**Grain-Speed-Sacrifice-Resolution, Latitude-Declines**." A faster film has larger grains, which reduces resolution, while a high-contrast film has a narrow exposure latitude.

### 3.5 Derivation: Exposure Through an Object (Beer–Lambert)

**Given:** A monoenergetic X-ray beam of incident intensity $I_0$ passes through a material of thickness $x$ and linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Differential Form.**
The intensity lost per unit thickness is proportional to the intensity:
$$-\frac{dI}{dx} = \mu I$$

**Step 2 — Separate and Integrate.**
$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Integral.**
$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 4 — Express Exposure.**
Since exposure $E$ is proportional to intensity for a given time:
$$E = E_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** of radiographic contrast generation: a flaw of thickness $\Delta x$ produces an exposure difference $\Delta E = E_0 \mu \, e^{-\mu x} \Delta x$, which the film converts to a visible density difference.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Layered Structure of an Industrial Radiographic Film

```mermaid
graph TD
    layer1[Layer 1: Protective Overcoat - Gelatin] --> layer2[Layer 2: Emulsion - Silver Halide in Gelatin]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3: Subbing Layer - Adhesion Promoter]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4: Film Base - Polyester PET 0.18 mm]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5: Subbing Layer - Lower Adhesion]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6: Emulsion - Lower Silver Halide]
    layer6 --> layer7[Layer 7: Protective Overcoat - Lower Gelatin]

    subgraph EXPOSURE_DIRECTION
        dirA[Incident X-ray or Gamma Beam] --> layer1
    end

    note1[Note: Double emulsion nearly doubles speed. Emulsion thickness 10-20 micrometres per side. Crystals are AgBr with traces of AgI. Grain size 0.1-3 micrometres.]
    layer4 -.- note1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The H and D Characteristic Curve Regions

```mermaid
graph LR
    regA[Region A: Base plus Fog - D0 below 0.30] --> regB[Region B: Toe - Curved onset of image]
    regB --> regC[Region C: Straight Line - Useful radiographic range]
    regC --> regD[Region D: Shoulder - Saturation of silver halide]
    regD --> regE[Region E: Solarization - Density reversal rare in NDT]

    axisX[Horizontal axis: log base 10 of Exposure E in Roentgen] -.- regC
    axisY[Vertical axis: Optical Density D from 0 to 4] -.- regC

    slopeNote[Slope of Region C equals Gamma. Higher gamma means higher contrast. Lower gamma means wider latitude.]
    regC -.- slopeNote
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — ASTM Film Speed Classification Trade-off Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    clsA[ASTM Class 1: Very Slow - Direct exposure only] --> clsAdesc[100 lp/mm resolution. Fine grain. Use thin light alloy welds aerospace]
    clsB[ASTM Class 2: Medium Speed - Direct or Lead screens] --> clsBdesc[80 lp/mm resolution. General purpose welds castings]
    clsC[ASTM Class 3: Fast - Lead screens preferred] --> clsCdesc[40 lp/mm resolution. Thick sections low activity sources]
    clsD[ASTM Class 4: Very Fast - Fluorescent screens] --> clsDdesc[30 lp/mm resolution. Very thick steel high speed pipeline]

    clsA --> clsB
    clsB --> clsC
    clsC --> clsD

    speedNote[Speed increases left to right. Resolution decreases left to right. Graininess increases left to right.]
    clsAdesc -.- speedNote
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Film Processing Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1: Loading cassette in darkroom safe light] --> step2[Step 2: Exposure to X-ray or Gamma]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Develop in developer solution 20-24 deg C for 5-8 minutes]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Rinse in stop bath acetic acid 30 seconds]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Fix in fixer solution 5-10 minutes]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Wash in running water 15-30 minutes]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Dry in dust free cabinet 30-45 minutes]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Interpret on viewing illuminator with D 2.0 to 4.0]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Functional Architecture of Intensifying Screen Action

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[X-ray or Gamma Source] --> obj[Test Object with Flaw]
    obj --> primary[Primary Radiation Reaches Screen]
    obj --> filmA[Direct Film Exposure - Lower energy photons]

    primary --> leadScr[Lead Intensifying Screen 0.02 to 0.15 mm]
    leadScr --> sec1[Secondary Electrons e-]
    leadScr --> sec2[Characteristic Lead X-rays]
    leadScr --> sec3[Low Energy Scatter]

    sec1 --> filmA
    sec2 --> filmA
    sec3 --> filmA
    filmA --> dev[Developed Latent Image in Film Emulsion]

    benefitNote[Benefit: Exposure time reduced by factor 2 to 16. Contrast improved at low kV. Disadvantage: Slight loss of sharpness due to electron scatter.]
    dev -.- benefitNote
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**
Define the term **optical density** of a radiographic film. Mention the instrument used to measure it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Optical density $D$ is the logarithmic measure of the blackening of a developed radiographic film, defined as $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$, where $I_0$ is the intensity of light incident on the film and $I$ is the intensity transmitted through it. A perfectly transparent film has $D = 0$; an opaque (fully black) film has $D \to \infty$.
- **Instrument (1 mark):** A **photoelectric densitometer** (also called a transmission densitometer) is used to measure optical density by comparing the light transmitted through the film to that through an unexposed reference area.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**
List **any six qualities** that determine the suitability of a radiographic film for NDT applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six essential qualities are:
1. **Speed (Sensitivity)** – ability to produce a usable image at low exposure.
2. **Contrast (Gamma)** – rate of change of density with log exposure.
3. **Graininess** – visual texture from developed silver grains.
4. **Resolution / Definition** – ability to record fine detail, in lp/mm.
5. **Latitude** – range of exposure producing acceptable density.
6. **Fog (Base + Fog)** – unwanted density, must be ≤ 0.30.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part A:** Students often list only 4–5 qualities and lose a mark. Memorize the mnemonic **"S-C-G-R-L-F"** (Speed-Contrast-Graininess-Resolution-Latitude-Fog). Always write the units: lp/mm for resolution, Roentgen for exposure, dimensionless for density and gamma.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE format):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) With a neat diagram, describe the construction of a double-emulsion industrial radiographic film. Explain the function of each layer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Protective Overcoat (top) | Hardened gelatin, ~1 µm | Resists abrasion, pressure marks, and handling damage during loading/unloading. |
| 2. Emulsion (top) | AgBr (95–98 %) + AgI microcrystals in gelatin, 10–20 µm | Site of **latent image** formation when radiation causes Ag⁺ → Ag⁰. |
| 3. Subbing Layer (top) | Gelatin + chrome alum | Provides firm adhesion between emulsion and base; prevents peeling during processing. |
| 4. Film Base | Polyester (PET), 0.18 mm | Provides mechanical strength, dimensional stability, flexibility, and optical clarity. |
| 5. Subbing Layer (bottom) | Same as Layer 3 | Mirrors upper subbing layer for symmetrical construction. |
| 6. Emulsion (bottom) | Same as Layer 2 | Doubles sensitivity, increases film speed, reduces exposure time. |
| 7. Protective Overcoat (bottom) | Same as Layer 1 | Bottom-side abrasion protection. |

**Diagram:** Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 1** in Section 4.1 above.

**Key engineering points (for full marks):**
- The double emulsion nearly **doubles the film speed** because radiation from either side can create a developable image.
- The **gelatin matrix** is not merely a binder—it also reacts with by-products of development to control contrast and grain growth.
- **Polyester base** replaced older cellulose triacetate base in modern films due to superior dimensional stability and tear strength.
- Total film thickness is typically **0.45 – 0.50 mm** including all layers.

**(b) Explain the H & D characteristic curve of a radiographic film. Discuss its significance in selecting a film for industrial radiography. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The H & D Curve:**
- The H & D (Hurter & Driffield) curve plots **optical density (D)** on the Y-axis against the **logarithm of exposure (log₁₀ E)** on the X-axis.
- It has **five distinct regions**:
  1. **Base + Fog region** — minimum density $D_0 \le 0.30$ even at zero exposure.
  2. **Toe** — slow initial rise in density; represents the threshold sensitivity of the emulsion.
  3. **Straight line (useful region)** — linear relationship: $D = \gamma \log_{10}(E) + C$.
  4. **Shoulder** — density approaches saturation as more silver halide is exhausted.
  5. **Solarization** — density decreases with further exposure; not used in NDT.

**Significance in Industrial Radiography:**
- **Film Speed** is read directly from the curve: faster films lie to the **left** (less exposure required).
- **Contrast (γ)** is the slope of the straight-line portion: $γ = \Delta D / \Delta(\log_{10} E)$.
- **Latitude** is the horizontal width of the useful region: wider curve = wider exposure tolerance.
- For **weld inspection** of thin sections → high γ (Class 1).
- For **variable thickness castings** → moderate γ with wide latitude (Class 2).
- The curve also reveals the **exposure limits** above which solarization (loss of information) occurs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]

**(a) Classify the different types of intensifying screens used in industrial radiography. Explain the action of lead screens. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Intensifying Screens:**

| Type | Material | Action | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead Screens (Metal)** | Pure lead foil, 0.02–0.15 mm thick | Emit **secondary electrons** and characteristic X-rays when struck by primary radiation. | Universal; mandatory for gamma radiography. |
| **Fluorescent Screens** | Calcium tungstate (CaWO₄) or rare-earth phosphors (Gd₂O₂S, LaOBr) coated on cardboard | Convert X-ray energy to **visible light** which then exposes the film. | Very fast exposures; medical radiography. |
| **Salt Screens** | Salt-impregnated paper | Old technology, rarely used today. | Historical; replaced by lead and fluorescent screens. |
| **No Screen (Direct)** | Film alone | Film responds only to primary radiation. | Highest resolution, slow speed, thin sections only. |

**Action of Lead Screens:**
1. **Primary radiation** strikes the lead foil after passing through the object.
2. The lead atoms absorb photons and emit **photoelectrons** and **Compton recoil electrons**.
3. These **secondary electrons** have a much stronger photographic effect on the silver halide emulsion than the original X-ray photon (because electrons have higher mass stopping power in gelatin).
4. Additionally, lead emits **characteristic K-shell X-rays** (Pb Kα ≈ 75 keV, Pb Kβ ≈ 84 keV) which can also expose the film.
5. The result is a **2 to 16× reduction in exposure time**, and improved **subject contrast** at low kV settings.

**Front vs Back Lead Screens:**
- **Front screen** (toward source) absorbs some low-energy scatter before it reaches the film, reducing fog.
- **Back screen** (away from source) is the main intensifier; usually thicker (e.g., 0.10 mm back, 0.05 mm front).

**(b) Discuss the qualities of a good radiographic film in detail. State the ASTM film speed classification. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Seven Qualities (with engineering relevance):**

1. **Speed (Sensitivity):** Defined as the reciprocal of exposure required to produce a density of 1.0 above fog. A good film should have sufficient speed to keep exposure times reasonable, but not so fast that graininess becomes excessive. Industrial films span 4 ASTM speed classes (see below).

2. **Contrast (Gamma, γ):** The slope of the straight-line portion of the H & D curve. **High contrast** is required for detecting small thickness variations (e.g., cracks, lack of penetration in welds); **low contrast** is preferred for components with large thickness variations (e.g., castings).

3. **Graininess:** A function of silver halide crystal size. Acceptable if the RMS granularity value is below the threshold specified in ASTM E1815. Fine grain is required for high-resolution work.

4. **Resolution:** Measured in line pairs per millimetre (lp/mm). A high-quality industrial film should resolve at least **70 lp/mm** (Class 2) and up to **100 lp/mm** (Class 1).

5. **Density:** The developed film should reach a density of **2.0 to 4.0** in areas of interest, measured with a calibrated densitometer. Density below 2.0 is too "thin"; above 4.0 is too "dense" for accurate viewing.

6. **Latitude:** The exposure range over which density remains in the useful 0.5 to 2.0 range. Wide latitude simplifies radiography of complex geometry; narrow latitude gives superior contrast.

7. **Fog (Base + Fog):** Should not exceed **0.30** for any industrial film. Sources of fog include:
   - Chemical fog (over-development)
   - Age fog (expired film)
   - Light leaks (damaged cassette)
   - Stray radiation (poor shielding)
   - Thermal fog (storage at high temperature)

**ASTM E1815 Film Speed Classification:**

| ASTM Class | Relative Speed | Typical Screen | Grain | Resolution | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class 1** | Slowest (1×) | None (direct) | Very fine | 100 lp/mm | Thin light alloy welds, aerospace |
| **Class 2** | Medium (2×) | Optional lead | Fine | 80 lp/mm | General welds, castings |
| **Class 3** | Fast (4×) | Lead required | Medium | 40 lp/mm | Thick steel, Ir-192 |
| **Class 4** | Fastest (8×) | Fluorescent | Coarse | 30 lp/mm | Very thick steel, Co-60 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Common Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing speed with sensitivity** in units: students write "speed = Roentgen" but speed is dimensionless (relative).
> 2. **Mixing up H & D curve axes:** density is on Y-axis, log exposure on X-axis. Drawing it reversed is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the formula** $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ and writing the wrong base (some use natural log).
> 4. **Not specifying front vs back lead screens** in the answer — only mentioning "lead screens" is incomplete; lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No diagram or rough sketch** in construction questions — KTU awards a separate "diagram mark" worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Misspelling "Hurter and Driffield"** or writing "H and D curve" without explanation.
> 7. **Mixing up ASTM Class numbers** (e.g., saying Class 1 is fastest — it is actually the slowest).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Qualities of Film Screens: Last-Minute Revision Bullets**

- **Radiographic film = Polyester base + double AgBr emulsion + protective overcoat.** Total thickness ≈ 0.45 mm.
- **Optical density:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$; typical useful range is **2.0 to 4.0**; measured with a **densitometer**.
- **Gamma (γ):** slope of H & D straight line. Higher γ → higher contrast, lower exposure tolerance.
- **Latitude:** width of useful exposure range on the H & D curve. Wider latitude = more forgiving but lower contrast.
- **Fog:** unwanted density ≤ **0.30** is the industrial acceptance limit.
- **Resolution:** Class 1 ≈ **100 lp/mm**, Class 4 ≈ **30 lp/mm**. Inversely related to speed.
- **ASTM Classes:** Class 1 (slowest, finest grain) → Class 4 (fastest, coarsest grain).
- **Lead screens:** secondary electrons + characteristic X-rays reduce exposure time by 2–16×. Back screen is usually thicker than front.
- **Fluorescent screens:** convert X-rays to visible light; very high speed but lower resolution; mostly for medical/very thick steel.
- **Film processing:** Develop (20–24 °C, 5–8 min) → Stop bath → Fix (5–10 min) → Wash (15–30 min) → Dry (30–45 min).
- **Storage:** Cool, dry, away from radiation — 10–21 °C, 30–50 % RH; respect expiry date.
- **Quick mnemonic — Seven Qualities:** **"S-C-G-R-L-D-F"** (Speed, Contrast, Graininess, Resolution, Latitude, Density, Fog).
- **Quick mnemonic — H & D regions:** **"B-T-S-Sh-So"** (Base+fog, Toe, Straight line, Shoulder, Solarization).
- **Key constant:** $1\,\text{R} = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{C/kg}$ in SI units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Examiner's Tip:** For the KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least one **2-mark sub-question** asking you to "**state the H & D curve and identify the gamma**," and at least one **diagram-based question** on film structure or lead screen placement. Always pair the diagram with a **labelled table of functions** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality of a Good Radiograph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiographic Quality** is defined as the combined measure of the ability of a radiographic image to reveal the presence, size, shape, and location of a discontinuity or flaw in the test object, expressed quantitatively through **Radiographic Sensitivity** and qualitatively through **Image Quality** parameters such as **Density**, **Contrast**, **Sharpness**, and **Latitude**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Quality of a Good Radiograph** refers to a set of measurable image characteristics that determine whether a radiograph can be reliably used for **acceptance/rejection** decisions in accordance with codes such as **ASTM E142**, **ASTM E1025**, **ASME Sec V**, and **EN 1435**.

The quality of a radiograph is evaluated using three primary technical benchmarks:

| Benchmark | Definition | Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (D)** | The degree of blackening of the film | **1.5 to 4.0** (typical), **2.0 to 3.5** (preferred) |
| **Sensitivity (S)** | Smallest detectable discontinuity as % of thickness | **1% to 2%** (weld) / **0.5% to 1%** (castings) |
| **Contrast (ΔD)** | Density difference between adjacent regions | Should be visible to the naked eye |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala-Specific KTU 2024 Note:** The KTU 2024 syllabus for PESFT521 emphasizes the parameters that govern the radiographic image and the role of the **Image Quality Indicator (IQI) / Penetrameter** as a yardstick for quality assessment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Photography" Paradigm

Imagine you are taking a photograph of a friend standing behind a glass wall with tiny invisible scratches on it.

- If the **lighting** (X-ray exposure) is wrong — friend is too dark or overexposed → **Bad Density**
- If the **focus** is poor (geometric blur from a wide beam cone) → **Loss of Sharpness**
- If the **camera** cannot resolve small scratches (poor IQI wire) → **Low Sensitivity**
- If the **film** cannot distinguish between the glass and the scratches → **Poor Contrast**

Just as a good photograph must be **sharp, properly exposed, and have visible detail**, a **good radiograph** must have the right **density**, exhibit sufficient **contrast**, show **geometric sharpness**, and possess high enough **sensitivity** to detect the smallest relevant flaw.

> [!TIP]
> A radiograph is not judged by its "beauty" but by its **engineering utility** — can a Level-II interpreter confidently identify a 1 mm defect in a 25 mm weld? That confidence is what *quality* means.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Standards governing radiograph quality:**
> - **ASTM E1025** — Standard practice for hole-type and wire-type IQI design
> - **ASTM E747** — Wire-type IQI specification
> - **EN 1435** — European standard for radiographic testing of welds
> - **ASME Section V, Article 2** — Radiographic examination (used in pressure vessels)
> - **Density measurement:** Densitometer calibrated to **0.00–5.00** D range
> - **Viewing illuminator intensity:** **≥ 1614 lux (150 foot-candles)** for transmission view, with masking for higher densities

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Radiographic Quality

A good radiograph is built on **three mutually independent pillars**. If any one fails, the image fails.

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — Radiographic Density (D)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density** is the logarithmic measure of film blackening, defined as the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of incident light (I₀) to transmitted light (I).

The density governs the overall **lightness/darkness** of the developed film.

**Physical significance:**
- **Low density (< 1.5)** → Film appears pale, lacks differentiation, signals **underexposure**.
- **High density (> 4.0)** → Film appears jet black, signals **overexposure** or **viewing limitations**.
- **Sweet spot (2.0 – 3.5)** → Maximum information content for a human observer on a calibrated viewer.

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Radiographic Contrast (ΔD)

Contrast is the **difference in density** between two adjacent regions of the radiograph — for instance, between a sound region of the weld and a region containing a crack or porosity.

**Types of contrast:**
- **Subject (or Material) Contrast:** Inherent difference in X-ray absorption between the flaw and the parent material. Governed by **atomic number (Z)** difference and **thickness** difference.
- **Film Contrast (γ):** The intrinsic ability of the film-emulsion to translate exposure differences into density differences. Characterized by the **Characteristic Curve (H & D Curve)**.
- **Total Radiographic Contrast:** Combination of subject contrast and film contrast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** A high-energy X-ray beam yields **low subject contrast** (X-rays "see through" everything). A low-energy beam yields **high subject contrast** but lower penetration. The radiographer must balance this trade-off.

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Radiographic Sharpness (Unsharpness, U)

Sharpness is the **clarity of the boundary** between two regions of different densities. It is **lost** when an image appears blurry — this loss is called **unsharpness (U)**.

There are **three types of unsharpness:**

| Type | Cause | Symbol | Effect on Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Unsharpness** | Divergent beam from finite focal spot | U_g | Most significant; increases with object-to-film distance |
| **Film (Intrinsic) Unsharpness** | Movement of electrons in film emulsion | U_f | Constant; property of film type |
| **Screen (Screen) Unsharpness** | Light diffusion in intensifying screens | U_s | Increases with thicker screens |

The **total unsharpness** is given by vector addition:

$$U_t = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_s^2}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table compiles **every** formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 3 — Quality of a Good Radiograph. These are the same equations used by KTU University examiners when setting Part B numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Optical Density** | $D = \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{I_0}{I} \right)$ | Dimensionless | I₀ = incident, I = transmitted light |
| 2 | **Geometric Unsharpness** | $U_g = \dfrac{d \times t}{D - t}$ | mm | d = focal spot, t = object-to-film, D = source-to-film |
| 3 | **Total Unsharpness** | $U_t = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_s^2}$ | mm | Vector (Pythagorean) sum |
| 4 | **Percent Sensitivity (2-1-2 Rule)** | $S \, (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Smallest visible wire dia}}{\text{Specimen thickness}} \times 100$ | % | "2-1-2" = 2T, 1T, 2T wires on IQI |
| 5 | **Percent Sensitivity (Hole IQI)** | $S \, (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Hole diameter (T/4 or T/2)}}{\text{Specimen thickness}} \times 100$ | % | EN 1435: T = base metal thickness |
| 6 | **Contrast Ratio** | $\Delta D = D_{\max} - D_{\min}$ | Dimensionless | Difference in densities |
| 7 | **Subject Contrast** | $C_s = \dfrac{I - I_f}{I}$ | Dimensionless | I = intensity through matrix, I_f = through flaw |
| 8 | **Film Gamma (Slope)** | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta D}{\log(E_2) - \log(E_1)}$ | Dimensionless | Slope of H&D curve in linear region |
| 9 | **Image Quality Indicator (Wire-type)** | $S_w = \dfrac{d_{\text{wire}}}{t_{\text{specimen}}} \times 100$ | % | ASTM E747 |
| 10 | **Source-to-Image Distance (SID)** | $SID = D$ (in geometric unsharpness formula) | mm | Standard ≥ 700 mm for field radiography |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (KTU Board Examiner Insight):** In the sensitivity formula, the **smallest visible wire** on the IQI is taken — NOT the diameter of the IQI itself. The IQI diameter is unrelated to sensitivity; only the **wire diameter** matters. Many KTU students lose 2–3 marks by confusing these.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Radiograph Quality |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blade inspection — sensitivity ≤ 0.5%, contrast crucial |
| **Nuclear** | Pressure vessel welds — ASME Sec III demands 1%–2% sensitivity |
| **Oil & Gas** | Pipeline girth welds — EN 1435 acceptance class B |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull plate welds — IACS UR Z17.1 |
| **Defence** | Ammunition casings — defect detection down to 0.3% thickness |
| **Foundries** | Cast valve bodies — ASTM E192 (reference radiographs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A radiograph that **fails** the IQI sensitivity check is **rejected outright** — irrespective of how well the rest of the parameters look. This is a hard regulatory rule across all major standards.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optical Density from Beer-Lambert Framework

### Step 1: Statement of Need
We must relate the **amount of light passing through the developed film** to the **amount of exposure** the film received during X-ray bombardment.

### Step 2: Define the Transmittance
Let $I_0$ be the intensity of light incident on a developed radiograph and $I$ be the intensity of light transmitted through the film. Define:

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0}$$

where $T$ is the **transmittance** of the film (a fraction between 0 and 1).

### Step 3: Define the Opacity
The reciprocal of transmittance is the **opacity**:

$$\text{Opacity} = \frac{I_0}{I} = \frac{1}{T}$$

### Step 4: Take the Logarithm
Radiographic density is defined as the **logarithm (base 10)** of the opacity:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_0}{I} \right)$$

### Step 5: Logarithm Expansion

$$D = \log_{10}(I_0) - \log_{10}(I)$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example
Suppose a film transmits only 1% of the incident light. Then:

$$D = \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{0.01} \right) = \log_{10}(100) = 2.0$$

A density of **2.0** corresponds to the mid-range preferred viewing density. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometric Unsharpness (Ug)

### Step 1: Geometry Setup
Consider a point source of X-rays of finite focal-spot size $d$ (mm), located at a distance $D$ (mm) from the film. An object of thickness effectively $t$ above the film (object-to-film distance) is being imaged.

The penumbra (fuzzy edge) on the film has width $U_g$.

### Step 2: Similar Triangles
By the principle of **similar triangles**, the geometry of projection forms two similar triangles:

- Triangle 1: focal spot edge to opposite focal spot edge → height $D$, base $d$
- Triangle 2: penumbra width on film → height $t$, base $U_g$

### Step 3: Ratio Equate
Setting the ratios equal (similar triangles theorem):

$$\frac{U_g}{t} = \frac{d}{D - t}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Ug
Cross-multiplying and isolating $U_g$:

$$U_g \cdot (D - t) = d \cdot t$$

$$U_g = \frac{d \times t}{D - t}$$

### Step 5: Practical Limiting Case
If $t \ll D$ (object very close to the film):

$$U_g \approx \frac{d \times t}{D}$$

This is the **limit of best possible sharpness** — a thin object placed **flush against the film** gives a sharp image.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example
Given: focal spot $d = 2$ mm, source-to-film $D = 700$ mm, object-to-film $t = 25$ mm.

$$U_g = \frac{2 \times 25}{700 - 25} = \frac{50}{675} \approx 0.074 \text{ mm}$$

This is well below the 0.2 mm threshold — radiograph will be **sharp**. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percent Radiographic Sensitivity (Wire IQI Method)

### Step 1: Define Sensitivity
Sensitivity $S$ in percentage is defined as the ratio of the **smallest discernible discontinuity** to the **thickness of the material** through which the radiation passed, expressed as a percentage.

### Step 2: Identify the Smallest Visible Wire
On a **wire-type IQI** (ASTM E747), wires of progressively increasing diameter are arranged in order. After exposure, the radiographer scans the IQI image and identifies the **smallest-diameter wire** that is clearly visible on the film.

Let this wire diameter be $d_w$ (mm).

### Step 3: Express the Ratio
The material thickness through which the ray passed is $T$ (mm). The sensitivity is:

$$S = \frac{d_w}{T}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Percentage

$$S (\%) = \frac{d_w}{T} \times 100$$

### Step 5: Apply the "2-1-2 Rule"
For welds, the IQI contains three essential wires whose diameters correspond to:
- **2T** wire (2% of thickness)
- **1T** wire (1% of thickness)
- **2T** wire (2% of thickness)

A **Class 1** weld (per ASME) requires the **2T wire** to be visible.  
A **Class 2** weld (per ASME) requires the **1T wire** to be visible.

### Step 6: Worked Example
For a 20 mm thick weld, the IQI has wires of diameter: 0.40, 0.50, 0.63, 0.80, 1.00, 1.25, 1.60, 2.00 mm.
- 1T = 1% of 20 mm = 0.20 mm
- 2T = 2% of 20 mm = 0.40 mm

The smallest visible wire on the film is 0.50 mm. Then:

$$S = \frac{0.50}{20} \times 100 = 2.5\%$$

This is **poorer** than the 1% target — radiograph **fails** the Class 2 acceptance. ❌

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Unsharpness from Three Independent Sources

### Step 1: Independence Assumption
Geometric, film, and screen unsharpness are statistically **independent** processes, so their variances add.

$$U_t^2 = U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_s^2$$

### Step 2: Solving for Ug Given a Target Total Unsharpness
The maximum acceptable total unsharpness is **0.020 inch (~ 0.51 mm)** per ASME Sec V, Article 2 (for Class 1 and 2 welds). Given a film with $U_f = 0.10$ mm and screens with $U_s = 0.15$ mm:

$$U_g = \sqrt{U_t^2 - U_f^2 - U_s^2} = \sqrt{0.51^2 - 0.10^2 - 0.15^2}$$

$$U_g = \sqrt{0.2601 - 0.01 - 0.0225} = \sqrt{0.2276} \approx 0.477 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Use the Geometric Unsharpness Formula in Reverse
Given $U_g = 0.477$ mm, $d = 2$ mm, $D = 1000$ mm, solve for maximum $t$:

$$t = \frac{U_g \cdot D}{d + U_g} = \frac{0.477 \times 1000}{2 + 0.477} = \frac{477}{2.477} \approx 192.6 \text{ mm}$$

The object must be **within 192 mm of the film** to maintain sharpness. ✅

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does a Radiograph "Pass"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Insight:** The examiner expects students to *integrate* all three pillars (density, contrast, sensitivity) into a single decision. The matrix below is a working template.

| Check # | Test Performed | Acceptance Criterion | Action if Failed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density of sound weld region | $1.5 \le D \le 4.0$ | Re-expose with corrected kV/mA·min |
| 2 | IQI sensitivity (2T wire visible) | 2-1-2 rule satisfied per code | Re-expose with higher quality film or different kV |
| 3 | Geometric unsharpness | $U_g \le 0.51$ mm (ASME) | Reduce $t$ or increase $D$ |
| 4 | Subject contrast visible | $\Delta D \ge 0.10$ | Use lower kV (if penetration allows) |
| 5 | Film artifacts | None (no scratches, fingerprints) | Discard film, re-shoot |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define radiographic sensitivity. Mention its significance. (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Radiographic sensitivity is defined as the ability of a radiograph to reveal the **smallest possible discontinuity** as a function of the **total material thickness** through which radiation passed. Mathematically:

$$S \, (\%) = \frac{d_w}{T} \times 100$$

where $d_w$ is the smallest visible wire diameter on the IQI and $T$ is the specimen thickness. A sensitivity of **1% – 2%** is typically required for weld inspection. **Significance:** Sensitivity quantifies the **minimum flaw size** a radiograph can resolve. A higher sensitivity (smaller percentage) means the radiograph can detect smaller defects — which is critical for safety-critical components like pressure vessels and aerospace parts. Sensitivity is measured using **Image Quality Indicators (IQI)** such as wire-type (ASTM E747) or hole-type (ASTM E1025) penetrameters.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between geometric unsharpness and film unsharpness? (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$) | Film Unsharpness ($U_f$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Finite size of the X-ray focal spot | Movement of electrons within film emulsion |
| **Control** | Reduce focal spot $d$, increase SID $D$, reduce $t$ | Choose finer-grain film (slower speed) |
| **Formula** | $U_g = \dfrac{d \times t}{D - t}$ | Tabulated by film manufacturer (~ 0.05–0.20 mm) |
| **Variability** | Can be controlled by the radiographer | A fixed property of the film type |

Geometric unsharpness is the **dominant** factor in field radiography and is the only one the operator can directly control by adjusting source-to-film distance and object-to-film distance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `Choice 1 — Full-Length Comprehensive Question`

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question for ESE]**

#### **Part (a): Discuss in detail the various factors affecting the quality of a good radiograph. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The quality of a good radiograph is governed by the **three pillars** discussed in Section 2.1, supported by **secondary factors** enumerated below.

**1. Radiographic Density (D):**
Density depends on **exposure** ($E = kV \times mA \times time$), **film speed**, **processing conditions** (developer temperature, time, chemistry concentration), and **spectral quality** of the X-ray beam. For weld radiography, density in the **weld region** should be between **2.0 and 3.5**, while density in the **parent metal** should be between **1.5 and 4.0**. Below 1.5, the radiograph is too pale; above 4.0, the viewer cannot resolve fine detail even with a high-intensity illuminator.

**2. Radiographic Contrast (ΔD):**
Contrast depends on **subject contrast** (inherent absorption difference) and **film contrast** (slope of H&D curve). Subject contrast is enhanced by:
- **Lower kV** (X-rays become more discriminating — but at the cost of penetration)
- **Higher atomic number (Z) difference** between flaw and matrix (e.g., a tungsten inclusion in steel is much easier to detect than a slag inclusion in steel)
- **Thicker specimens** (more absorption differential available)

Film contrast is enhanced by using **high-contrast (slow-speed, fine-grain) films** such as ASTM Class I or II.

**3. Radiographic Sharpness (Unsharpness, U):**
Sharpness is governed by **three sources of unsharpness**:
- **Geometric ($U_g$):** $U_g = d \cdot t / (D - t)$. Reduced by smaller focal spot, greater SID, and object placed close to film.
- **Film ($U_f$):** Property of the emulsion; reduced by finer-grain films.
- **Screen ($U_s$):** Reduced by using **thin lead-foil screens** or no screens at all.

**4. Radiographic Sensitivity (IQI):**
The Image Quality Indicator must be visible at the required sensitivity level. Sensitivity depends on:
- **Subject contrast** — higher contrast reveals thinner wires.
- **Geometric sharpness** — sharper images reveal smaller wires.
- **Density** — too low or too high density masks small IQI features.
- **Film grain** — finer grain reveals smaller wires.

**5. Secondary Factors:**
- **Scatter radiation** — controlled by **lead diaphragms**, **filters**, and **backing plates**.
- **Geometric distortion** — caused by **angled rays**; minimized by **alignment** of source, object, and film.
- **Artifacts** — caused by **film handling**, **processing errors**, **double exposure**; controlled by procedure and discipline.

---

#### **Part (b): Explain in detail the construction and application of wire-type and hole-type Image Quality Indicators (IQI) as per ASTM standards. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Wire-Type IQI (ASTM E747):**
- **Construction:** A set of **parallel wires** (typically 7 to 12 wires) of progressively increasing diameter mounted on a **transparent plastic or rubber strip**. The wire diameters follow the geometric progression: $0.20, 0.25, 0.32, 0.40, 0.50, 0.63, 0.80, 1.00, 1.25, 1.60, 2.00, 2.50$ mm, etc.
- **Essential wires:** Three wires — **2T, 1T, 2T** — are mandatory. T is the thickness in inches (or mm, depending on the standard).
- **Placement:** The IQI is placed on the **source side** of the specimen (preferred per ASME) with the wires running **perpendicular** to the weld axis.
- **Identification on radiograph:** The radiographer reads the IQI from the side containing the **2T wires**, identifying the smallest visible wire.

**Hole-Type IQI / Penetrameter (ASTM E1025):**
- **Construction:** A thin rectangular plate (of the same material as the test object) containing **three holes** of diameters **1T, 2T, 4T** (in terms of plate thickness). The plate thickness is typically **1%, 2%, or 4%** of the specimen thickness.
- **Placement:** On the **source side** of the specimen. The 1T hole is the smallest and most critical.
- **Sensitivity reading:** The smallest visible hole determines sensitivity.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Wire-Type (E747) | Hole-Type (E1025) |
|---|---|---|
| Discriminator | Diameter of smallest visible wire | Diameter of smallest visible hole |
| Minimum visible feature | Often smaller (better sensitivity) | Generally larger (poorer sensitivity) |
| Standard for welds | ASTM E747, EN 1435 W1, W6, W10 | EN 1435 W1, W6, W10 |
| Identification markers | Lead "F" markers | Thickness stamped on plate |
| Preferred in | Aerospace, nuclear | Pressure vessels, pipelines |

**Why both exist:** Different industries have inherited different standards. The hole-type was historically simpler to manufacture; the wire-type provides finer granularity and is the **modern preferred choice** in ASME Sec V.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `Choice 2 — Alternative Comprehensive Question`

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Model]**

#### **Part (a): Derive the formula for geometric unsharpness and explain how it influences the quality of a radiograph. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** *(Already provided in Section 3.2)*

Recap of key result:

$$U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t}$$

where:
- $d$ = focal spot size (mm)
- $t$ = object-to-film distance (mm)
- $D$ = source-to-film distance (SID, mm)

**Effect on Quality:**

Geometric unsharpness is the **single most important controllable factor** affecting the sharpness (and hence sensitivity) of a radiograph. As $U_g$ increases, the **edges of discontinuities become blurred**, and the smallest visible IQI wire increases in diameter — directly **degrading sensitivity**.

**Numerical Illustration:**

| Case | $d$ (mm) | $t$ (mm) | $D$ (mm) | $U_g$ (mm) | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 25 | 700 | 0.074 | Sharp ✅ |
| B | 4 | 25 | 700 | 0.148 | Acceptable ✅ |
| C | 4 | 100 | 700 | 0.667 | Unacceptable ❌ |
| D | 2 | 25 | 1500 | 0.034 | Excellent ✅ |

**Practical Implication:** To reduce $U_g$:
- Use a **smaller focal spot** X-ray tube (e.g., 1.5 mm vs. 4 mm).
- Increase the **source-to-film distance (SID)** by moving the tube further back.
- Bring the **object closer to the film** (use of positioning jigs and fixtures).

> **ASME Limit:** $U_g \le 0.020$ inch (0.51 mm) for Class 1 and 2 welds.

---

#### **Part (b): A 25 mm thick steel weld is radiographed using an X-ray source of focal spot 3 mm. The source-to-film distance is 1000 mm. The object is 50 mm from the film. Calculate (i) the geometric unsharpness, (ii) whether the radiograph is acceptable as per ASME Sec V. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Specimen thickness $T = 25$ mm
- Focal spot size $d = 3$ mm
- Source-to-film distance $D = 1000$ mm
- Object-to-film distance $t = 50$ mm

**Part (i): Geometric Unsharpness Calculation**

$$U_g = \frac{d \times t}{D - t}$$

Substituting:

$$U_g = \frac{3 \times 50}{1000 - 50} = \frac{150}{950} = 0.1579 \text{ mm}$$

**Converted to inches:**

$$U_g = \frac{0.1579}{25.4} \approx 0.00622 \text{ inch}$$

**Part (ii): ASME Sec V Acceptance Check**

**ASME Limit for Class 1 & 2 welds:** $U_g \le 0.020$ inch (~ 0.51 mm)

**Comparison:**

$$0.00622 \text{ inch} < 0.020 \text{ inch} \quad \checkmark$$

**Verdict: ACCEPTABLE** ✅

**Sensitivity Check (additional):**
For a 25 mm weld, the IQI set (per ASTM E747) would contain wires of diameters corresponding to 1% (0.25 mm), 2% (0.50 mm), and 4% (1.00 mm) of thickness. If on the developed film, the **0.50 mm wire (2T)** is the smallest visible wire, then:

$$S = \frac{0.50}{25} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

This is acceptable for **ASME Class 1** welds.

**Conclusion:** The radiograph satisfies both the **geometric unsharpness criterion** and the **2T wire visibility criterion** of ASME Sec V. The radiograph is acceptable for Class 1 weld inspection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Marks-Loss Traps — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Confusing "sensitivity" with "IQI thickness":** The IQI thickness equals 1%, 2%, or 4% of the specimen — but **sensitivity is determined by the smallest *wire* visible, not the IQI thickness itself.** Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit in geometric unsharpness formula:** Mixing mm and inches. **Always convert** before plugging into ASME limits (which use inches).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "– t" in the denominator:** Some students write $U_g = d \cdot t / D$, omitting the object-to-film distance. This gives an **inaccurate (over-optimistic)** answer.
>
> 4. **Not specifying which standard:** KTU 2024 expects **explicit mention of ASTM E747, ASTM E1025, or ASME Sec V** when discussing IQI. Generic answers lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Missing the "essential image" requirement:** For Class 3 welds, not only must the 1T wire be visible, but the **essential image (weld geometry)** must also be clear. Many students forget this.
>
> 6. **Confusing density with contrast:** Density is a **single value** at a point; contrast is a **difference** between two points.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision — bullet-points for "Quality of a Good Radiograph"**

- ☐ **Quality** is the **combined measure** of a radiograph's ability to reveal defects — quantified by **sensitivity** and characterized by **density, contrast, and sharpness**.
- ☐ **Three pillars:** Density, Contrast, Sharpness — all must be acceptable for the radiograph to "pass."
- ☐ **Density formula:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$. Target range **1.5 to 4.0**.
- ☐ **Geometric unsharpness formula:** $U_g = d \cdot t / (D - t)$. Limit **≤ 0.51 mm** (ASME).
- ☐ **Total unsharpness:** $U_t = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_f^2 + U_s^2}$ — vector (Pythagorean) sum.
- ☐ **Sensitivity formula:** $S (\%) = (d_w / T) \times 100$ — based on **smallest visible wire**, not IQI thickness.
- ☐ **2-1-2 Rule:** 2T, 1T, 2T wires — the three essential wires of a wire-type IQI.
- ☐ **ASTM E747** = wire-type IQI standard; **ASTM E1025** = hole-type IQI standard.
- ☐ **Wire-type preferred** in modern ASME Sec V; **hole-type** still common in EN 1435 and pressure vessel codes.
- ☐ **IQI placement:** on the **source side** of the specimen, perpendicular to the weld axis.
- ☐ **Subject contrast** depends on **Z difference** between flaw and matrix and **kV energy**.
- ☐ **Film contrast (γ)** is the slope of the H&D curve in its linear region.
- ☐ **Scatter radiation** degrades contrast — controlled by diaphragms, filters, lead backing.
- ☐ **Density too low** → underexposure; **density too high** → overexposure; both fail acceptance.
- ☐ **Viewing illuminator** must be **≥ 1614 lux** (150 foot-candles); density 4.0+ requires **high-intensity viewer**.
- ☐ **Defective radiograph** (artifacts, low IQI visibility, wrong density) is **rejected outright** — no re-judging.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Film Processing** in Non-Destructive Testing (NDT), specifically in **Radiographic Testing (RT)**, is the sequence of chemical and physical operations performed on an **exposed radiographic film** under controlled darkroom conditions to convert the invisible **latent image** (formed by differential X-ray or gamma-ray exposure of silver halide crystals in the emulsion) into a **permanent, visible radiographic image** (radiograph) suitable for interpretation and defect analysis.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, film processing is treated as a **wet-chemical photographic process** governed by **ISO 11699-1 / ASTM E1815** standards for industrial radiography, comprising five sequential stages: **Development → Stop Bath → Fixing → Washing → Drying**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Film processing is the bridge between **image formation** (Module 2 — exposure geometry, IQI placement) and **image interpretation** (Module 4 — defect recognition). A perfect exposure ruined in the darkroom is still a failed inspection. The examiner **always** tests the student's understanding of *why* each stage is needed, not just *what* it is.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you have taken a **photograph using an old film camera**. After clicking the picture, the film looks completely blank — but it actually holds a *hidden* picture made of tiny chemical changes. To make this hidden picture appear and last forever, you must take the film through a series of **chemical baths in a darkroom**.

Film Processing in industrial radiography works on the **exact same principle**, but with much **stricter control** because:
- Industrial films are **slower** (finer grain) than consumer films.
- The image must reveal **defects as small as 1–2% of the wall thickness**.
- The radiograph becomes a **legal/QC document** that may be kept for 20+ years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Standard Reference (KTU 2024):**
> - **ASTM E1815** — Standard Practice for Radiographic Examination Using Industrial X-Ray Film
> - **ISO 11699-1** — Non-destructive testing — Industrial radiographic film — Part 1: Classification of film systems
> - **EN 584-1** — Equivalent European classification
> - **H&D Curve (Hurter & Driffield, 1890)** — The fundamental sensitometric tool for film characterization.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard development temperature:** **20 °C (68 °F)** (range 18–24 °C acceptable per ASTM E1815)
- **Standard development time:** **5 minutes** (for manual processing) — varies by film type (3–8 min)
- **Fixer retention time:** **2× clearing time** (minimum 5 minutes)
- **Final wash water temperature:** **20–25 °C** with flowing water
- **Recommended archival storage:** **21 °C, 30–50% Relative Humidity** (per ISO 18901)
- **Film speed classification (ASTM E1815):** Classes 1 (slowest, finest grain) → 4 (fastest, coarsest grain)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of Film Processing

#### **Stage 1: Development (Reduction Stage)**
- The exposed film is immersed in a **Developer solution** (a reducing agent) for a controlled time at a controlled temperature.
- **Chemical reaction:** Silver halide crystals (AgBr / AgCl) that were *struck* by X-ray photons (or visible light from intensifying screens) are reduced to **metallic silver (Ag⁰)** — the black component of the image.
- **Developer composition (4 essential components):**
  1. **Reducing agent** — *Hydroquinone* (gives high contrast, slow) + *Phenidone / Metol* (gives low contrast, fast). Together they form **MQ developer** (Metol-Hydroquinone).
  2. **Preservative (Antioxidant)** — *Sodium sulphite (Na₂SO₃)* — prevents the developer from oxidizing in air.
  3. **Accelerator (Activator)** — *Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃)* or *Borax* — maintains alkaline pH (typically **pH 10–11.5**).
  4. **Restrainer (Anti-fog agent)** — *Potassium bromide (KBr)* — prevents unexposed crystals from being developed (chemical fog).

**Key Reaction (Simplified):**

$$\text{AgBr (exposed crystal)} + \text{Developing agent (Hydroquinone)} \longrightarrow \text{Ag}^{0} \text{ (black metallic silver)} + \text{HBr} + \text{Oxidized developer}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the image appear black where X-rays passed through?** Because more photons → more exposed AgBr crystals → more metallic silver deposited → denser (darker) regions. Where a defect (crack, porosity) absorbed fewer X-rays, *fewer* crystals were exposed, leaving those areas **lighter** on the developed film.

#### **Stage 2: Stop Bath (Acid Neutralization)**
- Film is transferred to a **dilute acetic acid solution (1–2%)** for **30–60 seconds**.
- **Purpose:** To instantly **halt the development reaction** by neutralizing the alkaline developer, preventing over-development.
- Prevents developer carry-over into the fixer (which would contaminate and exhaust the fixer prematurely).

#### **Stage 3: Fixing (Stabilization Stage)**
- Film is immersed in **Fixer solution** (sodium/ammonium thiosulphate — "hypo").
- **Purpose:** To **dissolve and remove unexposed, undeveloped silver halide crystals** from the emulsion. These unexposed crystals are still light-sensitive — if not removed, the film would turn black on exposure to ambient light.
- **Chemical reaction:**

$$\text{AgBr (unexposed)} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Na[Ag(S}_2\text{O}_3)_2] \text{ (water-soluble complex)}$$

- **Two-step fixing process:** *Fixer penetrates emulsion → dissolves AgBr → washes away complex.*
- **Fixing time** = **2 × Clearing time** (clearing time = time for milky appearance to disappear when film is viewed against light).

#### **Stage 4: Washing (Residue Removal)**
- Film is washed in **clean, running water (20–25 °C)** for **15–30 minutes** (manual) or **5–10 minutes** (automatic).
- **Purpose:** Removes all residual **thiosulphate (hypo) and silver-thiosulphate complexes**. Any leftover fixer causes the image to **fade/yellow over time** — catastrophic for archival radiographs.
- **Archival test (Residual thiosulphate):** Should be < **0.7 mg/dm²** for permanent records (ISO 18901).

#### **Stage 5: Drying**
- Film is dried in a **dust-free drying cabinet** at **30–40 °C** with filtered air circulation.
- **Avoid:** Excessive heat (>50 °C) → emulsion damage; direct sunlight → fading; rapid drying → water-spot marks.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Standard Value** | **Unit** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical Density** | $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | 0.5 → 4.0 (useful range) | dimensionless | Measures film blackening |
| **H&D Curve Slope (Average Gradient)** | $\bar{G} = \dfrac{D_2 - D_1}{\log E_2 - \log E_1}$ | 2.5 → 4.5 (typical) | dimensionless | Indicates film contrast |
| **Film Speed** | $S = \dfrac{1}{E_{\text{min}}}$ | Class 1 → 4 (ASTM) | 1/R | Inverse of exposure required |
| **Reciprocity Law** | $I \cdot t = \text{constant}$ | valid only in mid-range | — | Fails for very long/short exposures |
| **Development Temperature Tolerance** | $\Delta T \le \pm 0.5$ °C | 20 °C nominal | °C | Critical for consistency |
| **Fixer pH** | — | 4.0 – 4.6 | pH | Acidic (after hardener) |
| **Developer pH** | — | 10.0 – 11.5 | pH | Alkaline |
| **Archival Wash Time (manual)** | $t_w$ | 15 – 30 | min | Removes residual hypo |
| **Residual Thiosulphate Limit** | $\left[\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-}\right]_{\text{res}}$ | $\le 0.7$ | mg/dm² | ISO 18901 archival spec |
| **Film Storage Temp** | $T_{\text{store}}$ | $\le 24$ | °C | Below 50% RH |
| **Fog Density (Base + Fog)** | $D_0$ | $\le 0.30$ | — | Acceptable upper limit (Class I) |
| **H&D Characteristic Curve** | $D$ vs $\log E$ | S-shaped | — | Maps exposure → density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the **H&D (Hurter-Driffield) Characteristic Curve** — *toe, straight-line, shoulder, solarization*. Questions on the *slope of the straight-line portion* (Average Gradient $\bar{G}$) and *film speed* (inverse of $E_{\text{min}}$) are **guaranteed every semester**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace (ASME Sec V, ASTM E1742):** Aircraft engine components require radiographs that retain detail for **20+ year service lives** — proper archival processing is mandatory.
- **Nuclear (ASME Sec III):** Reactor pressure vessel welds — radiographs kept for the **plant's entire 40-year design life**.
- **Pipeline Construction (ASME B31.3 / API 1104):** Girth weld radiographs must survive **field handling** — improper washing leads to fading in 6–12 months.
- **Shipbuilding / Offshore:** Heavy-section welds on offshore platforms use **Class 1/2 films** processed in **mobile darkrooms** with strict chemical control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Optical Density and the H&D Curve
