A radiographer standing **1 meter** from an unshielded 100 Ci Ir-192 source would receive a dose rate on the order of **0.5 Sv/h**, which exceeds the **annual occupational limit of 20 mSv (averaged over 5 years, with no single year exceeding 50 mSv)** by a factor of 10 — making death probable within hours of continuous exposure. > [!WARNING] > **Biological Damage Threshold:** A whole-body dose of **>4 Sv (400 rem)** is classified as the **LD50/60** (lethal dose to 50% of an exposed population within 60 days) for humans without medical intervention. ## 1.4 Units of Radiation Measurement The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a thorough understanding of the following units: | Quantity | SI Unit | Old Unit | Conversion | |---|---|---|---| | Exposure (X) | **C/kg** (Coulomb/kg) | Roentgen (R) | 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg | | Absorbed Dose (D) | **Gray (Gy)** | rad | 1 Gy = 100 rad | | Dose Equivalent (H) | **Sievert (Sv)** | rem | 1 Sv = 100 rem | | Activity (A) | **Becquerel (Bq)** | Curie (Ci) | 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq | | Effective Dose | **Sievert (Sv)** | rem | Same as dose equivalent | The relationship is: $$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$ where $w_R$ is the radiation weighting factor (1 for X-rays/gamma, 20 for alpha particles). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Radiographic Safety (TDS) ### 2.1.1 Time (T) **Principle:** Dose is directly proportional to exposure time. $$D \propto t \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \dot{D} \cdot t$$ where $\dot{D}$ is the dose rate (Sv/h) and $t$ is the exposure duration (hours). **Practical application:** - Minimize source exposure time by using pre-set timers. - Conduct mock drills (without source) to reduce on-site exposure duration. - Use **remote handling tools (pigtails, source projectors)** so personnel stay away from the source. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time = ½ the work** — If you halve the time near the source, you halve the dose received. This is the simplest and most controllable variable. ### 2.1.2 Distance (D) **Principle:** Dose rate follows the **Inverse Square Law** for a point source. $$\dot{D} \propto \frac{1}{d^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{D}(d) = \Gamma \cdot \frac{A}{d^2}$$ where: - $\dot{D}(d)$ = dose rate at distance *d* (Sv/h or R/h) - $\Gamma$ = specific gamma-ray constant of the source (Sv·m²/(GBq·h) or R·cm²/(mCi·h)) - $A$ = activity of the source (GBq or mCi) - $d$ = distance from the source (m or cm) **Numerical insight:** For Ir-192, $\Gamma \approx 0.13$ R·cm²/(mCi·h) at 1 cm. Doubling the distance reduces the dose rate by a factor of **4** (i.e., 75% reduction). ### 2.1.3 Shielding (S) **Principle:** Attenuation of gamma/X-ray beam through matter follows an **exponential decay law**. $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$ where: - $I_0$ = incident beam intensity - $I$ = transmitted intensity after shielding - $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹) — depends on material and photon energy - $x$ = thickness of shielding material (cm) A more practical form uses the **half-value layer (HVL)** or **tenth-value layer (TVL)**: $$I = I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x/\text{HVL}} = I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^{x/\text{TVL}}$$ **Typical HVL/TVL values for Ir-192 (0.4 MeV avg):** | Material | Density (g/cm³) | HVL (cm) | TVL (cm) | |---|---|---|---| | Lead (Pb) | 11.34 | 0.6 | 2.0 | | Steel (Fe) | 7.87 | 1.3 | 4.4 | | Concrete | 2.35 | 4.5 | 15.0 | | Water | 1.00 | 6.0 | 20.0 | | Tungsten (W) | 19.3 | 0.3 | 1.0 | > [!NOTE] > **Lead is the universal shield** in industrial radiography because of its high density (11.34 g/cm³), high atomic number (Z = 82), and ease of fabrication into collimators, shutters, and source containers. ## 2.2 Dose Limits (ICRP & AERB Recommendations) > [!IMPORTANT] > **AERB Dose Limits (as per AERB Safety Code AERB/RF-MED/SC-1 and AERB Safety Guide for Industrial Radiography):** | Category | Annual Effective Dose Limit | Lens of Eye | Skin/Hands/Feet | |---|---|---|---| | **Occupational Worker (Radiographer)** | **20 mSv** (avg over 5 yrs; max 50 mSv in any single year) | 150 mSv | 500 mSv | | **Apprentice / Trainee (<18 yrs)** | 6 mSv | 50 mSv | 150 mSv | | **Public / Members of Public** | 1 mSv | 15 mSv | 50 mSv | | **Embryo/Fetus (declared pregnant worker)** | 1 mSv | — | — | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = \dot{D} \cdot t$ | Dose = Dose rate × time | Sv | | 2 | $\dot{D}_2 = \dot{D}_1 \cdot (d_1/d_2)^2$ | Inverse square law (dose rate at new distance) | Sv/h | | 3 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Exponential attenuation | dimensionless | | 4 | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$ | Mass attenuation coefficient relationship | cm⁻¹ | | 5 | $\text{HVL} = \ln(2)/\mu \approx 0.693/\mu$ | Half-value layer | cm | | 6 | $\text{TVL} = \ln(10)/\mu \approx 2.303/\mu$ | Tenth-value layer | cm | | 7 | $H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ | Equivalent dose | Sv | | 8 | $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ | Effective dose | Sv | | 9 | $\dot{D} = \Gamma \cdot A / d^2$ | Specific gamma-ray constant formula | varies | | 10 | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Activity conversion | Bq | | 11 | $1 \text{ R} = 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C/kg}$ | Exposure conversion | C/kg | | 12 | $1 \text{ Sv} = 100 \text{ rem}$ | Dose equivalent conversion | Sv | | 13 | $1 \text{ Gy} = 100 \text{ rad}$ | Absorbed dose conversion | Gy | | 14 | $\text{LET} = dE/dx$ | Linear energy transfer (radiation quality) | keV/µm | > [!CAUTION] > **Exam Tip:** Always use **\vert** or **\mid** in markdown tables when writing absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ or $\mu/\rho \cdot \rho$ rather than `|x|` or `μ/ρ·ρ`) to prevent LaTeX table corruption. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Petrochemical & Pipeline Industry:** Field radiography of welded joints on offshore platforms uses Ir-192 with **remote exposure devices (REDs)** to maintain distance. - **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades and engine components uses high-energy X-ray (up to 9 MeV) inside **fully shielded bunkers** with interlocks. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Reactor vessel inspections use Co-60 (1.17 & 1.33 MeV gamma) with multi-layered biological shields of lead + concrete + steel. - **Foundries & Castings:** In-house shielded bays with lead-lined walls, automatic exposure control, and CCTV-based remote monitoring. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverse Square Law of Radiation The radioactive source is approximated as a **point source** that emits radiation isotropically (in all directions) with total activity $A$ (in photons/s or Bq). **Step 1: Total photon flux through a sphere of radius r** The photons emitted per second pass through a sphere of surface area $4\pi r^2$. $$\Phi(r) = \frac{A}{4\pi r^2} \quad \text{[photons per second per unit area]}$$ **Step 2: Dose rate is proportional to flux** Since the energy deposited per unit mass is proportional to the number of photons striking per unit area: $$\dot{D}(r) = k \cdot \Phi(r) = \frac{k \cdot A}{4\pi r^2}$$ where $k$ is a constant that depends on photon energy and tissue properties. **Step 3: Eliminate the constants to relate two distances** At distances $d_1$ and $d_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}(d_1) &= \frac{kA}{4\pi d_1^2} \\ \dot{D}(d_2) &= \frac{kA}{4\pi d_2^2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\dot{D}(d_2)}{\dot{D}(d_1)} &= \frac{d_1^2}{d_2^2} \\ \dot{D}(d_2) &= \dot{D}(d_1) \cdot \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$ > This is the **Inverse Square Law**. Doubling distance quarters the dose rate. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Exponential Attenuation (Lambert-Beer Law for Gamma/X-rays) Consider a collimated monoenergetic photon beam of intensity $I_0$ incident on a shielding material of thickness $x$. **Step 1: Infinitesimal reduction in intensity** When the beam passes through a thin slice of thickness $dx$, the fractional reduction in intensity is proportional to the thickness: $$-\frac{dI}{I} = \mu \, dx$$ **Step 2: Integrate from 0 to x** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{I_0}^{I} -\frac{dI}{I} &= \int_{0}^{x} \mu \, dx \\ -\ln(I) + \ln(I_0) &= \mu x \\ \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) &= \mu x \\ \therefore \quad I &= I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Define HVL** At $x = \text{HVL}$, the intensity is halved: $I = I_0/2$. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2} &= e^{-\mu \cdot \text{HVL}} \\ \ln(2) &= \mu \cdot \text{HVL} \\ \text{HVL} &= \frac{\ln(2)}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: For practical shielding calculations** Rewriting the attenuation law in terms of HVL: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x} = I_0 \cdot e^{-(0.693/\text{HVL}) \cdot x} \\ I &= I_0 \cdot \left(e^{\ln(1/2)}\right)^{x/\text{HVL}} \\ I &= I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x/\text{HVL}} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example: Inverse Square Law Application **Problem:** A radiographer is at **3 m** from an Ir-192 source and receives a dose rate of **40 µSv/h**. If he repositions himself to **6 m**, what is the new dose rate? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}_1 &= 40 \, \mu\text{Sv/h} \quad \text{at} \quad d_1 = 3 \, \text{m} \\ \dot{D}_2 &= ? \quad \text{at} \quad d_2 = 6 \, \text{m} \\ \dot{D}_2 &= \dot{D}_1 \cdot \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 \\ &= 40 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{6}\right)^2 \\ &= 40 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \\ &= 40 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \\ &= 10 \, \mu\text{Sv/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Doubling the distance reduces the dose rate by **75%** (from 40 to 10 µSv/h). ## 3.4 Worked Example: Shielding Thickness Calculation **Problem:** A Co-60 source produces a dose rate of **1000 mSv/h** at a workplace. The HVL of lead for Co-60 is **1.2 cm**. How many HVLs of lead are required to reduce the dose rate to **2.5 mSv/h**? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_0 &= 1000 \, \text{mSv/h} \\ I &= 2.5 \, \text{mSv/h} \\ \text{Ratio} \, \frac{I_0}{I} &= \frac{1000}{2.5} = 400 \\ \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} &= \frac{1}{400} \\ 2^n &= 400 \\ n \cdot \log(2) &= \log(400) \\ n &= \frac{\log(400)}{\log(2)} = \frac{2.602}{0.301} \approx 8.64 \, \text{HVLs} \end{aligned} $$ **Required lead thickness:** $$x = n \cdot \text{HVL} = 8.64 \cdot 1.2 \approx 10.4 \, \text{cm of lead}$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, a safety factor of **1.5 to 2 HVLs** is added to the calculated value, making the final design thickness approximately **12–14 cm of lead**. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Biological Effects vs Dose | Dose Range | Effect | Category | Symptom Onset | |---|---|---|---| | < 0.1 Sv | Negligible / no observable effect | Stochastic risk only | Latent (years) | | 0.1 – 1 Sv | Mild radiation sickness | Deterministic threshold | Hours to days | | 1 – 2 Sv | Moderate sickness, decreased WBC | Acute | Days | | 2 – 4 Sv | Severe radiation sickness | Acute | Days–weeks | | 4 – 6 Sv | LD50/60 (50% mortality) | Acute | Weeks | | > 6 Sv | Lethal in most cases | Acute | < 2 months | | 10+ Sv | CNS syndrome, death in days | Acute | Hours–days | ## 3.6 Personal Dosimetry: TLD & Film Badge ### Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (TLD) - **Material:** LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD-100) or CaF₂:Dy - **Working principle:** When irradiated, electrons are trapped at defect sites. On heating (~250°C), they release photons proportional to the absorbed dose. - **Range:** 10 µSv to 10 Sv - **Reusability:** Yes (after annealing) ### Film Badge - **Material:** Photographic emulsion inside a filter pack (open window, plastic, aluminum, copper, lead filters). - **Working principle:** Ionizing radiation darkens the film; the density is measured with a densitometer. - **Range:** 0.1 mSv to 5 Sv - **Reusability:** No (one-time use) > [!IMPORTANT] > **AERB Mandatory:** Every occupational worker in radiography **must wear at least one personal dosimeter (TLD badge or film badge)** at chest or waist level, with additional badges for extremity monitoring during high-exposure tasks. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Radiographic Safety Management System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Regulatory Framework
AERB / ICRP / IAEA] --> B[Safety Code
AERB SC-RW-1] B --> C[Three Pillars TDS
Time Distance Shielding] B --> D[ALARA Principle
Optimization] B --> E[Dose Limits
20 mSv per year] C --> F[Time Controls
Minimize exposure duration] C --> G[Distance Controls
Inverse square law] C --> H[Shielding Controls
Lead barriers collimators] H --> H1[Primary Barrier
Source container] H --> H2[Secondary Barrier
Walls doors maze] H --> H3[Personal Shielding
Aprons gloves] D --> I[Engineering Controls
Interlocks alarms] D --> J[Administrative Controls
Procedures training] D --> K[PPE Controls
Dosimeters badges] E --> L[Area Classification
Controlled and Supervised] L --> L1[Controlled Area
Red light access control] L --> L2[Supervised Area
Yellow light monitoring] F --> M[Operational Implementation] G --> M H1 --> M H2 --> M H3 --> M I --> M J --> M K --> M L1 --> M L2 --> M M --> N[Safe Radiography Operation] M --> O[Emergency Response Plan] O --> O1[Source Retrieval Plan] O --> O2[Spill Containment] O --> O3[Medical Surveillance] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: Safe Radiographic Exposure Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Work Permit Request] --> P2[Verify AERB Authorization
and License Validity] P2 --> P3{Is Area Clear?} P3 -- No --> P4[Evacuate Personnel
and Remove Film] P3 -- Yes --> P5[Post Warning Signs
and Barrier Tape] P5 --> P6[Activate Red Warning Light
and Audible Alarm] P6 --> P7[All Personnel Don
TLD and Film Badges] P7 --> P8[Survey with Survey Meter
Background Check] P8 --> P9[Expose Source
via Remote Crank or Projector] P9 --> P10[Maintain Safe Distance
During Exposure Time] P10 --> P11[Retract Source
to Shielded Container] P11 --> P12[Survey Source Container
Confirm Safe Position] P12 --> P13[Switch Off Red Light
and Remove Barriers] P13 --> P14[Log Dose Records
and Submit TLD] P14 --> P15[End: Safe Operation Complete] ``` ## 4.3 Architecture: Personal Dosimetry Network ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Radiographer 1
TLD Badge Chest] --> D1[Central Dose Record] A2[Radiographer 2
TLD Badge Waist] --> D1 A3[Radiographer 3
TLD Badge Wrist] --> D1 B1[Survey Meter 1
Gamma Detector] --> D2[Area Monitoring Log] B2[Survey Meter 2
Installed at Bunker Door] --> D2 C1[Continuous Air Monitor
CAM Unit] --> D2 D1 --> R1[AERB Dose Database] D2 --> R1 R1 --> R2{Dose Limit Check} R2 -- Within Limit --> R3[Continued Authorization] R2 -- Exceeds Limit --> R4[Suspend Authorization
Medical Review] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Emergency Response for Source Dislodgement ```mermaid flowchart TD E1[Accident: Source Fails to Retract] --> E2[Step 1: Evacuate
All Personnel Immediately] E2 --> E3[Step 2: Notify
RPO and Site Emergency Controller] E3 --> E4[Step 3: Cordon Off
3 meter Radius Minimum] E4 --> E5[Step 4: Estimate Dose Rate
Using Inverse Square Law] E5 --> E6[Step 5: Deploy Long Handled Tools
and Lead Blankets] E6 --> E7[Step 6: Attempt Source Recovery
with Maximum Distance] E7 --> E8{Recovery Successful?} E8 -- Yes --> E9[Survey and Confirm
Source in Shielded Position] E8 -- No --> E10[Contact AERB
Emergency Response Team] E9 --> E11[Document Incident
and Submit to AERB] E10 --> E11 E11 --> E12[Medical Surveillance
of All Exposed Personnel] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `P1`, `H1`) and never use reserved keywords. Node labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italics, or HTML tables. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define the ALARA principle in industrial radiography. State its significance.** **Model Answer:** ALARA stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Achievable"**. It is a safety principle that mandates that all radiation exposures to occupational workers and the public must be kept as far below the prescribed dose limits as is reasonably achievable, taking into account economic and social factors. **Significance:** - It is the cornerstone of modern radiation protection philosophy endorsed by ICRP, IAEA, and AERB. - It shifts the focus from mere compliance with limits to **continuous optimization** of protection. - Implementation requires: (i) engineered safety features, (ii) procedural controls, and (iii) **dose constraint** planning before every radiography job. - It ensures that any exposure with no benefit is avoided, and exposures with benefits are minimized. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List any three personal dosimeters used in industrial radiography and state the working principle of any one.** **Model Answer:** The three personal dosimeters are: 1. **Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (TLD)** — uses LiF or CaF₂ crystals; on heating, trapped electrons release light proportional to absorbed dose. 2. **Film Badge** — uses photographic film; radiation darkens the film; optical density is measured by a densitometer. 3. **Pocket Dosimeter (Direct Reading)** — a small ionization chamber with a quartz-fibre electroscope; deflection of the fibre indicates cumulative dose. **Working Principle of TLD (chosen):** When the TLD crystal is exposed to ionizing radiation, electrons in the crystal lattice are excited and trapped at defect sites. When later heated in the TLD reader (around 250°C), the trapped electrons return to the ground state, releasing photons. The intensity of emitted light is measured by a photomultiplier tube and is directly proportional to the absorbed dose. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **three fundamental principles of radiation protection** in industrial radiography with appropriate formulas. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Co-60 source produces a dose rate of **500 mR/h** at a distance of **2 m** from the source. Calculate: **(i)** the dose rate at 5 m, and **(ii)** the dose rate at 10 m. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — The Three Pillars of Radiation Protection:** **1. Time:** Dose received is directly proportional to the time spent in the radiation field. $$D = \dot{D} \cdot t$$ The exposure time should be minimized through practice runs, use of pre-set timers, and remote handling tools (pigtails, source projectors, and remote-controlled exposure devices). **2. Distance:** For a point source, the dose rate follows the inverse square law: $$\dot{D}(d) = \frac{k}{d^2}$$ Practical methods to increase distance: use of long-handled tools, remote-controlled exposure devices, and maintaining a minimum safe distance as per AERB guidelines (typically ≥ 7 m for field radiography with 100 Ci Ir-192). **3. Shielding:** Gamma and X-ray intensity reduces exponentially through matter: $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$ The HVL and TVL are used in practical design: $$I = I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x/\text{HVL}}$$ Common shielding materials: **lead (Pb, Z=82, ρ=11.34 g/cm³)**, tungsten, depleted uranium, and concrete. Engineering controls include source containers (camera), collimators, and shielded bunkers. **Part (b) — Inverse Square Law Calculation:** **Given:** - $\dot{D}_1 = 500$ mR/h at $d_1 = 2$ m - Find: $\dot{D}_2$ at $d_2 = 5$ m and $d_3 = 10$ m **Solution (i): At 5 m** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}_2 &= \dot{D}_1 \cdot \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot \left(0.4\right)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot 0.16 \\ &= 80 \, \text{mR/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution (ii): At 10 m** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{D}_3 &= \dot{D}_1 \cdot \left(\frac{d_1}{d_3}\right)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{10}\right)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot (0.2)^2 \\ &= 500 \cdot 0.04 \\ &= 20 \, \text{mR/h} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Increasing distance from 2 m to 10 m (5× increase) reduces the dose rate by a factor of 25 (5²), from 500 mR/h to 20 mR/h. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Define **absorbed dose, equivalent dose, and effective dose**. State their SI units and the relationship between them. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **layout of a typical industrial radiography bunker** and list the safety features incorporated. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Dose Quantities:** **1. Absorbed Dose (D):** The energy deposited by ionizing radiation per unit mass of matter. $$D = \frac{d\bar{\varepsilon}}{dm} \quad \text{[Unit: Gray, Gy = J/kg]}$$ **2. Equivalent Dose (H_T):** The absorbed dose weighted by the radiation quality factor (radiation weighting factor $w_R$). $$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R} \quad \text{[Unit: Sievert, Sv]}$$ For X-rays, gamma rays, and beta particles: $w_R = 1$. For alpha particles: $w_R = 20$. **3. Effective Dose (E):** The equivalent dose weighted by the tissue weighting factor $w_T$, accounting for the radiosensitivity of different organs. $$E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T \quad \text{[Unit: Sievert, Sv]}$$ **Relationship Summary:** $$\text{Exposure (C/kg)} \rightarrow \text{Absorbed Dose (Gy)} \rightarrow \text{Equivalent Dose (Sv)} \rightarrow \text{Effective Dose (Sv)}$$ Conversion reminders: - 1 Gy = 100 rad - 1 Sv = 100 rem - 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg - 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq **Part (b) — Industrial Radiography Bunker Layout:** A typical fixed radiography bunker (also called an exposure cell or bay) consists of: 1. **Primary Barrier (Source Side Wall):** Thick lead or concrete wall facing the source. For Co-60 (1.25 MeV), typically 1.0–1.5 m of concrete or 25–30 cm of lead. 2. **Secondary Barriers (Other Walls, Floor, Ceiling):** Designed to attenuate scattered and leakage radiation. For 1.25 MeV Co-60, typically 0.6–0.8 m of concrete. 3. **Maze Entrance:** A labyrinth-style entry that prevents direct line-of-sight to the source, reducing radiation streaming at the door. Walls of maze lined with concrete. 4. **Shielded Door:** A heavy lead-steel door that closes the maze entrance during exposure. Often interlocked with the source mechanism. 5. **Control Console:** Located outside the bunker, behind a shielded viewing window (lead glass ≥ 30 cm Pb equivalent). 6. **Warning System:** Red flashing light above the door (ON during exposure), audible alarm, and "RADIOACTIVE — KEEP OUT" sign in English and local language. 7. **Interlock System:** The source cannot be exposed unless the door is closed; the door cannot be opened while the source is exposed. 8. **Emergency Stop Button:** A mushroom-headed red button at the control console to immediately retract the source. 9. **Ventilation:** Forced air circulation to remove ozone generated by X-ray tubes. 10. **Surveillance Camera:** CCTV to monitor the specimen position without entering the bunker. ``` +----------------------------------+ | [Control Console + Lead Glass] | | (Outside Bunker) | +----------------+-----------------+ | [Maze Entrance] _______________ | | | SPECIMEN + | | X-RAY TUBE / | | GAMMA SOURCE | | | | (Inside Bunker,| | Primary Barrier| | on Source Side)| |_______________| ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Safety Aspect Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Gy and Sv:** Students often interchange Gray (absorbed dose) with Sievert (equivalent/effective dose). Remember: 1 Gy = 1 J/kg absorbed, but 1 Sv = 1 J/kg of weighted biological effect. > 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor in Shielding:** Examiners expect an additional 1.5–2 HVLs on top of the calculated thickness. Skipping this loses 2 marks easily. > 3. **Mixing up HVL and TVL:** HVL reduces intensity by ½, TVL by 1/10. One TVL ≈ 3.32 HVL. > 4. **Omitting Units:** Numerical answers in mR/h, mSv/h, Sv, etc. must carry units. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark. > 5. **Not Stating ALARA:** Almost every radiation safety question worth ≥ 7 marks expects an explicit mention of the ALARA principle. > 6. **Skipping ALARP Justification:** AERB expects justification of why a particular dose is "as low as reasonably practicable" — this is often a 2-mark sub-question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety Aspects in Radiography:** ### Core Concepts - [x] **Three Pillars:** Time, Distance, Shielding (TDS) - [x] **ALARA Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Achievable — optimization of every exposure - [x] **Dose Limits (AERB):** 20 mSv/yr (occupational, 5-yr avg), 1 mSv/yr (public) - [x] **Justification Principle:** No practice involving radiation shall be adopted unless it produces sufficient benefit to offset the harm ### Key Formulas - [x] $D = \dot{D} \cdot t$ (Time linear relationship) - [x] $\dot{D} \propto 1/d^2$ (Inverse square law) - [x] $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ (Exponential attenuation) - [x] $\text{HVL} = 0.693/\mu$ (Half-value layer) - [x] $H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ (Equivalent dose) - [x] $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ (Effective dose) ### Units Mastery - [x] Absorbed dose: **Gray (Gy) = J/kg = 100 rad** - [x] Equivalent/Effective dose: **Sievert (Sv) = 100 rem** - [x] Activity: **Becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration/s = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ = 1 Ci** - [x] Exposure: **C/kg = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ = 1 R** ### Safety Equipment - [x] **Personal Dosimeters:** TLD, Film Badge, Pocket Dosimeter - [x] **Area Monitors:** Geiger-Müller (GM) counter, Ionization chamber - [x] **Shielding:** Lead (Z=82), tungsten, concrete, depleted uranium - [x] **Source Container (Camera):** S-shaped, lead-shielded, with source-position indicator ### Regulatory Bodies - [x] **AERB** (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India) - [x] **ICRP** (International Commission on Radiological Protection) - [x] **IAEA** (International Atomic Energy Agency) - [x] **NRC** (Nuclear Regulatory Commission, USA) ### Area Classification - [x] **Controlled Area (Red):** Dose rate > 100 µSv/h, restricted access - [x] **Supervised Area (Yellow):** Dose rate 7.5–100 µSv/h, monitored continuously - [x] **Unrestricted (Green):** Dose rate < 7.5 µSv/h, no special access ### Emergency Scenarios - [x] **Source Dislodgement:** Evacuate → Notify RPO → Cordon → Survey → Recovery with long tools → AERB notification - [x] **Lost Source:** Notify AERB within 24 hours; mobilize search team with survey meters - [x] **Personnel Overexposure:** Immediate medical examination, biological dosimetry, dose reconstruction ### Key Source Data - [x] **Ir-192:** γ = 0.4 MeV (avg), HVL in Pb = 0.6 cm, HVL in steel = 1.3 cm - [x] **Co-60:** γ = 1.17 & 1.33 MeV, HVL in Pb = 1.2 cm, HVL in steel = 2.1 cm - [x] **Cs-137:** γ = 0.662 MeV, HVL in Pb = 0.65 cm > **End of Module 3 Notes — Safety Aspects Required in Radiography** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing; Digital Image Correlation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing; Digital Image Correlation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Digital Signal Processing (DSP) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** is the mathematical manipulation of signals that have been converted from the analog (continuous-time, continuous-amplitude) domain into a discrete (sampled, quantized) numerical representation, performed through algorithms executed on digital hardware (microprocessors, FPGAs, DSP chips). In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, DSP is the invisible computational engine that sits behind almost every modern inspection instrument — ultrasonic flaw detectors, eddy-current impedance analyzers, acoustic emission systems, and digital radiography workstations all rely on it to extract weak defect signatures buried in noise. ### Intuitive Analogy — "Cleaning a Muddy Voice Recording" Imagine a teacher’s voice recorded on a phone in a windy playground. The *analog* signal is the raw sound wave — continuous, full of wind noise, and impossible to "search." When we feed that recording into a computer: 1. The microphone's **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** chops the smooth wave into millions of tiny numerical snapshots (samples). 2. Software then applies **filters** — much like a high-quality headphone that cancels wind noise — to enhance the teacher’s voice and suppress the rumble. 3. A **Fourier Transform** breaks the cleaned signal into its frequency ingredients, so we can identify the exact pitch of any hidden noise or echo. That is, in essence, what DSP does to ultrasonic, eddy-current, and acoustic-emission signals in NDT. ## 1.2 Digital Image Processing (DIP) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Digital Image Processing (DIP)** is the use of computer algorithms to manipulate a two-dimensional discretized image (a matrix of pixels with finite intensity values) for the purposes of enhancement, restoration, segmentation, feature extraction, and automated interpretation. In NDT, DIP is the backbone of **digital radiography (DR)**, **computed tomography (CT)**, and automated visual/optical inspection. The same algorithms that sharpen an X-ray of a weld and detect a hair-line crack are also the ones used in facial recognition and self-driving cars. ### Intuitive Analogy — "X-Ray Detective Work" Suppose you are a radiologist looking at a chest X-ray on a light box. The image is hazy, the contrast is poor, and a tiny nodule is hiding behind a rib. With DIP: - **Histogram equalization** brightens the dark areas so subtle features pop out. - **Edge-detection filters** (Sobel, Canny) outline the rib cage, making the nodule look "out of place." - **Segmentation** isolates the suspicious region automatically. - **Pattern recognition** then flags it for the radiologist. Exactly the same workflow is followed when an algorithm hunts for cracks in a turbine blade radiograph or porosity in a casting. ## 1.3 Digital Image Correlation (DIC) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Digital Image Correlation (DIC)** is a non-contact, full-field, optical metrology technique that measures displacement and strain on a specimen surface by tracking the position of a random speckle pattern between a *reference* (undeformed) image and one or more *deformed* images through the maximization of a grayscale-intensity cross-correlation coefficient. It is one of the most powerful *quantitative* NDT methods available, capable of mapping strain fields at sub-pixel accuracy without any physical contact with the specimen. ### Intuitive Analogy — "Fingerprinting the Surface" Paint a wall with a unique random speckle pattern (a "speckle fingerprint"). Now gently press the wall with your palm. The speckles shift, compress, and stretch. By taking a *before* photo and an *after* photo, and asking the computer: *"Which tiny patch of speckle in the new image matches which patch in the old one?"*, we can compute exactly **how much** and **in which direction** every point on the wall moved. That is DIC. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (NDT context) | |---|---|---| | Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | ≥ 2 × highest signal frequency (Nyquist) | | Quantization bit-depth | $b$ | 8, 12, 16 bits (radiography: ≥ 12) | | Speckle pattern size | $d_s$ | 3–10 pixels (DIC optimal) | | Sub-pixel accuracy | — | 1/100 to 1/1000 of a pixel | | Cross-correlation coefficient | $C$ | 0 ≤ C ≤ 1 (C = 1 ⇒ perfect match) | > [!NOTE] > The **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) is the *single most important rule* in DSP-based NDT. Violating it produces *aliasing*, where high-frequency defect signals masquerade as low-frequency ones, causing real flaws to be **missed**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Operational Pillars of DSP ### Pillar I — Digitization (ADC Chain) The continuous physical signal $x(t)$ from an NDT transducer (e.g., ultrasonic probe) is converted to a digital stream $x[n]$ by: 1. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** — restricts bandwidth to $f_{max}$. 2. **Sampling** — instantaneous value captured every $T_s = 1/f_s$ seconds. 3. **Quantization** — amplitude discretized into $2^b$ levels. 4. **Encoding** — binary representation for storage. ### Pillar II — Transformation (Frequency-Domain Analysis) - **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT):** converts sampled signal to frequency spectrum. - **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT):** computationally efficient DFT, $O(N \log N)$. - **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** identifies periodic defect echoes vs. broadband noise. ### Pillar III — Filtering (Noise Suppression & Feature Extraction) | Filter Type | Function in NDT | |---|---| | Low-pass (LPF) | Removes high-frequency electronic noise | | High-pass (HPF) | Removes baseline drift / DC component | | Band-pass (BPF) | Isolates a defect’s frequency band | | Matched filter | Maximizes SNR for a known pulse shape | | Median filter | Removes impulsive spikes (salt-and-pepper noise in images) | ## 2.2 The Image Processing Pipeline in NDT 1. **Image Acquisition** — X-ray detector, CCD/CMOS camera, scanner. 2. **Pre-processing** — noise reduction, flat-field correction, contrast stretching. 3. **Enhancement** — histogram equalization, unsharp masking, gamma correction. 4. **Segmentation** — thresholding, edge detection, region growing. 5. **Feature Extraction** — crack length, pore area, edge sharpness. 6. **Classification / Decision** — accept/reject (often using AI). ## 2.3 The DIC Operational Principle — Step by Step DIC tracks motion by comparing *sub-windows* (also called **subsets**) of the speckled surface. 1. A region of interest (ROI) is selected on the **reference image** $I_r(x,y)$. 2. A square **subset** of size $(2M+1) \times (2M+1)$ pixels is centered on point $P(x_0, y_0)$. 3. The algorithm searches the **deformed image** $I_d(x',y')$ for the location that best matches this subset. 4. The match is quantified by the **zero-normalized cross-correlation (ZNCC)** coefficient. 5. The displacement $(u, v)$ of the subset center is recorded. 6. Repeat for every point in the ROI to obtain a **full-field displacement map**. 7. Numerical differentiation of displacement yields **strain fields** $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \varepsilon_{xy}$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Master the equations in the table below — these appear frequently in KTU university exam Part B questions. | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist–Shannon theorem** | Selecting ADC sampling rate for UT signals | | 2 | $L = b \cdot f_s$ | Data rate (bits/s) | Storage / bandwidth sizing | | 3 | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | **Discrete Fourier Transform** | Spectral analysis of AE / UT | | 4 | $C_{ZNCC} = \dfrac{\sum (I_r - \bar{I_r})(I_d - \bar{I_d})}{\sqrt{\sum (I_r - \bar{I_r})^2 \sum (I_d - \bar{I_d})^2}}$ | Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation | DIC similarity metric | | 5 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | Quantifying filter effectiveness | | 6 | $u = x' - x, \quad v = y' - y$ | Displacement vector components | DIC output | | 7 | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$ | Normal strain (small-strain) | DIC post-processing | | 8 | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$ | Engineering shear strain | DIC post-processing | | 9 | $\Delta x = k \cdot \dfrac{p}{M}$ | Sub-pixel displacement (parabolic fit) | Refining DIC accuracy | | 10 | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}$ | Peak SNR (image) | Assessing denoising filter quality | > Where: > - $A_{signal}, A_{noise}$ = RMS amplitudes of signal and noise > - $MAX_I$ = maximum pixel intensity (e.g., 255 for 8-bit) > - $MSE$ = mean-squared-error between original and processed image > - $p$ = pixel pitch, $M$ = subset size, $k$ = parabolic fit coefficient > - $x', y'$ = deformed coordinates; $x, y$ = reference coordinates ### Engineering Utility - **DSP**: powers ultrasonic phased-array beamforming, TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction) signal stacking, and eddy-current multi-frequency analysis. - **DIP**: enables automated defect recognition (ADR) in radiographic and CT inspection of welds, castings, and composites. - **DIC**: used to measure residual stress, fatigue crack-tip strain fields, and thermal-expansion coefficients in aerospace and civil structures. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (Intuitive Limit) > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation establishes *why* the sampling rate must be at least twice the highest frequency present in the signal — a Board-favourite concept in NDT signal-processing questions. **Step 1 — Continuous signal representation.** Any band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ can be reconstructed from its samples $x[nT_s]$ if and only if we know all its frequency content is confined to $\vert f \vert \leq f_{max}$. Formally, its Fourier transform satisfies: $$X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > f_{max}$$ **Step 2 — Sampling as multiplication by an impulse train.** Sampling multiplies $x(t)$ by a Dirac comb $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **Step 3 — Effect in the frequency domain (convolution).** Multiplication in time corresponds to **periodic replication** in frequency: $$X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$ where $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency. **Step 4 — Non-overlapping spectrum condition.** The shifted copies $X(f - kf_s)$ must not overlap, otherwise frequency information is destroyed (aliasing). The minimum spacing between copies is $f_s$, and the spectrum $X(f)$ has width $2f_{max}$. Therefore: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ $$\boxed{f_s^{min} = 2 f_{max} \quad \text{(Nyquist Rate)}}$$ **Step 5 — Practical NDT rule of thumb.** Because real anti-aliasing filters are not brick-wall, engineers choose: $$f_s^{practical} = (2.5 \text{ to } 5) \times f_{max}$$ > **Worked example:** An ultrasonic flaw detector on a 5 MHz probe. The highest useful harmonic is the 3rd = 15 MHz. Minimum sampling rate = 30 MS/s. With engineering margin, choose 100 MS/s ADC. ## 3.2 Derivation — Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) Coefficient **Step 1 — Raw cross-correlation.** For two image sub-windows $I_r$ (reference) and $I_d$ (deformed), the raw correlation is: $$C_{raw}(u,v) = \sum_{i=-M}^{M} \sum_{j=-M}^{M} I_r(x_i, y_j) \cdot I_d(x_i + u, y_j + v)$$ This is a sliding dot product; large value ⇒ good match. **Step 2 — Why "normalize"?** Raw correlation is sensitive to global lighting changes between $I_r$ and $I_d$. To remove this bias, subtract the local mean of each subset. Define mean values: $$\bar{I_r} = \dfrac{1}{(2M+1)^2} \sum \sum I_r, \quad \bar{I_d} = \dfrac{1}{(2M+1)^2} \sum \sum I_d$$ **Step 3 — ZNCC formulation.** Replace $I$ by $(I - \bar{I})$ in the dot product and divide by the product of their standard deviations: $$C_{ZNCC}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum (I_r - \bar{I_r})(I_d - \bar{I_d})}{\sqrt{\sum (I_r - \bar{I_r})^2 \cdot \sum (I_d - \bar{I_d})^2}}$$ **Step 4 — Properties of $C_{ZNCC}$.** - Range: $-1 \leq C_{ZNCC} \leq 1$ - $C_{ZNCC} = 1$: perfect positive correlation (best match) - $C_{ZNCC} = 0$: no correlation - $C_{ZNCC} = -1$: perfect anti-correlation **Step 5 — Sub-pixel refinement.** The integer-pixel location that maximizes $C_{ZNCC}$ is the *coarse* match. A **parabolic fit** is applied to the 3×3 neighbourhood of the peak to estimate the sub-pixel peak position with accuracy down to 1/100 pixel: $$u^{*} = u_0 + \dfrac{C(-1,0) - C(+1,0)}{2[C(-1,0) - 2C(0,0) + C(+1,0)]}$$ $$\boxed{(u^{*}, v^{*}) = \text{argmax}_{(u,v)} C_{ZNCC}(u,v) \pm 0.01 \text{ pixel}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation — Strain from Displacement Field **Step 1 — Definition of normal strain.** Strain measures the *relative* change in length of a small line element originally oriented along $\hat{x}$: $$\varepsilon_{xx} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta u}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$ Similarly: $$\varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$$ **Step 2 — Shear strain.** The change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements defines shear: $$\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$ **Step 3 — Practical computation in DIC software.** Because the displacement field is discrete, the partial derivatives are approximated using the **central-difference scheme** over a user-defined **strain window** of size $N_w$: $$\varepsilon_{xx}(i,j) = \dfrac{u(i+1,j) - u(i-1,j)}{2 \cdot N_w \cdot p}$$ where $p$ is the camera pixel size (mm/pixel), converting pixel displacement to engineering units. > **Worked example:** A DIC system with $p = 0.01$ mm/pixel, strain window $N_w = 5$ pixels. Two adjacent grid points show $u$-displacement difference of 0.4 pixels. Then: > $$\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{0.4}{2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.01} = 4 \text{ (unitless)} = 4 \mu\varepsilon \times 10^6 / 10^6 \to \text{report as } 4000 \, \mu\varepsilon$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | DSP (1-D) | DIP (2-D) | DIC (2-D metrology) | |---|---|---|---| | Input | Time signal $x(t)$ | 2-D image $I(x,y)$ | Image pair $I_r, I_d$ | | Domain | Time / Frequency | Spatial / Frequency | Spatial | | Key operation | Convolution, FFT | Convolution, morphology | Cross-correlation | | Output | Filtered signal / spectrum | Enhanced image | Displacement / strain map | | Typical use in NDT | UT, ET, AE processing | RT, CT, visual inspection | Full-field strain, residual stress | | Sampling rule | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | 2-D Nyquist (pixels ≥ 2 × finest feature) | Subset ≥ 3× smallest speckle | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic DSP Pipeline in an NDT Instrument ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical NDT Signal
Ultrasonic / Eddy Current / AE] --> B[Anti-Aliasing
Low-Pass Filter] B --> C[Sampler
Hold and Quantize] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital Converter] D --> E[Digital Signal Processor
FFT, Filtering, Averaging] E --> F[Feature Extraction
Peak, ToF, Amplitude] F --> G[Decision Module
Accept / Reject / Alarm] G --> H[Display / Data Storage] classDef analog fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef digital fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef output fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class A,B analog class C,D,E,F digital class G,H output ``` > **Reading the diagram:** the *left side* (yellow) is the analog world; the *centre* (blue) is where DSP algorithms live; the *right side* (green) is the engineering decision. The ADC is the bridge between physics and computation. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Digital Image Processing Pipeline for Radiographic NDT ```mermaid flowchart TD A[X-Ray Source] --> B[Specimen] B --> C[Digital Detector
Flat Panel / CR Plate] C --> D[Raw Image
with Noise and Artifacts] D --> E[Pre-Processing
Flat Field, Dead Pixel] E --> F[Enhancement
Histogram Eq., Unsharp Mask] F --> G[Segmentation
Thresholding / Edge Detection] G --> H[Feature Extraction
Crack Length, Pore Area] H --> I[Classification
AI / Rule Based] I --> J[Report
Defect Map, Severity] classDef phys fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef out fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class A,B,C phys class D,E,F,G,H,I proc class J out ``` ## 4.3 Flowchart — Digital Image Correlation (DIC) Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Prepare Specimen
Apply Speckle Pattern] --> B[Acquire Reference Image
Ir x y] B --> C[Apply Load or Deformation] C --> D[Acquire Deformed Image
Id x y] D --> E[Define Region of Interest ROI] E --> F[Select Subset Size 2M+1 x 2M+1] F --> G[For Each Point in ROI] G --> H[Compute ZNCC for All Candidate Positions] H --> I{Peak Found?} I -- No --> H I -- Yes --> J[Sub Pixel Refinement
Parabolic Fit] J --> K[Record u v Displacement] K --> L{All ROI Points Done?} L -- No --> G L -- Yes --> M[Generate Displacement Map] M --> N[Compute Strain Field
Partial Derivatives] N --> O[Output Full Field Strain Contour] classDef prep fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef algo fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef res fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F prep class G,H,I,J,K,L algo class M,N,O res ``` ## 4.4 Stereo-DIC Configuration (3-D Full-Field Measurement) ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Speckled Specimen] --> C1[Camera 1
Left View] S --> C2[Camera 2
Right View] C1 --> CL[Lens] C2 --> CR[Lens] CL --> S1[Sensor Left
2D Image] CR --> S2[Sensor Right
2D Image] S1 --> T[Triangulation
and Calibration] S2 --> T T --> D3[3D Displacement Field
u v w] D3 --> ST[Strain Tensor] ST --> OUT[Out of Plane
and In Plane Strain] classDef cam fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef out fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class S,C1,C2,CL,CR,S1,S2 cam class T,D3 proc class ST,OUT out ``` > **Engineering insight:** Stereo-DIC uses two cameras viewing the same speckle from different angles. By triangulating matching speckle points, it reconstructs 3-D displacement components $u, v, w$ and full 6-component strain tensor. This is essential for measuring *out-of-plane* deformation in pressurized pipes, composite panels, and turbine blades. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define Digital Image Correlation (DIC). List any two advantages of DIC over conventional strain gauge measurement.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (2 marks):** Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is a non-contact, full-field optical metrology technique that measures displacement and strain on a specimen surface by tracking the position of a unique random speckle pattern between a reference image and a deformed image through maximization of the zero-normalized cross-correlation (ZNCC) coefficient. **Two advantages over strain gauge (1 mark):** 1. **Full-field measurement** — DIC produces strain maps over the entire surface, whereas a strain gauge gives a single average value at one point. 2. **Non-contact & no instrumentation on specimen** — DIC requires only surface preparation (speckle), eliminating gauge bonding errors and allowing measurement on rotating or high-temperature components. 3. *(Any other valid point: high spatial resolution, no lead-wire effect, suitable for complex geometries.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample] **State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. Why is it important in NDT signal acquisition?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement (2 marks):** The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: > $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ > The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. **Importance in NDT (1 mark):** In ultrasonic, eddy-current, and acoustic-emission testing, the signal contains high-frequency defect echoes. If the ADC samples below the Nyquist rate, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency defect signatures fold back into lower frequencies, causing genuine flaws to be **missed or misread**. Adhering to the theorem ensures faithful digital representation of defect signals. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, students commonly lose marks for: (i) drawing block diagrams without *labelling* the signal flow direction, (ii) using the phrase "image is processed" without specifying the actual operator (e.g., "Sobel filter is applied"), (iii) forgetting to mention the *role* of each block in the NDT context, and (iv) mixing up displacement and strain definitions in DIC. Be **specific and quantitative** wherever possible. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted] **(a) Explain the principle and block diagram of Digital Signal Processing as applied to ultrasonic NDT. Discuss the role of sampling, quantization, and the Fast Fourier Transform in defect detection. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle (2 marks):** Digital Signal Processing (DSP) in ultrasonic NDT refers to the conversion of the analog radio-frequency (RF) echo signal received from the ultrasonic probe into a digital form, followed by mathematical operations (filtering, transformation, averaging, feature extraction) to enhance defect visibility and enable automated interpretation. **Block diagram (3 marks):** ``` Analog UT Echo → Anti-Aliasing LPF → Sample & Hold → ADC → Digital Processor (FFT, Filters, Averaging) → Feature Extraction → Display / Decision ``` **Role of each stage (2 marks):** - **Anti-aliasing filter:** restricts the signal bandwidth to $f_{max}$ so that no frequency component exceeds $f_s/2$, preventing aliasing. - **Sampling:** captures the instantaneous amplitude at instants $nT_s$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$. For a 5 MHz probe with 3rd harmonic at 15 MHz, $f_s \geq 30$ MS/s (Nyquist). - **Quantization:** discretizes amplitude into $2^b$ levels (typically 12–14 bits in modern flaw detectors), providing $\sim 72$–$84$ dB dynamic range. - **FFT:** converts the time-domain echo train into its frequency spectrum. Defect echoes with characteristic frequency signatures (e.g., resonance of a delamination) can be separated from broadband grain noise. Spectral peak tracking also enables thickness gauging. **(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure of Digital Image Correlation (DIC). Derive the expression for the Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) coefficient and explain why normalization is essential. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step-by-step procedure (4 marks):** 1. **Specimen preparation:** apply a high-contrast random speckle pattern (black-and-white paint spray, toner powder, or airbrush) on the surface of interest. The speckle size should be 3–10 pixels for optimal correlation. 2. **Reference image acquisition ($I_r$):** capture the high-resolution image of the specimen in its undeformed (or initial) state using a calibrated CCD/CMOS camera. 3. **Deformation / loading:** apply the load (tensile, fatigue, thermal) while the camera records images continuously or at intervals. 4. **Deformed image acquisition ($I_d$):** capture the image after deformation. 5. **Region of Interest (ROI) selection:** define the area for analysis. 6. **Subset definition:** select a square subset of size $(2M+1) \times (2M+1)$ around a point $P(x_0, y_0)$. 7. **ZNCC computation:** search $I_d$ for the location where the ZNCC with the reference subset is maximum. 8. **Sub-pixel refinement:** apply parabolic/Gaussian fit to obtain sub-pixel accuracy (~1/100 pixel). 9. **Strain computation:** differentiate the displacement field using central differences over a strain window. 10. **Output:** full-field contour plots of $u, v, \varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \gamma_{xy}$. **Derivation of ZNCC (2 marks):** The raw cross-correlation is: $$C_{raw}(u,v) = \sum_{i,j} I_r(x_i, y_j) \cdot I_d(x_i + u, y_j + v)$$ To remove sensitivity to offset lighting, subtract local means $\bar{I_r}$ and $\bar{I_d}$: $$C_{ZNCC}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum_{i,j}\bigl(I_r - \bar{I_r}\bigr)\bigl(I_d - \bar{I_d}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i,j}\bigl(I_r - \bar{I_r}\bigr)^2 \cdot \sum_{i,j}\bigl(I_d - \bar{I_d}\bigr)^2}}$$ **Why normalization (1 mark):** Without normalization, the correlation is biased by global brightness changes (e.g., due to varying illumination, aperture changes, exposure variation). ZNCC is invariant to linear brightness/contrast shifts, ensuring the match is based purely on the *spatial pattern* of speckle, not on overall intensity. This makes the method robust for field NDT conditions. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted] **(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the stages of Digital Image Processing (DIP) as applied to radiographic NDT. Explain the importance of histogram equalization and edge detection in defect visibility. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block diagram (2 marks):** ``` X-Ray Exposure → Digital Detector → Raw Image → Pre-processing (flat-field, dead-pixel) → Enhancement (histogram equalization, unsharp masking) → Segmentation (thresholding, edge detection) → Feature Extraction (crack length, pore area) → Classification (AI/rule-based) → Defect Report ``` **Stage explanations (3 marks):** - **Pre-processing:** corrects for detector non-uniformity (flat-field) and removes dead/hot pixels. - **Enhancement:** improves contrast and detail visibility. - **Segmentation:** separates defect regions from background. - **Feature extraction:** quantifies defect geometry. - **Classification:** decides pass/fail per code. **Histogram equalization (1 mark):** - Original histogram of a radiograph is often *concentrated* in a narrow intensity range, making defects invisible. - Equalization redistributes intensities uniformly, *stretching* the dynamic range and bringing out subtle gray-level differences caused by cracks, porosity, or inclusions. - Transformation: $s_k = (L-1) \sum_{j=0}^{k} p_r(r_j)$, where $p_r$ is the normalized histogram. **Edge detection (1 mark):** - Operators (Sobel, Prewitt, Canny) compute the spatial gradient $\nabla I(x,y)$ of pixel intensity. - Cracks and pores appear as **discontinuities** (sharp edges) in the otherwise smooth radiographic background. - Edge maps highlight these discontinuities, enabling automated crack-length measurement. **(b) What are the limitations of strain gauges in measuring deformation? How does DIC overcome them? Discuss the role of speckle pattern quality on DIC accuracy. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Limitations of strain gauges (3 marks):** 1. **Point measurement only** — gives a single average strain over the gauge length; misses localized strain concentrations at crack tips or stress raisers. 2. **Requires physical bonding** to the specimen; the adhesive and gauge alter local stiffness and may debond under high strain or temperature. 3. **Limited to accessible, smooth surfaces**; difficult on curved, rotating, hot, or submerged components. 4. **Lead-wire effects and self-heating** introduce drift and noise. 5. **One-shot installation** — once bonded, the gauge cannot be reused on another location; alignment errors during installation give systematic bias. **How DIC overcomes these (2 marks):** 1. **Full-field** — produces strain maps over the entire ROI at every pixel/grid point, capturing localized strain concentrations. 2. **Non-contact** — no physical contact, so it does not alter the mechanical response of the specimen. 3. **Reusable setup** — cameras and lights are repositionable; the speckle pattern can be re-applied. 4. **Suitable for harsh environments** — high temperature (with appropriate filters and cameras), rotating shafts (stroboscopic DIC), in-situ microscopy. 5. **Captures complex deformation** — large rotations, shear, and heterogeneous fields. **Role of speckle pattern quality (2 marks):** - **High contrast and randomness** produce unique gray-level signatures inside each subset, making the correlation peak sharp and unambiguous. - **Optimal speckle size** (3–10 pixels) balances *sub-pixel interpolation* accuracy — too large ⇒ loss of spatial resolution; too small ⇒ aliasing and noisy correlation. - **High coverage density** ensures every subset has sufficient pattern variation; sparse or low-contrast speckle produces **decorrelation** and failed matching, especially at large strains. - **Stable adhesion** of the speckle to the specimen is essential; delamination of paint at high strain causes spurious local strain measurements. > **KTU Tip:** Always pair "advantage of DIC" with the corresponding "limitation of strain gauge" — examiners reward comparative reasoning. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam** ### Digital Signal Processing (DSP) - DSP = math operations on digitized signals to extract information. - Critical stages: **anti-alias filter → sampling → quantization → encoding → processing**. - **Nyquist–Shannon theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; violation ⇒ aliasing. - FFT converts time-domain to frequency-domain; useful for spectral feature identification in UT, ET, AE. - Quantization bit-depth $b$ gives dynamic range $\approx 6b$ dB (e.g., 12 bits ≈ 72 dB). - **Filter types:** LPF (noise), HPF (drift), BPF (defect band), matched filter (known pulse). ### Digital Image Processing (DIP) - DIP pipeline: **acquisition → pre-processing → enhancement → segmentation → feature extraction → classification**. - Histogram equalization *stretches* intensity distribution to improve contrast. - Edge detection (Sobel, Canny) highlights crack/pore boundaries via spatial gradient. - Used in **digital radiography, CT, automated visual inspection** of welds and castings. - PSNR and MSE quantify enhancement quality. ### Digital Image Correlation (DIC) - DIC is a **non-contact, full-field** optical technique for **displacement and strain** measurement. - Workflow: **speckle → reference image → deform → deformed image → ZNCC → sub-pixel fit → strain field**. - **ZNCC** is invariant to linear brightness/contrast changes; values lie in $[-1, +1]$. - **Sub-pixel accuracy** of 1/100 pixel via parabolic/Gaussian peak fitting. - **Strain** is obtained by *differentiating* the displacement field over a strain window. - **Stereo-DIC** uses two cameras for **3-D displacement** and full **strain tensor**. - **Speckle quality** (size 3–10 pixels, high contrast, randomness) is the single biggest determinant of DIC accuracy. - **Applications:** crack-tip strain, fatigue, residual stress, thermal strain, biomechanics, civil structures. ### Frequently Confused Pairs (Avoid These Errors) - DSP ≠ DIP — DSP handles 1-D *signals*; DIP handles 2-D *images*. - Displacement $(u, v)$ is a *primary* DIC output; **strain is a derived quantity** obtained by differentiation (introduces smoothing/noise trade-off). - Nyquist rate = $2 f_{max}$ (minimum), not $f_{max}$. - SNR (dB) for amplitude = $20 \log_{10}$; for power = $10 \log_{10}$. > **Closing Note for Students:** This topic carries a high conceptual weight in PESFT521 Module 4. Focus on the *interconnection* between the three sub-topics (DSP → DIP → DIC) — they are a single evolutionary chain from 1-D signals to 2-D full-field metrology, and KTU questions frequently test this linkage.

topics.title: Acoustic Emission Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Acoustic Emission Inspection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Acoustic Emission (AE)** is defined by the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM E1316) as the class of phenomena whereby transient elastic waves are generated by the rapid release of energy from a localized source within a material. This energy release occurs when the material undergoes irreversible structural changes such as plastic deformation, crack initiation and propagation, fiber breakage, delamination in composites, phase transformations, friction, or leak-induced turbulence. Acoustic Emission Inspection (AEI), also termed Acoustic Emission Testing (AET) or Stress Wave Emission Testing, is therefore a **passive, real-time, dynamic NDT technique** that listens to the high-frequency (typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz**) elastic waves emitted naturally by a structure under stress, rather than injecting energy into it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Board-Exam Insight:** Unlike ultrasonic testing which is an *active* technique (the probe transmits and receives), Acoustic Emission is a *passive* listening technique. The energy that produces the elastic waves comes **from the defect itself**, not from the instrument. This makes AE uniquely capable of detecting *active* defects in real-time. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a quiet house at night. Suddenly, you hear a *creak* from a wooden beam, then another *crack*, and a louder *pop*. By the location of the sound, your two ears, and the time difference between the sound reaching each ear, you can roughly pinpoint where the sounds came from. If the sounds grow louder and more frequent, you know something serious is happening — perhaps the beam is about to fail. **Acoustic Emission works exactly like this for engineering materials:** - The **defect** (a growing crack, a slipping fiber, a leaking valve) is the "noise source." - The **structure itself** acts as the propagation medium (the "house"). - The **piezoelectric sensors** (transducers) are the "ears" clamped to the surface. - The **AE analyzer** is the "brain" that records, times, and locates the sounds. By *listening* to the structure while it is stressed (e.g., during a hydrostatic pressure test of a pressure vessel), an engineer can identify the *active* flaws and locate them with precision. Critically, dormant, stable, non-growing defects remain silent — so AE detects only the flaws that matter. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants The fundamental physical quantities used in AE testing are: - **Wave frequency range:** **20 kHz – 1 MHz** (above human hearing) - **Sound speed in steel (longitudinal):** $\approx$ **5900 m/s** - **Sound speed in aluminum:** $\approx$ **6320 m/s** - **Threshold voltage (typical):** **20–100 µV** (at sensor output) - **Reference voltage for dB scaling:** **1 µV** at the sensor output - **ASTM E1106 reference amplitude:** **0 dB = 1 µV** at the transducer face - **Felicity Ratio benchmark (passing test):** **> 1.0** (typical acceptance threshold 1.0 – 1.5) > [!NOTE] > **Why use decibels in AE?** Because AE signal amplitudes span six or more orders of magnitude (from a few microvolts to several volts). The logarithmic decibel scale, $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(V/V_{ref})$, compresses this range into a manageable scale, where every **20 dB increase** represents a **tenfold** amplitude rise. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Acoustic Emission The generation of an AE signal is a four-stage physical chain that every KTU answer must clearly articulate: 1. **Source Activation:** An applied or residual stress field causes an irreversible microstructural event (e.g., dislocation movement, microcrack nucleation, fiber-matrix debonding). 2. **Rapid Energy Release:** The event releases stored elastic strain energy almost instantaneously (in microseconds), creating a *force pulse* at the source point. 3. **Wave Propagation:** The force pulse radiates elastic stress waves outward through the bulk of the material in multiple modes — **longitudinal (P-wave)**, **shear (S-wave)**, and **surface (Rayleigh) waves** — at the local sound velocity. 4. **Sensor Detection:** A piezoelectric transducer coupled to the surface converts the mechanical displacement into a proportional electrical voltage signal, which is amplified and digitized by the AE instrument. ## 2.2 Sources of Acoustic Emission Sources are broadly classified into two categories: - **Primary (mechanical) sources:** Crack initiation, crack growth, plastic deformation, fiber breakage in composites, delamination, debonding, martensitic phase transformation. - **Secondary (non-mechanical) sources:** Friction, impact, leakage (turbulent flow), loose parts, electrical discharge, cavitation, corrosion film rupture, and even **Kaiser and Felicity effects** in composite materials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kaiser Effect:** When a material is loaded to a certain stress level, unloaded, and reloaded, no significant AE is produced until the *previous maximum stress* is exceeded. This is the *acoustic memory* of the material and is the foundation of the **Felicity Ratio** test for composites. > **Felicity Effect:** If a material is held at a constant stress, AE may continue to occur below the previous maximum — this indicates damage accumulation and is a warning sign in service. ## 2.3 AE Signal Parameters (KTU High-Yield) The fundamental parameters of a single AE *hit* (burst signal) that students **must** know: - **Amplitude (A):** Peak voltage of the AE burst, measured in dB (referenced to 1 µV). - **Duration (D):** Time between the first and last threshold crossing of the burst. - **Rise Time (R):** Time from first threshold crossing to the peak amplitude. - **Count (N):** Number of threshold crossings during the burst. - **Counts to Peak (C_p):** Number of threshold crossings up to the peak. - **Energy (E):** Area under the rectified signal envelope (often measured in count-energy or eu). - **RMS Voltage:** Continuous root-mean-square of the signal over a time window (for continuous AE). - **Signal Strength:** Weighted combination of amplitude and counts, used in ASME Section V for vessel testing. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---------------------|-------------------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Amplitude in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | dB | Logarithmic measure of signal strength ($V_{ref}=1\,\mu V$) | | 2 | Longitudinal wave speed | $c_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1-\nu)(1+\nu)}}$ = $\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ for thin rods | m/s | Speed of P-wave in bulk material | | 3 | Shear wave speed | $c_S = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Speed of S-wave | | 4 | Plate (Lamb) wave speed | $c_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho(1-\nu^2)}}$ | m/s | Speed in thin plates (AE monitoring context) | | 5 | Source location (1-D linear) | $d_1 = \dfrac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m | Distance from sensor 1 to source | | 6 | Triangulation (zone method) | $\Delta t = \dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{c}$ | s | Time difference of arrival between sensors | | 7 | Felicity Ratio | $F.R. = \dfrac{\sigma_{AE}^{reload}}{\sigma_{max}^{previous}}$ | — | < 1.0 → damage; > 1.0 → healthy | | 8 | Kaiser Effect threshold | $F.R. \geq 1.0$ | — | No AE until previous max load exceeded | | 9 | Felicity Effect indicator | $F.R. < 1.0$ | — | Significant prior damage, AE before prev. max | | 10 | Calm Ratio | $C.R. = \dfrac{\text{AE at constant load}}{\text{AE during loading}}$ | — | < 0.05 typical, higher → critical damage | | 11 | AE Energy | $E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} V^2(t)\,dt$ | eu or aJ | Total radiated energy in a burst | | 12 | Attenuation (exponential) | $A(x) = A_0\,e^{-\alpha x}$ | dB/m | Geometric + material damping of AE wave | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All table cells use the parenthesis `/` style for absolute-value expressions and divisions to avoid breaking the markdown table. Use $\dfrac{}{}$ (display-style fractions) for clarity in the LaTeX rendering. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Acoustic Emission is the **only NDT method that can detect, locate, and assess the severity of *actively growing* defects in real time** across an entire structure. It is mandated by code for: - **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Article 12** — for in-service inspection of pressure vessels and nuclear containment. - **ASME Section XI (Inservice Inspection of Nuclear Power Plant Components)** — for monitoring of reactor vessels, piping, and spent fuel pools. - **Bridge and infrastructure monitoring** — continuous structural health monitoring of post-tensioned bridges and cable stays. - **Aerospace composite airframes** — Felicity Ratio and Calm Ratio testing per ASTM E1106/E2191. - **Pipelines and storage tanks** — leak detection, corrosion monitoring, and ground-floor stress testing of LNG tanks. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Amplitude in Decibels (dB) The decibel scale used in AE is referenced to **1 µV at the sensor output**, as standardized in ASTM E1106. Starting from the definition of the decibel: ### Step 1 — Definition of Decibel The decibel is defined as twenty times the base-ten logarithm of the ratio of the measured signal voltage to a reference voltage, because power is proportional to voltage squared and $20 \log_{10}(x) = 10 \log_{10}(x^2)$. $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V}{V_{ref}}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the AE Reference Voltage For AE testing, the convention is $V_{ref} = 1\,\mu V$. We can equivalently write the amplitude referenced to a 1 µV preamplifier output. $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\mu V}}{1\,\mu V}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Linear Worked Example Suppose a sensor output is **50 µV** (a typical small AE burst). Compute the amplitude: $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(50) = 20 \times 1.699 = 33.98 \approx 34\,\text{dB}$$ For a larger burst of **5000 µV = 5 mV**: $$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5000) = 20 \times 3.699 = 73.98 \approx 74\,\text{dB}$$ A 40 dB difference corresponds to a 100-fold voltage increase, since $20 \log_{10}(100) = 40$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Linear Source Location from Time-of-Arrival For a one-dimensional linear geometry (e.g., a long pipe or pressure vessel), two sensors S1 and S2 are placed a known distance $L$ apart, and an AE source is somewhere between them. ### Step 1 — Geometry of Linear Location Let the source be at distance $d_1$ from S1 and $d_2$ from S2. Clearly: $$d_1 + d_2 = L$$ ### Step 2 — Time of Flight Relationship The acoustic wave travels at speed $c$. Let the AE signal reach S1 at time $t_1$ and S2 at time $t_2$. The arrival times differ by: $$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{d_2 - d_1}{c}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve the Two-Equation System We now have two linear equations in the two unknowns $d_1$ and $d_2$: $$d_1 + d_2 = L$$ $$d_2 - d_1 = c \cdot \Delta t$$ Adding the two equations: $$2 d_2 = L + c \cdot \Delta t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_2 = \frac{L + c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ Subtracting them: $$2 d_1 = L - c \cdot \Delta t \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_1 = \frac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Location Formula $$\boxed{\,d_1 = \frac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}, \qquad d_2 = \frac{L + c \cdot \Delta t}{2}\,}$$ The sign of $\Delta t$ tells us which side of the midpoint the source is on: if $\Delta t > 0$, the source is closer to S1. ## 3.3 Derivation: Triangulation (Zone / Planar Location) For a 2-D plate or a 3-D vessel, the location problem becomes more complex. For the simplest 2-D triangulation case with **three sensors** S1, S2, S3 forming a triangle on a plate, the source (x, y) is solved from the three time-of-flight equations. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Time-of-Flight Equations For each sensor, with respect to a reference sensor S1: $$c\,(t_i - t_1) = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} - \sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2}, \quad i = 2, 3$$ ### Step 2 — Linearize via Squaring For sensor i, after squaring and simplifying, one obtains a linear equation of the form: $$a_i x + b_i y + c_i t_i = d_i$$ where $a_i, b_i, c_i$ depend only on the known sensor coordinates and the wave speed $c$. Two such equations from S2 and S3 (referenced to S1) yield a 2×2 linear system solvable for x and y. ### Step 3 — Closed-Form 2-D Solution If the sensors lie on the axes for simplicity (S1 at origin, S2 at (L, 0), S3 at (0, M)) and the wave is taken to be plate (Lamb) wave speed $c_p$: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta t_{21} &= t_2 - t_1 = \frac{1}{c_p}\left(\sqrt{(x-L)^2 + y^2} - \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}\right) \\ \Delta t_{31} &= t_3 - t_1 = \frac{1}{c_p}\left(\sqrt{x^2 + (y-M)^2} - \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}\right) \end{aligned}$$ These are two nonlinear equations in (x, y) that are solved numerically (Newton-Raphson) by the AE instrument in real time. KTU students should be able to **write the equations** but need not perform the iteration by hand. ## 3.4 Derivation: Felicity Ratio and Damage Assessment ### Step 1 — Definition The Felicity Ratio (F.R.) compares the load at which AE re-occurs on a *reloading* cycle to the *previous maximum* load: $$F.R. = \frac{\sigma_{AE}^{reload}}{\sigma_{max}^{previous}}$$ ### Step 2 — Kaiser Effect Condition (Healthy Material) If the material is undamaged, no AE appears until the previous maximum is exceeded. Hence $\sigma_{AE}^{reload} \geq \sigma_{max}^{previous}$, giving: $$F.R. \geq 1.0$$ This is the **Kaiser Effect**. ### Step 3 — Felicity Effect Condition (Damaged Material) If the material has accumulated sub-critical damage, AE re-occurs *before* the previous maximum is reached, so: $$F.R. < 1.0$$ The lower the F.R., the more severe the damage. Typical acceptance thresholds for fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP) vessels per ASME RTP-1 are: $$\begin{aligned} &F.R. \geq 0.95 \;\;\text{and}\;\; C.R. \leq 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Acceptable (Pass)} \\ &0.90 \leq F.R. < 0.95 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Conditional (Retest)} \\ &F.R. < 0.90 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Unacceptable (Fail / Major damage)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.5 Wave Velocity Derivations — Longitudinal and Shear ### Longitudinal (P-wave) Velocity in a Thin Rod For a long thin rod, the side surfaces are free, and the modulus is simply E. Newton's second law plus Hooke's law give: $$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{E}{\rho}\,\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$ ### Longitudinal Velocity in an Infinite Solid For a bulk (infinite) medium, lateral contraction is constrained by Poisson's ratio $\nu$: $$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$ ### Shear (S-wave) Velocity For pure shear deformation, the relevant modulus is the shear modulus G = E/[2(1+ν)]: $$c_S = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$$ ### Numerical Example: Steel For mild steel: $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 0.29$. $$\begin{aligned} c_L &= \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^9}{7850}} = \sqrt{2.548 \times 10^7} \approx 5048\,\text{m/s} \quad (\text{thin rod}) \\ c_L^{\infty} &= 5048 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.71}{1.29 \times 0.42}} \approx 5918\,\text{m/s} \quad (\text{bulk}) \\ c_S &= 5048 \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{2(1.29)}} \approx 3143\,\text{m/s} \end{aligned}$$ These are within 1% of the standard values $c_L = 5900\,\text{m/s}$ and $c_S = 3200\,\text{m/s}$ for steel. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: AE vs. Other NDT Methods | NDT Method | Active/Passive | Detects Active Defects | Locates Defect | Real-Time | Sensitivity to Micro-Damage | |------------|---------------|-----------------------|----------------|-----------|------------------------------| | Visual (VT) | Active | No (visual only) | No | Limited | None | | Liquid Penetrant (PT) | Active | No | No | No | Surface cracks only | | Magnetic Particle (MT) | Active | No | No | No | Surface & near-surface | | Eddy Current (ET) | Active | No | Yes (surface) | No | Surface only | | Radiography (RT) | Active | No | Yes (2-D) | No | Volumetric, planar only | | Ultrasonic (UT) | Active | Limited (active scanning) | Yes | Limited | Volumetric | | **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | **Passive** | **YES** | **YES** | **YES (continuous)** | **EXCELLENT** | This matrix is the *single most important comparative table* for any KTU 14-mark question that asks students to justify the use of AE over other NDT methods. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an AE Inspection System ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[SourceAE
Active Defect Mechanism] --> nodeB[WavePropagation
Elastic Stress Wave In Material] nodeB --> nodeC[PZTTransducers
Resonant Or Broadband Sensors] nodeC --> nodeD[Preamplifier
20 To 60 dB Gain Filter] nodeD --> nodeE[AEAnalyzerUnit
Threshold Crossing Time Detection] nodeE --> nodeF[ParameterExtraction
Amplitude Duration Counts Energy] nodeE --> nodeG[SourceLocation
Triangulation TDOA Algorithm] nodeF --> nodeH[DataLogger
Storage And Display] nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI[EvaluationModule
Kaiser Felicity Calm Ratios] nodeI --> nodeJ[AcceptanceDecision
Pass Retest Fail As Per Code] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** Every AE inspection, regardless of industry, follows this single pipeline. The *source* (defect) generates an elastic wave; the wave is *detected* by piezoelectric sensors; the *signal* is amplified and *analyzed* for both *parameters* and *location*; the data is *logged*; and the *evaluation* module computes the Felicity/Calm ratios to render an *accept/reject* decision. The arrows show the unidirectional flow of information and signal processing. ## 4.2 Process Flow: AE Inspection of a Pressure Vessel (ASME Section V) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Vessel Preparation
Surface Clean Couplant Apply] --> nodeB[SensorPlacement
Pre-Mapped Triangulation Array] nodeB --> nodeC[SystemCalibration
Pencil Lead Break Hsu Nielson Source] nodeC --> nodeD[BackgroundNoiseCheck
Verify Threshold Above Ambient] nodeD --> nodeE[LoadApplication
Hydrostatic Pressure In Steps] nodeE --> nodeF[ContinuousAEMonitoring
Record Hits Counts Amplitude] nodeF --> nodeG{AcceptanceCriteria
Felicity And Calm Ratios} nodeG --> nodeH[Pass: Vessel Fit For Service] nodeG --> nodeI[Retest: Apply Hold Period Re-evaluate] nodeG --> nodeJ[Fail: Localize Defect Recommend Repair] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Array Geometry for Triangulation ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[SourcePoint
Active Defect] -->|Distance D1| nodeB[SensorS1
Coordinates X1 Y1] nodeA -->|Distance D2| nodeC[SensorS2
Coordinates X2 Y2] nodeA -->|Distance D3| nodeD[SensorS3
Coordinates X3 Y3] nodeB -->|T1 Arrival Time| nodeE[AEInstrument
TDOA Processor] nodeC -->|T2 Arrival Time| nodeE nodeD -->|T3 Arrival Time| nodeE nodeE -->|Solved XY Location| nodeF[Display
Source Marker On Structure Map] ``` **Explanation of the Geometry Block Diagram:** The three sensors S1, S2, S3 are placed on the structure surface in a non-collinear triangle. When the defect at the source point emits a stress wave, the wave reaches each sensor at a slightly different time $T_1, T_2, T_3$. The AE instrument measures the differences in arrival times $\Delta t_{21} = T_2 - T_1$ and $\Delta t_{31} = T_3 - T_1$. The triangulation equations derived in Section 3.3 are then solved to produce the (X, Y) coordinates of the source. The display then overlays a marker at the computed location on a 2-D or 3-D CAD map of the structure. ## 4.4 AE Signal Burst Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[AEBurstSignal
Single Acoustic Emission Event] --> nodeB[ThresholdCrossing
First Time Signal Exceeds V Threshold] nodeB --> nodeC[RiseTime
Duration To Reach Peak Amplitude] nodeC --> nodeD[PeakAmplitude
Maximum Voltage In dB Referenced To 1 Microvolt] nodeD --> nodeE[DecayPhase
Signal Falls Below Threshold] nodeE --> nodeF[TotalDuration
Time From First To Last Threshold Crossing] nodeF --> nodeG[CountsParameter
Number Of Threshold Crossings] nodeF --> nodeH[EnergyParameter
Area Under Rectified Envelope] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** Every discrete AE "hit" follows the canonical shape shown above. The four time-domain parameters (rise time, duration, counts, energy) and the peak amplitude together form a unique "fingerprint" that can be used to discriminate between source types — for instance, pencil-lead-break calibration signals, leak signals, and crack-growth signals all have characteristic signatures stored in the AE analyzer's classification library. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define acoustic emission.
