List any four sources of acoustic emission in engineering materials.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** Acoustic Emission (AE) is the phenomenon of generation of transient elastic stress waves in a material due to the rapid release of energy from a localized source caused by irreversible microstructural changes such as plastic deformation, crack initiation/growth, fiber breakage, delamination, or phase transformation (ASTM E1316). > > **Four Sources of AE:** > > 1. **Crack initiation and growth** in metals and composites (the most important source for NDT). > 2. **Plastic deformation** in ductile materials (dislocation glide, twinning). > 3. **Fiber breakage and matrix cracking** in fiber-reinforced composites. > 4. **Leak-induced turbulence** in pressurized fluid systems (acoustic emission of liquids and gases). > > *(Other acceptable answers: friction between mating surfaces, debonding, martensitic phase transformation, cavitation, electrical discharge.)* ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the Kaiser Effect? How is it related to the Felicity Ratio in acoustic emission testing of composite pressure vessels?** **Model Answer:** > **Kaiser Effect:** When a material is loaded to a certain stress level, unloaded, and then reloaded, no significant acoustic emission is observed until the previous maximum stress level is exceeded. The material exhibits an "acoustic memory" of the highest stress previously applied. This is the Kaiser Effect, first reported by Joseph Kaiser in 1950. > > **Relation to Felicity Ratio:** The Felicity Ratio is a quantitative measure of the departure from the Kaiser Effect and is defined as: > > $$F.R. = \frac{\text{Stress at which AE re-occurs on reloading}}{\text{Previous maximum stress}}$$ > > For a material exhibiting a *perfect* Kaiser Effect, $F.R. \geq 1.0$, indicating no significant damage. If $F.R. < 1.0$, the Kaiser Effect is violated (the Felicity Effect is observed), and the lower the ratio, the more severe the accumulated damage. Thus, the Felicity Ratio is a *damage severity indicator* based on the *Kaiser Effect memory test* and is the standard acceptance criterion for FRP vessels per ASME RTP-1 and ASTM E1106. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 4] **(a)** Explain the fundamental principle of Acoustic Emission Testing. Describe the working of a piezoelectric AE transducer with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With suitable equations, describe the linear and triangulation methods for locating an AE source. A vessel wall has two AE sensors S1 and S2 separated by 2 m, and an AE source produces a time difference of arrival $\Delta t = +50\,\mu s$ (S2 receives later). If the longitudinal wave speed in steel is 5900 m/s, calculate the distance of the source from S1. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): > **Principle of AE Testing:** Acoustic Emission Testing is a *passive* NDT technique based on the principle that when a material is subjected to mechanical or thermal stress, microstructural changes (such as crack growth, plastic deformation, or fiber failure) release stored elastic strain energy in the form of **transient elastic stress waves** (typically in the 20 kHz – 1 MHz range). These waves travel through the structure and are detected by piezoelectric sensors coupled to the surface. The AE instrument records the parameters of the bursts (amplitude, counts, duration, energy) and may also use the time-of-arrival at multiple sensors to **locate the source**. The technique detects only *active* (growing or moving) defects and is therefore ideal for in-service structural health monitoring. > > **Piezoelectric Transducer Working:** A piezoelectric AE sensor contains a piezo-ceramic disc (commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate, PZT) polarized in the thickness direction. When the elastic wave reaches the sensor, the mechanical vibration compresses and stretches the piezo element. Due to the direct piezoelectric effect, this produces a proportional electrical charge/voltage across the electrodes. A wear-plate protects the disc, and a backing material damps the resonance to control the bandwidth. The block diagram is: > > **Block Diagram of an AE System (full architecture):** > > $$\text{AE Source} \rightarrow \text{Stress Wave} \rightarrow \text{PZT Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Preamplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{AE Analyzer} \rightarrow \text{Display}$$ > > **Key Sub-Blocks:** > > - **PZT Sensor (resonant or broadband):** Converts mechanical displacement to voltage. > - **Preamplifier (40 dB typical gain):** Boosts the µV-level signal to a workable mV level and provides low-impedance drive. > - **Bandpass Filter (20 kHz – 1 MHz):** Rejects mechanical and electrical noise. > - **Threshold Detector:** Identifies the start of an AE burst. > - **Parameter Extractor:** Measures amplitude, duration, counts, energy, rise time. > - **Time Measurement (TDC):** Provides arrival times for source location. #### Model Solution for (b): > **Linear Source Location (1-D):** > > For two sensors S1 and S2 separated by distance L on a long linear structure, the source location is given by: > > $$d_1 = \frac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}, \qquad d_2 = \frac{L + c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ > > where $d_1$ and $d_2$ are the distances from the source to S1 and S2 respectively, $c$ is the wave speed, and $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ is the difference in arrival times. > > **Triangulation Method (2-D Plate):** > > For a planar structure, three (or more) sensors are placed in a triangle. The source coordinates (x, y) are solved from the system of equations: > > $$c\,(t_i - t_1) = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} - \sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2}, \quad i = 2, 3$$ > > These two equations in (x, y) are solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson) by the AE instrument in real time. > > **Numerical Calculation:** > > Given: $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $\Delta t = +50\,\mu s = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}$, $c = 5900\,\text{m/s}$. > > $$c \cdot \Delta t = 5900 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.295\,\text{m}$$ > > $$d_1 = \frac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{2 - 0.295}{2} = \frac{1.705}{2} = 0.8525\,\text{m}$$ > > $$\boxed{d_1 \approx 0.853\,\text{m from S1}}$$ > > **Interpretation:** Since $\Delta t > 0$ (S2 receives the wave later), the source is closer to S1, which is consistent with $d_1 = 0.853\,\text{m} < L/2 = 1.0\,\text{m}$. The distance from S2 would be $d_2 = 1.148\,\text{m}$, and the two sum to $L = 2\,\text{m}$ as required. ### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 4 — Alternative Choice] **(a)** What is the Felicity Ratio? Explain how the Felicity Ratio and Calm Ratio are used to assess the integrity of a fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP) pressure vessel during acoustic emission testing. Give the typical acceptance criteria. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare Acoustic Emission Testing with Ultrasonic Testing in terms of principle, defect detectability, real-time capability, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): > **Felicity Ratio Definition:** The Felicity Ratio (F.R.) is defined as the ratio of the stress at which significant acoustic emission re-appears on reloading to the maximum stress previously applied: > > $$F.R. = \frac{\sigma_{AE}^{reload}}{\sigma_{max}^{previous}}$$ > > **Use in FRP Vessel Integrity Assessment:** The vessel is subjected to a *proof load* cycle, then unloaded. The vessel is *reloaded* in steps, and the load at which AE first re-appears is noted. The Felicity Ratio is then calculated. In a *healthy* FRP vessel exhibiting the **Kaiser Effect**, $F.R. \geq 1.0$. If the vessel has matrix microcracking, delamination, or fiber-matrix debonding, the Kaiser Effect is *violated* (Felicity Effect observed), and AE re-occurs at a lower load, giving $F.R. < 1.0$. > > **Calm Ratio:** The Calm Ratio (C.R.) is the ratio of the AE count rate at a *constant hold* stress to the AE count rate during the *immediately preceding* loading step. It indicates whether the damage is still actively progressing after loading is complete. > > $$C.R. = \frac{\text{AE rate at constant load (hold)}}{\text{AE rate during loading}}$$ > > **Typical Acceptance Criteria (ASME RTP-1 / ASTM E2191 for FRP vessels):** > > | Parameter | Pass | Conditional (Retest) | Fail | > |-----------|------|----------------------|------| > | Felicity Ratio (F.R.) | $\geq 0.95$ | $0.90 \leq F.R. < 0.95$ | $< 0.90$ | > | Calm Ratio (C.R.) | $\leq 0.05$ | $0.05 < C.R. \leq 0.10$ | $> 0.10$ | > | Composite criterion | Both Pass | Either conditional | Either fails | > > The vessel is deemed fit for service only if *both* F.R. $\geq 0.95$ and C.R. $\leq 0.05$ are met. A conditional rating requires a hold-and-retest after a defined relaxation period (e.g., 1 hour) to allow for any viscoelastic recovery. Failure mandates repair or scrapping. #### Model Solution for (b): > **Comparison Table: AE vs. UT** > > | Parameter | Acoustic Emission (AE) | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | > |-----------|------------------------|------------------------| > | **Principle** | Passive — listens to elastic waves *emitted by the defect itself* during stress. | Active — *transmits* a pulse and receives the echo from a discontinuity. | > | **Energy Source** | The defect (released strain energy). | The UT probe (piezo transmitter). | > | **Defect Type Detected** | Only *active* (growing) defects; dynamic processes. | All geometric discontinuities (active or dormant), including cracks, voids, delaminations. | > | **Defect Locatability** | Yes — by triangulation from multiple sensors, in real time. | Yes — by echo time-of-flight, requires manual or automated scanning. | > | **Real-Time / Continuous Monitoring** | Yes — continuous, full-volume coverage. | No — point-by-point scanning required. | > | **Sensitivity to Micro-Damage** | Excellent — detects sub-millimeter fiber breakage or microcracking. | Limited by wavelength and probe frequency; may miss sub-wavelength features. | > | **Background Noise Sensitivity** | High — mechanical and electrical noise must be filtered. | Moderate — couplant and surface preparation help. | > | **Loading Requirement** | Yes — structure must be stressed (e.g., hydrotest). | No — performed at rest. | > | **Sensor Coupling** | Permanent or semi-permanent mounting; couplant required. | Manual scanning with couplant gel. | > | **Coverage Speed (full structure)** | Fast — whole structure monitored in one test. | Slow — every square inch must be scanned. | > | **Typical Applications** | Pressure vessel in-service monitoring; bridge SHM; FRP vessel Felicity test; leak detection; pipeline integrity. | Weld inspection; thickness gauging; flaw sizing; bond testing. | > | **Code References** | ASME Section V Article 12; ASME Section XI; ASTM E1106/E2191. | ASME Section V Article 4; ASTM E114, E164, E213. | > > **Conclusion:** AE and UT are *complementary*, not competitive. UT is the *static* defect detection workhorse; AE is the *dynamic* defect severity monitor. For *in-service* integrity assessment of critical pressure equipment, AE is the only NDT method that directly answers the question, *"Is anything growing right now, and where?"* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > - **Do NOT confuse AE with UT.** AE is *passive* (the defect is the source); UT is *active* (the probe is the source). Writing "AE uses an ultrasonic probe to inject sound into the material" will cost **at least 3 marks**. > - **Always mention the Kaiser Effect** when discussing Felicity Ratio — the two are inseparable in any KTU 14-mark question. > - **Do NOT state the wave speed formula incorrectly.** The longitudinal wave speed in a thin rod is $c_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$, while in bulk it is $\sqrt{E(1-\nu)/[\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]}$. Mixing the two is a 2-mark penalty. > - **Numerical problems require units at every step.** Showing "$c \cdot \Delta t = 0.295$" without units (m) is a typical 1-mark deduction. > - **In triangulation, do not assume linear geometry.** The 1-D formula $d_1 = (L - c\Delta t)/2$ applies only to *two-sensor linear* arrays. For 2-D plates, three (or four) sensors and the non-linear TDOA system are required. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire)** > > - AE is a **PASSIVE** NDT method — the defect itself emits the elastic wave. > - The **frequency range** of AE is **20 kHz – 1 MHz** (above human hearing). > - The **ASTM reference** for AE amplitude is **0 dB = 1 µV at the sensor output**. > - The **longitudinal wave speed** in steel is **≈ 5900 m/s**; in aluminum **≈ 6320 m/s**. > - **Two key AE parameters** for code-compliance testing: **Felicity Ratio** and **Calm Ratio**. > - **Kaiser Effect:** No AE on reloading until the previous maximum stress is exceeded — F.R. ≥ 1.0. > - **Felicity Effect:** AE re-occurs below the previous maximum — F.R. < 1.0 — indicates prior damage. > - **ASME RTP-1 acceptance:** F.R. ≥ 0.95 AND Calm Ratio ≤ 0.05. > - **Source location in 1-D:** $d_1 = (L - c \Delta t)/2$ with two sensors; sign of $\Delta t$ indicates side. > - **Source location in 2-D/3-D:** **Triangulation** with at least three sensors and TDOA algorithm. > - **AE sensors are piezoelectric (PZT)** — they convert mechanical displacement to voltage. > - **ASME code references:** Section V Article 12 (in-service); Section XI (nuclear); ASTM E1106/E2191 (FRP). > - **Major advantage of AE:** It detects **active, growing defects in real time** over the *entire structure* — no other NDT method does this. > - **Major limitations of AE:** Requires a **load** to activate defects; high **noise sensitivity**; needs **calibration** (pencil-lead break — Hsu–Nielson source); cannot size defects directly. > - **Calibration source:** The **Hsu–Nielson source** (0.5 mm HB lead, 2.5 mm protrusion, broken at 30° to surface) produces a standard AE signal used to verify sensor coupling and system response. > - **AE monitoring is mandated** for in-service inspection of nuclear pressure vessels, LNG tanks, and FRP chemical process vessels. > - The four canonical AE parameters per burst are: **Amplitude, Counts, Duration, Rise Time** — plus **Energy**. > - **Signal strength** (ASTM E1106) is the weighted count-amplitude product used in ASME Section V for vessel testing. > - **Wave attenuation** in AE follows $A(x) = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ — high-frequency AE attenuates faster, so **low-frequency (50–100 kHz) sensors** are used for long-range monitoring of large structures like bridges.

topics.title: Thermography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thermography** (also called *Infrared Thermography* or *Thermal Imaging NDT*) is a non-contact, non-destructive testing technique that maps the surface temperature distribution of a test object using the infrared (IR) radiation emitted by the body. Any discontinuity (cracks, delamination, voids, inclusions, corrosion under insulation, or moisture ingress) modifies the local heat flow path, producing a measurable temperature contrast on the surface which is captured and visualised as a **thermogram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Thermography is a NDT method based on the principle that every object above absolute zero (0 K / -273.15 °C) emits electromagnetic radiation in the infrared region (0.78 µm – 1000 µm), and the intensity & spectral distribution of this radiation is a function of surface temperature and emissivity. Defects alter this radiation pattern, enabling detection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Fever Scanner"

Imagine using a **forehead IR thermometer** on a patient: it measures the *heat* coming off the skin to detect fever, without touching the body. Thermography in NDT works on exactly the same idea — but for engineering components:

- **Healthy patient (sound material):** Uniform heat signature → smooth, even "thermal image".
- **Sick patient (defective zone):** A crack, void, or delamination acts like a "hot spot" or "cold spot" because it blocks or accelerates the normal heat flow — just as a tumour shows up as a warmer region on a medical IR scan.
- **Camera role:** The IR camera is the "doctor's eyes" — converting invisible IR energy into a visible colour-coded map (thermogram).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Thermography doesn't "see" the defect itself. It sees the **thermal fingerprint** (temperature disturbance) that the defect *creates*. This is why thermography is often called a *global survey* technique — it scans large areas rapidly.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ) | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** | Total radiated power |
| Wien's displacement constant (b) | **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K** | Peak IR wavelength |
| Absolute zero | **0 K / -273.15 °C** | Reference for thermal emission |
| IR atmospheric window (MWIR) | **3 – 5 µm** | Best transmission band |
| IR atmospheric window (LWIR) | **8 – 14 µm** | Best transmission band |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle — Why Objects Emit IR

When atoms and molecules in a material vibrate (translational, rotational, vibrational motion), they emit electromagnetic radiation. At **temperatures above absolute zero**, this emission falls dominantly in the **infrared (IR) band** of the electromagnetic spectrum. The governing physics rests on three foundational laws of blackbody radiation.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Thermal Stimulation** — The test object is either in a naturally heated state (Passive Thermography) or externally heated (Active Thermography) using a flash lamp, halogen heater, ultrasonic probe, or hot water spray.
2. **Heat Propagation** — Heat flows through the solid via conduction. Where defects exist, thermal conductivity changes locally, modifying the heat flow.
3. **Surface Emission** — The surface emits IR radiation proportional to T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann).
4. **IR Detection** — An IR camera (focal plane array of microbolometers or photon detectors) captures the emitted radiation.
5. **Signal Processing** — Radiance is converted to temperature using Planck's law inversion, accounting for emissivity (ε) and background reflection.
6. **Visualisation** — Output displayed as a colour-coded thermogram on a monitor.
7. **Defect Recognition** — A trained thermographer interprets thermal contrasts (ΔT) to identify, size, and characterise flaws.

## 2.3 Types of Thermography

### A. Passive Thermography
- The component is **naturally at a different temperature** from its surroundings.
- **No external heat source** is applied.
- Examples: building envelope inspection (heat leakage), electrical hot spots, furnace liners, refractory monitoring.

### B. Active Thermography
- An **external thermal stimulus** is intentionally applied to provoke a thermal contrast.
- Sub-types:
  - **Pulsed Thermography (PT)** — Brief flash (milliseconds), then observe transient cooling.
  - **Lock-in Thermography** — Sinusoidal modulated heating; phase image analysis.
  - **Vibrothermography** — Ultrasound excites friction at crack faces → localised heat.
  - **Microwave Thermography** — Microwave heating of dielectric materials.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** Inside the table, do **NOT** use the `|` pipe symbol (it breaks markdown tables). All absolute-value bars use `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Variable Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $E_b$ = emissive power (W/m²), $\sigma$ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴), $T$ = absolute temp (K) | Total IR power emitted by a blackbody |
| 2 | **Grey-body Emissivity** | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $E$ = actual emissive power, $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0 to 1) | Real materials are grey bodies, not perfect blackbodies |
| 3 | **Net Heat Exchange** | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $q$ = net heat flux, $T_1$, $T_2$ = body & surroundings | Used in IR camera calibration |
| 4 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | $\lambda_{max}$ = peak wavelength (m), $b$ = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K | Selects IR detector spectral band |
| 5 | **Planck's Radiation Law** | $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]}$ | $C_1, C_2$ = Planck constants | Spectral radiance distribution |
| 6 | **Emissivity Definition** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E}{E_b}$ | $E$ = actual radiance, $E_b$ = blackbody radiance | Determines how "IR-visible" a surface is |
| 7 | **Thermal Diffusivity** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat | Governs heat flow in active thermography |
| 8 | **1-D Heat Diffusion Time** | $t \approx \dfrac{d^2}{\alpha}$ | $d$ = depth of defect, $\alpha$ = diffusivity (m²/s) | **Inspection time planning** in pulsed thermography |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** Aerospace composite inspections (CFRP wing skins), power plant condition monitoring (boiler tubes, refractory), electronics (PCB hot spots), petrochemical (corrosion under insulation — CUI), civil engineering (concrete delamination in bridges).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak IR Wavelength from a Heated Specimen (Wien's Displacement Law)

**Problem:** A turbine blade in service operates at 1100 K. A thermographer needs to choose the correct IR detector band. Determine the peak emission wavelength and identify the appropriate IR window.

**Given:**
- Surface temperature, $T = 1100$ K
- Wien's constant, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K

**Step 1 — State Wien's Displacement Law:**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}}{1100\ \text{K}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator explicitly:**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{3}}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898}{1.1} \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\lambda_{max} = 2.6345 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 2.63\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (KTU Board Style):**

Since $2.63\ \mu\text{m}$ lies inside the **MWIR (Mid-Wave IR) atmospheric window (3 – 5 µm)**, an InSb or HgCdTe (MCT) photon detector is suitable. For NDT of cooler engineering components (RT to 400 K), the LWIR (8 – 14 µm) band with a microbolometer is preferred.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radiative Heat Transfer Between a Defective Spot and the Surroundings

**Problem:** A delamination in a composite panel (with surface coating of $\varepsilon = 0.92$) creates a "hot spot" at 348 K, while the surrounding sound region is at 323 K. Calculate the net IR energy emitted by the hot spot relative to the surroundings, per square metre.

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 348$ K (hot spot)
- $T_2 = 323$ K (surroundings)
- $\varepsilon = 0.92$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)

**Step 1 — Write the net radiative exchange equation:**

$$
q = \varepsilon\,\sigma\,(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_1^{4}$:**

$$
T_1^{4} = (348)^{4}
$$

$$
T_1^{4} = 348 \times 348 \times 348 \times 348
$$

$$
T_1^{4} = 1.46 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_2^{4}$:**

$$
T_2^{4} = (323)^{4}
$$

$$
T_2^{4} = 1.086 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference:**

$$
T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = (1.46 - 1.086) \times 10^{10} = 3.74 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $\varepsilon$ and $\sigma$:**

$$
q = 0.92 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.74 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
q = 0.92 \times 5.67 \times 37.4
$$

$$
q = 0.92 \times 212.06
$$

$$
q = 195.1\ \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation (mandatory in KTU answer scripts):**

A net flux of **~195 W/m²** is a very high contrast for an IR camera; even microbolometers with NETD < 50 mK can detect such a hot spot easily. This is the basis of **passive thermography of electrical faults and refractory hot spots** in boilers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inspection Time in Pulsed Thermography (Depth of Detection)

**Problem:** A carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) panel has thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. We wish to detect a sub-surface delamination at depth $d = 1$ mm = $10^{-3}$ m. Calculate the optimum time window for inspection.

**Step 1 — Use the characteristic diffusion time relation:**

$$
t \approx \frac{d^{2}}{\alpha}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
t \approx \frac{(10^{-3})^{2}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$
(10^{-3})^{2} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$
t \approx \frac{10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.2\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The optimal inspection window is **0.2 s after the heat flash** for a 1 mm deep defect. For deeper defects the wait time grows quadratically: a 2 mm defect requires ~0.8 s; a 5 mm defect requires ~5 s. This is why **pulsed thermography is best suited for near-surface defects (0.1 – 5 mm)** in composites.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Active Thermography Modes

| Mode | Energy Source | Best Depth | Inspection Time | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pulsed Thermography | Xenon flash | 0.1 – 5 mm | ms to s | Composite delamination, CFRP |
| Lock-in Thermography | Modulated halogen | 0.5 – 10 mm | seconds–minutes | Aerospace honeycomb |
| Vibrothermography | Ultrasound (20 kHz) | Surface cracks | continuous | Friction-fit cracks in metals |
| Microwave Thermography | Microwave (GHz) | 1 – 50 mm | seconds | Dielectric materials, GFRP |
| Eddy Current Thermography | Induced eddy currents | 0.5 – 3 mm | seconds | Surface cracks in conductors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermographic NDT System

The following Mermaid diagram represents the end-to-end signal and energy flow in an IR thermography inspection, mapping each subsystem and the interactions among them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Specimen Under Test] --> B{Defect Present?}
    B -- No --> C1[Uniform Heat Flow]
    B -- Yes --> C2[Distorted Heat Flow]
    A --> D[Thermal Stimulus Source]
    D --> D1[Flash Lamp / Halogen / US Probe / MW]
    D1 --> A
    C1 --> E[Surface IR Emission]
    C2 --> E
    E --> F[IR Optics Lens Germanium or ZnSe]
    F --> G[IR Detector Array MWIR 3-5 um or LWIR 8-14 um]
    G --> H[Read Out Integrated Circuit ROIC]
    H --> I[Signal Processor ADC and Calibration]
    I --> J[Emissivity Correction Module]
    J --> K[False Colour Thermogram Display]
    K --> L[Thermographer Analysis]
    L --> M[Defect Characterisation and Report]
    P1[Background Radiation] --> F
    P2[Atmospheric Attenuation] --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node-Id Safety Rule Applied:** Every identifier is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `SpecimenUnderTest` → `A`, etc.). No reserved words used. Labels are clean uppercase text, no bold/italic inside double-quoted strings.

## 4.2 Decision Logic — Active vs. Passive Thermography

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[NDT Problem Received] --> Q1{Is component self-heated or thermally different from surroundings?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Passive[Passive Thermography - No external source - Inspect immediately]
    Q1 -- No --> Active[Active Thermography - Apply external stimulus]
    Active --> Q2{What is defect depth?}
    Q2 -- Surface to 1 mm --> PT[Pulsed Thermography - Flash lamp]
    Q2 -- 1 to 5 mm --> LI[Lock-in Thermography - Modulated heating]
    Q2 -- Metal surface cracks --> VT[Vibrothermography - Ultrasonic excitation]
    Passive --> Inspect[Capture Thermogram and Analyse]
    PT --> Inspect
    LI --> Inspect
    VT --> Inspect
    Inspect --> Result[Defect Map and Report]
```

## 4.3 Functional Workflow Sequence (Operational Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Surface Preparation and Emissivity Setting] --> S2[Step 2 Mount Camera at 0.5 to 1 m]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Focus and Set Thermal Range]
    S3 --> S4{Active or Passive Mode?}
    S4 -- Passive --> S5[Step 4a Begin Recording Thermogram]
    S4 -- Active --> S6[Step 4b Apply Heat Pulse]
    S6 --> S5
    S5 --> S7[Step 5 Capture Image Sequence at Fixed Frame Rate]
    S7 --> S8[Step 6 Post Process: Subtraction, Phase Image, FFT]
    S8 --> S9[Step 7 Identify Thermal Anomalies and Classify Defect]
    S9 --> S10[Step 8 Report and Documentation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks each (short answer). Part B = 14 marks with internal choice (typically sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each).

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Style]**
**"Define Thermography and list any four advantages of IR thermography as an NDT method." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Thermography is a non-contact, non-destructive testing method that uses infrared radiation emitted by a body to map its surface temperature distribution and identify subsurface discontinuities.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Non-contact** — No physical coupling or couplant needed; safe for hot, moving, or energised components.
2. **Large area coverage** — A single image covers several square metres; ideal for global surveys.
3. **Real-time results** — Thermograms displayed live on a monitor; rapid turnaround.
4. **Two-sided access not required** — Only one side of the component needs to be visible (single-sided inspection).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Style]**
**"State Stefan-Boltzmann law and Wien's displacement law. Give one application of each. (3 Marks)"**

**Model Answer:**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature, i.e., $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴). **Application:** Calculation of IR detector responsivity and camera temperature calibration.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** The peak wavelength of emission of a blackbody is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature, i.e., $\lambda_{max} = b / T$, where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. **Application:** Selection of the correct IR detector band (MWIR vs LWIR) for a given service temperature.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Option A
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Style, 14 Marks]**

**"Explain the working principle of Infrared Thermography. With neat block diagrams, describe the various components of a thermographic NDT system. Also, discuss active and passive thermography with suitable examples. (14 Marks)"**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle of IR Thermography:**

Every material above absolute zero (0 K) emits electromagnetic radiation in the infrared region. The intensity and spectral distribution of this emitted IR radiation are governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** ($E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$) and **Planck's radiation law**. When an IR-active defect (crack, void, delamination) is present inside a test object, it locally alters the heat flow path, producing a measurable temperature difference $\Delta T$ on the surface. An IR-sensitive camera detects this $\Delta T$ and displays it as a colour-coded image called a **thermogram**.

**Components of a Thermographic NDT System (Block Description):**

1. **Infrared Optics** — Germanium or Zinc Selenide (ZnSe) lenses; transparent to IR (3–14 µm) and block visible light.
2. **IR Detector Array** — Focal Plane Array (FPA); two main types:
   - **Photon (Quantum) Detectors:** InSb, HgCdTe (MCT) — fast, high sensitivity, require cryogenic cooling (77 K).
   - **Thermal Detectors:** Microbolometers (VOₓ or amorphous Si) — uncooled, lower cost, common in NDT.
3. **Read-Out Integrated Circuit (ROIC)** — Multiplexes detector signals to the electronics.
4. **Signal Processing Electronics** — ADC, noise filtering, emissivity compensation, atmospheric correction.
5. **Display & Storage** — LCD monitor, real-time thermogram, image archive.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Active vs. Passive Thermography:**

| Aspect | Active Thermography | Passive Thermography |
|---|---|---|
| External heat source | **Required** (flash, halogen, ultrasound) | **Not required** |
| Operating principle | Apply stimulus, observe transient response | Component is already at a temperature different from surroundings |
| Typical examples | Pulsed thermography of CFRP composites, lock-in thermography of honeycomb | Furnace refractory inspection, electrical hot-spot detection, building heat-loss survey |
| Detection capability | Subsurface defects (depth 0.1 – 10 mm) | Surface anomalies only |
| Equipment | Heat source + camera + synchroniser | Camera only |

**Suitable Examples:**

- **Active (Pulsed Thermography):** Inspection of a carbon-fibre composite wing skin for impact damage; a xenon flash lamp illuminates the surface and a 1–2 mm deep delamination appears as a hot spot during cooling.
- **Passive (Electrical):** Inspection of a high-voltage busbar in a substation; a loose contact shows up as an abnormally hot region (>60 °C) compared to neighbouring connections.

---

### Question B1 — Option B (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Style, 14 Marks]**

**"Discuss the fundamental laws of infrared radiation used in thermography. With derivations, show how (i) the peak wavelength of emission depends on temperature, and (ii) how emissivity affects the total radiated energy. (14 Marks)"**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Fundamental Laws of Infrared Radiation:**

1. **Planck's Radiation Law** — Defines the spectral emissive power $E_{\lambda}$ of a blackbody as a function of wavelength and temperature:

$$
E_{\lambda} = \frac{C_{1}}{\lambda^{5}\,\left[e^{C_{2}/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]}
$$

where $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16}$ W·m² and $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K. This law forms the basis of IR camera temperature measurement.

2. **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** — Total emitted energy per unit area:

$$
E_b = \sigma T^{4}
$$

3. **Wien's Displacement Law** — Peak wavelength of emission (derived by differentiating Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and equating to zero):

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}, \quad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}
$$

4. **Kirchhoff's Law** — At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. A good emitter is also a good absorber.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Peak Wavelength Derivation:**
By differentiating Planck's spectral distribution with respect to $\lambda$ and setting $\dfrac{dE_{\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$, we obtain the transcendental equation whose numerical solution gives:

$$
\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}
$$

**Example:** At T = 600 K, $\lambda_{max} = 4.83\ \mu\text{m}$ (MWIR window).

**(ii) Effect of Emissivity on Total Radiated Energy:**
A real material (grey body) emits only a fraction of blackbody radiation, defined by:

$$
E = \varepsilon \cdot E_b = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^{4}
$$

For polished aluminium, $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$, so reflected ambient radiation dominates the IR signal — leading to large errors. For anodised aluminium, $\varepsilon \approx 0.85$, making thermography reliable. **Therefore, surface preparation (painting with high-$\varepsilon$ coating or applying soot) is often essential for accurate quantitative thermography.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the T⁴ dependence** — Many students write Stefan-Boltzmann as $E = \sigma T$ instead of $T^4$. This single mistake costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing MWIR (3–5 µm) with LWIR (8–14 µm)** — LWIR is for room-temperature NDT (~300 K); MWIR is for high-temperature components (>500 K). Mixing these up is a common **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Not mentioning emissivity correction** — A thermography answer that does not discuss emissivity ($\varepsilon$) is **incomplete** and loses at least **2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping block diagram** — KTU examiners expect a labelled block diagram of the IR camera system; absence costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Thermography** = IR-based NDT that maps surface temperature to reveal defects.
- **Every body above 0 K emits IR** — governed by Stefan-Boltzmann ($E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$) and Planck's law.
- **Wien's Law** $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ → choose MWIR (3–5 µm) for hot parts, LWIR (8–14 µm) for ambient.
- **Two modes:** *Passive* (no external heat) vs *Active* (flash / halogen / ultrasound / microwave).
- **Common active modes:** Pulsed, Lock-in, Vibro-, Microwave, Eddy-Current Thermography.
- **Inspection time** for pulsed thermography: $t \approx d^2 / \alpha$ — deeper defects need longer wait.
- **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ governs how fast heat spreads in the material.
- **Atmospheric windows** (3–5 µm, 8–14 µm) — IR camera must operate in one of these to avoid atmospheric absorption.
- **Detector types** — Photon (cooled, InSb, MCT) vs Thermal (uncooled, microbolometer).
- **Emissivity** is critical: $\varepsilon$ close to 1 (matte black) gives accurate temperature; $\varepsilon$ close to 0 (polished metal) gives large errors.
- **Key applications:** Composite delamination, electrical hot spots, corrosion under insulation, refractory monitoring, building envelope heat loss, PCB fault detection, weld inspection.
- **Advantages:** Non-contact, two-sided access not required, large area scan, real-time, safe for live equipment.
- **Limitations:** Surface-only sensitivity, emissivity dependence, shallow depth penetration, requires temperature contrast, expensive cooled cameras for high sensitivity.
- **Always remember:** Thermography doesn't see the defect — it sees the **thermal fingerprint** that the defect creates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT521 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computed Tomography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Computed Tomography (CT)** is an advanced, image-based Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) and Evaluation (NDE) technique that generates **two-dimensional (2D) cross-sectional images** and **three-dimensional (3D) volumetric reconstructions** of the internal structure of an object. It operates on the principle of measuring the spatial distribution of the **linear X-ray attenuation coefficient** $\mu(x, y, z)$ from a large set of one-dimensional X-ray transmission profiles (called *projections* or *sinograms*) acquired at multiple angular positions around the object, and reconstructing them mathematically using algorithms such as **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** or iterative methods based on the **Radon Transform**.

Mathematically, CT is the numerical solution of the inverse problem of the **Beer–Lambert Law** integrated along a line $\mathcal{L}$ of length $L$:

$$I = I_0 \exp\left(-\int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(x,y)\, ds\right)$$

where $I_0$ is the incident X-ray intensity, $I$ is the detected intensity, and $\mu(x,y)$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** at spatial coordinate $(x,y)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — "Slicing a Loaf of Bread Without Cutting It"**
> Imagine you want to know what is *inside* a sealed, decorated cake without actually slicing it. You hold a bright flashlight behind the cake and trace the shadow (silhouette) on a piece of paper. The shadow gives you a **1D profile** of the cake's density along that viewing direction. Now, you walk around the cake and repeat the shadow-tracing from **hundreds of different angles**. A computer then mathematically *overlays* all these shadows. Where many shadows agree on a "dense spot," the computer marks it bright. The result is a complete, layered **map of the inside** — a CT image.

This is exactly how Computed Tomography works:
- **Light source** → X-ray tube (produces a fan/cone beam)
- **Shadow** → X-ray attenuation profile (projection)
- **Walking around the cake** → Rotation of source–detector around the object
- **Mathematical overlay** → Image reconstruction algorithm (Radon inversion)

### 1.3 Why CT is Considered the "Gold Standard" of NDT

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Significance**
> - Provides **full volumetric (3D) information**, unlike 2D radiography.
> - Eliminates **superposition / superimposition errors** inherent in film radiography.
> - Allows inspection of **complex geometries** (turbine blades, additively manufactured parts, composite layups).
> - Detects defects such as **voids, cracks, inclusions, delaminations, porosity, and density variations** with micron-level resolution.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Definitions

| Term | Symbol | Typical Engineering Value |
|------|--------|---------------------------|
| X-ray photon energy | $E$ | **20 keV – 450 keV** (industrial range) |
| Linear attenuation coefficient | $\mu$ | **0.1 – 50 cm⁻¹** (depends on material & energy) |
| Spatial resolution | $\delta$ | **1 µm – 500 µm** (µCT to industrial CT) |
| Voxel size | $V$ | **5 µm – 1 mm** |
| Density discrimination | $\Delta \rho / \rho$ | **0.1 % – 1 %** |
| Inspection volume | — | **few mm³ to ~ 1 m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Principle — X-ray Attenuation

When a polychromatic (or monochromatic) X-ray beam passes through a material, its intensity decreases due to four primary interaction mechanisms:

1. **Photoelectric absorption** (dominant at low energies, $E < 100$ keV)
2. **Compton scattering** (dominant at medium energies, $E = 100$ – 1000 keV)
3. **Rayleigh (coherent) scattering** (small contribution)
4. **Pair production** (only at $E > 1.022$ MeV — typically NOT used in industrial CT)

The combined effect is captured in the **mass attenuation coefficient** $\mu_m$, related to $\mu$ by:

$$\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$$

where $\rho$ is the local material density.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Acquisition

The CT scanning process follows a strict pipeline:

- **Step 1: Emission** — An X-ray tube emits a cone or fan beam (or a pencil beam in 1st-gen systems).
- **Step 2: Attenuation** — The beam is differentially attenuated as it traverses the object; the exit intensity $I$ carries integrated density information.
- **Step 3: Detection** — A linear (1D) or area (2D) detector array records $I$ at every angular position $\theta$ and translational position $t$.
- **Step 4: Rotation** — The source–detector pair rotates around the object (or vice versa) through $0° \le \theta \le 360°$ in discrete angular steps $\Delta \theta$.
- **Step 5: Projection Data** — All projections are stacked into a **sinogram** $P(t, \theta)$.
- **Step 6: Reconstruction** — A mathematical algorithm (Radon inversion, FBP, or iterative) converts the sinogram into a 2D slice $\mu(x,y)$.
- **Step 7: Stack & Visualize** — Adjacent slices are stacked to form a 3D voxel array and rendered for analysis.

### 2.3 The Radon Transform — Mathematical Foundation

> [!NOTE] **Historical Note**
> The mathematical underpinning of CT was formulated by **Johann Radon (1917)**. The Nobel Prize in Medicine (1979) was awarded to **Cormack and Hounsfield** for the practical invention of CT.

For a 2D object with attenuation function $f(x,y)$, the **Radon Transform** $\mathcal{R}\{f\}$ is the line integral of $f$ along a straight line parameterized by $t$ (perpendicular distance from origin) and $\theta$ (angle of the line):

$$\mathcal{R}\{f\}(t, \theta) = P(t, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, ds$$

This is the equation describing a single projection. CT reconstruction is the **inverse Radon transform**:

$$f(x, y) = \mathcal{R}^{-1}\{P(t, \theta)\}$$

### 2.4 Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — The Workhorse Algorithm

The classical reconstruction algorithm is **Filtered Back-Projection**, which has two stages:

**Stage A — Filtering in Frequency Domain:**
Each projection $P(t, \theta)$ is Fourier-transformed, multiplied by the **ramp filter** $\vert \omega \vert$, and inverse-transformed.

$$\tilde{P}(t, \theta) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}_{\omega \rightarrow t}\left[\vert \omega \vert \cdot \mathcal{F}_{t \rightarrow \omega}\{P(t, \theta)\}\right]$$

**Stage B — Back-Projection:**
The filtered projections are smeared back across the image plane and summed over all angles.

$$f(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\; \theta)\, d\theta$$

### 2.5 Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) — Standardized Scale

> [!IMPORTANT] **Industry-Standard Image Scale**
> Although primarily a medical CT unit, the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is also used in industrial CT for cross-material comparison:

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{material}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Material | Typical HU |
|----------|------------|
| Air | **−1000** |
| Water | 0 |
| Aluminium | **+2300** |
| Steel | **+7000 to +12000** |
| Titanium | **+10000** |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Save This Cheat Sheet for ESE Day**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \exp(-\mu x)$ | Beer–Lambert (monochromatic, homogeneous) | $I$ in counts/s |
| 2 | $I = I_0 \exp\!\left(-\int \mu\, ds\right)$ | Beer–Lambert (inhomogeneous path) | — |
| 3 | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$ | Linear vs. mass attenuation | cm⁻¹ vs. cm²/g |
| 4 | $\mu \approx K \frac{Z^n}{E^3}$ | Photoelectric dominance (K-edge effects) | — |
| 5 | $\sigma_c \propto Z/E$ | Compton linear coefficient trend | — |
| 6 | $P(t, \theta) = \int f(x,y)\, ds$ | Radon transform (projection) | dimensionless / mm |
| 7 | $f(x,y) = \int_0^\pi \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)\, d\theta$ | Back-projection | mm⁻¹ |
| 8 | $HU = 1000\,(\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | Hounsfield Unit | HU |
| 9 | $SNR \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}}$ | CT signal-to-noise scaling | — |
| 10 | $d_{\min} \approx 1.22 \lambda \cdot \frac{SDD}{SDO}$ | Geometric resolution (Fresnel) | mm |
| 11 | $\Delta t = \text{pitch} / N_{\text{det}}$ | Slice thickness (helical CT) | mm |
| 12 | $\text{CTDI} \propto N_{\text{slices}} \cdot I \cdot t$ | CT Dose Index (safety) | mGy |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades, CFRP composite layups, rocket nozzle liners for voids and impact damage.
- **Additive Manufacturing:** Layer-by-layer porosity analysis in 3D-printed metal parts.
- **Electronics:** Flip-chip bump inspection, BGA solder void quantification.
- **Automotive:** Battery cell inspection, casting porosity in cylinder heads.
- **Heritage & Archaeology:** Non-invasive analysis of artefacts, fossils, and mummies.
- **Geosciences & Petroleum:** Core-plug analysis for pore-network characterization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beer–Lambert Law from First Principles

Consider a thin slab of material of thickness $dx$ struck by photons of intensity $I(x)$. The probability of a photon interaction per unit path length is $\mu$, so the number of photons lost in $dx$ is:

$$dI = -\mu \cdot I(x)\, dx$$

Separating variables and integrating from $0$ to $x$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu x$$

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0 \exp(-\mu x)\,}$$

For an **inhomogeneous** object, the path integral form is obtained by slicing the path into infinitesimal elements $ds$:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0 \exp\!\left(-\int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(s)\, ds\right)\,}$$

The measurable quantity is the **projection**:

$$P(t, \theta) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int_{\mathcal{L}} \mu(s)\, ds$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Interpretation**
> The detector measures $I$, but the algorithm operates on the **log-attenuation** $P = -\ln(I/I_0)$. The term $-\ln(I/I_0)$ is called the **"projection data"** or **"line integral"** and is the actual input to the reconstruction algorithm.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coordinate Rotation for the Radon Transform

The line of integration $\mathcal{L}$ passing through point $(x,y)$ at angle $\theta$ and perpendicular distance $t$ from the origin satisfies:

$$t = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$$

A parametric representation of the line in terms of $s$ (arc length along the line):

$$x' = t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, \quad y' = t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta$$

Substituting these into the projection integral:

$$P(t, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu\!\left(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta\right) ds$$

This is the **Radon Transform** $\mathcal{R}\{\mu\}(t,\theta)$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)

This theorem is the bridge between projections and reconstructed images.

**Statement:** The 1D Fourier transform of a projection $P(t, \theta)$ with respect to $t$ equals a slice through the 2D Fourier transform of $\mu(x,y)$ at angle $\theta$ in frequency space.

Starting with the 2D Fourier transform of $\mu(x,y)$:

$$F(u, v) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(ux + vy)}\, dx\, dy$$

Converting to rotated coordinates $t, s$ via the rotation matrix:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta \\
y &= t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta
\end{aligned}$$

Since $u^2 + v^2 = \rho^2$ and along a radial slice the Jacobian is unity:

$$F(\rho\cos\theta, \rho\sin\theta) = \int P(t, \theta)\, e^{-j2\pi \rho t}\, dt = \mathcal{F}_t\{P(t, \theta)\}$$

**This means:** Every projection $P(t, \theta)$ is a *radial line* in 2D Fourier space. By acquiring projections at all $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, we fill Fourier space, and $\mu(x,y)$ is recovered by the inverse 2D FFT.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ramp Filter Derivation (FBP)

Starting from the inverse 2D Fourier transform of $F(u,v)$ in polar coordinates:

$$\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\infty} F(\rho, \theta)\, e^{j2\pi \rho(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)}\, \rho\, d\rho\, d\theta$$

Using the Central Slice Theorem $F(\rho, \theta) = \mathcal{F}_t\{P(t,\theta)\}$ and exploiting symmetry of projections ($P(t, \theta+\pi) = P(-t, \theta)$), the angular range reduces to $[0, \pi]$:

$$\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi}\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \rho \vert \mathcal{F}_t\{P(t,\theta)\}\, e^{j2\pi \rho t}\, d\rho\right]_{t = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta} d\theta$$

The inner integral is recognized as the inverse 1D FT of $\vert \rho \vert \cdot \mathcal{F}\{P\}$, which equals the **filtered projection** $\tilde{P}(t, \theta)$. Thus:

$$\boxed{\,\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\; \theta)\, d\theta\,}$$

The function $\vert \rho \vert$ in the frequency domain is called the **ramp filter** because of its linear magnitude profile.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Simple Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE] **Typical 2-mark sub-question in KTU ESE**

**Problem:** A 50 mm thick aluminium plate ($\mu_{Al} = 0.42 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 100 keV) is scanned by an industrial CT system. The detector counts are 50,000 photons in 1 second with the X-ray source ON but no object, and 12,000 photons per second when the object is placed. Determine:
1. The measured projection $P = -\ln(I/I_0)$.
2. The reconstructed linear attenuation coefficient assuming uniform material.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Compute the log-attenuation (the projection value):

$$P = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{12{,}000}{50{,}000}\right) = -\ln(0.24)$$

$$P = -(-1.4271) = 1.4271$$

**Step (b):** Convert to linear attenuation coefficient using $P = \mu x$:

$$\mu = \frac{P}{x} = \frac{1.4271}{5.0 \text{ cm}} = 0.2854 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Engineering Comment:** The measured $\mu$ differs from the tabulated $\mu_{Al} = 0.42$ because the laboratory X-ray beam is **polychromatic** (beam-hardening effect). Correction algorithms (e.g., pre-filter, dual-energy) are required for quantitative CT.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CT Generations

| Generation | Beam Geometry | Detector | Motion | Speed | Use Case |
|------------|---------------|----------|--------|-------|----------|
| **1st Gen** (Translate–Rotate) | Pencil beam | Single detector | Translate + Rotate | Very slow | Hounsfield's first scanner |
| **2nd Gen** (Translate–Rotate, fan) | Narrow fan | Linear array (~30) | Translate + Rotate | Slow | Early industrial CT |
| **3rd Gen** (Rotate–Rotate) | Wide fan | Curved linear array (~1000) | Rotate only | Fast | Modern medical CT |
| **4th Gen** (Rotate–Stationary) | Wide fan | Ring of stationary detectors | Source rotates | Very fast | High-speed industrial |
| **5th Gen** (Electron-beam) | Stationary e-beam | Ring of detectors | No mechanical motion | Ultra-fast | Cardiac imaging |
| **6th Gen (Helical / Spiral)** | Cone/fan | Multi-row | Continuous + translate | Volumetric | Standard modern CT |
| **7th Gen (Cone-beam CT)** | Cone | Flat-panel 2D | Rotate ± tilt | High resolution | Industrial µCT, NDT |

### 3.7 Image Quality Parameters — Quantitative Definitions

- **Spatial Resolution (SR):** Smallest distance between two resolvable features. Determined by focal spot size, detector pitch, and reconstruction kernel.

- **Contrast-to-Noise Ratio (CNR):**

$$CNR = \frac{\vert \mu_A - \mu_B \vert}{\sigma_{\text{background}}}$$

- **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF):** Frequency-domain measure of resolution; an edge or line-spread function is used.

- **Artifact Index (AI):** Quantification of streak, ring, beam-hardening, or motion artifacts in the reconstructed volume.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CT System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HVPS[High-Voltage Power Supply] --> XRT[X-ray Tube]
    XRT --> COLL[Collimator and Beam Shaping Optics]
    COLL --> OBJ[Test Object on Rotary Stage]
    OBJ --> DET[Linear or Area Detector Array]
    DET --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System with Preamplifier]
    DAQ --> PRE[Pre-processing, Flat-field and Log-normalization]
    PRE --> SINO[Sinogram Assembly in Memory]
    SINO --> FBP[Filtered Back-Projection Engine]
    SINO --> ITER[Iterative Reconstruction Engine]
    FBP --> VOL[3D Voxel Volume Builder]
    ITER --> VOL
    VOL --> VIEW[2D Slice and 3D Rendering Viewer]
    VOL --> ANALYZE[Defect Analysis, Dimensional Metrology, Porosity]
    ROT[Rotary Encoder and Stepper Motor Controller] --> OBJ
    CTRL[Central Console and Operator Interface] --> HVPS
    CTRL --> ROT
    CTRL --> DAQ
    CTRL --> FBP
    CTRL --> ITER
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **X-ray tube** (XRT) emits photons, shaped by a **collimator** (COLL) into a fan or cone beam.
- The **test object** (OBJ) sits on a precision **rotary stage** controlled by ROT.
- Transmitted X-rays strike the **detector** (DET), producing a 1D or 2D attenuation profile.
- The **DAQ** digitizes the signal, which is **pre-processed** to form projections.
- Projections are stored as a **sinogram** (SINO), the input to the reconstruction engines.
- The **reconstructed voxel volume** (VOL) is fed to a **viewer** (VIEW) and a quantitative **analysis module** (ANALYZE).
- A **central console** (CTRL) synchronizes all subsystems.

### 4.2 The CT Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Detector Data I_raw] --> B[Dark-current Subtraction]
    B --> C[Flat-field Normalization I / I_0]
    C --> D[Logarithmic Inversion -ln I/I_0]
    D --> E[Ring-correction and Sinogram Assembly]
    E --> F[Ramp Filtering in Frequency Domain]
    F --> G[Back-Projection Across Image Grid]
    G --> H[Reconstructed 2D Slice]
    H --> I[Stacking of Slices into 3D Volume]
    I --> J[Volume Rendering and Analysis]
```

### 4.3 Coordinate Geometry of a CT Projection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[X-ray Source] --> BEAM[Fan or Cone Beam]
    BEAM --> OBJ2[Object at Origin]
    OBJ2 --> ARR[Detector Array at Distance D]
    subgraph Coordinate Frame
        T[Parameter t, perpendicular distance from origin to beam centerline]
        THETA[Parameter theta, source-detector rotation angle]
    end
    ARR --> PROJ[Projection P of t comma theta]
    PROJ --> SIN[Sinogram Row at fixed theta]
```

### 4.4 Scanner-Generation Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[1st Gen: Pencil + Single Detector] --> G2[2nd Gen: Narrow Fan + Linear Detector]
    G2 --> G3[3rd Gen: Wide Fan + Curved Array]
    G3 --> G4[4th Gen: Stationary Ring + Rotating Source]
    G4 --> G5[5th Gen: Electron Beam, No Motion]
    G5 --> G6[6th Gen: Helical or Spiral, Continuous Motion]
    G6 --> G7[7th Gen: Cone Beam, Flat-Panel Detector]
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Takeaway**
> Each generation trades **speed**, **cost**, and **artifact level**. Industrial CT systems for NDT typically use **3rd-gen fan-beam** or **7th-gen cone-beam** architectures for the best balance of resolution, speed, and quantitative accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Computed Tomography. State **any two advantages** of CT over conventional film radiography.

**Model Answer:**

Computed Tomography (CT) is a Non-Destructive Evaluation technique that produces cross-sectional (2D) and volumetric (3D) images of an object by reconstructing the spatial distribution of its **linear X-ray attenuation coefficient** from X-ray transmission measurements acquired at multiple angular orientations. Mathematically, it is the **inverse Radon transform** of the projection data.

Two advantages over film radiography:
1. **Eliminates superimposition** — each slice isolates a thin layer, removing ambiguity from overlapping features.
2. **Provides 3D volumetric data** — defect location, orientation, and size can be measured in all three dimensions.
3. **Quantitative** — pixel values map to actual $\mu$ or HU, enabling material discrimination.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is a **sinogram**? Why is it called so?

**Model Answer:**

A **sinogram** is the 2D data array $P(t, \theta)$ formed by stacking all 1D projections $P(t, \theta_k)$ of an object, one row per angular position $\theta_k$. Each point in the object traces a **sinusoidal curve** in the $(t, \theta)$ plane, hence the name "**sino**-gram." It is the direct input to the reconstruction algorithm (FBP or iterative) and contains all the information needed to reconstruct the cross-sectional image.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **CT number (HU)** with **grayscale value**; HU is calibrated to water.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **logarithmic step** $-\ln(I/I_0)$ when writing the projection; examiners deduct full marks if you write only $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
> - **Do NOT** claim CT is a "2D radiography" — it is fundamentally a **tomographic 3D** modality.
> - **Do NOT** use Radon transform and FBP interchangeably; they are related but distinct.
> - Always state **units** for $\mu$ (cm⁻¹ or mm⁻¹).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the principle and working of a **3rd-generation fan-beam industrial CT system**. List its main components and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** In a 3rd-generation CT system, a **wide fan-shaped X-ray beam** from a single source illuminates a **curved linear detector array** containing $\sim 1000$ detector elements. The source–detector assembly rotates around the stationary object in a single rotational motion (no translation), collecting projections at every angular position.

**Main components and their roles:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **X-ray tube** | Generates the fan-beam photon flux. Energy chosen based on material and thickness (typically 50–450 keV). |
| **Collimator** | Defines the fan-beam aperture, controls scatter, and limits patient/dosage. |
| **Rotary stage (gantry)** | Provides precise, calibrated rotation with angular encoders. |
| **Curved detector array** | Captures all rays of the fan simultaneously — eliminates translate motion. |
| **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** | Amplifies, integrates, and digitizes detector signals per projection angle. |
| **Computer + reconstruction software** | Performs FBP / iterative reconstruction to obtain the slice. |
| **Display console** | Visualizes 2D slices and 3D renderings. |

**Working Steps:**
1. Object is placed on rotary stage between source and detector.
2. The X-ray tube emits a fan beam; each detector element records $I$.
3. Gantry rotates through $0° \le \theta \le 360°$ in steps of $\Delta\theta \approx 0.3°$ to $1°$.
4. For each $\theta$, the system logs the projection $P(t, \theta) = -\ln(I/I_0)$.
5. The full sinogram is reconstructed via Filtered Back-Projection.
6. The 2D slice is displayed; further slices are stacked for 3D analysis.

**Engineering advantage:** Faster than 1st and 2nd generation because the entire fan is captured per angular step — no translation needed.

---

**(b)** Derive the **Radon Transform** of a 2D attenuation function $f(x,y)$. State and explain the **Central Slice Theorem** with its engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Radon Transform Derivation:**

For a 2D object characterized by $f(x, y)$, consider a straight line $\mathcal{L}$ in the $xy$-plane defined by the perpendicular distance $t$ from the origin and the angle $\theta$ the normal makes with the $x$-axis. The line can be parameterized as:

$$x = t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta, \quad y = t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta$$

where $s$ is the arc length measured along the line. The Radon transform is the line integral of $f$ over $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\boxed{\,P(t, \theta) = \mathcal{R}\{f\}(t, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; t\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, ds\,}$$

This integral is precisely the **measured projection** at angle $\theta$ in CT, and represents the total attenuation of the X-ray along the line.

**Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem):**

**Statement:** The 1D Fourier transform of a projection $P(t, \theta)$ with respect to $t$ equals a 1D radial slice through the 2D Fourier transform $F(u, v)$ of the object $f(x, y)$ at the same angle $\theta$:

$$\mathcal{F}_t\{P(t, \theta)\} = F(\rho\cos\theta, \rho\sin\theta) = F(u, v)\big|_{u = \rho\cos\theta,\, v = \rho\sin\theta}$$

**Engineering Significance:**
1. **Bridges projection and image domains** — allows reconstruction in Fourier space.
2. **Drives Filtered Back-Projection** — the ramp filter $\vert \rho \vert$ is a direct consequence of converting polar to Cartesian coordinates.
3. **Explains undersampling artifacts** — insufficient angular sampling leaves gaps in Fourier space, producing streak artifacts.
4. **Justifies Nyquist sampling** — angular sampling rate must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency of the object for faithful reconstruction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm. With a block diagram, illustrate its two main stages and write the mathematical expressions for each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** FBP is the most widely used analytical reconstruction algorithm in CT. It converts a sinogram $P(t, \theta)$ into a 2D slice $\mu(x, y)$ via two sequential stages: **filtering** and **back-projection**.

**Block Diagram of FBP:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sinogram P of t comma theta] --> F1[Stage 1: 1D FFT of each projection]
    F1 --> F2[Multiply by Ramp Filter absolute value of omega]
    F2 --> F3[1D Inverse FFT to obtain filtered projection P-tilde]
    F3 --> B1[Stage 2: Smear back P-tilde across image grid at angle theta]
    B1 --> B2[Sum over all theta from 0 to pi]
    B2 --> OUT[Reconstructed Image mu of x comma y]
```

**Stage 1 — Filtering (Frequency Domain):**

For each projection angle $\theta$:

1. Compute the 1D Fourier transform: $\mathcal{F}_t\{P(t, \theta)\}$.
2. Multiply by the **ramp filter** $\vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain.
3. Compute the inverse 1D FT to obtain the **filtered projection** $\tilde{P}(t, \theta)$:

$$\tilde{P}(t, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \omega \vert \cdot \mathcal{F}_t\{P(t, \theta)\}\, e^{j2\pi \omega t}\, d\omega$$

The ramp filter compensates for the $1/\vert \rho \vert$ density of samples in Fourier space, restoring low-frequency information and sharpening edges.

**Stage 2 — Back-Projection:**

The filtered projection is "smeared back" along the direction of acquisition and integrated over all angles:

$$\boxed{\,\mu(x, y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta,\; \theta)\, d\theta\,}$$

**Discretization in practice:**

$$\mu(x, y) \approx \Delta\theta \sum_{k = 1}^{N_\theta} \tilde{P}(x\cos\theta_k + y\sin\theta_k,\; \theta_k)$$

**Engineering Note:** Without the ramp filter (i.e., direct back-projection), the reconstructed image would be blurred by a $1/r$ point-spread function. The ramp filter removes this intrinsic blur.

---

**(b)** A cylindrical aluminium rod of diameter **40 mm** is scanned with a monoenergetic 100 keV X-ray beam. The measured intensity without the rod is $I_0 = 80{,}000$ counts/s. With the rod, the count is $I = 22{,}500$ counts/s. Given $\mu_{Al} = 0.42 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 100 keV:
1. Calculate the **projection value** $P$.
2. Compare with the **theoretical** $P$ and comment.
3. Define **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** and compute the HU of aluminium relative to water ($\mu_w = 0.167 \text{ cm}^{-1}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the measured projection $P$:**

$$P = -\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\frac{22{,}500}{80{,}000}\right) = -\ln(0.28125)$$

$$P = -(-1.2685) \approx 1.269$$

**Step 2: Compute the theoretical projection assuming monoenergetic beam:**

$$P_{\text{theory}} = \mu_{Al} \cdot x = 0.42 \text{ cm}^{-1} \times 4.0 \text{ cm} = 1.68$$

**Comment:** The measured $P$ is **lower** than the theoretical monoenergetic value because **polychromatic X-ray beams** undergo **beam hardening** — low-energy photons are preferentially absorbed, leaving a "harder" (higher mean energy) beam. The effective $\mu$ in the lab is lower than the tabulated monoenergetic value. Correction methods include pre-filtering with metal sheets, dual-energy CT, or post-reconstruction polynomial correction.

**Step 3: Hounsfield Unit (HU):**

**Definition:** HU is a linear transformation of the linear attenuation coefficient, normalized to water:

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{material}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Computation for aluminium:**

$$HU_{Al} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.42 - 0.167}{0.167} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.253}{0.167}$$

$$HU_{Al} \approx 1000 \times 1.515 = 1515$$

**Result:** $HU_{Al} \approx +1515$ HU, indicating aluminium is much denser (more attenuating) than water. (Note: the exact value depends on the X-ray energy spectrum and reference water $\mu$ at the same energy.)

**Engineering Application:** In multi-material industrial CT, HU values allow **automatic segmentation** of constituents (e.g., separating aluminium alloy from a polymer-encased component).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize before KTU ESE)**

- **CT** = **Computed Tomography** = volumetric NDT based on **inverse Radon transform** of X-ray projections.
- **Core equation:** $I = I_0 \exp(-\int \mu\, ds)$ → projection $P = -\ln(I/I_0)$.
- **Algorithm:** **Filtered Back-Projection** = filter (ramp $\vert \omega \vert$) + back-project.
- **Central Slice Theorem:** 1D FT of a projection = radial slice of 2D FT of object.
- **CT generations:** 1st (pencil, translate-rotate) → 7th (cone-beam, flat-panel).
- **HU formula:** $HU = 1000(\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$.
- **Beam hardening:** polchromatic X-rays cause measured $\mu < $ monoenergetic $\mu$; corrected by pre-filter, dual-energy, or post-processing.
- **Resolution drivers:** focal spot size, detector pitch, geometric magnification, voxel size.
- **CT advantages:** no superimposition, full 3D, quantitative $\mu$, micron-level defect detection.
- **CT limitations:** cost, scan time, radiation safety, beam-hardening artifacts, scatter in dense parts.
- **Hounsfield scale anchors:** Air = **−1000 HU**; Water = **0 HU**; Metal = **+several thousand HU**.
- **Sinogram:** $P(t, \theta)$ 2D array — one row per angle, one column per detector channel.
- **Industrial vs. medical CT:** industrial uses higher energies (up to 9 MeV linac), denser materials, and flat-panel cone-beam detectors.
- **Standards to remember:** ASTM E1441 (CT practice), ASTM E1570 (CT system description), ASTM E1695 (performance).
- **Common CT artifacts:** beam hardening (cupping), ring (detector drift), motion blur, metal streak, scatter, partial volume.
- **Quick relation:** $CNR \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}}$, so increasing exposure (longer dwell, higher mA) improves contrast at the cost of dose.
- **Engineering use cases:** turbine blade inspection, AM part porosity, weld defect mapping, electronics BGA voiding, core analysis, composite delamination.
